title: Head, Customer Relationship Management
angle: Active Bombora intent (Email Marketing), strong digital spend, agency path via Rain the Growth Agency.

contacts.name: Brandon Dansie
contacts.title: Head, Customer Relationship Management
contacts.note: Primary advertiser-side contact identified via Winmo. Verify current role and contact details before outreach. (Source: Winmo)

sales_plays.title: CRM Signal to Premium Inventory Expansion
sales_plays.summary: 1-800 Contacts is an active customer with a won PMP deal and Bombora showing live intent around Email Marketing this week. Brandon Dansie owns CRM — this is a direct line to expand beyond the existing PMP into addressable and data-driven premium formats.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Brandon Dansie directly referencing the existing TWC Interactive PMP win from July 2024 and note that Bombora is showing 1-800 Contacts spiking on Email Marketing intent this week — position this as a timely reason to reconnect on expanding the relationship.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Brandon how the current PMP performance has informed their thinking on layering addressable or first-party data-driven buys on top of the existing deal — specifically whether Rain the Growth Agency is evaluating any incremental CRM-connected media channels for H2.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Bridge from the PMP foundation to premium on-property inventory — highlight that 1-800 Contacts' $60.9M digital footprint and $58.6M in Paid Social signal a brand comfortable with scale, and that CRM-connected addressable formats can close the loop between their email marketing intent and paid media reach.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The existing PMP closed at $1 in recorded value, suggesting it was a test or pilot entry point. With $60.9M in total digital spend and $43.6M in Search alone, even a 2-3% reallocation toward premium addressable inventory represents $1.2M-$1.8M in incremental opportunity — frame the expansion in those terms.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Brandon for a 20-minute call in the next two weeks to walk through one specific addressable format that maps to his CRM goals — offer to loop in Rain the Growth Agency's media planning team so the conversation is immediately actionable.

sales_plays.title: Displace Display & Online Video Underinvestment
sales_plays.summary: 1-800 Contacts is spending only $65K in Display and $461K in Online Video against a $60.9M digital base — a clear signal that a competitor or lower-quality vendor is holding those line items, or the budget is simply misallocated. This play targets Rain the Growth Agency to displace the incumbent and shift dollars to higher-impact premium video and display.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Rain the Growth Agency's media planning team (AOR for media planning and buying) with a benchmark observation: 1-800 Contacts is allocating only $65K to Display and $461K to Online Video against a $60.9M digital spend — that's less than 1% of digital budget in formats where brand-safe, premium inventory drives measurable lift for DTC health brands.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask the Rain the Growth Agency team who currently holds the Display and Online Video mandates for 1-800 Contacts, and whether those placements are being measured against the same performance KPIs as their $43.6M Search investment.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position premium CTV and Online Video inventory as the natural complement to 1-800 Contacts' existing $11.7M CTV spend — the Display and Online Video line items are underweight relative to CTV, and consolidating those formats under a single premium partner eliminates fragmentation and improves attribution for Brandon Dansie's CRM reporting.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If Display and Online Video were brought to even 2% of the $60.9M digital budget, that represents $1.2M — nearly 2x the current combined $526K. Frame the displacement as a budget efficiency story, not a net-new ask, since the dollars are already in the plan.

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a working session with Rain the Growth Agency's media planning lead within the next 30 days — offer a competitive audit showing where the current $526K in Display and Online Video is being placed versus where premium inventory would deliver better reach and brand-safe context for a health/vision DTC brand.

talk_track_opener: Hey Brandon, I'm reaching out because we have an existing relationship with 1-800 Contacts through the TWC Interactive PMP we closed last year, and I want to make sure we're building on that foundation rather than letting it sit as a one-time deal. You're the right person to talk to given your CRM ownership — and the timing feels right because our intent data is showing 1-800 Contacts actively researching Email Marketing solutions this week, which tells me there's a broader data-driven media conversation happening internally right now. Here's what caught my attention: 1-800 Contacts is running $60.9M in digital spend, including $58.6M in Paid Social and $43.6M in Search, but Display and Online Video together are only at $526K — less than 1% of your digital investment. That gap is either intentional or an opportunity, and I'd love to understand which. Can we get 20 minutes on the calendar with you and Rain the Growth Agency's planning team to talk through whether addressable and premium video formats could extend what your CRM data is already doing in paid channels?
open_questions: Since the TWC Interactive PMP closed in July 2024, how has 1-800 Contacts measured its performance, and has Rain the Growth Agency used those learnings to inform any expansion of programmatic or direct buys?

Who at Rain the Growth Agency owns the Display and Online Video line items for 1-800 Contacts, and are those placements currently being evaluated against the same performance benchmarks as Search and Paid Social?

Given that you own CRM at 1-800 Contacts, is there an active initiative to connect first-party email or subscriber data to paid media targeting — and if so, which channels is Rain prioritizing for that integration?

With $43.6M in Search spend, how is 1-800 Contacts thinking about upper-funnel investment to feed that Search demand — and is there a budget conversation happening for H2 around brand awareness formats like CTV or premium video?

Is Critical Mass involved in any digital media creative decisions that would affect how Display or Online Video assets are developed, and would they need to be part of a conversation about expanding into new premium inventory formats?

angle: Meaningful digital spend and a visible agency path via Colle McVoy.

sales_plays.title: Filtrete CTV-to-Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: 3M's Filtrete brand is already active on TWC properties (Amazon Q4 wins on Allergy, Very Hot, Very Cold) and spending $10.6M in CTV. The opportunity is to convert that programmatic/Amazon relationship into a direct, premium CTV and weather-triggered package before the spring allergy and summer heat planning window opens.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three Q4 2025 Filtrete Amazon wins (Allergy, Very Hot, Very Cold) and note that the spring allergy season planning window is approaching. Email or call Colle McVoy AOR contact and say: 'We delivered on all three Filtrete Q4 buys — Allergy, Very Hot, and Very Cold. I want to show you how a direct TWC deal for Q2 spring allergy could outperform the Amazon programmatic path on CPM efficiency and audience precision.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'For Filtrete's spring allergy push, is the Q2 budget currently allocated to the Amazon programmatic line, or is there a separate direct-buy pool at Colle McVoy we should be talking to?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered CTV inventory against the Amazon programmatic path — real-time pollen index and temperature targeting means Filtrete ads serve exactly when consumers are symptomatic, not just when a keyword fires. The two lost TWC-Interactive standard banner opps ($50K Q4'25, $100K Q1'26) signal the team is evaluating interactive formats; bundle those into the CTV pitch to close the display gap simultaneously.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 3M is spending $10.6M in CTV and $76.9M in total measured media. Even a 5% shift of the CTV budget from programmatic to direct TWC equals ~$530K in incremental direct revenue. Frame it to Colle McVoy as a cost-per-symptomatic-moment metric vs. a flat CPM — weather-triggered delivery typically reduces wasted impressions by 20-30% in comparable seasonal health campaigns.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Colle McVoy for a 30-minute Q2 planning call in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 30 minutes before end of [current month] to walk through a Filtrete spring allergy weather-trigger proposal? I'll bring a comp analysis of the Amazon programmatic CPMs vs. our direct CTV rate card so you have something concrete to take to the 3M team.'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars with TWC Premium Digital
sales_plays.summary: 3M is allocating $24M of its $55M digital budget to Paid Social — the single largest digital line item. With two consecutive TWC-Interactive losses ($50K and $100K) and no visible media-focused decision maker, the play is to route through Colle McVoy to reframe a portion of that Paid Social spend toward TWC's premium digital and online video inventory, where contextual relevance and brand-safe environments are a credible differentiator.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the spend data as a conversation opener with Colle McVoy: 'We can see that 3M's Paid Social line is running around $24M — the largest single digital channel in the mix. Given the brand-safety and measurement scrutiny social platforms are under right now, I'd like to share how TWC's premium digital environment has been performing for comparable consumer brands in the safety and home-care space.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'On the two TWC-Interactive proposals that didn't close in Q4'25 and Q1'26 — was the barrier budget timing, format fit, or a preference to consolidate spend on the social platforms? Understanding that helps me come back with something that actually fits the plan.'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Address the lost interactive banner opps directly. The $50K and $100K losses suggest either format or timing friction, not a relationship problem (given the Amazon wins). Propose a TWC premium digital package — Online Video + Internet Display + weather-contextual placements — as a brand-safe, measurable alternative to a portion of the $24M Paid Social allocation, specifically for 3M's Consumer and Office or Safety segments where contextual relevance is high.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): $24M in Paid Social at industry-average CPMs for a brand of 3M's scale implies significant reach but limited contextual signal. A $500K–$1M test reallocation to TWC Online Video ($1.8M current) and Display ($3.6M current) would represent a 14–28% expansion of 3M's existing TWC digital footprint and give Colle McVoy a clean A/B data set to justify further shifts at the next planning cycle.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer a competitive displacement brief: 'I can put together a one-page comparison of TWC premium digital vs. the top social platforms on brand-safety scores, viewability, and contextual relevance for 3M's consumer segments. Would that be useful to have ahead of your next internal planning review? I can have it to you within five business days.'

talk_track_opener: Hey [Colle McVoy contact], I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with the 3M Filtrete team through the Amazon channel — we closed three buys together in Q4 on the Allergy, Very Hot, and Very Cold segments — so I'm not a cold call here. I work with the direct and agency teams to help brands like Filtrete get more out of their weather-contextual media, especially when seasonal moments like spring allergy season are coming up fast. I was looking at 3M's overall media mix and noticed that Paid Social is running at roughly $24M — the biggest single digital line — while the TWC-Interactive proposals from Q4 and Q1 didn't close. I'd love to understand what was behind that so I can come back with something that actually fits the plan. And separately, with spring allergy planning probably starting now, is there a conversation worth having about moving the Filtrete CTV and digital buys from the Amazon programmatic path to a direct TWC deal where we can trigger on real-time pollen and temperature data? Happy to keep it to 30 minutes — just want to make sure you have the right options on the table before budgets lock.
open_questions: On the two TWC-Interactive proposals that didn't close — the $50K Q4'25 and $100K Q1'26 buys — was the sticking point budget timing, format preference, or something else we should address in the next proposal?

For Filtrete's spring allergy season, is the Q2 digital and CTV budget currently allocated to the Amazon programmatic line, or is there a separate direct-buy pool at Colle McVoy we should be working with?
Who at 3M or Colle McVoy owns the decision on shifting spend from Paid Social to premium digital channels — is that a brand-side call or does it sit with the agency investment team?

3M has six major business segments in the mix — is Filtrete the only brand currently being planned for weather-contextual media, or are there other segments like Safety or Health Care where seasonal triggers are part of the brief?

With $10.6M already in CTV, is the team evaluating a shift from programmatic CTV to direct publisher deals for any of the 2026 planning cycles, and if so, what metrics are driving that conversation internally?

title: Director of Marketing, In-Store Operations
angle: Active Bombora intent (Entertainment). Strong digital, agency path via Universal McCann.

contacts.name: Jay Wilkins
contacts.title: Director of Marketing, In-Store Operations and Transformation
contacts.note: Primary advertiser-side contact identified via Winmo. Verify current role and contact details before outreach. (Source: Winmo)

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Direct Upgrade: Scale the Active Relationship
sales_plays.summary: 7-Eleven is an active PMP customer (two won deals in 2024-2025) with $49.8M in digital spend, $41.1M of which sits in Paid Social. There is a clear opportunity to convert the existing PMP relationship into a direct, higher-value buy and capture a larger share of the digital budget before Q3 summer road-trip and convenience-retail tentpoles.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the active PMP relationship directly — 'We've been running PMP together since mid-2024 and the setup is working. I want to show Jay Wilkins and the Universal McCann team what a direct buy layered on top of that PMP foundation could unlock in terms of reach, targeting depth, and brand-family coverage across Speedway, Stripes, and Laredo Taco Company.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Universal McCann: 'Of the $49.8M in digital, how much is currently allocated to weather-triggered or location-contextual placements — and is that a gap the team is actively trying to close heading into summer?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct inventory as the missing layer above the PMP floor — weather-contextual targeting reaches convenience shoppers at the exact moment temperature, precipitation, or travel conditions drive a store visit, something Paid Social's $41.1M cannot replicate with the same real-time relevance.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The two lost direct deals totaled $350K ($300K in 1Q'24, $50K in 4Q/1Q Holiday Travel). Reframe: 'If we convert even half of what was proposed in those two cycles into a standing direct commitment, we're talking $150K-$175K in incremental value on top of the PMP base — and you get continuity instead of a one-time flight.'

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute working session with Universal McCann's planning lead before end of this month to map Q3 summer road-trip inventory against 7-Eleven's convenience and fuel locations — low commitment, high planning value.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Waste: Weather-Contextual vs. Algorithmic Feed
sales_plays.summary: With $41.1M flowing into Paid Social and two consecutive lost direct deals ($300K in 1Q'24, $50K in 4Q/1Q '25), there is a pattern of budget concentration in social and resistance to direct commitments. This play challenges the efficiency of that allocation by surfacing the contextual gap Paid Social cannot fill for a convenience retailer whose purchase triggers are weather- and moment-driven.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the Bombora Entertainment intent signal as a conversation starter with Jay Wilkins: '7-Eleven is showing strong interest in Entertainment content this week — that's a signal your customers are in a discovery and snacking mindset. Are your current Paid Social placements reaching them in that context, or are they competing in a crowded feed?'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When Universal McCann evaluates the $41.1M in Paid Social performance, what does the attribution model look like for in-store lift — specifically for impulse categories like beverages, snacks, and fuel — versus what you're seeing from contextual or weather-triggered placements?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC as the displacement candidate for a defined slice of Paid Social spend — not a full replacement, but a reallocation of 5-10% toward weather-contextual inventory that reaches convenience shoppers when heat, cold snaps, or storm prep directly motivate a 7-Eleven visit across the Speedway and Stripes brand family.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 5% reallocation of the $41.1M Paid Social budget equals roughly $2M. Even a $500K-$1M test against TWC's weather-contextual and CTV inventory ($2.2M already in CTV) would represent a 23-45% expansion of the current TWC relationship and directly address the lost $300K 1Q'24 and $50K holiday direct deals.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive audit — offer to pull TWC audience overlap data against 7-Eleven's convenience-retail customer profile and share it with Universal McCann within two weeks, framed as a planning tool, not a pitch.

talk_track_opener: Hey Jay, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company — we've been working with your team and Universal McCann on the PMP side since 2024 and I'm glad that foundation is in place. I work with convenience and QSR retailers specifically and I've been looking closely at how brands like yours are thinking about the gap between social feed placements and real-time contextual moments.
open_questions: The PMP has been running since mid-2024 — how is Universal McCann currently measuring in-store lift attribution from that inventory, and is that data informing the Q3 summer planning cycle?
Of the $41.1M in Paid Social, how much is allocated to always-on versus tentpole or cultural-moment flights — and who at IPG MediaBrands owns the decision to test alternative contextual channels?

The $300K direct deal in 1Q'24 and the $50K holiday deal in 4Q/1Q '25 both went to loss — can you help me understand whether that was a budget timing issue, a value-gap in the proposal, or a competitive displacement by another publisher?
Is Jay Wilkins the right stakeholder for a conversation about expanding beyond the PMP into direct or CTV, or does that decision sit with a different contact at Universal McCann or IPG MediaBrands?

With Speedway, Stripes, and Laredo Taco Company each operating in distinct regional markets, is there a co-op or brand-family media budget structure we should be mapping to — or does everything flow through the 7-Eleven parent AOR relationship with Universal McCann?

title: VP, Brand Marketing and Customer Strategy
angle: Strong digital spend, agency path via Horizon Next.

contacts.name: Lindsey Schwartz
contacts.title: VP, Brand Marketing and Customer Strategy
contacts.note: Primary advertiser-side contact identified via Winmo. Verify current role and contact details before outreach. (Source: Winmo)

sales_plays.title: Shift Digital Dollars On-Property
sales_plays.summary: A Place for Mom runs $5M in digital spend (including $265K display, $255K CTV) almost entirely off-property through Kepler Group. This play targets Lindsey Schwartz to redirect a portion of that budget toward a direct, on-property solution that reaches in-market senior-living decision-makers with higher intent and lower waste.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Lindsey Schwartz referencing the $265K display + $255K CTV allocation managed by Kepler Group. Open with: 'I noticed A Place for Mom is investing meaningfully in programmatic display and CTV — I work with several senior-care and health-services brands who shifted a slice of that budget on-property and saw measurable lifts in qualified family inquiries. Worth a 20-minute conversation?'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Of the $5M in digital spend Kepler is managing, what percentage is currently reaching families who are actively researching senior living options versus broader awareness audiences — and how are you measuring that intent gap?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our on-property inventory as a direct path to high-intent caregiving audiences already in the decision funnel, contrasting with the broad-reach, low-context nature of the $265K display and $9K online video currently in the mix.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame it concretely: 'If we redirected even 10% of the $5M digital budget — roughly $500K — toward on-property placements reaching verified in-market senior-living researchers, what would a 15% improvement in qualified lead volume mean for your referral conversion economics?'

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Lindsey for a 20-minute discovery call in the next two weeks: 'I'd love to share two case studies from comparable senior-care referral brands before your next Kepler planning cycle. Are you free for a brief call the week of [specific date]?'

sales_plays.title: Agency Warm Path via Horizon Next
sales_plays.summary: Horizon Next holds the Media Planning and Media Buying scope for A Place for Mom. Since this is a net-new relationship with no CRM history, routing through an existing Horizon Next contact who already trusts our platform is a lower-friction entry point than cold outreach directly to Lindsey Schwartz.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Identify the Horizon Next buyer or planner assigned to the A Place for Mom account. Reach out referencing the $35.3M total estimated spend and say: 'I know you're managing media planning for A Place for Mom — we've been working with a few of your other clients on senior-health and care-services campaigns and I wanted to loop you in on something relevant before their next planning cycle.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask the Horizon Next contact: 'Is A Place for Mom actively evaluating any new digital or CTV partners for the upcoming planning period, or is the current Kepler-managed programmatic setup locked in through the year?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our platform as a complement — not a conflict — to Kepler's AOR digital role, emphasizing that on-property, direct-sold inventory sits outside the programmatic stack Kepler manages and gives Horizon Next an incremental option to bring to Lindsey Schwartz.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Reference the $255K CTV and $265K display line items as proof that A Place for Mom is already testing non-search digital formats. Frame the ask: 'A $200–300K test on our platform would be proportional to what they're already spending in display and CTV, with stronger audience intent signals for senior-living researchers.'

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask the Horizon Next contact to make a warm introduction to Lindsey Schwartz or to include our platform in the next media plan review: 'Would you be open to a brief three-way call with Lindsey's team so we can present the opportunity directly? I'll do all the prep work — you just make the intro.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Lindsey, I'm [Rep Name] from [Company] — we work with senior-care and health-services brands that are trying to reach families actively researching assisted living and memory care options, not just broad health audiences. We've helped brands in this space get more out of their digital budgets by connecting them with high-intent caregiving audiences who are already deep in the decision process. I was looking at A Place for Mom's media footprint and noticed you're running roughly $5M in digital — with display and CTV in the mix managed through Kepler — alongside a significant $16.6M broadcast investment. That's a serious commitment to reach, and it tells me you're trying to stay top-of-mind across the full funnel. My question is: how confident are you that the $265K in display and $255K in CTV are actually hitting families who are in active research mode for senior living, versus general health or lifestyle audiences? I'd love to share what we're seeing from comparable referral-service brands and whether there's a fit worth exploring.
open_questions: Of the $5M in digital spend managed by Kepler Group, how much is currently allocated to audiences who are actively researching senior living versus broader health or lifestyle segments?
Is Lindsey Schwartz the final decision-maker on new digital media partnerships, or does Horizon Next have authority to approve incremental test budgets within the media plan?

With $9M going to search and only $265K to display, is there a strategic reason mid-funnel digital formats have stayed small — or is that a gap the team is looking to address in the next planning cycle?

How is A Place for Mom currently measuring the contribution of the $255K CTV investment to referral volume, and what would need to be true for that budget to scale closer to the $16.6M broadcast line?

Does Kepler Group's AOR Digital scope include direct-sold inventory partnerships, or is their remit limited to programmatic and paid social — and would a direct-sold opportunity need to route through Horizon Next for approval?

contacts.name: Graham Retzik
contacts.title: Head, Creative Marketing & Brand
contacts.note: Primary advertiser-side contact identified via Winmo. Verify current role and contact details before outreach. (Source: Winmo)

sales_plays.title: Shift Digital Dollars On-Property
sales_plays.summary: A24 is spending $24.6M in digital, with $10.2M in Paid Social and the remainder fragmented across display, mobile, and online video. This play targets Graham Retzik to displace off-property programmatic and social spend with direct, premium inventory that matches A24's brand positioning.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Graham Retzik referencing A24's known digital mix — $10.2M in Paid Social and $601K in Online Video — and open with a direct observation: 'A24 titles earn cultural moments that Meta and YouTube algorithms commoditize. I want to show you what direct placement on [our property] does that paid social can't.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'When you're launching a title like a prestige A24 release, how are you currently measuring whether your $10.2M in Paid Social is reaching the right audience versus just the biggest audience?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position premium direct inventory as brand-safe, context-aligned placement — A24's arthouse identity is diluted in algorithmic feeds; our editorial environment mirrors the cultural credibility A24 titles carry.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Even a 10% reallocation of A24's $24.6M digital budget — roughly $2.5M — shifted to direct premium inventory represents a meaningful test with measurable lift against a defined title release window.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Graham for a 20-minute call in the next two weeks: 'I'd like to walk you through one comparable entertainment advertiser case study and get your read on whether the audience overlap makes sense for an upcoming A24 title.'

sales_plays.title: CTV Complement for Title Launches
sales_plays.summary: A24 is already investing $9.0M in CTV, signaling comfort with the format. This play approaches Graham Retzik as a net-new logo by positioning incremental CTV inventory alongside A24's existing buy to extend reach and frequency for title-specific campaigns.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open by acknowledging A24's $9.0M CTV commitment: 'You're clearly leaning into connected TV — that's a significant investment for an independent studio. I want to understand where you're buying and whether there are audience segments you're not reaching with your current partners.'
Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'For a title launch, how many CTV partners are you currently running through, and are you seeing diminishing reach as you scale spend past a certain threshold?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our CTV inventory as an incremental reach layer — not a replacement — that captures cord-cutters and streaming-native audiences who over-index for the arthouse and prestige content A24 produces.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A24's $9.0M CTV spend across a fragmented buy likely produces audience overlap above 40% by the third week of a campaign. Even a $500K–$1M test on our inventory can demonstrate net-new unique reach against A24's core 18–34 culturally engaged demo.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a specific next step: 'Can we set up a 30-minute session where I bring our audience data team to map our CTV reach against your current buy? I can have something ready within the week if you can share a general sense of your current CTV partners.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Graham, I'm [Rep Name] from [Company]. We work with entertainment advertisers at the level A24 operates — studios and distributors who care as much about where their creative lands as how many impressions it generates. I'm not calling to pitch a media kit; I'm calling because A24's titles earn cultural weight that most ad environments actively undercut. Looking at A24's media mix, you're running roughly $10.2M through Paid Social and $9.0M in CTV — that's a meaningful commitment, and it tells me you're investing seriously in awareness at scale. What I'm curious about is whether the environments you're buying in are actually reinforcing the A24 brand or just delivering volume. Would it be worth 20 minutes to look at whether there's an audience segment your current digital and CTV buys are leaving on the table?
open_questions: For a title like a wide A24 release, how are you currently splitting your $24.6M digital budget between brand-building placements and performance-driven buys — and is that balance intentional or inherited?
Your $9.0M CTV investment is significant for an independent studio — are you buying that directly, through a DSP, or is there an agency managing that relationship we should be aware of?
When you look at your $10.2M in Paid Social, are you measuring success by reach and frequency, or are there downstream title-performance metrics you're tying back to that spend?
Graham, who else at A24 is involved when you're evaluating a new media partner — does this stay within Creative Marketing, or does it involve a media planning or finance stakeholder?

Given A24's brand positioning around prestige and cultural relevance, how important is the editorial environment of an ad placement versus the audience data behind it when you're making a buy decision?

sales_plays.title: PMP to Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: AARP is an active PMP customer (two consecutive PMP wins in Jan 2025 and Jan 2026) but has lost two display/Senior Tech line-item opportunities totaling $90K. The goal is to convert the existing PMP relationship into a larger, direct premium package that captures budget currently flowing elsewhere in AARP's $100.3M digital spend.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the back-to-back PMP renewals (Jan 2025 and Jan 2026) as proof of partnership continuity. Email Anna Banks' team or route through BBDO Worldwide AOR contact: 'We've now renewed the TWC Interactive PMP two years running — I'd love 20 minutes to show you what a direct premium placement layer on top of that PMP could unlock for AARP's 2026 member-acquisition goals.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'The two Senior Tech and Display line items from Q4 2024 through Q1 2025 didn't close with us — can you help me understand whether those budgets went to a competing publisher or were reallocated internally, so I can build the right proposal?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's owned-and-operated premium inventory as a direct complement to the existing PMP — same audience, higher viewability guarantees, brand-safe editorial context that aligns with AARP's health, caregiving, and financial wellness messaging pillars.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): AARP spends $100.3M digitally, yet current confirmed TWC Interactive revenue is effectively nominal (two $1 PMP wins plus one $24K Senior Tech win). Even capturing 0.5% of the $100.3M digital budget represents ~$500K in incremental revenue — frame the $90K in lost display/Senior Tech opps as the immediate floor to recapture.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 20-minute working session with the BBDO Worldwide media planning lead and AARP's digital team before the next quarterly planning cycle to present a tiered premium package proposal — request a specific date within the next two weeks.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Spend to On-Property Video
sales_plays.summary: AARP allocates $14.4M to Paid Social and $5.3M to Online Video within its $100.3M digital budget. Given AARP's 50+ audience skew, on-property premium video inventory likely delivers stronger contextual alignment and brand safety than social platforms — creating a displacement opportunity routed through d. exposito & Partners, who handle multicultural media buying.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to d. exposito & Partners (Media Planning/Buying lead) with a data-driven opener: 'AARP is spending $14.4M on Paid Social and $5.3M on Online Video. I have audience overlap data showing how much of that 50+ reach we can replicate — and in some segments exceed — on TWC Interactive's owned properties with stronger brand-safety controls. Worth a quick look?'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'For the $14.4M in Paid Social, what KPIs is AARP currently optimizing against — reach among 50+, cost-per-member-acquisition, or brand lift — and are those benchmarks being hit consistently across platforms?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Present TWC Interactive's on-property video and display environment as a brand-safe, contextually relevant alternative: AARP's health, Medicare, and caregiving content themes map directly to TWC Interactive editorial verticals, reducing wasted impressions and platform-related brand-safety risk that social inventory carries.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 10% shift of AARP's $14.4M Paid Social budget equals $1.44M in redirected spend. Paired with the $5.3M Online Video line, even a partial reallocation of 15% ($795K) to TWC Interactive on-property video would represent a 30x+ increase over current confirmed TWC Interactive revenue — frame this against the two lost display opps ($90K combined) as evidence that the right package structure has not yet been presented.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send d. exposito & Partners a one-page audience equivalency brief — AARP's 50+ target on social vs. TWC Interactive on-property — within 48 hours, and request a 30-minute call the following week to walk through it with the media buying team before the next campaign flight is locked.

talk_track_opener: Hey, I'm reaching out from TWC Interactive — we've been working with AARP for the past two years on your PMP, and I wanted to build on that relationship directly. We've seen the PMP renew back-to-back in January 2025 and January 2026, which tells me there's real value in the partnership, and I think we're only scratching the surface of what we could be doing together given the scale of AARP's digital investment.
open_questions: The Senior Tech Q4 2025 opportunity for $60K was lost — did that budget go to a competing publisher, or was it pulled back internally, and what would have made the TWC Interactive proposal the right fit?
For AARP's $14.4M Paid Social budget, is d. exposito & Partners the primary decision-maker on platform allocation, or does Anna Banks' team set those guardrails at the CMO level?

The PMP wins are recorded at $1 — can you help me understand the actual media value flowing through those PMP deals so we can size the true relationship correctly and build a proportionate expansion proposal?

AARP's $30.4M Mobile App spend is the largest single digital line item — is that budget managed through BBDO Worldwide or a separate performance agency, and is TWC Interactive currently participating in any of that inventory?

Given AARP's advocacy focus on Medicare and Social Security, are there content-adjacency or brand-safety requirements that have historically ruled out certain publisher environments, and how does TWC Interactive's editorial context score against those criteria?

sales_plays.title: Digital Spend Upgrade for Local News Leader
sales_plays.summary: ABC7 LA runs $8.4M in digital spend through OMG23 with $4.3M concentrated in display and virtually nothing in online video ($1K) or mobile app ($25K). This imbalance is the opening — position premium video and mobile solutions against their underinvested channels.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Laura Gonzalez referencing ABC7's $4.3M display-heavy digital mix and note that their online video allocation ($1K) is a fraction of what comparable local broadcast brands invest in streaming and connected TV environments. Ask if that channel split is intentional or a gap worth exploring.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Laura: 'Given that ABC7's audience is consuming your news content across mobile and streaming, is OMG23 actively planning video and mobile budgets to follow that behavior — or is the $4.3M display line doing most of the heavy lifting right now?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position your platform's premium video and mobile inventory as the natural complement to ABC7's broadcast brand — helping them reach the same LA audience they serve editorially, now as an advertiser extending reach beyond their own airtime.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): With $8.4M in total digital spend and only $26K combined in video and mobile, even a 5% reallocation represents $420K in new channel investment. Frame your ask around a pilot that captures a portion of that shift.

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a 20-minute call with Laura Gonzalez in the next two weeks to walk through a channel mix analysis specific to ABC7's LA market audience — offer to bring a one-page benchmark showing how comparable local broadcast brands allocate digital spend across display, video, and mobile.

sales_plays.title: OMG23 Agency Warm Path via Disney/Marvel Roster
sales_plays.summary: OMG23 serves as ABC7's AOR for media planning and buying, and their client roster includes Walt Disney Studios, Marvel Studios, Lucasfilm, Pixar, and 20th Century. If your platform already touches any of those Disney-umbrella accounts through OMG23, use that relationship to get a warm introduction to the ABC7 planning team.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Identify your existing OMG23 contact who handles Disney, Marvel, or Pixar media buys. Reach out and reference the shared agency relationship — ask if they also touch the ABC7 LA account and whether they'd be open to a brief conversation about how you might support that client.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask the OMG23 contact: 'For the ABC7 account specifically, is the digital planning team focused on growing video and mobile, or is the current $4.3M display allocation locked in for the year?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Use the Disney/Marvel precedent as proof of execution quality — if OMG23 trusts your platform with tentpole entertainment campaigns, position that same capability as directly relevant to ABC7's need to reach LA audiences in premium, brand-safe environments.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): ABC7's trailing 12-month digital spend is $8.53M. A referral-sourced introduction that converts even a $250K video pilot represents a meaningful new logo win with a clear path to expansion given the scale of the overall budget.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask your OMG23 contact for a single email introduction to the ABC7 planning lead within the next 10 business days — offer to make it easy by drafting the intro note yourself, keeping it focused on the channel mix opportunity rather than a cold pitch.

talk_track_opener: Hey Laura, I'm [Rep Name] from [Company]. We work with a number of local broadcast brands and their agency partners — including teams at OMG23 — helping them extend their audience reach into digital video and mobile environments where their viewers are increasingly spending time. I wanted to reach out directly because ABC7 is doing something really interesting in the LA market with the 7 On Your Side brand and your Spanish-language content, and I think there's a conversation worth having about how your paid media mix reflects that audience breadth. Looking at ABC7's digital footprint, you're running a substantial $4.3M in display, which is a strong foundation — but the video and mobile lines are minimal relative to where LA audiences are actually consuming local news content right now. I'm not assuming that's a problem, but I'd love to ask: is OMG23 actively building out a video and mobile strategy for the coming planning cycle, or is that a channel mix question still on the table? Even 15 minutes would help me understand whether what we do is actually relevant to where you're headed.
open_questions: Is OMG23 currently planning any shift in ABC7's digital channel mix away from display toward video or mobile for the next budget cycle?
Who at OMG23 owns the day-to-day media planning for the ABC7 LA account — is it the same team that handles your Disney and Marvel buys?
Given ABC7's Spanish-language content and the '7 On Your Side' brand, are you running separate audience targeting strategies for those segments in paid media, or is it one unified plan?

The online video line is very small relative to your overall digital budget — is that a deliberate hold, or is it a channel you're looking to grow if the right partner and inventory quality are there?
Does Laura Gonzalez set the channel strategy direction, or does OMG23 drive those recommendations independently before bringing them to the ABC7 marketing team for approval?

sales_plays.title: Pataday to Portfolio: Expand Beyond Allergy
sales_plays.summary: Alcon has a proven, growing TWC relationship anchored entirely in Pataday allergy. With $32.9M in digital spend and three corporate divisions — surgical, pharmaceutical, and consumer vision care — there is significant untapped budget across brands like Systane, Dailies, and the surgical glaucoma line that Chuck Marshall owns.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the back-to-back Pataday wins — $505K in 2025 1H and $580K in 2026 1H — and open with: 'The Pataday allergy programs have performed two years running. I want to show you what that same weather-driven, intent-based approach looks like applied to Systane or Dailies, where dry eye and contact lens searches spike year-round, not just in pollen season.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Outside of Pataday, which Alcon brand has the most pressure on digital performance this year — is it Systane, Dailies, or something on the surgical side that Chuck's team is pushing?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Alcon's $24.8M in Paid Social and $3.0M in Display signal heavy investment in awareness and targeting. Position TWC's on-property weather-triggered inventory as a precision complement — reaching consumers at the exact moment environmental conditions (UV index, dry air, screen time) drive eye care need, something Paid Social alone cannot do.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The Pataday 2026 deal grew 14.8% YoY ($505K to $580K). If a second brand — Systane or Dailies — enters at a comparable $500K entry point, total TWC billings double. Frame it to Evan Jackson at Starcom: 'Two brands at $500K each is a more defensible media plan than one brand at $580K.'

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Evan Jackson for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to map which non-Pataday brands are in Starcom's 2026 2H planning cycle and whether a weather-trigger brief already exists for any of them.

sales_plays.title: Reclaim the $1M Big Ideas Budget
sales_plays.summary: Alcon's 2023 Pataday Big Ideas opportunity was lost at $1M — the largest deal in the CRM history. That budget went somewhere. With Bombora showing active campaign intent this week and two consecutive Pataday wins since, the relationship is strong enough to re-open the conversation about what displaced TWC and whether that incumbent can be challenged.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the relationship trajectory directly: 'We've won two Pataday programs back-to-back and the investment has grown. I'd be remiss not to bring up the Big Ideas program from 2023 — that was a $1M concept that didn't land with us. I'd love to understand what did win that budget and whether it delivered what you expected.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'What did the 2023 Big Ideas budget ultimately fund, and is that partner still active in the 2026 plan? I want to understand what we'd need to do differently to earn that level of investment.'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): If the $1M went to a programmatic or social platform, position TWC's direct, on-property, weather-contextual environment as something those platforms cannot replicate — a brand-safe, first-party data environment where Alcon's eye care message appears alongside content that creates the need (allergy forecasts, UV index, dry eye alerts).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Alcon spends $9.8M in CTV and $850K in Online Video. A $1M TWC premium video or sponsorship package represents less than 10% of their video budget but delivers contextual relevance that broad CTV buys lack. Show Evan Jackson at Starcom that a $1M TWC commitment is a precision layer, not an incremental ask.

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a creative strategy session with both Evan Jackson (Starcom) and the Norton Agency creative team within the next three weeks — bring a refreshed Big Ideas concept tied to a specific Alcon brand and a measurable outcome metric from the 2025 Pataday programs to anchor credibility.

talk_track_opener: Hey Evan, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with the Alcon team for a couple of years now — the Pataday allergy programs have run two consecutive years and the investment actually grew from $505K in 2025 to $580K in 2026, which tells me the performance story is holding up on your end. I also know you and the Starcom team are disciplined about where you put Alcon's $32.9M in digital, so I don't take that growth lightly. The reason I'm reaching out now is that we're seeing active campaign intent signals from Alcon this week, and I want to make sure we're in the room before the 2H planning cycle locks. My question is simple: outside of Pataday, which Alcon brand is under the most performance pressure right now — Systane, Dailies, or something on the surgical side? I have a specific idea about how the weather-trigger model we built for Pataday could translate to dry eye or contact lens, and I'd rather shape it around your actual priorities than guess.
open_questions: The Pataday programs have run two years in a row with growing investment — which other Alcon brands are in active digital planning for 2H 2026, and is Starcom already briefed on those?
The 2023 Big Ideas program was a $1M opportunity that went elsewhere — what ultimately won that budget, and is that partner still in the plan?
Alcon has $24.8M in Paid Social spend — who owns the decision to diversify into contextual or weather-triggered inventory, and does that sit with Evan at Starcom or with the brand team in Fort Worth?
Chuck Marshall runs U.S. Surgical Glaucoma and Academic Accounts — is there a digital or HCP media component to that business, or is it purely field sales driven?
The Amazon Deal ID programs for Pataday allergy ran in 2025 — are those being renewed or expanded in 2026, and is TWC positioned in that conversation alongside the Amazon buy?

sales_plays.title: Brand Family Expansion: Move IFC, WE tv & Sundance Into Premium Inventory
sales_plays.summary: AMC Networks has two consecutive PMP wins with TWC Interactive but the relationship is anchored to a single brand. With five entertainment brands in the portfolio and $69.3M in digital spend, there is clear whitespace to expand coverage across IFC, WE tv, Wedding Central, and Sundance Channel.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the back-to-back PMP wins (Feb 2025 and Jul 2024) with TWC Interactive and open with: 'We've delivered two consecutive wins for AMC — I want to make sure IFC, WE tv, and Sundance Channel are getting the same quality of inventory access, because right now they're not in the mix.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Kim Volonakis Granito or Kasha Cacy: 'Of your five entertainment brands, which ones have dedicated digital media budgets versus shared pool allocations — and is there a brand that's been underserved in premium placements?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a multi-brand PMP structure that mirrors the proven TWC Interactive setup but extends to IFC and WE tv, which skew toward distinct audience demos (indie film fans, women 25-54) that map cleanly to premium contextual targeting.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): AMC Networks runs $52.7M in Paid Social and $4.9M in Display — even a 5% reallocation of Display spend toward a multi-brand PMP would represent ~$245K in incremental premium inventory, with stronger audience addressability than open exchange.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Kasha Cacy for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to map each of the five brands to available inventory segments — come prepared with a brand-by-brand audience overlap analysis.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Waste: Redirect $52.7M Social Budget Toward Addressable Premium
sales_plays.summary: AMC Networks allocates $52.7M — the dominant share of its $69.3M digital budget — to Paid Social, while Online Video sits at just $894K. With Bombora signaling eCommerce intent this week, there is an opening to challenge the efficiency of social spend and redirect a portion toward addressable video and direct inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the Bombora eCommerce intent signal as a conversation starter with Kasha Cacy: 'We're seeing AMC Networks showing active research interest in eCommerce this week — are you exploring shoppable or performance-driven formats to complement your brand campaigns? That changes how we'd think about your inventory mix.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'With $52.7M going into Paid Social, what does your current measurement framework look like for attributing brand lift or tune-in to social versus premium video — and are you satisfied with what you're seeing?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position premium addressable video and direct inventory as a complement that delivers measurable tune-in lift and audience quality that walled-garden social cannot verify — especially relevant if AMC Networks is exploring eCommerce or performance outcomes tied to content launches.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Online Video is currently at $894K — a fraction of the $52.7M social budget. Shifting even 3-5% of Paid Social ($1.6M-$2.6M) into premium video would represent a 2-3x increase in addressable video investment and a meaningful test of incremental reach against AMC's core entertainment audiences.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a 2-week competitive audit — offer to run an audience overlap analysis showing how much of AMC's current social reach duplicates what premium video can deliver at lower CPM, and present findings to Kasha Cacy before the next planning cycle.

talk_track_opener: Hey Kasha, this is [Rep Name] from [Company] — we've been working with AMC Networks on the TWC Interactive PMP deals going back to mid-2024, so we have a real foundation here and I want to make sure we're building on it the right way. We closed two consecutive wins together and I think there's a lot more we can do across the full brand portfolio.
open_questions: Of the five brands in the AMC Networks portfolio — AMC, IFC, WE tv, Wedding Central, and Sundance Channel — which ones have dedicated digital media budgets versus drawing from a shared pool, and which brand is most underserved in premium placements right now?

The TWC Interactive PMP deals have been a consistent win two years running — who on your team owns the decision to expand that structure to additional brands, and is that a conversation that lives with you or with Kim Volonakis Granito?

With $52.7M going into Paid Social, what does your current measurement framework look like for attributing tune-in or brand lift specifically to social versus premium video environments — and are you confident in what you're seeing?

We're seeing AMC Networks flagged for active eCommerce research interest this week — is there a specific initiative or content format driving that, and how does it connect to your media planning for the next quarter?

Online Video is currently at $894K against a $69.3M digital budget — is that a deliberate allocation or a gap that hasn't been prioritized, and what would it take to run a meaningful test in premium addressable video?

sales_plays.title: Win Back AMC on Summer Heat
sales_plays.summary: AMC lost a TWC Interactive summer 2024 deal (closed May 31, 2024). With summer 2025 approaching and active Entertainment intent on Bombora, now is the window to re-engage before BarkleyOKRP/Known lock the summer plan.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email David Ferro at BarkleyOKRP referencing the May 2024 MAIM Adaptor Summer deal that didn't close. Acknowledge the gap directly: 'We didn't earn the business last summer — I'd like to show you what's changed and why summer 2025 is a different conversation.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask David Ferro: 'When you evaluated TWC Interactive last May, what was the primary reason AMC went another direction — was it targeting reach, pricing, or fit with the summer blockbuster calendar?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position against the specific objection from the 2024 loss. If it was reach, lead with summer heat-index audience scale. If it was fit, show how weather-triggered creative aligns with AMC's drive to fill seats on hot days when outdoor alternatives drop off.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): AMC is running $6.0M in Paid Social and $1.4M in Search — summer is clearly a priority spend window. Frame a weather-triggered display or video package against the $135K Internet Display and $56K Online Video lines as incremental, not competitive, to existing buys.
Step 5 (Next Step): Ask David Ferro for a 20-minute call before April 30 to walk through a summer heat audience brief — low commitment, ahead of when Known and BarkleyOKRP finalize Q2/Q3 buys.

sales_plays.title: Summer Heat Lead-In: Beat the Blockbuster Rush
sales_plays.summary: AMC's highest-traffic season correlates with summer blockbuster releases and hot weather driving indoor entertainment. Getting in front of Lauren Doyle and David Ferro in April — before summer plans are locked — is the timing edge.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference Bombora's active Entertainment intent signal this week alongside AMC's summer release calendar. Open with: 'AMC is showing strong Entertainment intent right now, and with the summer slate loading up, I want to get in front of you before your Q3 plan is set.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Lauren Doyle: 'For Food & Beverage specifically — concessions, A-List upsells — how are you currently activating digital media around the summer blockbuster windows, and is weather or heat a variable in that planning?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Connect summer heat to AMC's core value prop: hot days push consumers indoors, and weather-triggered media can amplify that behavioral signal at exactly the right moment — reaching moviegoers when the decision to go out is most weather-influenced.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): AMC's $6.0M Paid Social and $1.4M Search spend shows serious summer investment. A weather-triggered layer on top of the existing $56K Online Video or $135K Display budget could extend reach efficiency without cannibalizing current agency buys managed by BarkleyOKRP and Known.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send Lauren Doyle a one-page summer heat audience brief by April 25, then schedule a 20-minute call with her and David Ferro the first week of May — before Known finalizes the summer media plan.

talk_track_opener: Hey David, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We actually had a conversation with your team last spring around the MAIM Adaptor Summer 2024 program — it didn't come together, and I won't pretend otherwise. I'm reaching back out because summer 2025 is a genuinely different setup for us, and I think there's a real fit here worth a quick look before your plan locks. We work with entertainment and cinema brands specifically around weather-driven audience moments — the insight being that hot days are one of the strongest behavioral triggers for indoor entertainment decisions, which maps directly to AMC's peak season. I was looking at AMC's media mix this week and noticed active Entertainment intent in your Bombora profile, plus a meaningful investment across Paid Social and Search that signals summer is already on the radar. Given that Lauren Doyle is focused on Food and Beverage and concessions upsell, and you're managing direct investments, I think there's a 20-minute conversation worth having before April ends — specifically around how a weather-triggered layer could complement what BarkleyOKRP and Known already have in market. Would that be worth a quick call?
open_questions: When the MAIM Adaptor Summer 2024 program didn't move forward, was the primary objection around targeting capabilities, pricing, or fit with AMC's summer release calendar — and has that constraint changed for 2025?

Between BarkleyOKRP and Known, who owns the final call on incremental or partnership-driven media buys for the summer window — is that David Ferro's decision or does it route through Known's planning team?
For Lauren Doyle's Food & Beverage programs, is there a separate digital budget for concessions and A-List upsell activation, or does it flow through the same media plan BarkleyOKRP manages?
How far in advance does AMC typically lock its Q3 summer media plan — is the April-May window still open for new partners, or does planning close earlier given the blockbuster release calendar?
Is AMC currently using any weather or contextual triggers in its digital media — or is the summer strategy primarily driven by release dates and social content calendars?

steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the active 2025 PMP relationship and the pattern of lost direct campaigns. Say: 'We've had your PMP live since January and I've been looking at what the Q3 Movers and Q4 Incremental campaigns were trying to accomplish — those were $150K in direct opportunities we didn't capture together. I want to understand why so we can fix that.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'When the Q3 Movers campaign went elsewhere, what did the winning partner offer that our PMP couldn't match — was it audience guarantees, contextual alignment, or something on the reporting side?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's on-property direct inventory against AT&T's $294.2M digital footprint. Explain that PMP buys optimize for efficiency but miss premium contextual placements — weather-triggered, location-specific, and device-upgrade-moment inventory that aligns directly with AT&T's trade-in and bundle messaging.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): AT&T spends $57.9M on Display and $33M on Online Video programmatically. Even a 5% shift of Display spend to direct IO represents ~$2.9M in addressable budget. Frame the lost $300K in direct opps as a proof-of-concept gap, not a ceiling.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Andrea Ebert's team at Hearts & Science for a 30-minute working session before the next quarterly planning cycle to map one direct IO test against an active AT&T campaign flight — propose a specific two-week window and offer to bring audience overlap data to the meeting.

sales_plays.title: Digital Budget Displacement from Paid Social
sales_plays.summary: AT&T allocates $72.1M to Paid Social — the single largest digital sub-channel after Search. With social platform CPMs rising and brand-safety scrutiny increasing in the telco vertical, there is a clear opening to displace a portion of that budget toward TWC Interactive's premium contextual environment.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a market observation tied to AT&T's own spend mix. Say: 'Your team is running $72.1M through Paid Social — that's more than double your Online Video investment. Given where Meta and TikTok CPMs have moved in the last two quarters, I'd want to pressure-test whether that allocation is still returning the same cost-per-acquisition it was 18 months ago.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Is Hearts & Science benchmarking Paid Social performance against premium contextual alternatives, or is the $72M allocation largely locked in the annual plan at this point?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive as a brand-safe, high-intent contextual environment. AT&T's device upgrade and bundle offers perform best when served to consumers actively researching connectivity, home services, or technology — exactly the intent context TWC Interactive delivers, without the auction volatility of social platforms.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 10% reallocation of AT&T's $72.1M Paid Social budget equals $7.2M in displaceable spend. Even a conservative 3% test shift — $2.2M — would represent a meaningful new direct relationship and dwarf the $300K in lost direct campaign opportunities from 2023–2025.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send Andrea Ebert at Hearts & Science a one-page competitive context brief — Paid Social CPM trends vs. premium contextual benchmarks in the telco vertical — within 48 hours, and request a 20-minute call to walk through it before their next AT&T planning review.

talk_track_opener: Hey Andrea, I'm reaching out from TWC Interactive — we've had AT&T's PMP running with us since early 2024 and we renewed it again in January, so we have a real working relationship to build on. I've been digging into how that PMP activity maps against AT&T's broader digital investment and I want to be straight with you: we've had three direct campaign conversations — the Q4 2023, Q4 2024, and Q3 2025 Movers opportunities — that didn't close, and I think that gap is worth understanding together rather than just letting it sit.
open_questions: When the Q3 2025 Movers campaign and the Q4 2024 Incremental campaign went to other partners, what did those partners offer that TWC Interactive's direct proposal didn't match — was it audience scale, pricing, measurement, or something else?

Who at AT&T or Hearts & Science owns the final approval on shifting budget from programmatic PMP to direct IO — is that a planning decision Andrea's team makes, or does it require sign-off from the AT&T brand side?
Is the $72.1M Paid Social allocation reviewed on a quarterly basis or is it locked into the annual plan, and what would it take to run a controlled test against a premium contextual alternative?

How is Hearts & Science currently benchmarking TWC Interactive's PMP performance against other programmatic partners in AT&T's digital mix — are we being evaluated on reach, CPM, or post-click outcomes?

AT&T's digital spend is $294.2M annually — what percentage of that is currently allocated to partners with direct IO relationships versus purely programmatic access, and is there a strategic preference to shift that ratio?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Direct Upgrade: Scale What's Already Working
sales_plays.summary: Aaron's is an active customer with a won PMP deal in February 2025. This play converts that foothold into a broader direct buy across premium weather-adjacent and retail-moment inventory, leveraging the existing Truth Marketing + Media AOR relationship.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the February 2025 PMP win directly with the Truth Marketing + Media team. Open with: 'We closed a PMP deal together in February — I want to show you what performance data we have from that activation and talk about how we scale it before the summer retail window.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'How is Truth Marketing + Media currently allocating Aaron's $12.5M digital budget across partners — and is there a direct-buy tier above the PMP that's been evaluated for Q3 tentpole moments like back-to-school or 4th of July?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium on-property inventory as the logical next step above the PMP — higher viewability, guaranteed placement, and weather-contextual targeting that aligns with Aaron's lease-to-own retail model (customers shop when life disrupts: moves, weather events, new seasons).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Aaron's runs $3.4M in online video and $1.0M in paid social. A direct video buy on TWC property captures incremental reach against the same audience at a premium context signal. Even a 5% reallocation of the $3.4M OLV budget represents a $170K direct opportunity — well above the PMP entry point.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Truth Marketing + Media for a 30-minute planning session in the next two weeks to review PMP delivery data together and map a Q3 direct proposal ahead of their summer tentpole planning cycle.

sales_plays.title: Reclaim the 4th of July: Displace the 2023 Loss
sales_plays.summary: Aaron's lost two $200K 4th of July opportunities in Q3 2023 — both to an unknown competitor. With a live PMP relationship now in place and Truth Marketing + Media as AOR, this play re-opens the seasonal conversation from a position of earned trust rather than cold pitch.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the relationship, not the ask. Say: 'We've been working together since February and I want to be transparent — we lost two 4th of July proposals in 2023 totaling $400K. I'd like to understand what won that budget and whether there's a path to earn it this year.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Who at Aaron's or Truth Marketing + Media owns the Q3 seasonal tentpole budget, and what did the 2023 4th of July campaign ultimately run on — was it a competing weather or contextual platform, or did it shift to paid social?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): If the 2023 budget went to a competitor platform, position TWC's weather-moment targeting as uniquely suited to Aaron's lease-to-own model — consumers making big-ticket home furnishing decisions during summer holidays are precisely the audience activated by heat, storms, and seasonal life transitions.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Two lost deals at $200K each represent $400K in unearned Q3 revenue. Aaron's total trailing 12-month spend is $25.2M — a single seasonal direct buy is less than 2% of their annual media investment. Frame it as a low-risk test with a partner they already trust.

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a brief — even a 15-minute call — with the Truth Marketing + Media media buyer who handled the 2023 Q3 plan, specifically to understand what criteria the winning platform met that TWC did not, and present a revised Q3 2025 concept by end of this quarter.

talk_track_opener: Hey, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company — we've been working with the team at Truth Marketing + Media on Aaron's since earlier this year, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a bigger opportunity we haven't fully explored together. We closed a PMP deal in February, which is a great starting point, but when I look at Aaron's overall digital footprint — $12.5M in digital spend, $3.4M in online video alone — I think we're only touching a small fraction of what's possible on our platform. I also know we were in the conversation for the 4th of July in 2023 and didn't win it, and I'd like to understand why and fix that. Aaron's lease-to-own model is genuinely one of the strongest fits for weather-contextual advertising I've seen in retail — your customers make big-ticket decisions around life moments, and weather is one of the biggest triggers for those moments. I'd love 20 minutes with whoever at Truth Marketing + Media is planning the Q3 seasonal budget to show you what the February PMP delivered and map out what a direct buy could look like before the summer window opens. Who's the right person to loop in on that conversation?

What platform or partner captured the 4th of July budget in 2023 that Aaron's had originally considered for TWC, and is that relationship still active?

sales_plays.title: Displace HCP Programmatic Data Loss with Direct HCP Solution
sales_plays.summary: AbbVie's HCP Programmatic Data 2H'24 opportunity was lost at $15,000 — likely to a competing data or DSP vendor. This play re-enters that conversation with a differentiated, direct HCP-targeting solution that competitors cannot replicate, using the existing PMP relationship as the trust bridge.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the lost HCP Programmatic Data 2H'24 deal directly with Tracy Chavez. Acknowledge the outcome without dwelling on it: 'I know we didn't win the HCP data piece last cycle — I want to show you what's changed on our end and whether it's worth a fresh look for 2025.' This signals accountability and forward motion.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Who ultimately won that HCP data buy, and is the current vendor delivering verified HCP reach with contextual health environment adjacency, or is it purely audience-segment targeting running across open exchange inventory?' This surfaces the incumbent's weakness.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's HCP solution as contextually superior — healthcare professionals researching patient conditions actively use weather and health content environments. Contrast this with open-exchange programmatic HCP data, which lacks environment quality controls. Tie to AbbVie's oncology and immunology HCP audience needs, referencing Teri Huenink's role in Oncology International Marketing as a potential internal champion for a condition-specific HCP environment.

AbbVie spends $267.2M in digital; even a 0.5% reallocation toward a verified HCP contextual environment represents $1.3M+ in incremental TWC revenue. sales_plays.title: Displace Fragmented Display & Mobile Spend with TWC Owned-and-Operated Inventory

talk_track_opener: Hey Melissa — I'm reaching out because we have an active PMP relationship with Abbott going into 2026, and I want to make sure we're actually delivering value beyond the deal structure. Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the ask against the three lost seasonal buys ($300K combined in 2025). A $150K on-property summer grilling package represents half the missed spend, with weather-triggered creative that activates when grilling conditions peak — directly complementing Ace's existing $30.3M CTV and $14.6M digital footprint.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Michael for a 20-minute working session in the next two weeks to walk through a Q2/Q3 summer grilling proposal before Spark Foundry's planning cycle closes — offer to bring a weather-trigger creative brief as a starting point.

sales_plays.title: Displace Assembly's Display Budget with Premium On-Property Video
sales_plays.summary: Assembly manages Ace Hardware's digital scope and is responsible for the $2.4M Internet Display budget — a relatively small, likely remnant-heavy line item. This play targets that underperforming display allocation and displaces it with premium TWC on-property video and interactive units, routed through a direct conversation with Kim Lefko at Ace HQ to create internal pull.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Request a brief intro call with Kim Lefko, CMO at Ace Hardware HQ in Oak Brook, framing it around how peer home improvement brands are shifting low-CPM display dollars into weather-contextual video to drive measurable store traffic — reference Ace's own $2.4M display line as the starting point for the conversation.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Kim: 'How is Assembly currently measuring the performance of your $2.4M display investment, and is that budget tied to a specific outcome like foot traffic, app downloads, or online conversion — or is it more of a reach and frequency play?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's on-property interactive and online video units ($4.3M OLV already in Ace's mix shows comfort with the format) as a direct upgrade path — weather-triggered placements in lawn, garden, and grilling content environments deliver the contextual relevance that standard display cannot, especially ahead of the summer event and BBQ season.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A reallocation of even 50% of the $2.4M display budget ($1.2M) into TWC premium video and interactive would represent a 28x increase over the current $1 PMP base and benchmark favorably against Ace's $4.3M OLV spend — with weather-trigger lift data available to justify the shift to Kim and Spark Foundry.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer Kim a one-page competitive displacement brief showing display CPM benchmarks vs. TWC contextual video performance for home improvement advertisers — ask for 15 minutes to walk through it before end of the current quarter.

talk_track_opener: Hey Michael, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working together on the Ace Hardware PMP that renewed in January, so you know us. I wanted to reach out directly because we're seeing something in our intent data that I think is relevant to your planning right now. Bombora is showing Ace Hardware spiking on Event Management topics this week, which lines up exactly with the barbecue and summer outdoor living window that's about to open. I know the Q3 Summer Grilling buy and the Q2 Seasonal buy didn't come together with us last year, and I'd love to understand what got in the way so we can fix it this cycle before inventory tightens. We've got weather-triggered on-property placements in grilling and outdoor content that are a natural complement to what Ace is already running across CTV and paid social — and I think there's a real story to tell Kim's team about contextual reach that search and social can't replicate. Would you have 20 minutes in the next couple of weeks to look at a summer proposal together? I can bring a weather-trigger creative brief so we're not starting from a blank page.
open_questions: The Q3 Summer Grilling buy in July 2025 and the Q2 Seasonal buy in April 2025 both came back as losses — Michael, can you tell me whether those were budget reallocation decisions, a measurement gap on our end, or a timing issue with how we brought the proposal?

Kim, who at Ace Hardware owns the final sign-off on seasonal digital extensions beyond what Spark Foundry and Assembly plan — does that decision sit with you or is there a VP of Media or Brand below you who drives those calls?

Assembly is managing the digital scope including the $2.4M display budget — is that line item evaluated on its own performance metrics, or is it bundled into a broader digital efficiency target that makes it harder to reallocate?

With $41.9M in paid social and $91M in search already running, how is Ace currently measuring incremental reach from contextual or weather-triggered placements versus what those channels already deliver?

Bombora is showing Ace Hardware researching Event Management topics this week — is that tied to a specific summer campaign or retail event you're planning, and is there a media brief already in flight at Spark Foundry for that activation?
