sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Adidas USA has renewed TWC Interactive PMP deals in 2024, 2025, and 2026 — a clear pattern of programmatic commitment. With $44.7M in digital spend and $31.4M in paid social alone, there is meaningful budget to shift from PMP into higher-impact, direct premium placements that deliver stronger brand control and weather-contextual targeting for performance and apparel campaigns.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three consecutive PMP renewals (2024, 2025, 2026) with EssenceMediaCom and acknowledge the relationship. Open with: 'We've seen consistent PMP investment from the team at EssenceMediaCom across the last three planning cycles — we want to make sure you're getting the full value of what TWC's direct inventory can do beyond the PMP layer.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask EssenceMediaCom's planning lead: 'For Adidas's 2026 performance and apparel campaigns, how are you currently allocating between programmatic PMP and direct reserved buys — and is there a brand-safety or contextual targeting threshold that PMP isn't consistently hitting?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct premium inventory as the upgrade path — weather-triggered creative delivery, guaranteed share of voice during high-intent seasonal windows (spring running season, back-to-school, holiday), and brand-safe environments that PMP cannot guarantee at scale.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Adidas's digital spend is $44.7M, with $6.5M already in display and $2.2M in online video. A 10% reallocation from PMP to direct premium video and display represents a ~$870K incremental opportunity — with measurably higher viewability and contextual relevance than open-market PMP.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask EssenceMediaCom for a 30-minute working session before Q3 2026 planning locks: 'Can we get 30 minutes with your digital planning team in the next two weeks to walk through a direct vs. PMP performance comparison using your current TWC delivery data?'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars with Weather-Contextual Video
sales_plays.summary: Adidas USA is investing $31.4M in paid social — the single largest digital line item in a $108.8M total media budget. Social platforms offer limited weather-contextual targeting and increasing brand-safety scrutiny. TWC's premium video and contextual environments offer a credible displacement argument for performance apparel campaigns tied to seasonal and cultural moments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a spend observation routed through EssenceMediaCom: 'We noticed paid social is carrying $31.4M of Adidas's digital investment — a significant concentration. We've been working with other performance apparel brands to shift a portion of that into weather-contextual video that activates when conditions actually drive purchase intent for running, training, and outdoor gear.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'For Adidas's performance apparel lines, how is EssenceMediaCom currently measuring the incremental lift from paid social versus contextually-triggered placements — and is brand-safety environment quality part of the 2026 media evaluation criteria?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered video inventory as a direct complement — or partial replacement — for paid social on seasonal tentpole campaigns (spring running, back-to-school, winter training). Contextual relevance at the moment of weather-driven purchase intent is a capability social platforms cannot replicate.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 5% reallocation of the $31.4M paid social budget equals $1.57M in incremental TWC video spend. Against Adidas's $108.8M total budget, this is a rounding error in media mix but a material revenue expansion for the TWC relationship — and a testable hypothesis with a contained Q3 or Q4 pilot.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a contained pilot to Alasdhair Willis's team and EssenceMediaCom: 'We'd like to propose a $250K–$500K weather-contextual video test against one Adidas performance line this summer — structured so EssenceMediaCom can measure it cleanly against your paid social benchmarks. Can we align on a brief in the next three weeks?'

talk_track_opener: Hey, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with the Adidas team and EssenceMediaCom across three consecutive planning cycles — the TWC Interactive PMP has renewed in 2024, 2025, and 2026, so we have a real foundation to build on. I want to make sure we're being a strategic partner, not just a line item on the programmatic plan. Looking at Adidas's media mix, you're running $44.7M in digital and $31.4M of that is concentrated in paid social. For a performance apparel brand where seasonal conditions — temperature shifts, precipitation, the start of running season — directly drive purchase behavior, that's a meaningful opportunity to layer in weather-contextual targeting that social platforms simply can't replicate. I'd love to ask: as EssenceMediaCom is building out the 2026 digital plan, how are you thinking about contextual relevance versus reach-at-scale in the channel mix?
open_questions: The TWC Interactive PMP has renewed three years running — is EssenceMediaCom evaluating any direct or reserved buys alongside the PMP for 2026, or is programmatic the preferred execution path for all TWC inventory?

Who at EssenceMediaCom or Adidas owns the decision to shift budget from paid social into contextual or premium video — is that a planning-team call or does it require sign-off from Alasdhair Willis's team?
The Soccer RFP in Q2 2024 went to a competitor — what would need to be true about a TWC proposal for Adidas to bring that tentpole budget back to us for the next major cultural moment?

With $31.4M in paid social, how is EssenceMediaCom currently measuring incremental lift versus other digital channels — and is there an appetite to run a controlled test against a weather-contextual alternative?

As Adidas plans for retail tentpoles like back-to-school and holiday 2026, is there a planning window in the next 60 days where we could present a weather-contextual strategy before the digital budget is fully allocated?

sales_plays.title: Rain & Storm Season PMP Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Advance Auto Parts is an active PMP customer (two Won PMP deals in 2024 and 2025). With rain and storm season approaching, this play expands their existing PMP commitment into a broader, higher-value package that captures weather-triggered demand for wipers, batteries, and roadside essentials before the season peaks.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Kelly Decker and CC Haley Vanhook at Dentsu X referencing the two consecutive PMP wins (Jan 2025 and Jul 2024) and note that weather-triggered search and display intent for automotive parts spikes 6-8 weeks before peak storm season. Ask for 20 minutes to share a seasonal demand forecast specific to wiper blades, batteries, and emergency kits.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Kelly: 'For your rain and storm season push, are you currently activating separate budget lines for weather-triggered display versus your always-on paid search — and does Dentsu X have flexibility to shift dollars if we can show incremental reach against in-market DIY buyers during storm events?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position an expanded PMP package that layers weather-triggered audience segments on top of their existing PMP activation — reaching the same DIY and professional repair audiences at the exact moment storm conditions drive urgency, something their $8.4M display buy running through open programmatic cannot do with the same precision or brand-safe environment.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Advance Auto Parts spends $10.3M in digital and $41.2M in paid search. Even a 2% reallocation of their display budget ($168K) into a weather-triggered PMP extension would represent a meaningful incremental test. Reference the $80K programmatic referred deal that was lost in Jan 2025 as proof there is budget appetite — frame this as the premium, direct path to the same audience.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Kelly and Haley for a 20-minute working session in the next two weeks to review a custom storm-season audience forecast and a tiered PMP expansion proposal — before Dentsu X locks Q2 media plans.

sales_plays.title: Programmatic-to-Direct Displacement: Recover the Lost $80K
sales_plays.summary: A $80K programmatic referred deal was lost in January 2025, likely to an open-market or DSP-routed buy. This play targets Dentsu X's Haley Vanhook to displace that programmatic spend with a direct, on-property solution that offers better brand safety, audience quality, and weather-contextual targeting than the incumbent programmatic path.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Haley Vanhook at Dentsu X directly, acknowledging the January 2025 programmatic deal that did not close and asking a single, low-pressure question: 'Where did that $80K ultimately land, and are you satisfied with the brand safety and audience match you got from that buy?' This surfaces dissatisfaction without attacking the incumbent.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Haley: 'When Dentsu X evaluates programmatic versus direct for Advance Auto Parts' display budget, what are the top two criteria Kelly Decker cares most about — is it CPM efficiency, audience verification, or contextual alignment with weather and seasonal moments?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's on-property direct buy as the displacement solution: weather-contextual inventory that reaches DIY buyers actively checking conditions before a storm — the moment they are most likely to search for wipers, batteries, and emergency supplies. Unlike the open programmatic path that lost the $80K deal, a direct buy guarantees placement in a verified, high-intent environment that aligns with Advance Auto Parts' core seasonal demand triggers.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Advance Auto Parts' total trailing 12-month spend is $53.7M, with $8.4M in display alone. The $80K programmatic deal represents less than 1% of their display budget — a low-risk test to prove direct outperforms open programmatic on brand safety and weather-contextual relevance. Frame the ask as a head-to-head pilot, not a full displacement.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a side-by-side 60-day pilot: $40K direct on-property buy running concurrent with their existing programmatic activity, with agreed KPIs (CTR, viewability, brand safety score) shared back to Kelly Decker. Ask Haley to confirm the right timing to bring this to Kelly before Q2 budgets are finalized.

talk_track_opener: Hey Kelly, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working together on the PMP side for the past two years, with deals closed in both mid-2024 and early 2025, so you know we understand how to connect Advance Auto Parts with in-market buyers at the right moment. We also work closely with Haley and the Dentsu X team on the planning side, so I want to make sure we're all aligned heading into what looks like a strong storm season window. The reason I'm reaching out now is that your rain and storm season demand window is coming up fast, and based on what we see in weather-triggered search and display behavior, the brands that lock in premium, contextual inventory 6-8 weeks ahead of peak conditions consistently outperform those that activate reactively. You're already running $8.4M in display and $41.2M in search — I'd love 20 minutes to show you a seasonal demand forecast specific to wiper blades, batteries, and roadside essentials, and walk through how an expanded PMP package could capture that intent before your competitors do. Would you and Haley have time in the next two weeks?
open_questions: For the rain and storm season, does Advance Auto Parts run separate budget lines for weather-triggered moments, or does that spend come out of the always-on display and search allocation that Dentsu X manages?
Kelly, when the $80K programmatic deal didn't move forward in January, where did that budget ultimately land — and are you satisfied with the performance and brand safety of that placement?
Is Dentsu X currently using any weather-data integrations or contextual triggers in the programmatic buys they run for Advance Auto Parts, or is targeting primarily audience-based?
Who at Advance Auto Parts owns the final sign-off on incremental digital test budgets above the existing PMP commitment — does that stay with you, Kelly, or does it escalate above Director level?

Given that your paid search spend is nearly 4x your display spend, is there a strategic conversation happening internally about shifting more budget toward upper-funnel display to capture demand before the search query — especially for seasonal categories like batteries and wipers?

sales_plays.title: Scale Beyond PMP Into Full Digital Suite
sales_plays.summary: AdvoCare is an active customer with a won PMP deal (closed Feb 2025) and $4.3M in total digital spend. The current footprint is a single PMP line — a fraction of their addressable budget. This play expands the relationship across Online Video, Paid Social, and Display where AdvoCare is already spending heavily with other partners.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the Feb 2025 PMP win directly — 'Scott, we closed your first TWC Interactive PMP in February and I want to make sure we're delivering value before we talk about anything else. Can we do a quick performance review?' Use that conversation to establish a baseline before pitching expansion.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Of your $4.3M in digital, how much is currently allocated to Online Video and who are your primary partners there — because that's where we see the most incremental reach for health and wellness brands like AdvoCare.'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): AdvoCare spends $1.3M on Online Video and $226K on Paid Social with other partners. Position TWC Interactive's audience targeting against health, energy, and wellness intent segments to capture share of those existing line items — not ask for new budget.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Your current digital footprint is $4.9M annually. If we can capture even 15% of your Online Video and Paid Social spend — roughly $230K — that's a 230x increase from your current $1 PMP investment with us. That's a realistic benchmark based on comparable health and wellness advertisers on our platform.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we schedule a 30-minute performance review of the February PMP alongside a brief audience overlap analysis for your energy drink and weight-loss lines? I can have that ready within the week — does Thursday or Friday work for you, Scott?'

sales_plays.title: New Year / Q1 Wellness Window Pre-Buy
sales_plays.summary: AdvoCare's core product lines — energy drinks, weight-loss, and nutritional supplements — are directly tied to the annual January wellness demand surge. This play positions a Q4 pre-buy conversation to lock in premium inventory before the window opens, leveraging AdvoCare's existing $117K in Search and $1.3M in Online Video as proof of intent-driven investment.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): 'Scott, weight-loss and energy supplement search volume historically spikes 30–40% in the first two weeks of January. Given AdvoCare's product portfolio — energy drinks, weight-loss, nutritional lines — I want to make sure you're not competing for inventory in January when you could own it in December. That's the conversation I want to have now.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'How far in advance does AdvoCare typically plan its Q1 media — and is the January wellness push a formal campaign moment or more of an always-on budget decision for Scott and the team?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): AdvoCare already invests $117K in Search and $1.3M in Online Video — channels that are highly competitive in Q1. Position TWC Interactive's CTV ($453K already in their mix) and premium display as a way to reach wellness-intent audiences ahead of the auction price spike, locking in CPMs before demand inflates.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Your CTV investment is already at $453K. A targeted Q1 wellness pre-buy of $75–100K in incremental CTV and Online Video — booked in Q4 — could deliver the same reach at 15–20% lower CPMs than a January spot buy. Based on your current $4.9M annual spend, that efficiency gain is material.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to put together a Q1 wellness audience package specific to AdvoCare's energy and weight-loss lines — no commitment, just a proposal. If I can get that to you by [specific date two weeks out], would you be willing to review it with your team before Thanksgiving planning locks in?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Scott, this is [Rep Name] from TWC Interactive — we've been working together since we closed your PMP deal back in February, and I want to make sure that investment is performing before I ask for anything else. I work with health and wellness brands at your spend level and I've been looking at how AdvoCare's digital mix is structured. You're running $4.3M across digital — with $1.3M in Online Video, $226K in Paid Social, and $453K in CTV — and right now we're only a small piece of that. I think there's a real opportunity to change that. I was looking at your product lines — energy drinks, weight-loss, nutritional supplements — and the Q1 wellness window is one of the most competitive inventory moments of the year for exactly those categories. Given that you're already spending in CTV and Online Video, I'd love to understand how you're planning Q1 right now and whether there's appetite to lock in some of that inventory before January auction prices spike. Would a quick 30-minute call this week make sense to walk through what we're seeing?
open_questions: Of your $1.3M in Online Video spend, which partners are currently receiving the majority of that budget and how are you measuring performance against them?
Is the February PMP buy managed directly by Scott's team or is there an agency involved that we should be looping in for expansion conversations?
How does AdvoCare plan its Q1 wellness campaigns — is there a formal planning cycle in Q3 or Q4, or is it managed on a rolling basis?
Across AdvoCare's product lines — energy drinks, weight-loss, skin care — are media budgets managed at the brand level or consolidated under a single digital budget that Scott controls?
What would need to be true about the February PMP performance for AdvoCare to consider allocating a meaningful portion of the $4.3M digital budget to TWC Interactive in the next planning cycle?

sales_plays.title: Broadcast-to-Digital Bridge: Scale Aetna's $68K Programmatic Win Into a Full Enrollment Portfolio
sales_plays.summary: Aetna is an active customer with a confirmed programmatic win ($68K, Q4 2023) during Open Enrollment. With $47.6M in Broadcast and only $270K in CTV, there is a significant gap between where Aetna spends and where their audience is migrating. This play targets Carrah Kalat and Neena Koyen to expand the existing programmatic relationship into CTV, OLV, and premium direct inventory ahead of the next enrollment cycle.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Neena Koyen at UM Worldwide referencing the Q4 2023 programmatic win: 'Neena, the Aetna enrollment campaign we ran together in Q4 2023 delivered — I want to show you how we can scale that success into CTV and premium video before the next Open Enrollment window opens. Aetna is currently at $270K in CTV against $47.6M in Broadcast. That gap is the opportunity.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When UM and KINESSO are planning Aetna's next enrollment push, what KPIs are you using to evaluate CTV and OLV performance relative to Broadcast — and is there a budget threshold where you'd shift dollars from linear?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position Weather Channel and TWC digital properties as a high-intent, health-adjacent environment — audiences actively checking weather are also planning daily routines, making them receptive to health plan messaging during enrollment season. Contrast this against the $3.9M in Display and $4.1M in OLV currently spread across lower-quality inventory.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Your current CTV investment is $270K against a $92.5M total spend footprint — less than 0.3%. If we moved even 2% of Broadcast dollars into premium CTV with us, that's roughly $950K in incremental, measurable reach. The Q4 2023 programmatic win at $68K proved the model works. Let's size what a full enrollment flight looks like.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar in the next two weeks — you, me, and whoever owns CTV planning at KINESSO — to walk through a specific enrollment-season CTV proposal built around Aetna's audience targets?'

sales_plays.title: Reclaim the Lost $150K: Displace the Incumbent on Aetna's AMHQ Interactive Buy
sales_plays.summary: Aetna lost a $150K AMHQ Interactive opportunity in Q4 2023 to a competing platform or buy. With $16.1M in Paid Social and $15M in Search already in market, Aetna is clearly investing in digital — but that $150K walked out the door. This play targets the specific decision-makers at Ogilvy New York and UM to understand what won that budget and position a stronger, data-backed alternative for the next planning cycle.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to the Ogilvy New York team (AOR) with a direct reference: 'I want to revisit the AMHQ Interactive proposal from Q4 2023 — we lost that $150K opportunity and I'd rather understand why than let it happen again. Aetna's digital mix is sophisticated: $16.1M in Paid Social, $15M in Search, $4.1M in OLV. I believe we left real value on the table and I want to show you what a revised proposal looks like.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'What specifically caused the AMHQ Interactive buy to go elsewhere in Q4 2023 — was it pricing, audience targeting, measurement capabilities, or a competing platform that offered something we didn't?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's AMHQ and interactive formats as uniquely suited for health insurance brand awareness — a contextually safe, high-attention environment that Paid Social and Search cannot replicate. Emphasize brand-safe, premium context versus the performance-only channels where Aetna's $31M+ in Social and Search already operates.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Aetna spends $92.5M in total media. The $150K AMHQ buy represents less than 0.2% of that — a low-risk test with high brand visibility upside. If the incumbent who won that budget in Q4 2023 can't show you incremental reach beyond what your $16.1M Paid Social already delivers, that's the conversation I want to have.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Give me 20 minutes before your next Aetna planning session — I'll bring a side-by-side showing what the $150K AMHQ buy would have delivered versus whatever ran instead, and a revised proposal for the next cycle. What does your planning calendar look like in the next three weeks?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Neena, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we worked together on the Aetna enrollment programmatic campaign in Q4 2023, the one that closed at $68K through KINESSO. I'm reaching out because I've been looking at Aetna's broader media mix and I think there's a real gap we can help close before the next enrollment season hits. Aetna is running $47.6M in Broadcast and only $270K in CTV — and separately, we lost a $150K AMHQ Interactive opportunity last cycle that I'd like to understand and win back. I'm not calling to pitch blindly — I want to understand what's driving those allocation decisions so we can bring you something that actually fits the plan.
open_questions: When KINESSO executed the Q4 2023 enrollment programmatic buy, what audience segments performed best — and are those same segments being carried into the next planning cycle?
Who at Ogilvy New York or UM made the final call on the AMHQ Interactive buy in Q4 2023, and what criteria drove that decision away from us?
Is Carrah Kalat at CVS Health setting top-down media mix mandates that affect how UM allocates between Broadcast and CTV, or does UM have independent discretion on channel allocation?

sales_plays.title: Digital Mix Upgrade: From Paid Social to Premium Video
sales_plays.summary: ALDI is already an active customer with $76.7M in trailing spend, but $31.5M of that is concentrated in Paid Social. This play converts a portion of that social budget into higher-impact premium video and CTV inventory, deepening the relationship through Publicis Media.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Lisa Torres at Publicis Media referencing ALDI's current $31.5M Paid Social allocation and note that CPM efficiency in social has compressed industry-wide in Q1-Q2 — ask for 20 minutes to share a reallocation model built specifically for ALDI's value-grocery positioning.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Lisa: 'Of ALDI's $31.5M in Paid Social, how much is performance-driven versus brand-building, and is there a Q3 or Q4 tentpole — like Back-to-School or Holiday — where Caitlyn's team is pushing for broader reach beyond social feeds?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position premium online video and CTV as the reach extension layer that Paid Social cannot deliver — contextually safe, full-screen, unskippable environments that reinforce ALDI's 'high quality at low prices' brand message to households already in grocery-buying mode.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): ALDI's current CTV spend is $7.0M against $31.5M in Paid Social — a 4.5:1 ratio that is inverted versus category benchmarks. Shifting even 10% ($3.15M) of Paid Social into premium CTV/OLV would nearly double their CTV footprint and extend reach into cord-cutter households not reachable via broadcast's $703K buy.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Lisa Torres for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to co-build a Q3 Back-to-School scenario model — offer to bring a category-level grocery retail benchmark deck to anchor the conversation.

sales_plays.title: Displace the Q4 Loss Pattern: Win the Next $5K Before It Becomes Another Lost Opp
sales_plays.summary: Four consecutive Q4/2025 opportunities were lost at $5K each — a pattern suggesting a competitor or internal team is capturing small tactical buys that should be ours. This play diagnoses the loss pattern and displaces the incumbent on the next cycle by engaging Caitlyn Luchsinger directly on creator and influencer integration.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the CRM pattern directly in an internal prep note, then reach out to Caitlyn Luchsinger — ALDI's Head of Creator Marketing — with a specific hook: 'We've worked with ALDI's team at Publicis on larger buys, but I noticed creator-led activations may be going a different direction. I'd love to understand how you're thinking about creator content distribution heading into H2.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Caitlyn: 'When you're briefing creator campaigns, are you distributing that content through paid amplification on our platform, or is it staying organic — and who owns that paid amplification decision, you or Lisa's team at Publicis?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): If creator content is being amplified through a competitor or staying organic, position paid amplification of Caitlyn's creator assets through our premium inventory as a direct performance upgrade — same creative, broader reach, measurable lift against ALDI's grocery consideration metrics.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): ALDI's $3.2M Mobile App spend and $3.7M Online Video budget suggest active investment in performance-oriented digital. Even a $50K-$100K creator amplification test represents a 1.5-3% add-on to existing OLV spend — low risk, high visibility for Caitlyn's team to prove creator ROI internally.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Caitlyn for a 20-minute call this month — before Q3 planning locks — to share one case study of a grocery or value-retail brand that used creator content amplification to drive measurable basket-size lift. Keep the ask small and diagnostic, not a pitch.

Quick question for you: is there a Q3 or Q4 tentpole — Back-to-School, Holiday — where ALDI's team is looking to extend reach beyond social and really drive brand consideration at scale? I'd love 20 minutes to show you a reallocation model we built specifically for value-grocery advertisers in ALDI's position.

The four Q4/2025 opportunities that closed as lost at $5K each — were those going to a specific competitor platform, or were they reallocated internally within ALDI's budget?

sales_plays.title: CTV-to-Direct Upgrade for LYBALVI
sales_plays.summary: Alkermes is an active customer with a won PMP deal in 2024 and $14.5M in CTV spend. The opportunity is to convert programmatic/PMP activity into a premium direct buy that delivers stronger audience guarantees for the LYBALVI schizophrenia indication.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the won Alkermes-PMP-2024-TWC-Interactive deal with Anthony Lehmayer and acknowledge the existing relationship. Open with: 'Anthony, we delivered on the PMP we closed in August — I want to show you what a direct buy layered on top of that could unlock for LYBALVI reach and frequency control.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Anthony: 'With $14.5M committed to CTV, what does your current measurement framework look like for reaching psychiatrists and prescribers versus general audience — and where are you seeing the biggest gaps in verified HCP delivery?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium direct inventory against the PMP's audience variability. Direct buys on The Weather Channel's CTV environment offer deterministic HCP targeting with guaranteed impressions — something PMP floors cannot ensure for a specialty pharma brand like LYBALVI.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Alkermes is spending $14.5M in CTV today. Even shifting 10-15% of that budget ($1.45M–$2.2M) from open/PMP to a direct guaranteed buy would materially improve verified delivery against the schizophrenia-adjacent audience Alkermes needs for LYBALVI.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Anthony for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to walk through a custom audience overlap analysis showing how TWC's direct CTV inventory indexes against the LYBALVI target — low commitment, high signal value.

sales_plays.title: Recapture the Lost LYBALVI Digital Buy
sales_plays.summary: The Alkermes-Lybalvi 2024-TWC-Interactive opportunity was lost in June 2024. With $11.9M in total digital spend and $1.2M in Mobile App alone, a competitor is currently capturing budget that TWC should own. This play targets displacement of the incumbent digital partner.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Acknowledge the lost deal directly with Anthony: 'We didn't win the LYBALVI digital buy last June — I'd like to understand what the winning partner offered that we didn't, because Alkermes's digital footprint has grown and I think the conversation looks different now with $11.9M in play.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Who is currently running your LYBALVI digital and mobile display — and are you seeing the brand safety and contextual relevance you need for a psychiatric medication in those placements?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's contextual environment — weather-driven health content, sleep and wellness adjacency — as a brand-safe, high-relevance displacement of generic display or mobile app networks. LYBALVI's schizophrenia and sleep-adjacent indications align naturally with TWC's health and wellness content moments.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Alkermes is spending $301K in Internet Display and $1.2M in Mobile App — roughly $1.5M in digital channels where TWC can compete directly. Winning back even the display portion at $301K restores lost ground and opens the door to the broader $11.9M digital wallet.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a head-to-head pilot: a 60-day digital display and mobile test against TWC's health-adjacent inventory, benchmarked against current partner performance metrics. Ask Anthony to agree on a measurement KPI in the next call so the test has a clear win condition.

talk_track_opener: Anthony, I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working together since the PMP we closed last August, and I want to make sure we're building on that foundation rather than leaving opportunity on the table. We have real skin in the game with Alkermes and I want to earn more of your trust with results, not just pitches. On the LYBALVI side specifically, I've been looking at how your $14.5M CTV investment and $11.9M digital spend are structured, and I think there's a meaningful gap between what you're getting from programmatic and open marketplace buys versus what a direct, guaranteed placement in a contextually relevant environment could deliver for a specialty psychiatric brand. Given that the LYBALVI digital buy went another direction last June, I'm curious — what did that partner offer that made the difference, and is the current setup actually hitting your HCP reach targets the way you need it to?
open_questions: When the LYBALVI digital buy went to another partner in June 2024, what was the primary factor — pricing, targeting capabilities, or something else — and has that partner delivered against your expectations?
With $14.5M in CTV, how much of that is flowing through guaranteed direct buys versus PMP or programmatic, and do you have visibility into verified HCP delivery rates across those channels?
Who owns the media planning and buying decision for LYBALVI — is it entirely internal with you, or is there an agency or media partner involved that we should be aligned with?

Given that LYBALVI targets a psychiatric indication, how important is contextual brand safety in your current digital and mobile placements, and are you satisfied with where the $1.2M in Mobile App spend is running today?
As Alkermes advances its pipeline in sleep medicine and narcolepsy alongside schizophrenia, are there additional brands or indications beyond LYBALVI where you're evaluating media partners for 2025?

sales_plays.title: PMP to Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Allbirds has renewed TWC Interactive PMP deals in 2024, 2025, and 2026, but all three closed at $1 — indicating programmatic floor pricing with no incremental investment. With $74.5M in total trailing spend and $40.3M alone in Paid Social, there is clear budget capacity to move into higher-impact, direct-sold inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three consecutive PMP renewals (2024, 2025, 2026) and open with: 'Kelly, we've had Allbirds in our ecosystem three years running — I want to show you what brands at your spend level are doing beyond the PMP to drive measurable lift during retail tentpoles like Earth Day and back-to-school.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Kelly Olmstead: 'When Sid Lee is planning your seasonal tentpole windows, what KPIs are they optimizing against on weather-contextual placements — is it reach, ROAS, or brand consideration?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct-sold, weather-triggered CTV and display units as a natural complement to Allbirds' sustainability narrative — e.g., rain/cold-weather moments that make comfort and eco-material messaging land harder than static social.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Allbirds spends $11.0M on Internet Display and $7.3M on Online Video. Even a 10% reallocation from those channels into TWC direct-sold inventory represents $1.8M+ in incremental revenue — and moves the relationship from a $1 PMP line to a meaningful direct partnership.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute working session with Kelly and Donetta Allen at Sid Lee before their next planning cycle: 'Can we get 30 minutes with you and Donetta in the next two weeks to walk through a seasonal activation proposal for Q3 tentpoles?'

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Budget Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Allbirds allocates $40.3M — over 54% of total media spend — to Paid Social, a channel facing rising CPMs, signal loss post-iOS, and brand-safety scrutiny. This concentration creates a displacement opportunity to redirect even a fraction of that budget toward TWC's contextually relevant, brand-safe environments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a market observation routed through Donetta Allen at Sid Lee: 'Donetta, we're seeing several DTC footwear and apparel brands actively rebalancing away from Meta/TikTok CPM inflation — given Allbirds' $40.3M Paid Social commitment, I'd love to share what that shift looks like in practice.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'What percentage of that $40.3M Paid Social budget is performance-driven versus brand-building, and is Sid Lee seeing diminishing returns on any of those placements heading into H2?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered display and CTV as a brand-safe, contextually precise alternative — Allbirds' wool and natural-material messaging aligns naturally with seasonal weather moments (first cold snap, spring rain) that TWC can activate against in real time.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 5% reallocation of the $40.3M Paid Social budget equals $2.0M. Benchmarked against Allbirds' existing $3.6M CTV spend, adding $2M in TWC direct inventory would nearly double their CTV footprint with a publisher that already has a proven relationship.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer Sid Lee a competitive analysis deck: 'I can put together a two-page displacement analysis showing CPM benchmarks and reach overlap between your current Paid Social mix and TWC inventory — can I send that to you and Donetta by end of this week?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Kelly, I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Allbirds for three consecutive years through our PMP partnership — 2024, 2025, and now 2026 — so you're already in our ecosystem and we know the brand well. I work with several DTC footwear and apparel advertisers at your spend level and I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a meaningful gap between what you're getting today and what's possible. You're running $74.5M in total media, with over $40M concentrated in Paid Social and $11M in display — and your current TWC relationship is entirely programmatic floor. The brands I see getting the most out of us are using weather-contextual, direct-sold placements to make their sustainability story land at exactly the right moment — first cold snap, spring rain, the kind of moment when a wool runner or a recycled-material jacket is top of mind. I'd love to ask you one question: when Sid Lee is mapping your seasonal tentpole calendar, are weather moments currently part of that planning conversation, or is that a gap we could help fill together?
open_questions: When Sid Lee builds Allbirds' seasonal tentpole calendar — Earth Day, back-to-school, holiday — are weather-contextual moments currently part of the media planning framework, or is that a gap?
Who at Sid Lee or internally at Allbirds owns the decision to shift budget from Paid Social into new channels — is that Donetta Allen, Kelly Olmstead, or a joint call?
The $40.3M Paid Social allocation is significant — is Sid Lee seeing CPM inflation or reach saturation on Meta or TikTok that's prompting any rebalancing conversation for H2?

The current TWC relationship has been PMP-only for three years — has there been any internal discussion about moving into direct-sold or sponsorship inventory, or has that conversation not come up yet?
Allbirds is already spending $3.6M in CTV — what's driving that investment, and is there a target for growing the CTV footprint as a percentage of total media in the next planning cycle?

With $16M in CTV spend and $18.1M in digital, there is clear appetite to move from programmatic access to direct, guaranteed premium placements — expanding wallet share within the existing relationship.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the recently won Ally Bank-PMP-2025-TWC-Interactive deal with Elizabeth Lopez at EssenceMediaCom. Open by acknowledging the partnership is live and ask for a brief check-in call to share performance benchmarks from comparable FinServ PMP campaigns.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Elizabeth Lopez: 'Now that the PMP is running, how is the team evaluating whether programmatic access is delivering the brand-safe, premium placements Andrea Brimmer's team expects at the $16M CTV investment level?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position direct-sold, guaranteed inventory as the natural next step — eliminating auction volatility, securing premium dayparts and contextual adjacencies that a PMP floor cannot guarantee, especially relevant for a brand like Ally that competes on trust and rate messaging.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Ally spends $16M in CTV and $18.1M in digital. Even a 10% shift of CTV budget from programmatic to direct-sold premium represents ~$1.6M in incremental guaranteed spend — with measurably higher viewability and brand-safe context that supports Ally's savings-focused, educational brand positioning.

sales_plays.title: Search Intent Signal: Displace Incumbent with Contextual Search Alignment
sales_plays.summary: Bombora flags Ally Bank showing active intent around Search Marketing this week. With $5.7M in paid search spend and $12.4M in paid social, there is an opening to challenge how Ally's current search and social mix is being planned — and position our contextual or search-adjacent solutions as a smarter complement or replacement to incumbent channels.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the Bombora signal directly: 'Our intent data shows Ally Bank is actively researching Search Marketing right now — we wanted to reach out before your next planning cycle because we have a specific angle on how contextual and search-aligned placements can extend what your $5.7M search budget is already doing.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Andrea Brimmer or Elizabeth Lopez: 'When your team evaluates the $5.7M in paid search, are you finding that search alone is capturing in-market savings and CD shoppers, or are there gaps where consumers are researching rates but not yet converting through search?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our inventory as a search-intent complement — reaching consumers who are actively researching high-yield savings, CDs, and investing (Ally Invest) in contextually relevant environments, capturing demand that paid search misses because users haven't yet typed a query.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Ally's $1.2M in display and $160K in online video suggest the contextual/display line is significantly underfunded relative to the $12.4M paid social and $5.7M search budgets. A reallocation of even $500K–$1M from lower-performing display or social toward contextually targeted, intent-aligned placements could close the gap between search-driven and awareness-driven conversion.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send a one-page intent-to-inventory map — showing where Ally's target savings audience is actively researching financial products this week — and request a 15-minute call with Elizabeth Lopez at EssenceMediaCom to review it before the next media planning session.

talk_track_opener: Hey Elizabeth — this is [Rep Name] from [Company]. We work with your team on the Ally Bank business, and I wanted to connect now that the TWC Interactive PMP deal is live. On a separate note — our Bombora intent data flagged Ally Bank as actively researching Search Marketing this week, which caught our attention given the $5.7M search budget and the $12.4M in paid social. I'm curious whether the team is finding that search is fully capturing in-market savings and CD shoppers, or if there are gaps where consumers are researching rates but not yet converting through a query. Would you have 20 minutes in the next couple of weeks to walk through both of these angles together?

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-contextualized CTV and display inventory as a complement to the existing PMP — rare disease patients and caregivers over-index on weather-adjacent content consumption, and Alnylam's current $120K CTV spend suggests they are testing but not scaling.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Alnylam spends $120K in CTV today against a $10.1M total budget — that is roughly 1.2% of spend in a channel that typically captures 15-20% of pharma budgets at this tier. sales_plays.title: Pipeline Milestone Timing Hook

sales_plays.title: PMP to Premium Direct Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Alzheimer's Association has held a TWC Interactive PMP for three consecutive years (2024–2026). With $39.2M in digital spend and $9.5M in online video alone, there is clear headroom to expand from the current PMP relationship into premium direct placements, high-impact display, or branded content that the PMP deal does not currently capture.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three-year PMP partnership and congratulate Michael Reich on the continuity. Note that the 2026 PMP win closed January 3 and ask if the team has had a chance to review performance data from the 2025 flight — use that as the entry point.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Michael Reich: 'Beyond the PMP, where is the $10.3M in display and $9.5M in online video currently running, and are those buys delivering the caregiver and early-detection audience quality you need at that scale?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position premium direct inventory — high-impact homepage takeovers, branded content units, and contextually targeted video — as the natural complement to the existing PMP. The PMP secures efficiency; direct buys secure brand safety, share of voice, and audience precision that programmatic cannot guarantee.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The current PMP is a $1 deal value on record — a placeholder. Against a $39.2M digital budget and $9.5M in online video, even capturing 5% of the video line as premium direct represents roughly $475K in incremental revenue. Frame the ask around a pilot that is proportional to what they already spend.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute working session with Michael Reich and Karen Seamen at Cramer-Krasselt before the end of the month to walk through a premium inventory proposal built around the caregiver and dementia-awareness audience segments — low commitment, high relevance.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Spend with Contextual Video
sales_plays.summary: Alzheimer's Association is spending $9.7M in paid social and $9.5M in online video — a combined $19.2M in digital video and social. Bombora signals show active intent around Search Marketing this week, suggesting the team is evaluating channel mix. This is the moment to make a case for redirecting a portion of paid social dollars toward contextually safe, premium video environments where brand adjacency matters for a cause-driven nonprofit.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the Bombora signal: 'I noticed your team is actively researching search marketing and channel strategy right now. That tells me you may be in a planning or reallocation conversation — I want to make sure we are part of it.' Address this to Karen Seamen at Cramer-Krasselt as the agency relationship holder.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Karen Seamen: 'For the $9.7M in paid social, how are you measuring brand-safe adjacency and audience quality versus what you are seeing from premium video environments — and is there pressure from Michael Reich to show differentiated reach beyond social platforms?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Argue that for a nonprofit like the Alzheimer's Association, brand context is not optional — appearing alongside dementia-related editorial content, caregiver resources, and health journalism in a premium environment drives credibility that social feeds cannot replicate. Position premium contextual video as a displacement of the lower-quality social impressions, not an add-on.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If even 10% of the $9.7M paid social budget — roughly $970K — shifted to premium contextual video, the Alzheimer's Association would gain guaranteed brand-safe placements, verified audience segments, and direct publisher accountability. Contrast this with the opaque auction environment where the current $9.7M is running.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send Karen Seamen a one-page competitive context brief — showing where dementia-awareness and caregiver-targeted campaigns perform best across premium video versus social — within 48 hours, with a follow-up call scheduled for the same week while the search marketing intent signal is still active.

talk_track_opener: Hey Michael, this is [Rep Name] from [Company] — we have been working with the Alzheimer's Association team for three consecutive years now through the TWC Interactive PMP, most recently closing the 2026 deal in January, so I feel like I have a real sense of what you are trying to accomplish across awareness, caregiver support, and early detection outreach. I wanted to reach out directly because I think there is a meaningful opportunity we have not yet explored together. Looking at your overall digital investment — $39.2M across display, video, paid social, and search — and knowing that your current PMP with us is just one piece of that, I am curious: for the $9.5M you are putting into online video and the $9.7M in paid social, are those buys delivering the brand-safe, contextually relevant environment that a cause like Alzheimer's awareness really demands? I would love 30 minutes with you and Karen at Cramer-Krasselt to show you what a premium direct layer on top of the existing PMP could look like — no obligation, just a concrete proposal built around your audience.
open_questions: The TWC Interactive PMP has renewed three years in a row — what would need to be true for you to expand that relationship into direct premium placements for the 2026 campaign cycle?

For the $9.7M in paid social, who owns the decision on whether that budget stays on social platforms or gets reallocated — is that Michael Reich directly or does Cramer-Krasselt drive that recommendation?
Given the Alzheimer's Association's brand sensitivity around dementia and caregiver content, how are you currently vetting brand-safe adjacency for the $9.5M in online video spend?

The Bombora signal shows active research into search marketing this week — is that a new channel you are testing, or is it a sign that you are reconsidering how the $6.7M in search spend is being allocated?
Lopez Negrete Communications is handling multicultural creative — is there a separate media budget tied to multicultural audiences that is being planned independently from the Cramer-Krasselt scope?

sales_plays.title: Amazon Fashion x TWC Premium Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Amazon is an active customer with $1.25M+ in recent TWC wins across Health and Prime verticals. Amazon Fashion CMO Allie Oosta represents an untapped brand within the Amazon family — no Fashion-specific spend is visible in CRM history, creating a clear expansion lane.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the Q2 2026 AMZ Health win ($650K) and the Q1 2026 Health win ($600K) as proof of TWC's performance for Amazon verticals. Open with: 'We've delivered strong results for your Health team two quarters running — I want to make sure Allie Oosta and the Fashion team are seeing the same opportunity heading into fall tentpole season.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Allie Oosta: 'How is Amazon Fashion currently allocating media between brand awareness and performance-driven placements, and is there a dedicated budget for weather-triggered or seasonal fashion moments like back-to-school and fall transition?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-audience targeting as a natural fit for fashion retail — coat season, rain gear, activewear — connecting real-time weather signals to Amazon Fashion purchase intent at the moment consumers are actively thinking about seasonal wardrobe needs.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Amazon's total digital spend is $2.525B with $785.5M in Display and $78.3M in Online Video. Even a 1% reallocation of Display toward TWC's premium fashion-contextual inventory represents ~$7.8M in incremental opportunity. Frame the ask as a Q3 test at $500K–$750K to benchmark against existing Merkle-managed programmatic buys.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Allie Oosta for a 30-minute working session before July 1 to map TWC's fashion-weather audience segments against Amazon Fashion's Q3 and Q4 tentpole calendar — specifically Prime Day, back-to-school, and fall transition windows.

sales_plays.title: Merkle Programmatic-to-Direct Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Merkle holds the AOR for Digital Programmatic on the Amazon account. Amazon's $785.5M in Internet Display and $78.3M in Online Video are largely flowing through programmatic pipes managed by Merkle. The PMP deal (Won, $1, Jan 2026) signals a programmatic relationship exists but may be undermonetized — a direct I/O path via TWC Interactive could deliver better CPMs, brand safety, and weather-contextual targeting that programmatic cannot replicate.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Engage Dessiah Davis at Omnicom/Merkle directly, referencing the existing PMP relationship (Jan 2026) and the two Health wins. Say: 'Dessiah, we've been working with your team on the Health business and the PMP — I want to show you where a direct TWC Interactive buy is outperforming programmatic delivery for Amazon's category peers, specifically on viewability and weather-contextual engagement rates.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Dessiah: 'On the current PMP and programmatic buys for Amazon, what KPIs are you optimizing against — is it reach and frequency, viewability, or lower-funnel conversion signals — and are there placements where Merkle feels programmatic is leaving brand-safety or contextual quality on the table?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct I/O inventory as a premium complement — not a replacement — to Merkle's programmatic stack. Highlight that weather-triggered creative served via direct buy eliminates auction inefficiency, guarantees placement in high-intent weather-check moments, and provides Amazon with first-look access to TWC's exclusive audience segments unavailable in open programmatic.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Amazon spends $170.3M on Mobile App and $78.3M on Online Video. A direct TWC Interactive buy targeting even 5% of the Mobile App budget ($8.5M) in weather-contextual placements — tied to Prime Benefits Speed messaging already active in Q2 2026 at $350K — would represent a 24x scale of current direct investment with proven creative already in-market.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a side-by-side programmatic vs. direct TWC performance audit using Q1 and Q2 2026 Health campaign data. Ask Dessiah for a 20-minute call in the next two weeks to review the data before Q3 planning locks — framing it as protecting Merkle's recommendation quality to Amazon, not displacing Merkle's role.

talk_track_opener: Hey Allie — I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working closely with your Amazon Health team for the past two quarters, including a $650K campaign that closed in March and a $600K Health buy that launched in Q1. The results have been strong enough that I wanted to make sure the Fashion team was part of the conversation before your Q3 planning locks in. We've also got an active Prime Benefits campaign running right now, so we're already embedded in how Amazon thinks about reaching consumers at high-intent moments. I was looking at how Amazon Fashion is positioned heading into fall — back-to-school, seasonal wardrobe transitions, outerwear — and there's a real gap I keep seeing. Your Health team is using weather-contextual targeting to reach consumers when they're most receptive, but Fashion hasn't tapped into that yet. With $785M flowing through display and Merkle managing the programmatic side, there's a meaningful opportunity to test a direct TWC buy that connects weather signals — first cold snap of fall, rain events, temperature drops — directly to Amazon Fashion purchase moments. I'd love 30 minutes before July 1 to walk you through how we'd structure a Q3 pilot. Would that work?
open_questions: Allie, the Health team has run two strong campaigns with us — has that performance data been shared across Amazon's brand teams, and does Fashion have a separate media budget or does it pool with broader Amazon retail planning?

Dessiah, on the current PMP structure with TWC — is that being actively trafficked through Merkle's programmatic stack, and what would it take to convert a portion of that to a direct I/O buy for a specific campaign like Amazon Fashion or Prime Day?

For Amazon Fashion's Q3 and Q4 planning, are weather-triggered moments like the first cold snap or fall temperature drops already part of your media timing strategy, or is seasonal planning driven more by calendar dates like back-to-school and Prime Day?

Given that Merkle holds the programmatic AOR, who owns the decision to shift spend from programmatic to direct placements — is that a Merkle recommendation, an Amazon internal call, or a joint decision between Allie's team and Omnicom?

The Prime Benefits Speed campaign is live right now at $350K — is there appetite to extend that creative into a companion fashion or seasonal unit before the campaign ends, or does each Amazon brand vertical require a fully separate approval process?

sales_plays.title: PMP to Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: American Airlines has two consecutive won PMP deals (2024 and 2025) through TWC Interactive, confirming an active and trusted relationship. This play converts that programmatic PMP foundation into higher-value direct or sponsorship inventory, expanding wallet share beyond the current $1 placeholder deals into meaningful guaranteed spend.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email or call Amanda Lewensky at EssenceMediaCom referencing the back-to-back PMP wins in July 2024 and February 2025. Acknowledge the consistency and use it as proof of performance: 'We've now run two consecutive PMP campaigns together — I want to show you what a direct buy or sponsorship layer on top of that programmatic base could unlock for American's travel audience targeting.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Amanda: 'Beyond the PMP buys, where is EssenceMediaCom currently allocating American's $9.6M CTV and $9.9M display budget — and is any of that going to weather or travel-intent contextual environments outside of what we're already running together?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's owned-and-operated inventory as a natural upgrade path — travelers actively checking weather before booking are a high-intent audience that complements American's existing $54.5M search spend. A direct buy captures that intent moment before the search query is even made.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): American's total estimated media spend is $113.7M, with $48M in digital and $9.6M in CTV. The current PMP relationship represents a fraction of that. Even a 5% shift of the digital display budget ($9.9M) toward a direct TWC buy would represent ~$495K in incremental revenue — a concrete expansion target to bring to Ron DeFeo's team.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Amanda for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar before end of month to walk through a direct buy proposal that builds on the two PMP campaigns? I'll bring audience overlap data showing how TWC complements the search and CTV spend American is already running.'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Display with Weather-Intent CTV
sales_plays.summary: American Airlines is spending $34.5M in Paid Social and $1.5M in Online Video — a heavy skew toward social platforms for video and display. This play challenges the efficiency of that allocation by positioning TWC's CTV and contextual video inventory as a higher-intent, less cluttered alternative for travel-minded audiences.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Amanda Lewensky with a pointed observation: 'American is running $34.5M through Paid Social — that's the single largest digital line item outside of search. I want to share some data on how travel-intent audiences on social compare to weather-triggered CTV audiences when it comes to flight consideration and booking behavior.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'How is EssenceMediaCom currently measuring the contribution of Paid Social to American's lower-funnel conversion — and is there pressure from Ron DeFeo's team to demonstrate clearer attribution from that $34.5M?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's CTV environment delivers travel-planning audiences at the moment of weather-driven decision-making — a context that social feeds cannot replicate. For an airline whose bookings are directly tied to weather events, disruptions, and seasonal travel windows, contextual alignment in CTV is a structural advantage over social display.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): American's CTV spend is $9.6M today. If even 10% of the $34.5M Paid Social budget were reallocated to TWC CTV, that represents $3.45M in potential displacement revenue — moving dollars from a crowded, attribution-challenged environment into a measurable, brand-safe, travel-contextual one.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer a competitive analysis deliverable: 'Give me two weeks and I'll put together a side-by-side showing CTV audience quality and attribution benchmarks for travel advertisers versus Paid Social norms. Would that be useful to bring into your next planning conversation with American's team?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Amanda, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together on American Airlines' PMP buys — you've got two consecutive wins with us, July 2024 and February 2025 — so I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a real opportunity to build on that momentum rather than just renew the same structure. We've earned the trust, and I want to make sure we're maximizing what that relationship can do for American's media goals. Looking at American's overall spend profile — $113.7M in total estimated media, $48M in digital, $9.6M in CTV, and $34.5M in Paid Social — I see a couple of places where TWC could be doing a lot more work. The PMP deals are a starting point, but American's audience is fundamentally weather-driven: people checking forecasts before they book, before they pack, before they fly. I'd love to ask you one question: outside of what we're running together in PMP, where is the team currently leaning for travel-intent CTV and contextual display — and is there appetite to test a direct buy or a reallocation conversation before the next planning cycle?
open_questions: Outside of the two PMP campaigns we've run together, which other publishers or platforms are currently receiving American's CTV and digital display budget — and is TWC being considered for any direct or guaranteed buys in the current planning cycle?
How is EssenceMediaCom measuring the ROI of American's $34.5M Paid Social investment, and has there been internal pressure from Ron DeFeo's team to demonstrate clearer attribution from that channel?
Is the buying decision for a direct or sponsorship-level TWC deal made at the EssenceMediaCom level, or does it require sign-off from Ron DeFeo or another stakeholder at American Airlines directly?

American is spending $54.5M in paid search — is the team exploring any upper-funnel or mid-funnel tactics specifically designed to feed that search demand before competitors capture the query, and where does TWC fit in that conversation today?

Given that EssenceMediaCom Canada also holds AOR responsibilities, is there a separate budget and planning process for Canadian media, and would a TWC expansion conversation need to be run through both teams independently?

sales_plays.title: Scale AE's Digital Investment Into Premium Weather-Driven Moments
sales_plays.summary: American Eagle is an active PMP customer with two consecutive won deals in 2024-2025. With $81.4M in digital spend and $71.5M concentrated in Paid Social, there is a clear opportunity to expand their TWC footprint into higher-impact, weather-triggered inventory that complements their existing PMP relationship.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins ('We've now delivered back-to-back successful PMP campaigns for AE in 2024 and into 2025') and note that Craig Brommers and Jamie D'Attoma already have confidence in the TWC relationship — use that trust as the entry point for a broader conversation about Q3/Q4 planning.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jamie D'Attoma: 'Given that AE is putting $71.5M into Paid Social, what does the current measurement framework look like for incremental reach — and are there audiences you're hitting on social that you'd want to reinforce with contextually relevant, weather-moment inventory?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered display and online video units as a complement to AE's paid social spend — when a cold snap hits a key market, AE's fleece and outerwear creative can serve in the exact moment of weather-driven purchase intent, something Paid Social alone cannot time to real-world conditions.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): AE's current TWC PMP deals are logged at nominal value; the $150K Spring 2024 opportunity that was lost shows there is budget appetite at that level. With $81.4M in digital spend, even a 1% reallocation toward weather-triggered TWC inventory represents $814K in incremental investment — frame the expansion ask in the $150K–$300K range as a low-risk test against their existing social budget.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jamie D'Attoma for a 30-minute planning call before end of current quarter to map AE's Q3 tentpole calendar (back-to-school, fall transition) against TWC's weather-moment inventory windows — specific ask: 'Can we get 30 minutes with you and Craig's team before [specific date 3 weeks out] to build a weather-moment overlay on your Q3 plan?'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Overconcentration With TWC Direct & CTV
sales_plays.summary: AE has $71.5M locked in Paid Social — a single-channel concentration risk — while their CTV spend sits at only $463K and Online Video at $1.0M. Two prior TWC opportunities (Spring 2024 at $150K and Q4 2023 at $50K) were lost, likely to social-first budget decisions. This play targets displacing a slice of that social budget with TWC's direct and CTV offerings by surfacing the reach and brand-safety gaps that Paid Social cannot solve.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a data-led observation to Craig Brommers or Jamie D'Attoma: 'We've looked at AE's media mix and noticed $71.5M is concentrated in Paid Social versus $463K in CTV — at a time when platform signal loss and brand-safety scrutiny on social are at an all-time high. We think there's a case to be made for rebalancing.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Craig Brommers: 'When you look at your Paid Social performance today versus 18 months ago, how has signal quality and CPM efficiency trended — and is there internal pressure to diversify reach channels ahead of any further platform changes?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct-sold inventory and CTV as a brand-safe, weather-contextualized alternative that reaches AE's 15–34 demo in high-intent moments (weather app check before a shopping trip, CTV in-home during a weather event) — environments where AE's Aerie and American Eagle creative can own the moment without auction volatility or brand-safety risk.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): AE's CTV spend is $463K against a $98.1M total media budget — that is less than 0.5% of spend in a channel that now commands 30%+ of premium video attention. The lost Q4 2023 TWC deal was $50K; the Spring 2024 loss was $150K. A displacement ask of $250K–$500K shifted from Paid Social to TWC CTV and direct represents less than 1% of their social budget but would 2–3x their current TWC relationship value.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive audit — offer to build a side-by-side reach and frequency analysis showing TWC CTV + direct versus AE's current social mix for a specific upcoming tentpole (back-to-school or fall launch). Ask: 'If we put together a reach overlap analysis for your Q3 back-to-school window by [specific date], would that be a useful input for your planning conversation with Craig's team?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Jamie — this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've had a great run with the AE team over the past 18 months, with two consecutive PMP wins, and I wanted to reach out now while Q3 planning is still open. We work with a number of major specialty retailers in AE's tier, and we're seeing real lift when weather-triggered inventory is layered into existing digital plans — particularly for brands with strong outerwear and seasonal apparel stories like American Eagle and Aerie. I wanted to get your perspective on how we can build on what's already working.
open_questions: Given that two consecutive TWC PMP deals have closed, what would need to be true for AE to move a portion of the relationship into direct-sold or reserved inventory for Q3 and Q4?
With $71.5M in Paid Social, how is Craig's team currently measuring incremental reach beyond social platforms — and is there a mandate to diversify before the next planning cycle?
The Spring 2024 and Q4 2023 TWC opportunities were lost — can you share what drove those decisions so we can make sure this year's proposal is structured differently?
Who owns the CTV and online video budget decision at AE — is that Craig Brommers directly, or does it run through a separate media planning team at your agency?

As AE plans for back-to-school and the fall seasonal transition, is there a specific audience segment — Aerie versus American Eagle, or a particular age demo — where you feel current reach is weakest and where a weather-moment overlay would be most valuable?

sales_plays.title: Shift Mobile Spend On-Property
sales_plays.summary: American Family is actively researching Mobile this week per Bombora, yet their $2.5M Mobile App budget is almost certainly flowing through Digitas/Publicis to off-property programmatic inventory. This play displaces that fragmented spend with a direct, on-property mobile solution tied to their automotive insurance vertical.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Annie Richardson at Digitas referencing the live Bombora mobile intent signal: 'We're seeing American Family spike on Mobile research this week — wanted to reach out before that planning conversation closes. We have on-property mobile inventory in the automotive insurance category that Digitas clients have used to move buyers from awareness to quote-start.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Of the $2.5M American Family is currently allocating to Mobile App, how much is running through open-exchange programmatic versus direct publisher relationships — and is Sherina Smith's team satisfied with the view-through attribution they're getting from that mix?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): American Family's mobile budget is likely spread across fragmented programmatic supply via Digitas and Publicis Media, creating brand-safety and attribution gaps. On-property mobile placements deliver verified automotive-intender audiences with first-party contextual signals that open exchange cannot replicate.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Even redirecting 20% of the $2.5M Mobile App budget ($500K) from programmatic to on-property direct would concentrate spend against in-market auto insurance shoppers rather than broad audience pools — a measurable improvement in cost-per-quote-start that we can model before the buy.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Annie, can we get 20 minutes with you and the AmFam planning team before end of this month to walk through a mobile audience overlap analysis against their current programmatic segments? I can have something specific to American Family's automotive vertical ready within 48 hours.'

sales_plays.title: CMO Warm Introduction via Agency Advocate
sales_plays.summary: With no prior CRM history at American Family, the fastest path to Sherina Smith (CMO - Enterprise) runs through Annie Richardson at Digitas, who controls media planning and buying. This play builds agency trust first, then earns a warm introduction to the brand side.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Call Annie Richardson directly: 'Annie, I work with several Digitas clients in the insurance vertical and noticed American Family's mobile intent is elevated this week. Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'RPA owns digital media buying and social for Honda — are they currently allocating mobile budget through programmatic only, or is there a direct IO mobile line in the 2026 plan we should be competing for?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's on-property mobile weather-contextual inventory against the $45.1M Honda already spends on Mobile App — argue that PMP CPMs leave impression quality and contextual relevance on the table compared to a direct premium mobile placement tied to weather-driven auto shopping moments.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Honda spends $45.1M on Mobile App broadly. Even a 5% shift of that budget to a direct premium mobile buy represents ~$2.25M in incremental spend — frame the lost $100K Q2 2025 IO as a proof-of-concept opportunity to re-open at a larger commitment.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Spend from Dailey to TWC Social/Display

Use the active PMP relationship and Honda's Acura/Power Equipment brand family to route incremental social and display dollars through TWC rather than Dailey-controlled channels.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Honda's Paid Social spend is $96.2M and Internet Display is $49.7M — a 3% reallocation from Dailey-managed social to a TWC direct display/video test equals ~$4.4M. Frame the lost $50K Power Equipment IO as a low-risk entry point to prove out the displacement thesis.

sales_plays.title: Reactivate the Q4 2023 Relationship

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Charlie: 'When you look back at Q4 2023, what was the deciding factor that pushed you toward a different partner — was it pricing, targeting capability, or something else about the proposal structure?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): If the loss was on price or scale, position against the $13.7M CTV and $22.5M Search spend — Amica is clearly investing heavily in performance channels, and TWC's weather-triggered audience targeting can complement that mix with contextual precision that broad CTV buys can't replicate.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the opportunity against Amica's $123.3M total media footprint. Even recapturing a modest 1% of annual spend represents $1.2M+ in partnership. The two lost deals totaled $20K — the re-entry ask should be sized proportionally larger to reflect Amica's actual investment level.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Charlie for a 20-minute working session in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 20 minutes on the calendar before end of [current month] to walk through one specific targeting use case for Amica's auto book? No pitch deck — just a conversation about fit.'

sales_plays.title: Weather Window Planning Conversation
sales_plays.summary: Amica's $22.5M in Search and $35.5M in Paid Social suggest a performance-first media mix. Weather-driven automotive demand windows (winter driving, storm seasons) are a plausible planning hook given the vertical and prior Winter Weather proposal. Use this as a forward-looking planning question — not a confirmed campaign fact — to get in front of Renee Gilmore and Media by Mother before Q4 budgets are set.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Renee Gilmore at Amica and reference the prior WinterWeather WWT/TTD proposal from October 2023 as context: 'We put together a winter weather targeting proposal for your team about a year ago. I'd love to understand how you're thinking about weather-driven demand moments for the auto book heading into Q4 — whether or not that proposal was the right fit at the time.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Renee: 'For Amica's auto insurance line, does your team plan media around specific weather or seasonal demand windows — like winter storm season or spring renewal periods — or is the strategy more always-on across the year?'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): With $35.5M in Paid Social and $13.7M in CTV already in market, Amica has the creative infrastructure to activate quickly. A weather-triggered contextual buy at even $250K–$500K could drive measurable lift in branded search volume during high-intent weather moments — a metric their Search-heavy mix would make easy to track.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a planning conversation, not a proposal: 'Would it be useful to share a brief on how other mutual insurers have used weather windows in their Q4 auto planning? I can send it over this week and we can decide together if a follow-up call makes sense.'

sales_plays.summary: Amplifon USA is an active TWC customer with two consecutive PMP wins (July 2024 and January 2025). steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins with Brianna Pieschel: 'We've had two successful PMP activations together — July 2024 and January 2025 — and I want to show you what a direct buy on TWC could unlock that the PMP layer can't.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Brianna: 'Of Miracle-Ear's $39.1M in Paid Social spend, how much is being evaluated for reallocation toward contextually relevant, weather-driven environments where hearing health intent is higher?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct inventory as a contextual upgrade — weather-triggered moments (cold, wind, loud outdoor conditions) align naturally with hearing aid consideration. Direct buys give Miracle-Ear first-look placement, guaranteed impressions, and creative control that a PMP cannot guarantee.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Amplifon's CTV spend is only $65K against $45.9M in total digital — that's less than 0.15% of digital budget in CTV. A direct buy expansion into TWC's CTV and premium digital inventory represents a clear whitespace opportunity, even a modest $500K shift from Paid Social would represent a 7x increase in their TWC investment.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Brianna for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to present a custom direct buy proposal mapped to Miracle-Ear's Q3 planning cycle — offer to loop in Teresa Barreira at Publicis.Sapient to align on media planning priorities.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars: Capture Miracle-Ear's $39.1M Reallocation Opportunity
sales_plays.summary: Amplifon USA is spending $39.1M on Paid Social — the dominant share of their $45.9M digital budget — while CTV sits at just $65K and Online Video at $134K. This imbalance creates a displacement opportunity: make the case that TWC's premium video and CTV inventory delivers superior reach, targeting, and brand safety for the Miracle-Ear audience versus social platforms.

We're seeing brands in the hearing health and senior-care space shift 10-15% of that budget to premium CTV and contextual video — and we want to show you what that looks like on TWC.'
