sales_plays.title: Upgrade PMP to Premium Direct
sales_plays.summary: BetterHelp has renewed their TWC Interactive PMP deal twice (2024 and 2025), signaling consistent platform trust. With $80.6M in total media spend and $13.3M already in CTV, there is clear budget appetite to move from programmatic PMP access into guaranteed premium direct inventory with richer targeting and brand-safe environments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins with Matthew Armstrong — 'You've now run with us through two PMP cycles, July 2024 and January 2025. I want to show you what the next tier of that relationship looks like in terms of reach and control.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Matthew: 'Of the $13.3M you're putting into CTV right now, how much of that is running programmatically versus direct guaranteed deals, and where are you seeing the most drop-off in brand-safe inventory quality?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's owned-and-operated premium CTV and weather-contextual display as a step up from PMP — guaranteed impressions, first-party audience segments around health and wellness intent, and brand-safe environments critical for a mental health brand like BetterHelp.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): BetterHelp spends $13.3M in CTV. Even shifting 20% of that spend ($2.66M) from open PMP to a direct guaranteed package unlocks priority placement, viewability guarantees, and dedicated account support — measurable against their existing PMP baseline from the 2024-2025 cycles.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Matthew for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to walk through a custom CTV direct package proposal built against their existing PMP performance data — 'I can have a draft proposal to you by [specific date] if you can give me 30 minutes with you and Lesley Bielby at DiMassimo Goldstein.'

sales_plays.title: Capture Search Dollars with On-Platform Audience Targeting
sales_plays.summary: BetterHelp is spending $32.1M on Digital Search — their single largest digital channel — while their on-platform digital presence (Display: $419K, Mobile App: $491K, Online Video: $1.2M) is comparatively thin. This signals over-reliance on Google/Bing for demand capture and an opportunity to displace a portion of that search budget with TWC's intent-rich, contextually relevant audience targeting that reaches users earlier in the mental health consideration journey.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a data-backed observation to Lesley Bielby at DiMassimo Goldstein: 'We've been looking at BetterHelp's media mix — $32.1M in search is a significant commitment to bottom-funnel demand capture. We think there's a real case for shifting some of that investment upstream to intercept the mental health consideration moment before someone types a query.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Lesley: 'When someone searches for therapy or mental health support, what does DiMassimo Goldstein believe is happening in the days or weeks before that search — and are you currently investing to influence that pre-search window?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-contextual and health-interest audience segments as a pre-search demand generation layer — reaching users during high-stress, high-anxiety moments (weather disruption, seasonal transitions) when mental health consideration spikes, before they reach a search engine.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): BetterHelp's display ($419K), mobile app ($491K), and online video ($1.2M) total roughly $2.1M on-platform digital — less than 7% of their $32.1M search spend. A modest reallocation of even 5% of search budget ($1.6M) into TWC's audience-targeted digital products would represent a 76% increase in their current on-platform digital footprint and create measurable upper-funnel lift to support search efficiency.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive analysis session with Lesley and Matthew: 'I'd like to bring our insights team to show you where BetterHelp's mental health audience is over-indexing on our platform versus what search alone can capture — can we get 45 minutes on the calendar within the next three weeks?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Matthew, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together now through two consecutive PMP cycles — the July 2024 deal and the January 2025 renewal — so you know we're not starting from scratch here. I wanted to reach out because I think we've only scratched the surface of what's possible given BetterHelp's overall media footprint. Looking at your mix, you're running $13.3M in CTV and $32.1M in search — that's a lot of weight on demand capture. What I'm curious about is whether you're seeing the brand-safe, premium inventory quality you need on the CTV side through the PMP, or if there are gaps that a direct guaranteed deal could solve. And separately, with that much search spend, I'd love to understand how DiMassimo Goldstein is thinking about the pre-search moment — because we have some data on where mental health consideration starts on our platform that I think would be worth 30 minutes of your time.
open_questions: Of the two PMP deals you've run with us in 2024 and 2025, what performance metrics have been most important to Matthew Armstrong's team — and where have you felt the PMP model has limited what you could measure or control?

With $32.1M going into paid search, who at BetterHelp or DiMassimo Goldstein owns the decision to test upper-funnel channels as a way to improve search efficiency — is that Matthew Armstrong, or does that conversation live with Lesley Bielby on the strategy side?

BetterHelp's display and mobile app spend combined is under $1M against an $80M total budget — is that a deliberate strategic choice, or is it a reflection of where measurement and attribution have been harder to prove?

Given that BetterHelp is a mental health platform, how does DiMassimo Goldstein currently evaluate brand-safety and contextual adjacency requirements when selecting programmatic versus direct inventory partners?

Are there specific audience segments — for example, users experiencing high-stress life moments or seasonal mental health triggers — that BetterHelp is actively trying to reach but finding difficult to access through your current search and social mix?

sales_plays.title: Big Lots Brand Family Expansion into Video & Display
sales_plays.summary: Big Lots is an active TWC Interactive PMP customer with two consecutive won deals but zero spend in Online Video and minimal Display ($27K). With $7.3M flowing through Paid Social and a broad multi-category retail footprint, there is clear whitespace to expand into premium video and richer display formats on Weather Channel inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive TWC Interactive PMP wins (Feb 2025 and Sept 2024) and congratulate Dominic Ollhoff on the continuity. Note that Big Lots currently runs $0 in Online Video and only $27K in Display against a $7.4M total digital budget — and position this conversation as a natural next chapter with a partner they already trust.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Dominic: 'Given that nearly all of your $7.4M digital budget is concentrated in Paid Social, what would it take internally to justify testing a premium video format that reaches Big Lots shoppers in a high-intent, weather-contextual environment?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's streaming and connected-TV video inventory as the missing upper-funnel complement to Big Lots' heavy Paid Social spend — weather-triggered video ads can surface home goods, furniture, and seasonal deals at the exact moment consumers are planning purchases driven by weather context.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Big Lots spends $7.3M on Paid Social but $0 on Online Video. Even a 5% reallocation — roughly $365K — into TWC video would represent a 36,500x increase over current Display investment and open a measurable incremental reach channel against the same value-seeking audience.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Dominic and loop in Craig Woerz at Media Storm to join a 30-minute product walkthrough of TWC's video and premium display formats before the end of this quarter — framed as informing Media Storm's next planning cycle.

sales_plays.title: Retail Tentpole Planning Hook via Agency Path
sales_plays.summary: Big Lots' Bombora intent signal around Entertainment this week, combined with the retail vertical's natural tentpole calendar (back-to-school, fall home refresh, holiday), creates a planning-season opening to engage Craig Woerz at Media Storm before budgets are locked for Q3/Q4 activations.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Craig Woerz at Media Storm, referencing the active TWC Interactive PMP relationship and noting that Big Lots is showing live Bombora intent around Entertainment — a signal that aligns with seasonal home-entertainment and furniture purchase cycles heading into fall.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Craig: 'As you're building out Big Lots' Q3 and Q4 media plan, which tentpole moments — back-to-school, fall home refresh, or holiday — are you prioritizing, and is there budget flexibility to layer in weather-contextual placements that map to those windows?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered and contextual inventory as a planning-stage add that amplifies Big Lots' value messaging during high-purchase-intent weather moments — cold snaps driving home goods, rain events driving indoor furniture browsing — timed to the tentpoles Craig is already planning around.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Big Lots' current TWC PMP investment is logged at $1 per deal — indicating early-stage or test-level commitment. Framing a Q3/Q4 tentpole package as a step-up investment, even a $150K–$250K seasonal activation, would represent a meaningful and measurable expansion of the existing relationship ahead of Big Lots' highest-traffic retail season.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Craig for a 20-minute planning call in the next two weeks — before Q3 budgets finalize — to share TWC's retail tentpole packages and map them to Big Lots' seasonal calendar. Keep the ask low-friction: 'Just want to make sure you have our inventory options in front of you before the plan is locked.'

sales_plays.title: Holiday Gifting Lead-In Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Bloomingdale's is an active PMP customer with two consecutive won deals in 2024-2025. With holiday and gifting season approaching, now is the time to expand their investment from programmatic PMP into premium direct placements and on-property sponsorships before Q4 inventory tightens.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins (Jan 2025 and Jul 2024) with Frank Berman or Martha Tiller: 'You've run with us twice now through PMP — we want to show you what the brands that go bigger in Q4 are doing differently this year before the holiday inventory window closes.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'For this holiday and gifting season, is Bloomingdale's planning to lead with fashion, beauty, or home goods — and is the goal driving in-store traffic, digital conversion, or both?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a move from PMP-only to a direct premium package: Weather-triggered creative tied to first cold snap drives urgency for gifting and outerwear — a proven format that PMP alone cannot deliver with the same contextual precision.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Bloomingdale's has $15.6M in digital spend and $6.0M in Mobile App alone. Even a 5% reallocation from their broader digital mix toward a premium direct holiday package represents a $780K opportunity — well above the $200K Black Friday Takeover they passed on in Q4 2023.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar before end of [current month] to walk through a holiday gifting proposal built around your top categories? I want to make sure you have first right of refusal on the premium placements before they're gone.'

sales_plays.title: Reclaim the Black Friday Takeover
sales_plays.summary: Bloomingdale's passed on a $200K Black Friday Weekend Takeover in Q4 2023 and a $50K Holiday Campaign — budget that likely went to a competing platform. With Bombora showing active Retail Technology intent this week, a competitor is likely in conversation. Get in now to displace that spend before Q4 commitments are locked.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the lost opportunity directly: 'In Q4 2023, we had a $200K Black Friday Takeover on the table for Bloomingdale's that didn't close. I'd like to understand what went to another platform instead — and show you what that package looks like in 2025 with weather-triggered and contextual layers added.'
Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Which platform captured your Black Friday and holiday budget in 2023 and 2024, and what did performance look like against your in-store and digital sales goals?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Bombora signals Bloomingdale's is actively researching Retail Technology — likely evaluating new or existing ad tech partners. Position TWC's weather-triggered, high-intent holiday audience as a displacement of a lower-context competitor: 'Whoever has that budget right now can't connect a cold front in the Northeast to a Bloomingdale's coat or gift purchase the way we can.'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Bloomingdale's spends $54.4M in Search and $4.7M in Paid Social — platforms optimized for lower-funnel. The $200K Black Friday Takeover represents less than 0.4% of their total digital investment, but delivers upper-funnel contextual reach at a moment of peak consumer intent that search and social cannot replicate.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to send you a one-page comparison of what the 2023 Black Friday package looked like versus what we're offering in 2025 — can I get that to you and Frank Berman this week, then schedule a 20-minute call to walk through it before Q3 planning closes?'

steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Courtney McSherry referencing the back-to-back PMP wins in July 2024 and February 2025. Acknowledge the consistency and ask for 20 minutes to share performance benchmarks comparing PMP vs. direct-sold TWC inventory for CPG pet brands.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Courtney: 'Of the $62.9M in digital you're managing, how much is currently allocated to premium reserved placements versus open-market or PMP buys — and is there a mandate to shift that mix heading into H2?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct-sold weather-triggered inventory as the natural next step beyond PMP — same audience, but with guaranteed share-of-voice, first-look creative placements, and weather-moment targeting that aligns Blue Buffalo's 'real ingredients' messaging to high-intent pet owner moments (e.g., cold-weather feeding behavior, seasonal pet health spikes).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The two PMP deals closed at nominal $1 values — likely test or remnant buys. Even a modest direct-sold commitment of $200K–$500K (comparable to the $200K Q3 2023 Feeding Campaign opp) would represent a 200x+ step-up from current TWC investment and capture a measurable share of the $62.9M digital budget.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social with Weather-Contextual Video
sales_plays.summary: Blue Buffalo is spending $35.7M on Paid Social — the single largest digital line item — with only $1.9M in Online Video. TWC's premium video and CTV environment offers a brand-safe, contextually relevant alternative that can capture pet owner attention during high-engagement weather moments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference Blue Buffalo's $35.7M Paid Social spend and the current Bombora Education intent signal. Open with: 'We're seeing Blue Buffalo actively researching content and education topics this week — that maps well to something we're seeing across CPG pet brands shifting video dollars away from social feeds into contextual environments. I'd love to share what's working.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Courtney: 'How are you currently measuring brand-safe reach and viewability on your Paid Social buys — and is there pressure from General Mills to demonstrate upper-funnel brand equity metrics beyond social engagement?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's Online Video and CTV inventory ($19M CTV comp nationally) as a displacement vehicle for a portion of the $35.7M Paid Social budget. Blue Buffalo's brand story — founded after a dog's cancer diagnosis, real meat first — resonates in lean-back, high-attention video environments far more than a scrolled social feed. Weather-triggered video (e.g., cold snap = 'keep your pet warm and well-fed' messaging) delivers contextual relevance Paid Social cannot replicate.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Shifting even 5% of the $35.7M Paid Social budget ($1.785M) into TWC video/CTV would nearly double Blue Buffalo's current $1.9M Online Video spend and represent a meaningful new revenue line for TWC — while giving Courtney a differentiated channel story for internal reporting.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send Courtney a one-page CPG pet brand case study showing video completion rates and brand lift in weather-contextual environments by end of this week, then schedule a follow-up with Karen Bloore Hunt at Horizon to discuss Q3 budget reallocation before the upfront planning window closes.

talk_track_opener: Hey Courtney, I'm [rep name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working with Blue Buffalo for the past couple of years and I wanted to personally follow up on the momentum we've built together. We've had two successful PMP campaigns run through Horizon, and I think we're just scratching the surface of what's possible given the scale of your digital investment. I work with a number of CPG pet brands at your tier and I've seen real results when teams move beyond programmatic into more intentional, weather-contextual placements. With Blue Buffalo's brand story — real ingredients, no fillers, founded on a dog's health — the contextual alignment with weather moments is genuinely compelling, not just a media tactic. I also noticed your team has been active in education-related content research this week, which tells me there may be appetite to explore new formats or audiences. Given that you're managing over $62M in digital and $35M in Paid Social alone, I'd love to ask: how are you thinking about channel mix heading into H2, and is there a conversation happening internally about shifting any of that toward premium video or reserved placements? I'd love to bring Karen at Horizon into a quick planning call before Q2 closes.

Are there specific retail tentpole moments — like National Pet Month in May or holiday gifting in Q4 — where Blue Buffalo has dedicated campaign budgets that haven't been mapped to a weather-contextual strategy yet?

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's weather-triggered video inventory reaches consumers at the exact moment home environment becomes top of mind — a cold front, a heat wave, a rainy weekend. Bob's core categories (living room, bedroom, dining) are purchased when people are spending time at home. Paid Social interrupts; TWC's weather-moment video aligns. Position a $1M–$2M shift from Paid Social into TWC Online Video as a test-and-learn displacement with clear attribution.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Bob's spends $8.6M on Online Video today. Adding $1M–$2M in TWC weather-triggered video represents a 12–23% expansion of that line item while displacing equivalent Paid Social spend. Given TWC's audience skews toward homeowners and household decision-makers — the exact buyer for Bob's $58M broadcast-supported brand — the contextual alignment justifies a premium CPM over social inventory.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive analysis deliverable: 'Let me put together a one-page CPM and audience-quality comparison between Bob's current Paid Social buys and what a TWC weather-moment video package would look like at the same budget. I can have that to you and Gene Turner at Horizon Next by end of next week — does that work?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Stephen — I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Bob's through Horizon Next on a couple of PMP programs, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think we're only scratching the surface of what's possible given the scale of what Bob's is doing in digital and CTV. You're running $61.3M in digital and $40.6M in CTV — that's a serious investment, and I want to make sure TWC is earning a bigger share of it in the right way. Here's what caught my attention: Bob's is showing active intent signals around Education content this week, which historically correlates with the back-to-school and apartment-setup furniture cycle. At the same time, I know the $50K Winter MAIM package we proposed last December didn't make it across the finish line, and I'd love to understand what stood in the way — because the weather-moment case for Bob's core categories is genuinely strong. Living room, bedroom, dining room furniture — these are purchases people make when they're home, and weather drives that. Can I ask: when Horizon Next is planning Bob's seasonal tentpole windows, how much of the digital budget is being allocated against weather or contextual triggers versus flat always-on placements?
open_questions: When the $50K Winter MAIM package was declined in December 2024, was that a budget timing issue, a competing priority, or a performance concern about the unit itself — and has anything changed in how Bob's is thinking about seasonal direct buys for 2025?
Stephen, who at Bob's owns the decision to shift budget between Paid Social and Online Video — is that a CMO-level call or does it run through Horizon Next's planning team with Gene Turner?
Of Bob's $12.6M Mobile App spend, how much is currently tied to weather or contextual triggers versus retargeting and loyalty-based placements?
Is Bob's planning any campaign activity around back-to-school or fall home-refresh tentpoles this year, and has Horizon Next already locked those budgets or is there still allocation flexibility?

The two PMP wins have been at nominal value — is there a performance threshold or attribution model Bob's would need to see proven before committing to a direct, guaranteed buy with TWC at a higher investment level?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Boehringer Ingelheim has three consecutive PMP wins (2024, 2025, 2026) at nominal $1 deal values, signaling programmatic access without meaningful investment. With $178.6M in digital spend and $29.2M in CTV, there is clear budget capacity to convert this PMP relationship into a premium direct buy across Weather Channel inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three-year PMP streak — 'You've been activating our inventory programmatically since mid-2023. I want to show Christina Chieffalo and Todd Sussman what that same audience looks like when accessed through a direct, guaranteed placement with full creative control and brand-safe context.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Of the $178.6M Boehringer Ingelheim allocates to digital, how much is currently running in guaranteed direct deals versus open or private marketplace — and is there a performance gap between the two?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct premium units against the PMP's limitations — no guaranteed impressions, no creative sequencing, no contextual alignment. Boehringer's pharma brands (Jardiance, Spiriva, etc.) require brand-safe, condition-relevant environments that programmatic cannot guarantee.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'The lost Jardiance Q1 2025 opportunity was $100,000. A direct premium package at even 10% of Boehringer's $29.2M CTV budget would represent $2.9M in incremental spend — with measurable reach guarantees the PMP cannot deliver.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we get 30 minutes with Christina and Todd Sussman at FCB New York before the next planning cycle to walk through a direct vs. PMP performance comparison using your existing TWC activation data? I can have a proposal ready within the week.'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social & Display with Contextual Video
sales_plays.summary: Boehringer Ingelheim allocates only $1.7M to Paid Social and $8.0M to Internet Display — relatively thin lines within a $178.6M digital budget. These channels face increasing scrutiny in pharma for brand safety and targeting limitations. This play routes through Broadhead (digital AOR) to redirect a portion of that spend toward TWC's contextual online video and mobile app inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Broadhead, the digital media buying AOR, with a specific data point: 'Boehringer currently has $8.0M in display and $1.7M in paid social — channels that pharma compliance teams are increasingly flagging for adjacency risk. I want to show you a contextual alternative that maps condition states to weather triggers, which is directly relevant to respiratory and cardiovascular brands.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When Broadhead evaluates display and paid social performance for Boehringer's brands, what is the primary KPI — and are there compliance or brand-safety constraints that limit where those dollars can actually run?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's mobile app ($16.0M already in Boehringer's mobile line shows comfort with the format) and online video ($18.6M) inventory offers pharma-compliant, contextually relevant placements tied to weather conditions that trigger symptoms — cold, humidity, temperature swings — directly relevant to Jardiance, Spiriva, and Dulera.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'If Broadhead reallocated even 25% of the $9.7M combined display and paid social budget to TWC contextual video and mobile, that's roughly $2.4M in new direct spend — replacing lower-accountability inventory with condition-triggered, measurable placements.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to send Broadhead's digital team a one-page contextual targeting brief specific to Boehringer's respiratory and cardiovascular portfolio. Can we set a 20-minute call with the digital planning lead at Broadhead this week to confirm the right brand priorities before I build it out?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Todd, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel. We've been working with Boehringer Ingelheim for a few years now — you've got active PMP agreements running through 2026, and Broadhead has been part of that digital buying relationship. We know the account, we know the brands, and we've earned a seat at the table. What I'm noticing is that Boehringer is running $178.6M in digital with $18.6M already in online video and $16.0M in mobile — but the TWC relationship is still sitting at the programmatic level. Given that respiratory and cardiovascular conditions are directly tied to weather patterns, and that your display and paid social lines are relatively small at $8.0M and $1.7M respectively, I think there's a real conversation to have about upgrading the PMP to a direct premium buy and redirecting some of that display spend into contextual video. Would it make sense to get 30 minutes on the calendar with Christina Chieffalo and the Broadhead digital team to walk through what that looks like?
open_questions: The PMP agreements have been active for three consecutive years — is the programmatic path a deliberate strategy from Broadhead, or is it simply the path of least resistance that no one has challenged?
Who at Boehringer — Christina Chieffalo or someone else on the brand team — owns the decision to move budget from programmatic to direct guaranteed placements?
The Jardiance Q1 2025 deal was lost at $100,000 — what was the deciding factor, and is that campaign need still active under a different budget line or brand team?

Broadhead holds digital media buying for Boehringer — are they also setting the KPIs for display and paid social, or does Christina Chieffalo's payor access marketing team set performance benchmarks independently?

With $8.0M in display and $1.7M in paid social, is Boehringer seeing the brand-safety and compliance scrutiny that other pharma advertisers are experiencing on those channels, and has that created any internal pressure to shift formats?

sales_plays.title: Shift CTV Dollars to Premium Direct Inventory
sales_plays.summary: Boll & Branch is running $6.2M in CTV and $1.2M in Online Video — likely through programmatic pipes. This play positions a direct, brand-safe premium environment as a higher-quality alternative that matches their luxury positioning and reduces waste.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Kristen Deyko referencing Boll & Branch's $6.2M CTV investment and note that programmatic CTV often places luxury brands alongside non-premium content — a brand-safety risk for a brand built on trust and craftsmanship. Attach one concrete example of a premium direct placement that a comparable luxury home goods brand ran.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Kristen: 'Where is the majority of your $6.2M CTV buy currently running — direct deals or programmatic exchanges — and how are you measuring brand-safe adjacency for Boll & Branch's creative?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our premium, editorial-adjacent CTV and Online Video inventory as a direct replacement for the programmatic slice of their $6.2M CTV + $1.2M Online Video budget — environments where the soft-texture, sustainability story lands with an audience already in a premium mindset.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Even redirecting 15% of their $6.2M CTV spend (~$930K) into a direct premium buy would represent a meaningful first test. Benchmark against their $209K Display and $250K Mobile App lines to show this is a proportionate, low-risk reallocation.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 20-minute call with Kristen Deyko in the next two weeks to walk through a custom audience overlap analysis showing how our inventory reaches their luxury bedding buyer — no commitment, just data.

sales_plays.title: Ivan Ristic Agency Bridge — PR-to-Paid Media Referral
sales_plays.summary: Diffusion PR is Boll & Branch's AOR for public relations. Ivan Ristic as President and Co-Founder is a senior relationship entry point who can bridge earned media strategy to paid media planning and warm-introduce us to the internal media decision-maker.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Ivan Ristic at Diffusion PR directly, acknowledging Diffusion's PR AOR role for Boll & Branch. Reference Boll & Branch's active Bombora intent signal around 'Marketing' this week as a timely reason to connect — the brand is clearly in a planning mode and PR and paid media strategies are converging.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Ivan: 'As you're shaping Boll & Branch's earned media narrative around sustainability and premium craftsmanship, do you have visibility into how their $6.2M CTV and $3.7M digital budget is being allocated — and is there a media agency or internal buyer we should be coordinating with to make the paid environment match the PR story?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Frame the conversation around brand coherence — Diffusion is building a premium PR story for Boll & Branch, and fragmented programmatic placements can undercut that narrative. A direct premium media partnership reinforces the same brand equity Ivan's team is building in earned channels.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Point to Boll & Branch's combined $6.2M CTV + $716K Paid Social + $1.2M Online Video as the paid ecosystem where brand story consistency matters most. A referral introduction from Ivan to Kristen Deyko or the internal media lead could unlock a conversation about aligning even a portion of that spend.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Ivan for a 15-minute call this week to explore whether there is a joint agency-client briefing opportunity — position it as additive to Diffusion's scope, not competitive. Offer to bring a one-page brand-safe media brief tailored to Boll & Branch's luxury positioning as a conversation starter.

talk_track_opener: Hey Kristen, I work with premium media partners helping luxury retail brands make sure their paid environments actually match the brand story they've built. We work with brands in the home and lifestyle space where the visual and editorial context of where an ad runs is just as important as the creative itself. I wanted to reach out because I know Boll & Branch has built something really differentiated around organic materials and craftsmanship, and that story deserves to live in the right environments. Looking at your media footprint — $6.2M in CTV and another $1.2M in Online Video is a serious investment — and I'm curious whether the placements you're getting through your current buys are consistently reflecting the premium, trust-first brand Boll & Branch has built. Would it be worth 20 minutes to look at where that inventory is actually running and whether there's a smarter allocation that protects the brand while improving performance?
open_questions: Is your $6.2M CTV budget being purchased through a programmatic DSP, a media agency, or direct publisher deals — and who owns that buying decision internally?
How does Boll & Branch evaluate brand-safe adjacency for CTV and Online Video placements given the premium positioning of the brand?
Is Diffusion PR's scope limited to earned media, or are they involved in advising on paid media strategy and channel mix as well?
With $6.8M in Search and $716K in Paid Social already running, is there a defined upper-funnel awareness strategy that your CTV and Online Video spend is meant to support?
As you plan for retail tentpole moments — gifting seasons, cultural moments — how far in advance is Boll & Branch locking in media commitments, and who leads that planning process?

sales_plays.summary: BRP is an active customer with a won Can-Am Off-Road deal ($260,671) and a PMP deal in place, but Ski-Doo, Sea-Doo, Lynx, and Marine brands appear underrepresented in the TWC/Weather Channel environment. This play expands the active Can-Am relationship into adjacent BRP brand lines.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the active Can-Am Off-Road FY27 1H26 win ($260,671) and the live PMP deal with Lauren Lytle at Cramer-Krasselt. Open by saying: 'Lauren, the Can-Am Off-Road campaign is live and performing — I want to show you how we can extend that same contextual targeting logic to Ski-Doo and Sea-Doo before their respective demand windows open.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Lauren Lytle: 'Is Cramer-Krasselt managing media planning for Ski-Doo and Sea-Doo under the same AOR Social scope, or are those brands planned separately — and who owns the digital budget for those lines?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): BRP's digital spend is heavily concentrated in Paid Social ($28.8M of $31.6M digital). TWC's contextual weather and outdoor intent environment offers a premium, brand-safe complement to social — reaching powersports consumers in high-intent planning moments that social feeds miss.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): BRP's trailing 12-month spend is $33.8M, yet current TWC investment is under $320K across won deals. Even a 1% reallocation from the $28.8M Paid Social budget equals $288K in incremental TWC spend — a realistic expansion target given the existing PMP infrastructure already in place.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Lauren to schedule a 30-minute brand-by-brand planning session in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar before end of February to map Ski-Doo and Sea-Doo seasonal windows against our audience data — I'll bring a draft proposal so you have something concrete to react to?'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Overweight with Premium Digital
sales_plays.summary: BRP allocates 85% of its $33.8M media budget to Paid Social ($28.8M), leaving display, video, and CTV dramatically underfunded relative to industry norms. This play challenges the social overconcentration by positioning TWC's CTV ($1.3M already in market) and digital environments as higher-quality, less-saturated alternatives for the Can-Am and Ski-Doo audiences.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a data observation to Julie Tourville: 'Julie, looking at BRP's media mix, $28.8M — roughly 85 cents of every digital dollar — is going into Paid Social. That's a significant concentration risk, especially as social CPMs rise and algorithm reach declines. I'd love to share what we're seeing from comparable powersports advertisers on TWC.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Julie: 'When you evaluate the Can-Am Off-Road campaigns, are you seeing diminishing returns on Paid Social reach at current spend levels — and is there internal pressure to diversify the digital mix heading into FY27?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's CTV and contextual digital inventory reaches outdoor, adventure, and weather-aware audiences — the exact consumer profile buying Can-Am, Ski-Doo, and Sea-Doo products. Unlike social, TWC placements are brand-safe, non-skippable in CTV, and tied to high-intent contextual moments (weather, trail conditions, boating season).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): BRP already spends $1.3M in CTV and has a live PMP. The two recent lost opportunities — On Road FY27 Q126 ($55,251) and Ski-Doo Display PG ($50,000) — represent $105K in recoverable revenue. Winning those back plus a modest shift of 2% of the $28.8M Paid Social budget ($576K) would more than triple BRP's current TWC footprint.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer Julie a competitive media mix analysis: 'I can put together a one-page view showing how BRP's digital allocation compares to two or three powersports competitors on our platform — would that be useful to have before your next agency review with Cramer-Krasselt? I can have it ready within a week.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Lauren, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working together on the Can-Am Off-Road FY27 campaign and the PMP that went live at the start of the year. I appreciate the partnership and want to make sure we're delivering value across the full BRP portfolio, not just Can-Am. I work with a number of powersports and outdoor recreation advertisers and I know how competitive the planning calendar gets this time of year. Looking at BRP's overall media footprint — $33.8M in trailing spend, with the vast majority sitting in Paid Social — I think there's a real opportunity to extend what's working on Can-Am into Ski-Doo and Sea-Doo before their peak demand windows. I'd love to ask you one quick question: is Cramer-Krasselt planning Ski-Doo and Sea-Doo media under the same scope, or are those brands handled separately? That would help me figure out the right conversation to bring you.
open_questions: The Can-Am Off-Road FY27 deal is live — is Cramer-Krasselt also managing digital media planning for Ski-Doo and Sea-Doo, or do those brands have separate agency relationships or in-house planning teams?

The Ski-Doo Display PG opportunity was lost in January — was that a budget timing issue, a targeting concern, or a competitive decision? Understanding that would help me bring the right solution back.
BRP's digital mix is heavily weighted toward Paid Social at $28.8M — is that a deliberate brand strategy or something the team is actively looking to rebalance heading into FY27 planning?
Julie Tourville owns Can-Am Off-Road and Marine globally — who is the equivalent marketing decision-maker for Ski-Doo and Lynx, and is that a conversation we should be having in parallel?
The PMP deal is in place — is Cramer-Krasselt using it actively for Can-Am buys, and is there appetite to extend that same PMP framework to cover other BRP brand lines without separate negotiations?

sales_plays.title: Displace Taboola with TWC Premium Content Discovery
sales_plays.summary: Taboola is currently named as Bombas' digital partner for content discovery. Given Bombas' $1.9M Internet Display and $4.5M Mobile App budgets, and their Bombora intent signal around Search Marketing this week, there is a clear opening to position TWC's premium contextual and content environments as a higher-quality, brand-safe alternative to Taboola's network inventory.

Note that $43.8M of their $47.1M spend sits in Paid Social, which leaves almost zero diversification buffer if platform costs rise or reach plateaus.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Krissie: 'When you look at your Paid Social performance today, where are you seeing diminishing returns on incremental spend — and has the team modeled what a 10% reallocation into premium direct inventory would do to reach and frequency curves?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our direct inventory as the channel that reaches the upper-funnel men's lifestyle audience Bonobos needs to grow beyond its existing retargeting pool — audiences that Paid Social algorithms are not surfacing because they have never clicked a Bonobos ad.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 5% shift from the $43.8M Paid Social budget equals $2.19M in redirected spend. Frame the conversation around what incremental reach and brand-safe contextual placements that $2.19M could buy versus the same dollars in a saturated social auction.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute working session with Krissie and, if appropriate, Seth Gaffney at Preacher to walk through a channel-mix audit — propose a specific date within the next two weeks before Q3 planning locks.

sales_plays.title: Preacher Agency Relationship Path
sales_plays.summary: Preacher holds the Creative scope for Bonobos. Seth Gaffney as Founder and Chief Strategy Officer is a senior relationship entry point who can open the door to Krissie Millan and influence media strategy conversations from the agency side.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Seth Gaffney at Preacher directly — not through a cold Bonobos pitch. Reference Preacher's creative work for Bonobos and acknowledge the brand's evolution into a full men's closet solution spanning 40+ curated brands. Ask Seth if he has a few minutes to share how Preacher is thinking about the media environment supporting the creative work they are producing.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Seth: 'As you are building creative for Bonobos, are you finding that the current media mix — which is heavily weighted toward Paid Social at roughly $43.8M — is giving the creative the right environments to perform, or are there placements you wish the brand was in that the current plan does not include?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our inventory as the premium contextual environment that makes Preacher's creative work harder — placements where the Bonobos brand story and the 40+ curated brand assortment can be told in a high-attention, brand-safe context that Paid Social feed units cannot match.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Reference that Bonobos is already investing $634K in Online Video and $768K in CTV — proof the brand is willing to invest in premium formats. Frame the ask as helping Seth make the case internally to Krissie Millan for expanding those premium line items meaningfully.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Seth for a warm introduction to Krissie Millan or an invitation to join a Preacher-Bonobos planning conversation — offer to bring a tailored audience and channel-mix brief to that meeting so it adds value to Preacher's own strategic recommendation.

talk_track_opener: Hey Krissie, I work with premium media brands that help men's lifestyle and apparel advertisers reach audiences they are not finding inside their existing paid social spend. We work with several retailers at a similar scale and brand positioning to Bonobos, and I have seen firsthand what happens when a brand with real breadth — 40-plus curated labels, a full closet solution — is almost entirely dependent on one channel for $43.8M of its $47M budget. The exposure is real, and so is the opportunity cost. I noticed Bonobos is already testing Online Video at $634K and CTV at $768K, which tells me the team is open to premium formats — those line items just have not scaled yet relative to the social concentration. I would love to spend 20 minutes understanding where your current Paid Social performance is plateauing and whether there is appetite to model what a modest reallocation into premium direct inventory could do for incremental reach before your next planning cycle locks. Would a quick call in the next two weeks work?
open_questions: Krissie, with $43.8M concentrated in Paid Social, where are you seeing the clearest signs of diminishing returns — is it CPM inflation, frequency caps, or audience saturation on your core men's lifestyle segments?
Who owns the media planning and buying decisions at Bonobos — is that managed in-house alongside Preacher, or is there a separate media agency we should be aware of?

As Bonobos has expanded to 40-plus curated brands, has the media strategy evolved to reflect that broader assortment story, or is the plan still primarily optimized around the original private label performance funnel?

The CTV and Online Video line items are relatively small at $768K and $634K respectively — is that a deliberate test-and-learn posture, or has the team not yet found the right partner or format to justify scaling those budgets?

With retail tentpole moments like back-to-school, fall fashion, and holiday coming up, at what point in the year does Bonobos' media plan typically lock — and is Seth Gaffney at Preacher involved in those planning conversations?

sales_plays.title: Displace the Display Drag
sales_plays.summary: Bose is running $2.7M in Internet Display — a historically low-engagement format — while investing heavily in Paid Social ($42.5M) and CTV ($15.1M). This play targets the underperforming display budget as a displacement opportunity, repositioning those dollars into higher-impact, on-property inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Jim Mollica referencing Bose's $2.7M Internet Display allocation and the Bombora signal showing active interest in Retail Technology this week. Frame it as a timely conversation: 'You're clearly thinking about retail-connected media — I want to show you where display dollars are leaking versus where they could be working harder.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Of the $2.7M currently running in Internet Display, what performance benchmarks is VaynerMedia using to justify that allocation — CTR, view-through, or something downstream like in-store lift?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position on-property, contextually relevant inventory against the low-engagement display stack. Bose's audience is actively in a purchase mindset on our platform; generic display is reaching them out of context.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Even a partial shift of the $2.7M display budget to on-property inventory — combined with Bose's existing $15.1M CTV and $12.3M Mobile App spend — creates a cross-screen sequence that closes the gap between awareness and conversion for premium audio buyers.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jim or route through Gary Vaynerchuk at VaynerMedia for a 20-minute working session before end of quarter to map current display placements against our audience overlap data — low commitment, high signal.

sales_plays.title: GaryVee Gateway
sales_plays.summary: Gary Vaynerchuk at VaynerMedia is Bose's AOR Creative lead and a high-profile industry voice. A relationship-first approach through Gary — before going direct to Jim Mollica — can unlock a warm introduction and position this as a strategic partnership rather than a cold vendor pitch.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Gary Vaynerchuk at VaynerMedia directly, referencing Bose's $42.5M Paid Social spend under VaynerMedia's creative stewardship. Acknowledge the scale of what they're managing and open with a peer-level observation: 'With $42.5M in Paid Social for Bose, you're already winning on social — I want to show you where we can extend that story into a channel VaynerMedia doesn't currently own for them.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'As AOR Creative for Bose, where does VaynerMedia see the biggest gap in the current media mix — is it upper-funnel reach, mid-funnel engagement, or closing the loop at retail tentpole moments like holiday or back-to-school?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our platform as the logical extension of VaynerMedia's Paid Social creative investment — a place where Bose's existing creative assets can be deployed in a high-intent, contextually relevant environment that VaynerMedia can claim as incremental value for their client.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Bose's total digital spend is $69.7M, with $42.5M already flowing through VaynerMedia's creative lens. Adding even a modest on-property activation — framed as a retail-moment play tied to Bose's Retail Technology intent signal — gives VaynerMedia a measurable new proof point for Jim Mollica.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Gary for a 15-minute call this week to share a one-page audience overlap brief built specifically around Bose's buyer profile — offer to co-present to Jim Mollica if the data is compelling. Time-bound: before the next quarterly planning cycle kicks off.

talk_track_opener: Hey Jim — I lead partnerships at [Media Co] and we work with a number of premium audio and consumer electronics brands navigating the same challenge you're facing: a large, fragmented digital footprint that's hard to optimize across. With $69.7M in digital spend and VaynerMedia running your creative, you're clearly not short on firepower — but I noticed something worth a quick conversation. Your Paid Social allocation is $42.5M, which tells me you're investing heavily in audience-first, creative-led media. At the same time, Bombora is showing Bose actively researching Retail Technology this week — which suggests your team is already thinking about where the next phase of media investment goes, especially heading into retail tentpole season. I'd love to spend 20 minutes showing you where brands at your spend level are finding incremental reach that their current mix isn't covering. Would a quick call this week or next make sense?
open_questions: How is VaynerMedia currently measuring the performance of Bose's $2.7M Internet Display spend — and what would need to be true for that budget to be reallocated?
Who at Bose owns the final sign-off on net-new media partnerships — does Jim Mollica approve directly, or does EssenceMediaCom Canada hold the media planning authority for new channels?
With $42.5M in Paid Social already running through VaynerMedia, is there appetite to test a new on-property channel that VaynerMedia could co-own and report on as incremental reach?

Given the active Retail Technology intent signal, is Bose currently in planning mode for a specific retail tentpole — holiday, back-to-school, or a product launch — where a new media partner could be piloted?
Bose's CTV investment is $15.1M but Online Video is only $1.7M — is that gap intentional, or is there a brief we could respond to that addresses cross-screen video sequencing for the audio category?

sales_plays.title: Upgrade PMP to Full Premium Stack
sales_plays.summary: Boston Scientific has two consecutive PMP wins (July 2024 and January 2025) but zero investment in CTV, Broadcast, or premium digital beyond the Interactive PMP. With $5.8M in digital spend and $2.8M already in CTV elsewhere, there is clear budget capacity and a logical upsell path to a broader premium package.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins — 'We've now delivered on the Interactive PMP twice, July 2024 and again January 2025. Mary Beth, I want to show you what a broader activation looks like given the scale of your $5.8M digital footprint and the $2.8M you're already putting into CTV.'
Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Across your current CTV and digital buys, are those campaigns unified under a single audience strategy, or are they running in separate silos by device or channel?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position the PMP relationship as the trust foundation — 'The PMP has proven the data match works for Boston Scientific. The next step is extending that same verified audience into CTV and premium video, where your $2.8M is already spending but without the targeting precision you've seen in the Interactive environment.'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Your current digital display and online video together represent $2.4M. A unified PMP extension into CTV and premium video could consolidate that reach under one deduplicated audience, reducing wasted impressions across the $2.8M CTV line and the $1.2M online video line simultaneously.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar before end of February — you, me, and ideally John Tuttle given his technology and facilities oversight — to walk through a cross-channel audience proposal built on the existing PMP foundation?'

sales_plays.title: Entertainment Intent Window — Patient Engagement Creative Push
sales_plays.summary: Bombora signals Boston Scientific is actively researching Entertainment content this week. For a medical device brand with $600K in Paid Social and $856K in Mobile App spend, this intent likely reflects interest in entertainment-adjacent content formats or patient engagement platforms — a timely hook to introduce premium video and mobile inventory before a potential campaign build-out.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the live signal — 'Our Bombora data flagged Boston Scientific surging on Entertainment topics this week. That's an unusual signal for a medical device brand, and it usually means someone internally is evaluating entertainment-format content or patient-facing engagement platforms. I wanted to reach out before that evaluation closes.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Is there a specific initiative — patient education, HCP engagement, or a consumer awareness push — that's driving the interest in entertainment-style content or streaming environments right now?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): 'If the goal is reaching patients or caregivers in a lean-back, high-attention environment, our CTV and premium online video inventory sits exactly at that intersection — and given your existing $2.8M in CTV spend, we already know that format is in the mix for Boston Scientific.'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Your $856K in Mobile App and $1.2M in Online Video today are running in fragmented environments. Shifting even a portion of that into a premium CTV or streaming context — where completion rates run 20-30 points higher than standard mobile video — materially improves the return on those existing line items without increasing total spend.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'The Entertainment intent signal is live right now, which means the internal conversation is happening this week. Can Mary Beth or someone on her team take a 20-minute call by Friday so I can share a format recommendation before the planning window closes?'

talk_track_opener: Mary Beth, I'm reaching out from [Your Company] — we've been working with Boston Scientific through two consecutive Interactive PMP campaigns, most recently closed in January 2025, so this isn't a cold call. We have a proven data relationship and a track record of delivery with your team. I wanted to connect directly with you given your role overseeing market access and marketing strategy, because what I'm seeing in your current media mix suggests there's a meaningful gap we can close together. Here's what caught my attention: Boston Scientific is running $2.8M in CTV and $5.8M across digital channels, but those investments appear to be operating independently of the PMP infrastructure we've already validated together. On top of that, our Bombora data flagged Boston Scientific surging on Entertainment topics this week — which for a medical device brand usually signals someone is actively evaluating new content formats or patient engagement environments. I'd love to ask you one question: are your CTV and digital campaigns currently sharing a unified audience strategy, or are they planned separately by channel? That answer would tell me exactly where the biggest efficiency gain is for you.
open_questions: The Interactive PMP has run twice now — is Mary Beth's team evaluating whether to expand that framework into CTV or video, or is the PMP currently treated as a standalone tactic separate from the broader media plan?
Who owns the CTV and digital planning decisions day-to-day — is that sitting with Mary Beth's marketing team directly, or is there an agency partner we should be looping in alongside John Tuttle?
The Bombora data shows Boston Scientific surging on Entertainment topics this week — is there a specific campaign or patient engagement initiative internally that's driving that research right now?
Across the $5.8M in digital spend, are the Paid Social, Display, Mobile App, and Online Video lines planned against a shared audience definition, or are they optimized independently by format?
Given the $2.8M already in CTV, what does success look like for that channel today — is the team measuring it on reach, completion rate, script lift, or something else tied to patient outcomes?

sales_plays.title: Search-Heavy Spend Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Bouqs is allocating $25.1M of their $26M total budget to Search, leaving almost no investment in premium direct inventory. This play challenges the over-reliance on Search and positions on-property direct buys as a way to capture demand earlier in the funnel and reduce CPL dependency on Google.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email or call Wendy Oliff referencing the public spend skew: 'Wendy, I noticed Bouqs is investing heavily in Search — roughly $25M of your ~$26M tracked media budget. That level of Search concentration usually signals strong intent capture downstream, but it can leave a brand invisible at the top of the funnel where gifting decisions actually start. I wanted to share how a few comparable floral and DTC brands have rebalanced that mix.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Of your current $25M+ in Search spend, how much is going to branded vs. non-branded terms — and are you seeing diminishing returns on non-branded gifting keywords like 'flower delivery' or 'anniversary flowers'?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position premium on-property display, online video, and CTV inventory as upper-funnel demand creation that feeds the Search funnel more efficiently — reducing cost-per-click on competitive non-branded terms by building brand familiarity before the search query happens.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'If even 2% of your $25.1M Search budget — roughly $500K — were shifted to on-property video and display, and it reduced your blended non-branded CPC by 10-15%, the math on incremental ROAS improvement is significant. We can model that against your current Search CPL benchmarks.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we schedule a 20-minute working session in the next two weeks where I bring a competitive spend analysis for the floral/gifting vertical and we pressure-test whether your current mix is leaving upper-funnel demand on the table?'

sales_plays.title: Tentpole Season Planning Window
sales_plays.summary: Bouqs's core business is driven by gifting tentpoles — Valentine's Day, Mother's Day, and seasonal moments. With $26M+ in annual spend and a retail vertical profile, engaging Wendy Oliff now, before Q4 planning locks, positions this as a strategic planning conversation rather than a reactive pitch.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Wendy Oliff with a planning-forward hook: 'Wendy, floral brands typically lock Valentine's Day and Mother's Day media plans 10-14 weeks out. Given Bouqs's scale — over $26M in tracked annual spend — I wanted to connect before your Q4/Q1 planning cycle closes to explore whether there's a fit for premium inventory around those windows.'
Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When does your team typically finalize media plans for Valentine's Day — and is that decision made in-house or through an agency partner we should loop in?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position on-property placements (CTV, online video, display) as high-visibility formats that complement Bouqs's existing $217K Paid Social and $86K Display spend during peak gifting windows, when auction prices on Search spike and efficiency drops.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'During Valentine's Day week, Search CPCs in the floral category can spike 40-60% above baseline. Brands that lock in premium direct placements ahead of that window typically see 20-30% lower effective CPM versus programmatic buys made in-flight. For a brand at your spend level, that efficiency gap is material.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd love to send you a one-page tentpole planning calendar with inventory recommendations sized to complement your current $217K Paid Social base — would that be a useful starting point before we get on a call?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Wendy, my name is [Rep Name] from [Company] — we work with a number of DTC and retail brands in the gifting and floral space, and I've been doing some homework on Bouqs ahead of reaching out. I wanted to be direct about why I'm calling rather than sending another generic email. Looking at Bouqs's publicly tracked media investment — which runs north of $26 million annually — it's clear your team is serious about performance media. The concentration in Search is notable and makes sense for a direct-response brand, but it also caught my attention as a potential opportunity. Most brands at your scale that are Search-heavy are either already exploring upper-funnel rebalancing, or they're about to feel pressure on non-branded CPCs as competitors bid up gifting keywords heading into Valentine's and Mother's Day season. I'm not here to tell you Search is wrong — I'm here to ask one question: when you look at your non-branded Search efficiency over the last 12 months, are you seeing the returns you'd expect for that level of investment, or is there a ceiling you're starting to hit? That answer usually tells us pretty quickly whether there's a conversation worth having.
open_questions: Of your $25.1M in Search spend, what percentage is going to non-branded gifting keywords versus branded Bouqs terms — and how has non-branded efficiency trended over the past two to three quarters?
With no agency roster visible externally, is all media planning and buying managed in-house under your team, or is there a performance agency or trading desk involved that we should be aware of?

When you look at your $217K in Paid Social alongside your Search investment, are those channels being used for prospecting, retargeting, or both — and how are you measuring incrementality between them?
For Valentine's Day and Mother's Day planning specifically, how far in advance does your team lock media commitments, and who else besides you is involved in approving those seasonal budgets?

Given Bouqs's farm-to-door and sustainability brand story, have you tested any premium editorial or contextual placements where that narrative can be told in more than a search headline or a social ad unit?

sales_plays.title: Standby Generator Season Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Briggs & Stratton is an active TWC customer with two won deals totaling $9,001. Their $270M total media footprint and $20.7M online video + $8.8M CTV spend signal significant budget available above current TWC investment. The standby generator category is weather-driven, and Q3-Q4 planning windows are the right moment to expand scope before storm season demand peaks.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the won Standby Generator Test in Q3-Q4 2025 ($9,000) and open with: 'Your team just ran a standby generator test with us heading into storm season — I want to show you what performance looked like and talk about scaling that before the next demand window opens.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Christine Davison: 'For the standby generator line, where does TWC sit in your media mix relative to your $20.7M online video and $8.8M CTV budgets — and is Two Rivers Marketing already planning Q3 2026 generator allocations?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered CTV and video inventory as the highest-intent environment for standby generator consideration — consumers actively checking severe weather forecasts are in the exact mindset to research backup power. The test proved the connection; now make it a committed buy.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Current TWC spend is $9,000 against a $270M total media budget — less than 0.004%. Even a modest reallocation of 1% of the $20.7M online video budget ($207K) would represent a 23x expansion of the current TWC relationship and align with the scale of their paid social ($19.9M) and display ($18.1M) investments.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute planning call with Christine Davison and Kimberly Bertrand at Two Rivers Marketing before end of current quarter to review Q3 2026 storm season inventory and present a weather-triggered CTV + video package proposal.

sales_plays.title: Displace Display & Mobile App Spend to TWC Premium Inventory
sales_plays.summary: Briggs & Stratton is running $18.1M in Internet Display and $14.2M in Mobile App spend — likely distributed across commodity programmatic networks via Two Rivers Marketing. TWC's on-property weather-contextual inventory offers a premium, brand-safe alternative with demonstrable intent alignment for outdoor power equipment and generator categories.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a data observation to Kimberly Bertrand at Two Rivers Marketing: 'You're running $18.1M in display and $14.2M in mobile app for Briggs & Stratton — I want to show you how a portion of that budget performs differently when it's running against real-time weather context rather than standard programmatic placements.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Kimberly: 'For the display and mobile app line items, what DSPs or networks are you currently buying through, and are you measuring viewability and brand-safety separately from performance metrics?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's mobile app is one of the most-used weather utilities in the U.S. A consumer checking rain forecasts before a weekend lawn project or monitoring a storm system is a high-intent Briggs & Stratton prospect. Displacing a portion of generic programmatic display with TWC on-property mobile and display units ties the ad context directly to the use-case moment.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If Two Rivers Marketing shifted even 5% of the combined $32.3M display + mobile app budget ($1.6M) to TWC on-property inventory, it would represent a 178x increase over the current $9,000 TWC relationship and would be a rounding error relative to Briggs & Stratton's $270M total spend — low risk, high differentiation.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send Kimberly Bertrand a one-page TWC mobile + display contextual targeting brief with outdoor power equipment category benchmarks by end of this week, with a follow-up call scheduled within 10 business days to align on Q2 or Q3 test budget.

talk_track_opener: Hey Christine, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we worked with your team and Two Rivers Marketing on the standby generator campaign that closed in September 2025. I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a meaningful opportunity we haven't fully explored together yet. We have deep roots in the outdoor power and generator space, and our audience data across weather-driven purchase intent is something I'd love to walk you through.
open_questions: For the standby generator campaign that ran in Q3-Q4 2025, what performance metrics did Two Rivers Marketing use to evaluate the TWC test, and did it hit the threshold to justify a larger 2026 commitment?
Is Christine Davison's electrification marketing budget managed separately from the core outdoor power equipment budget, and does Two Rivers Marketing handle media buying for both product lines?
Of the $18.1M in Internet Display and $14.2M in Mobile App spend, what percentage is currently running on contextual or weather-triggered placements versus standard programmatic audience targeting?
Who at Briggs & Stratton or Two Rivers Marketing owns the Q3 2026 storm season media planning decision, and when does that planning process typically begin?

Is Deloitte Digital managing any paid media activation for the electrification line, or is their scope limited to digital strategy and technology — and would they be a stakeholder in a TWC digital expansion conversation?

steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins ('We've been partners since mid-2024 and I want to make sure we're delivering full value for the Bulova brand, not just one slice of it') and request a brief working session with Patricia Schmoyer to review current channel mix.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Patricia: 'Of your $3.7M display and $3.4M CTV budgets, how much is currently allocated to contextually relevant, weather-aware environments — and is Mediassociates evaluating that split for 2025 planning?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's CTV and premium display inventory as a natural extension of the existing PMP — same brand-safe, high-intent audience, now reaching Bulova shoppers across the full funnel from awareness (CTV) to consideration (display) at the moment weather and lifestyle context is highest.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Bulova's current TWC PMP is booked at $1 placeholder value — even a 5% shift of the $3.7M display budget ($185K) or a test CTV line against the $3.4M CTV pool represents a meaningful incremental partnership. Frame the ask as a pilot expansion, not a full reallocation.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Patricia and Ben Kunz (CSO, Mediassociates) for a 30-minute joint call in the next two weeks to walk through a cross-channel proposal covering display and CTV — offer to bring a custom audience overlap analysis as the agenda anchor.

sales_plays.title: Retail Tentpole Planning Trigger
sales_plays.summary: Bulova's retail vertical and $17.5M spend profile make Q4 gifting season (Father's Day, Holiday, Valentine's Day) a natural planning window. With Mediassociates as AOR and We Are Social handling digital, getting into the planning conversation now — before budgets are locked — is the timing lever.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Ben Kunz at Mediassociates with a forward-looking note: 'As you and Patricia are likely entering Q3/Q4 planning for Bulova's key gifting windows, I wanted to get ahead of the conversation — our TWC audiences over-index heavily during holiday and Father's Day watch-gifting moments and I'd love to show you the data.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Ben: 'Which tentpole moments — Holiday, Valentine's Day, Father's Day — are driving the largest share of Bulova's media investment, and is the current channel mix (including the $1.6M paid social and $2.3M search) being re-evaluated for those windows?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive as the planning-stage partner that adds weather and seasonal context to Bulova's gifting campaigns — connecting the brand to consumers in the mindset of occasion-driven purchasing, layered on top of the existing PMP foundation.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Bulova spends $353K in mobile app and $78K in online video today — both underdeveloped relative to the $3.7M display and $3.4M CTV lines. A tentpole-specific video or mobile package tied to a gifting window is a low-risk, incremental test with clear attribution potential.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer Ben Kunz a gifting-season audience insight deck (TWC data on watch-category intent during Holiday and Father's Day) within one week, with a follow-up call scheduled before end of current quarter to align on any tentpole line items before Mediassociates finalizes buys.

talk_track_opener: Hey Patricia — I'm [rep name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Bulova for the past year through our PMP partnership, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think we're only scratching the surface of what we could be doing together. Your team is running a sophisticated, multi-channel program — $3.4M in CTV, $3.7M in display, $1.6M in paid social — and right now our relationship is really just one piece of that puzzle. What I'm seeing is that Bulova's core gifting moments — Holiday, Father's Day, Valentine's Day — are exactly where TWC's contextual and weather-driven audiences perform best for luxury retail. I'd love to ask you one question: as you and Mediassociates head into the next planning cycle, is the CTV and premium display allocation being revisited, and would it be worth 30 minutes to look at whether TWC can play a bigger role across those channels?
open_questions: The current PMP with TWC has been renewed twice — what's driving the decision to keep it as a standalone line rather than integrating it into a broader display or CTV strategy with us?
Who at Mediassociates or on Patricia's team owns the CTV planning decision for Bulova, and is that evaluated separately from the display and paid social budgets?
Which gifting tentpoles — Holiday, Father's Day, Valentine's Day — represent the largest share of Bulova's annual media investment, and how far in advance is Mediassociates locking those buys?
We Are Social handles Bulova's digital scope — is there a coordination point between We Are Social and Mediassociates on paid media, or do they operate independently on their respective channels?
Bulova's online video spend is currently $78K against a $3.4M CTV budget — is that a deliberate format split, or is there appetite to test premium video inventory as part of the next planning cycle?

sales_plays.title: Brand Family Expansion Beyond PMP
sales_plays.summary: Central Garden & Pet is an active customer with a won PMP deal in July 2024. With $7.6M in digital spend and only $1 in tracked revenue on record, there is significant whitespace to grow wallet share across their pet and garden brand portfolio.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the July 2024 PMP win and congratulate the team on the partnership. Open with: 'We closed our first deal together last summer and I want to make sure we're delivering value across more of your portfolio — you're running $7.6M in digital and we're only touching a sliver of it.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Rebecca Sears or John Mergen: 'Beyond the PMP we activated last year, which Central Garden & Pet brands — pet side or garden side — are currently underserved in terms of premium video or contextually targeted inventory?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-contextual targeting against their $1.5M display and $259K online video spend. Garden and pet care purchase intent is directly tied to weather conditions — frost dates, spring warm-up, flea/tick season — and TWC can activate against those triggers in real time across both verticals.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Their $259K online video allocation is thin relative to their $1.5M display budget. A reallocation of even 15% of display spend (~$225K) into weather-triggered OLV or CTV would represent a 63% increase in their current CTV investment of $358K, with measurable lift in contextual relevance.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask John Mergen at The Shipyard for a 30-minute brand mapping session before end of quarter: 'Can we get 30 minutes with you and the Central team in the next two weeks to map which brands have Q3 seasonal windows we should be planning against now?'

sales_plays.title: Seasonal Planning Window — Spring Garden & Pet Tentpole
sales_plays.summary: Central Garden & Pet's garden and pet care categories have strong spring seasonality. With Bombora showing active agency research intent this week, now is the right moment to get into their planning cycle before Q2 budgets are locked.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the Bombora signal as a conversation opener with John Mergen at The Shipyard: 'I noticed Central Garden & Pet is actively researching media agency solutions right now — I want to make sure TWC is part of whatever planning conversations are happening for spring.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'For the garden brands specifically, when does your team typically lock Q2 media plans — and is spring tentpole planning already underway for brands like Pennington or Scotts-adjacent lines?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered planning tools as a Q2 planning asset, not just a buy. Spring garden category demand — fertilizer, seed, pest control — spikes with first warm weekends and last frost dates by DMA. TWC can provide a seasonal demand forecast by geography to inform both The Shipyard's planning and Rebecca Sears' brand-level budget allocation.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Central is spending $790K in paid social and $760K in mobile app — formats that lack weather-context signals. Shifting even $150K of that spring activation into TWC weather-triggered mobile or display would reach consumers at the exact moment outdoor purchase intent peaks, improving efficiency against their existing $7.6M digital footprint.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer a concrete, low-commitment deliverable: 'Let me send you a one-page spring demand forecast for the top 10 DMAs most relevant to Central's garden footprint — no strings attached — and we can use that as the basis for a planning call with Rebecca's team before February is out.'

talk_track_opener: Hey John, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Central Garden & Pet since last summer when we activated the PMP together, and I wanted to reach out now because I think we're leaving a lot of value on the table heading into spring. Central is running $7.6M in digital spend across display, social, video, and mobile — and right now TWC is only a small piece of that picture. Given that both the garden and pet care categories are directly tied to weather patterns — spring warm-up timing, frost dates, flea and tick season onset — we're uniquely positioned to help Central activate smarter across more of their brand portfolio, not just one line item. I also noticed Central is actively researching agency partnerships this week, which tells me planning conversations are live. I'd love to get 30 minutes with you and Rebecca Sears' team to map out which brands have the biggest spring windows and where weather-triggered inventory could move the needle before Q2 budgets are locked. Would the week of [specific date] work for a quick planning call?
open_questions: Beyond the PMP we activated in July 2024, which Central Garden & Pet brands — pet or garden — are currently running media without a TWC component, and who owns those brand-level budgets?

With both The Shipyard and Zenith Media holding planning and buying scope, how is media strategy coordinated across the two agencies — and is there a single point of contact who owns cross-brand planning for spring?
Rebecca Sears holds the CMO title for Green Garden specifically — is there a separate marketing lead on the pet side, and would expansion conversations need to involve both brand teams?
Central's CTV spend is $358K today — is that figure expected to grow in 2025, and is there a target percentage of digital budget earmarked for premium video formats?

Given that Central is actively researching agency solutions this week per our intent data, is there a formal agency review or media planning RFP underway that we should be aware of before bringing a Q2 proposal?
