sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Direct Premium Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Chewy has won three consecutive TWC Interactive PMP deals (2024–2026) at nominal value. With $43.1M in CTV and $28.5M in digital spend, there is clear headroom to convert this relationship into a higher-value direct buy or premium sponsorship package.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three consecutive PMP wins with Liz Paquette and Jaclyn Posner: 'Chewy has been a consistent TWC Interactive partner since mid-2024 — I want to show you what a direct premium placement could unlock that the PMP floor can't.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Liz, given Chewy's $43.1M CTV investment, how much of that is currently buying guaranteed reach around weather-driven pet care moments like cold snaps, storm prep, or summer heat — versus just open-market inventory?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct weather-contextual CTV and on-property sponsorships as the upgrade path: pet parents actively checking weather before outdoor activities or storm prep are a high-intent, addressable audience that PMP buys can't guarantee.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Chewy's CTV spend is $43.1M; even a 5% reallocation (~$2.15M) into a direct TWC premium package would represent a 2,000x+ increase over current PMP contract value and unlock first-look inventory, branded integrations, and audience guarantees.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Liz and Jaclyn for a 30-minute Q1 planning session before February tentpole windows open: 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar this week to map Chewy's 2026 seasonal moments to TWC's premium inventory before the spring pet-care window fills up?'

sales_plays.title: Display & OLV Budget Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Chewy's Internet Display ($1.1M) and Online Video ($174K) allocations are dramatically underweight relative to their Paid Social ($25.3M) and Search ($99.7M) spend, signaling reliance on lower-funnel channels. This play targets reallocation of those thin display/OLV dollars from incumbent vendors to TWC's weather-contextual digital placements.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the data contrast to Jaclyn Posner at Arnold Worldwide: 'Chewy is spending $99.7M in search and $25.3M in paid social, but only $1.1M in display and $174K in online video — that's a significant gap in upper-funnel contextual reach. I want to show you where TWC fits.'
Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Who currently holds Chewy's display and OLV budget, and what performance benchmarks are those partners being held to — is it reach, brand lift, or something else?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Displace the incumbent display/OLV vendors by positioning TWC's weather-triggered digital placements: pet food, pharmacy, and Autoship messaging served contextually when weather signals (cold fronts, heat advisories) drive pet care urgency outperforms generic programmatic display.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Chewy's combined display + OLV spend is ~$1.27M. Capturing even 50% of that spend into TWC contextual placements is a concrete, low-risk test — and Bombora signals show Chewy is actively researching Entertainment, suggesting openness to new media contexts beyond pure performance channels.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a 60-day pilot displacement test to Jaclyn: 'Let us run a head-to-head against your current display vendor on a single pet health or Autoship campaign — same KPIs, TWC weather-contextual targeting. Can we scope that before end of January?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Liz — I'm [rep name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Chewy through the TWC Interactive PMP for three straight years now, most recently closing the 2026 deal in January, so this isn't a cold call — I want to build on what's already working. We also work closely with Jaclyn and the Arnold team, so you may have already heard our name in the room. Here's what caught my attention: Chewy is putting $43.1M into CTV and nearly $100M into search, but the display and online video lines are sitting at just over $1.3M combined — and the PMP relationship, while consistent, hasn't grown into the premium formats that your scale really warrants. Pet parents are one of the most weather-reactive consumer segments there is — storm prep, cold snaps, summer heat — and we can put Chewy's Autoship and pet health messaging directly in front of them at the exact moment that intent spikes. Can I get 30 minutes with you and Jaclyn to show you what that looks like for Chewy's 2026 seasonal calendar?
open_questions: The TWC Interactive PMP has renewed three years running — Liz, what would need to be true for Chewy to move a portion of the $43.1M CTV budget into a direct, guaranteed premium buy with us rather than staying on the PMP floor?

Chewy's search spend is nearly $100M — how is the brand thinking about balancing lower-funnel search performance against upper-funnel brand building as the pet e-commerce market gets more competitive?
Is Chewy planning any major tentpole campaigns around spring pet adoption season, summer outdoor moments, or holiday gifting — and are those being planned through Arnold, Co:Collective, or both?
The Bombora data shows Chewy researching Entertainment this week — is that tied to a specific campaign brief or a broader exploration of content-led media environments for 2026?

sales_plays.title: Cigna PMP-to-Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Cigna is an active PMP customer with two consecutive won deals (2024 and 2025) through TWC Interactive. With $64.4M in paid social and $1.6M in online video, there is significant budget to convert toward premium direct inventory and richer formats.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins (July 2024 and January 2025) with Heather Foy at Initiative. Open with: 'Heather, we've now run two successful PMP cycles together — I want to show you what a direct buy unlocks that the PMP can't deliver, specifically for Chris Atterberry's Medicare Marketing priorities.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Heather: 'For the Medicare open enrollment push, is Initiative currently evaluating any premium video or sponsorship placements that go beyond what the PMP deal covers — and does Chris have a separate direct-response budget we haven't tapped?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a direct buy against Cigna's $1.6M online video line — show how guaranteed premium placements and contextual adjacency (health, wellness, weather-driven content) outperform open-auction PMP CPMs for Medicare audience targeting.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Cigna's digital footprint is $70.8M total; online video is only $1.6M — less than 3% of digital spend. Even a modest shift of $500K from paid social ($64.4M) into a direct premium video package represents a 30%+ increase on their current video investment with us.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Heather for a 30-minute working session within the next two weeks to map a direct buy proposal against Initiative's 2025 media calendar — offer to bring a custom audience reach report for the Medicare 65+ segment before the call.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars with Premium Digital
sales_plays.summary: Cigna is concentrating $64.4M — over 90% of its digital budget — in paid social. This over-reliance creates brand safety, reach saturation, and measurement risk. A displacement play targets Initiative's Group Director Heather Foy with a diversification argument backed by Cigna's own spend imbalance.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a data observation to Heather Foy: 'I was looking at Cigna's media mix and noticed paid social is carrying $64.4M versus only $962K in display and $1.6M in online video. That's a concentration risk most CMOs in healthcare are actively trying to reduce right now — especially with brand safety scrutiny on social platforms.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Is Chris Atterberry or the Initiative team under any pressure from Cigna's brand team to diversify away from paid social — and has MRM flagged any creative performance concerns tied to social-only reach saturation in the Medicare segment?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our premium display ($962K is already a foothold) and online video inventory as the brand-safe, contextually relevant alternative — weather and health content environments align directly with Cigna's disability, life, and Medicare product messaging and reach audiences when health decisions are top of mind.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Reallocating even 5% of Cigna's $64.4M paid social budget ($3.2M) into our premium digital inventory would represent a 3x increase over their current $962K display spend and a 2x increase over their $1.6M online video — with measurable incremental reach beyond social's walled garden.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send Heather a one-page 'social concentration risk' brief with a proposed reallocation scenario by end of week, then schedule a 20-minute call with her and potentially a McCann Worldgroup creative contact to align on format fit before Initiative's next planning cycle.

talk_track_opener: Hey Heather, this is [Rep Name] from [Company] — we've worked together on the Cigna PMP deals in both mid-2024 and early 2025, so you know we can execute. I wanted to reach out because I've been looking at how Cigna's broader digital mix is structured and I think there's a real opportunity we haven't fully explored together yet. When I look at Cigna's digital investment — $64.4M in paid social against only $1.6M in online video and under $1M in display — it's a pretty significant concentration in one channel. I know Chris Atterberry's Medicare Marketing team is driving a lot of that, and I'd love to understand whether Initiative is actively looking at ways to diversify that mix or layer in more premium environments for the Medicare audience. Would it make sense to carve out 20 minutes this month to walk through what a direct or expanded buy could look like alongside the PMP we already have running?
open_questions: Heather, now that we've run two PMP cycles together, is Initiative evaluating any direct or guaranteed buys for Cigna's 2025 Medicare open enrollment period that would sit outside the PMP structure?

Given Cigna's Bombora activity around Retail Technology, is there a digital transformation or consumer experience initiative underway that might open new budget lines for addressable or data-driven media buys beyond the current PMP?

sales_plays.title: Displace Display & OLV Underperformers
sales_plays.summary: Circle K is spending $303K on Internet Display and only $41K on Online Video — both are low relative to their $11M digital footprint. This signals either underinvestment or dissatisfaction with current display/OLV partners. A displacement play targets those weak spots with a premium, high-reach alternative before EssenceMediaCom locks in next planning cycle.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Jonathan Lee at EssenceMediaCom referencing Circle K's $303K display + $41K OLV allocation — both are unusually thin relative to their $6.9M paid social and $2.9M mobile app spend. Open with: 'We noticed Circle K's display and OLV investment is a fraction of their broader digital mix — we have data suggesting that's a common sign of underperforming placements, and we'd love to show you what we're seeing.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jonathan: 'What KPIs is EssenceMediaCom using to evaluate Circle K's current display and OLV partners — and are those placements hitting benchmarks against the paid social and mobile app lines?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our inventory as a direct replacement for the underperforming $344K combined display/OLV spend — emphasizing brand-safe, contextually relevant environments that complement Circle K's fuel and convenience purchase moments, which generic display networks miss.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the displacement opportunity: even reallocating 50% of Circle K's $344K display+OLV budget to our platform represents $172K in net-new spend with measurable lift benchmarks we can guarantee against. Reference their $11M total digital spend as proof they have room to optimize.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jonathan for a 20-minute working session in the next two weeks to walk through a side-by-side performance comparison of their current display/OLV placements versus our audience reach for convenience retail — offer to bring a one-page data brief on Circle K's category benchmarks.

sales_plays.title: Grey Group Creative Sync → Greg Dean Warm Introduction
sales_plays.summary: Grey Group holds Circle K's AOR Creative relationship, while EssenceMediaCom owns media planning and buying. Building a relationship with Grey Group's strategy team creates a warm referral path to Greg Dean, Director of Domestic & International Marketing, bypassing cold outreach to the client directly.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Jonathan Lee at Grey Group's New York office (noting Grey Group is AOR Creative for Circle K) and lead with a creative-media alignment angle: 'We work with several convenience and fuel retail brands and we've seen strong results when creative and media partners co-plan around cultural moments — given Circle K's scale across the Americas and Europe, we think there's a story worth exploring together.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jonathan at Grey Group: 'When Circle K plans around retail tentpoles — fuel promotions, summer road trip season, holiday convenience moments — does the media plan get built in parallel with creative, or does EssenceMediaCom receive the brief after creative is locked?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our platform as a media partner that can be brought into the creative-media planning conversation early — specifically for Circle K's mobile app ($2.9M spend) and paid social ($6.9M spend) audiences, where creative format and media placement need to be co-designed for performance.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Reference Circle K's $11M digital spend and note that brands at this investment level typically see 15-20% efficiency gains when creative and media partners align on audience segmentation before campaign launch — make this concrete by offering a Circle K-specific audience overlap analysis.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jonathan Lee to facilitate a three-way intro call with Greg Dean and our team — framed as a 'creative-media alignment briefing' rather than a sales meeting — within the next 30 days, ahead of any Q3 tentpole planning cycles Circle K may be entering.

talk_track_opener: Hey Jonathan, I'm reaching out because we work with several large convenience and fuel retail advertisers, and when I looked at Circle K's digital mix — $11M in total digital spend, with $6.9M in paid social and $2.9M in mobile app — I noticed the display and online video lines are running unusually lean at $303K and $41K respectively. That kind of imbalance usually tells us one of two things: either those placements aren't performing, or they haven't been fully explored yet. Either way, it's worth a conversation. We also saw Circle K is actively researching demand generation right now, which lines up with what we're hearing from other convenience retail brands heading into summer planning. I'd love to spend 20 minutes showing you what we're seeing in the category and whether there's a fit — no deck, just data. Would the week of [insert date] work for a quick call?
open_questions: How is EssenceMediaCom currently evaluating the performance of Circle K's $303K display and $41K OLV placements — and are those lines up for review in the next planning cycle?
Does Greg Dean or the Circle K brand team have direct input on channel mix decisions, or does EssenceMediaCom hold full discretion on how the $11M digital budget is allocated?
Is Circle K planning any tentpole campaigns around summer road trip season or convenience retail cultural moments that Grey Group and EssenceMediaCom are co-planning right now?
Given Circle K's $2.9M mobile app investment, is there a connected media strategy that extends app audience data into display or video — or are those budgets managed in separate silos?
With Circle K operating across the Americas, Europe, and Asia, does Greg Dean's domestic marketing mandate include international digital channels, or is this a US-only media conversation?

sales_plays.title: Displace Display & Mobile App Spend with On-Property Video
sales_plays.summary: Coach is allocating $649K to Internet Display and $232K to Mobile App — relatively thin, fragmented channels compared to their $1.1M Online Video line. This play targets displacement of those lower-performing off-property buys into TWC on-property video and contextual placements with measurable brand lift.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the Bombora signal — Coach is actively researching APIs & Services this week, suggesting a tech and data evaluation is underway. Open to Neena Koyen at UM: 'We noticed Coach is in active research mode around APIs and services — that often signals a measurement or attribution conversation. I want to connect that to how your $649K display and $232K mobile app spend is being evaluated for performance.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'For the $649K in Internet Display and $232K in Mobile App, what does success look like — is it click-through, brand lift, or something else — and who at Coach or UM owns the performance benchmark for those lines?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's on-property video and contextual weather-triggered placements as a direct displacement of generic display and mobile app inventory. For a luxury brand like Coach, contextual alignment (travel, outerwear seasons, gifting moments) is a stronger brand signal than broad display networks.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Redirecting the combined $881K (display + mobile app) into TWC on-property video would represent a meaningful upgrade in contextual quality. Paired with Coach's existing $1.1M Online Video spend, this creates a consolidated ~$2M video presence with unified measurement — a cleaner story for Joon Silverstein's team to evaluate brand impact.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send Neena Koyen a one-page displacement analysis comparing TWC on-property video CPMs and contextual targeting against the current display/mobile app mix — request a 20-minute call within 10 days to walk through it before Q3 planning locks.

talk_track_opener: Hey Neena, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've worked together on two PMP activations for Coach — the most recent closed in January 2025 — and I wanted to reach out because I think there's a meaningful next step beyond what we've built so far. Coach is running $8.8M in CTV and $63.6M in search, which tells me brand investment is serious and performance accountability is real. I'd love to explore how we expand the TWC footprint to match that ambition. One thing caught my attention this week — Coach is showing active Bombora intent around APIs and Services, which often signals a measurement or attribution review is happening internally. At the same time, I see $649K in display and $232K in mobile app that could be working harder in a contextually relevant, on-property environment. Can I ask: when UM is evaluating those smaller digital lines for Coach, is the conversation about efficiency, brand safety, or something else entirely?
open_questions: The two PMP deals we've closed together are logged as placeholder values — can you help me understand what the actual investment behind those activations looked like, and what KPIs Coach used to evaluate their performance?

With Joon Silverstein as CMO, how much of the digital channel mix strategy is set at the brand level versus delegated to UM Worldwide — and who owns the decision to shift spend from programmatic to direct buys?
Coach is showing Bombora intent around APIs and Services this week — is that tied to a measurement or attribution review, and is that conversation happening at the UM level or internally at Coach?

For the $649K in Internet Display and $232K in Mobile App, what does the performance benchmark look like — and has there been a recent conversation about whether those lines are earning their place in the plan?

With Q3 retail tentpole and cultural-moment planning coming up, is UM already in briefing mode for Coach's fall and holiday campaigns, and is there an opportunity to get TWC into that planning conversation before budgets are locked?

sales_plays.title: Mobile-First Portfolio Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Coca-Cola is an active customer with won deals at CVS and Walgreens tied to hydration moments. Bombora shows live mobile intent this week, and their $29M Mobile App spend signals a prioritized channel — this is the moment to expand wallet share into premium mobile inventory before the next retail tentpole window.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the active Bombora mobile intent signal directly: 'We're seeing Coca-Cola trending on mobile research topics this week — given your $29M mobile app investment, I wanted to reach out before the summer hydration planning window closes.' Send this to Shakir Moin or route through Dentsu X's Bianca Guimaraes, who owns AOR Digital Social.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'The CVS Summer Hydration win in May and the Walgreens Spring Allergy win in April both performed — are those retail co-op budgets being planned again for 2026, and is mobile a bigger part of that mix this cycle?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's mobile weather-contextual inventory as the bridge between Coca-Cola's $29M mobile app spend and their proven retail hydration playbook — real-time temperature and humidity triggers that activate at the exact moment a consumer needs a cold beverage.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The CVS deal closed at $60K and the Walgreens deal at $40K — a combined $100K in won retail hydration revenue. A mobile-first expansion targeting both retail partners plus a direct brand layer could realistically 3x that to $300K+ for the 2026 summer cycle, consistent with the scale of their $343M digital footprint.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 20-minute working session with Dentsu X before end of Q1 2026: 'Can we get 20 minutes with your Dentsu X team in the next two weeks to map a mobile weather-trigger proposal against your 2026 retail tentpole calendar?'

sales_plays.title: Displace the 2025 Fall Hydration Loss
sales_plays.summary: The $50K Fall Hydration campaign was lost in October 2025 — likely to a competing platform or a budget cut. With summer 2026 planning underway and Bombora mobile intent live, this is the window to re-enter with a stronger proposal before the incumbent locks the budget again.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Acknowledge the loss directly and lead with a new angle: 'The Fall Hydration campaign in October didn't come together with us — I'd like to understand what did run and whether the results opened the door to revisit our approach for fall 2026.' Route this through Bianca Guimaraes at Mischief @ No Fixed Address, who holds AOR Creative and may have influenced the original brief.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'What platform or format ran the Fall 2025 Hydration campaign, and did the results justify the spend relative to what you saw from the CVS and Walgreens spring activations that did run with us?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): If the competing platform was social or programmatic display, position TWC Interactive's weather-triggered contextual environment as a displacement: Coca-Cola's $128.2M in Paid Social and $9.1M in Display are broad reach plays — TWC delivers intent-at-the-moment-of-need that social cannot replicate for a hydration message.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The two won retail hydration deals totaled $100,309. The lost fall deal was scoped at $50K. Winning it back plus layering in a mobile component (given live Bombora mobile intent) positions a $75K–$100K fall 2026 package — doubling the lost deal value and closing the gap in their Q3/Q4 digital mix.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive review meeting: 'Would Shakir's team or Dentsu X be open to a 30-minute debrief in February where we walk through what a fall 2026 hydration package would look like on our platform versus what ran last year?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Bianca, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Coca-Cola's retail partners on the hydration campaigns — the CVS Summer activation in May and the Walgreens Spring Allergy program in April both ran with us, so we have real context on what's working in that space. We're not starting from scratch here. What caught my attention this week is that Coca-Cola is actively researching mobile right now — our intent data is showing it as a trending topic for the brand. Given that Coca-Cola is already putting $29 million into mobile app spend and Dentsu X owns the digital social mandate, I wanted to get in front of you before the 2026 summer hydration calendar gets locked. The question I'd love to explore with you: are the CVS and Walgreens retail co-op budgets being rebuilt for 2026, and is there appetite to make mobile weather-context a bigger part of that activation strategy this cycle?
open_questions: The CVS and Walgreens hydration programs ran in spring 2025 — are those retail co-op budgets being rebuilt for 2026, and has the scope expanded to include additional retail partners?
The Fall 2025 Hydration campaign at $50K went to another platform — what ran, and did the results justify the spend compared to the spring activations that ran with us?
Dentsu X owns AOR Digital Social — is mobile planning for 2026 being led out of Dentsu X, or is Shakir Moin's team setting the channel strategy before it goes to the agency?
Coca-Cola is showing active mobile intent in our data right now — is that tied to a specific 2026 brief in flight, or is it early-stage research for a future campaign?
With $128.2M in Paid Social already committed, where does contextual or weather-triggered inventory fit in the media mix — is it additive budget or does it need to displace something to get approved?

sales_plays.title: Scale the PMP Win Into Full Portfolio
sales_plays.summary: Cole Haan has two consecutive PMP wins (Jan 2025, Jul 2024) but total closed revenue is nominal ($1 each), suggesting the relationship is active but undermonetized. The real spend is in Paid Social ($57M) and Search ($6.3M) — this play targets pulling a slice of that budget into premium inventory beyond the current PMP footprint.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins with Angela Tran at PMG and Scott Patt directly: 'We've had two successful PMP activations together in 2024 and early 2025 — I want to show you what the next tier of that partnership could look like given the scale Cole Haan is already running in Paid Social at $57M.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Angela Tran: 'Of the $57M running through Paid Social, how much is currently allocated to premium contextual environments versus open-auction placements — and is there a Q3 or Q4 budget review where that mix is being reconsidered?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium weather-contextual inventory as a complement to PMG's Creative Digital Social scope — not a replacement for Paid Social, but a high-intent, brand-safe layer that reaches Cole Haan's core consumer (active, lifestyle-oriented) at a moment of behavioral relevance (weather-driven footwear decisions).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The two lost opps in Q3–Q4 2023 totaled $72,000 in pipeline that didn't close. Reframe: 'If we can convert even a 0.5% shift of the $57M Paid Social budget into our premium environment, that's $285K in incremental reach — and we already have the PMP infrastructure in place from the 2024–2025 wins.'

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Angela Tran for a 30-minute working session before end of month to map Cole Haan's Q3 tentpole calendar against TWC's premium inventory packages — specific ask: 'Can we get 30 minutes with you and Scott before June 30 to walk through a Q3 expansion proposal?'

sales_plays.title: Entertainment Intent + Fall Tentpole Pre-Buy
sales_plays.summary: Bombora shows Cole Haan spiking on Entertainment intent this week. Combined with Cole Haan's retail vertical and the medium-confidence seasonal planning window for fall tentpoles (back-to-school, Fashion Week, holiday pre-planning), this is a timely opening to position a Q3/Q4 pre-buy before budgets are locked.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the intent signal in outreach to Angela Tran at PMG: 'Our Bombora data is showing Cole Haan actively researching Entertainment this week — that timing lines up with what we typically see from retail brands starting to plan fall cultural-moment campaigns. I wanted to reach out before those budgets get committed.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Is Cole Haan planning any campaign activations tied to fall cultural moments — Fashion Week, holiday gifting, or entertainment partnerships — and has PMG already allocated media against those tentpoles for Q4?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's contextual targeting connects Cole Haan's footwear and accessories narrative to weather-driven lifestyle moments (fall transition, first cold snap, holiday travel) — environments where entertainment and lifestyle content consumption peaks and purchase intent for premium footwear is highest.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Cole Haan runs $3.7M in Display and $101K in Online Video today. A targeted fall tentpole pre-buy layered onto the existing PMP wins could extend reach into high-attention entertainment and lifestyle contexts without cannibalizing the $6.3M Search budget — a net-new channel test with a defined flight window.

Step 5 (Next Step): Send Angela Tran a one-page Q4 tentpole planning brief by end of this week, referencing the Entertainment intent signal, and request a 20-minute call with her and Scott Patt before July 15 to align on whether a fall pre-buy fits PMG's planning timeline for Cole Haan.

talk_track_opener: Hey Angela — this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together on Cole Haan's PMP activations through 2024 and into early 2025, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a real opportunity we haven't fully explored yet. Given the scale Cole Haan is running — $57M in Paid Social, $6.3M in Search — the PMP wins we have together are a strong foundation, but they represent a fraction of what this partnership could look like.
open_questions: Of the $57M Cole Haan is running in Paid Social, how much is currently in premium or reserved inventory versus open-auction — and is PMG evaluating that mix for Q3 or Q4?
Who owns the final sign-off on net-new channel additions for Cole Haan — is that Scott Patt on the brand side, or does Angela Tran at PMG have budget authority for testing new partners?
The Bombora data shows Cole Haan researching Entertainment this week — is that tied to a specific fall campaign concept or partnership that PMG is currently scoping?

The Q3 and Q4 2023 proposals ($22K and $50K) didn't close — what was the primary objection at the time, and has anything changed in how Cole Haan evaluates contextual or display investments since then?

With Cole Haan's 500+ store global footprint, is the current TWC PMP activation running against a specific geographic market or product line, and is there appetite to expand that targeting for the fall season?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Colgate-Palmolive has two consecutive won PMP deals (2024 and 2025) through TWC Interactive, signaling an established programmatic relationship. The play is to convert that low-dollar PMP activity into a higher-value direct or premium sponsorship buy against their $62.6M digital footprint.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the back-to-back PMP wins — 'We've activated your TWC Interactive PMPs in both 2024 and 2025, and I wanted to share what we're seeing in performance data that points to an opportunity to do more together.' Lead with continuity, not a cold pitch.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask which brand within the Colgate-Palmolive portfolio — oral care, personal care, home care, or Hill's Pet Nutrition — is currently prioritized for incremental digital investment in H2 2025, since the PMP history doesn't reveal brand-level attribution.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a direct premium placement or branded content package on TWC's owned properties as the logical next step — moving from auction-based PMP exposure to guaranteed, contextually relevant inventory that aligns with Colgate's health and hygiene messaging.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): With $62.6M in total digital spend and only nominal PMP dollars captured so far, even a 2% shift of digital budget to a direct TWC buy represents roughly $1.25M in incremental revenue — frame this as closing the gap between their digital scale and their TWC investment.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute working session with the Direct Agents, Inc. digital team before end of Q2 2025 to map TWC's premium inventory against their active brand calendar — low commitment, high signal.

sales_plays.title: Displace the Paid Social Concentration
sales_plays.summary: Colgate-Palmolive routes $43.5M — nearly 70% of their $62.6M digital budget — through Paid Social, creating a concentration risk and a clear displacement opportunity. The play targets VML or Direct Agents, Inc. contacts to redirect a portion of that spend toward TWC's digital and CTV inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a data-backed observation routed through Direct Agents, Inc.: 'We noticed Colgate's digital mix is heavily weighted toward Paid Social — around $43.5M of a $62.6M digital budget. A lot of our shared clients in CPG are actively rebalancing away from walled gardens right now, and I wanted to bring that conversation to you.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Direct Agents, Inc. — the digital agency of record — whether Colgate has set a threshold for Paid Social concentration, and whether there is internal pressure to diversify into addressable CTV or premium display given rising CPMs on Meta and TikTok.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's $16.0M CTV relationship as proof of existing trust, and propose that a reallocation of even 5-10% of the $43.5M Paid Social budget into TWC's addressable CTV or display inventory ($7.0M currently) would reduce platform dependency while maintaining reach against Colgate's health-focused audience.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 5% shift from Paid Social ($2.2M) into TWC CTV or display would more than double Colgate's current non-social digital footprint with TWC, and can be benchmarked against the lost Elta MD High UV Q3 2024 deal ($15,000) as a starting proof-of-concept for premium brand-level buys.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a brief competitive audit — TWC pulls a category-level spend comparison showing how peer CPG brands allocate across Paid Social vs. premium digital — and shares it with Chris Turner at Ogilvy and the Direct Agents, Inc. team within two weeks.

talk_track_opener: Hey, I'm reaching out from TWC — we've been working with your team through Direct Agents, Inc. on the TWC Interactive PMP for two years running now, most recently closing the 2025 deal in February. So this isn't a cold call; we have real history together and I want to build on it. I work with a number of Colgate's peer brands in the CPG and personal care space and I've been tracking how they're evolving their media mix heading into the back half of 2025. One thing that stands out when I look at Colgate's digital footprint is that roughly $43.5M of your $62.6M digital budget is concentrated in Paid Social — that's a significant dependency on walled garden inventory at a time when CPMs there are climbing. I'd love to spend 20 minutes understanding whether there's appetite on your team or at Direct Agents to explore what a more diversified digital mix could look like, and whether the TWC relationship could play a bigger role. Would a quick call before end of May work?
open_questions: The TWC Interactive PMP has renewed two years in a row — who at Direct Agents, Inc. or on Colgate's internal team is championing that relationship, and are they also involved in decisions about direct or premium buys?
With $43.5M flowing through Paid Social, is there a formal review underway — either at Colgate or at VML or Direct Agents — to assess platform concentration risk or diversification targets for 2025?
The Elta MD High UV deal was evaluated in mid-2024 but didn't close — what was the deciding factor, and is that brand still actively planning media for the back half of 2025?

Colgate's portfolio spans oral care, personal care, home care, and Hill's Pet Nutrition — which brand or category has the most flexibility in its digital budget right now, and who owns that planning relationship?

Given that Ogilvy holds creative scope and VML is also on creative, where does media strategy and channel allocation actually get decided — is that Direct Agents, an internal team, or a separate media AOR we haven't identified yet?

sales_plays.title: Upgrade the PMP to Premium Direct
sales_plays.summary: Columbia has held an active PMP relationship since 2024 but the Q4 2025 Winter Campaign deal was lost at $50K. With $4.8M in CTV and $38.8M in Paid Social spend, there is clear budget scale to move from a $1 PMP placeholder into a meaningful direct buy that captures weather-triggered demand.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three consecutive PMP wins (2024, 2025, 2026) and acknowledge the Q4 2025 Winter Campaign loss at $50K. Tell Tom Russo: 'We've kept the PMP relationship healthy — I want to make sure we're actually delivering against Columbia's demand-gen goals, not just holding a seat at the table.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Tom Russo: 'With Demand Generation as a stated priority this week, where is the current PMP activation falling short — is it reach, targeting precision, or the inability to react to real-time weather signals that drive Columbia's purchase intent?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered direct inventory as the bridge from passive PMP access to active demand capture — specifically for the outdoor/active lifestyle moments (cold snaps, first snowfall, rain events) that Columbia's product line is built around.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Columbia spends $4.8M in CTV and $38.8M in Paid Social. Even a 1% reallocation from Paid Social into weather-triggered TWC direct inventory equals ~$388K — a 7,760x step-up from the current $1 PMP value. Frame the $50K lost Q4 deal as the floor, not the ceiling.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Tom Russo for a 30-minute working session before end of Q2 to map Columbia's 2H tentpole calendar (Back-to-School, Fall Launch, Holiday) against TWC weather-trigger windows — low commitment, high planning value.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Waste with Weather-Indexed CTV
sales_plays.summary: Columbia's digital mix is heavily skewed toward Paid Social at $38.8M versus only $318K in Online Video and $4.8M in CTV. This imbalance suggests over-reliance on social platforms where Columbia cannot leverage weather context. The play targets Daniel Bonder at adam&eveDDB to reframe a portion of that social spend as addressable CTV with weather intelligence.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Daniel Bonder at adam&eveDDB (AOR Creative) and acknowledge the creative work driving Columbia's brand. Open with: 'With $38.8M flowing through Paid Social, I want to show you where weather-indexed CTV can do what social algorithms can't — serve Columbia's jacket creative the moment a cold front hits a target DMA.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Daniel Bonder: 'When adam&eveDDB plans Columbia's seasonal creative flights, are you currently able to activate or suppress media based on real-time weather conditions, or is the social buy running on a fixed schedule regardless of what's happening outside?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered CTV as a displacement of the least-efficient portion of the $38.8M Paid Social budget — specifically the fixed-schedule, non-contextual impressions that run regardless of weather relevance to Columbia's outdoor product line.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Columbia's Online Video spend is only $318K versus $4.8M in CTV — the video investment is fragmented. Consolidating even $500K of Paid Social spend into weather-triggered TWC CTV would represent a 57% increase over current Online Video investment and deliver contextual relevance that social feeds cannot match.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer Daniel Bonder a creative-plus-data brief — a one-page weather-trigger map showing Columbia's top 10 DMAs, seasonal temperature thresholds, and recommended flight windows — deliverable within two weeks, no commitment required.

talk_track_opener: Hey Tom, I'm [rep name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Columbia through the PMP relationship since 2024 and I want to make sure that relationship is actually moving the needle for your ecommerce goals, not just sitting on a contract page. We saw the Q4 2025 Winter Campaign didn't come together and I'd rather understand why than let that pattern repeat heading into your biggest selling season. Here's what caught my attention this week: Bombora is showing Columbia actively researching Demand Generation right now, and when I look at your media mix — $38.8M in Paid Social, $4.8M in CTV, but only $318K in Online Video — it looks like there may be a gap between where the budget is and where weather-driven purchase intent actually lives. Columbia's customer is outside. They're checking the forecast before they buy a jacket. Are you currently able to trigger or adjust your media buys based on real-time weather conditions in your key DMAs, or is that a capability you haven't had a clean path to yet?
open_questions: The Q4 2025 Winter Campaign at $50K didn't close — was that a budget reallocation, a competing platform win, or a timing issue we can address for 2H 2026?
Tom, who owns the decision to expand beyond the current PMP structure — is that you, someone on the brand media team, or does it route through adam&eveDDB?
With $38.8M in Paid Social, are you seeing diminishing returns on any platforms, and is there internal pressure to diversify the digital mix heading into fall?
Does Columbia currently have the ability to trigger or pause media buys based on real-time weather conditions in specific DMAs, or is that a capability gap?
As you plan the 2H tentpole calendar — Back-to-School, Fall Launch, Holiday — how far in advance is adam&eveDDB locking media commitments, and is there still flexibility in the Q3 plan?

sales_plays.summary: Comcast is spending $106.3M in Paid Social and $119.7M in Search — nearly $226M concentrated in Google and Meta ecosystems. This play targets Steve Ciccarone and agency partner Renee Timchuk at r2integrated to shift incremental budget from lower-funnel social into premium CTV and Online Video inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Steve Ciccarone and CC Renee Timchuk at r2integrated referencing the two consecutive PMP wins (Jan 2025 and Jul 2024) and note that Constellation's CTV allocation ($156K) is running at less than 2% of their $8.8M Paid Social investment — a clear imbalance given where energy-category audiences are shifting.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's CTV inventory as a contextual complement to Constellation's existing PMP buys — weather-adjacent, high-attention environments that reach homeowners and business decision-makers who are actively managing energy costs, an audience Paid Social struggles to isolate at scale.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Constellation's total estimated media spend is $19.3M. Their current TWC SFDC revenue is $20 — less than 0.01% of wallet share. Even a modest reallocation of 5% of the $8.8M Paid Social budget into TWC CTV and Online Video represents a $440K incremental opportunity against a category where TWC has proven PMP performance.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Renee to schedule a 30-minute r2integrated agency briefing in the next two weeks to walk through TWC's CTV audience overlap with Constellation's current PMP segments — offer to bring a custom reach analysis using their existing PMP flight data.

sales_plays.title: Seasonal Customer Communication Window Planning
sales_plays.summary: Energy brands like Constellation face predictable seasonal demand windows — summer peak load and winter heating season — when customer communication and acquisition campaigns intensify. This play uses an upcoming planning window to get ahead of Q3/Q4 seasonal activation and re-open the Weather Triggers conversation that was lost in Q3 2023.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Steve Ciccarone now, referencing that the Q3 2023 Weather Triggers opportunity ($30K, lost) was ahead of its time — and that TWC's weather-triggered ad products have since matured significantly. Note that energy-vertical advertisers are beginning Q3 planning cycles and Constellation's $574K Digital spend is under-indexed on contextual triggers.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Steve: 'As Constellation plans summer and fall customer communication campaigns — whether for residential energy management or commercial acquisition — is there a specific weather-correlated behavior (high-demand days, heat advisories, storm prep) that your team has wanted to activate against but hasn't found the right targeting mechanism for?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Reconnect the Weather Triggers product to Constellation's core business reality: their customers make energy decisions in direct response to weather events. TWC's weather-triggered display and mobile inventory lets Constellation serve contextually relevant messaging at the exact moment of peak customer intent — something the $2.1M Display and $706K Mobile App spend cannot replicate without a weather signal layer.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost Q3 2023 Weather Triggers opp was valued at $30K — a test budget. Constellation's $706K Mobile App and $232K Online Video spend signal appetite for contextual digital formats. A properly scoped weather-trigger program layered onto their existing PMP buys could realistically target $150K–$250K, with clear seasonal flight logic tied to summer peak and winter heating windows.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a specific planning call with Steve and Renee Timchuk at r2integrated before end of current quarter — frame it as a 20-minute seasonal readiness review, not a pitch — and offer a one-page weather-trigger activation calendar mapped to Constellation's energy service regions.

talk_track_opener: Hey Steve, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working with Constellation through Renee and the r2integrated team on your PMP programs, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a meaningful gap in how we're supporting you. You're running close to $19M in total media spend, and your TWC relationship — while active and performing — is capturing a fraction of that. Your CTV investment is at $156K against $8.8M in Paid Social, and the Weather Triggers program that was explored back in 2023 never got a full run. I think the timing and the product are both in a better place now. I wanted to get 20 minutes with you and Renee to walk through two specific opportunities — one around CTV expansion tied to your existing PMP segments, and one around weather-triggered activation ahead of summer peak season. Would a quick planning call in the next two weeks work for you both?

sales_plays.title: Expand Beyond PMP Into Full Weather-Contextual Suite
sales_plays.summary: Cooper Tires is an active PMP customer (won Feb 2025 at $1 CPM entry point) with meaningful broadcast ($268K) and digital ($128K) spend on record. The opportunity is to grow the relationship from a single PMP line into a multi-format, weather-contextual package that mirrors how consumers actually shop for tires.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the Feb 2025 PMP win directly with Michiel Kramer: 'Michiel, we activated the PMP earlier this year and wanted to share some performance context and a logical next step given Cooper's current spend mix across broadcast and digital.' Use the Bombora Search Marketing intent signal as proof that Cooper's audience is actively in-market right now.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Michiel: 'Of your $128K in digital and $268K in broadcast, how much is currently tied to contextual or weather-triggered delivery versus always-on placements — and is that balance something you're looking to shift?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-contextual inventory as the connective tissue between Cooper's broadcast investment and their digital spend. Cooper's brand promise is 'peace of mind on the road' — weather-triggered ads at the moment of a rain or storm event are a direct expression of that promise, not just a media buy.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Cooper's current digital mix shows $42K in Online Video and $42K in Paid Social but only $12K in Display and $2K in Mobile App — clear whitespace. A weather-contextual display and mobile expansion of even $25K–$40K would represent a 2–3x lift on those underinvested lines and align with the $60K Q3 tire-preparation precedent from 2023.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Michiel for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks — before rain season ramps — to walk through a weather-contextual proposal built around Cooper's existing creative with BBH's Tasha Cronin looped in for production alignment.

sales_plays.title: Rain Season Lead-In: Get on Air Before the Storm
sales_plays.summary: Rain and storm season is the highest-intent purchase window for all-season and wet-weather tires. Cooper Tires has a documented Q3 tire-preparation campaign history ($60K, 2023) and active Search Marketing intent signals this week — the rep must get in front of Michiel Kramer and BBH's Tasha Cronin now, before the demand window opens and competitive share of voice locks up.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the timing urgency in outreach to both Michiel Kramer and Tasha Cronin at BBH: 'We're seeing Cooper Tires flagged in Bombora for Search Marketing intent this week, and rain season is 6–8 weeks out — historically the highest-conversion window for wet-weather tire consideration. I want to make sure we have inventory reserved before it gets competitive.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Tasha Cronin at BBH: 'Is BBH planning a rain or storm season push for Cooper this spring, and has a media brief gone out yet — or is this still in planning?' This surfaces whether there is a live RFP or a whitespace opportunity to shape the brief.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC as the only media environment where weather IS the content — a Cooper Tires ad running against a live severe weather alert or a local rain forecast is not interruption advertising, it is contextually perfect placement. Reference Cooper's brand pillar of 'peace of mind' as the creative through-line.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Cooper's Q3 2023 tire-preparation campaign was valued at $60K. A rain season lead-in package anchored in weather-triggered digital, online video ($42K already in their mix), and broadcast ($268K on record) could reasonably target a $75K–$90K seasonal activation — a 25–50% step-up from the 2023 precedent with measurable in-market timing lift.

Step 5 (Next Step): Send Michiel and Tasha a one-page rain season timing brief by end of this week — no commitment required — showing the demand curve for tire-related search and weather event frequency in Cooper's key markets. Ask for a 20-minute call to pressure-test the timing before the window opens.

talk_track_opener: Hey Michiel, I'm [rep name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working together since the PMP activation earlier this year in February, and I wanted to reach out personally because the timing feels important right now. We cover Cooper Tires as a priority account and I want to make sure we're ahead of the curve for you, not reactive. Here's what caught my attention this week: our intent data is showing Cooper Tires actively researching Search Marketing, and we're about six to eight weeks out from rain and storm season — historically the single highest-intent window for wet-weather tire consideration. Cooper's current digital mix has real momentum in Online Video and Paid Social, but there's meaningful whitespace in weather-contextual display and mobile that maps directly to your 'peace of mind' brand positioning. I'd love to ask you one quick question: when your team thinks about the spring media plan, is the goal to extend what worked in the Q3 2023 tire-prep campaign, or are you looking to try a different format mix this year?
open_questions: The Q3 2023 tire-preparation campaign ran at $60K — was that budget managed through BBH or directly by Michiel's team, and is a similar seasonal line item in the 2025 plan?
With BBH holding the Creative scope, who owns media planning and buying for Cooper's digital spend — is there a separate media AOR we should be aware of, or does BBH handle that as well?

Cooper's digital mix shows $42K each in Online Video and Paid Social but only $12K in Display — is that weighting intentional, or is Display underinvested because of measurement gaps or creative constraints?
The Feb 2025 PMP win was the re-entry after a Dec 2023 loss — what changed in the evaluation that brought Cooper back to TWC, and what would need to be true for us to expand scope this year?

Given the Bombora signal showing Search Marketing interest this week, is Cooper evaluating a shift toward more performance-oriented digital, or is this more about supplementing brand spend with search retargeting?

sales_plays.title: Costco Digital Stack Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Costco is an active PMP customer with two consecutive won deals. With $11M in search, $8.3M in paid social, and $7.2M in display already in market, there is a clear opening to expand their Weather Company footprint into premium direct and cross-channel formats that complement their existing PMP buys.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Jay Smith referencing the two consecutive PMP wins (Jan 2025 and Aug 2024) and note that Costco's $11M search + $8.3M paid social investment signals a full-funnel ambition that the current PMP alone doesn't fully serve. Ask for 20 minutes to show what's adjacent.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jay: 'Beyond the PMP placements you're already running with us, which part of the funnel — awareness, consideration, or conversion — is Deloitte Digital telling you is underperforming heading into your next retail tentpole window?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position Weather Company's premium direct inventory and contextual weather-triggered units as the missing upper-funnel layer that amplifies the existing PMP spend — reaching Costco members when weather conditions drive bulk-buy and seasonal purchase intent (e.g., pre-storm pantry stocking, summer outdoor, holiday entertaining).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Costco's current TWC PMP spend is nominal ($1 recorded value per deal), suggesting the relationship is early-stage. Even a modest direct buy at 5–10% of their $7.2M display budget represents a $360K–$720K incremental opportunity on a channel where they already trust the supply path.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jay to include Bevan Mahaney at Deloitte Digital on a 30-minute working session before end of quarter to map TWC premium formats against Costco's Q3/Q4 retail calendar — specifically any tentpole or cultural-moment campaigns already in planning.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars with Weather-Contextual Video
sales_plays.summary: Costco is spending $8.3M in paid social and $2.8M in online video — channels facing rising CPMs, signal loss post-cookie, and brand-safety scrutiny. Weather Company's contextual, premium video inventory offers a credible displacement argument routed through Deloitte Digital's Bevan Mahaney.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Bevan Mahaney at Deloitte Digital and reference Costco's $8.3M paid social + $2.8M online video allocation. Open with: 'We're seeing several retail clients re-evaluate social video CPMs this year — I'd like to show you what Costco's $2.8M video budget could do on a contextually targeted, brand-safe environment like TWC.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Bevan: 'When Costco's video creative runs on paid social today, how are you measuring view-through quality and brand-safe adjacency — and is that a metric Jay Smith's team is scrutinizing heading into the back half?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's online video and weather-triggered pre-roll as a premium, cookieless-ready alternative to social video — with contextual relevance (e.g., Costco's Kirkland pantry staples tied to weather events) that paid social algorithms cannot replicate. Emphasize brand-safe, first-party data environment vs. social platform risk.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Shifting even 15% of Costco's $8.3M paid social budget ($1.25M) into TWC contextual video would represent a 10x+ expansion over current TWC PMP spend and deliver measurable reach against Costco's 130M+ member base during high-intent weather moments.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Bevan to greenlight a 2-week pilot proposal — a weather-triggered video package tied to one upcoming Costco seasonal push (e.g., summer outdoor or holiday entertaining) — with a draft brief due before the next Deloitte Digital planning cycle closes.

talk_track_opener: Hey Jay, I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company — you and I have been working together through two PMP deals now, most recently closed in January, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think we're only scratching the surface of what's possible for Costco on our platform. We've built a strong foundation with the PMP, and I'd love to show you how that translates into a broader strategy. Looking at Costco's media mix — $11M in search, $8.3M in paid social, $7.2M in display — it's clear you're running a sophisticated full-funnel program, and I think there's a real gap where weather-contextual, premium inventory could be doing heavy lifting that your current channels can't. Specifically, I'm curious: as you and Bevan's team at Deloitte Digital plan for the back half of the year, which tentpole moments are you most focused on, and is upper-funnel awareness part of that conversation?
open_questions: The two PMP deals we've closed together have been a strong start — how is Jay's team currently evaluating TWC's performance relative to the rest of Costco's $7.2M display budget, and what metrics matter most to him?

Bevan, as Deloitte Digital manages Costco's creative scope, how are you currently briefing weather or seasonal context into the video and display creative — and is there a planning cycle we should be aligned to for Q3/Q4?
Costco's paid social spend is $8.3M — is Jay's team seeing CPM pressure or brand-safety concerns on those platforms that are creating any appetite to diversify into premium contextual environments?

The Bombora signal shows Costco researching 'Education' topics this week — is there a back-to-school, member education, or workforce campaign in planning that we should be building a contextual package around?

Given Costco's retail tentpole calendar — summer outdoor, back-to-school, holiday entertaining — which seasonal window is Deloitte Digital currently under-resourced on from a media perspective, and is there budget flexibility to test a weather-triggered format before that window opens?

sales_plays.title: Cotton Win-Back: Reactivate the 2023 Video & PMP Investment
sales_plays.summary: Cotton Incorporated ran two $100K Interactive/Video PMP deals on TWC in 2H 2023 and went dark after Q4. With $18.8M in total trailing spend and $7.6M in digital, there is clear budget and appetite — the relationship just stalled. This play re-opens the door through GALE's Robert Shimasaki before Cotton's internal planning cycle locks spend.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Robert Shimasaki at GALE referencing the two $100K TWC Interactive/Video PMP deals from August–September 2023. Acknowledge the gap and lead with a forward-looking hook: 'We ran two strong PMP programs together in 2H 2023 — I'd love to share what's changed on our end and hear where Cotton's digital priorities are heading for the back half of 2025.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Robert: 'When Cotton ran the Video PMP in August 2023, what performance metrics were you optimizing against — and did the results influence how you allocated the digital budget in 2024?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-contextual targeting against Cotton's core consumer message — cotton as a natural, breathable fiber performs best when audiences are actively experiencing heat, humidity, or seasonal transitions. TWC's first-party weather data lets Cotton reach consumers at the exact moment comfort and fabric choice are top of mind.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Cotton's 2023 TWC commitment was $200K across two PMP deals. With $7.6M in digital spend overall, that represents less than 3% of their digital budget. A renewed program at $300–400K would still be under 5% of digital — a low-risk reactivation with measurable incremental reach against their CTV ($7.1M) and Broadcast ($3.8M) buys.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Robert for a 20-minute call in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 20 minutes on the calendar before end of [current month] to walk through a refreshed proposal? I want to make sure we're in front of Andrea Samber's team before the 2H planning window closes.'

sales_plays.title: Rain & Storm Season Lead-In: Cotton Performance Messaging Window
sales_plays.summary: Rain and storm season creates a high-relevance contextual window for Cotton Incorporated to reach consumers actively thinking about fabric performance, moisture management, and comfort. Cotton's prior TWC buys were in August–September 2023 — aligning with late-summer weather peaks. Getting in front of Andrea Samber and GALE now, before the demand window opens, positions TWC as the preferred contextual environment for Cotton's seasonal push.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Andrea Samber directly with a weather-anchored insight: 'Cotton's 2023 TWC campaigns ran right as late-summer heat and humidity peaked — that timing wasn't accidental. We're heading into another rain and storm season and I want to make sure Cotton has first-mover advantage on our contextual inventory before it's committed.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Andrea: 'Is Cotton Incorporated running any campaign messaging this year around fabric performance in heat or humidity — and is there a specific consumer segment or retail partner you're trying to move the needle on?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's weather-triggered ad units and contextual PMP inventory let Cotton serve performance-fabric messaging precisely when consumers are experiencing the conditions that make cotton's natural properties most relevant — rain, humidity, heat spikes. This is a direct extension of Cotton's 'The Fabric of Our Lives' brand positioning into a high-intent moment.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Cotton's prior TWC Video PMP deals were $100K each. A storm-season contextual package timed to May–August — Cotton's highest-relevance weather window — could be structured at $150–200K with weather-triggered delivery, giving Andrea a measurable lift story against their $548K paid social and $346K online video allocations.

Step 5 (Next Step): Send Andrea a one-page seasonal timing brief by [specific date 3 weeks out] showing TWC audience index data for cotton/apparel-relevant searches during rain and storm season, and request a 15-minute call to pressure-test the fit before GALE finalizes Cotton's 2H media plan.

talk_track_opener: Hey Robert, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We worked together on a couple of Cotton Incorporated programs back in 2023 — the Video PMP and Interactive packages that ran August through September. I know it's been a while, and I wanted to reconnect now because we've made some meaningful improvements to our contextual targeting and CTV capabilities that I think are directly relevant to where Cotton is spending today. Cotton's running $7.1M in CTV and $7.6M in digital — there's a real story to tell about how TWC fits into that mix, not as a standalone buy but as a contextual amplifier for the brand's core message. I'd love to get 20 minutes with you before GALE locks in Cotton's 2H plan. I noticed Cotton's prior TWC buys landed right at the peak of late-summer heat and humidity — that timing aligned really well with Cotton's fabric-performance story. We're heading into another rain and storm season, and I want to make sure Cotton has early access to our contextual inventory before it's committed. Can we find 20 minutes in the next couple of weeks to walk through what a refreshed program could look like?
open_questions: When Cotton ran the August and September 2023 TWC Video PMP deals, what KPIs were you measuring — and did those results factor into how digital video was allocated in 2024?
Who at Cotton Incorporated owns the final sign-off on digital and programmatic buys — does Andrea Samber have budget authority, or does it route through a broader marketing leadership team?
Is GALE currently managing Cotton's programmatic and PMP buys directly, or is there a separate trading desk or DSP relationship we should be aware of?
Cotton's online video allocation is $346K against a $7.6M digital budget — is there a strategic reason video has stayed small, or is that an area you're looking to grow in 2025?

Does Cotton Incorporated have any seasonal campaign windows tied to spring or summer retail activations — particularly around apparel or home goods partners — where weather-contextual targeting would be relevant?

sales_plays.title: CB2 & Land of Nod Brand Family Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Crate & Barrel is an active customer with two won deals in 2025, but current spend appears concentrated on the flagship brand. CB2 and Land of Nod represent untapped inventory against distinct audiences that PMG and YARD are not yet activating through TWC.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two active wins — the Google PD deal closed April 2025 and the PMP closed February 2025 — and open with: 'We've built a solid foundation for Crate & Barrel on TWC. I want to show Caroline and the PMG team how we can extend that same audience logic to CB2's younger-adult shopper and Land of Nod's parent segment, which are distinct from the flagship ICP.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Caroline Seklir at PMG: 'Are CB2 and Land of Nod currently on separate media plans, and is there a dedicated budget line for each brand, or are they rolled into the Crate & Barrel parent buy?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's lifestyle and home-interest audience segments as a natural fit for CB2's urban millennial renter profile and Land of Nod's 25-40 parent demo — audiences that behave differently on weather-influenced shopping days than the core Crate & Barrel buyer.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Crate & Barrel's total estimated spend is $91.9M, with $49.3M in digital. If CB2 and Land of Nod each represent even 10% of that digital budget ($4.9M each), TWC capturing a modest 2-3% share of each brand's digital spend would represent $200K-$300K in incremental revenue — well above the $1 PMP baseline currently on the books.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Caroline Seklir for a 30-minute brand-mapping session in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 30 minutes before end of May to walk through audience overlap across all three brands? I'll bring a one-pager showing how TWC indexes against CB2 and Land of Nod shoppers specifically.'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars with TWC Premium Inventory
sales_plays.summary: Crate & Barrel is spending $35.6M on Paid Social and $12.0M on Display — a combined $47.6M in digital channels with well-documented brand-safety and attention challenges. TWC offers a premium, contextually relevant alternative with measurable intent signals tied to home and lifestyle moments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with Janet Swanson Gill directly: 'Janet, I know lifecycle marketing owns a lot of the downstream engagement — I wanted to share something we're seeing with retail brands that are rebalancing away from social CPMs, which have climbed significantly in 2025, toward higher-attention environments. Given your $35.6M paid social footprint, I think there's a real conversation here.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Janet: 'When you look at your paid social performance for Crate & Barrel — particularly for tentpole moments like Memorial Day, back-to-college, or holiday — are you seeing the reach and attention quality you need, or is rising CPM pressure forcing trade-offs on frequency and creative format?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium CTV and on-property display inventory as a displacement candidate for a portion of the $12.0M display and $35.6M paid social budget — specifically for upper-funnel brand moments where contextual relevance (home, weather, seasonal living) outperforms algorithmic social targeting.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Crate & Barrel's current CTV spend is only $271K against a $49.3M digital base — that is less than 0.6% of digital in CTV. Comparable retail brands are allocating 5-8% of digital to CTV. Closing even half that gap would represent $2M-$2.5M in incremental CTV investment, a category where TWC has existing infrastructure and a won PMP relationship as proof of execution.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive audit: 'I can put together a two-page view showing where TWC inventory sits relative to your current social and display placements on brand-safety scores, attention metrics, and CPM efficiency. Can I get that to you and Caroline at PMG by the end of next week for a quick reaction?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Janet — this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with the PMG team on the Crate & Barrel PMP and the Google PD deal that closed earlier this year, so we have some real context on how your media is structured. I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a meaningful gap between what we're doing together today and what's possible given the scale of your overall program.
open_questions: Are CB2 and Land of Nod on separate budget lines within PMG's media plan, or are they consolidated under the Crate & Barrel parent brand buy?

Who at Crate & Barrel or PMG owns the decision to shift budget from paid social into premium display or CTV — is that Janet's call, or does it route through Caroline Seklir and the broader PMG strategy team?
Given that the Mobile App campaign lost in Q4 2023, what feedback came back from that evaluation — was it a pricing issue, an audience fit issue, or a measurement gap?
For retail tentpole moments like Memorial Day, back-to-college, and holiday, how far in advance is PMG locking in channel mix and creative formats — are Q3 and Q4 plans already in flight?

Is there a minimum CTV threshold Crate & Barrel needs to hit before it becomes a meaningful line item in the media plan, and what measurement currency would PMG require to justify scaling CTV spend beyond the current $271K?

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Antonia Lazar: 'For Credit Karma's automotive vertical campaigns, how are you currently planning around weather-driven demand spikes — like tax-refund season car buying or spring/summer purchase surges — and is that baked into your Q3 buy strategy yet?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-intent targeting as a direct upgrade path: Credit Karma's auto-finance audience (users checking credit scores before a car purchase) aligns precisely with weather-triggered automotive demand moments — something a PMP floor can't guarantee delivery against at scale.

sales_plays.title: Capture Paid Social Budget with Weather-Contextual Video
sales_plays.summary: Credit Karma is spending $18.3M in Paid Social — a channel with no weather-context signal and increasing CPM pressure. With $8.4M already in online video and active TWC buys, there is a clear displacement argument to shift a portion of social spend to contextually relevant, weather-triggered video inventory on TWC properties.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a data observation to Natasha Madan: 'Credit Karma is putting $18.3M into Paid Social, but social platforms can't tell you it's 28 degrees in Detroit and someone just searched their credit score before heading to a dealership. We can.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When your team evaluates Paid Social performance for the auto vertical, what's the primary KPI — is it reach, cost-per-click, or downstream credit application intent — and how is that benchmarked against your video buys?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's weather-contextual video inventory reaches consumers at the moment environmental conditions correlate with automotive purchase behavior — a targeting layer Paid Social platforms (Meta, TikTok) cannot replicate. Credit Karma's existing $8.4M online video allocation proves the format already works in their mix.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Shifting even 15% of the $18.3M Paid Social budget (~$2.75M) to TWC weather-contextual video would represent a 33x increase over current online video spend with TWC and deliver measurable incremental reach against high-intent auto-finance audiences.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to build a 1-page displacement brief for Antonia Lazar at Goodby, Silverstein & Partners showing side-by-side audience overlap and contextual relevance scores between their current Paid Social buys and TWC video — deliverable within one week, no commitment required.

talk_track_opener: Hey Natasha — I'm [rep name] from The Weather Channel. We've been working with Credit Karma on PMP buys since late 2024 and just closed our third deal together in May, so this isn't a cold call — I want to build on what's already working. We sit at the intersection of weather intelligence and consumer intent, which is a combination that's genuinely hard to replicate on other platforms. Your team at Goodby has been great to work with and I want to make sure we're bringing the full picture to your planning conversations. I was looking at Credit Karma's media mix and noticed you're running $18.3M in Paid Social alongside $8.4M in online video — and given how much of your automotive audience is making credit and financing decisions tied to seasonal purchase moments, I think there's a real gap we could close. I'd love to ask: when your team is planning automotive campaigns for Q3, how are you currently accounting for weather-driven demand windows — like the spring car-buying surge or back-to-school financing season — and is that something Antonia's team is building into the brief yet?
open_questions: The three PMP buys have all been through TWC Interactive — is there a specific performance metric that's kept Credit Karma coming back, and has that been formally benchmarked against other digital channels?
Who at Credit Karma owns the decision to move from PMP to a direct or reserved buy — is that Natasha Madan directly, or does Antonia Lazar at Goodby have authority to recommend that shift?
Credit Karma's Paid Social spend is $18.3M — how is that budget performing against the automotive vertical specifically, and is there any pressure to diversify away from Meta or TikTok CPM increases?

With Bombora showing Entertainment intent this week, is Credit Karma planning a campaign that blends lifestyle content with financial messaging, and would that open a new budget line separate from the automotive PMP buys?
