sales_plays.title: From PMP to Premium Direct: Unlock Crocs' Full Weather Audience
sales_plays.summary: Crocs holds active PMP deals (2024, 2025, 2026 wins at $1 each) but has never converted to a meaningful direct buy. Two consecutive $50K Marquee losses in Q1 2024 and Q1 2025 signal budget exists but the value case hasn't landed. This play converts the PMP relationship into a premium, on-property direct buy before Q2 sandal season planning locks.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three consecutive PMP wins (2024, 2025, 2026) with Ali Amarsy at Digitas and acknowledge the ongoing relationship. Open with: 'Ali, we've had active PMP deals running with Crocs three years straight — I want to show you what audiences you're leaving on the table by staying in the PMP lane versus going direct with us ahead of sandal season.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Ali: 'When Digitas is planning Crocs' Q2 warm-weather push, how are you currently using weather-triggered signals to decide when and where to activate the $53M digital budget — and is TWC part of that conversation or just a remnant channel?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's on-property premium inventory as the missing link between Crocs' $38.9M paid social spend (broad reach, low intent) and a high-intent weather-moment audience actively checking forecasts before outdoor activity and footwear decisions. TWC delivers the consumer at the exact moment weather makes Crocs relevant.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The two lost Marquee opportunities totaled $100K in unconverted pipeline. Crocs' total media footprint is $86.6M — TWC's current share via PMP is effectively $0 in real spend. Even a 0.5% reallocation from the $38.9M paid social budget equals $194K in incremental TWC direct revenue. Frame the ask around a pilot that is a rounding error against their social line.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Ali and loop in Terence Reilly's team for a 30-minute 'Weather Moment Planning' session in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 30 minutes with you and whoever owns Crocs' Q2 tentpole calendar before end of month to map weather triggers to your sandal launch windows? I'll bring audience data specific to warm-weather footwear intent.'

sales_plays.title: Displace Social Dollars: Weather-Intent vs. Scroll-Intent
sales_plays.summary: Crocs is allocating $38.9M to paid social — the single largest line in their $53M digital budget. Social delivers broad reach but low purchase-intent context. This play challenges Digitas to reallocate a portion of that social spend toward TWC's weather-triggered, high-intent environment, where Crocs' casual/outdoor positioning is contextually native.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a data provocation to Ali Amarsy at Digitas: 'Crocs is putting $38.9M into paid social this year. I want to show you a context comparison — what a Crocs ad looks like next to a weather alert for a 75-degree weekend versus what it looks like between two competitor posts in a social feed. One of those moments has purchase intent baked in.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When Digitas evaluates the $38.9M social allocation for Crocs, what KPIs are you optimizing against — and is contextual relevance or weather-moment alignment part of the measurement framework at all?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's on-property placements reach consumers actively planning outdoor activity around weather — the exact behavioral state that makes someone consider buying Crocs. Unlike social, where Crocs competes for attention in a cluttered feed, TWC delivers Crocs into a moment of active environmental decision-making. This is programmatic-to-direct and off-property-to-on-property displacement in one move.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Crocs' $544K online video spend and $1.6M display spend suggest they already test smaller contextual formats. A $250K–$500K reallocation from the $38.9M social budget (less than 1.3%) into TWC premium direct would represent a 15–30x increase over current TWC engagement and give Digitas a clean A/B story: social intent vs. weather intent, same creative, measurable lift.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive context audit: 'Let me pull a two-week snapshot of how Crocs' category competitors are using weather-triggered placements versus social. I can have that to you by [specific date 7 days out] — would that be a useful input for Digitas' Q2 channel mix review with Terence's team?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Ali, I'm [rep name] from The Weather Company — we've been working with your team at Digitas on the Crocs PMP for three consecutive years now, 2024 through 2026, so you know we're not a cold call. We're embedded in the buy, and I want to make sure we're actually delivering the value Crocs' brand deserves at their scale. Here's what caught my attention: Crocs is running $38.9M through paid social and another $27.8M in search — that's a massive investment in high-volume, lower-context environments. Meanwhile, the TWC relationship is sitting at the PMP level with no real direct activation, and two Marquee opportunities in Q1 2024 and Q1 2025 didn't close. I'd love to understand what the hesitation was there, because from where I sit, a brand built around outdoor casual wear has a natural home in weather-moment media. Before Digitas locks the Q2 channel mix for Crocs' sandal season, can we get 30 minutes to map weather triggers to their tentpole calendar and see if there's a smarter place for even a small slice of that social budget?
open_questions: The Q1 Marquee opportunities in 2024 and 2025 both came close but didn't close at $50K — what was the specific objection from Digitas or Crocs' team that killed those deals, and has anything changed in how you evaluate contextual media for Crocs?

Who at Crocs — Terence Reilly's team or someone else — owns the final sign-off on channel mix decisions above the PMP level, and does Digitas have autonomy to recommend a direct buy or does it require brand-side approval?

With $38.9M going into paid social for Crocs, how is Digitas currently measuring contextual relevance or weather-moment alignment as a KPI, and is there a framework in place to compare social intent versus environmental intent?

Is Crocs planning any Q2 or summer tentpole campaigns — sandal launches, cultural moments, collaborations — where weather-triggered activation would be part of the media brief, and when does that planning process start internally?

Crocs operates globally — is the $86.6M spend figure U.S.-only, and are there specific domestic markets or DMAs where weather sensitivity is highest and where a geo-targeted TWC buy would make the most sense to pilot?

sales_plays.title: Shift Dannon's $15.5M Paid Social to Premium On-Property Video
sales_plays.summary: Dannon is over-indexed in Paid Social ($15.5M) relative to Online Video ($391K) and CTV ($12.6M). This play targets the imbalance by positioning premium on-property video inventory as a higher-quality, brand-safe alternative that delivers the reach and engagement Paid Social promises but increasingly underdelivers for a family-friendly CPG brand.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Archana Deshmukh at Wavemaker (AOR for Media Planning & Buying) with a one-page audit framing: 'Dannon is spending roughly 40x more in Paid Social ($15.5M) than Online Video ($391K) — we see a rebalancing opportunity that could improve brand-safe reach for Oikos, Light + Fit, and Danimals without increasing total spend above the current $35.3M run rate.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Archana: 'When Wavemaker evaluates Dannon's Paid Social allocation against brand safety and viewability benchmarks, where are the biggest gaps in verified, premium video reach for the yogurt portfolio?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position on-property premium video and CTV adjacency as the direct answer — brand-controlled environments, verified audiences, and content alignment with Dannon's family and wellness positioning that Paid Social feeds cannot guarantee.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the shift concretely: reallocating even 10% of the $15.5M Paid Social budget ($1.55M) into on-property video would represent a 4x increase over Dannon's current $391K Online Video spend, with measurable lift in brand recall metrics we can benchmark against their existing $12.6M CTV investment.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute working session with Archana and Maren Zanotti (Director, Brand Management) in the next two weeks to walk through a channel mix model specific to Dannon's yogurt portfolio — offer to bring a CPG vertical case study showing comparable reallocation results.

sales_plays.title: Leverage Wavemaker Network to Open Dannon via Peer CPG Reference
sales_plays.summary: Since Dannon is net-new with no CRM history, a cold outreach to Archana Deshmukh at Wavemaker carries risk of low response. This play uses a warm referral path — identifying a peer CPG brand already active on our platform and also managed by Wavemaker — to create a credible, relationship-first introduction before any direct pitch.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Identify one existing Wavemaker-managed CPG client on your active roster and ask your internal account team or Wavemaker relationship owner: 'Would your team be open to a brief intro connecting Dannon's planning team to how [peer CPG brand] is using our platform for their yogurt or dairy-adjacent campaigns?' Frame it as a peer learning conversation, not a sales call.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): In the warm intro conversation with Archana, ask: 'As you're planning Dannon's media mix for the next retail tentpole window, what's the one channel where you feel the brand's $35.3M spend is hardest to attribute back to in-store lift or purchase intent?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Use the peer CPG reference point to validate that brands with similar Paid Social-heavy mixes ($15.5M in Dannon's case) have found measurable incrementality by adding on-property inventory — let the referral do the credibility work before you present a formal proposal.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Reference Dannon's own numbers to make it tangible: 'If the approach that worked for [peer brand] applied to even a portion of Dannon's $19.5M digital budget, we'd be looking at a test that fits well within existing Wavemaker planning cycles and doesn't require a net-new budget conversation with Maren's team.'

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Archana for a 20-minute intro call with Maren Zanotti (Director, Brand Management) framed as a 'channel planning conversation' — not a vendor pitch — ideally timed 6-8 weeks ahead of Dannon's next retail tentpole planning cycle so it lands as a planning resource, not a reactive ask.

talk_track_opener: Hey Archana, I'm [Rep Name] from [Company] — we work with Wavemaker across several CPG portfolios on premium video and on-property inventory solutions. I wanted to reach out specifically about the Dannon business because we've been looking at how brands in the yogurt and dairy space are allocating their digital mix heading into key retail windows.
open_questions: When Wavemaker evaluates Dannon's Paid Social performance against brand safety and viewability standards, what benchmarks are you currently using — and where are the biggest gaps?
Who at Dannon — Maren Zanotti or someone else on her team — owns the final decision on channel mix shifts within the existing $35.3M budget, and how does that approval process work with Wavemaker?
How is Dannon currently measuring the incremental contribution of the $15.5M Paid Social investment relative to the $12.6M CTV spend in terms of purchase intent or in-store lift?
Is Wavemaker planning Dannon's next retail tentpole or cultural-moment campaign now, and is there an open brief where a premium on-property video test could be scoped in?

Are there other Wavemaker-managed CPG clients in adjacent categories — dairy, snacks, beverages — where you've seen on-property video outperform Paid Social that we could use as a reference point for a Dannon conversation?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Direct Upgrade Across Brand Portfolio
sales_plays.summary: Darden is an active customer with a won PMP deal in July 2024. With $41.2M in digital spend and brands like Olive Garden, LongHorn, and Cheddar's each running distinct campaigns, there is clear whitespace to expand from the existing PMP relationship into direct, premium inventory across multiple brand lines.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the July 2024 PMP win with TWC Interactive and open with performance: 'We closed your first deal with us mid-2024 — I want to share what we're seeing from that buy and talk about how we can build on it across your broader brand portfolio, starting with Olive Garden and LongHorn, which are your two heaviest spenders.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Right now, is Havas buying your digital inventory separately by brand, or is there a unified digital strategy across Darden's full restaurant portfolio — and who at Havas owns that consolidation conversation?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): 'Your $41.2M digital footprint is fragmented across paid social, display, mobile app, and online video — with display at only $1.1M, there's a significant gap in premium contextual environments. A direct buy gives Havas more control, better brand safety, and audience alignment that a PMP floor can't guarantee.'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'If we shift even 10% of your $41.2M digital budget — roughly $4.1M — from open programmatic into direct premium placements, we're talking about a meaningful lift in viewability and reach quality. Your CTV line is already at $35.3M, which tells us Darden is comfortable with premium video investment. Let's extend that logic to digital.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we set up a 30-minute working session with you and Bre Rossetti at Havas Boston in the next two weeks? I'll bring a portfolio-level proposal showing how we'd structure buys across at least three Darden brands — no commitment, just a concrete starting point.'

sales_plays.title: Displace Display Incumbents with Premium Contextual
sales_plays.summary: Darden's Internet Display spend is only $1.1M out of $41.2M in total digital — a signal that current display partners are underperforming or underutilized. This play targets the gap by displacing low-quality display incumbents with a premium contextual solution tied to Darden's dining and lifestyle audiences.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the data anomaly: 'Laura, I was looking at Darden's media mix and something stood out — your display investment is $1.1M against $41.2M in total digital. That's less than 3%. Either display isn't working for you, or it's been deprioritized. I'd love to understand which one it is before we talk about what we can do differently.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Who are your current display partners, and what KPIs is Havas using to evaluate them — is it click-through, viewability, or something tied to foot traffic or reservation lift?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): 'If display has been underperforming, the likely culprit is audience context — generic display networks don't put Olive Garden or LongHorn in front of people who are actively planning a dining occasion. Our contextual targeting puts Darden's brands in front of food, lifestyle, and local intent audiences at the moment of decision, not after the fact.'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Your broadcast spend is $89.4M and CTV is $35.3M — you're clearly investing in reach. A $3-5M reallocation from underperforming display incumbents into premium contextual display would represent less than 2% of your total $174.5M media budget, but could meaningfully improve digital attribution across your 2,100+ locations.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Let me put together a one-page competitive audit showing what your current display dollars are likely buying versus what a premium contextual buy looks like for a brand like Olive Garden. I can have that to you and Bre Rossetti at Havas by end of next week — does that work?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Laura, I'm [Rep Name] from [Company] — we've been working with Darden since mid-2024 when we closed your first PMP deal through Havas. I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a bigger opportunity here that we haven't fully explored together yet. We've seen strong results from that initial buy and I'd love to show you what's possible at the portfolio level across your brand family.
open_questions: Is Havas buying digital inventory separately by Darden brand — Olive Garden, LongHorn, Cheddar's — or is there a consolidated digital strategy across the full portfolio that Bre Rossetti's team manages?
Who owns the decision to move spend from programmatic PMP into direct buys — is that Laura Israel at Darden, Havas, or a joint approval process?
What KPIs is Darden using to evaluate display performance — viewability, foot traffic attribution, reservation lift — and are current display partners hitting those benchmarks?
Given that display is only $1.1M of your $41.2M digital budget, has that channel been actively tested and deprioritized, or has it simply not been a focus in recent planning cycles?

With Darden's CTV investment at $35.3M, is there an appetite to align digital display and online video buys with the same premium audience strategy you're using in CTV, or are those channels managed with different goals?

This play converts that initial PMP relationship into a broader direct buy across CTV, high-impact display, and branded content to capture a larger share of their $9.8M digital and $721K CTV budgets.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Melissa Wisehart at PHD USA referencing the February 2025 PMP win and note that David Yurman's $721K CTV investment signals appetite for premium video — open with: 'Melissa, the TWC Interactive PMP we activated in February is a great foundation. I want to show you how a direct CTV and high-impact digital package could amplify what that PMP started, especially ahead of key retail tentpoles like Mother's Day and Holiday.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Melissa: 'Of David Yurman's $9.8M digital budget, how much is currently allocated to premium contextual environments versus open exchange or programmatic — and is Carolyn's team looking to shift more toward brand-safe, curated placements this planning cycle?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's owned-and-operated weather and lifestyle inventory as a natural fit for David Yurman's affluent, gift-occasion-driven buyer — weather-triggered creative can reach high-intent shoppers planning seasonal purchases (Valentine's Day, Mother's Day, Holiday) at the exact moment context is most relevant.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): David Yurman currently has $140K in Internet Display and $1.1M in Online Video with us. A direct premium package targeting 2x that Online Video commitment ($2.2M) plus incremental high-impact display would represent a meaningful but achievable step-up from the current PMP baseline, staying well within their $9.8M digital envelope.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Melissa for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar before end of April to walk through a custom package proposal for Q3 and Holiday? I'll bring audience data and a draft flight plan so you can pressure-test it with Carolyn's team.'

sales_plays.title: Reclaim the Search Budget: Shift Intent Dollars to Premium Brand Environments
sales_plays.summary: David Yurman's $16.5M Search budget dwarfs every other channel and likely funds heavy Google/Bing spend capturing bottom-funnel demand. This play challenges PHD USA to reconsider whether that allocation is over-indexed on conversion at the expense of upper-funnel brand equity, and positions TWC as a premium mid-funnel environment that drives the intent Search is trying to harvest.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Carolyn Dawkins directly with a brief, CMO-level observation: 'Carolyn, David Yurman's search investment — which we estimate at $16.5M — is doing a great job capturing demand, but the brands that win in luxury jewelry are also the ones creating that demand upstream. I'd love to share a quick POV on how weather-moment targeting is driving mid-funnel intent for luxury retail peers before the Holiday planning cycle locks.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Carolyn: 'When you look at your $16.5M search spend, how much of that is truly incremental demand versus capturing intent that your brand and social campaigns already created — and is there internal pressure to prove brand-level ROI beyond last-click attribution?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium contextual inventory as the mid-funnel bridge that makes Search more efficient — when affluent consumers see David Yurman messaging in a high-attention, weather-relevant moment (gifting season, cold-weather jewelry occasions), they search with higher purchase intent, lowering CPAs downstream and giving Carolyn a brand equity story alongside performance metrics.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Even a 3-5% reallocation of the $16.5M Search budget ($495K–$825K) into a TWC premium brand package would represent a 68x–114x increase over the current $7K PMP baseline, and would be a rounding error against Search spend while meaningfully funding a full-funnel test with measurable lift data.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer a no-commitment attribution analysis: 'I can pull together a 1-pager showing how luxury retail advertisers on our platform have used mid-funnel placements to reduce search CPAs. Can I send that to you and Melissa Wisehart at PHD by end of this week so it's in hand before your next planning review?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Melissa, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with PHD and the David Yurman team since the TWC Interactive PMP closed in February, and I wanted to reach out now — before Q3 and Holiday planning gets locked — because I think there's a real opportunity to build on that foundation in a meaningful way. We work with a number of luxury retail and jewelry brands, and the results we're seeing when premium contextual environments are paired with gift-occasion moments are genuinely strong. I wanted to share some of that with you directly.
open_questions: Melissa, of David Yurman's estimated $9.8M digital budget, how much is currently flowing through open programmatic versus curated PMPs or direct buys — and is there a mandate from Carolyn's team to shift more toward premium, brand-safe environments this year?

Carolyn, when you look at the $16.5M Search investment, how are you currently measuring whether that spend is creating new demand versus harvesting intent that your brand campaigns upstream already generated?

Melissa, now that the TWC Interactive PMP is live, who at PHD or David Yurman owns the decision to expand that relationship into a direct or guaranteed buy — is that a joint call between you and Carolyn, or does it sit with the brand team?
Is David Yurman currently running any CTV or online video outside of what's placed through PHD USA, and are there any direct publisher relationships the brand team manages independently of the AOR?

As you plan for Holiday 2025, what does David Yurman's ideal upper-funnel media mix look like — and are there specific audience segments or geographic markets where Carolyn is looking to grow brand awareness among new luxury buyers?

steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Lisa Horton and CC Angela Tran at ASTRSK referencing the two consecutive TWC Interactive PMP wins in 2024 and 2025. Even a modest shift of 5% of their digital budget ($675K) into CTV and premium on-property video would represent a 16x increase in CTV presence heading into the highest-intent bridal gifting period.

sales_plays.title: Displace Mobile App Spend to Premium Programmatic Direct
sales_plays.summary: David's Bridal is concentrating $3.5M of their digital budget in Mobile App — a format that often runs through third-party ad networks with limited brand safety and audience transparency. This play challenges that allocation and repositions TWC as a premium programmatic-direct alternative with verified weather and lifestyle audience data.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the Bombora signal showing David's Bridal is actively researching Retail Technology this week. Open with: 'I noticed your team is evaluating retail technology solutions right now — that often signals a broader look at media efficiency and measurement. I want to share something specific to your $3.5M mobile app investment.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Lisa Horton: 'Of the $3.5M currently running in mobile app, how much is going through open exchange versus direct or PMP deals — and are you getting the audience verification and brand-safe context you need for a brand in post-bankruptcy recovery mode?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's programmatic direct and PMP inventory as a brand-safe, data-rich alternative to open mobile exchange spend. David's Bridal's core customer — brides, prom shoppers, quinceañera families — is highly weather-aware and seasonally driven. TWC's first-party weather intent data can target these audiences more precisely than generic mobile app networks, with full transparency.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Their existing TWC PMP deals have won twice at a combined $2 in recorded SFDC revenue — suggesting test-level budgets. Redirecting even 10% of the $3.5M mobile app spend ($350K) into a TWC programmatic direct deal would represent a 175x increase over current TWC investment and deliver measurable lift against a verified bridal and formal-wear audience.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a side-by-side performance comparison — offer to run a 30-day TWC programmatic direct test against a matched mobile app segment, with a shared measurement framework. Ask Angela Tran to confirm the right timing to present this to Lisa Horton before the holiday planning cycle locks.

talk_track_opener: Hey Angela, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've had a great run with David's Bridal — two consecutive PMP wins in 2024 and 2025 — and I want to make sure we're positioned well heading into their biggest seasonal window before budgets get locked. I work with a number of bridal and formal-wear advertisers and the holiday and gifting lead-in is consistently their highest-intent period, so I'd love to get ahead of that together. I was looking at David's Bridal's current media mix and noticed they're running $3.5M in mobile app and only $40K in CTV — and our Bombora signals show their team is actively researching retail technology right now, which tells me there's a real appetite for evaluating what's working. Given the two PMP wins we already have on the books, I think there's a natural conversation to have about expanding into weather-triggered CTV and premium on-property formats for the holiday window. Would you have 20 minutes this week or next to look at a proposal together before their Q4 planning cycle closes?

sales_plays.title: UGG-to-HOKA Brand Family Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Deckers is an active customer with a won PMP deal and a history of UGG-focused engagement. With $226M in total media spend and HOKA representing a high-growth performance brand, there is clear whitespace to expand from UGG activations into a multi-brand TWC strategy before fall/winter tentpole planning locks.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the active PMP relationship and the Q4 2024 Holiday Pulse loss. Email Cori Mires and copy Emily Malone at Jellyfish: 'We know UGG and TWC audiences align strongly — we want to show you how HOKA's run and trail audience maps to our weather-driven intent data before your Q3 planning cycle closes.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Emily Malone: 'Is HOKA being planned separately from UGG at Jellyfish, and is there a unified Deckers brand calendar we should be building against for Q4 and holiday tentpoles?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered audience segments as a natural fit for both UGG (cold-weather lifestyle) and HOKA (active outdoor, trail running) — two distinct audience moments on one platform, reducing fragmentation across Deckers' $58.6M digital budget.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Deckers spends $30.7M in Paid Social and $9.9M in Display. Even a 5% reallocation of those two lines toward TWC's premium digital inventory equals roughly $2M in incremental spend — frame this as consolidation efficiency, not new budget.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute brand planning call with Cori Mires and Emily Malone in the next two weeks to map HOKA and UGG seasonal moments to TWC audience availability before Jellyfish finalizes Q3/Q4 flowcharts.

sales_plays.title: Holiday Pulse Win-Back via Direct Displacement
sales_plays.summary: The Holiday Pulse opportunity was lost in October 2024. With $24.7M in CTV and $91.7M in Broadcast spend, Deckers is investing heavily in premium video. This play targets displacing a competitor holding that holiday budget by re-engaging before the Q3 planning window closes.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the lost Holiday Pulse opp directly with Emily Malone at Jellyfish: 'We lost the Holiday Pulse conversation in October — I want to understand what won that budget so we can come back with a stronger case for this cycle. Deckers' $91.7M broadcast investment tells us holiday video is a priority.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Emily Malone: 'What did the winning partner offer on the Holiday Pulse that TWC didn't — was it reach, targeting, format, or pricing — and is that budget already committed for Q4 2025?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's CTV + weather-trigger combination as a direct displacement of whoever won the Holiday Pulse budget — specifically for UGG's cold-weather demand window, where TWC's first-party weather intent data creates a targeting advantage no general CTV platform can replicate.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Deckers already spends $24.7M in CTV. If TWC captures even 10% of that line for a weather-triggered UGG holiday flight, that is $2.47M — and it comes with measurable lift data to justify renewal, unlike the incumbent who won on price alone.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive comparison session — 45 minutes with Cori Mires and Emily Malone — where TWC presents a side-by-side of last year's Holiday Pulse proposal versus a revised Q4 2025 package, targeting a meeting before end of current quarter.

talk_track_opener: Hey Cori, I'm reaching out from The Weather Channel — we've been working with Deckers through Jellyfish on the PMP side, and I wanted to connect directly because I think there's a bigger story we haven't told yet. We have an active relationship, but given Deckers' scale — over $226 million in total media spend across broadcast, CTV, and digital — I think we're only touching a small piece of what's possible, especially as you head into fall and holiday planning. What caught my attention is that HOKA and UGG have very different seasonal demand windows, but both are deeply tied to weather — cold snaps drive UGG intent, and trail and run conditions drive HOKA. TWC sits right at that intersection with first-party weather data and audience targeting that no other platform has. We lost the Holiday Pulse conversation last October, and I'd love to understand what we missed so we can come back stronger. Is there 30 minutes on your calendar this month to walk through how we'd map both brands to our Q4 audience availability before Jellyfish locks the flowcharts?
open_questions: When Jellyfish lost the Holiday Pulse budget to a competitor in October 2024, what platform or format won that spend — and is that partner already on the plan for Q4 2025?
Is HOKA being planned on a separate budget and calendar from UGG at Jellyfish, or is there a unified Deckers brand planning process Emily Malone oversees?
The two HOKA opportunities at TWC in 2023 and 2024 both closed as losses — was that a targeting fit issue, a pricing issue, or a timing issue on our end?

sales_plays.title: PMP to Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Dell has renewed TWC Interactive PMP deals three consecutive years (2024, 2025, 2026). This play converts that low-dollar PMP relationship into a meaningful direct buy by surfacing premium inventory and audience capabilities that PMP pipes cannot access.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three consecutive PMP renewals with Donald Heath or Elizabeth Lopez — 'Dell has been a consistent TWC Interactive PMP partner since 2023, and I want to show you what sits above the PMP layer that your $253M digital budget could unlock directly.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Elizabeth Lopez at EssenceMediaCom: 'Of Dell's $63.6M in Display and $37.3M in Online Video, how much is currently flowing through open or curated PMP versus direct IO, and where are you seeing the biggest CPM efficiency gaps?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's direct premium inventory — contextual weather-moment targeting, exclusive homepage and high-impact units — as the layer that PMP bids routinely lose or underprice, directly addressing CPM volatility in Dell's display and OLV lines.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Dell spends $100.9M combined across Display ($63.6M) and Online Video ($37.3M). Even a 5% shift of that combined spend to direct premium IO represents ~$5M in incremental revenue and gives Dell guaranteed placement quality that the current $1 PMP deal value does not reflect.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Elizabeth Lopez for a 30-minute inventory walkthrough before end of current quarter — 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar this month so I can show you the specific units and audience segments that sit outside what your current PMP setup can reach? I'll bring a one-pager mapped to Dell's display and OLV lines.'

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Budget Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Dell allocates $75.3M to Paid Social — the single largest digital sub-channel. This play challenges the efficiency and brand-safety assumptions behind that allocation and positions TWC Interactive's premium contextual environment as a higher-quality alternative for a portion of that spend.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a data observation to Donald Heath: 'Dell is running $75.3M through Paid Social — more than double your Online Video spend. I want to have an honest conversation about what that environment delivers for a brand like Dell versus what a premium contextual buy can do.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When EssenceMediaCom evaluates the $75.3M Paid Social line, what KPIs are you optimizing against — is it reach, lower-funnel conversion, or brand equity metrics — and are those benchmarks being hit consistently?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's weather-contextual and high-intent audience environment against Paid Social's brand-safety and attention concerns — Dell's tech and printing products (Donald Heath's scope) benefit from contextual relevance that social feeds cannot replicate at scale.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Redirecting even 8% of Dell's $75.3M Paid Social budget (~$6M) into TWC Interactive premium digital would represent a 6x increase over the current PMP relationship value and deliver measurable brand-safe impressions against in-market tech buyers.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a head-to-head measurement pilot to Donald Heath — 'Would Dell be open to a Q3 test where we run a matched audience segment across TWC Interactive premium inventory alongside your current Paid Social buy and compare attention and brand recall scores? I can have a proposal to Elizabeth at EssenceMediaCom within the week.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Donald, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company Interactive. We've been working with Dell for three consecutive years on the TWC Interactive PMP — renewals in 2024, 2025, and most recently January 2026 — so you know we're already part of the media mix. I wanted to reach out because I think there's a meaningful gap between what that PMP relationship reflects on paper and what we could actually be doing together given Dell's scale.
open_questions: Of Dell's $63.6M Display and $37.3M Online Video budgets, how much is currently allocated to direct IO versus PMP or open exchange — and is EssenceMediaCom looking to shift that ratio in the next planning cycle?

Who at Dell or EssenceMediaCom owns the decision to move budget from Paid Social into premium contextual or display environments — is that Donald Heath's call, or does it sit with a broader brand team?
What KPIs is Dell using to evaluate the $75.3M Paid Social investment, and are those benchmarks being met consistently across the Dell Printing & Imaging line specifically?
Is EssenceMediaCom currently planning any retail tentpole or back-to-school campaigns for Dell that would benefit from weather-contextual or high-intent audience targeting in Q3?

The current PMP deals have renewed at nominal value three years running — has there been an internal conversation about what a more strategic, direct relationship with TWC Interactive could look like at a spend level that reflects Dell's actual digital scale?

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar with you and the PHD USA team before end of April to walk through a summer heat activation proposal for Smirnoff — before upfront commitments lock in?'

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Budget Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Diageo allocates $156.1M to Paid Social — by far the largest single digital line item — managed through Dentsu X as AOR Digital. With Bombora signaling Entertainment intent this week, there is an opening to position TWC's premium video and contextual display as a performance alternative that displaces a slice of that Paid Social spend toward higher-quality, brand-safe environments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Rob Walker at PHD USA via Dentsu X's AOR Digital relationship: 'I noticed Diageo is showing active interest in Entertainment content this week per our intent data. With $156M flowing through Paid Social, I want to share how brands in CPG are reallocating 5–10% of that budget to premium contextual video to improve brand safety scores and reduce wasted impressions — and I think there's a specific angle for Diageo's portfolio.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When Dentsu X and PHD USA evaluate the Paid Social mix today, what are the top two performance concerns — is it brand safety, audience quality, rising CPMs, or something else — and is there any internal pressure from Edward Pilkington's team to diversify away from walled gardens?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): 'TWC's premium display ($7.7M Internet Display benchmark in your category) and Online Video ($9.6M) environments offer brand-safe, contextually relevant placements tied to weather and lifestyle moments — directly relevant to spirits consumption occasions. This is not a replacement for Paid Social scale, but a premium complement that protects brand equity while Paid Social handles reach.'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'A 3% reallocation of the $156.1M Paid Social budget equals roughly $4.7M. Even a 1% test — $1.5M — would make Diageo one of TWC's top CPG partners and give PHD USA a clean A/B data set to bring back to Edward Pilkington showing incremental reach and brand safety lift versus the walled garden baseline.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I can put together a one-page displacement model showing estimated reach, CPM benchmarks, and brand safety metrics versus Paid Social norms — can I send that to you and the Dentsu X team by end of this week for a quick reaction before your next planning cycle?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Rob, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company — we've been working with the PHD USA and Diageo team on the PMP partnership that's been active since 2024, and I wanted to reach out personally ahead of the summer season. We have a strong foundation together, and I think there's a real opportunity to build on it before the warm-weather window opens. The last time we had a Smirnoff summer activation on the table it was a $250K opportunity that didn't come together in time — I don't want that to happen again this year. On the displacement side, I've been looking at Diageo's digital mix and the $156M in Paid Social is a significant concentration. We're seeing CPG brands proactively carve out a small slice of that for premium contextual environments — not to replace scale, but to protect brand equity and add a weather-intent layer that social platforms simply can't offer. Given that your team is showing active interest in Entertainment content right now per our intent signals, the timing feels right. Would you be open to a 30-minute call this month to look at both angles — expanding the summer activation and exploring what a small Paid Social reallocation test could look like for the Diageo portfolio?
open_questions: The Smirnoff Summer 2025 direct buy didn't close — was that a timing issue, a budget reallocation, or a performance concern we should address in a 2026 proposal?
For the 2026 summer season, has PHD USA already locked in media commitments for Smirnoff and other warm-weather spirits brands, or is there still open budget being planned?

With $156M flowing through Paid Social managed by Dentsu X, is there any internal conversation at Diageo or PHD USA about diversifying away from walled gardens — either for brand safety or CPM efficiency reasons?
Edward Pilkington owns Marketing and Innovation — is the push for new channel testing coming from his office, or is it more of a PHD USA/Dentsu X-led initiative at the planning level?

Given Diageo's active Entertainment intent signal this week, is there a specific brand or campaign in the portfolio that's being planned around entertainment or cultural moments we should be building a proposal around?

sales_plays.title: PMP Win to Premium Inventory Expansion
sales_plays.summary: DICK'S has an active PMP relationship (won Jan 2026) but four consecutive lost opportunities totaling $950K suggest the programmatic entry point has not converted to broader premium or direct buys. This play leverages the live PMP as a proof point to expand into higher-impact, on-property inventory before key retail tentpoles.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the active PMP deal closed January 3, 2026 and congratulate the team on getting it live. Tell Emily Silver or Jason Xenopoulos at VML that you want to share early performance data from the PMP and use it as a springboard to discuss Q2/Q3 planning — specifically where Weather Channel inventory can amplify DICK'S $102M digital footprint during spring outdoor and back-to-school windows.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jason Xenopoulos at VML: 'Now that the PMP is running, what KPIs is Emily's team using to evaluate whether to scale programmatic into direct or premium formats — and who owns that decision for the spring and summer planning cycle?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's on-property premium video and contextual weather-triggered display as the natural next step above the PMP floor — connecting DICK'S outdoor gear and athletic apparel assortment to consumers actively checking conditions before a run, hike, or game. The $998K online video line and $3M display line signal underinvestment in premium digital environments relative to the $34.8M paid social spend.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The four lost opps from Spring through Holidays 2025 totaled $950K in unawarded budget. Reframing even 50% of that as a premium direct buy alongside the live PMP would represent a 475x revenue expansion over the current $1 PMP deal value and bring TWC closer to earning a proportional share of the $311.9M total media budget.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jason at VML for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to review PMP delivery data together and map a Q2 premium package proposal — frame it as a planning input for Emily's team before the spring outdoor season demand window opens.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars with Weather-Contextual Video
sales_plays.summary: DICK'S allocates $34.8M to paid social — the single largest digital line item — while online video sits at just $998K. This imbalance, combined with four consecutive lost TWC proposals, suggests budget is flowing to social incumbents. This play challenges that allocation by positioning TWC premium video as a higher-intent, lower-clutter alternative for performance-driven retail campaigns.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a data observation to Emily Silver: 'We noticed DICK'S is investing $34.8M in paid social versus under $1M in online video. That ratio is unusual for a retailer with $108.7M in broadcast spend — it suggests social is doing heavy lifting that premium video environments might do more efficiently. I'd love to share what we're seeing from comparable sporting goods and outdoor retail advertisers on TWC.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Emily: 'When your team evaluates paid social performance against brand and conversion goals, what's the biggest gap you're trying to close — reach, contextual relevance, or cost per outcome — and is there appetite to test a reallocation into premium video this year?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered video units as a direct displacement of social feed inventory: higher attention, contextual alignment to outdoor activity intent, and no algorithm dependency. DICK'S sells gear for weather-dependent activities — running, hiking, team sports, skiing — making weather-moment targeting a natural fit that social platforms cannot replicate.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Shifting even 5% of the $34.8M paid social budget ($1.74M) into TWC premium video would represent a 1,740x increase over the current $998K online video line and give DICK'S a meaningful test of contextual video at scale. Against a $311.9M total spend base, this is a low-risk reallocation with measurable incrementality.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive audit — offer to build a two-page media mix analysis showing how TWC video complements (not replaces) DICK'S existing social and broadcast investment. Ask Emily or Jason Xenopoulos at VML for a 20-minute call within the next 10 days to validate the framing before you invest in building it.

talk_track_opener: Hey Emily — I'm [rep name] from The Weather Channel. We've been working with your team and VML on the programmatic side, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a bigger conversation worth having. We have an active PMP running as of January, and I'd love to use that as a foundation to talk about what's next for DICK'S across our premium inventory. Here's what caught my attention: DICK'S is putting $34.8M into paid social and under $1M into online video, while running $108.7M in broadcast — that's a mix that tells me there may be a gap in contextual digital video that we're well-positioned to fill. Weather is literally the trigger for most of what DICK'S sells — people check the forecast before they run, hike, ski, or coach a game. I'd love to ask you one question: when your team is planning the spring and summer tentpole moments, how are you thinking about connecting those weather-dependent purchase triggers to your digital video strategy?
open_questions: Now that the PMP is live, what performance benchmarks is Emily's team using to decide whether to scale it or shift budget toward direct and premium formats in Q2?
Who at VML or internally at DICK'S owns the decision to reallocate from paid social into premium digital video — is that Jason Xenopoulos's team or someone on Emily Silver's side?

The four proposals from Spring through Holiday 2025 didn't close — can you help me understand whether that was a budget timing issue, a pricing issue, or a fit issue with the specific formats we proposed?

DICK'S has $108.7M in broadcast and $29.3M in CTV — is there a connected TV or streaming strategy that TWC's CTV inventory could plug into, or is CTV being managed separately from the digital and social budgets?

When DICK'S plans tentpole moments like back-to-school or holiday, how far in advance is the media mix locked — and is there a planning window in the next 60 days where a TWC premium proposal would land at the right time?

sales_plays.title: Dillard's Portfolio Depth: From PMP to Premium Direct
sales_plays.summary: Dillard's has two consecutive won PMP deals (Jan 2025 and Jul 2024) through Mindstream Media Group, signaling a reliable programmatic relationship. The expansion play converts that PMP foundation into higher-impact direct and cross-channel buys across weather, video, and display to capture retail tentpole moments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Saira Habash at Mindstream referencing the two back-to-back PMP wins (Jul 2024, Jan 2025) and note that Dillard's $2.9M display + $9K online video mix suggests video is underweighted relative to their $7.5M search spend — open the door to a planning conversation.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Saira: 'For Dillard's upcoming retail tentpole moments — Back-to-School, Fall Fashion, and Holiday — is the current PMP allocation designed to scale, or are there direct placements Mindstream is still sourcing elsewhere?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered direct inventory as the bridge between Dillard's programmatic PMP buys and the high-reach, brand-safe video and display formats their $9K online video spend suggests they haven't fully activated yet.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Dillard's current online video spend is $9K against a $5.3M digital base — less than 0.2% of digital. Even a modest shift of $250K into weather-contextual video would represent a 27x increase in video investment and align with how their fashion and beauty categories respond to seasonal demand signals.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Saira for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to map Dillard's Q3–Q4 tentpole calendar against TWC inventory — offer to bring a custom weather-retail correlation deck for Dillard's key categories.

sales_plays.title: Capture Dillard's Paid Social Budget from Competing Platforms
sales_plays.summary: Dillard's is spending $2.3M in Paid Social — a significant line item that is currently flowing outside TWC's ecosystem. With active Bombora intent around Entertainment and a fashion-forward brand identity, there is a clear case to redirect a portion of that social spend toward TWC's contextually relevant, brand-safe environments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Kermie Gladstone, Budget Director, referencing that Dillard's $2.3M paid social allocation is a known line item and that TWC is seeing strong retail advertiser results in contextual display and video formats that outperform social CPMs on brand-safe reach — tie to the active Entertainment intent signal from Bombora this week.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Kermie: 'Of the $2.3M in paid social, how much is performance-driven versus brand awareness — and is Mindstream evaluating any reallocation toward channels with stronger contextual relevance for Dillard's fashion and beauty audience?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's audience targeting and weather-contextual placements as a brand-safe, measurable alternative to social for Dillard's awareness goals — particularly for beauty and home categories where purchase intent aligns with seasonal and lifestyle moments rather than social scroll behavior.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If Dillard's shifted just 15% of the $2.3M paid social budget ($345K) to TWC contextual display and video, it would represent a 12x increase over current online video spend ($9K) and a meaningful diversification of their $5.3M digital mix away from walled-garden social platforms.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive audit — offer to show Kermie and Saira Habash a side-by-side of TWC audience reach vs. paid social reach for Dillard's core demographic in the next 10 business days, framed as a Q3 planning input.

talk_track_opener: Hey Saira, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with the Dillard's account through Mindstream for the past year-plus — we closed two PMP deals together, most recently in January 2025 — so I wanted to reach out directly as you're likely deep in Q3 and Q4 planning right now. I have a lot of respect for how Mindstream manages the Dillard's media mix and I want to make sure we're being a real partner, not just a line item. We've been looking at Dillard's current digital footprint — strong search at $7.5M, solid display at $2.9M, and $2.3M in paid social — and one thing that stands out is that online video is essentially untouched at $9K. Given that Dillard's is a fashion and beauty brand where visual storytelling drives purchase intent, that feels like a gap we could help close together. I'm also seeing some active intent signals around Entertainment from Dillard's this week, which makes me think there may be a cultural-moment or lifestyle angle worth exploring. Would you have 30 minutes in the next couple of weeks to walk through how TWC's inventory could layer into the tentpole planning Mindstream is doing for Dillard's second half?
open_questions: The two PMP buys in 2024 and 2025 have been relatively modest — is Mindstream's intent to keep Dillard's TWC investment at the PMP level, or is there appetite to test direct placements for Q3 or Q4 tentpole moments like Back-to-School or Holiday?

Dillard's online video spend is currently at $9K against a $7.5M search budget — who owns the decision to scale video investment, and is that a Kermie Gladstone call or does it sit with Saira Habash at Mindstream?

With $2.3M going into paid social, how is Mindstream measuring the brand-awareness contribution of that channel for Dillard's fashion and beauty categories versus what a contextual or weather-triggered buy could deliver?

The Bombora signal shows Dillard's active on Entertainment topics this week — is there a specific campaign or cultural-moment initiative in planning that's driving that research, and could TWC's lifestyle or entertainment-adjacent inventory be relevant?

Given Dillard's HQ in Little Rock and a regional department store footprint, is there a geo-specific or weather-triggered strategy Mindstream is considering for markets where seasonal transitions drive apparel and home purchase cycles?

sales_plays.title: Rain & Storm Season Scale-Up
sales_plays.summary: Discount Tire is an active customer with $260K in won revenue across Q1-Q2 2026. With rain and storm season approaching, this play expands their current TWC-Interactive investment into higher-impact, weather-triggered formats before the demand window opens.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email or call Jeffrey Lack (CMO/VP Marketing) referencing the back-to-back Q1 and Q2 2026 wins ($260K combined across four placements) and note that storm and rain season historically drives a measurable spike in tire-related search and purchase intent — position this as the right moment to get ahead of that curve before competitors lock in inventory.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jeffrey: 'Of your $56.2M in paid search and $28.3M in digital spend, how much is currently allocated to weather-triggered or contextually timed buys — and is ANC optimizing that timing proactively or reactively?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Bridge to TWC's weather-triggered ad products — specifically real-time storm and precipitation targeting — which can activate Discount Tire creative precisely when consumers in their 39-state footprint are experiencing rain, ice, or severe weather events, directly at the moment of highest tire-replacement intent.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Their Q2 2026 TWC-Interactive buy was $120K and Q1 was $80K combined — a weather-triggered expansion to $250K-$300K for Q3 storm season would represent a 2x step-up aligned to their $93M total media footprint, where TWC currently captures less than 0.3% of wallet share.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jerry Cifarelli at ANC to schedule a 30-minute weather-targeting product walkthrough with Jeffrey's team before end of April — frame it as a pre-season planning session, not a sales call, to lock in inventory before Q3 demand peaks.

sales_plays.title: Shift Digital Display & OLV Budget On-Property
sales_plays.summary: Discount Tire spends $4.6M in Internet Display and $5.0M in Online Video — neither of which is currently captured by TWC. This play displaces a portion of that off-property spend by demonstrating superior contextual relevance and weather-moment targeting on TWC's owned and operated properties.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the $4.6M Internet Display and $5.0M Online Video line items visible in their media mix and open with: 'We've earned trust across your Q1 and Q2 TWC-Interactive buys — but we're not yet part of the $9.6M you're putting into display and online video. I'd like to show you why that's the highest-leverage shift we could make together this year.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jerry Cifarelli at ANC: 'Who owns the display and OLV planning for Discount Tire — is that a separate team from the TWC relationship, and what KPIs are those placements being measured against today?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's on-property display and pre-roll OLV inventory as a direct displacement of generic programmatic display — TWC delivers contextual adjacency to weather content that is uniquely relevant to a tire buyer deciding whether their tires are safe in wet or icy conditions, something a standard DSP buy cannot replicate.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Even a 15% reallocation of the $9.6M display and OLV budget ($1.44M) to TWC on-property inventory would represent a 5x increase over their current $260K annual TWC spend — and would still leave 85% of that budget with existing partners, making it a low-risk test with high upside.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a side-by-side performance comparison — ask ANC to share current display and OLV benchmarks (CTR, VCR, or store-visit lift) so TWC can build a matched proposal by May 1, ahead of Q3 planning lock.

talk_track_opener: Hey Jeffrey, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working together through ANC across your Q1 and Q2 buys this year, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think we're leaving something real on the table heading into storm season. You've got 1,200-plus stores across 39 states, and rain and severe weather are literally the moments when consumers decide they need new tires — that's not a hypothesis, it's what we see in search and engagement data every spring and summer. Looking at your overall media mix, you're running $56M in paid search and another $9.6M across display and online video — but TWC is only capturing about $260K of that so far in 2026, which is less than a third of a percent of your total spend. I'd love to spend 30 minutes with you and Jerry before the end of April to show you specifically how weather-triggered inventory on our owned properties could either expand what's working in your TWC-Interactive buys or shift a slice of your display budget into a context that's directly tied to the moment a tire purchase decision gets made. Would that be worth a conversation?
open_questions: Of your $56.2M in paid search spend, how much is currently allocated to weather-triggered or contextually timed activations — and is ANC managing that timing proactively or based on historical calendars?
Who at Discount Tire or ANC owns the $4.6M Internet Display and $5.0M Online Video budgets — is that the same team managing the TWC-Interactive relationship, or a separate planning group?
Jeffrey, as you head into Q3 planning, what does success look like for storm and rain season — are you measuring store traffic lift, tire category search share, or something else?
The Q1 2025 loss of $100K — was that a budget timing issue, a competitive displacement, or a scope change? Understanding that helps us protect the relationship going into Q3.
Is ANC currently running any weather-triggered or real-time contextual buys for Discount Tire outside of TWC — and if so, what platforms and what performance benchmarks are they hitting?

sales_plays.title: Shift Digital Dollars On-Property
sales_plays.summary: Disney Pictures is spending $10.4M in digital — with $4.1M in Mobile App and only $302K in Online Video — while CTV sits at just $2.8M against $21.1M in Broadcast. This play targets the imbalance: displace fragmented off-property digital spend (especially the $51K Display and underperforming Online Video) by redirecting budget toward premium on-property inventory where Disney's own IP and audience data create a defensible moat.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Andrea Cassetta at OMG23 referencing the $10.4M digital allocation and note the gap between Mobile App ($4.1M) and Online Video ($302K) — ask if the mix reflects a deliberate shift or a planning artifact heading into their next flight.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Which digital channels are currently delivering the lowest cost-per-completed-view or cost-per-acquisition against their film release windows, and is OMG23 benchmarking those against on-property alternatives?

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position on-property premium video and CTV inventory as a direct upgrade from the $51K Display and $302K Online Video lines — same audience, higher attention, brand-safe environment that aligns with Disney Pictures' IP quality standards.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If even 20% of the $10.4M digital budget ($2.08M) shifts on-property, that more than doubles their current $2.8M CTV investment — with first-party audience targeting that off-property display cannot replicate.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Andrea for a 20-minute working session in the next two weeks to walk through a side-by-side channel efficiency comparison using their current $38.6M total spend as the baseline — no pitch deck, just data.

sales_plays.title: Morgan Di Stefano Warm Introduction
sales_plays.summary: With no CRM history and OMG23 as AOR, the fastest path to a Disney Pictures media decision-maker is a warm referral through Morgan Di Stefano, VP Communications at Disney Branded Television, who sits within the same Walt Disney Studios umbrella and likely has cross-divisional relationships with the Pictures marketing and media team.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Research any shared LinkedIn connections or industry events (e.g., upfront presentations, NAB, Disney content showcases) where Morgan Di Stefano has been publicly visible, then send a brief, personalized LinkedIn note referencing Disney Branded Television's content slate and a specific observation about how Disney Pictures' $21.1M Broadcast spend compares to the broader Disney portfolio.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Morgan who on the Disney Pictures side owns the paid media and partnership strategy for theatrical releases — specifically whether that sits with an internal brand team or flows entirely through OMG23.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Frame the conversation around how Disney Branded Television and Disney Pictures could benefit from a unified on-property media strategy — reducing reliance on fragmented third-party digital (the $51K Display line is a credible example of inefficiency) in favor of coordinated premium placements.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Reference that Disney Pictures' trailing 12-month spend of $38.6M is heavily weighted to Broadcast ($21.1M) with CTV at only $2.8M — a referral introduction could unlock a conversation about closing that gap with a partner who understands the Disney brand at scale.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Morgan for a single email introduction to the Disney Pictures media lead or their OMG23 counterpart — offer to make it easy by drafting the intro note for her to forward, keeping her time commitment under five minutes.

talk_track_opener: Hey Andrea, I'm reaching out because we work with a number of entertainment advertisers running significant digital budgets through OMG23, and I wanted to share something specific to the Disney Pictures account that caught my attention. I focus on helping media teams get more performance out of their existing digital mix — particularly where fragmented placements are underdelivering relative to premium alternatives.
open_questions: Is the $4.1M Mobile App allocation at Disney Pictures primarily driving app installs for Disney+ or supporting theatrical release campaigns, and how is OMG23 measuring its incrementality?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Direct Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Disney Theatrical Productions is an active customer with a won PMP deal in Jan 2026, but three consecutive lost interactive opportunities (Hoppers, Fantastic Four, Thunderbolts) signal a gap between what TWC is pitching and what OMG23 is approving. This play expands the existing PMP relationship into premium direct placements by reframing value around Disney's $348.8M digital footprint and Rita Ferro's known priority on brand-safe, high-impact environments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the January 2026 PMP win directly with Andrea Cassetta at OMG23: 'Andrea, the PMP we activated in January is a great foundation — I want to show you what performance looks like when we layer in direct premium inventory on top of it, especially with summer tentpoles coming.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Andrea: 'For the next Disney Theatrical release after Hoppers, what does OMG23 need to see in a proposal — reach guarantees, contextual alignment, or something else — to get it past the approval stage?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium direct inventory as the brand-safe, high-impact layer missing from the current PMP-only buy — directly addressing the pattern of lost interactive deals on Fantastic Four ($75K), Thunderbolts, and Elio ($50K) by offering guaranteed placements with contextual weather and cultural-moment targeting.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Disney spends $56.3M on Online Video and $35.6M on Internet Display — TWC's premium direct can capture a meaningful share of that $91.9M combined budget with measurable lift over the existing PMP baseline, which currently sits at effectively $1 in booked revenue.
Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Andrea for a 30-minute working session before July 1 to co-build a Q3 proposal for the next theatrical release — framed as a planning conversation, not a pitch.

sales_plays.title: Displace the Interactive Incumbent on Theatrical Releases
sales_plays.summary: Three lost interactive opportunities in 12 months (Hoppers 2026, Fantastic Four, Thunderbolts/Elio) suggest a competing vendor is winning Disney Theatrical's interactive budget. This play targets Rita Ferro directly to understand who holds that spend and positions TWC as the superior alternative, using Disney's $22.5M CTV and $27.5M Mobile App spend as proof of scale appetite.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Rita Ferro, President of Global Advertising, with a direct observation: 'Rita, we've been in conversations with OMG23 on several Disney Theatrical releases this past year — Fantastic Four, Thunderbolts, Elio — and we keep getting close but not closing. I'd love 15 minutes to understand what we're missing and whether there's a better way to structure our partnership.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Rita: 'Who currently owns the interactive and digital video budget for Disney Theatrical releases, and what does that vendor deliver that you haven't been able to get from TWC proposals so far?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Once the incumbent is identified, position TWC's combination of premium contextual targeting, weather-triggered creative delivery, and brand-safe environments as a differentiated alternative — especially relevant for theatrical releases where opening-weekend timing and audience mood are critical.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Disney's total digital spend is $348.8M with $54.4M in Paid Social and $56.3M in Online Video — even a 5% reallocation from the incumbent toward TWC interactive placements represents over $17M in incremental budget, dwarfing the $175K in lost interactive opportunities from the past 12 months.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Rita for a brief executive alignment call — 20 minutes before Q3 planning locks — to present a revised interactive framework built specifically around Disney Theatrical's release calendar.

talk_track_opener: Rita, I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company — we work with your team at OMG23, specifically Andrea Cassetta, and we have an active PMP relationship with Disney Theatrical Productions that started in January. We've been part of several conversations around your theatrical releases over the past year, including Fantastic Four, Thunderbolts, and Elio, and I want to be straightforward: we've won the PMP but lost the interactive opportunities, and I think there's a smarter way to structure what we bring to the table for your team.
open_questions: Which vendor is currently winning the interactive budget for Disney Theatrical releases like Fantastic Four and Thunderbolts, and what are they delivering that our proposals haven't matched?

Does Rita Ferro or OMG23's Andrea Cassetta hold final approval authority on interactive and digital video buys for theatrical releases, or is there a separate brand-side decision-maker at Disney Theatrical?

sales_plays.title: Digital Portfolio Expansion: From Display to Premium CTV
sales_plays.summary: Dollar General is an active customer with $1.1M in digital and $10.6M in display spend but only $205K in CTV — a significant gap given their 20,000-store footprint and value-shopper audience. This play targets Tony Rogers and Julia Horwitz to shift budget from lower-funnel display into premium CTV and online video, where Dollar General is underweighted relative to their scale.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Julia Horwitz at Goodway Group referencing Dollar General's $205K CTV investment versus $10.6M in display. Open with: 'Julia, given the scale of Dollar General's display program, I wanted to share how a few of our retail clients are rebalancing toward CTV to drive incremental reach among value shoppers — audiences that are increasingly cord-cutting and harder to reach via traditional display.'
Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Julia: 'Is Goodway currently modeling CTV as a reach extension to display for Dollar General, or are those budgets managed as separate line items with separate KPIs?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position premium CTV as the natural complement to Dollar General's $5.2M online video and $36.4M paid social spend — a mid-funnel bridge that connects social awareness to in-store conversion across their 48-state footprint.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the opportunity: Dollar General's $205K CTV spend represents less than 0.5% of their tracked digital investment. Moving even 10% of their $10.6M display budget into CTV would represent a 5x increase — and we can model incremental reach against their existing value-shopper segments.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Julia for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to walk through a CTV reach analysis built specifically on Dollar General's current display audience data. Offer to bring a pre-built model to the call.

sales_plays.title: Retail Tentpole Planning: Get Ahead of Back-to-School and Q4
sales_plays.summary: Dollar General's Bombora intent signal around 'Education' this week suggests early planning activity that likely maps to back-to-school — one of their highest-volume retail tentpoles. This play uses that signal as a planning conversation opener with Julia Horwitz at Goodway Group, positioning our solutions ahead of the demand window before budgets are locked.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the Education intent signal directly when reaching out to Julia Horwitz: 'Julia, we're seeing Dollar General showing elevated research activity around Education topics this week — which made me think you may already be in early planning mode for back-to-school. Are you mapping out media for that window yet?'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'For Dollar General's back-to-school push, is the media mix currently weighted toward paid social and display like the broader annual plan, or are you testing different formats for that tentpole specifically?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our platform as a way to extend Dollar General's $36.4M paid social and $10.6M display investment into high-intent, contextually relevant environments during the back-to-school planning and purchase window — reaching parents and caregivers when they are actively shopping for value.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Dollar General's 20,000+ stores in 48 states means back-to-school is a national event for them. Even a modest test budget layered onto their existing digital framework — say, $150K-$200K in targeted placements — could be benchmarked against their current $352K mobile app and $5.2M online video spend to measure incremental lift.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Julia for a planning call in the next 10 days — before back-to-school creative is finalized — to share a tentpole media brief we can build around Dollar General's value-shopper audience. Keep it low-commitment: 'Even if budgets aren't set yet, this would give you something to react to internally.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Julia, this is [Rep Name] from [Company] — we work with your team at Goodway on a number of retail accounts, and I've been supporting the Dollar General relationship. I wanted to reach out because we've been doing some analysis on how Dollar General's digital mix compares to where value-retail advertisers are seeing the strongest returns right now. One thing that stood out: Dollar General is running $10.6M in display and $36.4M in paid social, but only $205K in CTV — which is a pretty significant gap for a brand with 20,000 stores and a national value-shopper audience that is increasingly shifting to streaming. We're also seeing Dollar General showing intent signals around Education topics this week, which made me think back-to-school planning might already be on your radar. I'd love to spend 30 minutes walking through a CTV reach model we built using Dollar General's existing audience segments — and if the timing works, we could tie it to a tentpole brief for back-to-school. Would you have time in the next two weeks?
open_questions: Julia, is Goodway currently modeling CTV as a reach extension to Dollar General's $10.6M display program, or are those managed as entirely separate budget lines with different KPIs?
For back-to-school, does Dollar General run a unified national campaign or does Goodway localize creative and placements by DMA given the 20,000-store footprint?
Tony Rogers owns the CMO seat — is he involved in tentpole media decisions, or does Julia and the Goodway team have authority to greenlight a test budget independently?
The recent Q3 and Q4 pitches in our CRM all closed at $5K — were those evaluated against a specific format or audience test, and what would a more meaningful pilot look like from your side?
With $36.4M in paid social, is Dollar General actively looking to diversify platform risk, or is the current concentration a deliberate performance-driven choice that would need new data to shift?
