sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Direct Upgrade: DashPass & Merchant Audience Expansion
sales_plays.summary: DoorDash has renewed TWC Interactive PMP deals three consecutive years (2024, 2025, 2026). The lost DashPass Q2 2024 opp ($500K) and lost Aldi MX campaign ($150K) signal unmet demand for premium, direct inventory. This play converts the existing PMP relationship into a broader direct buy across weather-triggered and tentpole moments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three consecutive PMP wins and congratulate Kofi Amoo-Gottfried or Andy Lindblade (Wavemaker) on the 2026 renewal. Open with: 'We've now partnered on three straight years of PMP — I want to show you what incremental reach looks like when we move a portion of that budget into direct, guaranteed inventory, especially around the seasonal planning windows Wavemaker is likely already mapping for Q3 and Q4.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Andy Lindblade at Wavemaker: 'When you lost the DashPass Q2 2024 buy and the Aldi MX campaign with us, what was the primary objection — was it inventory scale, audience match, or pricing model? Understanding that helps us structure the right direct package for the next planning cycle.'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered, location-aware audiences against DoorDash's core use case — bad weather, extreme heat, and major cultural moments (Super Bowl, March Madness, summer grilling) are exactly when delivery intent spikes. Direct inventory guarantees DoorDash owns those moments rather than competing in open auction.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): DoorDash's total media spend is $236.3M trailing 12 months, with $95.8M in digital. The two lost opps alone represent $650K in missed direct revenue. A direct package at even 0.5% of their digital budget is ~$479K incremental — a realistic ask given the existing PMP relationship and Wavemaker's planning infrastructure.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Andy Lindblade for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to map DoorDash's Q3 tentpole calendar against TWC's weather and cultural-moment inventory packages — before Wavemaker locks Q3 budgets.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Spend: Weather-Triggered Video vs. Meta/TikTok
sales_plays.summary: DoorDash allocates $39.8M to Paid Social — the single largest digital sub-channel. With $6.6M in Online Video and $12.8M in Mobile App, there is a clear appetite for video and mobile formats. This play makes the case that a portion of Paid Social spend should shift to TWC's premium CTV and mobile video inventory, where weather context drives measurably higher delivery intent.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a data observation to Kofi Amoo-Gottfried: 'Your paid social line is $39.8M — nearly 42% of your total digital spend. We're seeing DoorDash competitors use weather-triggered mobile and CTV video to intercept delivery intent at the exact moment someone decides not to cook. That's a moment Meta and TikTok can't own the way we can.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'What KPIs is Wavemaker using to evaluate Paid Social performance for DoorDash — is it app installs, DashPass subscriber growth, or order volume? Knowing the primary metric tells us whether a weather-triggered video unit can directly compete on your existing scorecard.'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's CTV ($21M in DoorDash's own CTV budget signals they value the format) and mobile video inventory can be layered with real-time weather triggers — rain, extreme heat, snow events — that correlate directly with delivery order spikes. This is contextual relevance Paid Social platforms cannot replicate at the local market level.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Even a 5% reallocation of DoorDash's $39.8M Paid Social budget equals ~$2M in potential TWC video/mobile investment. Against DoorDash's existing $21M CTV spend, a weather-triggered video extension is a natural adjacent buy, not a new budget ask — it's a reallocation story Wavemaker can defend internally.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer Kofi or Andy a competitive displacement brief — a one-page showing weather-event delivery intent lift data versus standard social benchmarks — and request a 20-minute review call within 10 business days, before Wavemaker's next quarterly channel mix review.

talk_track_opener: Hey Andy, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've had a great run with DoorDash — three straight PMP renewals going back to 2024 — and I wanted to reach out before Wavemaker locks in Q3 planning. I've been looking at how the relationship has grown and where there's still whitespace we haven't activated together. DoorDash's digital spend is running at roughly $95.8M, with $39.8M sitting in Paid Social and only $6.6M in Online Video. Given that delivery intent spikes are directly tied to weather moments — rain, heat waves, winter storms — I think there's a real case for shifting a portion of that Paid Social budget into weather-triggered video and mobile formats where we can own the context, not just the audience. The two opps we didn't close in 2024 — the DashPass Q2 buy and the Aldi MX campaign — tell me there's appetite for bigger direct investment, and I'd love to understand what the sticking points were so we can structure something that actually fits Wavemaker's planning model. Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar in the next two weeks to map DoorDash's Q3 tentpole moments against our inventory?
open_questions: When the DashPass Q2 2024 and Aldi MX campaign opps didn't close with us, what was the primary objection from Wavemaker or DoorDash — was it pricing, inventory scale, or audience match?
Who at DoorDash — Kofi Amoo-Gottfried or someone on his team — owns the decision to shift budget from Paid Social into premium video or contextual formats for 2026?
Is Wavemaker currently planning any Q3 or Q4 tentpole campaigns around summer delivery moments, back-to-school, or fall sports that we should be positioning against now?
What KPIs is DoorDash using to evaluate the three PMP renewals — are they measuring app installs, DashPass subscriber growth, order volume lift, or brand awareness?
Does DoorDash have a separate media budget for merchant co-marketing campaigns like the Aldi MX buy, or does that come out of the same Wavemaker-managed pool as the DashPass and brand campaigns?

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Brian Potter: 'Of your $2.3M in paid social, how much is currently driving measurable subscription or trial starts versus brand awareness — and is that balance something you're looking to shift?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position against the paid social-heavy allocation — $2.3M in paid social vs. only $71K in online video and $126K in display suggests an imbalance. Introduce our platform as the channel that captures high-intent business and finance audiences already in a buying or subscribing mindset, directly complementing the WSJ and Barron's subscriber acquisition goals.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame it concretely: even a 5% reallocation of the $2.3M paid social budget ($115K) into our inventory could be tested as a pilot — benchmark against their current $328K mobile app spend to show comparable or lower CPAs for qualified financial news audiences.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 20-minute call with Brian Potter in the next two weeks specifically to walk through a custom audience overlap analysis between our users and Dow Jones's target subscriber profile — low commitment, high signal of intent.

sales_plays.summary: Since Dow Jones is net-new with no CRM history, routing through Charlie McKittrick, CSO & Partner at Mediahub Worldwide New York, is the lowest-friction path to earn credibility and access to the $3.5M digital budget before a direct pitch lands cold.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Anchor the conversation in Dow Jones's existing CTV spend of $312K — suggest that our audience data can help Mediahub prove cross-channel lift between CTV and digital, giving Charlie a measurement story he can bring back to Brian Potter and the Dow Jones marketing team.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Charlie for a 30-minute working session — framed as a media planning conversation, not a sales call — to share audience insights specific to the financial news and business media vertical. sales_plays.title: Take 5 Car Wash Interactive — Three-Loss Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Take 5 Car Wash has lost three consecutive Interactive/TWC deals totaling $200K (Q4 2023, Q1 2024, Q4 2024). The incumbent is holding the budget. This play targets a displacement by reframing the value proposition through the lens of the proven Take 5 Oil Change CTV win.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Acknowledge the pattern directly with Rob Walker at Dentsu X: 'We've pitched Take 5 Car Wash Interactive three times — Q4 2023, Q1 2024, and Q4 2024 — and lost each time. I'm not here to pitch a fourth identical proposal. I want to understand what the incumbent is delivering that we haven't matched, and whether there's a different format or data angle worth exploring.'
Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'What did the winning vendor offer on those three Car Wash buys that made the difference — was it pricing, audience targeting, measurement, or something else entirely?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Reframe away from Interactive/display toward weather-triggered CTV or mobile video for Take 5 Car Wash — a format where weather-driven demand signals (rain, pollen, road salt season) can be tied to real-time activation, differentiating from whatever static display solution won the prior three cycles.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The three lost Car Wash deals totaled $200K. The Take 5 Oil Change CTV win demonstrates Driven Brands already trusts this channel for a sister brand. A weather-triggered Car Wash CTV test at $75K–$100K is a lower ask than prior pitches and carries a proven internal precedent.

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a brief loss-review conversation with Rob Walker: 'Give me 20 minutes to understand the Car Wash brief before the next planning cycle. I'm not pitching — I just want to make sure if we come back, we come back with something worth your time.' Aim to have this conversation 6–8 weeks before Q3 planning opens.

talk_track_opener: Hey Doug, hey Rob — I'm [Rep Name] from [Company]. We've been working with the Dentsu X team on the Take 5 Oil Change CTV campaign that closed in November, and I wanted to connect while that momentum is fresh. We have a real relationship here, not just a transaction, and I want to make sure we're being useful across the broader Driven Brands portfolio, not just one brand. Here's what caught my attention: Driven Brands is running $46.4M in total media, with $22.6M concentrated in Paid Social and only $2M in CTV — and that CTV is currently all sitting on one brand. Meanwhile, Take 5 Car Wash has had three consecutive Interactive buys go to a competitor. I'd love to spend 30 minutes understanding how the portfolio is planned across brands at Dentsu X, and whether the CTV structure we built for Take 5 Oil Change is something worth replicating for Maaco, Meineke, or Car Wash before Q2 budgets are locked. What does your calendar look like in the next two weeks?

sales_plays.title: Alaskan Hardgear Brand Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Duluth Trading is an active customer with $16.8M in digital spend, but Alaskan Hardgear — their premium outdoor sub-brand — likely has untapped dedicated media investment. This play targets Barb Case to carve out incremental budget for Alaskan Hardgear as a distinct brand line, separate from the core Duluth Trading umbrella.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Barb Case referencing the active Bombora intent signal around 'Campaigns' this week and the strong CTV investment ($8.2M) already in market. Open with: 'Barb, I noticed Duluth Trading is actively researching campaign strategies right now — given your CTV footprint, I wanted to share something specific to Alaskan Hardgear before Q3 planning locks.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Is Alaskan Hardgear currently planned as a separate line item in your media budget, or does it roll up under the core Duluth Trading brand allocation?' — this surfaces whether there is whitespace to create a dedicated brand budget.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position premium on-property placements and high-impact digital video (currently only $1.6M Online Video vs. $13.2M Paid Social) as the right environment to build Alaskan Hardgear's distinct identity among outdoor enthusiasts — audiences already in-market for durable gear.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Duluth Trading's current Online Video spend is $1.6M against a $16.8M digital total — just 9.5%. A modest reallocation of even $500K from Paid Social into premium video for Alaskan Hardgear would create a dedicated brand moment without touching overall budget. Frame the incremental ask against the $13.2M Paid Social line as a test-and-learn.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Barb for a 20-minute call this week or next to walk through a brand-specific media framework for Alaskan Hardgear ahead of fall planning. Offer to loop in Adel Grobler at Planet Propaganda (AOR Creative Digital) so the creative and media conversation happen together.

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Displacement — Shift Budget to Premium Digital
sales_plays.summary: Duluth Trading's digital mix is heavily weighted toward Paid Social at $13.2M, with Internet Display at only $365K and Mobile App at $76K. Five consecutive Q4 lost opportunities at $5K each suggest the relationship has stalled at a low spend level. This play challenges the Paid Social concentration and positions premium programmatic or direct digital as a higher-ROI alternative for reaching Duluth's core 'doer' audience.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the spend imbalance directly with Adel Grobler at Planet Propaganda (AOR Creative Digital): 'Adel, looking at Duluth Trading's digital mix, $13.2M is concentrated in Paid Social while Display sits at $365K. I want to show you where that audience is actually spending time outside of social feeds — and why the creative Planet Propaganda is building deserves a better environment.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'What performance benchmarks is Duluth Trading using to evaluate Paid Social efficiency right now — CPM, ROAS, or something else — and when did you last run a head-to-head test against premium programmatic or direct placements?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position premium digital inventory (on-property, contextually relevant to workwear and outdoor audiences) as a displacement of underperforming Paid Social dollars. Duluth Trading's brand — built on durability and authenticity — is misaligned with the cluttered, low-trust social feed environment. Premium placements reinforce brand equity while reaching the same in-market buyer.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Even a 10% shift from the $13.2M Paid Social budget ($1.32M) into premium digital would represent a 3.6x increase over current Display spend ($365K) and create a meaningful test. Frame this against the five Q4 lost pitches at $5K each — the relationship has been stuck at a micro-budget level and this reframes the conversation at a scale that matches Duluth's actual $16.8M digital investment.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a 30-minute working session with Adel Grobler and, if possible, Barb Case, to review a side-by-side audience overlap analysis showing where Duluth's target 'doer' customer is reachable outside of Paid Social — deliver this before the next planning cycle opens.

talk_track_opener: Hey Barb, I'm [Rep Name] from [Company] — we've been working with Duluth Trading on your media strategy and I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a meaningful opportunity we haven't fully explored together yet. We have a strong foundation with your CTV investment at $8.2M and your broader digital footprint at $16.8M, and I've been watching how brands like yours are evolving their mix heading into the back half of the year. Here's what caught my attention this week: Duluth Trading is showing active intent around campaign planning right now according to our Bombora signals, and when I look at your digital breakdown, $13.2M is sitting in Paid Social while Online Video is at $1.6M and Display is at $365K. That feels like an imbalance worth a conversation — especially for a brand like Alaskan Hardgear that deserves its own premium media moment. I'd love to ask you one question: is Alaskan Hardgear currently planned as its own budget line, or does it roll up under the core Duluth Trading allocation? That answer would shape everything I'd want to bring to you.
open_questions: Is Alaskan Hardgear currently planned as a separate media budget line, or does it roll up under the core Duluth Trading brand allocation — and who owns that decision?
What performance metrics is Duluth Trading using to evaluate the $13.2M Paid Social investment, and when did you last benchmark it against premium digital alternatives?
Barb, with five Q4 pitches that didn't move forward, can you help me understand what was missing — was it the product fit, the price point, or the timing of the ask?
Is Adel Grobler at Planet Propaganda the right person to loop in on a media mix rebalancing conversation, or does that decision live with your internal team first?

As you head into fall and holiday planning, are there specific tentpole moments — like workwear gifting season or outdoor retail peaks — where Duluth Trading is looking to increase share of voice beyond what Paid Social can deliver?

sales_plays.title: Summer Heat Cooling Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Dyson is an active PMP customer with three consecutive won deals (2024–2026). The summer heat lead-in window is the highest-value moment to expand their TWC footprint from PMP-only into premium on-property placements targeting consumers actively searching for cooling and air purification solutions.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three consecutive PMP wins (2024, 2025, 2026) with Justin Burt and note that Memorial Day weekend historically marks the start of peak cooling search volume on Weather.com — get in front of him in late March/early April before Universal McCann locks Q2 budgets with Sarah Robertson at IPG MediaBrands.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Justin: 'Beyond the PMP buys, how is Dyson currently activating against the summer heat spike — are cooling fans and purifiers getting dedicated budget separate from the always-on digital line?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's on-property premium video and high-impact display units (vs. the $429K Online Video and $1.3M Display currently going off-property) as the contextually relevant environment where consumers are actively checking heat forecasts and making purchase decisions — not just passively scrolling.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Dyson's total digital spend is $28M, yet TWC's share is essentially $1 symbolic PMP buys. Even a 2% reallocation of the $20.1M Paid Social budget into TWC premium on-property video and display represents a $400K+ incremental opportunity tied directly to a proven heat-intent audience.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Justin for a 20-minute call the week of April 7 to walk through a summer heat audience insights deck — offer to have it co-branded with Universal McCann's planning team so Sarah Robertson can fold it into Q2 upfront conversations.

sales_plays.title: Programmatic-to-Direct Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Dyson lost two Q1 2024 deals — a $100K direct environmental campaign and a $3,945 DV360 data deal — suggesting programmatic buying through DV360 is the incumbent path. This play targets displacing DV360-routed spend with a direct, data-enriched TWC deal that delivers weather-triggered creative and first-party audience targeting unavailable programmatically.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Acknowledge the two lost Q1 2024 deals directly with Justin Burt: 'We know the environmental campaign and the DV360 data deal didn't come together last year — I want to show you what's changed on our direct data and targeting side that makes a direct buy materially different from what you can access through DV360 today.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'When the DV360 buys run against weather-adjacent inventory, are you getting weather-triggered creative delivery or just contextual keyword targeting — and who at Universal McCann owns that programmatic spec?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct deal as the only path to weather-triggered dynamic creative (e.g., ads that activate when the heat index exceeds 90°F in a given DMA) — a capability DV360 cannot replicate with the same precision because it lacks TWC's proprietary forecast data layer.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost $100K Q1 2024 environmental campaign is the baseline. A direct summer heat campaign combining weather-triggered video (pulling from the $429K Online Video line) and display (pulling from the $1.3M Internet Display budget) could be structured at $150K–$200K with measurable lift data — a clear upgrade from the $1 PMP symbolic buys.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send Justin and Sarah Robertson at IPG MediaBrands a one-page comparison of DV360 programmatic reach vs. TWC direct weather-triggered delivery — framed as a planning tool, not a pitch — by end of this week, with a 15-minute debrief call the following Tuesday.

talk_track_opener: Hey Justin, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company — we've been working with Dyson on the TWC Interactive PMP for three years running now, so I wanted to reach out directly before the summer planning cycle kicks into gear. We've seen strong renewal momentum on your end and I think there's a real opportunity to build on that foundation going into Q2 and Q3. I wanted to connect with you and loop in Sarah Robertson's team at Universal McCann at the right moment. On our side, we're seeing Dyson's cooling and purification products show up as a natural fit for the summer heat lead-in window — Memorial Day through Labor Day is when weather-driven purchase intent for fans and air purifiers spikes hardest on our platform. Given that Dyson is running $28M in digital and $20M-plus in Paid Social, I'm curious: is the summer heat window getting its own dedicated activation budget this year, or is it folded into the always-on social line? I'd love 20 minutes to share what we're seeing in the data.
open_questions: Justin, the PMP buys have renewed three years in a row — is there a separate Q2/Q3 budget line specifically for cooling and air purification seasonal campaigns, or does that roll up into the always-on digital allocation managed by Universal McCann?

When Universal McCann runs DV360 programmatic against weather-contextual inventory, are they getting weather-triggered creative delivery or just keyword and contextual targeting — and who owns that spec on the agency side?

The two Q1 2024 deals — the $100K environmental campaign and the DV360 data deal — didn't come together. Was that a budget timing issue, a targeting capability gap, or something else we should address differently this year?

Dyson is showing Bombora intent around Entertainment this week — is there a content partnership or streaming-adjacent initiative in planning that we should be aware of as we think about video formats for the summer?

Sarah Robertson holds AOR scope at IPG MediaBrands — is she the right person to loop in for a direct premium deal conversation, or does Universal McCann handle TWC buys independently at the planning level?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: eBay has renewed TWC Interactive PMP deals three consecutive years (2024, 2025, 2026). The relationship is proven but capped at nominal PMP value. This play converts that trust into a premium, higher-CPM direct buy — weather-contextual or audience-guaranteed — ahead of eBay's key retail tentpole windows (Back-to-School, Holiday).
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three-year PMP renewal streak directly with Julie Loeger or through iProspect's Kara Coyle: 'eBay has activated our PMP three years running — 2024, 2025, and again in January 2026. That consistency tells us the audience is working. I want to show you what a guaranteed, weather-triggered direct buy could unlock that the PMP floor can't.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask iProspect: 'For eBay's Q3 Back-to-School and Q4 Holiday pushes, what KPIs is the team optimizing toward — is it reach and frequency against a defined audience, or are you chasing lower-funnel conversion signals? That answer shapes whether a guaranteed direct deal or an audience-extension layer makes more sense.'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-contextual inventory as the bridge between eBay's $33.2M display spend and its $23.0M online video spend — a premium environment where weather moments (first cold snap, back-to-school heat waves) trigger category-relevant creative (outerwear, electronics, sporting goods) that eBay's marketplace breadth is uniquely built to monetize.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): eBay's trailing 12-month total media spend is $234.4M, with $33.2M in display and $23.0M in online video. Even a 2% reallocation from those two lines into a premium TWC direct deal represents roughly $1.1M in incremental investment — a meaningful step up from the current nominal PMP commitment and well within iProspect's planning authority.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Kara Coyle at iProspect for a 30-minute working session before the Q3 planning lock — specifically to review a weather-moment audience proposal mapped to eBay's top five seasonal categories. Suggest the week of [current date + 3 weeks] to stay ahead of the planning window.

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Budget Displacement
sales_plays.summary: eBay allocates $27.2M to Paid Social — the single largest digital sub-channel outside of Search. With social platforms facing brand-safety scrutiny and rising CPMs, this play positions TWC's premium video and display inventory as a brand-safe, audience-verified alternative that can absorb a portion of that budget with measurable reach efficiency.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a market-level observation to Julie Loeger: 'eBay is running $27.2M through Paid Social. That's a significant commitment in an environment where platform brand-safety controls and auction CPMs are both moving in the wrong direction for advertisers. I want to show you what TWC's verified audience environment looks like as a complementary — or partial replacement — channel.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'On the $27.2M Paid Social line, what percentage is performance-driven versus brand-building? And is iProspect benchmarking social CPMs against any premium publisher alternatives right now, or is the allocation largely locked for the year?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's online video ($23.0M is eBay's current OLV spend benchmark) and display environments offer brand-verified, contextually relevant placements — weather moments tied to eBay's category breadth (collectibles, auto parts, fashion) — without the auction volatility or content-adjacency risk of social feeds. Position this as a brand-safe OLV and display layer that iProspect can test against social performance metrics.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 10% test reallocation from eBay's $27.2M Paid Social budget equals $2.72M — enough to run a meaningful TWC premium video and display flight across Q3–Q4 tentpole periods. Benchmark that against eBay's existing $23.0M OLV spend to frame it as an audience extension, not a net-new budget ask.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive analysis deck — TWC audience reach vs. Paid Social platform reach among eBay's core buyer segments — delivered to Kara Coyle at iProspect within two weeks. Frame it as a planning tool for the Q3/Q4 media mix review, not a sales pitch.

talk_track_opener: Hey Julie — I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel. We've been working with eBay through iProspect for three consecutive years on our Interactive PMP, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think we're leaving real value on the table. The renewals tell me the audience is performing, but the PMP structure caps what we can actually build together. Looking at eBay's media mix — $27.2M in Paid Social, $33.2M in display, $23.0M in online video, against a total spend footprint of $234M — there's a clear opportunity to bring TWC's weather-contextual premium inventory into a more strategic role ahead of Back-to-School and Holiday planning. I'd love 20 minutes to walk through one specific idea: how a weather-triggered direct buy could connect eBay's category breadth to the moments when buyers are actually in-market. Would you or Kara Coyle at iProspect be the right starting point for that conversation?
open_questions: The TWC Interactive PMP has renewed three straight years — can you walk me through how iProspect evaluates PMP performance for eBay, and what would need to be true for that relationship to move into a guaranteed direct buy?
On eBay's $27.2M Paid Social budget, who at iProspect or eBay owns the decision to test alternative channels — is that Kara Coyle's team, or does it escalate to Julie Loeger's office?

With eBay's Q3 Back-to-School and Q4 Holiday windows coming up, at what point does iProspect lock the media mix for those flights — and are there any open budget lines we could influence before that lock?
eBay's display spend is $33.2M and OLV is $23.0M — is there a performance gap between those two formats that a weather-contextual premium buy could help close, or are both lines hitting their KPIs?

Joan is eBay's AOR for Creative and The Many is also in the mix — when TWC brings a contextual or weather-moment creative concept to the table, does that route through iProspect first, or do we need Joan involved from the start to move quickly?

This play targets Fleur Lee directly as the Alzheimer's disease marketing lead.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Fleur Lee referencing Eisai's Leqembi HCP marketing mandate and the known gap: $69K in paid social against a $4.2M digital budget suggests HCP social channels like LinkedIn and programmatic HCP networks are underutilized for a specialty neurology audience. Lead with a single insight, not a pitch.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Fleur: 'For Leqembi, what percentage of your HCP digital investment is currently reaching neurologists and memory care specialists versus broader digital audiences — and how are you measuring that reach quality?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our HCP-targeted audience solutions against the specific pain of broad digital waste — Eisai is spending $395K in display and $412K in online video with no visible HCP-verified targeting layer. We can layer verified HCP identity data onto those existing formats.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If we capture even 10% of Eisai's $4.2M digital budget ($420K), that represents a meaningful first engagement. Frame it as a pilot against their existing online video or display line — both already active at $412K and $395K respectively.

sales_plays.summary: Genuine holds the digital agency scope for Eisai, making Doug Barba a warm entry point to understand how digital dollars are planned and where new partners are evaluated. This play uses the agency relationship to build internal credibility before approaching Fleur Lee directly.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Doug Barba at Genuine, acknowledging their digital scope on Eisai and referencing the Leqembi HCP campaign as the context. Open with: 'We work with several pharma digital teams on HCP-verified targeting for neurology brands — wanted to connect on whether Eisai's Leqembi digital planning has room for a conversation about specialist audience reach.'

With $4.2M in digital spend under their management, a new HCP targeting layer adds measurable value to their deliverables for Eisai without disrupting the media plan.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Reference that Eisai's paid social allocation is $69K against a $4.2M digital total — less than 2% of digital in social. Suggest to Doug that a modest reallocation of even $150K-$200K into HCP-verified social or programmatic could meaningfully improve specialist reach metrics they report back to Fleur Lee.

talk_track_opener: Hey Fleur, I work with pharma marketing teams focused on HCP reach for specialty brands — particularly in neurology and oncology where verified audience quality really matters. steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a data observation to Jason Xenopoulos at VML: 'Looking at Electrolux's current digital mix, paid social is running at $14.8M versus $320K in online video. For a brand with proven weather-trigger performance — three Air Conditioning wins in 2025 — that video allocation looks significantly underleveraged. I'd like to show you what comparable home appliance brands are doing differently.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Is the current paid social concentration a deliberate brand strategy from Jennifer's team, or is it partly a function of where VML has historically had easier activation paths — and is there appetite to rebalance heading into 2026 planning?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC premium online video and CTV as a displacement target for underperforming social impressions. Weather-contextual video reaches consumers at the exact moment appliance need is triggered (heat waves for AC, cold snaps for heating, storms for laundry). This is a brand-safe, measurable environment versus social feed placements.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Electrolux's CTV spend is $627K today. Industry benchmarks for home appliance brands show CTV + online video allocations of 15-20% of digital budgets. Against Electrolux's $17.2M digital spend, that implies a $2.6M–$3.4M target range — versus the current $947K combined. Displacing even $1.5M from paid social into weather-triggered video closes that gap and aligns with the proven Air Conditioning campaign model already in the CRM.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive audit deck — delivered to Jason Xenopoulos at VML within two weeks — showing home appliance brand video allocation benchmarks and TWC weather-trigger performance data from the 2025 Air Conditioning wins, with a specific recommendation for Electrolux's 2026 upfront planning cycle.

talk_track_opener: Hey Jennifer — I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel. We've been working with Electrolux for a while now, including the PMP deal that closed in January and the Air Conditioning weather-trigger campaigns your team ran through mid-2025. Those wins are exactly the kind of contextual, high-intent moments we built our platform around, and I think we've only scratched the surface of what's possible across the full Electrolux brand portfolio. I work closely with Jason's team at VML on the media side as well, so I have a pretty clear picture of how the current buy is structured.
open_questions: The Air Conditioning weather-trigger campaigns performed well in 2025 — is VML planning to extend that contextual weather model to other Electrolux appliance categories like laundry or kitchen for 2026, or was that a one-off seasonal activation?
With $14.8M running through paid social, who at Electrolux or VML owns the decision to rebalance digital channel mix — is that Jennifer's team, Jason's group at VML, or a joint planning process?

Bombora is showing active Search Marketing intent from Electrolux this week — is that tied to a specific campaign brief or RFP that VML is working on, and is there a role for weather-contextual inventory in that plan?
The January 2026 PMP deal is live — how is performance tracking so far, and are there optimization levers or additional inventory packages we should be discussing before the next planning cycle?

For Electrolux's fall retail tentpole planning — Black Friday, holiday gifting — has VML already allocated the digital budget, or is there still flexibility to incorporate premium direct placements before inventory locks up?

sales_plays.title: Orforglipron Pipeline to Full Portfolio Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Eli Lilly has an open $200K Orforglipron proposal in flight and a Won PMP deal on the books, but Zepbound ($350K), Ebglyss ($100K), and Emgality ($300K) were all lost. This play converts the active Orforglipron opportunity into a multi-brand commitment while reopening the lost brand budgets through Zenith.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Neil Vendetti at Zenith referencing the active Orforglipron Proposal Plus ($200K, due 2026-03-31) and the Won PMP deal from January. Acknowledge the relationship is working and ask for a 20-minute call to show how bundling Orforglipron with at least one additional Lilly brand unlocks better inventory access and pricing efficiency.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Neil: 'Across the Zepbound, Ebglyss, and Emgality budgets that went elsewhere in Q4 2025 and Q1 2026, what was the primary reason TWC didn't win — was it audience reach, pricing, or a competing platform relationship?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-contextual and condition-aware targeting as uniquely suited to Lilly's DTC portfolio — GLP-1 weight management (Zepbound/Orforglipron), dermatology (Ebglyss), and migraine (Emgality) all have patient populations that over-index on TWC properties for health-adjacent content consumption.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Lilly's combined lost revenue across Zepbound, Ebglyss, and Emgality totals $750K in a single cycle. Winning back even two of those brands alongside the $200K Orforglipron close would represent a $550K+ incremental gain on an account already spending $103M+ in digital. Frame the PMP win as proof of execution.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Neil Vendetti for a joint call with Lina Polimeni's team before the Orforglipron proposal deadline of March 31, 2026, to present a unified multi-brand media package — one deck, one conversation, one consolidated buy.

sales_plays.title: Displace Broadcast Waste with Premium Digital Direct
sales_plays.summary: Eli Lilly spends $456.7M in Broadcast and $109.9M in CTV but only $103M in Digital total, with Online Video at just $7.8M. This play challenges the broadcast-heavy allocation by showing Lilly and Zenith how TWC's premium digital direct inventory delivers addressable, condition-aware reach that broadcast cannot match at comparable CPMs.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Neil Vendetti at Zenith with a one-page data snapshot: Lilly's $456.7M broadcast spend vs. $7.8M online video spend — a 58:1 ratio that is out of step with how pharma patients actually research conditions and medications. Reference the Bombora signal showing Lilly's current interest in Retail Technology as evidence that Lilly is actively exploring new digital touchpoints beyond traditional media.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Given that Lilly is running LillyDirect for home delivery and direct patient access, how is the current broadcast-heavy plan reaching patients at the moment they are actively researching treatment options online rather than passively watching TV?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium digital direct — contextual health and weather-triggered placements, high-impact video units, and condition-correlated audience segments — as the bridge between Lilly's $456.7M broadcast investment and the addressable digital layer that broadcast cannot provide. Emphasize that TWC is not asking to replace broadcast but to capture the intent moment broadcast misses.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 1% reallocation of Lilly's $456.7M broadcast budget equals $4.6M in incremental digital direct spend. Against a current online video baseline of $7.8M, that shift would nearly double Lilly's addressable video footprint. Frame TWC's existing PMP win as the on-ramp to a direct IO relationship at that scale.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a 30-minute working session with Neil Vendetti and a Zenith planning lead before Q3 2026 upfront commitments lock, specifically to model a broadcast-to-digital reallocation scenario for one Lilly brand — suggest Zepbound given the $350K lost opp and the GLP-1 category's high digital research behavior.

talk_track_opener: Hey Neil, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together on the Lilly PMP buy that closed in January, and I wanted to reach out directly because we have an active Orforglipron proposal in flight with a March 31 deadline and I think there's a smarter way to structure that conversation. We've earned a seat at the table with Lilly across 43 opportunities, and I want to make sure we're using that relationship to its full potential rather than going brand by brand. Here's what caught my attention: Lilly is spending $456.7M in broadcast and $109.9M in CTV, but online video is sitting at just $7.8M — and we lost Zepbound, Ebglyss, and Emgality totaling $750K in a single planning cycle. At the same time, Bombora is showing Lilly actively researching Retail Technology right now, which tells me they're thinking about new digital channels and patient access touchpoints like LillyDirect. My question for you is simple: when you look at how Lilly patients are actually researching GLP-1 options or migraine treatments online, is the current broadcast-heavy plan reaching them at that intent moment — and is there an opening to bring TWC into that conversation before Q3 commitments lock?
open_questions: Across the Zepbound, Ebglyss, and Emgality buys that went elsewhere in Q4 2025 and Q1 2026, which platforms captured that spend and what was the primary objection to TWC at the time?
Who at Lilly — beyond Lina Polimeni's corporate brand team — owns the individual brand media decisions for Zepbound and Orforglipron, and does that sit with brand marketing or a separate DTC team?
Given Lilly's $456.7M broadcast commitment, is Zenith's 2026 planning process actively evaluating any reallocation toward addressable digital, or is broadcast allocation locked for the year?

With Lilly's Bombora signal showing active interest in Retail Technology, is there a specific initiative — potentially tied to LillyDirect or pharmacy partnerships — that is driving that research and could benefit from digital media support?

The open Orforglipron Proposal Plus is at $200K — is there flexibility to structure that as part of a multi-brand package before March 31, or does each brand budget get approved independently within Zenith's process?

sales_plays.title: Brand Family Expansion: Scale ARMR & EBNT Into Premium Inventory
sales_plays.summary: Energizer has active wins across AC Pro, EBNT Video, EBNT PDI, and Meta — all in the AMJ window. The ARMR Display/Video opp is still at Verbal ($13,400). This play upgrades the ARMR deal and cross-sells premium on-property placements across the broader Energizer brand portfolio before the JAS window opens.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the four recent AMJ wins — AC Pro ($98,564), EBNT Video ($95,000), EBNT PDI ($75,000), and Meta ($40,000) — and open by acknowledging the momentum: 'We've closed four programs together this quarter totaling over $308K. I want to make sure we're set up to carry that into JAS before budgets lock.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Leyland Streiff at Universal McCann: 'For JAS, are ARMR and the broader Energizer auto portfolio being planned as separate line items, or is there a consolidated automotive budget we should be building against?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): The ARMR Display/Video opp at $13,400 is undersized relative to the $95K–$98K video and PDI programs already won. Position a premium on-property video or high-impact display package that mirrors the EBNT Video structure — same audience targeting, scaled to ARMR's seasonal demand window.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Energizer's total trailing 12-month spend is $68.9M, with $34M in digital and $22.7M in CTV. The current TWC relationship captures roughly $308K of that. Closing ARMR at a comparable EBNT Video rate ($95K) and adding a JAS brand-family package could 3x the ARMR commitment and push total TWC share meaningfully closer to the digital mix.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Leyland for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to map the JAS planning calendar for all Energizer auto brands — frame it as a brief review of what performed in AMJ to inform JAS allocations.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Spend: Shift $24.5M Social Budget Toward Premium Video
sales_plays.summary: Energizer's digital mix is heavily weighted toward Paid Social at $24.5M — nearly 72% of the $34M digital budget — while Online Video sits at only $925K. With $22.7M already in CTV, there is a clear appetite for video. This play makes the case to redirect a portion of the social budget into premium contextual video inventory where weather-driven automotive intent is highest.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a data observation to Lori Shambro or Leyland Streiff: 'Looking at Energizer's digital mix, Paid Social is running at $24.5M versus $925K in Online Video. Given the CTV investment of $22.7M, I want to share how other automotive advertisers are rebalancing social dollars into premium video to capture in-market intent at the moment consumers are actually shopping.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When Universal McCann evaluates the Paid Social investment for auto brands like ARMR and AC Pro, what performance metrics are being used to justify that allocation versus video — is it reach, cost-per-engagement, or something else?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium contextual video inventory as a direct complement to the existing CTV buy — same household, same weather-driven automotive intent signal, but reaching the consumer earlier in the research journey before they hit social. The EBNT Video win ($95,000) already validates the video format with this client.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Even a 5% reallocation of the $24.5M Paid Social budget equals $1.225M in incremental video spend. Benchmarked against the $95K EBNT Video CPM and performance, a shift of that scale would represent a 12x expansion of Energizer's current TWC video footprint and deliver measurable reach against weather-triggered automotive demand windows.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to build a one-page channel mix comparison — Paid Social versus premium contextual video — using Energizer's own spend ratios, and share it with Leyland at Universal McCann ahead of the next planning cycle. Ask for a date to deliver it within 10 business days.

talk_track_opener: Hey Leyland, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've had a strong run together this quarter — AC Pro, EBNT Video, EBNT PDI, and the Meta program all closed in AMJ, totaling over $308K across the Energizer portfolio. I appreciate the partnership and want to make sure we're building on that momentum rather than starting from scratch each cycle. We work with a number of automotive advertisers at a similar scale to Energizer, and the ones seeing the strongest seasonal lift are the ones who lock in JAS inventory before the summer demand window closes — especially for products tied to weather-driven behavior like battery and auto care. Looking at Energizer's digital mix, there's a significant concentration in Paid Social at $24.5M, and Online Video is sitting at $925K despite a $22.7M CTV investment — that gap is actually an opportunity. I'd love to spend 30 minutes with you mapping the JAS calendar for ARMR and the broader auto brands, and share how a modest rebalance toward premium contextual video could extend the reach you're already getting in CTV. Would the week of [specific date two weeks out] work for a quick planning call?
open_questions: For JAS planning, is Universal McCann managing ARMR and AC Pro as a consolidated automotive budget or as separate brand-level line items — and who owns the final allocation decision between Leyland's team and Lori Shambro's group?
The ARMR Display/Video opp has been at Verbal for $13,400 — is there a specific approval or scope change holding that from closing, and is the JAS extension being planned separately?
When Universal McCann evaluates the $24.5M Paid Social investment for Energizer's auto brands, what KPIs are being used to benchmark performance against the $22.7M CTV buy?
Are there Energizer brand families outside of automotive — batteries, lighting, personal care — that have separate digital budgets we haven't yet mapped a program against?

Does Energizer's planning calendar for weather-sensitive auto products like AC Pro and ARMR follow a fixed seasonal schedule, or is it responsive to actual weather forecasting data that we could help activate against?

sales_plays.summary: Equifax has won two consecutive PMP deals (2024 and 2025) at nominal value, signaling a test-and-learn posture. The expansion play converts that proven PMP relationship into a higher-commitment, premium direct buy across Weather Channel's full portfolio — targeting Manjush Varghese's digital transformation mandate.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins — 'Equifax has now run with us twice through the TWC Interactive PMP, in July 2024 and again in January 2025. Before we renew on the same terms, I want to show you what a direct buy unlocks that the PMP can't deliver.' Lead with Lisa Moakler Long at Digitas, who owns media planning and buying.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Digitas: 'With Equifax's $771K in digital and $329K in CTV already in market, what performance KPIs from the PMP runs are you still not hitting — reach ceiling, frequency control, or contextual targeting depth?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position Weather Channel's direct inventory as the answer to PMP limitations — guaranteed premium placement, first-party weather-intent audience segments, and brand-safe contextual alignment with Equifax's financial wellness and credit monitoring messaging.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Equifax's trailing 12-month total media spend is $26.2M, with $25.1M concentrated in Search. TWC's current share is nominal. A direct buy at even 1% of total spend — $262K — would represent a 260x increase over current PMP deal value and diversify Equifax's reach beyond search-dependent acquisition.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Lisa Moakler Long for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to review PMP performance data together and map a Q3 direct buy proposal aligned to Manjush Varghese's digital transformation priorities.

sales_plays.title: Search Budget Displacement — CTV and Display Capture
sales_plays.summary: Equifax allocates $25.1M — 96% of its tracked media spend — to Search, leaving CTV at $329K and Display at $34K severely underfunded. This displacement play makes the case to Digitas and Manjush Varghese that Weather Channel's CTV and contextual display can capture upper-funnel demand that Search alone cannot build.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a data observation to Digitas: 'Equifax is running $25.1M in Search against $329K in CTV and $34K in Display. That ratio tells us Equifax is harvesting existing demand, not building it. Weather Channel can fix the top of that funnel.' Reference the existing TWC PMP relationship as proof of trust.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Manjush Varghese or Lisa Moakler Long: 'Is Equifax seeing diminishing returns or rising CPCs in Search, and has leadership set a goal to grow brand consideration among consumers who aren't yet searching for credit monitoring?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position Weather Channel CTV and premium display as a demand-creation layer — reaching Equifax's target financial consumers during high-engagement weather moments (storm prep, tax season planning, financial stress events) before they enter the Search funnel, reducing Equifax's dependence on expensive Search CPCs.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Equifax's CTV spend is $329K today. Shifting even 2% of the $25.1M Search budget — roughly $502K — into Weather Channel CTV would more than double their current CTV investment and create measurable upper-funnel lift. Mobile App spend at $349K also signals audience-first thinking that TWC's mobile inventory directly complements.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive audit deck — delivered to Lisa Moakler Long at Digitas within one week — showing how peer FinServ brands are allocating CTV and contextual display alongside Search, with a specific TWC audience package mapped to Equifax's credit monitoring and identity protection product lines.

talk_track_opener: Hey Lisa, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working together on the Equifax PMP for the past two cycles, most recently closing in January 2025. I wanted to reach out directly because I think we're leaving real value on the table for Equifax and I'd rather surface that now than at renewal time. We've seen Equifax's media mix — $25.1M in Search, $329K in CTV, and two nominal PMP wins with us — and the story that tells is a brand that's very good at harvesting demand but hasn't yet invested in building it at the top of the funnel. Weather Channel sits exactly where that upper-funnel opportunity lives: high-intent, emotionally engaged consumers making financial decisions during weather-driven life moments. I'd love 30 minutes with you and potentially Manjush's team to walk through what a direct buy or a CTV expansion could look like for Q3 — nothing to approve, just a working session to see if the numbers make sense. Would the week of [specific date] work for a quick call?
open_questions: What performance metrics from the two TWC PMP runs — reach, frequency, conversion lift — are you sharing back with Manjush Varghese's team, and are those results hitting the benchmarks Equifax set internally?

With $25.1M in Search, is Equifax seeing rising CPCs or audience saturation in any of its core credit monitoring or identity protection keyword clusters that would justify testing upper-funnel demand creation?

Does Manjush Varghese's digital transformation mandate include a specific goal to grow Equifax's brand consideration among consumers who are not yet in-market for credit products — and is that measured separately from Search conversion KPIs?

Is Digitas currently evaluating any CTV or premium video partners beyond the TWC PMP for Equifax's 2025 upfront planning, and what would it take to move a portion of the Search budget into a guaranteed direct buy?

Given that Macquarium holds the Experiential AOR scope for Equifax, is there a connected digital-to-experiential campaign strategy where Weather Channel's contextual audience data could support a broader brand moment Equifax is planning in 2025?

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's CTV and premium digital inventory as a brand-safe complement to Equinox's heavy Paid Social reliance. With $823K already in CTV and $853K in Digital, there is a clear on-ramp to scale those lines — not start from scratch.

sales_plays.title: New Year Resolution Window — Early Lock-In

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a 20-minute call with Kali Cushing before end of August to present a Q4 2025 New Year campaign concept — framed as early-access planning, not a cold pitch. Offer to bring audience reach data specific to the January fitness consideration window.

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Direct Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Etsy has won two PMP deals (Jan 2025 and Jul 2024) at nominal value, signaling an active but undermonetized relationship. With $53.5M in CTV and $45.2M in digital spend, there is clear budget capacity to expand into premium, higher-CPM direct placements beyond the current PMP footprint.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two won PMP deals (Etsy-PMP-2025-TWC-Interactive, Jan 2025; Etsy-PMP-2024-TWC-Interactive, Jul 2024) in your opener to Laura Janness at Mediahub Worldwide. Acknowledge the partnership is active and ask how performance has tracked against Etsy's branding KPIs — Bombora shows Branding intent spiking this week, making this timely.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Laura: 'Given Etsy's $53.5M CTV investment, how much of that is currently running through guaranteed direct buys versus programmatic, and is there a threshold where you'd want more audience certainty around tentpole moments like Mother's Day or the holiday gifting window?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium direct inventory — weather-triggered, contextually relevant to Etsy's handmade/gifting narrative — as the natural upgrade from PMP. Etsy's sellers thrive when buyers are planning gifts around seasonal and weather moments; TWC's audience is in active planning mode during those windows.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost Mobile App IM & Video opp in Q1 2024 was valued at $100K — that's the floor of what a single premium unit can deliver. With Etsy's $45.2M digital budget and only $1.7M in online video currently, even a 5% shift of online video spend toward premium direct represents ~$85K in incremental revenue. Frame the expansion as closing the gap between PMP pricing and direct CPMs.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Laura for a 30-minute working session with Brad Minor's team before end of Q2 to map TWC's tentpole calendar against Etsy's gifting moments (Mother's Day, back-to-school, holiday). Offer to bring a custom audience overlap analysis using Etsy's buyer profile versus TWC's weather-engaged audience.

sales_plays.title: Mobile Video Win-Back via Agency Whitespace
sales_plays.summary: Etsy's Mobile App IM & Video deal was lost in Q1 2024 at $100K — likely to a competing mobile or social platform. With $25.1M in Paid Social and only $3.1M in Mobile App spend, there is a clear incumbent holding mobile video budget that TWC can challenge by offering weather-contextual mobile video at scale.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with Orchard (AOR Creative) as the entry point, referencing the lost Mobile App IM & Video opp from Feb 2024. Tell Laura Janness at Mediahub: 'We came close on the mobile video piece last year — I want to understand what the winning solution offered that we didn't, and whether the criteria have shifted heading into H2.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'The $3.1M in Mobile App spend is relatively small against Etsy's $25.1M Paid Social investment — is mobile video being evaluated as a standalone branding channel, or is it bundled into social buys right now? Who owns that decision between Mediahub and Orchard?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Displace the incumbent by positioning TWC's mobile video as a weather-moment trigger for Etsy's gifting use case. When it rains, people shop online. When temperatures drop, handmade gifts spike in search. TWC's mobile video can serve Etsy creative at the exact moment a buyer's mindset shifts toward gifting — something a social feed algorithm cannot replicate with the same intent signal.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost opp was $100K. Etsy spends $86.3M in Search and $25.1M in Paid Social — channels that capture demand but don't create it. A $150K–$200K mobile video test on TWC represents less than 0.2% of Etsy's digital budget and directly addresses the Branding intent signal Bombora flagged this week, which aligns with Brad Minor's CMO mandate around brand-building.

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a creative alignment call with both Mediahub (Laura Janness) and Orchard within the next two weeks, before Q3 planning locks. Bring a mock weather-triggered mobile video concept using Etsy's existing gifting creative to show the displacement case visually, not just verbally.

talk_track_opener: Hey Laura, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working together on the PMP side since mid-2024 and I wanted to reach out while the relationship is fresh heading into summer planning. We've got two wins on the books together and I think there's a real opportunity to build on that before Q3 budgets get locked.
open_questions: The two PMP deals we've won together are recorded at nominal values — how is Mediahub evaluating performance on those buys, and what would need to be true for you to shift a portion of Etsy's $53.5M CTV budget into guaranteed direct placements with us?

When the Mobile App IM & Video deal went to a competitor in February 2024, what was the primary reason — was it audience reach, pricing, or format capabilities, and has that changed heading into H2 planning?

Bombora is showing Branding intent for Etsy this week — is that tied to a specific campaign Brad Minor's team is planning, or is it a broader strategic shift in how Etsy is thinking about upper-funnel investment?
Between Mediahub and Orchard, who owns the final call on mobile video and online video channel selection — is that a joint recommendation or does one agency lead?

Etsy's online video allocation is $1.7M against a $45.2M digital budget — is that intentionally conservative, or is there a performance threshold that would unlock more video investment if the right partner demonstrated it?

sales_plays.title: Win Back Eversource: From PMP Trials to Scaled Digital
sales_plays.summary: Eversource ran four separate opportunities with us in 2023 — including a $75K TWC Interactive winter buy — but all closed as lost. This play re-engages Caroline Pretyman by acknowledging the prior relationship and showing what has changed in our targeting and audience capabilities since those deals fell through.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Caroline Pretyman referencing the Q4 2023 TWC Interactive winter campaign ($75K) and the three PMP tests from June, September, and December 2023. Acknowledge the relationship went quiet and open with: 'We had some activity together in 2023 — I wanted to reconnect and understand what wasn't working so we can come back with something more relevant.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask what specifically caused the 2023 buys to not continue — was it performance, measurement, internal budget shifts, or an agency decision? This surfaces whether the barrier was product, process, or relationship.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position against the gap: Eversource is currently spending $2.1M in digital (including $500K paid social, $444K display, $161K mobile app) but only $15K in CTV. If the 2023 PMP tests underdelivered on reach or targeting precision, reintroduce our CTV + weather-triggered audience solution as a more accountable alternative to broad display.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The $75K winter 2023 buy was a meaningful test. If we can show that a comparable CTV investment — even at 2x that level — reaches Eversource's residential customers during peak demand and outage communication moments, the ROI case is grounded in their existing $2.1M digital baseline.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Caroline for a 20-minute call before end of month to walk through what we've built since 2023 — specifically our utility-vertical audience segments and weather-trigger capabilities. Offer to bring a one-page comparison of what the 2023 PMP delivered vs. what a 2025 CTV test could look like.

sales_plays.title: Seasonal Planning Window: Customer Education Before Peak Demand
sales_plays.summary: Bombora shows Eversource actively researching 'Education' this week, consistent with a utility preparing customer-facing communications around seasonal demand, energy assistance programs, or outage readiness. This play positions a planning conversation now — before the summer or winter demand window opens — so Eversource can activate media in time.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the Bombora Education intent signal directly: 'We're seeing signals that Eversource is researching customer education content right now — that tracks with what utilities typically do ahead of high-demand seasons. Are you planning a push around energy assistance, outage prep, or usage management programs?'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Caroline: 'What does your customer communication calendar look like for the next 90 days — are there specific programs like bill assistance, energy efficiency, or outage alerts that need media support before the next seasonal peak?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Eversource's current digital mix skews heavily toward display ($444K) and paid social ($500K) but has minimal CTV ($15K). For customer education campaigns — especially around safety, financial assistance, and outage alerts — CTV and weather-triggered digital reach residential audiences at home, when the message is most relevant.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Eversource spends $685K in broadcast today. A CTV complement at even 10-15% of that broadcast budget ($68K–$100K) would extend reach to cord-cutters and light TV viewers in their service territory — a segment that display and paid social alone are unlikely to saturate.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a brief planning call in the next two weeks, framed as a 'seasonal readiness check-in' — not a pitch. Offer to bring a proposed media timing calendar showing when Eversource's customer education messages would land hardest relative to weather and demand patterns in their service area.

talk_track_opener: Hey Caroline, I'm [rep name] from Weather Company — you may recognize us from the campaigns your team ran with us in 2023, including the winter TWC Interactive buy and a few PMP tests across the year. We've made a lot of changes to our utility-vertical targeting and CTV capabilities since then, and I wanted to reconnect rather than just send another cold pitch. I noticed Eversource is showing strong research activity around customer education right now, which makes sense given where we are in the planning calendar for utilities. With $2.1M already going into digital and a significant broadcast investment, I'm curious — is there a specific customer communication initiative coming up, like energy assistance outreach or outage preparedness, where you're still figuring out the right media mix? I'd love to understand what's on your plate before I suggest anything.
open_questions: The 2023 winter TWC Interactive buy and the PMP tests all closed as lost — can you help me understand what the sticking point was: was it performance, measurement, internal priorities, or something on the agency side?

Who owns the media planning and buying decisions for Eversource's digital and broadcast budgets — is that managed in-house with you, or routed through an agency of record we should also be talking to?
With Bombora showing Eversource researching customer education content right now, what specific programs are you preparing to communicate — energy assistance, outage readiness, efficiency rebates?
Your CTV investment is currently $15K against $685K in broadcast — is that a deliberate allocation, or is CTV something the team hasn't fully evaluated yet for customer communication campaigns?

What does your seasonal media calendar look like for the next two quarters — are there specific demand windows, like summer peak or winter storm season, where you need to be in-market with residential customers ahead of time?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Direct Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Expedia has three consecutive PMP wins (2024, 2025, 2026) with TWC Interactive, signaling a stable programmatic relationship. The play is to convert that PMP foundation into higher-value direct and premium inventory — capturing more of Expedia's $356.2M digital budget and $98.6M CTV spend before the next annual renewal cycle.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Rosabel Murai at Team One referencing the three-year PMP run with TWC Interactive and open with: 'You've trusted our PMP inventory three years running — I want to show you what the brands sitting above that layer are doing differently with Expedia-level budgets.' Attach a one-page comp showing CTV and premium direct performance benchmarks for travel advertisers at $98M+ CTV spend.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Rosabel: 'Of Expedia's $98.6M in CTV and $54.9M in online video, how much is currently running through guaranteed direct deals versus open or PMP auction — and is Jochen's team satisfied with the brand-safety and contextual alignment they're getting today?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's premium direct and CTV inventory as the natural upgrade path: weather-contextual targeting aligns Expedia's travel intent messaging to real-time conditions (flight searches spike during weather disruptions), something PMP pipes alone cannot deliver at scale.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Expedia's digital spend is $356.2M with $95.5M in display and $54.9M in online video. Even a 5% reallocation from open/PMP to premium direct represents ~$17.5M in incremental addressable budget. Frame the ask as a $500K–$1M pilot against the CTV line, benchmarked against the existing PMP CPMs.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Rosabel for a 30-minute working session with her and the Publicis.Sapient digital team before the Q3 planning cycle to map Expedia's CTV and video allocation against TWC Interactive's premium inventory calendar — propose a specific date two weeks out.

sales_plays.title: Vrbo & Brand Family Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Expedia lost two non-PMP pitches in 2024 — a Holiday pitch (Q4 2024, $50K) and a Vrbo Always On pitch (Q3 2024, $50K) — suggesting a competing vendor captured those budgets. With Expedia Group operating multiple travel brands (Vrbo, Hotels.com, Cars.com adjacencies), the play is to displace the incumbent on Vrbo's always-on digital line and expand the relationship across the brand family.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the lost Vrbo Always On opportunity directly with Rosabel Murai: 'We pitched Vrbo's always-on program in Q3 2024 and didn't win it — I'd like to understand what the winning solution offered that we didn't, and whether that program is up for review heading into 2026.' This signals accountability and opens a displacement conversation without being adversarial.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'For Vrbo's always-on digital spend, who owns that relationship today — is it consolidated under Team One and Publicis.Sapient, or is Vrbo running a separate agency or direct buy — and what KPIs is that program being held to?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's travel-intent audience and weather-triggered contextual signals as uniquely suited to Vrbo's vacation rental category — travelers searching for beach or mountain rentals are directly influenced by seasonal weather outlooks, giving Vrbo a targeting advantage competitors cannot replicate on generic display networks.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost Vrbo Always On pitch was scoped at $50K — likely a test budget. Expedia Group's $136.8M in paid social and $95.5M in display suggest the always-on line is substantially larger in execution. Frame a revised proposal at $250K–$500K, showing cost-per-travel-intent-action benchmarks versus the incumbent's reported CPMs.

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a 20-minute call with Rosabel and the Vrbo brand team specifically — not a general Expedia meeting — to do a post-mortem on the Q3 2024 loss and present one updated data point (TWC Interactive travel-intent reach against Vrbo's core demographic) before the Q3 2025 planning window closes.

talk_track_opener: Hey Rosabel, this is [Rep Name] from TWC Interactive. We've been working together on Expedia's PMP for three consecutive years now — 2024, 2025, and just renewed again in January 2026 — so I wanted to reach out as a partner, not just a vendor, to share something I'm seeing across travel advertisers at Expedia's spend level. With Expedia Group running over $2.4 billion in total media annually, including $98.6M in CTV and $356M in digital, the brands getting the most out of that investment are the ones layering premium direct and contextual buys on top of their PMP foundation — not instead of it, but above it. I also know we missed on the Vrbo Always On pitch in Q3 2024 and the Holiday program in Q4, and I'd rather have an honest conversation about why than pretend those didn't happen. I have a specific idea for how we close that gap on the Vrbo side heading into 2026 planning. Would you have 30 minutes before the end of the month to walk through what the Vrbo always-on program looks like today and whether there's a fit for a revised approach — and separately, to map Expedia's CTV allocation against what we're seeing work for travel brands at your scale?
open_questions: For the Vrbo Always On program that went to a competitor in Q3 2024, is that relationship still in place heading into 2026 planning, or is it up for review — and what would it take to get TWC Interactive back in consideration?

Of Expedia's $98.6M in CTV spend, how much is currently running through guaranteed direct deals versus PMP or open auction, and is Jochen Koedijk's team satisfied with the brand-safety controls they're getting today?
Is the Publicis.Sapient digital team involved in planning the always-on Vrbo and brand-family digital budgets, or does Team One own that allocation independently?
The Holiday Proactive Pitch in Q4 2024 was also lost — was that a seasonal campaign that recurs annually, and is there a 2025 Holiday planning conversation already underway that we should be part of?

Across Expedia Group's brand portfolio — Expedia, Vrbo, Hotels.com — are media buys consolidated under a single agency plan or do individual brands run separate agency relationships and budgets that we should be addressing separately?

sales_plays.title: PMP to Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: EyeBuyDirect has two consecutive won PMP deals (2024 and 2025) with TWC Interactive, signaling a reliable but minimal footprint. With $26.7M in digital spend and only $141K in Online Video, there is significant headroom to expand into higher-impact, premium direct placements beyond the current PMP entry point.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins — 'Branden, we've now run two successful PMP campaigns together in 2024 and 2025. I want to show you what brands at your spend level — $26.7M in digital — are doing beyond PMP to drive incremental reach and conversion.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Of your $16.9M in paid search and $13.1M in paid social, how much is currently going toward upper-funnel brand awareness versus direct-response conversion — and where are you seeing the biggest efficiency gap?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): EyeBuyDirect's Online Video allocation is only $141K against a $26.7M digital budget — less than 1%. Premium direct video and high-impact display on Weather Channel properties can extend brand storytelling to audiences already in a purchase mindset, complementing the performance-heavy search and social mix.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'If we shifted even 3% of your $26.7M digital budget — roughly $800K — into premium direct placements, we can model the incremental reach against your current PMP baseline and show you the CPM efficiency delta versus your $6.6M display spend.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar before end of quarter to walk through a custom media plan that shows what a premium direct expansion looks like alongside your existing PMP buys? I can have a draft to you within 48 hours of our call.'

sales_plays.title: Displace Social Spend with Weather-Contextual Targeting
sales_plays.summary: EyeBuyDirect is allocating $13.1M to Paid Social — nearly half their digital budget — with minimal investment in contextually relevant environments. Weather-triggered and seasonal moments (UV index spikes, allergy season, summer sun) are high-intent windows for eyewear and sunglasses that social feeds cannot natively leverage.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): 'Branden, I was looking at your media mix — $13.1M in Paid Social is a significant commitment. I want to share how a few direct-to-consumer eyewear brands are reallocating a portion of social spend into weather-contextual environments and seeing stronger purchase intent signals, especially around seasonal sunglass demand windows.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When you plan for seasonal peaks — spring UV season, summer travel, back-to-school — are you currently using any contextual or weather-triggered signals to time your paid social or display buys, or is it primarily calendar-based?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Weather Channel's first-party weather and location data lets EyeBuyDirect serve eyewear and sunglass creative precisely when UV index, allergy forecasts, or seasonal transitions are top of mind for consumers — a context that $13.1M in social spend cannot replicate at the moment of environmental relevance.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Even a 5% reallocation from your $13.1M social budget — roughly $655K — into weather-contextual placements would give us enough scale to A/B test cost-per-visit and ROAS against your current social benchmarks. Given your existing PMP relationship, we can fast-track the data integration.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to send you a one-page brief this week showing two or three weather-trigger scenarios mapped to EyeBuyDirect's product lines — sunglasses for UV peaks, blue-light glasses for indoor season. Would Thursday or Friday work for a quick 20-minute review call with you?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Branden, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together through our TWC Interactive PMP deals in both 2024 and 2025, so you already know the quality of our audience and inventory. I wanted to reach out because I've been looking at how EyeBuyDirect's broader media mix is structured and I think there's a real opportunity we haven't tapped yet together. You're running $26.7M in digital spend with a heavy lean into search and social — which makes total sense for a direct-to-consumer eyewear brand — but your video and contextual display footprint is pretty lean relative to that overall investment. I'm seeing brands in your space use weather and seasonal signals to time eyewear campaigns around UV index spikes and allergy season in ways that paid social just can't replicate natively. Given that we already have a working relationship, I'd love to show you what a more integrated plan could look like. Quick question to make sure I'm building the right proposal: when you're planning for seasonal peaks like spring sunglass season or back-to-school, are you currently using any environmental or contextual triggers to time your buys, or is it mostly driven by the marketing calendar?
open_questions: Your PMP buys with us in 2024 and 2025 were both relatively small commitments — what would need to be true for you to consider a larger, more integrated direct buy alongside the PMP?
With $13.1M going into Paid Social, who owns the decision to test or reallocate budget into new contextual channels — is that you, Branden, or does it involve a media agency or performance team?
When you plan sunglass or UV-lens campaigns, are you currently using any weather or environmental data signals to time creative delivery, or is it purely calendar and promotional-event driven?
