sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Portfolio Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Geico has renewed its TWC Interactive PMP three consecutive years (2024, 2025, 2026). With $1.2M in CTV and $357K in Display already in the mix, there is clear appetite for premium digital inventory — but the PMP deals are logged at $1, signaling a floor-level entry point with significant headroom to grow into higher-value placements.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three-year PMP renewal streak — 'Geico has trusted TWC Interactive PMP every year since 2024, and we want to make sure the 2026 activation is the most impactful yet.' Pull the $1.2M CTV line as proof of Geico's willingness to invest at scale in premium video environments.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Elizabeth Paul at IPG MediaBrands: 'Beyond the PMP, where are the incremental budget pools for 2026 — is CTV the growth channel, or is there pressure to diversify into high-impact display or mobile app formats given the $38K mobile app spend we're seeing?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-contextual CTV and high-impact display units as a natural upgrade path from the existing PMP — same trusted relationship, broader reach, and contextual relevance that a pure PMP deal cannot deliver at scale.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Geico's trailing 12-month estimated media spend is $3.79M. The current PMP deals are booked at $1 each. Even a modest expansion to $150K–$250K in incremental CTV or display inventory represents a 150x–250x lift on the PMP line and moves Geico closer to activating against its full digital budget potential.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Elizabeth Paul for a 30-minute working session before end of Q1 2026 to present a tiered 2026 expansion proposal — PMP baseline plus two premium add-on options — so IPG MediaBrands can include it in Geico's upfront planning cycle.

sales_plays.title: Weather-Window Auto Insurance Demand Play
sales_plays.summary: Automotive insurance demand correlates with weather-driven events — winter road hazards, spring storm season, and hurricane season all spike claims awareness and consumer shopping behavior. With Geico's $1.2M CTV investment and $357K in display already active, a quarter-ahead planning conversation with The Martin Agency and IPG MediaBrands can align creative and media to these demand windows before competitors move.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Arianna Lewko (CMO) or Elizabeth Paul (EVP, The Martin Agency) in late Q1 2026 — ahead of spring storm season — referencing Geico's existing CTV investment: 'With $1.2M already committed to CTV, we want to make sure Geico's creative is in-market and contextually aligned when weather-driven insurance shopping peaks this spring.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When Geico plans media around seasonal demand windows — winter road conditions, spring storms, hurricane season — does the brief come from The Martin Agency creative side first, or does IPG MediaBrands lead with the channel strategy?' This surfaces who owns the timing decision.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered ad delivery as a precision tool for Geico's existing CTV and display budget — ads surface to consumers in real time when weather conditions make auto insurance top of mind, without requiring incremental spend.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Geico's $357K display and $61K online video budgets are currently running without documented weather-contextual triggers. Reallocating even 20% of that combined $418K into weather-activated placements could materially improve relevance scores and conversion rates during the highest-intent demand windows.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a joint planning call with Elizabeth Paul at The Martin Agency and the IPG MediaBrands team before March 15, 2026 — specifically to map Geico's 2026 creative calendar against TWC's seasonal demand forecast and identify two or three weather windows where contextual activation makes the strongest case.

talk_track_opener: Hey Elizabeth — I'm [rep name] from The Weather Channel. We've been working with Geico through the TWC Interactive PMP for three straight years now, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think we're leaving real value on the table heading into 2026. Geico is one of our most consistent partners in the automotive insurance space, and that tenure gives us a strong foundation to build on.
open_questions: The TWC Interactive PMP has renewed three years running — is the 2026 activation already locked into IPG MediaBrands' plan, or is there flexibility to layer in additional formats like weather-triggered CTV or high-impact display alongside it?
Geico's CTV spend is $1.2M versus $13K in standard digital — who at IPG MediaBrands owns the CTV budget allocation decision, and is that separate from the PMP buy?

When The Martin Agency develops seasonal creative for Geico — winter driving, spring storms, hurricane season — does the media plan get built around those windows, or does IPG MediaBrands set the calendar independently?

The PMP deals in our system are booked at $1 — can you help me understand how the actual investment flows through the buy, and whether there's a separate line item we should be mapping to for 2026 expansion conversations?

Arianna Lewko is listed as Geico's CMO — for a conversation about expanding the TWC relationship beyond the PMP, would that conversation happen at the agency level with you and IPG, or does it require direct CMO alignment first?

sales_plays.title: Summer Heat Lead-In: Expand PMP to Premium Direct
sales_plays.summary: Generac is an active TWC customer with multiple PMP wins in 2025. With summer heat season approaching and $13.3M in digital spend on the table, now is the time to convert PMP inventory into a premium direct buy with weather-triggered targeting before the demand window opens.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Cynthia Lambertson at Lockard & Wechsler referencing the successful 2025 PMP wins (Q2 and Q3) and flag that summer heat events historically spike generator consideration. Note that last year's Always-On Video opp was lost — position this as a chance to lock in premium video inventory before Q2 heat season fills up.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Cynthia: 'For the summer heat window, is Generac planning to activate against weather-triggered moments programmatically again, or is Amanda Teder's team open to a direct placement that guarantees share-of-voice when heat alerts fire in key markets?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered direct buy as the upgrade from PMP — guaranteed impressions during high-intent heat moments, not just auction-based access. Generac's message lands when homeowners are actively thinking about power resilience, not just browsing.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Generac spends $13.3M in digital and $3.0M in CTV. Even a 10% shift of digital spend ($1.33M) into a weather-triggered direct video package would represent a meaningful step up from the nominal PMP wins and directly compete with the $999K already allocated to Online Video.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Cynthia for a 20-minute call with Amanda Teder's team before April 30 to walk through a summer heat activation proposal — framed as protecting the inventory they need before it's gone.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Budget with On-Property Weather Video
sales_plays.summary: Generac allocates $7.9M to Paid Social — the single largest digital line item — yet TWC's on-property weather video offers contextually superior placement for a product that is literally driven by weather events. This play targets a reallocation conversation before summer planning is locked.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Amanda Teder directly, acknowledging the active TWC relationship and referencing the $7.9M Paid Social investment. Open with: 'We've seen Generac lean heavily into social for awareness — we want to show you a context where the weather IS the creative trigger, and you don't have to pay for it.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Amanda: 'When you look at your $7.9M in Paid Social, how much of that is upper-funnel awareness versus performance-driven, and are you seeing the cost-per-consideration you'd expect heading into peak generator season?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC on-property weather video as a displacement of inefficient social spend — when a severe weather alert fires in Atlanta or Houston, a Generac pre-roll on weather.com reaches a homeowner in an active decision mindset, not a passive scroll. Social can't replicate that contextual urgency.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Generac's total digital footprint is $13.3M, with $7.9M in Paid Social and only $999K in Online Video. Shifting even $1.5M–$2M of social budget to TWC on-property video would more than double their current video investment and reach audiences at the highest-intent weather moments — with measurable lift data TWC can provide post-campaign.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send Amanda a one-page weather-moment targeting brief specific to Generac's top 10 DMA markets by generator purchase index — request a 15-minute review call with her and Cynthia Lambertson by May 9 before summer budgets are finalized.

talk_track_opener: Hey Cynthia, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working together on Generac's PMP buys through 2025 and really appreciate the partnership. We've got a strong track record with the account across Q2, Q3, and into 2026 planning, and I wanted to get in front of you early before the summer heat season fills our premium inventory. We know Generac's business moves with the weather, and we want to make sure they're not left competing for auction inventory when the moments that matter most hit. I've been looking at Generac's digital mix — $7.9M in Paid Social, $999K in Online Video, and strong PMP activity with us — and I think there's a real opportunity to shift some of that social budget into weather-triggered on-property video that puts Generac's message in front of homeowners exactly when a heat event or storm alert fires. Can we get 20 minutes on the calendar before the end of April to walk through a summer activation proposal for Amanda's team?
open_questions: The Always-On Video opp was lost in June 2025 — can you share what drove that decision, and has anything changed in how Amanda's team is thinking about video for summer 2026?
Who ultimately signs off on shifting budget between Paid Social and other digital channels — is that Amanda Teder's call, or does it go through a broader Generac marketing committee?

For the summer heat season, is Generac planning to activate weather-triggered targeting again through PMP, or is there appetite to explore guaranteed direct placements that lock in share-of-voice before inventory tightens?
How is Lockard & Wechsler currently measuring the performance of Generac's $7.9M Paid Social investment — are you optimizing toward awareness, consideration, or dealer lead generation?
The big pitch opp for Q1-4 2026 is still open — what does Generac need to see in a proposal to move that from Lead/Idea Generation to an active buy before Q2 planning closes?

sales_plays.title: GE Appliances Ice Maker Season Expansion
sales_plays.summary: GE is an active customer with won deals across OLV and Ice Outdoor Activity in Q3-Q4 2025. This play expands the existing Opal Ice Maker relationship into a broader, higher-spend direct buy ahead of the summer demand window, converting current digital/display spend into premium on-property placements.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two won 'GE Q4 Ice Outdoor Activity' deals from September 2025 and the 'GE Appliances Opal Ice Makers_OLV' win from July 2025. Email Nina Akerley Lindsey and copy OMD's team noting: 'Your Q3-Q4 ice maker campaign performed well on our platform — we want to bring you a Q2 plan before the summer demand window opens, while inventory is still available at preferred rates.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask OMD: 'For the Opal Ice Maker line, what KPIs drove the Q3-Q4 buys — was it reach, conversion lift, or weather-indexed impressions — and are those the same metrics guiding the 2026 summer plan?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position premium on-property weather-triggered video and interactive units as a natural upgrade from the $301K Online Video and $566K Display spend currently running, tying ad delivery to high-heat-index days when ice maker purchase intent peaks.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): GE's trailing 12-month spend is $10.8M with $5.7M in digital. The existing OLV line is $301K — a modest share. A weather-triggered on-property video package at $500K-$750K would still represent less than 7% of total spend while concentrating impressions in the highest-intent window.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute working session with OMD's planning lead before March 30 (the open 'Elcano GE WAC Q2 Display' proposal deadline) to present a side-by-side comparison of current display allocation vs. a weather-indexed video expansion package.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Budget Into Premium CTV + Interactive
sales_plays.summary: GE is allocating $1.0M to Paid Social — a channel managed partly by WillowTree (AOR Digital) — while CTV sits at only $627K. This play targets Giant Spoon and OMD to redirect a portion of the social budget into higher-impact CTV and interactive formats where GE already has a proven win record.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Giant Spoon (AOR Creative + Digital Media Buying) noting the spend imbalance: 'GE is running $1.0M in Paid Social versus $627K in CTV. Given the interactive PMP deal we closed in January 2026, we have performance data that makes a strong case for rebalancing toward premium video environments — worth a look before Q2 plans lock.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Giant Spoon: 'Is the $1.0M Paid Social allocation being evaluated against the same brand-safety and viewability standards as your CTV buys, and is there a formal mid-year review where channel mix is on the table?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Use the January 2026 'General Electric Company-PMP-2026-TWC-Interactive' win as proof of performance. Argue that the interactive PMP format delivers the engagement depth of social — comments, dwell time, interaction rate — inside a brand-safe, premium environment that Paid Social platforms cannot guarantee.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Shifting even 25% of the $1.0M Paid Social budget ($250K) into CTV or interactive PMP would increase GE's on-property CTV investment by roughly 40% from the current $627K base, concentrating spend where GE already has a closed deal and measurable results.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to build a one-page channel comparison deck — Paid Social vs. CTV/Interactive PMP on brand safety, CPM efficiency, and engagement benchmarks — and request a 20-minute call with Giant Spoon and OMD jointly before the Q2 Display proposal (deadline March 30, 2026) is finalized.

talk_track_opener: Hey Nina — I'm [Rep Name] from [Company]. We've been working with your team and OMD for a while now, and we've closed several deals together including the Opal Ice Maker OLV campaign last July and the Q4 Ice Outdoor Activity buys in September. We also just wrapped the interactive PMP deal in January, so we have real performance data from your campaigns on our platform. I wanted to reach out now — before Q2 plans lock — because we're seeing a pattern in GE's media mix that I think represents a real opportunity. You're running about $1.0M in Paid Social and $627K in CTV, but your interactive and video buys with us have been the ones winning and renewing. Given that your total digital footprint is $5.7M and the Opal line has a clear weather-driven demand window coming up this summer, I'd love to show you what a rebalanced plan looks like — one that builds on what's already working. Would you or your OMD team have 30 minutes before the end of March to walk through a channel comparison and a Q2 ice maker proposal we've been building?
open_questions: For the Opal Ice Maker OLV and Q4 Ice Outdoor Activity campaigns, which performance metrics — reach, completion rate, or downstream search lift — are OMD and GE using to evaluate renewal and budget sizing for 2026?
Is the $1.0M Paid Social budget managed by WillowTree under their AOR Digital scope, and does Giant Spoon have visibility into that allocation when building the integrated media plan?
With the Q2 Display proposal currently in development, who at OMD or Giant Spoon is the final decision-maker on format mix — is it Steve Gordon's team or someone on the media planning side?
Does GE Healthcare's CMO Nina Akerley Lindsey have budget authority over the appliance and ice maker campaigns, or does that sit with a separate GE Appliances marketing lead?

Is there a formal Q2 or H1 planning review scheduled with OMD where channel reallocation — including a potential shift from Paid Social to CTV or interactive — could be introduced before budgets are locked?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Direct Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Georgia Pacific has two active PMP wins on record but has lost three larger direct buys totaling $320K. The goal is to convert the existing PMP relationship with Paras Shah and Sandy Song into a premium direct or sponsorship commitment by demonstrating incremental reach and brand-safety value above what the PMP delivers.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Paras Shah referencing the two active PMP wins (2024 and 2025) and note that Georgia Pacific's total media footprint is $656M — position the conversation as a portfolio review, not a new pitch. Subject line: 'Building on your TWC PMP — what's next for GP's digital mix.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Paras: 'The PMP has been running for two years — where are you seeing the strongest performance signals, and what KPI would justify moving a portion of the $29.5M display or $20.9M online video budget into a direct, guaranteed environment?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium direct inventory (weather-contextual placements, high-viewability OLV) as the logical upgrade from PMP — same audience, but with guaranteed delivery, brand-safe editorial context, and first-look access that a PMP bid cannot guarantee.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost $250K Q1 2024 direct buy and the lost $50K 1H 2026 proactive together represent $300K in revenue that went elsewhere. A direct package at even $150K would 3x the current PMP contract value and represent less than 0.1% of Georgia Pacific's $250.4M digital spend.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Paras and loop in Sandy Song at FIG for a 30-minute 'PMP performance + premium direct' review before end of Q2 — offer to bring a custom audience overlap analysis comparing PMP delivery to TWC's direct reach for Georgia Pacific's core consumer segments.

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Budget Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Georgia Pacific allocates $100.6M to Paid Social — the single largest line in their $250.4M digital budget. With signal loss, CPM inflation, and brand-safety concerns on major social platforms, there is a credible case to redirect a portion of that spend toward TWC's contextual and weather-driven digital environments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Send Paras Shah a one-page 'Social vs. Contextual' brief citing Georgia Pacific's $100.6M paid social allocation and recent industry data on Meta/TikTok CPM inflation and measurement fragmentation. Frame it as a portfolio diversification conversation, not a takeaway pitch.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Paras: 'With $100.6M in paid social, how are you currently measuring incremental reach beyond your social audiences — and is FIG benchmarking social CPMs against contextual alternatives this planning cycle?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's contextual OLV ($20.9M is already in Georgia Pacific's mix) and weather-triggered display as a brand-safe, measurable alternative that reaches consumers in high-intent moments — e.g., weather-driven purchase decisions for Georgia Pacific's paper, packaging, and building products lines — without the brand-safety and signal-loss risks of social platforms.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 5% reallocation of the $100.6M paid social budget equals $5M — even a 1% shift ($1M) would represent a 1,000x increase over the current PMP contract value and give Georgia Pacific a meaningful test-and-learn data set against a new contextual channel.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a co-branded 'Social Complement Study' with FIG's Sandy Song — a 60-day pilot using TWC's contextual OLV and display inventory, with a shared measurement framework, to be scoped and agreed before the Q3 planning cycle locks in August.

talk_track_opener: Hey Paras, I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Georgia Pacific for a couple of years now through your PMP — and I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a bigger opportunity we haven't fully explored together. We work with a number of large CPG and consumer brands at your scale, and we've been helping teams like yours think about how to get more out of their digital mix beyond programmatic. Looking at Georgia Pacific's footprint — $100.6M in paid social, $29.5M in display, $20.9M in OLV — there's a real question about where contextual, weather-driven environments fit in that stack. I know FIG is your AOR and Sandy Song is running point on the agency side, so I'd love to set up a quick 30-minute call with you and Sandy to walk through what we're seeing from the PMP data and share a couple of ideas for Q3. Would that be worth 30 minutes before the summer planning cycle locks?
open_questions: The PMP has been active since 2024 — what performance metrics is your team using to evaluate it, and has FIG benchmarked it against your direct buys on other platforms?
Who at Georgia Pacific or FIG owns the final decision on shifting budget from paid social into contextual or programmatic-direct environments for Q3?
The 1H 2026 proactive buy was lost in March — can you share what the winning proposal offered that ours didn't, so we can close that gap going forward?
With $100.6M in paid social, how is your team thinking about measurement and incrementality as signal loss continues to affect Meta and other platforms?

Does Georgia Pacific's building products division run separate media planning from the consumer brands, and is there a separate budget owner we should be talking to for weather-adjacent seasonal campaigns?

sales_plays.title: Urgent Care, Urgent Reach
sales_plays.summary: GoHealth Urgent Care is a net-new prospect spending ~$4.5M annually across CTV, digital, and search with no prior relationship. This play targets Emily Placella, Director of Brand, to open a conversation around audience quality and patient acquisition efficiency.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Emily Placella referencing GoHealth's $914K online video investment and $820K CTV spend — note that both channels are competing for the same patient-intent audience and ask if she's seeing overlap or frequency waste across those buys.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Emily: 'When you look at your $2.5M digital mix — especially the split between online video and CTV — how are you currently measuring which placements are actually driving new patient visits versus brand awareness?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position your platform's verified health-consumer audience segments and closed-loop attribution against GoHealth's challenge of proving that $140K display and $266K mobile app spend is reaching in-market patients — not just broad demos.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame it concretely: GoHealth is spending ~$4.5M annually; even a 10% reallocation of underperforming display and mobile app spend ($40K) toward higher-intent placements could meaningfully shift new patient acquisition cost — use category benchmarks to anchor the conversation.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 20-minute discovery call within the next two weeks: 'I'd love to show you how two other urgent care networks we work with are attributing walk-in visits directly to digital placements — can we find 20 minutes before end of month?'

sales_plays.title: Patient-Intent ABM Surround
sales_plays.summary: With $402K in paid social and $266K in mobile app spend, GoHealth is already trying to reach patients at the individual level. This ABM play positions your platform as the precision layer that makes those investments more accountable by targeting the specific decision-maker and their team with a tailored message.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Run a targeted ABM campaign against GoHealth's brand and marketing team — anchored on Emily Placella's title and GoHealth's known spend profile — using LinkedIn Sponsored Content and display retargeting that mirrors GoHealth's own $140K display investment, demonstrating the tactic firsthand.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): When Emily engages, ask: 'Your paid social spend is $402K — is that being managed in-house or through an agency, and who owns the audience targeting decisions day to day?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Connect GoHealth's virtual care expansion and walk-in center network to the need for geo-precise, health-intent audience targeting — show how your platform can activate against patients actively searching for urgent care in GoHealth's specific market footprints.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Illustrate that GoHealth's $751K search budget is capturing bottom-funnel intent but the $820K CTV and $914K online video spend is operating without the same precision — your platform can bridge that gap by syncing upper-funnel exposure to lower-funnel search behavior, improving ROAS across the full $4.5M portfolio.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer a personalized audience analysis — 'Give me your top five GoHealth markets and I'll come back with a patient-intent audience map showing the addressable reach we can deliver against your current CTV and video spend. No commitment, just data.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Emily, I'm [Rep Name] from [Company] — we work with health systems and urgent care networks across the country to help them connect their media spend directly to patient acquisition outcomes, not just impressions. I know that's a crowded pitch, so I'll be specific about why I'm reaching out to you. Looking at GoHealth's media footprint, you're running roughly $820K in CTV and $914K in online video simultaneously — that's a significant investment in reaching patients before they need care. The question I keep coming back to is whether those two channels are being measured against the same patient-intent signals or operating in separate silos. I'd love to understand how your team is currently thinking about attribution across that upper-funnel spend — are you seeing the CTV and video investment show up in new patient visit data, or is it still mostly a brand awareness line item on the plan?
open_questions: Your CTV spend is $820K and online video is $914K — are those buys being planned and measured together, or are they running through separate vendors with separate reporting?
Who owns the decision on adding or testing a new media partner at GoHealth — is that Emily's call alone, or does it go through a broader marketing leadership team or procurement process?
The $266K in mobile app spend is interesting for an urgent care network — is that focused on driving GoHealth app downloads, retargeting existing patients, or acquiring new ones?

With $751K in search running alongside $1.7M in upper-funnel video and CTV, how is your team currently connecting the dots between someone seeing a GoHealth ad and then searching for urgent care in their area?
Is there an agency or media buying partner involved in any portion of the $4.5M spend, or is the full plan being executed and optimized by the internal GoHealth marketing team?

sales_plays.title: Displace Display & OLV Spend to Premium Inventory
sales_plays.summary: Goldman Sachs is running $4.8M in Internet Display and $3.2M in Online Video through Carat USA. This play targets that $8M+ in programmatic/open-web spend and positions a direct premium alternative that delivers the brand-safe, high-attention environment a firm like Goldman Sachs requires.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Adam Rubin at Carat USA referencing the $4.8M Display + $3.2M OLV allocation and note that open-web programmatic environments carry measurable brand-safety risk for a financial institution of Goldman Sachs's stature. Ask for 20 minutes to show how peers in FinServ are shifting a portion of that budget to direct, premium inventory with verified audiences.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Adam: 'Of the $4.8M in Display and $3.2M in OLV, what percentage is currently running in open-exchange programmatic versus curated private marketplace deals, and what brand-safety guardrails does Carat have in place for Goldman Sachs today?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position direct premium inventory as the answer to brand-safety and attention gaps in open-web display — emphasizing contextually relevant, high-income, financially engaged audiences that align with Goldman Sachs's wealth management and investment banking messaging.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the opportunity around even a 15% reallocation of the $8M Display + OLV pool — roughly $1.2M — moving from open-exchange CPMs to a premium direct environment with verified audience quality, reducing wasted impressions against non-target demos.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Adam Rubin for a 20-minute inventory walkthrough before their next Carat/Goldman Sachs QBR — propose a specific date two weeks out and offer to bring a one-page competitive analysis of open-web vs. premium direct performance benchmarks in FinServ.

sales_plays.title: 72andSunny Creative Sync to Unlock Fiona Carter
sales_plays.summary: Bombora shows Goldman Sachs is actively researching Agencies this week, and 72andSunny holds the AOR Creative Social scope. A relationship-first approach through 72andSunny's team can create a warm path to Fiona Carter, CMO, before a cold outreach attempt.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to the 72andSunny account team handling Goldman Sachs — reference their AOR Creative Social scope and the Bombora signal showing Goldman Sachs researching agency partnerships this week. Position yourself as a media partner who can make their creative work harder by pairing it with premium, brand-safe inventory and high-income audience targeting.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask the 72andSunny contact: 'As you plan Goldman Sachs's social and creative roadmap, are there media environments outside of paid social where Fiona Carter has expressed interest in showing up — particularly for the wealth management or Marcus consumer lines?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position your inventory as a complement to 72andSunny's paid social creative — specifically the $10.6M Paid Social budget — by offering a cross-channel extension that reaches high-net-worth audiences in premium editorial contexts where social cannot.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Note that Goldman Sachs's $10.6M Paid Social spend is the single largest line in their $21.7M digital budget. Even a 10% cross-channel extension test — roughly $1M — into premium direct inventory would diversify reach against affluent audiences that social platforms underindex on.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask the 72andSunny contact to make a warm introduction to Fiona Carter or her direct report for a 30-minute media strategy conversation — offer to co-present with 72andSunny so the meeting feels additive to their agency relationship, not competitive.

talk_track_opener: Hey Adam, my name is [Rep Name] from [Company] — we work with several major FinServ brands helping them move programmatic display and online video dollars into premium, brand-safe direct environments where audience quality is verified and brand adjacency is controlled. I know Carat manages all media for Goldman Sachs, so I wanted to come to you first before going anywhere else. We've seen real traction with financial institutions that are re-evaluating open-exchange exposure, especially for a brand at Goldman's level. Looking at Goldman Sachs's media mix, the $4.8M in Internet Display and $3.2M in Online Video represent a meaningful portion of their $21.7M digital budget — and a lot of that is likely running in open-web environments that carry brand-safety risk. I'm also seeing Bombora data showing Goldman Sachs is actively researching agency relationships right now, which tells me there may be a strategic conversation happening internally about how media and creative partners are being evaluated. I'd love 20 minutes to show you what a curated private marketplace or direct premium deal could look like against Goldman's target audience — high-net-worth individuals and institutional decision-makers — and how it benchmarks against what you're currently getting from open exchange. Would you be open to a quick call before your next Goldman QBR?
open_questions: Of the $4.8M in Internet Display and $3.2M in Online Video, what percentage is currently running in open-exchange programmatic versus curated private marketplace deals, and what brand-safety standards does Carat enforce for Goldman Sachs?
Who at Goldman Sachs — Fiona Carter's team or Carat USA — owns the final decision on shifting digital display and OLV allocations between programmatic and direct buys?

Given 72andSunny's AOR Creative Social scope, is there a coordinated strategy between 72andSunny and Carat USA to extend Goldman's paid social creative into premium off-social environments, or are those budgets managed in silos?
With Goldman Sachs's Bombora signal showing active agency research this week, is there a formal agency or media partner review underway that we should be aware of before proposing a partnership?
Goldman Sachs runs $2.7M in Broadcast and $377K in CTV — is there a strategic intent to grow the CTV line as a complement to linear, and if so, who is leading that conversation internally?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Direct Upgrade: Scale What's Working
sales_plays.summary: GoodRx is an active PMP buyer (two won PMP deals in 2025) with $82.6M in total media spend and a $20.9M digital footprint. The opportunity is to convert low-dollar PMP commitments into a premium direct buy that captures more of their digital budget and unlocks sponsorship and branded content inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two won PMP deals (Q1 2025 and Q1 2026, both at $1 placeholder value) and the $50K App Install win from Q3 2025. Email Ryan Sullivan and copy Matthew Wakeman at Mekanism: 'We've had three active deals together in the last 12 months — I want to show you what a direct buy unlocks that PMP can't.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Ryan Sullivan: 'Of your $20.9M digital budget, how much is currently allocated to premium contextual environments versus open-exchange or PMP — and is there a performance gap you're trying to close?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position Weather Channel's health and allergy content as a direct-buy environment where GoodRx's prescription savings message lands with high-intent users actively searching for medication cost relief — something PMP targeting cannot guarantee at the contextual level.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): GoodRx's lost $500K Allergy Sponsorship (2H 2024) signals they've evaluated this inventory before. A direct buy at even 25% of that lost deal value ($125K) would represent a 2.5x step-up from the $50K App Install win and a meaningful test of premium contextual ROI against their $20.9M digital line.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Matthew Wakeman at Mekanism for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to review Q2/Q3 2026 planning calendars and identify one campaign where a direct contextual buy can be tested against their existing PMP baseline.

sales_plays.title: Allergy Sponsorship Win-Back: Displace the Incumbent
sales_plays.summary: GoodRx lost a $500K Allergy Sponsorship opportunity in 2H 2024 — likely to a competing health or weather-adjacent publisher. With $28.3M in Broadcast and $17.8M in CTV spend, GoodRx clearly invests in mass-reach seasonal health moments. The play is to identify who captured that budget and displace them with a differentiated Weather Channel allergy sponsorship package.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with the lost deal directly: 'We had a $500K allergy sponsorship on the table with your team in 2024 — it didn't close with us, and I want to understand what won that budget so we can come back with something more compelling for 2026.' Send this to Ryan Sullivan with a cc to Matthew Wakeman at Mekanism.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Who ended up running the allergy sponsorship in 2H 2024 — was it a health publisher, a broadcast partner, or a digital platform — and what did performance look like against your prescription savings conversion goals?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position Weather Channel's allergy forecast content as the only environment where allergy sufferers are actively seeking real-time symptom and treatment context — not just health content consumption. GoodRx's coupon and price-comparison message is uniquely suited to intercept users at the moment of medication intent, not just awareness.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): GoodRx spends $28.3M in Broadcast and $17.8M in CTV — they fund large seasonal buys. A $500K allergy sponsorship represents less than 1% of their total $82.6M media budget. Frame the ask as a low-risk displacement test: match or beat the incumbent's CPM and add Weather Channel's exclusive allergy index data integration as a differentiator no broadcast or health publisher can replicate.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive displacement brief — a one-page side-by-side of Weather Channel allergy sponsorship vs. the likely incumbent (WebMD, Healthline, or a broadcast network) — and ask Matthew Wakeman at Mekanism to review it on a 20-minute call before Q2 2026 upfront planning locks.

talk_track_opener: Hey Ryan, I'm [rep name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working with your team and Mekanism on a few deals over the past year, including the PMP buys and the App Install campaign that closed in Q3 2025. We have real history here, and I want to make sure we're actually earning a bigger piece of your digital investment, not just sitting in the PMP queue. Here's what caught my attention: GoodRx is running $20.9M in digital and $28.3M in broadcast, but our closed deals together have been in the $50K range — and we had a $500K allergy sponsorship that didn't land in 2024. That's a gap I think we can close. When your team is planning for allergy season or any health moment where prescription savings intent is highest, Weather Channel's contextual environment is the one place broadcast reach and digital precision overlap. Can I get 20 minutes with you and Matthew to show you what a direct buy looks like against your current PMP baseline?
open_questions: The $500K allergy sponsorship didn't close with us in 2H 2024 — which publisher or platform captured that budget, and is that relationship up for review heading into 2026 planning?

Ryan, who owns the final call on premium sponsorship buys at GoodRx — does that decision sit with you, or does Matthew and the Mekanism team have autonomous budget authority for deals in the $250K–$500K range?

Your digital mix shows $11M in Paid Social but only $235K in Display — is that a deliberate channel strategy, or is there a performance gap in contextual environments that you haven't found the right partner to solve?
The App Install campaign in Q3 2025 was a $50K test — what did performance look like, and what would a result need to show to justify scaling that to a six-figure direct buy?

GoodRx's prescription savings message is highly seasonal around allergy, cold, and flu cycles — how far in advance is your team locking Q3 2026 health-moment inventory, and is there a planning window we should be inside of right now?

sales_plays.title: PMP to Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Google has renewed PMP deals three consecutive years (2024, 2025, 2026) at nominal value. With $810.7M in digital spend and $215.5M in Mobile App alone, there is clear headroom to convert the existing PMP relationship into a higher-value direct or programmatic-guaranteed buy tied to Google's retail and AI marketing priorities.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three-year PMP renewal streak — 'Google has trusted us with a PMP placement every year since 2024, and I want to make sure we're delivering proportional value against your $810.7M digital investment. I'd love 20 minutes with Marvin Chow's team to explore what a more strategic partnership looks like heading into 2027.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask EssenceMediaCom Canada's team — 'Given that Hook owns AOR Digital for Google, how is spend currently being allocated between programmatic PMP and direct buys across your weather-contextual and mobile app inventory, and where are the gaps in reach or targeting precision?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a programmatic-guaranteed or direct deal against the pain of PMP price volatility and limited priority access — Google's $215.5M Mobile App spend and $39.3M Online Video budget are natural fits for guaranteed inventory with weather-triggered contextual targeting.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The current PMP wins are recorded at $1 each — even a modest direct deal at $250K–$500K would represent a 250x–500x increase in realized value from this relationship, still a fraction of Google's $810.7M digital footprint.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 20-minute planning call with Hook (AOR Digital) before end of this quarter to map Google's Q3–Q4 tentpole calendar and identify one campaign where a direct buy outperforms the existing PMP setup.

sales_plays.title: Displace the $200K Lost Q4 Budget
sales_plays.summary: Google lost a $200,000 Q4 2023 pitch and a $6,359 PMP deal in December 2023 to a competitor. With Bombora showing active Education intent this week and Marvin Chow now leading AI and Consumer Marketing, there is a fresh stakeholder angle and a content-aligned moment to recapture that budget before it gets locked into another vendor for Q4 2026.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with the Bombora signal as a warm, relevant hook — 'We're seeing Google trending on Education topics in our intent data this week, which lines up with what Marvin Chow has been building around AI literacy and consumer education. I wanted to reach out before your Q4 planning locks in, because we have contextual inventory that maps directly to that audience.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'The $200K Q4 2023 campaign went to another partner — what did that buy deliver, and is the team satisfied with how that incumbent is performing against Google's AI and consumer education messaging goals today?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position weather-contextual and CTV inventory as a displacement option — Google's $38.3M CTV spend and $140.1M Display budget suggest they value premium contextual environments, and the incumbent who won Q4 2023 likely cannot offer the same weather-triggered, moment-based targeting at scale.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Recapturing even the $200K Q4 2023 budget level would represent a 200,000x increase over the current $1 PMP deal value, and Google's total Broadcast spend of $285.1M signals willingness to invest heavily in the right premium environment.

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a 30-minute discovery call with Hook (AOR Digital) and EssenceMediaCom Canada before the end of this month — specifically to map Google's Education and AI consumer campaign calendar and identify which Q4 placements are still uncommitted.

talk_track_opener: Hey Angie, this is [Rep Name] from [Company]. We've been working with Google through the PMP program for three consecutive years now — 2024, 2025, and the 2026 renewal — and I want to make sure that relationship is actually delivering against the scale of what Google is investing in digital, which is north of $810 million. I also work closely with Hook and EssenceMediaCom Canada, so I have good context on how the AOR structure is set up. I'm not calling to pitch a product — I want to understand where the current setup is falling short. We're seeing Google trending on Education topics in our intent data this week, which feels aligned with what Marvin Chow is building around AI and consumer education. Given that, I'm curious — are there Q4 tentpole campaigns or AI-focused consumer moments where the current media mix isn't giving you the contextual precision or premium inventory access you need? Even 15 minutes to map that out would be valuable before your planning window closes.
open_questions: The Q4 2023 $200K campaign went to another vendor — is that incumbent still holding that budget for Q4 2026, and has the team evaluated their performance against Google's current AI and consumer education goals?

With Hook owning AOR Digital and EssenceMediaCom Canada owning media buying, who at Google's internal marketing team — ideally within Marvin Chow's org — is the final sign-off on premium direct and CTV buys above a certain threshold?

Google's Mobile App spend is $215.5M — how much of that is currently running through PMP versus direct or programmatic-guaranteed deals, and is there a preference to shift more toward guaranteed inventory for brand-safety or targeting reasons?
Bombora is showing Google trending on Education topics this week — is that tied to a specific campaign or product launch that has media budget attached, or is it still in the strategy phase?

The three PMP renewals have been processed at nominal value — is that a reflection of how the PMP is being used internally, or is there a budget owner we haven't connected with who could authorize a larger direct commitment?

sales_plays.title: PMP to Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Groupon has two consecutive won PMP deals (2024 and 2025) with TWC Interactive, signaling a stable relationship. This play converts that programmatic foundation into a higher-value direct buy — capturing more inventory, better targeting, and incremental reach against Groupon's $78.9M total media footprint.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Angela Tran at Investis Digital referencing the two consecutive TWC Interactive PMP wins (July 2024 and January 2025) and open with: 'Given back-to-back renewals, I want to show you what a direct buy unlocks that the PMP floor can't — starting with guaranteed inventory and first-look access during retail tentpole windows.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Angela: 'For Groupon's Q3 and Q4 planning — are there specific deal categories or merchant verticals, like restaurants or local experiences, where Emily Fobian's team needs guaranteed reach that programmatic can't promise?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a direct buy as the natural next step: Groupon's $14.2M in Display and $77K in Online Video show appetite for premium placements — a direct deal gives Emily's team priority access to weather-triggered and cultural-moment inventory that PMP auctions lose to higher bidders.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Groupon spends $78.9M in total media annually. The two PMP wins are currently booked at $1 each — placeholder spend that represents zero incremental revenue. A direct buy at even 0.5% of their $7.8M digital budget is a $39K+ deal with real CPM efficiency gains over open auction.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Angela for a 20-minute call with Emily Fobian's team before end of this quarter to walk through a direct buy proposal timed to Groupon's next retail tentpole planning cycle — offer to bring a one-page inventory brief specific to restaurant and local experience categories.

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Budget Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Groupon allocates $16.3M to Paid Social — the single largest line in their digital mix after Search. This play challenges the efficiency of that spend by positioning TWC Interactive's audience targeting and contextual reach as a lower-CPM, higher-intent alternative for Groupon's local deal discovery use case.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a data-led observation to Angela Tran: 'Groupon's Paid Social sits at $16.3M — nearly double their $14.2M Display investment. For a marketplace built on local deal discovery, we see a real opportunity to shift some of that budget toward audiences who are actively in a buying mindset, not just scrolling a feed.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'How is Investis Digital currently measuring Paid Social performance for Groupon — is the primary KPI new customer acquisition, app installs, or merchant category-level conversion? And where are the underperforming segments?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's contextual and weather-triggered inventory against Paid Social's passive audience: consumers checking weather before weekend plans are already in a local activity mindset — a natural fit for Groupon's restaurant and experience deals that Emily Fobian's team promotes.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If Groupon reallocated even 5% of their $16.3M Paid Social budget ($815K) toward contextually relevant, intent-driven placements on TWC Interactive, the CPM efficiency gain and audience quality improvement would be measurable against their existing attribution model — and we can build a test-and-learn framework to prove it.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a 30-day pilot: a defined budget ($50K–$100K) carved from Paid Social, targeting local experience and restaurant deal audiences, with a side-by-side performance comparison against their current Paid Social benchmarks. Ask Angela to set up a call with Emily Fobian's team this month to scope it.

talk_track_opener: Hey Angela, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've worked with Groupon through TWC Interactive on back-to-back PMP deals — July 2024 and January 2025 — so you know we can execute. I want to make sure we're actually moving the needle for Emily's team, not just renewing placeholder buys. Groupon is running $78.9M in total media, with $16.3M in Paid Social and $14.2M in Display — that's a serious investment. What I'm curious about is whether the current mix is doing the heavy lifting for local deal discovery, especially for restaurant and experience categories. Are there tentpole moments or seasonal windows coming up where Emily's team needs guaranteed reach that programmatic just can't deliver? I'd love 20 minutes to show you what a direct buy or a small Paid Social reallocation test could look like in real numbers.
open_questions: The two PMP deals with TWC Interactive are both booked at $1 — are those placeholder buys, or is there actual media value flowing through them that we should be measuring against Groupon's performance benchmarks?

For Emily Fobian's restaurant and local experience categories, who owns the final call on shifting budget between Paid Social and display or video channels — is that Emily's team, Investis Digital, or a joint decision?
Groupon's Paid Social is at $16.3M — what KPIs is Investis Digital using to evaluate that channel's performance for Groupon, and are there categories or audience segments where it's underdelivering?

With Q3 and Q4 retail tentpole moments on the horizon, has Groupon's team started locking in media commitments for summer experience deals or holiday gifting campaigns, and is there still open budget to allocate?

Groupon's Online Video spend is only $77K against $14.2M in Display — is there a strategic reason video is deprioritized, or is that a gap we could help close with a direct buy that includes video inventory?

sales_plays.summary: Guitar Center has run TWC Interactive PMPs in both 2024 and 2025. This play converts that programmatic relationship into a higher-value direct buy layered with weather and seasonal targeting ahead of key retail tentpoles like Back-to-School, Holiday, and NAMM-adjacent demand windows.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Kelly Thompson at Guitar Center referencing the two consecutive TWC Interactive PMP wins (July 2024 and January 2025) and open with: 'We've delivered on both PMP commitments — I want to show you what a direct buy unlocks that the PMP floor can't.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Kelly: 'When you're planning around Back-to-School or Holiday gifting — which are Guitar Center's two biggest retail tentpoles — how far in advance is Empower Ocean Media Group locking in placements, and are you currently getting first-look inventory on high-intent weather moments?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct buy as the path to premium, non-auctioned inventory tied to weather triggers — cold snaps driving indoor activity, rainy weekends spiking instrument browsing — that a PMP deal cannot guarantee at scale across Guitar Center's 304-store footprint.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Guitar Center's total digital spend is $39.3M, with $24.8M in Paid Social and only $4.2M in Display. A direct buy upgrade from the current $1 PMP baseline to even a $150K–$250K direct package represents a meaningful diversification of a channel that is currently under-indexed versus social.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Kelly for a 20-minute working session with Robbie Clyne at Empower Ocean Media Group before end of Q3 to map a Holiday 2025 direct buy proposal — specific ask: 'Can we get 20 minutes on calendar with you and Robbie before August 15 so we can bring a Holiday inventory recommendation?'

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Budget Displacement: Capture Guitar Center's $24.8M Social Overhang
sales_plays.summary: Guitar Center allocates $24.8M — 63% of its $39.3M digital budget — to Paid Social, a channel with rising CPMs and limited contextual relevance. This play positions TWC's premium audience and weather-contextual environment as a brand-safe, high-intent alternative that Empower Ocean Media Group should be diversifying into.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Robbie Clyne at Empower Ocean Media Group directly and lead with a market observation: 'Retail clients leaning 60-plus percent into Paid Social are seeing CPM inflation of 15–20% YoY heading into Holiday. I want to show you a displacement scenario for Guitar Center that protects reach while reducing social dependency.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Robbie: 'Of Guitar Center's $24.8M in Paid Social, how much is performance-driven versus brand awareness — and is Kelly Thompson open to testing a contextual weather-trigger buy as a brand awareness alternative ahead of Holiday?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Present TWC's audience as reaching the same 25–54 household decision-makers Guitar Center targets on social, but in a lean-back, high-attention environment — and with weather-moment targeting that aligns instrument and recording gear purchases to seasonal indoor activity spikes across Guitar Center's 304-store markets.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Even a 5% reallocation of the $24.8M Paid Social budget equals $1.24M redirected to TWC. Frame this to Robbie as a testable Q4 pilot — $200K–$400K — that can be measured against Guitar Center's existing PMP baseline from the 2024 and 2025 wins.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer Robbie a competitive displacement deck specific to Guitar Center's vertical and spend profile — ask: 'Can I send you a one-pager by Friday that shows how two comparable music or consumer electronics retail clients shifted budget from social to contextual and what the outcome looked like?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Kelly — this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel. We've had the pleasure of working with Guitar Center twice now through Empower Ocean Media Group, closing PMPs in July 2024 and again in January 2025, so I appreciate the relationship and want to build on it rather than just renew it. We've earned a seat at the table and I want to make sure we're using it well for you heading into the back half of the year. Here's what caught my attention: Guitar Center is running $39.3M in digital, with $24.8M of that concentrated in Paid Social — and the PMP relationship with us is still at the early-stage floor. Given that you're planning around major retail moments like Back-to-School and Holiday gifting across 304 stores, I'd love to ask: is Empower Ocean Media Group currently building any weather-contextual or seasonal trigger strategy into your media plan, or is that a gap we could help close together before Holiday inventory locks up?
open_questions: The two PMP deals we've closed were both at nominal value — is there a budget threshold or performance benchmark Guitar Center needs to see before Empower Ocean Media Group would consider a direct buy at meaningful scale?
Who owns the final sign-off on shifting budget away from Paid Social — is that Kelly Thompson's decision, or does it require approval above the Director of Integrated Marketing level?
How is Guitar Center currently measuring the performance of its $24.8M Paid Social investment, and is there a ROAS or CPM benchmark that a contextual buy would need to beat to earn a test budget?
Is Empower Ocean Media Group building a Holiday 2025 media plan for Guitar Center right now, and if so, what is the deadline for new partners or formats to be included in that plan?

Guitar Center operates 304 stores across diverse climate markets — is there any current strategy to align digital media timing or creative to local weather conditions, or is the media plan currently national and undifferentiated by market?

sales_plays.title: Tentpole Planning Window
sales_plays.summary: Gymshark is a high-velocity retail brand spending $17.3M annually across media. Engaging now — before Q4 tentpole and New Year fitness season planning locks — positions us as a strategic partner rather than a last-minute vendor.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Max Ackerman at Social Chain referencing Gymshark's heavy Paid Social concentration ($6.1M) and ask if they are actively mapping incremental reach channels for Q4 and New Year fitness tentpoles — the single biggest cultural moment in the fitness vertical.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Max: 'As you plan Gymshark's Q4 and January fitness-season budgets, which channels are you pressure-testing for incremental reach beyond Paid Social, and is CTV or streaming video part of that conversation?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our CTV and Online Video inventory as the complement to Gymshark's existing $780K Online Video and $177K CTV spend — showing how addressable, fitness-audience-indexed reach extends their Paid Social without cannibalizing it.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Gymshark's current CTV investment is $177K against a $17.3M total spend — less than 1% of budget. Even a modest reallocation of 5% from Paid Social ($305K) into CTV/streaming could meaningfully expand upper-funnel reach ahead of the January fitness surge.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Max for a 20-minute planning call in the next two weeks — before Q4 budgets finalize — to share a fitness-audience reach analysis specific to Gymshark's target demo. Offer to bring a one-page channel-mix benchmark for the activewear vertical.

sales_plays.title: Social Chain Relationship Bridge
sales_plays.summary: Social Chain holds the digital social scope for Gymshark and Max Ackerman is the Projects Director. A relationship-first approach through Max creates a warm internal path to Apryl McCall on the brand side, avoiding cold outreach to a net-new logo.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Max Ackerman at Social Chain directly — not through a generic agency contact — acknowledging Social Chain's digital social scope for Gymshark and expressing interest in understanding how they are thinking about channel diversification for the brand's $17.3M media mix.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Max: 'When Gymshark evaluates new media partners, does that conversation typically start with you and Social Chain, or does Apryl McCall's team on the brand side drive that process independently?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position yourself as a resource for Max — offer a competitive channel-mix analysis showing how Gymshark's $6.1M Paid Social and $2.5M Mobile App spend compares to peer activewear brands, framing it as something Max can bring to Apryl as a value-add from Social Chain.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Gymshark's $177K CTV spend relative to $780K Online Video and $6.1M Paid Social suggests an underdeveloped upper-funnel. Helping Max quantify that gap — and the audience overlap savings available through our platform — gives him a concrete story to bring to the brand team.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Max for a 15-minute intro call this week and offer to send a one-page Gymshark-specific reach gap analysis in advance. If he is open to it, request a warm intro to Apryl McCall for a joint planning conversation before Q4 budgets close.

talk_track_opener: Hey Max, I work with [Company] helping performance-driven retail brands like Gymshark find incremental reach beyond their core Paid Social investment. We work with a number of activewear and fitness brands and have a pretty clear view of how the channel mix shifts heading into the New Year fitness season — which is obviously one of the biggest demand windows in your vertical. I noticed Gymshark is running a significant Paid Social program — around $6.1M — alongside a growing Mobile App and Online Video presence, but CTV looks relatively light relative to the overall spend. I am curious whether that is a deliberate choice or just a gap that has not been fully explored yet. Would it be worth a quick 20-minute call to walk through a channel-mix benchmark for the activewear space and see if there is anything worth bringing to Apryl's team before Q4 planning locks?
open_questions: Gymshark is running $6.1M in Paid Social — are you finding that audience reach is starting to plateau, or is there still meaningful scale left in that channel heading into Q4?
When Gymshark evaluates a new media partner, does that decision sit with Max Ackerman and Social Chain, or does Apryl McCall's team on the brand side own the final call?

The CTV line is at $177K against a much larger video and social footprint — is that a deliberate test-and-learn posture, or is there appetite to scale it if the right inventory and targeting story is there?
How far out is Gymshark planning for the January fitness season, and are channel mix decisions for that window already locked or still in flight?
Gymshark has a strong content and community engine — are you currently using any of that owned content in paid CTV or streaming placements, or is it primarily living in social formats?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Inventory Expansion
sales_plays.summary: H&M is an active customer with two consecutive PMP wins (Jan 2025, Jul 2024). They carry $25.7M in digital spend and $3.8M in CTV — significant budget that is largely flowing outside our ecosystem. This play converts the existing PMP relationship into a broader, higher-value direct buy across premium inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins — 'We've delivered on the H&M PMP buys in July 2024 and January 2025. I want to share performance data from those campaigns and show Christopher Arce and the Universal McCann team how we can scale what's working into our premium direct inventory ahead of the back-to-school and fall fashion windows.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Universal McCann's Irena Milev: 'Of H&M's $17.5M in Paid Social and $2.5M in Display, how much is currently allocated to contextually relevant, brand-safe environments versus open-exchange programmatic — and is there pressure to improve brand suitability scores this year?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium direct inventory as the natural upgrade from PMP — contextually aligned weather-triggered placements that connect H&M's seasonal apparel drops (outerwear, transitional collections) to real-time consumer intent moments, reducing waste versus open-exchange buys.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): H&M's current Online Video spend is only $166K against $3.8M in CTV — a clear imbalance. A shift of even 10% of the $17.5M Paid Social budget (~$1.75M) into premium video and display direct buys would represent a 1,750x increase over current Online Video investment and diversify reach beyond social platforms.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we schedule a 30-minute working session with you and the Universal McCann planning team before end of Q2 to walk through a Q3 tentpole activation proposal — specifically back-to-school and fall transition — using the PMP performance data as the baseline?'

sales_plays.title: Reclaim the $150K Lost Pitch — Displace the Incumbent
sales_plays.summary: H&M's Q2 2024 pitch was lost at $150,000. With two subsequent PMP wins now on the books, the relationship has been rebuilt. This play uses the earned trust from those wins to re-open the conversation that was lost and displace whoever captured that budget.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out directly to Christopher Arce: 'Since the Q2 2024 conversation, H&M has run two successful PMP campaigns with us. I'd like to revisit the proposal that didn't move forward last May — the landscape has changed, and I think the case is stronger now, especially given where H&M's digital mix sits today.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When the Q2 2024 proposal didn't move forward, what was the primary objection — was it budget timing, incumbent contract lock-in, or a performance benchmark we didn't meet? Understanding that helps me bring the right solution this time.'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): If the loss was to a competing platform capturing share of H&M's $25.7M digital budget, position TWC's unique weather-intent data layer as non-replicable — no social platform or DSP can connect apparel purchase intent to real-time weather conditions at the ZIP-code level the way TWC can, making it additive rather than competitive.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The original lost deal was $150,000. H&M's trailing 12-month spend is $40.4M. A recovered and expanded version of that proposal — even at $300K–$500K — represents less than 1.25% of total media spend, a low-risk test with measurable lift potential against their $7.6M Search and $2.5M Display budgets.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to send you a one-page recap of what the two PMP campaigns delivered, alongside a revised proposal addressing the Q2 2024 gap. Can I get that to you by [specific date 5 business days out] and schedule 20 minutes to walk through it with you and the Universal McCann team?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Christopher, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company — we've been working with the H&M team through Universal McCann on the PMP buys that closed in July 2024 and January 2025, so we have some real performance data in hand. I wanted to reach out now because we're heading into the planning window for Q3 and fall, and I think there's a meaningful opportunity to build on what's already working rather than keep the relationship at the PMP level. Looking at H&M's overall digital mix — $17.5M in Paid Social, $3.8M in CTV, and only $166K in Online Video — there's a clear gap in premium contextual video that we're well-positioned to fill, especially for a fashion retailer where seasonal relevance is everything. I'd love to understand how Universal McCann is thinking about the Q3 tentpole calendar and whether there's appetite to run a direct buy test alongside the existing PMP activity. Would a quick 30-minute session with you and the UM planning team before end of Q2 make sense?
open_questions: When the Q2 2024 proposal didn't move forward, was the primary barrier budget timing, an incumbent platform commitment, or a specific performance benchmark we didn't meet — and has that barrier changed?
Of H&M's $17.5M Paid Social budget, how much is Universal McCann looking to diversify into non-social environments in 2025, and is there a brand-suitability or reach-saturation concern driving that?
Who at H&M or Universal McCann owns the decision to move from PMP to direct-buy relationships — is that Christopher Arce's call, or does it require sign-off from a broader brand or procurement team?

How is H&M currently planning media around Q3 tentpoles like back-to-school and fall fashion drops — is Universal McCann building those plans now, and is there a budget already earmarked for contextual or weather-triggered activations?

Given H&M's $3.8M CTV investment, is the team seeing incremental reach against the target demo, or is there overlap with the Paid Social audience that's creating pressure to find new, unduplicated inventory sources?

Email or call Rob Pennington directly: 'Rob, we've been running your PMP since March and wanted to share some performance benchmarks — and a packaging idea that could extend that reach into premium placements before the holiday planning cycle kicks off.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'With $20.8M going into mobile app and $25.7M into paid social, where are you seeing the strongest ROAS right now — and is there a format or audience segment you feel is still underserved heading into Q4?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium on-property inventory (weather-triggered video, homepage takeovers, branded content) as the next logical step above the PMP floor — guaranteed placements with contextual relevance that programmatic can't replicate, especially for seasonal grocery moments like holiday meal planning and Texas weather events.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Your PMP investment is already live. Layering in $150K–$250K in direct premium placements — comparable to what was proposed in the Spring Grilling and Mosquito/Sunscreen packages — gives you guaranteed share-of-voice during peak grocery intent windows, not just auction-based exposure.'

sales_plays.title: Reclaim the Lost Seasonal Budget
sales_plays.summary: H-E-B lost four consecutive seasonal opportunities totaling $475K (Pharmacy Q3/Q4, Favor Delivery, Mosquito/Sunscreen, Spring Grilling) — all in 2025. This play identifies where that budget went, surfaces a competitive displacement angle, and re-enters via Spark Foundry's Dancie Perugini Ware with a smarter seasonal packaging approach.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Dancie Perugini Ware at Spark Foundry: 'Dancie, we've had four proposals with H-E-B this year — Spring Grilling, Mosquito/Sunscreen, Favor Delivery, and Pharmacy — and lost all four. I'd rather understand why than keep pitching blind. Can we spend 20 minutes so I can learn what we're missing and whether there's a smarter way to structure these?'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'For the Favor Delivery and Pharmacy proposals specifically — was it a budget timing issue, a format mismatch, or did another platform win that spend? Knowing that helps us build something that actually fits how Spark plans H-E-B's seasonal calendar.'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): If budget went to a competitor (e.g., Hulu, YouTube, or a DSP), position TWC's weather-contextual targeting as uniquely suited for Texas-specific grocery moments — mosquito season, summer heat, hurricane prep — that generic platforms can't trigger with the same relevance. H-E-B's Texas-only footprint (435 stores) maps directly to TWC's geo-precision.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'The four lost proposals totaled $475K. If even two of those seasonal packages had run, H-E-B would have had weather-triggered reach across Texas during peak purchase intent windows — mosquito/sunscreen in May, pharmacy in Q3. That's audience alignment a national DSP buy can't replicate at the DMA level across 435 Texas stores.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to bring a revised seasonal packaging framework to Spark before H-E-B locks Q1 2026 budgets — something built around their actual tentpole calendar, not one-off proposals. Can we set up a working session in the next two weeks?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Rob, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with H-E-B through your Spark Foundry team since the PMP launched in March, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think we're leaving real value on the table. You're running $68.2M in digital — with $20.8M in mobile app and $25.7M in paid social — and the PMP is a solid foundation, but it's the floor, not the ceiling of what we can do together. We've had four seasonal proposals this year that didn't land, and rather than keep pitching one-offs, I want to understand your planning process better so we can build something that actually fits. What does your Q4 and Q1 2026 planning calendar look like right now, and who at Spark is driving the seasonal digital strategy?

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Publicis North America: 'Is the spring Flonase brief already written, or is there still room to shape the pollen data strategy before budgets are committed?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position Weather Channel's real-time and forecast pollen data products as the natural spring extension of the fall buy — same creative framework, same audience targeting logic, but timed to tree and grass pollen onset rather than ragweed. Emphasize that contextual pollen triggers drive measurable lift in OTC allergy purchase intent.

A spring mirror buy — Forecast Pollen + Current Pollen + Health display — at comparable unit value could double the annual Flonase display investment with TWC at minimal incremental planning cost for Publicis.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 20-minute working session with Claire Edmondson and the Publicis media buying lead before January 31 to walk through the spring pollen calendar and lock a first-look on inventory — framed as a planning call, not a pitch.

sales_plays.title: TUMS Grilling Win-Back via Programmatic-to-Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: The $100K TUMS Grilling Forecast opportunity was lost on July 11, 2025. The likely displacement was a competing contextual or programmatic vendor. Re-enter now by repositioning TWC's direct weather-trigger inventory as superior to programmatic proxies — and use the spring planning cycle as the reset moment before summer grilling briefs are written again.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the lost TUMS Grilling Forecast opp directly with Publicis North America's media buying contact: 'We lost the TUMS grilling brief last July — I want to understand what won and whether there's a path to earn that back for summer 2026. I also want to show you what a direct weather-trigger buy looks like versus what you may have run programmatically.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When the TUMS grilling campaign ran last summer, how was weather-based contextual targeting executed — was it programmatic through a DSP, or direct with a weather data provider? And did the team measure any correlation between grilling-weather days and TUMS sales velocity?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): If the displacement was a DSP-based weather segment, position TWC's direct inventory as the source-of-truth that DSPs resell at a markup with latency. TWC's forecast triggers fire at the moment of grilling intent — not 24 hours later via a third-party segment. For a digestive brand tied to food occasions, timing precision is the entire value proposition.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost TUMS opp was $100K. Haleon's digital display spend is $22.4M annually. A direct weather-triggered TUMS summer program — grilling forecast + heat index triggers across mobile and display — is a rounding error against total digital spend but a meaningful share-of-wallet recovery for TWC. Frame it as a $150-200K test with clear measurement against grilling-day foot traffic or e-commerce lift.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send a one-page TUMS grilling trigger proposal — showing specific weather conditions, DMAs, and flight dates — to the Publicis media buying lead by February 14, ahead of any summer brief kickoff. Ask: 'Who should I address it to on the TUMS brand team at Publicis?'

Looking at your digital footprint — $187M in digital spend annually and a proven Flonase display playbook from fall — it feels like spring is the natural expansion. I also noticed the TUMS grilling brief didn't come our way last summer, and I'd love to understand what the team ended up running so we can make a stronger case for 2026. Can we get 20 minutes on the calendar before the end of January to walk through the spring pollen calendar and the TUMS opportunity together?

With Merkle holding the research AOR, is there any first-party or syndicated data showing a correlation between pollen count days and Flonase e-commerce or retail lift that we could use to sharpen the spring targeting logic?

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Steve: 'Of Hanesbrands' $8.2M in CTV, how much is currently running on guaranteed direct versus auction-based PMP, and is Mike Faircloth's ecommerce team setting separate KPIs from the brand side?'
