Step 4 (Value Quantification): Hanesbrands' trailing 12-month spend is $17.26M total; their PMP deals closed at nominal $1 value, meaning the measurable direct revenue opportunity is essentially uncaptured. Even a 10% shift of their $8.2M CTV budget to direct premium represents an $820K incremental opportunity.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Steve DiRado for a 20-minute working session in the next two weeks to walk through a Q3 premium inventory proposal — offer to bring a custom audience overlap analysis for Hanesbrands' core underwear and activewear lines before Hearts & Science finalizes upfront buys.

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Budget Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Hanesbrands allocates $2.5M to Paid Social managed by Elevate as AOR Digital — a budget that can be partially displaced by TWC's mobile and digital display capabilities, which offer weather-contextual targeting that social platforms cannot match for a basics apparel brand.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Steve DiRado at Hearts & Science and separately flag Elevate's $2.5M paid social scope — reference that Hanesbrands' $106K mobile app spend through TWC is dramatically underweight relative to their overall digital mix, and propose a side-by-side efficiency comparison.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'How is Elevate currently measuring incrementality on the $2.5M paid social budget, and is there a portion of that spend that's been flagged as underperforming or up for reallocation in H2?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered mobile and display units as a performance alternative to social — when temperatures drop or a cold snap hits a key DMA, Hanesbrands' thermal underwear and fleece lines are contextually relevant in a way that social feed placements cannot replicate in real time.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Hanesbrands spends $1.6M on Internet Display and $106K on Mobile App through current channels — shifting even 15% of the $2.5M paid social budget ($375K) into weather-contextual mobile and display would more than triple their current TWC mobile investment and create a measurable test case for Elevate to present to Mike Faircloth.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a 30-day pilot framework to Steve DiRado at Hearts & Science — a defined $150K weather-triggered mobile test against one Hanesbrands brand line (e.g., Champion activewear or Hanes Comfort Flex) with a clear incrementality read before Q4 planning locks.

talk_track_opener: Hey Steve, I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company — we've been working with Hanesbrands through the PMP relationship that's been active since late 2023, and I wanted to connect directly with you given your role in client business transformation at Hearts & Science. We've seen two consecutive PMP wins with Hanesbrands in 2024 and early 2025, and I think there's a meaningful step-up conversation worth having before upfront commitments get finalized. Looking at Hanesbrands' media mix — $8.2M in CTV, $2.5M in paid social, and $7.8M in digital overall — there's a real gap between what the current PMP structure captures and what premium direct and weather-contextual inventory could deliver for a basics apparel brand that lives and dies by seasonal demand shifts. I'd love 20 minutes to walk you through what a Q3 and Q4 direct buy could look like, and specifically how we'd size a test that Elevate could measure cleanly against the paid social baseline. Would the week of [specific date two weeks out] work for a quick working session?
open_questions: Of Hanesbrands' $8.2M in CTV spend, how much is currently running on guaranteed direct placements versus auction-based PMP, and is there a performance gap between the two that's been flagged internally?
Is Mike Faircloth's ecommerce team at American Casualwear setting media KPIs independently from the brand side, and does Hearts & Science manage both budget pools or just the brand media?
How is Elevate measuring incrementality on the $2.5M paid social budget, and is any portion of that allocation under review for reallocation heading into H2 planning?

As Hearts & Science finalizes upfront commitments for Q3 and Q4, what does Hanesbrands' approval process look like for adding a new premium direct line item — does it require sign-off from Faircloth directly or does Steve DiRado have discretion?

Has Hanesbrands tested weather-contextual or moment-based targeting against any of their seasonal campaigns — back-to-school, holiday, or cold-weather activewear — and if so, who owns that measurement framework?

sales_plays.title: Digital Mix Upgrade: From Paid Social to Premium Video
sales_plays.summary: Harbor Freight Tools is an active customer with $99.3M in digital spend, but $64.8M of that is concentrated in Paid Social with only $2.4M in Online Video. This play targets Georgia Douglas to shift a portion of that social budget into higher-impact premium video inventory before the spring/summer tool-buying season.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Georgia Douglas referencing the current relationship and a specific observation: 'Georgia, we noticed Harbor Freight is putting $64.8M into Paid Social versus just $2.4M in Online Video — we think there's a real efficiency story worth exploring before Q2 demand picks up for tools and outdoor projects.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When you look at your Paid Social performance today, what's your cost-per-consideration metric versus what you're seeing from video placements — and is there a threshold where you'd reallocate?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position premium online video as the bridge between Harbor Freight's existing $22.3M CTV investment and its underweighted $2.4M Online Video line — a natural extension that reaches the same DIY and automotive audience with higher attention and brand recall than social feed formats.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'If we moved even 10% of your $64.8M Paid Social budget — roughly $6.5M — into premium video, and video delivers 2x the brand recall lift we've seen in comparable tool/automotive verticals, that's a meaningful efficiency gain on a spend base you're already committing.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar in the next two weeks to walk through a channel mix analysis specific to Harbor Freight's Q2 planning cycle? I'll bring the data, you bring your Q2 goals.'

sales_plays.title: Win Back Q4 Budget: Displace the Incumbent from Five Lost Pitches
sales_plays.summary: Harbor Freight Tools has five consecutive Q4/2025 lost opportunities totaling $25,000 — all marked 'Sales Pitch | Lost.' This play targets understanding what displaced us and repositioning with a differentiated angle tied to Harbor Freight's active Retail Technology intent signal and their $33.5M Search spend.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the pattern directly with Georgia Douglas: 'Georgia, we've had five conversations with your team in Q4 that didn't land — rather than pitch again, I'd like to understand what the winning solution offered that we didn't. We're seeing Harbor Freight showing active intent around Retail Technology right now and I think there's a connection to your Search strategy worth discussing.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'In the Q4 decisions that went another direction, was the gap primarily audience reach, pricing, attribution capabilities, or something else — and is that same vendor now handling your $33.5M Search budget?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): If the incumbent won on attribution or retail media measurement, position against that gap directly: offer a closed-loop retail attribution solution that connects Harbor Freight's $3.4M Display and $3.7M Mobile App spend to in-store and online conversion — something a pure social or search vendor can't provide end-to-end.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Harbor Freight is spending $99.3M digitally. If the incumbent can't connect your Display and Mobile App spend to actual tool purchases — even a 5% improvement in attributed conversion efficiency on that base is worth roughly $5M in recaptured media value.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'm not asking for a full pitch meeting — just a 20-minute debrief call this week so I can understand what the winning solution looked like. If there's no gap, I'll say so. If there is, I'll come back with something specific. Does Thursday or Friday work for you?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Georgia, I'm [Rep Name] — we've been working with Harbor Freight for a while now and I want to make sure we're actually adding value to your planning, not just showing up with decks. I know your team has a serious media operation: $99.3M in digital, $22.3M in CTV, $70M in broadcast — you're not a brand that needs to be educated on reach. What I do want to talk about is a specific imbalance I'm seeing in your mix. You're running $64.8M through Paid Social and only $2.4M in Online Video — and given that Harbor Freight's audience skews toward hands-on DIY and automotive buyers who are actively researching tools, I think there's a premium video story that your current mix is leaving on the table. I'm also seeing active intent signals around Retail Technology from your team this week, which makes me think there's a measurement or attribution conversation worth having alongside the media mix one. Can we find 30 minutes before your Q2 plan locks to walk through what a rebalanced digital mix could look like for Harbor Freight specifically?
open_questions: In the five Q4/2025 pitches that went another direction, what did the winning vendor offer that tipped the decision — was it pricing, attribution, audience targeting, or something else?
Who owns the decision on digital channel mix at Harbor Freight — does Georgia Douglas control the full $99.3M digital budget, or is Search managed separately by a different stakeholder or agency?

What's Harbor Freight's current measurement framework for Paid Social — are you tracking brand lift and consideration, or primarily direct response and ROAS, and how does that compare to your CTV measurement approach?

Is there an agency currently handling media planning or buying for Harbor Freight's digital spend, and if so, are they involved in the Retail Technology evaluation showing up in your intent data this week?

As you plan for Q2 and the spring tool-buying season, what does success look like for Harbor Freight's media mix — are you trying to grow brand consideration among new DIY buyers, defend share with existing customers, or both?

sales_plays.title: Tentpole Planning Window
sales_plays.summary: Haribo's $36M broadcast-heavy mix and Bombora intent around Retail Technology suggest active planning. CPG confectionery brands like Haribo live and die by seasonal tentpoles — Halloween, Valentine's Day, Easter. This play opens a planning conversation before those windows lock, positioning our inventory as a complement to their broadcast spend.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Seth Klugherz referencing Haribo's strong broadcast investment ($36M) and the contrast with their relatively light CTV ($545K) and digital footprint ($870K). Note that confectionery brands are typically finalizing Q4 Halloween and Q1 Valentine's/Easter media plans right now and ask if he has 20 minutes to share how Haribo is thinking about the digital layer this cycle.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Seth: 'As you plan around Halloween and Valentine's Day, where does digital video and CTV fit in your current media model — are those channels being planned through BarkleyOKRP, or is that still largely a broadcast-first conversation?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our CTV and digital video solutions as the incremental reach layer on top of Haribo's $36M broadcast base — targeting households that broadcast misses, with frequency control and audience segmentation that linear TV cannot provide for a brand like Haribo that needs to reach both parents and kids.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Haribo's CTV spend is $545K against $36M in broadcast — roughly 1.5% of their video budget. Even a modest rebalancing to 5-8% CTV would represent $1.8M–$2.9M in incremental digital video investment. Frame this as a test-and-learn budget, not a reallocation ask.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 20-minute discovery call with Seth Klugherz before end of month — specifically to understand how Haribo is structuring its tentpole media calendar so we can bring a relevant proposal before agency budgets lock.

sales_plays.title: BarkleyOKRP Agency Door
sales_plays.summary: BarkleyOKRP holds Haribo's AOR Digital Social and Public Relations scope, making them the most direct agency path into Haribo's digital budget decisions. Since there is no prior CRM history with Haribo, warming the relationship through Mark Shevitz at BarkleyOKRP's strategy layer is a lower-friction entry point than going direct to Seth Klugherz cold.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Mark Shevitz, Chief Strategy Officer at BarkleyOKRP, referencing that his team holds digital and social AOR scope for Haribo. Open with a strategic observation: Haribo's digital spend ($870K) is significantly underleveraged relative to their $36M broadcast investment, and Bombora is showing active Retail Technology intent from Haribo this week — a signal worth a conversation.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Mark: 'As you think about Haribo's digital roadmap for the next two tentpole windows, is the current digital budget structured to grow, or is the broadcast line still absorbing most of the incremental investment?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our platform as a tool BarkleyOKRP can bring to Haribo as a proof point — helping them demonstrate digital incrementality against Haribo's broadcast base, which strengthens BarkleyOKRP's strategic value to the client.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Haribo's $870K digital spend versus $36M broadcast represents a 2.4% digital ratio — well below CPG category norms. If BarkleyOKRP can help shift even $500K–$1M of that broadcast budget into measurable digital video, that is a meaningful agency win and a story Mark can bring to Seth Klugherz.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Mark for a 30-minute working session — framed as a strategic briefing on how other CPG confectionery brands are structuring their digital-to-broadcast mix heading into tentpole season — with the explicit goal of identifying whether there is a joint opportunity to bring to Seth at Haribo.

talk_track_opener: Hey Seth, I'm reaching out because we work with a number of CPG brands in the confectionery and snack space, and I noticed something in Haribo's media profile that I thought was worth a quick conversation. Your broadcast investment is substantial — $36 million is a serious linear commitment — and that tells me Haribo is serious about reach and brand awareness at scale. That's exactly the kind of advertiser we build solutions for.
open_questions: How is Haribo currently thinking about the balance between broadcast and digital video — is there an internal mandate to grow the digital share, or is the $36M broadcast line expected to hold as the primary vehicle?
For tentpole windows like Halloween and Valentine's Day, who owns the digital media planning decision — is that Seth Klugherz directly, or does BarkleyOKRP have budget authority on the digital side?
The Bombora signal we're seeing around Retail Technology is interesting — is Haribo actively evaluating retail media networks or in-store digital as part of a broader commerce strategy?
With three agencies holding different scopes, how does Haribo coordinate digital and broadcast planning — is there a unified media calendar or does each agency plan independently?
Haribo's online video spend is currently $25K — is that a deliberate test budget, or is video digital simply not a priority channel yet for the brand?

sales_plays.title: Employee Benefits Brand Expansion via Premium Inventory
sales_plays.summary: Hartford is an active PMP customer with two consecutive won deals. Laura Marzi owns the Employee Benefits CMO seat, and Crossmedia handles media buying — a clear path to expand beyond PMP into higher-impact, direct-sold inventory tied to the Employee Benefits portfolio.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Peyton Sutton at Crossmedia referencing the two consecutive PMP wins (July 2024 and January 2025) and note that Hartford's $7.9M digital footprint — including $8.0M in Online Video and $26.1M in Paid Social — signals appetite for premium brand environments beyond programmatic. Ask for 20 minutes to show what direct-sold inventory could add to the mix.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Peyton: 'For the Employee Benefits line Laura Marzi is driving, where is Crossmedia seeing the biggest gap between reach and qualified audience quality right now — is it in the awareness layer or closer to consideration?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct-sold, contextually relevant inventory against the commoditized reach Hartford is buying programmatically. With Bombora showing Hartford surging on Education intent this week, align Employee Benefits messaging to audiences actively researching education-linked benefits topics.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Hartford's current PMP buys are logged at $1 each — nominal test spend. Against a $7.9M digital budget and $26.1M in Paid Social, even a 3-5% reallocation to direct-sold premium inventory represents a $240K–$400K incremental opportunity. Frame it as a brand-safety and audience-quality upgrade, not a budget add.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Peyton for a 20-minute call by end of week to walk through a custom audience proposal for the Employee Benefits line — offer to bring a one-page inventory map before the call so Crossmedia can pressure-test fit ahead of time.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Waste with Targeted Premium Digital
sales_plays.summary: Hartford is spending $26.1M in Paid Social — the single largest digital line item — a channel with rising CPMs, brand-safety concerns, and limited contextual relevance for insurance and benefits buyers. This play targets Crossmedia's planning team to redirect a portion of that budget toward premium, intent-aligned digital environments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a data-led observation to Peyton Sutton: 'Hartford is running $26.1M through Paid Social — that's nearly 3x the Online Video spend and more than 6x Internet Display. We're seeing a lot of FinServ advertisers quietly pulling back on social CPMs and reinvesting in contextual environments where insurance and benefits intent is higher. Worth a conversation?'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'When Crossmedia evaluates the $26.1M Paid Social allocation for Hartford, what performance benchmarks are you using to justify that weight — is it cost-per-lead, brand lift, or something else — and how is it holding up against other channels?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium digital inventory as a contextually safer, intent-matched alternative. With Hartford's Bombora Education surge active this week, audiences researching education-linked financial and benefits topics are reachable in brand-safe environments — something Paid Social cannot guarantee at scale for a regulated FinServ brand.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 10% reallocation of the $26.1M Paid Social budget equals $2.61M in redirected spend. Even a 5% shift — $1.3M — would represent a significant net-new revenue opportunity relative to Hartford's current $1 PMP deal value on record. Frame the conversation around efficiency and brand safety, not just reach.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive audit — offer to build a side-by-side showing Hartford's current Paid Social CPM benchmarks versus TWC's premium digital CPMs for FinServ and Benefits audiences. Ask Peyton for a 30-minute working session within two weeks, before Crossmedia's next planning cycle locks.

talk_track_opener: Hey Peyton, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Crossmedia on Hartford's PMP buys — two deals closed, most recently in January — so you know we can execute. I wanted to reach out because I've been looking at Hartford's broader digital footprint and I think there's a real opportunity we haven't tapped yet. Hartford is running $26.1M in Paid Social and $8.0M in Online Video, and we're also seeing Bombora data showing Hartford surging on Education intent right now — which maps directly to Laura Marzi's Employee Benefits portfolio. Given that context, I'm curious: where is Crossmedia currently seeing the biggest performance gap for Hartford in digital — is it audience quality, brand safety, or something else entirely? I'd love 20 minutes to show you what we're seeing.

Hartford's Paid Social spend is $26.1M — nearly 3x Online Video. What performance benchmarks is Crossmedia using to justify that weighting, and has brand safety in regulated FinServ categories come up as a concern?
The two TWC PMP deals are logged at $1 nominal value — were those test buys or placeholder entries? What would it take to move Hartford from PMP test spend to a direct-sold commitment?

With Laura Marzi focused on Employee Benefits and Bombora showing Hartford surging on Education intent this week, is there an active campaign or RFP in flight for benefits awareness that we should be positioning against?
Primacy handles Hartford's digital scope separately from Crossmedia's media buying — how does that division of responsibility affect who approves new digital inventory partners or premium placements?

sales_plays.title: Pet Tentpole Timing Window
sales_plays.summary: Hartz is a net-new prospect with ~$10.9M in trailing spend but only $1.9M in CTV and $25K in Online Video — well below category norms. Approaching Cutwater ahead of Q4 pet retail tentpoles (National Pet Month, holiday gifting) creates a planning-first entry point before budgets are locked.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Christian Hughes at Cutwater referencing Hartz's current CTV allocation ($1.9M) relative to their $7.5M broadcast spend. Note that pet category competitors are increasingly shifting video budgets toward streaming ahead of holiday and National Pet Month planning cycles — and ask if Cutwater is evaluating incremental CTV/OLV options for Hartz this planning season.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Christian: 'As you build out Hartz's video strategy for the back half, how are you thinking about the balance between linear broadcast and streaming — and is there a specific tentpole or retail moment driving that conversation right now?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our CTV and OLV inventory as the bridge between Hartz's strong broadcast foundation ($7.5M) and their underweighted digital video presence ($25K OLV) — emphasizing audience precision for pet owner segments that linear can't deliver.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the opportunity concretely: Hartz's current OLV spend ($25K) is less than 0.3% of their total media budget ($10.9M). Even a modest reallocation of 5% from broadcast into CTV/OLV represents ~$375K in incremental streaming investment — a realistic pilot scope to propose.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Christian for a 20-minute planning call in the next two weeks: 'I'd love to share how pet care brands similar to Hartz are structuring their CTV mix ahead of holiday. Would a quick call before end of month work to compare notes before your planning cycle closes?'

sales_plays.title: Cutwater Warm Introduction to Dai
sales_plays.summary: Since Cutwater holds full AOR scope — creative, media planning, media buying, and social — Christian Hughes is the most credible warm path to Hartz's VP of Marketing, Dai Kageyama. Building the agency relationship first creates a referral bridge to the brand-side decision maker.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Christian Hughes at Cutwater with a value-first message: share a brief, relevant insight on how pet care CPG brands are structuring paid social and CTV budgets in 2025 — referencing Hartz's current $803K paid social and $1.9M CTV split as a natural conversation anchor without revealing you pulled their spend data explicitly.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Christian: 'When Dai Kageyama's team evaluates new media partners, does that conversation typically start with you at Cutwater, or does Hartz's marketing team run a parallel track — and who tends to own the final call on video and digital investment?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Once Christian confirms the decision flow, position yourself as a resource that makes Cutwater look proactive — offer to co-present a pet category audience insights deck to Dai's team, framing it as Cutwater bringing forward-looking data to their client.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Reinforce to Christian that Hartz's $60K search spend and $25K OLV represent clear whitespace — and that a co-presented recommendation to shift even a fraction of the $7.5M broadcast budget toward addressable video strengthens Cutwater's strategic value to Hartz as their AOR.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Christian for a specific, low-commitment next step: 'Would you be open to a 15-minute call so I can share what we're seeing across pet care advertisers this year? If it's relevant, I'd love your take on whether it's worth bringing to Dai.' Target this call within 10 business days.

talk_track_opener: Hey Christian, I'm [Rep Name] from [Company] — we work with a number of AOR shops managing CPG and pet care clients across CTV, digital video, and paid social. I wanted to reach out because I've been paying attention to how pet category brands are navigating the shift from linear into streaming, and Cutwater's work caught my attention given the full-scope remit you hold across creative and media for clients in this space. We've helped agencies in similar positions bring data-backed video recommendations to their clients that strengthen the AOR relationship, not just add a vendor to the mix. I noticed that Hartz's current media footprint leans heavily on broadcast — around $7.5M — with a relatively small CTV and online video presence by comparison. That's actually a pattern we see a lot with heritage pet brands, and there's a real planning opportunity in the next 60 to 90 days as retail tentpoles and holiday campaigns start to take shape. I'm curious — as you're building out Hartz's video strategy for the back half of the year, is there a specific moment or retail window driving the conversation, and how are you thinking about the broadcast-to-streaming balance heading into that cycle?
open_questions: Cutwater holds full AOR scope for Hartz — does Christian Hughes typically initiate new media partner conversations on Hartz's behalf, or does Dai Kageyama's team run those evaluations independently?
Hartz's broadcast spend is $7.5M versus $1.9M in CTV — is that ratio a deliberate strategic choice by the Hartz team, or is it a legacy allocation that Cutwater is actively looking to rebalance?
With only $25K in online video spend, is Hartz currently running any CTV or OLV tests, or has digital video simply not been a priority in recent planning cycles?
Which pet care retail moments — National Pet Month, summer seasonal, holiday — are highest priority for Hartz's media investment, and how far in advance does Cutwater typically lock those plans?
Hartz's paid social sits at $803K — is that budget managed separately from the broader video and display strategy, or does Cutwater optimize across all digital channels as a unified plan?

steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the S'mores Q2-2026 IO that just went live ($152,941) and congratulate Vijiti Dixit or Lisa Torres on the activation. Use it as a bridge: 'Now that S'mores is live, I want to show you how we're activating Reese's and Kit Kat advertisers in the same weather-driven demand windows — and where Hershey's has open inventory we haven't touched yet.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Across Reese's, Hershey's, and your snack portfolio, which brands have the most aggressive Q3-Q4 retail tentpole goals — Halloween, Holiday, Valentine's — and are those planned centrally through Publicis or brand by brand?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered audience segments and seasonal planning tools against Hershey's known reliance on cultural-moment retail windows. Confectionery purchase intent spikes are directly tied to weather and seasonal cues — TWC's first-party data connects those moments to in-store and online conversion for CPG brands.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Your total estimated media spend is $362M. Our current recognized revenue is $42. Even capturing 0.5% of your digital budget — roughly $770K — would represent an 18x expansion from where we are today. The S'mores IO proves the model works; let's scale it across the brand family.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we get 30 minutes with you and Lisa Torres at Publicis before end of Q2 to map Hershey's Q3-Q4 brand calendar against our seasonal inventory? I'll bring a strawman plan for at least three brands.'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars with Premium Weather-Contextual Video
sales_plays.summary: Hershey allocates $98.8M to Paid Social — the single largest digital line item — while Online Video sits at just $3.1M and Internet Display at $3.7M. The Q4-2025 Holiday pitch was lost ($50K), signaling a gap in our value story. This play targets Publicis's Lisa Torres to redirect a slice of the Paid Social budget toward TWC's premium contextual video environment, where brand-safe, weather-moment targeting outperforms social feed placements for confectionery impulse categories.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Lisa Torres, President of Publicis Multicultural Practice, and acknowledge the lost Holiday pitch from Q4-2025. 'Lisa, I know we didn't land the Holiday program last fall — I want to share what's changed in our contextual video offering and why I think it's a direct answer to what you were weighing against us.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Of Hershey's $98.8M in Paid Social, how much is performance-driven versus brand-building? And when you're evaluating brand-safe premium video environments for confectionery, what's the primary metric Hershey holds you accountable to — reach, frequency, or sales lift?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's premium CTV and online video inventory delivers confectionery audiences in high-intent weather moments (cold snaps driving hot cocoa and s'mores demand, warm weekends driving outdoor snacking) — contexts that social feeds cannot replicate. Position this as incremental reach against audiences Paid Social is missing, not a wholesale replacement.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Hershey spends $3.1M on Online Video today versus $98.8M on Paid Social. Shifting even 2% of Paid Social — roughly $1.98M — into TWC contextual video would 64x your current online video investment with us and give Publicis a measurable brand-safe alternative to benchmark against social CPMs.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to send you a one-page displacement brief showing TWC video CPMs, brand-safety scores, and a comparable confectionery case study before our next call. Can I have your email confirmed and a 20-minute slot in the next two weeks to walk through it together?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Vijiti — I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Hershey on a few activations, including the S'mores program that just went live in Q2, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think we're only scratching the surface of what we can do together across your brand portfolio. We've helped CPG brands like yours connect confectionery demand spikes to real weather and seasonal moments — the kind of contextual triggers that drive impulse purchase for chocolate and snacks in ways that most media environments can't replicate at scale.
open_questions: The S'mores IO is live in Q2 — who at Hershey owns the performance review for that activation, and what KPIs will determine whether it gets renewed or scaled to other brands?

Of the $98.8M allocated to Paid Social, how much is managed centrally through Publicis versus directly by individual brand teams at Hershey — and does that split affect where incremental video budget decisions get made?
The Holiday Q4-2025 pitch was not awarded — can you share what the primary objection was, whether it was pricing, targeting capability, or a competing platform that won that budget?

With Vijiti Dixit as International Chief Growth Officer, is there an international media budget for markets outside North America that flows through Publicis, and could TWC's global weather data be relevant to those plans?

As Hershey plans Q3-Q4 retail tentpole campaigns for Halloween and Holiday 2026, is the brand-level budget already locked, or is there still an open window to influence how contextual and weather-triggered media gets allocated?

sales_plays.title: PMP to Premium Direct: Upgrade Hertz's Weather-Contextual Buy
sales_plays.summary: Hertz has renewed a $1 PMP deal three consecutive years (2024, 2025, 2026). With $47M in digital spend and $27.3M in Paid Social alone, there is significant budget headroom to move Hertz from a nominal PMP into a premium, direct, high-impact placement package. Bombora signals active campaign planning intent this week — ideal moment to pitch an upgrade.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three consecutive PMP wins with Meg Gallagher or Seth Gaffney at Preacher: 'We've had a TWC Interactive PMP in place with Hertz every year since 2024 — I want to make sure we're maximizing what that relationship can do for the brand heading into your next campaign cycle. Bombora is showing Hertz actively researching campaign planning right now, so the timing felt right to reach out.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Meg, with $27.3M going into Paid Social and $12.7M into Display, what's the current thinking on contextual or weather-triggered environments — are those being evaluated as part of your next campaign brief with Preacher?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): 'The PMP has been a great foundation, but it caps your access to our highest-performing placements — premium homepage takeovers, weather-moment triggers tied to road trip and travel intent, and direct integrations that Paid Social and Display buys can't replicate. Given Hertz's positioning around travel freedom and vehicle variety, contextual weather moments are a natural fit.'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Your current PMP is valued at $1 — essentially a reserved seat. Hertz's peer set in Travel is investing $250K–$750K in direct premium packages with us. Even a modest step-up to $150K–$200K in direct buys would represent less than 0.5% of your $47M digital budget while unlocking placements that outperform standard display by 2–3x on travel intent audiences.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar this week or next — you, me, and Seth at Preacher — to walk through two or three premium package options before your next campaign brief is locked? I can have a tailored proposal ready in 48 hours.'

sales_plays.title: Displace Social Spend: Shift Share from Paid Social to High-Intent Travel Audiences
sales_plays.summary: Hertz is allocating $27.3M to Paid Social via Shadow Lion, representing 58% of their digital mix. This concentration creates a displacement opportunity: reframe TWC Interactive's travel-intent audience as a more efficient alternative for reaching in-market car renters, particularly as social CPMs rise and signal loss from platform privacy changes erodes targeting precision.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Seth Gaffney at Preacher (strategy lead) with a data-led observation: 'Seth, I've been looking at Hertz's digital mix — $27.3M in Paid Social is a significant concentration. With Meta and TikTok signal loss accelerating and CPMs trending up, I wanted to share some data on how travel advertisers are diversifying into high-intent contextual environments to protect efficiency.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'What's the current benchmark Hertz is using to evaluate Paid Social performance — is it ROAS, cost-per-rental, or brand lift — and has Shadow Lion flagged any efficiency concerns in the last two quarters?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): 'TWC Interactive reaches travelers actively planning trips — people checking weather for their destination, researching drive routes, or preparing for a vacation. That's a fundamentally different intent signal than social scrolling. For Hertz, that means reaching someone 48–72 hours before they need a car, not after the algorithm guesses they might.'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'If Hertz shifted even 5% of the $27.3M Paid Social budget — roughly $1.35M — into TWC Interactive placements, you'd be buying against verified travel-intent audiences at a scale and context that social platforms can't replicate. Our Travel vertical benchmarks show 30–40% higher engagement rates versus standard social display for rental and mobility brands.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to put together a one-page competitive analysis showing TWC Interactive's travel-intent audience versus Paid Social reach for the Hertz target demo. Can I send that to you and Meg Gallagher by end of week, and schedule a 20-minute call to walk through it before your Q3 planning kicks off?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Meg — this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Hertz for three consecutive years now on the TWC Interactive PMP, and I really value the relationship we've built. I wanted to reach out because our intent data is showing Hertz actively researching campaign planning this week, and I didn't want that window to pass without making sure we're part of the conversation early. I also wanted to be honest with you — the PMP we have in place is a great foundation, but I think we're leaving real value on the table given the scale of what Hertz is doing in digital. With $47M in digital spend and $27.3M going into Paid Social, there's a meaningful opportunity to bring TWC Interactive into a more strategic role — whether that's premium direct placements tied to travel-intent moments or a contextual layer that complements what Shadow Lion is doing on social. What does your next campaign cycle look like, and is there room to explore what a bigger partnership could do for the brand?
open_questions: The TWC Interactive PMP has renewed three years running — who at Hertz or Preacher is championing that renewal, and what's the primary use case it's been serving in the media plan?

With $27.3M in Paid Social managed by Shadow Lion, how is Hertz evaluating performance efficiency heading into the next planning cycle — and has signal loss from platform privacy changes come up in those conversations?

Meg, as Senior Director of Brand and Creative, how involved are you in media channel selection versus Preacher's strategy team — and is Seth Gaffney the right person to loop in on a premium placement conversation?

Hertz has subsidiaries including Hertz Local Edition and Hertz Equipment Rental — are any of those brands running separate media budgets or agency relationships that we should be aware of for a broader partnership conversation?
Given the Bombora signal showing active campaign research this week, what's the timeline for your next campaign brief being finalized — and is there a budget lock date we should be working toward?

sales_plays.summary: HPE has won two consecutive PMP deals (2024 and 2025) through TWC Interactive, signaling a stable relationship. This play converts that PMP foundation into a broader direct buy across premium placements and formats HPE is not yet accessing — specifically Online Video and Mobile App inventory where they already spend heavily ($4.4M and $2.1M respectively).
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the back-to-back PMP wins with Mark Thoveson or Ali Amarsy at Digitas: 'You've now run two consecutive TWC Interactive PMP campaigns — I wanted to come to you before Q3 planning locks to show you what's sitting just outside that deal that your competitors are buying direct.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Of HPE's $4.4M in Online Video and $2.1M in Mobile App spend, how much is currently flowing through TWC inventory versus other publishers — and is Digitas evaluating direct placements for the back half of 2025?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): HPE's PMP buys have been recorded at $1 deal value each, suggesting test or remnant-tier entry. A direct buy unlocks guaranteed premium placements, first-look inventory, and custom executions in Online Video and Mobile App that PMP floors cannot guarantee — directly aligned to HPE's existing spend priorities.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): HPE spends $4.4M on Online Video and $2.1M on Mobile App across its digital mix. Even capturing 10% of that spend in direct TWC placements represents a $650K incremental opportunity — against a current TWC relationship valued at effectively $2 in recorded deal value.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we set up a 30-minute working session with you and Ali Amarsy at Digitas before end of June to walk through our premium video and mobile inventory packages ahead of Q3 budget finalization?'

sales_plays.title: Search Intent Signal: Displace Incumbent Search Spend Before Q3
sales_plays.summary: Bombora shows HPE is actively researching Search Marketing this week. HPE allocates $1.2M to Digital Search — a budget that is almost certainly flowing to Google or a competing search-adjacent platform. This play uses the live intent signal to open a conversation about redirecting or supplementing that spend through TWC's search and contextual products.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the live signal when reaching Ali Amarsy at Digitas: 'Our Bombora data flagged HPE surging on Search Marketing intent this week — that's a real-time signal I wanted to get in front of you immediately, because it maps directly to a gap I see in how HPE's $1.2M search budget is currently allocated.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Where is HPE's $1.2M in search spend currently placed — is it purely Google/Bing, or is Digitas testing any contextual or weather-triggered search adjacency products for the automotive or enterprise verticals?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's contextual and weather-triggered search adjacency products reach high-intent audiences at the moment environmental conditions drive purchase decisions — a capability that standard SEM cannot replicate. For HPE's enterprise and automotive-adjacent audiences, this is a differentiated layer on top of existing search investment, not a replacement.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): HPE's $1.2M search budget is the smallest line in a $16.9M digital mix — suggesting search is underleveraged or tested minimally. Shifting even 15% ($180K) from incumbent search platforms to TWC contextual search products would represent a meaningful new revenue line while giving Digitas a testable proof point against current SEM performance.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I can send Ali a one-page comparison of TWC contextual search versus standard SEM performance benchmarks for enterprise advertisers by end of this week — would that be a useful input for Digitas's Q3 planning review with HPE?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Mark — I'm reaching out from The Weather Company. We've had a strong run together with the TWC Interactive PMP campaigns in both 2024 and 2025, and I wanted to connect before Q3 planning locks to make sure we're in the conversation at the right level. Our team works closely with Digitas and Ali Amarsy's group on the strategy side, so I want to make sure what I'm bringing you is actually useful for where HPE is headed. Here's what caught my attention this week: our Bombora data shows HPE surging on Search Marketing intent right now, and when I look at your digital mix — $4.4M in Online Video, $2.1M in Mobile App, $1.2M in Search — I see a lot of spend that isn't yet running through our premium direct inventory. The PMP deals are a great foundation, but I think there's a real opportunity to get more out of the relationship. Quick question for you: is Digitas currently evaluating any direct or contextual buys for the back half of 2025, or is the search and video budget already committed to incumbents?

Of HPE's $4.4M in Online Video spend, how much is Digitas allocating to premium publisher direct versus programmatic open exchange — and is there a performance benchmark that would justify shifting more to direct?

Bombora shows HPE researching Search Marketing this week — is that tied to a specific Q3 campaign initiative, or is Digitas doing a broader audit of HPE's search and contextual strategy?

HPE's vertical is listed as Automotive — is the $16.9M digital budget supporting an automotive product line, an enterprise solution targeting automotive OEMs, or a mix, and does that affect how Digitas plans seasonal or weather-driven demand windows?

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium direct inventory and weather-triggered creative as a complement to the existing PMP — specifically targeting the $38.8M Paid Social spend that lacks contextual relevance signals. TWC's lean-in weather content moments (storm coverage, seasonal forecasts) create high-attention environments where pet nutrition messaging — especially prescription and science-backed lines — earns outsized recall.

CPG pet brands running weather-indexed CTV with us have seen 20%+ lift in message association versus run-of-network placements — I can pull comparable case studies.'

This play converts the existing trust into a larger, direct premium deal.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins with Kendall Gibbs at iProspect Fort Worth — 'We've delivered on the 2024 and 2025 PMP commitments and I want to show you what the next level looks like given Hilton's $91.4M digital footprint.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Kendall: 'Of Hilton's $91.4M digital budget, how much is currently allocated to premium direct buys versus programmatic PMP — and is Mark Weinstein pushing for more brand-safe, high-impact placements heading into the back half?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a direct premium package (high-impact display, OLV, or CTV) as the natural upgrade from PMP — better brand safety, guaranteed inventory, and first-look access that programmatic cannot guarantee, directly aligned with Hilton's luxury and convenience brand portfolio.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Hilton's current PMP wins are booked at nominal $1 value — even a modest shift of 1% of their $91.4M digital spend into a direct premium buy represents $914K in incremental revenue. Frame the ask against their $186.5M search spend as proof they invest heavily when ROI is clear.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Kendall for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to map Hilton's H2 planning calendar and identify one brand (e.g., Conrad or Embassy Suites) as a pilot for a direct premium package proposal.

sales_plays.title: CTV Budget Displacement from Competing Platforms
sales_plays.summary: Hilton is spending $45.3M in CTV and $53.8M in Broadcast — a combined $99.1M in video budget that is almost certainly fragmented across multiple CTV platforms. This play targets displacement of a competing CTV vendor by positioning superior reach, targeting, and measurement for Hilton's travel-intent audience.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with Mark Weinstein or route through Kendall Gibbs at iProspect: 'Hilton is running $45.3M in CTV — I'd like to show you where we're seeing travel-intent audiences that your current CTV partners are likely missing, especially across our premium streaming and connected-TV inventory.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Kendall: 'Which CTV platforms are currently receiving the largest share of Hilton's $45.3M CTV budget, and how is iProspect measuring cross-platform reach and frequency deduplication across those buys?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Highlight that fragmented CTV buying across multiple platforms creates frequency waste and reach gaps — position your inventory as a consolidation opportunity with deterministic travel-intent targeting that aligns with Hilton Honors loyalty data and the full brand family (Conrad, Doubletree, Hampton Inn, Homewood Suites).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If Hilton is splitting $45.3M across four or more CTV platforms, consolidating even 20% ($9M) into a single premium CTV partner reduces duplication waste and improves measurable reach. Benchmark against their $53.8M broadcast spend to show CTV can deliver comparable reach at lower CPM with better attribution.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive audit — offer to run a two-week reach-and-frequency overlap analysis against Hilton's current CTV mix at no cost, with a readout to Kendall Gibbs and the iProspect team within 30 days.

talk_track_opener: Hey Kendall, this is [Rep Name] from [Company] — we've been working with the iProspect team on Hilton's PMP buys going back to 2023, and we closed two consecutive deals in 2024 and 2025, so I feel like we have a real foundation to build on here. I work with a number of travel and hospitality brands at this spend level and I know how much pressure AOR teams are under to show efficiency across a portfolio this size. I was looking at Hilton's media mix and noticed that the $45.3M CTV budget and $91.4M digital spend are both areas where we have inventory and audience capabilities that go well beyond what the current PMP structure allows us to show you. I'm curious — as iProspect plans Hilton's H2 digital and video strategy, is there a conversation happening about consolidating some of that CTV fragmentation or moving select brands like Conrad or Embassy Suites into more premium direct placements? I'd love 30 minutes to show you what that could look like with real numbers.
open_questions: Of Hilton's $91.4M digital budget, what share is currently committed to direct premium buys versus programmatic PMP, and is iProspect looking to rebalance that ratio for H2?
Who at Hilton — Mark Weinstein's team or iProspect — owns the final decision on CTV platform selection and budget allocation across the $45.3M CTV line?
How is iProspect currently measuring cross-platform frequency deduplication across Hilton's CTV and Broadcast spend of $99.1M combined?
Are any of Hilton's six sub-brands — particularly Conrad or Embassy Suites at the luxury tier — running separate media plans with distinct KPIs that could support a standalone premium pilot?

sales_plays.title: Shift CTV Dollars On-Property
sales_plays.summary: Hims & Hers is running $61.9M in CTV and $28.2M in digital through Tinuiti, with heavy programmatic and off-property exposure. This play targets the gap between their current scatter/programmatic CTV buy and the precision, brand-safe, contextually aligned inventory a direct publisher relationship delivers.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Jeremy Cornfeldt at Tinuiti referencing Hims & Hers' $61.9M CTV footprint and note that programmatic CTV CPMs have risen 18-22% YoY industry-wide — open with 'We're seeing clients in health & wellness recapture efficiency by moving a portion of programmatic CTV to direct, guaranteed inventory. Worth a 20-minute conversation about what that could look like for Hims & Hers?'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jeremy: 'Of the $61.9M Hims & Hers is spending in CTV today, what percentage is running programmatic versus direct-guaranteed, and where are the biggest brand-safety or frequency-capping pain points?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position direct, on-property CTV inventory against the fragmentation and CPM inflation Tinuiti is managing programmatically — emphasize contextual alignment for sensitive health categories (sexual health, weight loss, mental health) where brand-safe, premium environments reduce compliance risk and improve viewer trust.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If Hims & Hers shifted even 15% of their $61.9M CTV budget ($9.3M) to direct on-property inventory, model the CPM efficiency gain and frequency control improvement — frame it as recovering $1-2M in wasted impressions based on typical programmatic tax in the health vertical.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jeremy for a 20-minute call before end of the current quarter to walk through a custom audience overlap analysis between Hims & Hers' core demographics (18-45, health-conscious, condition-specific) and our first-party viewership data — low commitment, high signal.

sales_plays.title: Hilary Coles Brand Innovation Referral
sales_plays.summary: Hilary Coles, Co-Founder & SVP Brand & Innovation, is a high-credibility internal champion for new media partnerships. This play uses a warm referral path — ideally through a mutual contact in the DTC health or telemedicine space — to get a direct conversation with Hilary before the agency filters the opportunity.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Identify a mutual connection in the DTC health, wellness, or telemedicine vertical who has worked with Hilary Coles or the Hims & Hers brand team — leverage LinkedIn to find a shared contact and request a warm intro framed around brand innovation and audience strategy, not a media pitch.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Once connected with Hilary, ask: 'As you scale across sexual health, weight loss, and mental health simultaneously, how are you thinking about keeping the brand message cohesive across CTV and digital when each condition has such a different audience profile?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our platform as a brand innovation partner — not just an inventory vendor — that can help Hims & Hers build condition-specific audience segments at scale while maintaining a unified brand halo, something programmatic buys through Tinuiti cannot deliver with the same precision.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Reference Hims & Hers' $173.4M total media footprint and note that brand-direct partnerships in the health vertical typically generate 30-40% higher brand recall lift versus programmatic equivalents — frame even a $5-10M test as a measurable brand equity investment Hilary can own internally.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Hilary for a 30-minute brand strategy session — not a sales call — to share audience insights specific to Hims & Hers' core conditions, with a concrete deliverable (a custom audience overlap report) delivered within one week of the meeting.

talk_track_opener: Hey Jeremy, I'm [Rep Name] from [Publisher]. We work with a number of AOR media teams managing large health and wellness portfolios, and I've been looking closely at the Hims & Hers account. With $61.9M in CTV and another $28.2M in digital running through your team, you're managing one of the more complex health media buys in the DTC space — especially given the sensitivity of the categories they're in: sexual health, weight loss, mental health. That's a lot of programmatic exposure in environments where brand safety and contextual fit really matter.
open_questions: Of the $61.9M Hims & Hers is spending in CTV, what percentage is running programmatic versus direct-guaranteed, and are there specific brand-safety incidents or frequency issues that have come up in the health category?
Who at Hims & Hers — Hilary Coles or someone on her team — has final approval on net-new publisher partnerships above a certain spend threshold, and does that decision sit with brand or media?

Given that Tinuiti holds both planning and buying for Hims & Hers, is there a separate innovation or test-and-learn budget that sits outside the core AOR scope where new publisher relationships get evaluated?

With Hims & Hers marketing across five distinct health conditions simultaneously, how is audience segmentation currently handled in the CTV and digital buys — are condition-specific campaigns running separately or under a unified brand campaign structure?

Hims & Hers' Internet Display spend is only $4.1M against $173.4M total — is that a strategic choice to deprioritize display, or is there appetite to grow that line if the right premium, contextually safe environment was available?

sales_plays.title: CTV-to-Direct Premium Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Hobby Lobby is an active customer with a won PMP deal and $32.7M in CTV spend. This play expands the relationship by converting programmatic/PMP activity into premium direct inventory and layering in weather-triggered and cultural-moment targeting ahead of key retail tentpoles.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the July 2024 PMP win with Scott Harris and Chris Ferrel at TRG. Open with: 'The PMP we activated last summer performed well enough that I want to show you what a direct buy could unlock — especially with your CTV budget at $32.7M, there's real room to move up the quality stack before Q4 planning locks in.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Scott Harris: 'When TRG is allocating that $32.7M in CTV, how much of it is going through open exchange versus guaranteed direct — and is there a quality floor you're trying to hit for brand-safe environments?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position Weather Channel's direct CTV inventory as the premium complement to the existing PMP: contextually relevant for home décor and seasonal craft moments (fall, holiday, Valentine's Day), with guaranteed placement in high-attention weather-driven content that aligns with Hobby Lobby's southern and midwestern store footprint.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The current PMP closed at $1 — a proof-of-concept. With $32.7M in CTV spend on the table, even a 5% shift to direct premium inventory represents a $1.6M+ opportunity. Frame the ask around a pilot allocation that scales from the existing relationship.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Chris Ferrel at TRG for a 30-minute planning session in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar before your Q4 upfront planning cycle closes — I want to bring a specific CTV direct proposal mapped to Hobby Lobby's tentpole calendar and store geography.'

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Budget Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Hobby Lobby's digital mix is heavily skewed toward Paid Social at $28.4M — nearly 91% of their $31.2M digital budget — with almost no investment in display ($17K) or online video ($244K). This play challenges the over-reliance on social platforms and positions Weather Channel's digital and video inventory as a brand-safe, intent-aligned alternative.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the data imbalance: 'Scott, I was looking at your digital mix and noticed $28.4M is sitting in Paid Social while display is at $17K and online video at $244K. That's a significant concentration risk — especially given recent brand-safety scrutiny on social platforms for values-aligned brands like Hobby Lobby.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Is TRG actively evaluating any reallocation of that Paid Social budget into channels with stronger brand-safety controls and first-party audience targeting — or is the $28.4M essentially locked for the year?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position Weather Channel's digital and online video inventory as a direct displacement candidate: brand-safe environment, contextual relevance for home décor and craft audiences in Hobby Lobby's core southern and midwestern markets, and Bombora-confirmed Entertainment intent signals this week suggesting an audience actively engaged in content consumption.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Shifting even 3-5% of the $28.4M Paid Social budget — roughly $850K to $1.4M — into Weather Channel digital and video would represent a 3,000%+ increase over the current $244K online video investment and establish a meaningful diversified digital presence.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer a competitive audit: 'Let me put together a two-page displacement analysis showing what a $1M reallocation from Paid Social into our digital and video inventory would look like in terms of reach, brand-safety scores, and audience overlap — can I get that to Chris at TRG by end of next week?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Scott, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we worked together on the PMP deal last summer that TRG helped activate, and I wanted to reconnect now that you're likely in planning mode for the back half of the year. We work with a lot of retail brands in the arts, craft, and home décor space, and we've been seeing strong performance when CTV and digital buys are tied to contextual moments — weather shifts, seasonal transitions, the kinds of triggers that actually get people into stores or onto a site. I was looking at your media mix and a couple of things stood out to me. Your CTV investment is substantial at $32.7M, and I think there's a real opportunity to move some of that from programmatic into direct premium inventory before Q4 locks in. At the same time, your digital budget is almost entirely in Paid Social — $28.4M out of $31.2M — which is a pretty concentrated position for a brand with Hobby Lobby's values profile. I'd love to ask Chris at TRG: is there appetite to explore what a more diversified digital and CTV strategy looks like heading into the holiday and cultural-moment calendar — even just as a planning conversation?
open_questions: When TRG allocates Hobby Lobby's $32.7M CTV budget, what percentage is currently going through guaranteed direct buys versus programmatic or open exchange — and is there a quality or brand-safety floor driving that split?
Is Scott Harris or Chris Ferrel at TRG the primary decision-maker when it comes to shifting budget between channels — for example, moving dollars from Paid Social into video or CTV?
Given Hobby Lobby's values-aligned brand positioning, has there been any internal conversation about reducing exposure on social platforms where brand-safety controls are less predictable?

What does Hobby Lobby's retail tentpole calendar look like for the back half of the year — are fall harvest, Halloween, and holiday campaigns already in planning, and is TRG locking those budgets now?
The July 2024 PMP closed at $1 — was that a test budget, and if performance was positive, what would it take to scale that into a larger direct or guaranteed CTV commitment for 2025?

sales_plays.title: Direct Premium Expansion Off PMP Losses
sales_plays.summary: Home Depot is an active customer with a $99K direct win in January 2026, but has lost three consecutive PMP and off-property opportunities totaling $1.115M in Q1-Q2 2026. This play converts that lost programmatic spend into a premium direct relationship by expanding the existing direct foothold.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the January 2026 direct win ($99,142) with Molly Battin's team and acknowledge the three recent PMP losses ($500K Spring, $450K Q2 PMPs, $165K Off-Prop Data). Tell RJ DiBella at OMD Atlanta: 'The direct buy performed — I want to show you why the PMP path kept losing and how we protect that budget differently going forward.'
Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask RJ: 'When the $500K Spring PMP and $450K Q2 PMP buys didn't close, where did that budget ultimately land — did it shift to another publisher, or did it go unspent?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position direct guaranteed inventory as the alternative to PMP auction risk — Home Depot's $445.5M in paid search and $187.8M in digital signal a brand that demands measurable, controllable reach. Direct buys eliminate the floor-price volatility that likely killed the PMP deals.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The $99K direct win is a proof point. Scaling to even 25% of the $1.115M lost across the three PMP opps represents a $278K incremental direct opportunity — frame it as recapturing budget that already had internal approval.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask RJ for a 30-minute working session before the Q3 planning cycle to map a direct package proposal — 'Can we get 30 minutes with you and the planning team before end of May to bring a Q3 direct proposal that addresses what the PMPs couldn't deliver?'

sales_plays.title: Mobile Intent Signal — Displace Competing Mobile Buy
sales_plays.summary: Bombora shows Home Depot actively researching Mobile this week, and their $16.8M Mobile App line is the smallest digital sub-channel — suggesting either a gap in current mobile investment or active evaluation of a new mobile partner. This play displaces whoever currently holds that mobile budget.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the live Bombora signal to RJ DiBella at OMD Atlanta: 'Our intent data shows Home Depot in active research mode on Mobile right now. With $16.8M in Mobile App spend on the books, I want to make sure we're in the conversation before that budget gets committed elsewhere.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'The $16.8M Mobile App line is notably smaller than your $77.5M Paid Social and $445.5M Search — is that a deliberate allocation, or is there a mobile gap you're trying to close this planning cycle?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position against the incumbent mobile partner by highlighting audience precision for home improvement intent — Home Depot's core customer (homeowner, DIYer, contractor) is identifiable through weather-triggered, location-based, and in-market signals that generic mobile networks can't match at scale.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Home Depot's total digital spend is $187.8M. Mobile App at $16.8M is only 9% of digital — if a competitor is capturing the mobile-in-market homeowner audience that Home Depot's $203.2M broadcast and $52M CTV are trying to reach, the attribution gap is costing them. Even shifting 15% of Paid Social ($11.6M) into a high-intent mobile environment is a testable displacement scenario.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I can put together a mobile audience overlap analysis against your current $16.8M Mobile App buy in the next week. Would Molly Battin's team want to see that before the Q3 brief goes out — or should we route it through you at OMD first?'

talk_track_opener: Hey RJ, this is [Rep Name] from [Company] — we've been working with the Home Depot team since the direct buy closed in January, and I've been watching how the Q1 and Q2 PMP conversations played out. Three opportunities totaling over a million dollars didn't close, and I don't think that's a Home Depot budget problem — I think it's a format problem, and I want to fix it before Q3 planning locks in. Here's what caught my attention this week: our Bombora data shows Home Depot actively researching Mobile right now, and when I look at the media mix — $445M in Search, $77M in Paid Social, $52M in CTV — the $16.8M Mobile App line looks like the one channel with real room to grow. I'd love 30 minutes to show you two things: how we convert the PMP losses into a direct package that actually closes, and where we think the mobile gap is relative to what a competitor may already be capturing. Who's the right person to loop in on your side for that conversation?
open_questions: When the $500K Spring PMP and $450K Q2 PMP buys didn't close, where did that budget go — did it shift to another publisher or get reallocated internally?
Is Molly Battin's team driving the mobile research we're seeing in intent data, or is that coming from a specific category or brand within the Home Depot portfolio?
Who at OMD Atlanta or Home Depot owns the final call on direct vs. programmatic format decisions — is that RJ's team or does it go up to the client side?
The Mobile App line sits at $16.8M against a $187.8M digital total — is that a deliberate cap, or is there appetite to grow mobile if the right partner and measurement framework are in place?

Given three consecutive PMP losses, is there a specific floor price, audience requirement, or reporting standard that we missed — and what would a winning direct proposal need to include to clear that bar?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Direct Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Honeywell is an active PMP buyer (two consecutive won deals in 2024-2025) against a $10.4M total media footprint managed by Carat USA. The expansion opportunity is to convert PMP line items into higher-value direct or sponsorship buys and to bring untouched digital sub-channels — particularly the $368K Online Video and $840K Mobile App budgets — onto Weather Company inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins (Jan 2025 and Jul 2024) in your outreach to Shawna Ross at Carat USA Chicago. Open with: 'Shawna, the Honeywell PMP has performed across two straight cycles — I want to show you what a direct premium buy unlocks that the PMP floor can't deliver, specifically around contextual targeting tied to Honeywell's industrial and automotive verticals.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Shawna: 'Of Honeywell's $840K mobile app and $368K online video allocations, how much is currently running through Weather Company inventory versus other SSPs — and is Carat evaluating any consolidation of those buys for the back half of 2025?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position Weather Company's direct premium units (high-impact video, branded content, and contextual weather-triggered display) as the upgrade path from PMP. Honeywell's industrial and automotive audiences index strongly against weather-sensitive use cases — fleet safety, HVAC demand, and energy management — all of which align with Weather Company's contextual signals.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Honeywell's current confirmed Weather Company spend is nominal ($1 PMP placeholders). Against a $10.4M total digital footprint, even a 5% shift of the $9.7M digital budget represents ~$485K in incremental direct spend. Frame the ask around capturing a share of the $840K mobile app and $368K online video lines specifically.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Shawna for a 30-minute working session before end of Q3 2025 to walk through a custom audience overlap analysis between Honeywell's industrial/automotive targets and Weather Company's verified contextual segments — low commitment, high signal value for Carat's planning cycle.

sales_plays.title: Allergy Win-Back + Competitive Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Honeywell lost a $50K Allergy Q2 2024 opportunity, suggesting budget went to a competing contextual or health-adjacent publisher. With Carat USA holding full media buying AOR scope and Dentsu Creative as AOR, there is a clear agency path to re-enter this budget with a stronger contextual case and displace whoever captured that spend.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out directly to Shawna Ross at Carat USA referencing the lost Allergy Q2 2024 opportunity: 'Shawna, we missed on the Honeywell Allergy Q2 buy last year — I'd like to understand what won that budget and show you what we've built since then on the contextual health and seasonal side that changes the comparison.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Who captured the Allergy Q2 2024 budget — was it a health-vertical publisher, a programmatic contextual buy, or a search-heavy allocation? Understanding the incumbent helps me show Carat exactly where Weather Company's pollen and allergy forecast data creates a differentiated audience moment that a generic health publisher can't replicate.'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Weather Company owns the most scaled, real-time allergy and pollen forecast content in digital media. Honeywell's home air quality and HVAC product lines (tied to its $9.7M digital spend) are a natural fit for weather-triggered allergy creative — displacing a generic health or programmatic buy with a contextually precise, brand-safe environment that Dentsu Creative can build against.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost opportunity was $50K. Reframe the conversation around the full seasonal allergy window (Q1-Q2 annually) and position a $150K-$200K direct contextual package that bundles pollen forecast sponsorship, weather-triggered display from the $1.6M Internet Display budget, and mobile push units from the $840K Mobile App line — a 3-4x expansion of the original lost deal.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Meredith Winczewski's team (via Shawna at Carat) for a brief to review Honeywell's 2025 allergy/air quality campaign objectives before Q1 2026 planning locks — propose a 20-minute creative alignment call with Dentsu Creative and Carat together to show how Weather Company's contextual data layer changes the media plan.

talk_track_opener: Hey Shawna, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Carat on the Honeywell PMP for two consecutive cycles now — the Jan 2025 and mid-2024 buys — and I wanted to connect directly because I think we're only scratching the surface of what Honeywell's budget can do on our platform. You're managing a $10.4M total media footprint for them, and right now our confirmed share of that is minimal relative to what the contextual fit actually supports across their industrial, automotive, and home comfort lines.
open_questions: Shawna, of Honeywell's $840K mobile app and $368K online video budgets, how much is currently allocated to Weather Company inventory versus other SSPs or direct publishers — and is Carat evaluating any consolidation for H2 2025?

Who owns the decision to upgrade the Honeywell PMP from a programmatic floor buy to a direct premium package — is that a Carat planning call or does Meredith Winczewski's team at Honeywell need to approve the incremental investment?
What publisher or platform captured the Allergy Q2 2024 budget that we lost — was it a health-vertical buy, a programmatic contextual allocation, or did it shift to paid search?

Is Dentsu Creative building seasonal or weather-triggered creative for Honeywell's home comfort and air quality lines, or is the current creative strategy evergreen — and would a contextual trigger layer change their production approach?

With Carat holding full AOR scope across planning and buying for Honeywell, what does the 2026 annual planning calendar look like — and when does the Q1 allergy season budget need to be committed to lock preferred inventory on Weather Company?

sales_plays.title: Win Back HubSpot via SmartBug Agency Path
sales_plays.summary: HubSpot lost a $200K TWC-Interactive awareness deal at end of 2024. With $40.5M in total media spend and SmartBug Media handling digital social and creative, there is a clear agency-routed path to re-engage before 2025 planning cycles solidify.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Heather Hiltzik at SmartBug Media directly. Reference the closed HubSpot-2025 Awareness opp that ended 12/31/2024 and acknowledge the timing did not work out. Ask if HubSpot's 2025 awareness strategy has been finalized or if there is still whitespace in the digital plan.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Heather what specific performance gaps or audience reach concerns caused HubSpot to pass on the TWC-Interactive proposal — was it pricing, targeting capability, or audience fit against their CTV-heavy mix?

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-contextual audience targeting as a complement to HubSpot's existing $6.3M CTV and $9.6M paid social spend — not a replacement. Frame it as incremental reach in moments when audiences are actively planning purchases.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The original opp was $200K. With $40.5M in total spend and only $190K in display, there is clear underinvestment in contextual digital. A revised proposal at even 0.5% of total spend represents a $200K+ opportunity with measurable incremental reach.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Heather for a 20-minute call in the next two weeks to walk through a revised proposal tailored to HubSpot's 2025 awareness goals — offer to bring updated audience data specific to their vertical.

sales_plays.title: Automotive Demand Window Planning Hook
sales_plays.summary: HubSpot serves automotive customers and operates in a vertical where weather-driven demand windows (spring selling season, end-of-year clearance) influence media planning. Use this as a forward-looking planning question to get ahead of Q2 budget conversations with Kipp Bodnar's team.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Kipp Bodnar directly via LinkedIn or email. Reference HubSpot's significant investment in CTV ($6.3M) and paid search ($17.9M) and note that TWC has worked with automotive-adjacent brands to align media timing with weather-driven consumer behavior shifts heading into spring.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Kipp whether HubSpot's media calendar for Q2 2025 accounts for seasonal demand spikes in the automotive vertical — specifically whether their agency, SmartBug Media, is building weather-contextual triggers into the awareness plan.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather audience segments as a planning layer that sits on top of HubSpot's existing paid social and CTV investment — helping SmartBug Media activate HubSpot campaigns at the precise moment automotive consumer intent peaks seasonally.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): HubSpot's $17.9M search spend suggests aggressive intent capture. TWC's contextual layer could improve upper-funnel efficiency by reaching audiences before they enter search — a planning conversation worth having given the scale of their current investment.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a brief planning session with both Kipp Bodnar and Heather Hiltzik at SmartBug Media before end of February — framed as a Q2 readiness check, not a sales pitch — to map weather demand windows against HubSpot's existing 2025 media calendar.

talk_track_opener: Hey Heather, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We worked with HubSpot late last year on a $200K awareness proposal through TWC-Interactive — the timing did not come together before year-end, but I wanted to reconnect now that 2025 planning is underway. I know SmartBug is handling HubSpot's digital social and creative, and I wanted to make sure you had the full picture on what we can do before the spring calendar locks in. Looking at HubSpot's media mix — $6.3M in CTV, $17.9M in search, $9.6M in paid social — there is a real opportunity to add a contextual layer that activates against weather-driven demand moments, especially relevant given their automotive vertical clients. I am curious: when the 2024 proposal did not move forward, was it a fit issue or more of a timing and budget cycle thing? That would help me put together something that actually maps to where HubSpot is headed this year.

Is Kipp Bodnar the final decision-maker on awareness media investments, or does SmartBug Media have budget authority for digital channels within a set threshold?
