sales_plays.title: Expand Beyond Show-Specific Buys Into Always-On Brand Presence
sales_plays.summary: Hulu has consistently activated title-specific campaigns (Paradise 2, All's Fair, OMITB) through TWC-Interactive, totaling $365K in won revenue. The opportunity is to convert episodic, show-by-show spend into a sustained always-on subscriber acquisition package that mirrors the scale of their $227.6M digital footprint.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three consecutive won campaigns — OMITB ($50K, Aug 2025), Everyday FY25 ($200K, Sep 2025), and All's Fair ($115K, Oct 2025) — and open with Sarah Greenberg by acknowledging the pattern: 'You've activated with us three times in the back half of 2025 and the results clearly held up. I want to show you what a year-round presence looks like at the same efficiency, rather than rebuilding the plan each title cycle.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Sarah: 'When you're planning subscriber acquisition for a title like Paradise 2, how much of the brief is driven by the show's premiere window versus a standing always-on acquisition goal — and who at Publicis Imagine or OMG23 owns that always-on line item?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's always-on inventory as the connective tissue between title launches. Hulu's $52.6M in Paid Social and $23.6M in Online Video signal heavy upper-funnel investment — TWC can anchor the mid-funnel with contextually relevant, weather- and moment-aware placements that sustain subscriber intent between premiere spikes.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The three 2025 wins averaged $121.7K per activation. An always-on annual commitment at even 3x that average ($365K) matches the exact total of all three prior buys combined — with the added efficiency of a single negotiated rate and no re-planning cost per title. Frame this against the $337M total spend as a rounding error with outsized continuity value.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Sarah and loop in Jennifer Rodis at OMG23/Publicis Imagine for a 30-minute working session before the Paradise 2 campaign ($100K, currently in-flight) wraps — specifically to map the post-Paradise subscriber acquisition calendar and identify the always-on whitespace. Propose the meeting within the next two weeks.

sales_plays.title: Displace Programmatic Display Spend With Premium Direct Inventory
sales_plays.summary: Hulu allocates $21.1M to Internet Display, likely running through programmatic channels via Publicis Imagine or OMG23. Given the active TWC relationship and proven direct-buy performance, there is a clear displacement opportunity to shift a portion of that programmatic display budget into premium, brand-safe direct placements on TWC properties.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with Jennifer Rodis at Publicis Imagine (AOR for Media Planning and Buying) by referencing the $21.1M Internet Display line: 'You're running a meaningful display budget for Hulu — we've seen the direct-buy side perform well across three campaigns last year. I want to show you what a side-by-side looks like between your current programmatic CPMs and what we can deliver direct, especially for a brand where context and brand safety matter as much as reach.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jennifer: 'Of the $21.1M Hulu is spending in Internet Display, how much is running through open exchange versus PMPs — and is there a brand safety or viewability threshold that's creating friction in the programmatic stack right now?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC direct inventory as a premium alternative to open-exchange display: guaranteed viewability, contextually aligned audiences (weather, lifestyle, entertainment-adjacent content), and zero brand safety risk — all attributes that matter for a streaming brand competing for subscriber attention against Netflix, Max, and Peacock.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Even a 10% shift of the $21.1M Internet Display budget represents $2.1M in incremental TWC direct spend — a 5.7x increase over the total $365K won in 2025. Frame the test as a $250K–$500K pilot displacement against one programmatic line item, with a head-to-head performance comparison built into the reporting.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to pull a TWC inventory and CPM proposal scoped specifically to Hulu's display audience targets and deliver it to Jennifer Rodis within five business days. Ask for a 20-minute call to confirm the audience parameters before building the proposal.

talk_track_opener: Hey Sarah — I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've worked together on three campaigns over the past year, OMITB, the Everyday FY25 push, and All's Fair, and each one came through cleanly. I wanted to reach out directly because I think we've been operating in a title-by-title mode when there's a bigger conversation to have about subscriber acquisition as a continuous effort, not just a launch sprint. You're running over $337 million in total media, with more than $227 million in digital alone — and right now we're a small but consistently performing slice of that. I'd love to change that ratio. Looking at your digital mix, you've got $21.1 million in Internet Display and $23.6 million in Online Video that I think we can make work harder through direct, contextually relevant placements rather than open-exchange programmatic. We have the Paradise 2 campaign in-flight right now, which is a natural moment to zoom out and ask: what does the rest of 2026 look like for subscriber acquisition, and is there a standing always-on budget that Publicis Imagine or OMG23 is holding that we haven't tapped into yet? Would you be open to a 30-minute session with Jennifer Rodis before Paradise 2 wraps to map that out?
open_questions: The F26 Everyday Campaign was lost after the FY25 version won at $200K — can you help me understand what changed in the brief or the budget allocation that caused that shift, so we can build the right proposal for the next cycle?

Between Publicis Imagine and OMG23 both holding AOR media planning and buying responsibilities for Hulu, which agency owns the always-on subscriber acquisition budget versus the title-specific launch budgets?

Of Hulu's $21.1M Internet Display spend, how much is currently running through open exchange versus private marketplace deals, and is there a viewability or brand safety floor that's creating inefficiency in the programmatic stack?

Sarah, when you're evaluating media partners for subscriber acquisition, are you optimizing primarily against cost-per-trial, cost-per-subscriber, or a brand awareness metric — and does that KPI differ between title-launch campaigns and the Everyday always-on program?

With Paradise 2 currently in-flight at $100K, is there a post-campaign review process with Jennifer Rodis or the OMG23 team where we could present a 2026 always-on proposal, or does that planning conversation happen separately from the title-specific debrief?

sales_plays.title: Shift Humana's $550K CTV Budget to Premium Direct
sales_plays.summary: Humana is running only $550K in CTV against $125.6M in broadcast — a massive imbalance suggesting their programmatic or off-property CTV buys are not converting at scale. This play targets Spark Foundry's media planning team to displace low-yield CTV spend with a direct, premium inventory partnership.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Ed Castillo at Spark Foundry referencing the disparity: Humana is investing $125.6M in broadcast but only $550K in CTV — a ratio that suggests CTV is either underperforming or being treated as a test line. Ask if that allocation reflects a strategic choice or a gap in premium supply access.
Step 2 (Discovery Question): What performance benchmarks is Spark Foundry using to evaluate Humana's current CTV buys, and are those buys running programmatically or direct-to-publisher?

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position direct CTV inventory as the bridge between Humana's broadcast-scale reach and the addressability their $29M digital budget is chasing — without the fraud and brand-safety risk of programmatic pipes.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If even 5% of Humana's $125.6M broadcast budget migrated to premium direct CTV, that's $6.3M in incremental spend with measurable household-level reach — a 10x expansion of their current $550K CTV line.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Ed Castillo for a 20-minute working session before Humana's next planning cycle to walk through a CTV efficiency audit comparing their current $550K placement against direct premium benchmarks.

sales_plays.title: Leverage Entertainment Intent Signal via CMO Warm Intro
sales_plays.summary: Bombora shows Humana is actively researching Entertainment content this week. This is an opportunity to use a mutual connection or agency intermediary to route a warm introduction to Jennifer Ilecki, CMO, framing a content partnership or sponsorship conversation around Humana's evident interest in entertainment-adjacent media.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Identify any shared contacts between your organization and Jennifer Ilecki or THIRD EAR (Humana's AOR Creative Multicultural agency). Reference Bombora's Entertainment intent signal as the reason for the timely outreach — Humana is actively in-market on this topic right now.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Is Humana's Entertainment interest tied to a specific audience segment — multicultural, Medicare-eligible, or employer group — that THIRD EAR is currently developing creative for?

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position an entertainment-integrated sponsorship or branded content opportunity that aligns with THIRD EAR's multicultural creative mandate and Humana's need to reach diverse health insurance audiences in culturally resonant environments.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Humana's $19.6M in paid social and $2.5M in mobile app spend signals strong investment in audience-targeted digital — an entertainment content partnership can extend that targeting into premium, brand-safe environments at a fraction of the CPM risk of their current $1.3M display line.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask the referring contact or Ed Castillo to make a warm email introduction to Jennifer Ilecki within the next 10 business days, while the Entertainment intent signal is still active, for a 15-minute discovery call.

talk_track_opener: Hey Ed, I'm reaching out because we work with a number of health and benefits advertisers at the national scale Humana operates, and I wanted to bring something specific to your attention before your next planning cycle. We've built direct premium inventory relationships that are purpose-built for advertisers making the transition from broadcast-dominant budgets into addressable CTV — exactly the kind of shift that's hard to execute cleanly through programmatic pipes. I noticed Humana is carrying $125.6M in broadcast and only $550K in CTV right now, and I wanted to understand whether that's a deliberate hold or a supply access issue we might be able to solve together.
open_questions: Is Humana's current $550K CTV buy running programmatically through a DSP or direct with specific publishers, and what KPIs is Spark Foundry using to evaluate its performance against the $125.6M broadcast line?
Who at Humana — Jennifer Ilecki's team or Spark Foundry — owns the decision to shift budget from broadcast into CTV or digital video, and what does that approval process look like?
Is THIRD EAR's multicultural creative currently being activated in digital video or CTV environments, or is it primarily running in social and broadcast formats?
What is driving Humana's Entertainment intent research right now — is this tied to a specific campaign, a new audience segment, or an upcoming planning brief for 2025?

Given Humana's $19.6M paid social investment, is Spark Foundry seeing diminishing returns on audience targeting in social that would support a case for diversifying into premium direct video inventory?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Hy-Vee is an active PMP customer (won Jan 2025 and Jul 2024) with $17.2M in digital spend and $3.6M in CTV. The opportunity is to convert their existing PMP relationship into higher-value direct buys — premium placements, sponsorships, or weather-triggered units — before Midwest retail tentpoles (summer grilling, back-to-school, holiday) hit the planning calendar.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Sara Easton referencing the Jan 2025 PMP win and note that Hy-Vee's $3.6M CTV line and $3.1M mobile app spend signal readiness for premium on-property placements. Ask for a 20-minute planning call to preview Q3/Q4 inventory before Dentsu locks budgets.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Sara: 'For your summer grilling and back-to-school windows, is Dentsu currently allocating CTV and mobile budgets through the PMP, or is there a separate direct-buy process — and who owns that decision between you and Sebastian Aresco?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position premium direct inventory (weather-triggered homepage takeovers, sponsored content units) as the logical next step above the PMP — delivering guaranteed share-of-voice during Midwest weather moments that drive grocery foot traffic, something the PMP cannot guarantee.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Hy-Vee's $17.2M digital budget includes only $459K in display and $746K in online video — both well below category norms for a $44M total spender. Shifting even 10% of paid social ($820K) into premium on-property direct buys would represent a meaningful step-up and a testable incremental line.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Sara and Sebastian for a joint 30-minute Q3 planning session in the next two weeks — offer to bring a custom inventory deck showing weather-moment and tentpole availability specific to Hy-Vee's Midwest footprint.

sales_plays.title: RedRox High-Impact Win-Back via Dentsu
sales_plays.summary: Hy-Vee lost the $400K RedRox High-Impact deal in September 2024 and the $50K Q3 MAIM deal in August 2024. Both losses likely went to a competing premium or high-impact vendor. With Dentsu as AOR for media planning and buying, the displacement path runs through Sebastian Aresco — reframe the value proposition before the next annual planning cycle locks competing vendors in.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Contact Sebastian Aresco at Dentsu directly, referencing the $400K RedRox High-Impact opportunity that closed in September 2024. Acknowledge the outcome professionally and ask: 'We'd like to understand what the winning solution offered that we didn't — can we get 15 minutes to close that loop and show you what's changed in our high-impact product?'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Sebastian: 'For the high-impact and rich-media line items you're currently running for Hy-Vee, what performance benchmarks is the current vendor being held to — and are those being met heading into the back half of 2025?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's high-impact formats against the incumbent by leading with Midwest-specific audience reach — Hy-Vee's core geography — and weather-contextual targeting that a generic high-impact vendor cannot replicate at scale for a Midwest grocery chain.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The $400K RedRox loss plus the $50K MAIM loss total $450K in displaced revenue. Re-winning even the high-impact line at $400K against Hy-Vee's $17.2M digital budget is a credible ask — less than 2.5% of their digital spend — and a natural complement to the existing PMP relationship already on the books.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Sebastian for a 20-minute product demo call within the next 10 business days, specifically focused on high-impact and contextual formats. Offer a side-by-side benchmark comparison against the format type that won the September 2024 deal.

talk_track_opener: Hey Sara, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Hy-Vee through the PMP since mid-2024 and just renewed again in January — so I wanted to reach out directly rather than route everything through Sebastian. We've got a good foundation and I want to make sure you're getting full value from it heading into the back half of the year. I know Dentsu is managing the day-to-day buys, but I'd love 20 minutes with you to make sure the strategy we're building together is aligned with what you need at the brand level.
open_questions: The PMP has been renewed twice now — is Sara or Sebastian the right person to discuss moving any of those buys to guaranteed direct placements for Q3 tentpoles like back-to-school or summer grilling?
When the RedRox High-Impact deal was awarded in September 2024, what did the winning vendor offer that drove the decision — was it format, audience, or pricing?
Of Hy-Vee's $8.2M paid social budget, how much is being evaluated for reallocation in the 2025 annual plan, and is contextual or weather-triggered display on Dentsu's radar as an alternative?
Does Sara Easton have direct budget authority over the digital line items, or does every incremental spend above the PMP require Dentsu approval through Sebastian Aresco?
Is Hy-Vee planning any retail media or shopper marketing campaigns tied to Midwest seasonal moments in Q3 or Q4 2025 that haven't been briefed to vendors yet?

sales_plays.title: Displace Assembly's Digital Allocation
sales_plays.summary: Assembly holds AOR Media Planning and Buying for Hyatt, directing $16.1M in digital spend. This play challenges whether that allocation — particularly $9.6M in Paid Social and $2.4M in Display — is working as hard as direct, premium inventory could.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Jason Xenopoulos at Assembly acknowledging their creative partnership with Hyatt across five brand groups, then pivot: 'We've been looking at how Hyatt's $16.1M digital mix is distributed — specifically the $9.6M in Paid Social and $2.4M in Display — and we think there's a case for a more direct, brand-safe premium layer that Assembly could bring to Gary Dollens as a differentiated recommendation.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jason: 'Of the $9.6M Hyatt is running through Paid Social, how much is going to lower-funnel retargeting versus upper-funnel brand awareness — and is Gary satisfied with the brand-safety and contextual alignment of that inventory?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our premium, direct-sold inventory as the brand-safe, high-context alternative to the open-exchange and social placements currently absorbing Hyatt's display and social budget — especially relevant for Luxury and Lifestyle brand groups where contextual alignment matters.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame a $1–2M reallocation from the $9.6M Paid Social line as a testable shift: 'If even 15% of that social spend moved to premium direct inventory, Hyatt would be running roughly $1.4M through a channel with measurably higher viewability and brand-adjacency — a story Assembly could own with Gary.'

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jason for a 20-minute working session in the next two weeks: 'I'd like to show you a one-page inventory map against Hyatt's five brand groups — no pitch deck, just a conversation about where we fit in the mix Assembly is already managing.'

sales_plays.title: VML Creative-to-Media Referral Bridge
sales_plays.summary: VML holds AOR Creative for Hyatt globally. Because VML and Assembly operate as separate agencies on this account, VML's creative leadership — including Jason Xenopoulos in his Global CCO capacity — has a natural incentive to ensure their creative executions land in premium environments. This play uses VML as a warm referral path into Gary Dollens and the Assembly media team.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Jason Xenopoulos in his VML Global CCO role (distinct from his Assembly CEO hat): 'Jason, I know VML is doing the brand creative work across Hyatt's portfolio — Luxury through Essentials. We've been thinking about how to make sure that creative investment lands in the right media environments, and I'd love your perspective before we approach Assembly or Gary directly.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'When VML hands off creative to Assembly for Hyatt, do you have visibility into where that creative ultimately runs — and is there ever tension between the brand environments Gary Dollens wants and the inventory Assembly is buying at scale?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a joint conversation — VML creative + our premium inventory + Assembly's planning — as a way to close the gap between Hyatt's brand ambition (five distinct brand groups across 83 countries) and the commoditized inventory that often absorbs large digital budgets like Hyatt's $16.1M.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Reference Hyatt's $3.4M CTV spend as proof they already invest in premium video environments: 'Hyatt is already spending $3.4M in CTV — they clearly value premium context. We're asking whether the $247K in Online Video and the broader display mix reflects that same standard, or whether there's room to upgrade.'

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jason to make a warm introduction to Gary Dollens or the Assembly planning lead: 'Would you be comfortable copying me on a short note to Gary or the Assembly team — just framing us as worth a conversation? We'd do all the heavy lifting from there.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Jason — I work with [Your Company], and we've been spending time with Hyatt's media footprint ahead of a broader outreach. I know VML is doing the global creative work and Assembly is running media planning and buying, so I wanted to come to you first rather than cold-calling Gary Dollens or going around the agency relationship. We have a strong track record with travel and hospitality brands at this scale, and I think there's a real story we could build together for Hyatt's portfolio.
open_questions: Of the $9.6M Hyatt is running through Paid Social, how much is performance-driven retargeting versus upper-funnel brand awareness — and is Gary Dollens satisfied with the brand-safety standards of that inventory?
Who at Hyatt — Gary Dollens on the brand side or the Assembly planning team — ultimately signs off on channel mix decisions for the Luxury and Lifestyle brand groups specifically?
Does Assembly have a preferred premium direct partner already embedded in Hyatt's plan, or is the $2.4M Display budget running primarily through programmatic and open exchange?

Given that VML and Assembly are separate agencies on this account, how does creative performance feedback flow from Assembly back to VML — and is there a joint QBR where media environment quality gets discussed?
Hyatt is spending $3.4M in CTV but only $247K in Online Video — is that gap a deliberate strategic choice, or is it a planning artifact that Assembly hasn't revisited recently?

sales_plays.title: Mobile-First EV Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Hyundai is an active PMP customer with consecutive wins in 2024, 2025, and 2026, yet Mobile App spend sits at only $12.8M against a $49.1M digital total. Bombora shows live Mobile intent this week — a clear signal to expand the existing relationship into premium mobile inventory before a competitor does.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the active 2026 PMP win and Bombora's current Mobile intent signal. Email or call Lee Maicon at Canvas Worldwide: 'Lee, we're seeing Hyundai trending on Mobile intent in Bombora this week — given the 2026 PMP we just activated together, I wanted to flag a timely expansion opportunity before the window closes.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Lee: 'Canvas has $12.8M allocated to Mobile App for Hyundai right now — is that budget locked, or is there flexibility to shift incremental dollars into higher-impact mobile placements tied to the IONIQ and EV lineup?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position Weather Channel's mobile-first, weather-contextual inventory as the bridge between Hyundai's existing PMP commitment and the untapped $36.3M gap between total digital spend ($49.1M) and current mobile allocation ($12.8M). Weather-triggered mobile units align EV messaging to real-time driving-condition moments.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Hyundai's CTV spend is $7.2M versus $164.5M in Broadcast — a 4% CTV ratio. Mobile App at $12.8M is 26% of digital. Framing a $2M–$3M mobile expansion as a test-and-learn against the existing PMP infrastructure keeps risk low while adding measurable incremental reach against EV intenders.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Lee for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar before end of month to walk through a mobile expansion proposal scoped to the IONIQ line — I'll bring audience overlap data against your current PMP segments.'

sales_plays.title: IONIQ Direct Deal Displacement
sales_plays.summary: The IONIQ Forest campaign lost twice in March 2025 (two separate $50K opportunities at TWC Interactive), suggesting a competing placement or internal budget shift won that spend. This play targets displacing that incumbent solution by re-engaging with a stronger direct-deal structure tied to weather-driven EV demand moments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two IONIQ Forest losses from March 2025 directly with Lee Maicon: 'Lee, we lost the IONIQ Forest campaign in March — I'd like to understand what won that budget and whether there's a path to bring that spend back to TWC with a stronger contextual hook for the next EV push.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'What did the winning solution offer for IONIQ Forest that our proposal didn't — was it pricing, targeting capability, or a different channel mix?' This surfaces the exact gap to close.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): If the loss was on targeting or context, position TWC's weather-triggered EV creative units — cold-weather range anxiety, charging-station proximity, spring driving-season moments — as a differentiated layer that programmatic or social buys ($996K Paid Social) cannot replicate. Move the conversation from off-property programmatic to on-property direct.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The two lost IONIQ Forest opps totaled $50K — a small test budget. Frame a rebuilt proposal at $150K–$200K as a full-funnel direct deal that replaces fragmented programmatic spend (Internet Display at $8.3M, Online Video at $3.6M) with a consolidated, weather-contextual IONIQ campaign with measurable lift metrics.

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a brief debrief call with Lee Maicon and, if appropriate, loop in Sean Gilpin's team at Hyundai: 'Can we do a 20-minute debrief on the March decision — no pitch, just want to understand the criteria so any future IONIQ proposal is built to win. Would next Tuesday or Wednesday work?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Lee, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working together on the Hyundai PMP going back to 2024, and I'm glad we got the 2026 deal activated earlier this year. I wanted to reach out personally because we're seeing something specific in our intent data right now that I think is directly relevant to where Canvas is planning for Hyundai. Bombora is showing Hyundai trending on Mobile intent this week, and when I look at the media mix — $12.8M in Mobile App against $49.1M in total digital — there's a real gap between where the intent signal is pointing and where the dollars are currently concentrated. sales_plays.title: Digital Mix Expansion: From Social-Heavy to Full-Funnel
sales_plays.summary: ITW Global Brands is an active customer with $3.4M in digital spend, but 91% of that ($3.1M) is concentrated in Paid Social. This play targets Chad Ellman and Lauren Lytle to expand into underdeveloped channels — CTV, Online Video, and Display — to build a more balanced, full-funnel media mix across the ITW brand portfolio.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Lauren Lytle at Cramer-Krasselt referencing the current digital allocation: 'Lauren, I was looking at ITW Global Brands' current digital mix — $3.1M in Paid Social is doing a lot of heavy lifting. I wanted to share how a few of our other Home Services clients are using CTV and Online Video to extend reach beyond the social feed and drive stronger lower-funnel results. Would a 20-minute call this week make sense?'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Lauren: 'With $3.1M committed to Paid Social, where are you seeing the biggest gaps in the consumer journey — is it awareness at the top, or are you losing people before conversion?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position CTV and Online Video as the natural complement to Paid Social — CTV reaches cord-cutters and in-market homeowners that social algorithms miss, while Online Video reinforces brand messaging at a fraction of the $214K already committed to CTV, suggesting room to scale.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): ITW Global Brands currently spends only $11K on Online Video and $35K on Display against $3.1M in Paid Social. Shifting even 5% of the Paid Social budget (~$155K) into CTV/OLV could meaningfully extend reach among homeowners without cannibalizing existing social performance — frame this as incremental, not a reallocation.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Lauren to set up a joint call with Chad Ellman in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar with you and Chad before end of month to walk through a channel diversification proposal tailored to the ITW brand portfolio?'

sales_plays.title: Pre-Spring Home Improvement Timing Push
sales_plays.summary: ITW Global Brands operates in Home Services — a category with a well-established seasonal demand curve peaking in spring (March–May) as homeowners begin maintenance, repair, and improvement projects. With Bombora flagging active Marketing intent this week, now is the window to get in front of Lauren Lytle and Chad Ellman before Q1 media plans are locked.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Lauren Lytle this week, citing the Bombora Marketing intent signal: 'Lauren, we're seeing ITW Global Brands showing active research activity around Marketing right now — and given that spring is the peak demand window for home improvement brands, I wanted to connect before Q1 budgets are finalized. A few ideas I'd love to share.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Is the Q1/Q2 media plan for ITW's brand portfolio already locked with Cramer-Krasselt, or is there still flexibility to layer in incremental placements ahead of the spring home improvement season?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our CTV and digital solutions as a way to capture in-market homeowners in the February–April window — before competitors ramp up — using ITW's existing $214K CTV base as proof that the channel is already part of the mix and ready to scale.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): ITW's current Broadcast spend is only $97K versus $214K in CTV — the shift is already happening. A targeted pre-spring CTV/OLV push of $75K–$150K against homeowner audiences could front-run competitor spend and maximize ITW brand visibility during the highest-intent purchase window of the year.

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a specific, time-bound meeting: 'Can we get 20 minutes with you and Chad Ellman before January 31st to present a pre-spring activation concept built around the ITW brand portfolio? I want to make sure you have something concrete to react to before plans are set.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Lauren, this is [Rep Name] from [Company] — we work with Cramer-Krasselt on the ITW Global Brands account and have been supporting the digital program for some time now. I wanted to reach out because we've been doing a lot of work with Home Services brands heading into the spring season and I think there's a real opportunity we haven't fully explored together yet. I'm seeing ITW Global Brands showing active Marketing intent signals this week, which tells me the team is in planning mode right now. When I look at the current media mix — $3.1M in Paid Social doing the bulk of the work, with CTV at $214K and Online Video at just $11K — I can't help but think there's a full-funnel story we could be telling more effectively, especially as homeowners start their spring project research. Would it make sense to get 20 minutes on the calendar with you and Chad Ellman to walk through what that could look like for the ITW brand portfolio before Q1 plans are locked?

Given that Point B Communications handles Creative separately from Cramer-Krasselt's media buying role, how are campaign briefs coordinated across the two agencies when you're launching a new channel or format?

The five Q4 2025 proposals we submitted didn't move forward — can you help me understand what was missing, and what a proposal would need to look like to get serious consideration from Chad and the ITW team?

sales_plays.title: Opzelura/Zynyz Digital-to-Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Incyte is an active PMP customer with two consecutive won deals and $16M in digital spend. The Opzelura lead-gen opp from 2024 and the lost Zynyz Q3-Q4 2023 deal signal untapped brand-level budget that can be converted from programmatic/PMP into higher-impact direct placements.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins (Feb 2025 and Jul 2024) with Michael Kelly: 'Michael, we've now run two successful PMP campaigns together — I wanted to bring you a look at what brands like Opzelura and Zynyz could unlock by moving a portion of that spend into direct, guaranteed inventory on The Weather Channel's owned-and-operated properties, where we're seeing 2-3x the completion rates vs. open programmatic.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'For Opzelura and Zynyz specifically — are those brands currently being managed under the same digital budget you're overseeing, or are there separate brand teams and agencies we should be looping in to make sure the full picture is on the table?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Incyte's $16M digital mix is heavily weighted toward CTV ($18.2M) and online video ($1.5M), but display ($737K) and paid social ($938K) are fragmented. Position TWC's direct O&O inventory as a consolidation play: one premium contextual environment reaching patients and caregivers during high-engagement weather moments, replacing scattered open-market display.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost Zynyz deal was $120K and the Opzelura opp was scoped at $100K — both well below Incyte's digital run rate. A direct buy at even 1% of their $16M digital budget is $160K per brand, per quarter. Frame the ask as a brand-level test at $150K-$200K against a budget that already supports $18.2M in CTV.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar before end of month — I'll bring a one-page proposal showing exactly how a direct Opzelura placement on TWC O&O compares to what the PMP delivered, with CPM and completion benchmarks side by side?'

sales_plays.title: Zynyz Win-Back via Competitive Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Incyte lost the Zynyz Q3-Q4 2023 deal ($120K) to a competing media partner. With Zynyz still an active brand in Incyte's oncology portfolio and Incyte's CTV spend at $18.2M, there is a clear opening to displace the incumbent by demonstrating superior HCP and patient reach in the oncology/dermatology context.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Acknowledge the history directly with Michael Kelly: 'Michael, we didn't win the Zynyz business back in mid-2023 — I know that went another direction. I've been watching Zynyz's indication expansion and wanted to share something specific about how TWC's audience has shifted in the oncology and rare disease context that I think changes the calculus.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Who ended up running Zynyz media in 2023 and 2024 — is that still the same partner, and are you seeing the reach and frequency metrics you need against the payer and patient audiences you're targeting?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's contextual targeting against Incyte's Bombora-signaled interest in Retail Technology — a likely proxy for pharmacy/retail health channel strategy. TWC's weather-triggered audience segments can reach patients at the point of treatment decision (allergy season, skin flare triggers) in a way a generic digital partner cannot replicate for a dermatology/oncology brand like Zynyz.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The original Zynyz ask was $120K. Incyte now spends $18.2M in CTV alone. A re-scoped Zynyz direct buy at $150K-$175K is less than 1% of their CTV budget and can be structured as a Q3 2025 test with clear KPIs tied to payer marketing goals — directly relevant to Michael Kelly's Director of Payer Marketing role.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to send you a two-slide competitive comparison — TWC O&O vs. where Zynyz ran in 2023 — focused on verified reach in dermatology and oncology audiences. Would Thursday or Friday this week work for a 20-minute call to walk through it together?'

talk_track_opener: Michael, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working together on the TWC Interactive PMP campaigns, most recently closing the February 2025 deal. I work with pharma brands at your level of investment and I focus specifically on helping teams like yours get more out of the digital dollars that are already in motion. We've got a strong track record with Incyte across two consecutive PMP wins, and I think there's a real opportunity to build on that. Incyte's Bombora activity is showing interest in Retail Technology this week — is that connected to a pharmacy channel or specialty pharmacy initiative we should be aware of when thinking about how TWC's audience targeting could support patient access goals?

sales_plays.title: Mobile-to-CTV Bridge: Scale Sublocade Beyond App
sales_plays.summary: Indivior is an active customer with a won PMP deal in Feb 2025, but their CTV spend ($2.2M) is dramatically underweight relative to their $49.7M broadcast investment and $12.2M mobile app spend. This play expands the existing PMP relationship into premium CTV and digital video inventory to capture audiences already reached on linear.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the Feb 2025 PMP win with Havas Media Group and congratulate the team on activating. Tell Marie Brown and Jasmine Dadlani: 'You're running $12.2M in mobile app and $49.7M in broadcast — we want to show you where those two audiences overlap in our CTV environment, because right now only $2.2M of your $89.5M total spend is in CTV.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jasmine Dadlani: 'When Havas plans the Sublocade media mix, what KPI determines how much shifts from broadcast to CTV — is it reach duplication, cost-per-script, or something else driving that $2.2M ceiling?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-contextual CTV and premium OLV inventory as the connective tissue between Indivior's broadcast reach and their mobile app retargeting — patients who see a Sublocade spot on linear can be sequenced into a mobile app unit, closing the loop that the current $1.9M OLV budget cannot achieve at scale.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Their CTV spend is 2.5% of broadcast. Moving even 10% of the $49.7M broadcast budget ($4.97M) into TWC CTV would more than double their current CTV investment and extend addressable reach to cord-cutters not captured by linear — a segment highly relevant to the 18-45 OUD recovery audience.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jasmine to schedule a 30-minute Havas + TWC working session before the next Sublocade quarterly planning cycle to review a CTV reach-overlap analysis TWC will build using their existing PMP data. Propose a specific date within 2 weeks.

sales_plays.title: Reclaim Sublocade 2025: Displace the Feb Loss
sales_plays.summary: The Sublocade 2025 TWC-Interactive opportunity was lost on Feb 19, 2025 at $15,000 — a small deal that signals a competitive placement went elsewhere. This play re-engages Marie Brown and Havas to understand who won that budget and positions TWC's premium health-contextual environment against the incumbent.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Jasmine Dadlani at Havas Media Group directly: 'We were close on the Sublocade 2025 interactive unit in February — I want to understand what the winning partner offered that we didn't, because we've made changes to our health-contextual targeting suite since then and I think the answer might be different today.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'For the Sublocade interactive placement that went to another partner in February, was the decision driven by audience targeting capabilities, creative format, pricing, or publisher brand safety — and is that placement still active or up for review?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): If the incumbent won on targeting, position TWC's first-party weather and location data as uniquely relevant for OUD treatment — patients in weather-stressed, rural, or high-opioid-prevalence geographies are identifiable through TWC's data without relying on third-party health segments that face increasing regulatory scrutiny.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost deal was $15,000 — a test budget. Frame the re-engagement not as recovering $15K but as earning the right to a larger interactive allocation. Indivior spends $3.0M in display and $1.9M in OLV; even a 1% reallocation of those combined budgets ($49K) would be a 3x expansion over the lost unit.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Marie Brown for a 20-minute debrief call specifically on the February decision — frame it as a 'vendor feedback session' with no pitch agenda — to lower friction and gather the intelligence needed to displace the incumbent before the next planning cycle.

talk_track_opener: Hey Marie, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working with the Havas team on your PMP activation that closed in February, and I wanted to connect directly with you. We work with a number of specialty pharma brands running at your scale, and we've helped several of them bridge the gap between heavy broadcast investment and digital performance, particularly in CTV and mobile environments where patient audiences are increasingly hard to reach on linear alone. I wanted to share something specific to Indivior's mix before your next planning cycle.
open_questions: When the Sublocade 2025 interactive placement went to another partner in February, what capability or format did they offer that TWC didn't — and is that placement still running today?

Within Havas's current media plan for Sublocade, what threshold does CTV need to hit on reach or cost-per-script metrics before budget shifts from broadcast — and who at Indivior owns that benchmark decision, Marie or someone above her?
The $12.2M mobile app investment is significant — is that budget tied to a specific Sublocade patient support app, and is the media goal driving downloads, engagement, or script initiation?

With McKinney handling creative digital strategy and Havas owning media planning, who has the final call on interactive and rich media format selection — and was that the deciding factor in the February loss?

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask what the coffee test was designed to prove — was it incremental trial, daypart shift, or local market lift — and whether the outcome changed how Tombras is planning Sonic's Q1 local media mix.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC-Interactive's local weather and contextual targeting as uniquely suited to Sonic's morning daypart — cold-weather coffee demand signals can trigger geo-targeted creative in real time, something a standard social or display buy cannot replicate.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Sonic's prior test was scoped at $200K. Inspire's total digital footprint is $135.3M with $95.3M in paid social alone. sales_plays.title: Tentpole Planning Ahead
sales_plays.summary: Inspire Brands manages a multi-brand portfolio with $320M in annual media spend. With retail tentpole and cultural-moment planning cycles likely underway for Q1 and spring, this play opens a forward-looking conversation with Tombras and Anomaly before budgets are locked.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Robert Davis at The Tombras Group: 'We're seeing multi-brand QSR advertisers start Q1 and spring tentpole planning now — given Inspire's portfolio scale ($320M in annual spend), I wanted to get in front of you before those conversations close.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask which Inspire brands — Arby's, Buffalo Wild Wings, Sonic, Dunkin', or others — have the most aggressive Q1 local activation goals, and whether cultural moments like Super Bowl or Valentine's Day are being planned at the brand level or centrally through Inspire.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC-Interactive as a cross-brand amplifier — weather and moment-based triggers can be deployed across multiple Inspire brands simultaneously, giving Tombras and Anomaly a single activation layer that scales across the portfolio without duplicating creative or media buys.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Inspire's CTV spend is $15.3M and Mobile App is $14.6M — both are underleveraged relative to the $95.3M in paid social. A tentpole moment strategy that shifts even $3-5M of social spend into CTV and contextual mobile during high-intent weather windows could meaningfully improve cost-per-visit metrics across brands.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a 30-minute portfolio planning session with Robert Davis (Tombras) and a counterpart at Anomaly before November 1 — framed as a 'Q1 and spring moment readiness' review, not a pitch, to keep the ask low-friction and agency-relationship-first.

The Sonic coffee test suggested there's real interest in local, moment-based activation — I'd love to understand what that test taught you and whether there's a version of that thinking that scales across more of the portfolio. Would a quick call with you or Robert Davis at Tombras make sense in the next two weeks?

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars with On-Property Direct
sales_plays.summary: Instacart allocates $45.5M to Paid Social — the single largest digital sub-channel — yet TWC's on-property direct inventory remains untested in the CRM history. With two consecutive lost premium opportunities and PMP wins at nominal value, there is a clear displacement case to shift a portion of social budget to higher-intent, weather-contextual direct placements.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Laura Jones directly with a data-led observation: 'Instacart is putting $45.5M into Paid Social — we think a portion of that spend could work significantly harder on TWC's on-property inventory, where grocery and delivery intent spikes are measurable around weather events and stay-home moments.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When Universal McCann evaluates Paid Social performance for Instacart, what KPIs are they optimizing against — is it lower-funnel conversion, reach, or brand consideration — and is there a channel that's currently underperforming against those benchmarks?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC on-property direct as a precision alternative to broad social targeting: weather-triggered delivery intent (rain, cold snaps, storm prep) aligns exactly with Instacart's use case and reaches consumers at the moment of highest grocery delivery consideration — something a social feed cannot replicate.

sales_plays.title: Digital-to-Direct Upgrade: Scale Intel's $9.8M Digital Footprint On-Property
sales_plays.summary: Intel is an active PMP customer (two consecutive wins, 2024 and 2025) with $9.8M in digital spend heavily weighted toward Paid Social ($7.4M) and Display ($917K). The expansion opportunity is to convert programmatic/off-property budget into premium on-property direct buys that deliver higher-impact placements aligned to Intel's AI and performance computing messaging.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins — 'We've delivered on the TWC Interactive PMP in both 2024 and 2025, and I want to show Rubina Maharjan and the Dentsu Creative media planning team what a direct, on-property extension of that investment could unlock — especially given Intel's push into AI-optimized platforms where contextual adjacency matters.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Dentsu Creative's Jason Xenopoulos: 'Of Intel's $7.4M in Paid Social and $917K in Display, how much is currently running in premium contextual environments versus open-exchange or social feed inventory — and is there a Q3/Q4 planning window where Rubina's team is evaluating a shift toward higher-quality placements?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's on-property weather and tech-lifestyle audience as a direct upgrade from open-exchange display — Intel's AI and edge computing products resonate with the high-intent, device-aware audience actively checking weather on connected devices, bridging the gap between Intel's $154K Mobile App spend and a richer on-property mobile experience.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Intel's current TWC PMP deals closed at nominal value ($1 each), suggesting test/pilot status. Even a 5% reallocation of the $917K Display budget into a direct on-property buy represents ~$46K incremental, and a 2% shift from the $7.4M Paid Social line represents ~$148K — framing a $150K–$200K direct deal as a modest reallocation, not new budget.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jason Xenopoulos for a 30-minute media planning session in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 30 minutes with the Dentsu media planning team before the Q3 budget lock to walk through a direct placement proposal built around Intel's AI platform messaging and TWC's contextual audience data?'

sales_plays.title: Displace Open-Exchange Display: Capture Intel's $917K Display Budget with Premium Inventory
sales_plays.summary: Intel spends $917K on Internet Display, most likely running across open-exchange or DSP-sourced inventory. This play targets displacement of that spend — or a meaningful portion of it — by demonstrating that TWC's premium, brand-safe, contextually relevant display environment outperforms generic open-exchange placements for Intel's brand and product campaigns.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a competitive insight to Rubina Maharjan at Altera/Intel: 'We're seeing tech and semiconductor brands shift display budgets away from open-exchange inventory toward premium contextual environments — particularly as brand safety and viewability standards tighten. Given Intel's $917K in Internet Display, I'd like to show you what that budget could deliver on TWC versus where it's likely running today.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Where is Intel's current $917K display budget being managed — is Dentsu Creative running it through a DSP against open exchange, or is there already a curated PMP/direct component beyond the TWC deals we've done together?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's display inventory offers Intel verified brand-safe placements alongside weather-driven, high-intent content moments — directly relevant to Intel's edge computing and connected device narratives. This is a direct displacement argument against generic DSP-sourced display that lacks contextual relevance to Intel's AI and performance computing audience.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If Intel's $917K display is running at a typical open-exchange CPM of $3–$5, they're buying roughly 180M–300M impressions with low viewability and brand safety risk. A TWC premium direct buy at a higher CPM delivers fewer but far higher-quality impressions — reframe the conversation from volume to verified audience quality, and propose a $200K–$300K direct display package as a displacement test against their current DSP line.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I can put together a side-by-side comparison of open-exchange display benchmarks versus TWC on-property performance metrics for Intel's vertical — can I send that to you and Jason Xenopoulos at Dentsu Creative by end of this week, and schedule a 20-minute debrief before the Q3 planning cycle closes?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Rubina — this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've had a solid partnership with Intel over the past two years, closing TWC Interactive PMP deals in both mid-2024 and early 2025, so I'm not calling cold — I'm calling because I think we're leaving real value on the table given the scale of what Intel is investing in digital right now. I work with the Dentsu Creative team as well, including Jason Xenopoulos, and I want to make sure we're aligned on where TWC fits into the broader media plan. Here's what caught my attention: Intel is running roughly $9.8M in digital, with $7.4M in Paid Social and $917K in Internet Display — but the TWC relationship is still at pilot scale. For a brand pushing AI-optimized platforms and edge computing to a device-aware, high-intent audience, there's a strong case for moving a portion of that display and social budget into premium on-property placements where contextual relevance is built in. Before your Q3 planning window closes, I'd love to ask: is Dentsu currently evaluating any shift from open-exchange display toward curated, premium environments for Intel's brand campaigns?
open_questions: The TWC PMP deals in 2024 and 2025 both closed at nominal value — were those structured as test buys, and has Rubina's team or Dentsu evaluated scaling them based on performance data from those campaigns?
Of Intel's $917K in Internet Display, how much is currently managed through a DSP on open exchange versus curated PMPs or direct deals — and who at Dentsu Creative owns that line item day-to-day?

Intel's Paid Social spend is $7.4M — is Dentsu Creative seeing any pressure from Intel's marketing leadership to diversify away from social feed inventory, particularly for brand-level AI and platform campaigns?

Is Rubina Maharjan's scope at Altera/Intel focused on a specific product line or business unit, and does Dentsu Creative manage media separately for Altera versus Intel's core processor and AI platform brands?

sales_plays.title: Holiday Gifting Lead-In: Expand IHG's TWC Footprint Before Q4 Demand Peaks
sales_plays.summary: IHG is an active TWC customer with two consecutive PMP wins (Jan 2025, Jan 2026) and a $30K MLAC PG win in 2H 2024. The lost Cyber Sale Q4 2024 opp ($100K) signals unmet Q4 demand. With $117.9M in total media spend and a $65.4M search-heavy mix, there is clear room to expand into premium direct and on-property placements ahead of the holiday and gifting travel window.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Jeff Traverso at Mindshare referencing the two consecutive PMP wins and the lost $100K Cyber Sale opp from Q4 2024. Open with: 'Jeff, we've had strong momentum together on the PMP side — two wins in a row — but we left $100K on the table last October. Holiday travel planning starts earlier every year and I want to make sure IHG is positioned before that window closes.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jeff: 'For Q4 2025, is the Cyber Sale and holiday gifting budget being planned through Mindshare, and has Heather Balsley's team set a target for driving IHG One Rewards gifting card or premium brand bookings during that window?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered audience targeting as uniquely suited to IHG's multi-brand portfolio — travelers checking weather at a destination are in active trip-planning mode. Highlight that IHG's $8.5M CTV and $1.3M online video spend shows appetite for premium video, and TWC can extend that reach with contextually relevant placements tied to holiday and winter travel moments across InterContinental, Kimpton, and Holiday Inn brands.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost Q4 2024 Cyber Sale opp was $100K. IHG's digital spend is $24.2M, with only $1.3M in online video — a clear underweight relative to the $8.5M CTV investment. A $150K–$200K Q4 2025 TWC package (weather-triggered video + PMP extension) represents less than 1% of IHG's total media budget but closes the gap on the lost opp and adds incremental reach to an audience already in destination-search mode.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jeff for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks — before Mindshare locks Q4 plans — to co-build a Q4 2025 IHG holiday package proposal that addresses the Cyber Sale gap and adds a gifting/loyalty angle for Heather Balsley's team.

sales_plays.title: Displace Search Overweight: Shift IHG Budget from $65.4M Search to Weather-Intent Premium
sales_plays.summary: IHG's $65.4M search spend dwarfs every other channel and represents a concentration risk — high CPCs, commoditized inventory, and no contextual differentiation. TWC's weather-intent data creates a displacement argument: reach the same in-market traveler earlier in the decision journey, before they type a query, at a lower effective CPM and with brand-safe premium context.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Jeff Traverso at Mindshare with a data-led provocation: 'IHG is spending $65.4M in search — nearly 55% of total media. We're seeing travel brands shift 5–10% of search budgets into weather-intent upper-funnel to capture travelers before the query, not after. I'd like to show you what that looks like for IHG's brand portfolio.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When Mindshare evaluates IHG's search efficiency, what's the biggest frustration — rising CPCs on branded terms, OTA competition on non-branded, or difficulty attributing upper-funnel influence on search conversion?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's weather-triggered audience segments identify users actively planning travel to IHG destination markets (e.g., users checking tropical weather in January, ski destination forecasts in November). These users are pre-query — reaching them with a Kimpton or InterContinental brand message on TWC before they hit Google reduces IHG's dependence on expensive branded and non-branded search terms where OTAs outbid them.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 3% reallocation of IHG's $65.4M search budget equals ~$1.96M. Even a $250K–$500K TWC weather-intent pilot — less than 1% of search spend — would generate measurable upper-funnel lift data that Mindshare can use to justify a larger reallocation to Heather Balsley's team. Frame it as a test-and-learn with a clear Q4 2025 read.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a 20-minute 'search displacement audit' call with Jeff Traverso and a Mindshare digital planner within the next 10 days. Offer to bring TWC's travel vertical data showing search-to-weather-intent audience overlap for IHG's top destination markets as a pre-read.

talk_track_opener: Hey Jeff, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together on IHG's PMP business — two wins in a row, most recently in January 2026 — and I wanted to get ahead of Q4 planning before Mindshare locks budgets. I know IHG had a Cyber Sale opportunity last October that didn't come together, and I don't want to see that happen again this year, especially with holiday travel demand building earlier every cycle. We have a real solution for that window and I'd love 30 minutes to walk you through it before the planning calendar gets crowded. On the broader media mix side, I've been looking at IHG's spend profile — $65.4M in search is a significant concentration, and we're seeing travel brands use weather-intent data to reach the same in-market traveler before they ever type a query, which meaningfully reduces CPC pressure on branded terms. I'm curious: when Mindshare is evaluating IHG's Q4 efficiency, is search cost or OTA competition on non-branded terms the bigger pain point right now?
open_questions: Jeff, the Cyber Sale opp in Q4 2024 didn't close — was that a budget timing issue on IHG's side, a targeting concern, or something else we should address differently in the Q4 2025 proposal?

For IHG's holiday and gifting travel campaigns, does Heather Balsley's team set separate KPIs for loyalty (IHG One Rewards) versus direct booking revenue, and which metric drives the media investment decision?

With $65.4M in search spend, is Mindshare seeing margin compression on IHG's branded terms from OTA bidding, and has there been internal discussion about shifting budget to upper-funnel channels to reduce that pressure?

The two PMP wins have been structured at nominal value — is there appetite from Mindshare or IHG to move toward a larger programmatic guaranteed or direct IO relationship for Q4 2025 that reflects the scale of IHG's $117.9M total budget?

Kinetic Worldwide also holds an AOR role for IHG — is out-of-home or digital-out-of-home part of the Q4 holiday plan, and is there a cross-channel brief where TWC's weather-triggered data could inform both the digital and OOH buys?

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's audience targeting against Intuit's small business and personal finance segments — QuickBooks targets SMB owners and Credit Karma targets credit-building consumers, both of whom over-index on weather and local content consumption, a natural fit for TWC's contextual environment.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): TurboTax alone has committed $700K across two active deals. If QuickBooks and Credit Karma each mirror even 50% of that TurboTax investment, the incremental opportunity is $700K+ in net-new brand spend within the same agency relationship at Initiative.

sales_plays.title: Shift TurboTax Programmatic Spend to Direct TWC Deals

Open with: 'We've had three consecutive PMP wins together — 2024, 2025, and now 2026 — and I want to show you what JetBlue is leaving on the table by staying only in the programmatic lane.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Cassidy Wilber: 'Of JetBlue's $31.4M in Paid Social and $11.7M in Mobile App spend, how much is currently allocated to contextually relevant, travel-adjacent environments versus broad audience buys?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct, on-property inventory — weather-triggered placements, travel planning content, and destination pages — as a contextual upgrade that reaches JetBlue's audience when they are actively in a travel mindset, something a PMP buy cannot guarantee.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): JetBlue's current PMP deals closed at $1 each — essentially programmatic access fees. Even a 1% shift of the $48.8M digital budget into direct premium inventory represents ~$488K in incremental, higher-CPM spend with measurable lift over the PMP baseline.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute working session with Cassidy Wilber at Mediahub Worldwide New York before the next quarterly planning cycle to present a side-by-side PMP vs. direct performance comparison using TWC's travel vertical benchmarks.

sales_plays.title: Displace the Q4 Loss: Reclaim the Holiday Budget
sales_plays.summary: JetBlue's Q4 2024 Holiday pitch was lost at $50,000 — the only meaningful dollar-value opportunity in the CRM that did not close. That budget went somewhere else. With the 2025 holiday planning cycle approaching and JetBlue showing Bombora intent around Entertainment, this is the moment to displace the incumbent and re-enter the Q4 conversation.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the lost Q4 2024 opportunity directly with Ray Ramdin: 'We pitched JetBlue's holiday campaign last October and it went another direction. I'd like to understand what won that business and show you what we've built since then specifically for travel brands in Q4.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'What did the winning Q4 2024 partner deliver that we didn't — was it reach, format, audience targeting, or pricing — and is that partner already in conversations for Q4 2025?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): JetBlue's current Bombora intent signal around Entertainment maps directly to TWC's content ecosystem during fall and holiday travel planning. Position TWC's entertainment-adjacent and travel content as the environment where JetBlue's holiday messaging lands against an audience already in booking mode.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost Q4 2024 opportunity was $50,000. JetBlue's CTV spend sits at $1.3M and Online Video at $407K — both formats that TWC can serve at scale in a holiday context. A Q4 2025 package anchored in CTV and contextual video could recapture and exceed that $50K loss while pulling from existing budget lines already allocated.

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a brief 20-minute call with Ray Ramdin before August to present a Q4 2025 holiday proposal built around JetBlue's TrueBlue loyalty audience and the current Entertainment intent signal — framed as a first look before the broader agency RFP process begins.

talk_track_opener: Ray, I'm with The Weather Company — we've been working with JetBlue through Mediahub for three consecutive years on your PMP, and I want to be straight with you: those deals have been foundational but they're also the floor, not the ceiling, of what we can do together. We work with travel brands at every level of the funnel, and JetBlue's scale — $48.8M in digital, $1.3M in CTV, $31.4M in Paid Social — tells me there's real room to move beyond programmatic access and into placements that actually put JetBlue in front of someone the moment weather or a destination search triggers a travel decision. I also noticed we pitched your Q4 holiday campaign last October and it went another direction — I'd genuinely like to understand what won that business, because we've sharpened our Q4 travel offering considerably since then, and your current intent signals around Entertainment line up well with where we see travel audiences engaging this fall. JetBlue's PMP deals have been structured at nominal value — is that a procurement or agency preference, and is there a path to direct IO buys for premium placements outside the PMP framework?

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars with Weather-Contextual Display & Video
sales_plays.summary: Jockey allocates $10.9M to Paid Social — the dominant line in an $11.8M digital budget — while TWC Display ($121K) and Mobile App ($17K) are negligible. steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with Matthew Waller at Jockey or route through Jennifer Schneller — reference the Bombora 'Education' intent signal this week as a conversation starter: 'We're seeing Jockey showing active research interest in audience education formats — that aligns with something we're seeing across retail apparel brands shifting budget away from social feed placements.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Of Jockey's $10.9M in Paid Social, how much is performance-driven versus brand awareness — and is the team evaluating any channels that offer contextual relevance that social algorithms can't replicate?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered display, mobile, and online video inventory as a complement and partial displacement to Paid Social — when a cold front hits, Jockey's base layer or sleepwear creative reaches a high-intent audience in a brand-safe, contextually matched moment that no social feed algorithm can time as precisely.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Jockey spends $10.9M on Paid Social against $121K on TWC Display and $17K on Mobile App. A reallocation of just 3% of Paid Social (~$327K) into TWC weather-contextual formats would represent a 19x increase in TWC Display + Mobile investment and diversify reach beyond social platform dependency.

sales_plays.title: From PMP to Full Digital Stack
sales_plays.summary: John Hardy has two consecutive PMP wins (2024 and 2025) but their $2.4M digital footprint is heavily concentrated in Paid Social ($1.7M) with relatively thin investment in CTV ($125K), Online Video ($502K), and Display ($69K). This play expands the relationship beyond the existing PMP into premium video and audience-extension products.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Jonas Malpass referencing the two consecutive PMP wins and note that John Hardy's digital mix shows $1.7M in Paid Social against only $125K in CTV — open with: 'Jonas, given the momentum we've built together since July 2024, I wanted to share how a few brands in the sustainable luxury space are diversifying away from social CPM inflation into premium CTV and video environments.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jonas: 'With Paid Social representing roughly 70% of your digital spend, how are you thinking about frequency caps and audience fatigue heading into your next major collection launch — and is CTV part of the conversation for FY26?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium CTV and Online Video inventory as a brand-safe, high-attention complement to the existing PMP — specifically for reaching affluent, sustainability-minded consumers who over-index on weather and lifestyle content, which aligns with John Hardy's Balinese heritage and outdoor/travel aesthetic.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): John Hardy's current TWC CTV investment is $125K against a $2.4M digital budget — less than 6%. Shifting even 10% of the $1.7M Paid Social budget ($170K) into TWC CTV/OLV would more than double their presence on the platform and reduce reliance on a single channel prone to CPM volatility.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jonas for a 20-minute working session in the next two weeks to walk through a proposed audience overlap analysis showing how TWC's CTV reach complements — not duplicates — his existing Paid Social audiences. Offer to loop in Michael Sharp at Standard Black if creative alignment is needed.

sales_plays.title: Retail Tentpole Planning Window
sales_plays.summary: John Hardy is a luxury jewelry brand with $3.2M in trailing 12-month spend. Retail tentpoles (Valentine's Day, Mother's Day, Holiday) are high-stakes windows for jewelry. This play positions TWC as a planning partner ahead of the next major gifting moment — before budgets are locked with Standard Black.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Jonas Malpass 10–12 weeks before the next retail tentpole (e.g., Mother's Day or Holiday) referencing the active PMP relationship: 'Jonas, with our PMP partnership running strong since 2024, I wanted to get ahead of your next gifting season planning cycle — jewelry is one of the top gifting categories on TWC during [Mother's Day/Holiday], and I'd love to show you what the audience looks like.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'When does your team typically lock media plans for major gifting windows like Mother's Day or Holiday — and is Standard Black running that planning process, or does your team own the tentpole strategy directly?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Present TWC's gifting-season audience data for luxury jewelry — weather-driven travel and lifestyle audiences who are active gift purchasers — and connect it to John Hardy's sustainability and artisanal positioning as a differentiated gifting story vs. mass jewelry brands.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): John Hardy's $610K in Search spend signals strong intent-capture investment, but tentpole windows require upper-funnel awareness to drive that search volume. A targeted CTV or OLV flight of $75K–$150K in the 4–6 weeks before a gifting peak could measurably lift branded search queries — a metric Jonas, as VP of eCommerce, will care about.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send Jonas a one-page audience brief specific to luxury jewelry gifting on TWC within 48 hours, and request a 15-minute call with him — and optionally Michael Sharp at Standard Black — to align on whether a tentpole package fits the upcoming planning cycle.

Looking at John Hardy's footprint, you're running about $1.7M through Paid Social against $125K in CTV — and given the brand's story around Balinese craftsmanship and sustainable luxury, there's a premium audience on our platform that I think is genuinely underserved for you right now. sales_plays.summary: KIND LLC is a subsidiary of Mars, Incorporated. A successful first engagement with CMO Osher Hoberman creates a natural referral path into Mars's broader brand portfolio, which spans confectionery, petcare, and food — all heavy media spenders.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): KIND's combined media footprint of $30.2M (Broadcast $5.3M + CTV $4.3M + Digital $20.6M) is a meaningful test case. A successful pilot with KIND — even a $500K direct buy — gives Mars's central team a documented ROI data point to justify scaling across other brands.

Note that Kenvue's $194.1M digital spend signals room to consolidate more premium on-property inventory under one integrated plan rather than brand-by-brand buys.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Kathy: 'For 2026, is Publicis planning separate brand-level digital buys for Neutrogena, Zyrtec, and the broader Kenvue health portfolio, or is there appetite to consolidate into a single cross-brand sponsorship package that delivers unified reach and shared measurement?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a multi-brand on-property sponsorship — anchoring on the Allergy Tracker Sponsorship format (proven $2M idea already in the pipeline) and extending it to include Neutrogena UV/sun content integrations, leveraging weather-triggered contextual signals that align sun exposure and allergy season messaging.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The existing Allergy Tracker lead is valued at $2M and the Neutrogena Sun lead at $500K — a combined $2.5M opportunity already in motion. A consolidated cross-brand package could realistically target $3–4M, representing less than 1% of Kenvue's $405.5M trailing 12-month spend but delivering measurably higher integration value than fragmented buys.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Kathy to schedule a 30-minute working session before January 15, 2026 to align on a single 2026 Kenvue portfolio proposal that covers Neutrogena Sun, Zyrtec, and at least one additional Kenvue brand — so Publicis can present it as a unified plan to Andrea Matthews.

sales_plays.title: Displace Zyrtec STV Loss — Reclaim with Premium CTV Direct
sales_plays.summary: Kenvue's Zyrtec STV deal was lost in September 2025. With $55.6M in CTV spend and $151.2M in Broadcast, Kenvue is clearly investing heavily in video — but a competitor captured the Zyrtec STV budget. This play re-enters through a differentiated premium CTV direct offer that the losing incumbent cannot match.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the lost Kenvue-Zyrtec STV deal (closed 9/11/2025) directly with Kathy Miller: 'We know we didn't win the Zyrtec STV business this past cycle — we'd like to understand what the winning solution offered that we didn't, and show you what's changed on our end for 2026.' This signals accountability and opens a diagnostic conversation.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'For the Zyrtec STV buy that went elsewhere — was the decision driven primarily by CPM, by audience targeting capabilities, or by the incumbent's ability to bundle with broader linear or programmatic commitments?' This identifies the exact gap to displace.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a direct premium CTV package with contextual allergy-season triggers — moving Kenvue off programmatic STV (where the loss likely occurred) and onto guaranteed, brand-safe, on-property CTV inventory with first-party audience data. Contrast this with the programmatic/open-market STV approach the competitor likely used to win on price.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Kenvue spends $55.6M in CTV total. Even recapturing a modest 5–8% share of that budget for Zyrtec STV direct would represent $2.8M–$4.4M in incremental revenue — well above the original lost deal size. Frame this as a premium upgrade, not a price match.

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a 20-minute call with Kathy Miller and, if possible, Andrea Matthews before February 1, 2026 to present a revised Zyrtec 2026 CTV proposal with a head-to-head comparison of programmatic STV vs. direct premium CTV outcomes — using Kenvue's own prior won Allergy and PMP deals as proof of partnership performance.

talk_track_opener: Hey Kathy, this is [Rep Name] from [Company] — we've been working with the Kenvue team for a while now, and we've had some real wins together, including the Allergy deal and the TWC Interactive PMP earlier this year. I wanted to reach out because I think there's a meaningful opportunity we haven't fully explored yet across the broader Kenvue portfolio. Looking at what Kenvue is investing — over $405 million in trailing media spend, with $194 million in digital alone and $55 million in CTV — it's clear the ambition is there. What I'm noticing is that the buys are still largely brand-by-brand: Neutrogena Sun is its own lead, Zyrtec had its own STV buy, Allergy Tracker is separate. I'd love to spend 30 minutes with you before the end of January to explore whether there's a smarter way to consolidate some of that into a unified cross-brand package for 2026 — one that gives Publicis a stronger story to bring to Andrea and gives Kenvue better integration value per dollar. Would that be worth a quick conversation?
open_questions: For the Zyrtec STV deal that went to a competitor in September — was the primary decision factor CPM pricing, audience targeting capabilities, or bundling with a broader linear or programmatic commitment?
Is Publicis North America planning to bring Kenvue a unified 2026 digital plan across Neutrogena, Zyrtec, and other brands, or are brand teams still briefing and buying independently?
Who at Kenvue — Andrea Matthews or brand-level marketing leads — has final approval authority on the Neutrogena Sun at Target $500K opportunity before the 12/31/2025 deadline?
The Allergy Tracker Sponsorship has been in Lead/Idea Generation since April 2024 — what has prevented it from moving to a formal proposal or buy, and what would need to change for 2026?
Given that Kenvue allocates $128.2M to Paid Social versus only $3.3M to Online Video, is there internal discussion about rebalancing toward premium video and contextual placements for 2026?
