sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Kia has two consecutive won PMP deals (Jan 2025, Jul 2024) and $112.4M in digital spend, but the $100K High Impact + Data proposal was lost in Q1 2024. This play converts the active PMP relationship into a higher-value premium direct buy by building on trust with Julia Gutnik and Canvas Worldwide.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Julia Gutnik referencing the two consecutive PMP wins and note that Kia's digital mix — $44.5M paid social, $18.6M online video, $5M display — signals room to consolidate premium inventory under one direct relationship. Ask for 20 minutes to show what a Q3/Q4 direct package could look like alongside the existing PMP.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Julia: 'When you look at the $18.6M in online video and $5M in display, how much of that is currently running through open exchange versus curated or direct deals — and is Canvas Worldwide seeing the brand-safety and viewability benchmarks you need from the open exchange buys?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium direct inventory as the upgrade path from the PMP foundation already in place — same trusted relationship, but with guaranteed placement, first-look on high-impact formats, and audience data layering that the PMP SKUs don't include. Reference the lost $100K High Impact + Data proposal from Jan 2024 as unfinished business worth revisiting now that the relationship is proven.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the opportunity: Kia spends $62.9M on CTV and $18.6M on online video. Even shifting 5% of combined video spend ($4.1M) from open/PMP to a premium direct deal represents a meaningful step up. Show CPM efficiency and incremental reach data specific to Kia's Sorento, Telluride, and K4 audience targets.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Julia and Brendan Robertson at Canvas Worldwide to join a 30-minute working session before June 30 to review a custom Q3 premium package — framed as a planning conversation, not a pitch.

sales_plays.title: Broadcast Budget Displacement — Weather-Driven Demand Windows
sales_plays.summary: Kia spends $223M in broadcast and $62.9M in CTV but shows Bombora intent around Entertainment this week. This play targets Canvas Worldwide's media planning team to redirect a slice of broadcast dollars into TWC's on-property digital and CTV inventory ahead of weather-driven automotive demand windows (spring/fall model-year transitions).
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Brendan Robertson at Canvas Worldwide with a data-led observation: 'Kia is allocating $223M to broadcast and $62.9M to CTV — but weather-correlated purchase intent for SUV segments like Telluride and Sorento spikes in windows that linear TV can't target in real time. We have TWC audience data that maps those moments. Worth a look before Q3 planning locks?'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Brendan: 'For the Telluride and Sorento campaigns specifically, how is Canvas currently activating against weather-triggered purchase intent signals — and is that happening at the broadcast buy level, the digital level, or not yet at all?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-audience targeting as a displacement argument against untargeted broadcast GRPs — not a complement, but a smarter alternative for specific demand windows. Tie to Kia's Bombora Entertainment intent signal as evidence that Kia's audience is actively researching lifestyle and vehicle content, making contextual alignment on TWC properties highly relevant.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Illustrate that reallocating even 2% of Kia's $223M broadcast budget ($4.5M) into weather-triggered digital and CTV on TWC would reach in-market SUV shoppers at the precise moment weather conditions drive showroom consideration — with measurable attribution that broadcast cannot provide.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer Brendan a pre-built weather-audience analysis for Kia's top three models (Telluride, Sorento, K4) showing demand index by geography and season — deliverable within one week, no commitment required — with a follow-up call scheduled before Canvas Worldwide's Q3 broadcast planning cycle closes.

talk_track_opener: Hey Julia, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Kia and Canvas Worldwide for a couple of years now — most recently closing two PMP deals in July 2024 and January 2025 — so I want to come to you as a partner, not just a vendor. We've got a real track record together and I think there's a logical next step we haven't taken yet. Looking at Kia's media mix, you're running $18.6M in online video and $5M in display alongside $62.9M in CTV — and I know from the Q1 2024 conversation that High Impact plus Data formats were on the table but didn't close. Given where the relationship is now and the fact that Bombora is showing Kia active on Entertainment content this week, I think the timing is right to revisit a premium direct package that goes beyond the PMP. Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar with you and Brendan before the end of June to map out what a Q3 direct buy could look like?
open_questions: The $100K High Impact plus Data proposal lost in January 2024 — what specifically didn't land at the time, and has anything changed in how Kia evaluates high-impact digital formats since the two PMP deals closed?

For the Telluride and Sorento campaigns, is Canvas Worldwide currently using any weather-triggered or contextual audience signals to time digital activations, or is that targeting happening primarily at the broadcast planning level?

Julia, between you and Brendan Robertson at Canvas Worldwide, who owns the final decision on moving budget from open-exchange digital into premium direct deals — and is that a joint call or does it sit with one side?

Kia is spending $44.5M in paid social and $18.6M in online video — are those budgets being evaluated against a shared brand-safety and viewability standard, and is TWC's inventory currently being benchmarked against those social platforms?
With $62.9M already in CTV, is Kia looking to grow that allocation in 2025 or hold steady — and if growing, is the incremental budget coming from broadcast reallocation or net-new digital investment?

sales_plays.title: Kleenex Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Kimberly-Clark has an active PMP relationship (won Jan 2026) but two consecutive Kleenex direct buys were lost in 2025. This play converts existing PMP access into a premium direct/sponsorship package tied to Kleenex's allergy and cold-season demand window, expanding wallet share before the spring planning cycle closes.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the active PMP (Kimberly Clark-PMP-2026-TWC-Interactive, won Jan 7 2026) and acknowledge the two Kleenex direct losses ($50K each in Q4 2025 and Q1 2025). Open with: 'We've been a consistent PMP partner and I want to make sure we're solving whatever gap kept the Kleenex direct buys from crossing the finish line — before allergy season ramps.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'For the Kleenex allergy and cold-season campaigns, what did the PMP alone not deliver that the direct buy was supposed to fix — was it audience guarantee, contextual placement, or creative integration?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's on-property weather-contextual inventory as the bridge: Kleenex demand spikes with pollen counts and cold fronts. A direct sponsorship package ties Kleenex creative to real-time weather triggers that PMP pipes cannot guarantee, solving the contextual precision gap the PMP lost deals suggest.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Kimberly-Clark's total trailing 12-month spend is $284.5M, with $18.9M already in CTV and $4.5M in Online Video. A direct Kleenex package in the $150K–$250K range represents less than 0.1% of total spend but captures the high-intent weather-contextual moment the two $50K lost deals were chasing at a fraction of the digital display line ($9.5M).

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Melissa Kinch at Ketchum (President, US Consultancy) for a 20-minute call in the next two weeks to walk through a Kleenex allergy-season contextual package before Q2 planning locks — offer to bring a one-page audience overlap analysis against their existing PMP data as the entry point.

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Rebalance to Weather-Contextual
sales_plays.summary: Kimberly-Clark is heavily weighted toward Paid Social at $112M — 73% of their $152.9M digital spend. This play challenges whether social platforms are the right environment for CPG categories like Huggies, Depend, and Kotex that have strong weather and seasonal demand signals, and positions TWC's contextual digital inventory as a performance complement or partial displacement.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the spend imbalance: 'You're running $112M through Paid Social against $9.5M in Display and $4.5M in Online Video. For categories like Huggies and Depend where purchase intent is driven by life stage and routine — not social discovery — I want to show you what contextual weather-triggered inventory is doing for comparable CPG brands.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'On the Paid Social side, which KC brand families are hitting frequency caps or showing diminishing ROAS — and is there a category where the team feels the creative message isn't landing in the right mindset moment?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-contextual display and video inventory as a mindset-match solution: consumers checking weather are in a planning and household-management mindset that aligns with CPG staples (tissue, diapers, feminine care). This is a displacement of marginal Paid Social dollars into a higher-intent context, not a wholesale shift.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Even a 3% reallocation of the $112M Paid Social budget equals $3.36M — against a current TWC Online Video line of $4.5M, that would nearly double KC's contextual video presence. The $9.5M Display line shows they already buy display; the argument is quality of context, not format adoption.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a 30-minute working session with Melissa Kinch at Ketchum and any available KC media lead to review a side-by-side contextual vs. social audience overlap report for one brand (suggest Kleenex or Huggies) — deliverable in hand within one week, no commitment required.

talk_track_opener: Hey Melissa, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Kimberly-Clark on the PMP side since 2024 and just renewed again in January, so I want to make sure we're being a real partner and not just a line item. I also know the Kleenex direct campaigns didn't come together in 2025 — twice — and I'd rather understand why than pretend it didn't happen. Here's what caught my attention: KC is running $112 million through Paid Social and only $4.5 million in Online Video and $9.5 million in Display. For brands like Kleenex, Huggies, and Kotex, where demand is tied to seasons, life stages, and yes — weather — that feels like a mismatch between where the money is and where the intent moment actually lives. I'd love 20 minutes to show you what weather-contextual inventory is doing for CPG brands in similar categories. What does your calendar look like in the next two weeks?
open_questions: For the two Kleenex direct campaigns that didn't close in 2025 — was the primary objection budget timing, targeting capabilities, or measurement/attribution requirements from the KC brand team?
Who on the Kimberly-Clark media or brand side owns the final sign-off on direct digital buys above $50K — is that sitting with a KC in-house team or running through Ketchum?
On the $112M Paid Social line, which brand families — Kleenex, Huggies, Kotex, Depend — are being prioritized, and is there a category where the team is actively looking to test new channels in 2026?
Is the current PMP setup being used across all four KC business segments (Consumer Tissue, Healthcare, K-C Professional, Personal Care) or is it concentrated in one or two brands?

With retail tentpole moments like spring allergy season and back-to-school coming up, has the KC or Ketchum team already locked Q2 digital budgets, or is there still flexibility in the planning cycle?

steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two PMP wins directly — 'Kohl's has been running with us through PMP since mid-2024 and we renewed again in January 2025. I want to show Christie Raymond's team what a direct, premium placement looks like alongside that programmatic base, especially as you head into Back-to-School planning.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask what drove the loss on the 'WhereFamiliesComeFirst' deal in December 2024 — was it budget timing, creative readiness, or a competing priority — so we can rebuild the right package this cycle.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a direct TWC Interactive premium unit as the brand-safe, high-attention complement to the existing PMP buys — same audience, higher share of voice, better creative control for Kohl's owned brands like Sonoma Goods For Life and LC Lauren Conrad.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Kohl's spends $48M in digital and $45.4M in CTV. The two PMP wins are effectively $1 placeholder deals — even a $150K–$250K direct commitment represents less than 0.5% of their digital budget but delivers measurable incremental reach against the family and value-shopper audience.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Christie Raymond's team or route through Maria Freda at Horizon Media for a 30-minute planning call in the next two weeks to map a Q3 Back-to-School direct package before Horizon locks budgets.

sales_plays.title: Shift Search Dollars to High-Intent Weather-Contextual Display
sales_plays.summary: Kohl's allocates $222.6M to paid search — by far their largest single digital channel — while Internet Display sits at just $580K. This imbalance signals an over-reliance on bottom-funnel search and an opportunity to displace a slice of that spend toward contextual, weather-triggered display that drives upper-funnel demand for seasonal apparel and home categories.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the spend imbalance — 'Kohl's is putting $222.6M into search and only $580K into display. Search captures demand that already exists. We can help you create that demand earlier, especially for seasonal categories like outerwear, bedding, and back-to-school apparel, by reaching shoppers at the moment weather shifts their intent.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Christie Raymond's team: 'When Kohl's plans for a seasonal push — say, the transition from summer to fall apparel — how far upstream does your media mix start working, and is search doing the heavy lifting all the way through?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's weather-contextual display and online video inventory as a demand-creation layer that activates before shoppers hit Google — targeting families in Kohl's core markets when temperature drops or weather events signal a wardrobe or home goods need.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Reallocating even 1% of Kohl's $222.6M search budget — roughly $2.2M — into weather-contextual display and online video (currently at $2.2M total) would double their upper-funnel digital investment and create measurable lift data to justify further shift. Kohl's $36.6M in paid social shows they invest in audience-based targeting; this extends that logic to contextual moments.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a pilot brief — a $200K–$300K weather-contextual display test tied to the fall apparel transition window — and ask Horizon Media's Maria Freda to include it in the Q3 planning conversation already being set up through Play 1.

talk_track_opener: Hey Christie, I'm reaching out from The Weather Company's interactive team. We've been working with Kohl's since mid-2024 — you have two active PMP deals with us, the most recent renewed in January — so this isn't a cold call. I want to build on that foundation and show you what a more intentional investment looks like heading into your biggest seasonal windows. Here's what caught my attention: Kohl's is spending $222.6M in paid search and $580K in display — that ratio tells me you're very good at capturing demand but there may be a gap in creating it earlier in the funnel. Weather is one of the strongest behavioral triggers for the categories Kohl's owns — outerwear, bedding, back-to-school. I'd love to ask you one question: when your team plans a seasonal push for something like fall apparel or home goods, how early does your media mix start working upstream of search? That answer would shape exactly what I'd bring back to you.
open_questions: The 'WhereFamiliesComeFirst' deal closed at a loss in December 2024 — was that a budget timing issue, a creative readiness gap, or a competing priority we should plan around this cycle?

With $222.6M going into paid search, how does Christie Raymond's team think about the role of upper-funnel media in creating demand before shoppers are already in-market — especially for seasonal categories like outerwear or back-to-school?
Does Horizon Media's Maria Freda control the digital display and online video allocation independently, or does that flow through a separate planning team at Kohl's?
As Kohl's plans for retail tentpole moments like Back-to-School and Holiday 2025, how far in advance is Horizon locking channel budgets — and is there still flexibility in the digital mix for Q3?

Kohl's has strong owned brands like Sonoma Goods For Life and LC Lauren Conrad — is there a separate brand-building budget for those labels, or do they compete for the same performance-focused digital pool?

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars: Weather-Contextual Video vs. Meta Efficiency
sales_plays.summary: Kohler is allocating $30.7M — roughly 82% of their digital budget — to paid social, while online video sits at just $544K. This concentration creates a displacement opportunity: redirect a fraction of Meta/social spend toward weather-contextual video on TWC, where Kohler's home products have clear seasonal relevance and brand-safe, high-attention environments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): 'John, I noticed Kohler is running $30.7M through paid social — that's a significant concentration. I want to have a candid conversation about what a 3–5% reallocation into weather-contextual video could do for reach quality and brand consideration, particularly for your kitchen and bath lines heading into remodel season.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Is PMG currently running any incrementality or brand lift testing against your paid social investment to understand what's driving consideration versus what's just retargeting existing intent?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's weather-triggered video inventory reaches consumers actively planning home projects — a natural fit for Kohler's kitchen, bath, and generator product lines. Unlike paid social, TWC placements are brand-safe, non-skippable in key formats, and contextually aligned to weather events that drive home improvement decisions (storms, cold snaps, spring cleaning).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'A $750K–$1M investment in TWC video — less than 3% of your current paid social budget — would deliver premium, contextually relevant impressions against an audience with demonstrated home improvement intent. That's a testable, low-risk displacement with measurable brand lift upside against your $67.5M total spend base.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to put together a one-page reallocation scenario for PMG's Jon Dupuis showing the audience overlap and incremental reach story. Can I get 20 minutes with you this week to pressure-test the framing before I bring it to the agency?'

talk_track_opener: Hey John, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working with Kohler through PMG on your TWC Interactive PMP buys, most recently closing in January 2025, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a bigger story here than what we've built so far. With $30.7M running through paid social, is John Engberg or PMG running any brand lift or incrementality studies to isolate what's driving consideration versus retargeting existing demand?

Who at Kohler owns the decision to shift budget from paid social into premium publisher direct — is that a joint call between John Engberg and the brand team, or does PMG have discretion within the plan?
Are there specific Kohler product lines — generators, kitchen, bath — where you're seeing seasonal demand spikes that aren't currently being addressed with weather-contextual media?
As PMG plans Q3 and Q4 tentpole campaigns for Kohler, what does the approval timeline look like for adding a new direct publisher relationship to the media mix?

sales_plays.title: CTV & Premium Video Expansion from PMP Base
sales_plays.summary: Kroger is an active PMP customer (two consecutive annual PMP wins with Dentsu) but CTV spend sits at only $3.9M against $52.5M in Search and $34.4M in Digital. There is a clear gap between their programmatic relationship and premium video investment that a direct conversation with Jennifer Greber or Jill Metcalf can unlock.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins — 'We've had the Always-On PMP running through Dentsu since December 2025, and the 2026 PMP just closed in January. I want to make sure we're building on that momentum before Q3 tentpole planning locks in.' Use the Q3 2025 lost opp ($150K) as proof you know where the gaps are.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jill Metcalf at Dentsu: 'With Kroger's CTV budget sitting at $3.9M versus $52.5M in Search, is the current CTV allocation a deliberate weighting decision, or is there room to rebalance toward premium streaming inventory as measurement improves?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's on-property CTV and premium video as the natural next layer above the existing PMP — same audience data, higher-impact format, directly addressable to Kroger shoppers planning grocery and pharmacy trips around seasonal moments (summer grilling, back-to-school, holiday).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Kroger's total trailing-12 media spend is $168.4M. CTV is only 2.3% of that mix. Even a modest rebalance — moving 2 points of the $34.4M digital budget into CTV — represents a $6.9M incremental opportunity. Frame it as closing the gap the Q3 2025 loss left open.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jill Metcalf for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks specifically to map Kroger's Q3 and Q4 tentpole calendar against TWC's premium CTV inventory windows — before Dentsu's upfront planning cycle closes.

sales_plays.title: Displace Broadcast Dollars with Addressable Weather-Triggered Digital
sales_plays.summary: Kroger spends $48.0M in Broadcast — the single largest line in their media mix — while Internet Display sits at only $1.3M and Online Video at $2.0M. This imbalance signals legacy channel dependency. TWC can make a data-driven case to shift a portion of broadcast into addressable, weather-triggered digital that maps directly to Kroger's perishable and seasonal product categories.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a spend-pattern observation to Jennifer Greber: 'Kroger is running $48M in Broadcast against $1.3M in Display and $2M in Online Video. That ratio is unusual for a retailer your size in 2025 — I'd love to understand what's driving the broadcast commitment and whether there's appetite to test addressable alternatives.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When Kroger plans campaigns for weather-sensitive categories — produce, grilling, cold & flu, ice cream — is the current broadcast buy able to dynamically shift creative or targeting based on local weather conditions, or is that a gap in the current setup?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's weather-triggered digital inventory lets Kroger serve category-specific creative (e.g., soup and cold remedies when temps drop, grilling and produce when heat rises) at the local market level — something a national broadcast buy cannot do. This directly addresses the two lost opps: Q3 2025 ($150K) and Pharmacy/Immunization Q2 2025 ($100K), both of which have clear weather-seasonality ties.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Shifting just 5% of the $48M broadcast budget ($2.4M) into weather-triggered digital would represent a 185% increase over Kroger's current combined Display + Online Video spend ($3.3M). Frame the pilot as a measurable test against a single region or category before the Q3 summer season.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a brief creative-and-data alignment call with both Jennifer Greber and Jill Metcalf at Dentsu within the next three weeks — specifically to map two or three Kroger product categories to TWC weather triggers and build a Q3 pilot brief.

talk_track_opener: Hey Jennifer — I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Kroger through Dentsu on the Always-On PMP since late 2025, and the 2026 PMP just closed in January, so we have a real foundation to build on. I want to make sure that relationship is actually working as hard as it can for your team going into the back half of the year. Here's what caught my attention: Kroger is running $48 million in Broadcast and $52.5 million in Search, but CTV is at $3.9M and Display is at $1.3M — that's a pretty significant gap in the addressable, premium digital layer. And I noticed we had two opportunities in 2025 — Q3 and the Pharmacy/Immunization push — that didn't close. I'd love to understand what drove those decisions and whether there's a smarter way to structure the next conversation around your Q3 and Q4 tentpole planning. Would you or Jill Metcalf at Dentsu have 30 minutes in the next couple of weeks to dig into that?
open_questions: The Q3 2025 and Pharmacy/Immunization opportunities both closed as lost — can you help me understand what drove those decisions so we can structure the 2026 conversation differently?

With Dentsu holding AOR scope for both media planning and buying, does Jennifer Greber's team set category-level budget guardrails before Jill Metcalf's team allocates to channels, or does the agency have discretion to shift spend across formats?
Kroger's CTV investment is currently $3.9M — is that a deliberate cap tied to measurement standards, or is it a function of how the upfront commitments were structured with other partners?

For weather-sensitive categories like produce, grilling, cold remedies, and pharmacy, does the current broadcast buy have any dynamic creative or geo-targeting capability, or is it a fixed national schedule?

As Kroger plans retail tentpole moments — summer grilling, back-to-school, holiday — at what point in the year does Dentsu lock channel allocations, and who needs to be in the room for TWC to be included in that plan?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Direct Expansion
sales_plays.summary: LG has renewed the TWC Interactive PMP three consecutive years (2024, 2025, 2026). The $1 deal values signal a programmatic/PMP entry point — not a reflection of true investment capacity. With $140.4M in digital spend and $4.9M already in CTV, there is clear headroom to move LG from a low-commitment PMP line into premium direct and data-driven placements.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three-year PMP renewal streak with Jeremy Strege at BUNTIN: 'Jeremy, LG has activated the TWC Interactive PMP in 2024, 2025, and now 2026 — that's a consistent signal of trust in the inventory. I want to show you what a direct, audience-guaranteed buy alongside that PMP could unlock for Louis's team heading into the back-half retail window.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jeremy: 'Of LG's $4.9M CTV budget, how much is currently allocated to guaranteed premium placements versus open or PMP auction — and is BUNTIN evaluating a shift toward more direct deals for the appliance and TV lines in H2?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's premium direct inventory as the complement to the existing PMP: weather-triggered, contextually relevant placements for LG's appliance portfolio (washers, dryers, refrigerators) tied to seasonal demand moments like spring cleaning, back-to-school, and holiday gifting — categories where LG's $44.6M paid social and $17.1M display spend already signal heavy investment.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the lift: LG's trailing 12-month digital spend is $140.4M. Even a 1% reallocation from the broader digital mix into a premium direct CTV/weather-contextual package represents ~$1.4M in incremental investment — a meaningful step up from the current $1 PMP commitment and directly additive to the $4.9M CTV base.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jeremy for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar before end of month to walk through a tiered proposal — PMP as the foundation, premium direct as the growth layer — so you have something concrete to bring to Louis's team for H2 planning?'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars with CTV + Display
sales_plays.summary: LG allocates $44.6M to paid social — the single largest line item within its $140.4M digital mix. As social CPMs rise and measurement scrutiny increases, there is a displacement opportunity to redirect a portion of that budget toward premium CTV and contextual display where TWC Interactive can offer audience reach, brand-safe environments, and weather-moment targeting that social platforms cannot replicate.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a spend observation to Jeremy Strege: 'We track LG's media mix closely — paid social is running at $44.6M, which is nearly 32% of total digital. We're seeing a number of CE and appliance brands pull back on social allocation this year as CPMs climb and attribution gets murkier. I'd like to share what we're seeing and how TWC Interactive is capturing that reallocation.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Is Louis Giagrande's team currently evaluating the efficiency of the paid social spend against CTV and premium display — and is BUNTIN running any cross-channel attribution analysis that compares social delivery to contextual or weather-triggered placements for the appliance lines?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive as the brand-safe, high-intent alternative: weather-contextual display and CTV placements that reach consumers actively planning appliance and home electronics purchases — a targeting signal that social platforms cannot provide. LG's AI-powered and energy-efficient appliance messaging aligns directly with weather-driven need states (e.g., HVAC-adjacent refrigerator and washer demand in summer heat).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 10% shift from LG's $44.6M paid social budget equals $4.46M — nearly matching the entire current $4.9M CTV investment. Redirecting even half that increment ($2.2M) into TWC Interactive CTV and contextual display would more than double LG's current footprint with us while reducing social concentration risk.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive analysis deliverable: 'Let me put together a one-page reach and efficiency comparison — social CPM benchmarks versus TWC Interactive CTV and display for the appliance and TV categories — and share it with you and Jeremy before the end of this quarter so it's in hand for H2 budget conversations with Louis's team.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Jeremy, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company Interactive. We've been working with LG's team for three consecutive years now — the TWC Interactive PMP has renewed in 2024, 2025, and again in 2026, so I appreciate the continued trust. We work with a number of CE and appliance brands at your scale and we're seeing some real shifts in how they're structuring their digital mix heading into the back half of the year. I was looking at LG's media footprint and noticed that paid social is sitting at $44.6M — the largest single line in a $140.4M digital budget — while the CTV investment is at $4.9M and the PMP with us is still at an entry-level commitment. Given that Louis's team is clearly investing heavily across channels, I wanted to ask: is BUNTIN currently evaluating whether the social allocation is delivering the same efficiency as premium contextual or CTV placements for the appliance and TV lines — and is there a planning conversation happening now for H2 where it would make sense to bring a tiered TWC proposal to the table?
open_questions: Jeremy, of LG's $4.9M CTV budget, how much is flowing through guaranteed direct deals versus open marketplace or PMP — and is BUNTIN evaluating a shift toward more direct placements for the appliance and TV lines in H2?

When the Q1 2024 impression management proposals for LG's washer and television lines didn't move forward, was that a budget timing issue or a targeting/channel fit concern — and has that changed for 2025 planning?
Is Louis Giagrande's team setting separate KPIs for the appliance lines versus the TV and monitor lines, or is media planning unified across LG's full product portfolio?

How is BUNTIN currently measuring the efficiency of LG's $44.6M paid social spend against CTV and contextual display — and is there an attribution model in place that would support a reallocation conversation?

With retail tentpoles like Labor Day and Black Friday likely in active planning, what is the timeline for H2 channel mix decisions — and is there a budget review meeting coming up where a tiered TWC Interactive proposal would be timely?

sales_plays.summary: LVMH has two consecutive Won PMP deals (Jan 2025 and July 2024) and $4.2M in CTV plus $97.9M in digital spend. The summer heat window is approaching — get in front of Beth Haubenstock and Publicis/Digitas before Q2 planning locks to upgrade from PMP to a premium direct buy with weather-triggered creative activation.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Beth Haubenstock at Louis Vuitton and copy Jack Bedwani at Publicis referencing the two consecutive Won PMP deals (July 2024, January 2025) and note that summer heat search intent typically spikes in late May — position this as a proactive conversation before their Q2/Q3 budgets finalize. Subject line: 'Building on your 2024–2025 TWC PMP wins — summer timing opportunity.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Beth and the Publicis/Digitas team: 'Given that your current TWC PMP investments have run twice consecutively, what KPIs are you optimizing toward this summer — is the goal upper-funnel brand awareness for fashion and leather goods, or are you pushing harder into conversion for a specific brand like Louis Vuitton or Dior?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a weather-triggered direct buy on TWC O&O inventory: LVMH's fashion and fragrance brands (Louis Vuitton, Dior, Givenchy) have natural demand correlation with summer heat — consumers searching for warm-weather style, travel accessories, and gifting. A direct deal unlocks premium placements, guaranteed impressions, and real-time weather-triggered creative that a PMP cannot guarantee.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): LVMH is already deploying $4.2M in CTV and $87.9M in Paid Social. Even a modest reallocation of 1–2% of the Paid Social budget ($880K–$1.76M) into a weather-triggered TWC direct buy would represent a 880x–1,760x increase over the current $1 PMP deal value and deliver incremental reach against high-intent, in-market audiences that social platforms cannot provide.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute working session with Beth Haubenstock and the Digitas/Publicis planning leads before May 15 to walk through TWC's summer heat audience data and a draft direct deal structure — frame it as a planning input, not a pitch.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dominance with TWC Premium Video
sales_plays.summary: Paid Social represents $87.9M of LVMH's $97.9M digital budget — a 90% concentration that creates brand safety, saturation, and measurement risk for a luxury conglomerate. With Bombora showing active Campaigns intent this week, now is the moment to position TWC premium video and CTV as a brand-safe, high-attention alternative that displaces a slice of that social spend.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a data-backed observation to Jack Bedwani at Publicis: 'We're seeing LVMH showing active Campaigns intent on Bombora this week, and with $87.9M — roughly 90% of LVMH's digital budget — concentrated in Paid Social, we want to bring you a brand safety and reach diversification conversation before summer planning closes.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jack Bedwani: 'With luxury brands like Louis Vuitton and Dior, how is Publicis currently evaluating brand safety and contextual alignment on Meta and TikTok — and is there pressure from LVMH brand teams to diversify into more controlled, premium environments?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's premium video and CTV inventory ($4.2M already in LVMH's mix) offers brand-safe, contextually relevant placements alongside weather and lifestyle content — a natural fit for luxury fashion, fragrance, and travel accessories. Unlike social platforms, TWC provides transparent placement, no UGC adjacency risk, and weather-triggered relevance that aligns LVMH creative to real consumer moments.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): LVMH's current CTV investment is $4.2M against $87.9M in Paid Social. Industry benchmarks suggest luxury brands allocate 15–20% of digital to premium video/CTV for optimal brand equity. Closing that gap from the current ~5% to even 10% would mean moving ~$4–5M from Paid Social into TWC premium video — a displacement that Publicis can frame internally as a brand safety and reach quality upgrade.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send Jack Bedwani a one-page TWC brand safety and luxury vertical case study by end of week, then schedule a 20-minute call with the Digitas planning team to align on a Q3 test-and-learn budget before the summer heat window opens in late May.

talk_track_opener: Hey Beth — I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've had the pleasure of working with the LVMH team twice now, with your TWC Interactive PMP deals closing in July 2024 and again in January 2025, so I wanted to reach out proactively rather than reactively. We have a real relationship here and I want to make sure we're adding value ahead of your summer planning cycle, not showing up after budgets are locked. Here's what caught my attention: Bombora is showing LVMH actively researching Campaigns right now, and with summer heat search intent typically accelerating in late May, your fashion, leather goods, and fragrance brands have a natural window to reach high-intent consumers in a contextually relevant environment. Given that your digital mix is heavily weighted toward Paid Social — $87.9M out of $97.9M — I'd love to spend 30 minutes with you and the Publicis or Digitas team to explore whether a weather-triggered direct buy or a premium video expansion could complement what you're already doing. What does your planning calendar look like before mid-May?

With Publicis North America and Digitas both holding AOR media planning and buying scope, who owns the final budget allocation decision between programmatic and direct buys — is that Jack Bedwani's team or does it go back to Beth Haubenstock at the brand level?

Given that $87.9M of LVMH's digital spend is in Paid Social, is Publicis or the LVMH brand team actively evaluating brand safety or reach quality concerns on Meta or TikTok that could open the door to premium video diversification?

Are there specific LVMH brand families — Louis Vuitton, Dior, Hennessy, Sephora — that have separate media budgets and planning cycles, or is the TWC relationship managed at the group level through Publicis?

sales_plays.title: Reclaim the Lost Lazy Day Budget

sales_plays.title: Shift Digital Dollars On-Property
sales_plays.summary: Lactalis is running $15.4M in digital spend, with $4.0M in Paid Social and $2.8M in Display — significant budget flowing through off-property, third-party environments. This play challenges the efficiency of that allocation and positions a direct, on-property solution as a higher-signal alternative.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Megan Malagoli Patterson referencing Lactalis's $15.4M digital footprint and note that a meaningful share — including $2.8M in Display and $4.0M in Paid Social — is running in environments where audience signal and brand safety controls are limited. Ask for 20 minutes to share how comparable CPG dairy brands have reallocated similar budgets on-property for stronger attribution.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Megan, of the $15.4M in digital, how much of your Display and Social spend is tied to measurable outcomes like in-store lift or purchase intent — versus reach and frequency goals that are harder to close the loop on?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position on-property inventory as a direct replacement for the $2.8M Display line — same reach objective, but with first-party audience data, contextual alignment to food and grocery content, and closed-loop measurement that Paid Social and programmatic Display cannot match for a dairy brand like Président or Galbani.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If even 15% of the $15.4M digital budget ($2.3M) shifts on-property, frame the incremental efficiency gain: reduced waste from third-party cookie deprecation, stronger CPG purchase-intent targeting, and measurable lift versus the current $436K Online Video and $664K Mobile App lines that are underweighted relative to the overall mix.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Megan for a 20-minute discovery call before the next Zenith Media planning cycle — specifically to map which digital line items are up for reallocation in the next budget period. Suggest a date 2 weeks out.

sales_plays.title: Zenith Relationship Route via Neil Vendetti
sales_plays.summary: Zenith Media holds full planning and buying scope for Lactalis American Group. Neil Vendetti, President of Investment at Zenith, is the agency-side power contact. This play routes through the agency relationship to earn a warm introduction to Megan Malagoli Patterson and position the solution before the next planning cycle.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Neil Vendetti at Zenith Media directly — reference Zenith's planning and buying scope for Lactalis and note that you've been tracking the account's $27.9M total spend profile. Ask Neil if Lactalis is in active planning for any tentpole retail or cultural-moment campaigns (e.g., holiday, back-to-school, summer grilling) where incremental on-property digital inventory could complement the current Broadcast ($8.5M) and CTV ($3.9M) investment.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Neil, for Lactalis's digital mix — particularly the $4.0M in Paid Social and $2.8M in Display — is Zenith evaluating any on-property or publisher-direct options for the next planning period, or is the current allocation locked into programmatic channels?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position the ask as additive to Zenith's existing plan: on-property inventory that complements the $3.9M CTV buy with digital touchpoints in contextually relevant food and grocery environments — strengthening the full-funnel story Zenith is already building for Lactalis brands like Président, Galbani, and siggi's.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the agency value: bringing Neil a differentiated on-property option for Lactalis's $15.4M digital budget gives Zenith a performance story to tell the client — especially as third-party cookie deprecation pressures the $2.8M Display line and Paid Social CPMs continue to rise against the $4.0M allocation.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Neil for a 30-minute joint call with himself and Megan Malagoli Patterson to walk through a custom audience and inventory proposal tied to Lactalis's next seasonal planning window. Offer to prepare a one-page brief tailored to the Président or Galbani brand before the call.

talk_track_opener: Hey Megan, I'm reaching out because we've been looking closely at how leading CPG dairy brands are navigating the shift away from programmatic Display and third-party Social environments — and Lactalis's digital mix caught our attention. With $15.4M in digital spend and a strong Broadcast and CTV foundation already in place, there's a real opportunity to tighten the full-funnel story. We work with several brands in the dairy and specialty food space who've moved budget from open-web Display into on-property environments and seen measurable improvement in purchase-intent lift and attribution clarity. I'd love to understand how you're currently thinking about the performance of your $2.8M Display and $4.0M Paid Social lines — specifically, are those tied to closed-loop measurement today, or are they primarily reach and frequency vehicles? Even 20 minutes would help me understand whether what we're seeing with comparable CPG accounts is relevant to what you're planning next.
open_questions: Megan, of the $2.8M currently running in Display, how much of that is managed programmatically through Zenith versus direct publisher relationships — and are you seeing the attribution clarity you need from those buys?
Who at Lactalis owns the final sign-off on shifting digital budget between channels — is that a decision you make in partnership with Zenith, or does it go up to brand leadership above you?

With Président and Galbani targeting different consumer profiles, are you running unified digital campaigns across brands or managing separate budgets and KPIs for each — and how does that affect how Zenith plans the $15.4M digital allocation?

As third-party cookie deprecation accelerates, has Lactalis or Zenith begun evaluating first-party data partnerships or on-property inventory as a hedge against the $4.0M Paid Social and $2.8M Display lines?

Are there specific retail tentpole moments — holiday entertaining, summer grilling, back-to-school — where Lactalis is actively looking to increase digital investment, and is Zenith already in planning mode for those windows?

sales_plays.title: PMP to Premium Direct Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Lamps Plus has won two consecutive PMP deals (2024 and 2025) with TWC Interactive, signaling an established relationship and budget comfort. This play upgrades them from PMP into higher-impact, direct premium inventory to capture more of their $21.7M total media spend.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins with Mary Jensen: 'Lamps Plus has now run with us through two PMP cycles — 2024 and 2025 — and we're seeing strong renewal behavior. I want to show you what a step up into direct premium placements could unlock that PMP inventory can't guarantee.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Mary, of the $21.7M you're deploying across digital channels, how much is currently allocated to guaranteed, brand-safe premium environments versus open or PMP auction — and is that split intentional or a function of what's been available?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Lamps Plus's $832K in display and $439K in online video suggest they are underweighting high-impact video and contextual display relative to their $17.2M search spend. Position TWC Interactive's direct premium inventory as the brand-building complement to their performance-heavy search budget — reaching in-market home décor shoppers in a weather-contextual environment that aligns with lighting purchase triggers.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Your current PMP commitment is nominal in contract value but real in audience delivery. If we converted even 5% of your $4.5M digital budget into direct premium placements, that's $225K in guaranteed, viewable, contextually relevant impressions — with measurement we can tie back to your online video and display KPIs.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar before end of quarter — with you and whoever owns your digital display strategy — to walk through a premium package proposal built around your spring home refresh window?'

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Budget Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Lamps Plus is spending $1.6M in Paid Social — a channel with rising CPMs and limited contextual relevance for a lighting retailer. This play positions TWC Interactive's premium contextual and weather-driven inventory as a smarter alternative for the same audience at better efficiency.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a spend observation to Adrienne Cadena at Havas Street: 'Adrienne, we've been looking at Lamps Plus's channel mix — $1.6M in Paid Social is a meaningful line item. We're seeing a number of home and lighting retailers pull back from social CPMs and reallocate into contextual environments where purchase intent is more explicit. I'd love to share what that's looked like for comparable accounts.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'What performance benchmarks is Havas Street using to evaluate the Paid Social spend for Lamps Plus — is it ROAS, reach, or something else — and how is it performing against those targets right now?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's weather-contextual inventory as a displacement candidate for underperforming Paid Social dollars. Lighting purchases — indoor ambiance, outdoor fixtures — are meaningfully tied to seasonal and weather moments. A consumer checking weather before a weekend home project is a higher-intent target than a social scroll audience. TWC's environment delivers that context at scale.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'If we proposed a $400K test — roughly 25% of the current Paid Social budget — against TWC's contextual home and lifestyle inventory, we could run a side-by-side attribution comparison against your existing social benchmarks within a single quarter. That's a contained, measurable displacement test with no disruption to the broader $21.7M plan.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Adrienne, would it make sense to loop in Mary Jensen for a brief three-way call in the next two weeks? I can bring a one-page displacement rationale with comparable vertical data so it's a concrete conversation, not a pitch.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Mary, I'm reaching out from The Weather Company — we've been working with Lamps Plus now through two consecutive PMP cycles, most recently closed in January 2025, and I wanted to connect directly with you as Chief Growth Officer because I think there's a bigger opportunity we haven't fully explored together yet. We've earned some trust with your team, and I want to make sure we're putting that to work at the right scale.
open_questions: How are you currently evaluating the performance of your $1.6M Paid Social investment — and is that budget under review heading into the next planning cycle?
Who at Lamps Plus or Havas Street owns the decision to shift budget between digital channels like display, video, and social — is that Mary's call or a joint client-agency decision?
The two PMP deals have been nominal in contract value — is there a threshold or proof point that would unlock a larger direct or guaranteed buy with TWC?
With $17.2M concentrated in search, is there a strategic initiative underway to build more upper-funnel brand awareness, or is performance efficiency still the primary mandate?
Are there specific retail tentpole moments — spring home refresh, holiday lighting, outdoor summer — where Lamps Plus is actively looking to increase share of voice beyond search?

sales_plays.title: From PMP to Premium: Scale Into Weather-Contextual Direct
sales_plays.summary: Lands' End has two consecutive won PMP deals (2024 and 2025) totaling nominal value, while running $56.2M in total media spend with $22.2M in digital. The opportunity is to convert the existing PMP relationship into a higher-value direct buy that captures weather-contextual moments critical to an apparel brand.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins — 'Sarah, we've had active deals with Lands' End in both July 2024 and January 2025, which tells me your team sees value in our environment. I want to show you what a direct, premium buy looks like versus the PMP path you've been on.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Of your $22.2M in digital spend, how much is currently allocated to contextually triggered placements — specifically environments where weather or seasonal conditions are part of the targeting logic?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): 'Lands' End's core catalog and e-commerce business is driven by seasonal transitions — cold snaps, back-to-school, holiday. A direct buy on TWC gives you guaranteed premium inventory tied to those exact weather moments, not just programmatic remnant access through the PMP.'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Your current TWC revenue with us is $82 against a $56.2M total spend footprint — that's less than 0.2% of wallet. Even a modest reallocation of 1% of your $22.2M digital budget would represent a 27x increase in investment with us, with full contextual control.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we set a 30-minute session before end of Q2 with you and Wanda Pogue at VaynerMedia to walk through a weather-contextual media plan for Lands' End's fall transition window — no commitment, just a plan to react to?'

sales_plays.title: Displace Display Waste: Redirect Internet Display Budget to Weather-Triggered Inventory
sales_plays.summary: Lands' End is spending $2.8M on Internet Display — a channel with well-documented viewability and brand safety challenges. This play targets that specific budget line as a displacement opportunity, positioning TWC's premium contextual inventory as a measurably better alternative for an apparel brand dependent on seasonal demand.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): 'Wanda, I've been looking at Lands' End's media mix — $2.8M sitting in Internet Display. For an apparel brand where purchase intent spikes with weather events, that budget is working harder in some environments than others. I want to show you where it's likely underperforming.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'What viewability benchmarks and brand safety standards is VaynerMedia holding the current display partners to, and are those placements tied to any contextual or seasonal trigger logic?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): 'TWC's owned-and-operated inventory delivers display in a high-attention, weather-relevant context — when a consumer in the Midwest checks a cold front forecast, a Lands' End outerwear unit is the logical next impression. That's a displacement of generic display spend into a moment-matched environment.'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'If even $500K of the $2.8M Internet Display budget shifted to TWC weather-contextual display, you're reaching consumers at the exact moment seasonal apparel need is top of mind — a targeting signal no standard display exchange can replicate. That's a direct line to Lands' End's catalog-driven purchase cycle.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Let me send you a one-page competitive analysis of TWC display versus standard exchange display on attention and contextual relevance metrics by Friday — then we schedule 20 minutes with Sarah Sylvester's team to pressure-test it against Lands' End's fall planning calendar.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Sarah, I'm reaching out from The Weather Company — we've had active PMP placements running with Lands' End in both mid-2024 and early 2025, so this isn't a cold call. Your team has already validated that our environment works for the brand, and I want to build on that foundation rather than just renew what's in place. I work with apparel and retail brands specifically on how to use weather context as a demand signal, not just a targeting layer. With Lands' End's catalog and e-commerce model, seasonal transitions are your revenue engine — and I think we're underleveraged in how we support that. You're running $22.2M in digital and $2.8M in Internet Display — I'd love to ask you one question: how much of that spend is currently tied to any kind of weather or seasonal trigger logic, versus just demographic or behavioral targeting? That answer tells me a lot about where we can add the most value.
open_questions: The two PMP buys in 2024 and 2025 were both modest in scale — what would need to be true for Lands' End to move from a test-level PMP to a committed direct buy on TWC inventory?

Who at Lands' End or VaynerMedia owns the decision to reallocate budget from Internet Display to a new channel partner — is that Sarah Sylvester's call, or does Wanda Pogue's team drive that recommendation?
How is Lands' End currently using weather or seasonal signals in its digital targeting strategy, and is that logic built into the VaynerMedia planning process or handled separately?
With fall and holiday being critical windows for Lands' End's catalog business, at what point in the year does the media plan for Q4 get locked — and are we in that planning window now?

Lands' End runs $18.2M in Paid Social alongside $2.8M in Display — is the Display budget under pressure to justify its performance, or is it considered a stable part of the mix heading into the next planning cycle?

sales_plays.summary: Lane Bryant is an active PMP customer with two consecutive won deals but total SFDC revenue of only $153, signaling the relationship is real but severely underdeveloped relative to their $4.3M digital footprint. This play expands the conversation from a narrow PMP line item into a broader audience and format partnership.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Sara Tervo and CC Sky Vargas at Modco Media referencing the two consecutive PMP wins (2024 and 2025) and note that Lane Bryant's $4.2M Paid Social concentration creates meaningful reach risk — one platform algorithm change can disrupt the entire funnel. Open with: 'You've trusted us with two PMP cycles — I want to show you what a fuller partnership could unlock for the same audience.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Sara: 'Outside of Paid Social, where are you currently reaching plus-size shoppers who are actively in a purchase mindset but haven't yet engaged with a Lane Bryant ad?' — this surfaces the mid-funnel gap our weather-audience data can fill.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position Weather Channel's contextual audience targeting against Lane Bryant's Paid Social dependency. Shoppers checking weather before a shopping trip or seasonal wardrobe refresh are in a high-intent, low-competition moment — exactly where $24K in Display and $6K in Online Video today is leaving reach on the table.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Lane Bryant's current TWC investment is under $200 against a $4.3M total digital budget — less than 0.005%. A modest reallocation of even 2% of Paid Social spend (~$84K) into TWC display and video would represent a 400x increase in TWC investment and diversify reach across a proven, brand-safe environment.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute working session with Sara and Sky Vargas in the next two weeks to walk through a custom audience overlap analysis between Lane Bryant's current Paid Social targeting and TWC's plus-size fashion shopper segments. Offer to bring the data pre-built.

sales_plays.title: Retail Tentpole Planning: Get on the Calendar Before Q4 Budgets Lock
sales_plays.summary: Lane Bryant's Bombora intent signal around Entertainment this week, combined with retail vertical seasonality, suggests their team is in active planning mode for cultural-moment campaigns. This play positions TWC as a Q3/Q4 tentpole partner before agency budgets are finalized at Modco Media.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Sky Vargas at Modco Media directly, referencing the active PMP relationship and noting that Lane Bryant's Entertainment intent signal this week suggests planning activity is underway. Frame the outreach as a proactive planning conversation, not a pitch: 'We're seeing Lane Bryant in active research mode — wanted to get in front of you before Q4 plans are locked.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Sky: 'Which retail tentpoles — back-to-school, fall fashion, holiday gifting — are Lane Bryant's highest-priority cultural moments this year, and is media planning for those already in flight at Modco?' This identifies the specific windows where TWC can be inserted.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Connect TWC's seasonal weather content to Lane Bryant's core customer behavior — plus-size shoppers making wardrobe decisions tied to temperature shifts, fall layering, and holiday dressing. Weather is a natural trigger for apparel consideration, and TWC's contextual environment aligns with those exact moments.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Lane Bryant currently has $6K in Online Video on TWC — essentially a test. A tentpole-specific video campaign timed to a single cultural moment (e.g., fall fashion week or holiday) at even a $50K investment would represent an 8x increase and give Modco a measurable case study to bring back to Sara Tervo.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a brief agency planning call with Sky Vargas within the next 10 days — offer to bring a seasonal audience calendar showing when Lane Bryant's target customer is most active on TWC platforms, tied to specific fall and holiday weather patterns. Keep the ask light: 'Just 20 minutes to align on timing before budgets close.'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dominance: Shift $22.1M Toward Premium Video & CTV
sales_plays.summary: Paid Social commands $22.1M of Lavazza's digital mix—nearly 70% of the $31.9M digital budget—while CTV sits at only $2.6M. This play targets Carlo Colpo directly to challenge the over-reliance on social and position TWC's CTV and premium video inventory as a brand-safe, measurable alternative for a premium global coffee brand.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Carlo Colpo, CMO, with a brand-safety and premium positioning angle: 'Lavazza is one of the world's most recognized premium coffee brands—yet $22.1M of your digital investment is concentrated in Paid Social. I'd like to share how brands at your tier are rebalancing toward CTV and premium video to protect brand equity and improve measurable reach.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Carlo: 'Is the current $2.6M CTV allocation a deliberate ceiling, or is there a case being built internally to shift a portion of the $22.1M Paid Social budget toward higher-funnel, brand-safe environments ahead of a major retail moment like the holiday season?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's CTV and premium video inventory as the displacement vehicle: TWC reaches consumers in a lean-back, high-attention environment tied to weather and lifestyle context—directly relevant to Lavazza's hot beverage consumption occasions—without the brand-safety and measurement fragmentation risks of social platforms.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Lavazza's CTV spend of $2.6M against a $56.7M total budget is roughly 4.6%—well below CPG category benchmarks of 10-15% for premium brands. Closing even half that gap would represent a $3M–$5.9M CTV expansion opportunity, with TWC positioned to capture a meaningful share given the existing PMP relationship.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a brand equity and reach audit: 'I can put together a 2-page CTV reach analysis showing where Lavazza's $2.6M is leaving impressions on the table versus a rebalanced $5M+ CTV allocation. Can I send that to you and loop in Ania at Wavemaker for a joint review before August planning locks?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Ania, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel—we've worked together on two consecutive PMP deals for Lavazza, most recently closing in February 2025, so I wanted to come back to you with something more strategic ahead of the back half of the year. Lavazza is running $56.7M in total media, and we've earned a seat at the table through those PMP wins—I think there's a real conversation to have about what a direct buy or a CTV expansion could unlock that the current setup doesn't. I've been looking at Lavazza's digital mix—$31.9M in digital with $22.1M concentrated in Paid Social and only $2.6M in CTV—and for a premium global coffee brand, that balance feels like it's leaving brand-safe, high-attention inventory on the table. Weather is one of the single biggest drivers of hot and cold beverage consumption, and we have first-party data that maps directly to Lavazza's portfolio. I'd love 30 minutes to show you a weather-indexed consumption overlay for the coffee category and a direct buy proposal timed to Q4 tentpoles—would the week of July 28th work for a quick call?
open_questions: Of Lavazza's two consecutive TWC Interactive PMP buys, what performance metrics has Wavemaker used to evaluate them—and have those benchmarks informed any conversation about moving to a direct buy?
Who at Lavazza—Carlo Colpo as CMO or someone on his team—owns the decision to shift budget between Paid Social and CTV, and does that require a separate brief from what Wavemaker manages?
Is the $2.6M CTV allocation a hard cap set by Lavazza's global team, or is there flexibility at the regional or campaign level that Wavemaker can influence for Q4?
With $22.1M in Paid Social, is Lavazza actively measuring brand equity lift from that channel, or is it primarily a performance and conversion play—and has there been any internal push to diversify?

As Wavemaker plans Lavazza's Q4 tentpole calendar, are there specific cultural or seasonal moments—holiday gifting, New Year, Valentine's Day—where Lavazza has historically underperformed and is looking to invest more heavily?

steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins — 'Renee, Lisa — we've now run two successful PMP deals together, July 2024 and January 2025. Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's direct premium inventory — weather-triggered CTV, homepage takeovers, and editorial integrations — as the layer above the PMP that Publicis's programmatic desk cannot access, giving Lego Masterbrand brand-safe, high-attention environments tied to the cultural and seasonal moments (holiday gifting, back-to-school, spring play) that drive Lego's retail calendar.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar in the next two weeks — you, me, and Lisa Torres at Publicis — to walk through two or three specific premium formats that aren't available through the PMP and map them to Lego's Q3/Q4 tentpole windows?'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Spend with On-Property Video
sales_plays.summary: Lego Systems is allocating $73.8M to paid social and only $5.3M to online video — a significant imbalance for a visually-driven CPG brand. This play targets Renee Guida and the Publicis team to redirect a portion of paid social budget toward TWC Interactive's on-property video and CTV environments, where brand-safe, contextually relevant placements can outperform social feed inventory for Masterbrand awareness goals.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the spend imbalance as a conversation starter: 'Renee, I was looking at Lego's media mix and noticed $73.8M is going into paid social versus $5.3M in online video. For a brand where the product is inherently visual and play-based, that ratio stood out to me — I'd love to understand the strategic logic behind it and whether there's pressure on the social side given recent platform brand-safety conversations.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Is the paid social allocation driven by Publicis's programmatic efficiency targets, or is Masterbrand actively choosing social as the primary awareness vehicle — and how are you measuring brand lift versus direct response on that $73.8M?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's on-property video and weather-contextual CTV as a brand-safe alternative to social feed placements — offering Lego Masterbrand premium editorial context (family, home, lifestyle content), verified viewability, and audience targeting without the platform risk or algorithmic unpredictability of paid social environments.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'If we shifted even 5% of the $73.8M paid social budget — roughly $3.7M — into TWC Interactive on-property video and CTV, we could build a meaningful test against your existing $25.4M CTV baseline. That gives Renee a direct brand lift comparison between social and premium contextual video heading into the holiday gifting window, where Lego's retail stakes are highest.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to put together a one-page media mix scenario for Lisa Torres at Publicis showing the $3.7M reallocation model with projected reach and viewability benchmarks. Can I send that over by end of week and schedule a 20-minute call with you and Lisa to react to it?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Renee — I'm [rep name] from The Weather Company Interactive. We've been working with your team and the Publicis group for the past year-plus, and we've now closed two PMP deals together, most recently in January 2025. I wanted to reach out directly because I think we're only scratching the surface of what we can do together given the scale of Lego's media investment. Looking at your overall media mix — $222M in total spend, $25.4M in CTV, and $73.8M in paid social — I see a real opportunity to move beyond the PMP layer and put Lego Masterbrand in premium, brand-safe environments that are contextually tied to the moments families are actually planning purchases: weather shifts, holidays, back-to-school. I'd love to ask you one question: when Publicis is allocating that paid social budget, is Masterbrand getting the brand lift data you need to justify it versus a premium video or CTV alternative? That's exactly the conversation I want to have with you and Lisa Torres.
open_questions: The two PMP deals we've run together are booked at nominal value — is that a procurement structure from Publicis, or is Masterbrand intentionally keeping TWC Interactive in a test budget until a specific performance threshold is met?
Who at Publicis North America — beyond Lisa Torres — owns the actual budget allocation decision between paid social, CTV, and online video for Lego's Masterbrand campaigns?

With $73.8M going into paid social, how is Renee's team measuring brand lift versus direct response, and has there been any internal pressure to diversify away from social platforms given recent brand-safety or reach concerns?

Is Lego's Q4 holiday gifting media plan already locked with Publicis for this year, or is there still a planning window open where TWC Interactive could be included in a premium video or CTV scenario?

Are there specific Lego brand lines — City, Technic, licensed properties like Star Wars or Harry Potter — where Masterbrand is running separate media strategies that might have different agency contacts or budget owners than the core Masterbrand plan?

sales_plays.title: Rain Season Displacement: Own the Storm Window
sales_plays.summary: Les Schwab spends $41.8M digitally but has no documented relationship with us. Rain and storm season is a high-confidence timing trigger for tire demand in the Pacific Northwest. This play displaces current programmatic/display spend by positioning our inventory as a more precise, weather-contextual environment ahead of the demand window.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Aileen Hastings-Baxter at HQ referencing Les Schwab's $1.7M Internet Display and $2.3M Online Video allocation — note that broad programmatic buys miss the weather-contextual moment when Pacific Northwest drivers are actively thinking about tire safety. Mention that rain season lead-in (late August through October) is the highest-intent window for tire replacement decisions in OR, WA, and ID.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Aileen: 'When your display and online video dollars run through programmatic, how are you ensuring those impressions are reaching drivers in the days immediately before and during the first major rain events of the season — when purchase intent is highest?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our weather-triggered, contextually relevant digital environments against the $1.7M display and $2.3M online video spend currently running through non-weather-aware programmatic channels. Show how on-property placements tied to storm and rain forecasts deliver Les Schwab's message at the exact moment a driver sees wet roads and thinks about tires.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame a pilot against even 10% of the $1.7M display budget ($170K) in a weather-triggered contextual buy during the August–October window. Contrast that with the waste embedded in broad programmatic at scale — Les Schwab's $41.8M total digital spend means even marginal efficiency gains represent significant recaptured value.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 20-minute call with Aileen and Michael Raymond at Heat before end of July to walk through a weather-window media plan for Q3 — framed as a low-risk pilot displacement of a portion of the existing display line.

sales_plays.title: Heat Agency Referral: Creative-to-Media Bridge
sales_plays.summary: Deloitte Digital's Heat holds the AOR Creative Digital scope for Les Schwab. Michael Raymond as Creative Director is a warm entry point — agency-first outreach can generate an internal referral to Aileen Hastings-Baxter and accelerate trust-building with a net-new account spending $15M in Paid Social and $6.9M in Mobile App.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Michael Raymond at Heat directly, acknowledging his creative leadership on the Les Schwab account. Reference the scale of the digital program ($41.8M) and ask how his team is thinking about connecting Les Schwab's brand creative to high-intent media environments — specifically around the upcoming rain and storm season in the Pacific Northwest.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Michael: 'As you build creative for the fall tire season, is the media plan giving you placements that are contextually matched to weather moments — or is the creative landing in generic programmatic environments that don't reinforce the safety urgency Les Schwab's messaging is built around?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our platform as the media environment that makes Heat's creative work harder — weather-contextual, high-attention placements that align Les Schwab's tire safety messaging with the exact moment consumers feel the need. Tie this to the $15M Paid Social and $6.9M Mobile App spend as lines where creative-media alignment is most visible and measurable.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Note that Les Schwab's combined Paid Social and Mobile App spend of $21.9M represents significant creative distribution investment. Even a 5–10% reallocation toward contextually triggered, on-property inventory during the August–October window could meaningfully improve cost-per-engaged-driver metrics that Heat would be able to show Aileen Hastings-Baxter as a creative performance win.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Michael for a 15-minute informal conversation this month — framed not as a sales call but as a creative-media alignment conversation ahead of fall planning — and request an introduction to Aileen at Les Schwab HQ if the fit looks right.

talk_track_opener: Hey Aileen, my name is [Rep Name] with [Company] — we work with automotive advertisers across the Pacific Northwest to connect their media spend to the moments when drivers are most likely to act. I wanted to reach out specifically about Les Schwab because your scale is impressive — $41.8M in digital, a strong paid social presence, and real investment in mobile and video. That tells me your team takes performance seriously. We work with Heat on a number of accounts and have a lot of respect for what Michael Raymond's team builds creatively for Les Schwab.
open_questions: When your $1.7M display and $2.3M online video budgets run through programmatic, what targeting signals are being used — and is weather or seasonality one of them?
For the fall tire season, does Aileen or the broader Les Schwab marketing team own the media planning decisions, or does Heat at Deloitte Digital drive that strategy with client approval?

Given that Les Schwab's Bombora intent is currently showing interest in Entertainment, is there a broader audience expansion initiative underway that's influencing how you're thinking about digital channel mix for Q3 and Q4?

How does Les Schwab currently measure the performance of its $6.9M Mobile App spend relative to in-store tire service conversions — and is there a cost-per-visit or cost-per-appointment metric that a new media partner would need to hit?
