sales_plays.title: Maria Demaree Direct — Brand Visibility Timing Play
sales_plays.summary: With GALE holding AOR Creative All Media scope, the agency path is the primary route — but Maria Demaree at Lockheed Martin is the named advertiser contact and may influence or own brand-level decisions. This play uses the Q4 government budget cycle and defense industry visibility windows as a timing hook to open a direct conversation about premium brand formats.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Maria Demaree directly, acknowledging the existing TWC relationship and referencing that Lockheed Martin has been a consistent PMP partner since at least mid-2024. Note that Q4 is historically when defense and aerospace brands increase brand visibility spend ahead of government budget cycles and congressional sessions in January — and ask if that pattern holds for Lockheed Martin's planning.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Maria: 'As you plan into Q4 and early 2025, is there a brand awareness or thought leadership objective — particularly around national security or space — that isn't fully served by the current programmatic PMP setup?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's CTV inventory ($240K already in the mix) and high-impact digital formats as a brand-building layer on top of the performance-oriented PMP buys — specifically suited for reaching policy, government, and defense decision-maker audiences who consume weather and news content.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Lockheed Martin already spends $240K in CTV with TWC. A modest 25% expansion of that CTV line — roughly $60K — would bring total TWC investment closer to $3.4M and open the door to a more strategic partnership conversation with both Maria and GALE.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Maria for a 15-minute call in the next two weeks to share how TWC is activating CTV for other defense and government-adjacent advertisers, and to align on whether there's a Q4 brand moment worth building around — keeping GALE looped in as a follow-up step.

talk_track_opener: Hey Juan, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Lockheed Martin through GALE for a couple of years now — the PMP deals in 2024 and again in February 2025 are a great foundation, and I appreciate the partnership you've helped build there. I wanted to reach out because I've been looking at how the broader relationship could grow, and I think there's a real opportunity we haven't fully tapped yet. Lockheed Martin is running $709K in mobile app spend and $307K in online video — formats where TWC has strong, scaled inventory that isn't currently part of the PMP setup. I'm not looking to disrupt what's working, just to see if there's a format or audience gap where we could add value alongside the existing buy. Would you have 20 minutes before end of month to walk through Q3 performance together and see if there's a Q4 pilot worth testing?
open_questions: Juan, of Lockheed Martin's $2.6M digital budget, how much is currently flowing through TWC versus other DSPs or direct publisher relationships — and is there a format where you're not hitting your efficiency targets?
Who at Lockheed Martin — Maria Demaree or someone else on her team — owns the final sign-off on expanding a current vendor relationship versus adding a new one?
Is the current PMP setup with TWC evaluated on a campaign-by-campaign basis, or is there a longer-term preferred partner framework GALE is working within for Lockheed Martin's digital spend?
Lockheed Martin runs $709K in mobile app spend — is that being managed programmatically through a single DSP, or is it split across multiple partners, and is TWC currently part of that mix?

As Lockheed Martin plans Q4 and into 2026, is there a brand visibility or audience reach objective — particularly around defense, space, or national security narratives — that the current PMP buy isn't fully addressing?

sales_plays.title: Scale the PMP Win Into Full Portfolio
sales_plays.summary: Lululemon just signed a PMP deal (Jan 2026) after a string of Q3/Q4 losses. The door is open. Now is the time to expand that foothold into premium direct and contextual inventory before their next planning cycle locks budget.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the January 2026 PMP win directly — 'We're thrilled the TWC Interactive PMP is live. I wanted to connect with you and Chioma at Droga5 while momentum is fresh to talk about what's working and where we can go deeper together.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Nikki Neuburger or Chioma Aduba: 'Now that the PMP is running, which audience segments or dayparts are over-indexing for Lululemon — and are those being served by your current $44.3M paid social or $8.6M display budget, or is there a gap we can fill?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered, contextual inventory as a complement to Lululemon's existing paid social and display spend — reaching the active-lifestyle consumer at the exact moment weather drives purchase intent (cold snaps, spring warm-ups, marathon season).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Lululemon spends $95.6M digitally but only $692K in online video and $1.1M in mobile app. Even a 5% reallocation from paid social ($44.3M) into weather-contextual video and mobile represents ~$2.2M in incremental addressable budget on our platform.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute working session with Chioma Aduba at Droga5 before end of February to review PMP performance data together and map Q2 tentpole moments (spring running season, Earth Day) to available inventory packages.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars With Weather-Contextual Video
sales_plays.summary: Lululemon's $44.3M paid social allocation dwarfs their $692K online video spend. With Meta and TikTok facing signal loss and brand-safety scrutiny, there is a clear argument to shift a portion of that budget toward premium, brand-safe, contextual video environments where TWC can win.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a market observation, not a pitch — 'Nikki, we've seen several athletic and retail brands quietly pulling 10-15% of paid social budget into contextual video this year given signal loss post-iOS and brand-safety concerns on social platforms. Given Lululemon's $44.3M social footprint, I wanted to share what we're seeing and get your read.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When you look at your paid social performance today, where are you seeing the most erosion in targeting precision or brand-context alignment — and is Droga5 actively modeling any reallocation scenarios for 2026?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's premium video and mobile inventory delivers Lululemon's core active-lifestyle audience in a high-attention, brand-safe, weather-relevant context — something paid social cannot replicate. Weather triggers (e.g., first cold snap of fall, spring warm-up) align directly with Lululemon's seasonal product launches and run/train categories.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Lululemon's current online video spend is $692K against a $95.6M digital total — less than 1%. Shifting just 3% of the $44.3M social budget ($1.3M) into TWC video would represent a 188% increase in video investment and meaningful reach diversification with a verified active-lifestyle audience.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to build a custom displacement model for Chioma Aduba at Droga5 showing audience overlap between Lululemon's current social targeting and TWC's weather-contextual segments — deliverable within two weeks, no commitment required.

talk_track_opener: Hey Nikki, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Lululemon for a while now and just closed the TWC Interactive PMP together in January — really excited about where that's heading. We work with a number of premium athletic and retail brands helping them connect with active-lifestyle consumers at the moments weather actually drives their decisions, whether that's gearing up for a cold-weather run, planning a spring training block, or shopping ahead of a seasonal shift. I was looking at Lululemon's media mix and noticed something interesting — you're running $44.3M in paid social and only $692K in online video, which feels like a potential imbalance given how much your audience skews toward high-intent, active moments. I'd love to ask Chioma and your team one question: as you're planning Q2 and beyond, are you actively modeling any reallocation away from social into contextual or premium video environments — and if so, what would make a partner like TWC the right fit for that conversation?
open_questions: Now that the TWC Interactive PMP is live, which audience segments or weather-triggered moments are you most interested in optimizing toward — and is that being tracked against your paid social benchmarks?
Who at Lululemon or Droga5 owns the decision to reallocate budget between paid social and premium video — is that Nikki's team, Chioma's team, or a joint call?
Given the Q3 and Q4 2025 pitches that didn't close, can you share what the primary objection was — was it product fit, timing, pricing, or internal prioritization?

As you plan Q2 2026, are there specific tentpole moments — spring running season, Earth Day, a product launch — where you're actively looking for incremental reach outside of your current social and search channels?
Is Droga5 currently modeling any paid social reallocation scenarios for 2026, and if so, what criteria would a contextual video partner need to meet to be included in that evaluation?

sales_plays.title: Vyepti Brand Family Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Lundbeck has $943K committed to Vyepti 2026 via TWC-Interactive and a $30K Social Data win. With $19.4M in total estimated spend and only ~$1M captured, there is significant whitespace to expand beyond Vyepti into Lundbeck's broader CNS portfolio and premium inventory formats.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Eileen Sweeney referencing the Vyepti 2026 win closed December 9 and the Social Data package secured in May. Open with: 'We're set up well for Vyepti this year — I want to make sure we're also thinking about the rest of the Lundbeck portfolio before Q2 planning locks in.'
Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Eileen: 'Beyond Vyepti, which other Lundbeck brands are in active media planning right now, and are those budgets sitting with Ogilvy Health or a separate AOR?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC-Interactive's neurology and psychiatry audience targeting — weather-sensitive mood and migraine triggers, HCP-adjacent reach — as uniquely suited to Lundbeck's brain-disease-only portfolio, not just a single brand.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Lundbeck's trailing 12-month estimated spend is $19.4M. TWC-Interactive has captured roughly $1M of that. Framing: 'We're delivering proven results on Vyepti at $943K. If we can replicate that efficiency across even one additional brand, we're talking about a meaningful share of the remaining ~$18M.'
Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute portfolio planning call with Eileen and Sebastian Aresco before the end of January to map which Lundbeck brands have open media budgets for H1 2026.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social & Display Incumbents
sales_plays.summary: Lundbeck's digital mix shows $636K in Paid Social and $840K in Internet Display — neither of which is currently attributed to TWC-Interactive. These budgets are likely flowing to Meta, programmatic DSPs, or other publishers. This play targets displacement of those incumbents using TWC's premium contextual and audience data advantages.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two lost opportunities — the $100K Vyepti Q3'25 Incremental (June 2025) and the $100K Search Data loss (February 2025) — and reopen the conversation: 'We lost two bids last year on incremental and search data. I'd like to understand what won those dollars so we can come back with something sharper for 2026.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Sebastian Aresco at Ogilvy Health: 'For the Paid Social and Display line items in the Vyepti plan, which platforms are currently holding those budgets and what KPIs are they being evaluated against?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC-Interactive's contextual weather and health triggers as a brand-safe, premium alternative to Meta's Paid Social and open-web display — especially relevant for a pharma brand with strict compliance requirements around audience targeting and adjacency.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Lundbeck is spending $636K on Paid Social and $840K on Display — a combined $1.48M. Even a 25% shift of those dollars to TWC-Interactive represents ~$370K in net-new revenue, on top of the existing $943K Vyepti 2026 commitment.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive audit — offer to show Eileen and Sebastian a side-by-side of TWC's audience quality and brand-safety metrics versus current display and social placements within the next two weeks.

talk_track_opener: Hey Eileen, I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with the Lundbeck team on Vyepti for a while now — most recently closing out the 2026 package in December and the Social Data deal in May. So we have real history here and I want to make sure we're being as useful as possible heading into the rest of 2026 planning. We're not just thinking about Vyepti — we're thinking about Lundbeck as a whole. I noticed that your total estimated media investment is around $19 million annually, and we're currently a partner on roughly $1 million of that. I'd love to understand where the rest of the portfolio sits — specifically which other CNS or psychiatric brands are in active planning — and whether there's a conversation worth having before those budgets get locked. Would you and Sebastian have 30 minutes in the next couple of weeks to walk through the broader roadmap?
open_questions: Beyond Vyepti, which other Lundbeck CNS or psychiatric brands are in active media planning for 2026, and are those budgets managed through Ogilvy Health or a separate agency?
For the Q3'25 Incremental and Search Data bids we lost last year — what ultimately won those dollars, and what would have needed to be different about our proposal?
Who owns the media planning and buying decision for Lundbeck's Paid Social and Display line items — is that Sebastian at Ogilvy Health, or is there a separate media AOR we should be talking to?
How is Lundbeck evaluating brand-safety and audience quality standards for pharma digital advertising in 2026, especially given the regulatory environment around CNS drug promotion?
Is the $943K Vyepti 2026 commitment the ceiling for TWC-Interactive this year, or is there an incremental budget process mid-year where we could compete for additional investment?

sales_plays.title: EyeMed + Sunglass Hut Cross-Brand Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Luxottica is an active PMP customer with $298.9M in total media spend, but recent Sunglass Hut direct buys have been lost at $50K each. This play expands the active PMP relationship into premium direct inventory across multiple Luxottica brand families, starting with David Shepherd's EyeMed portfolio.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the active PMP wins (Jan 2025 and July 2024) and congratulate the team on the ongoing relationship. Tell David Shepherd: 'We've been delivering on the PMP side and I want to show you what a direct buy layer could add on top of that — especially as you head into spring and summer planning for EyeMed and Sunglass Hut.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask David: 'When you look at your $159.8M digital budget across Paid Social, Display, and Online Video, where are you seeing the weakest incremental reach — and is EyeMed being planned separately from the Sunglass Hut and Oakley buys?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium direct inventory as the connective tissue between the PMP foundation already in place and the brand-specific tentpole moments (spring allergy season for EyeMed, summer UV for Sunglass Hut) where contextual weather-driven targeting adds measurable lift above the PMP floor.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The two lost Sunglass Hut direct buys totaled $100K in missed spend. Against a $298.9M total budget, a consolidated direct buy across EyeMed, Sunglass Hut, and Oakley at even 0.5% of digital spend represents $800K in incremental revenue — frame this as a brand-family consolidation opportunity, not a new ask.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask David for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to map the Q2 and Q3 brand calendar across EyeMed and Sunglass Hut so TWC can build a unified proposal before agency briefs go out to Energy BBDO and McGarrah Jessee.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Overweight with Premium Video + Display
sales_plays.summary: Paid Social represents $131.6M of Luxottica's $159.8M digital budget — an 82% concentration that signals over-reliance on a single channel. This play targets Larry Gies at Energy BBDO to rebalance a portion of that spend toward premium contextual video and display where TWC can demonstrate incremental reach and brand-safe environments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Larry Gies, CSO at Energy BBDO, with a channel-mix observation: 'Larry, looking at Luxottica's digital allocation, Paid Social is carrying $131.6M of a $159.8M digital budget. That's a significant concentration — I'd love to share some reach-curve data showing where premium video and contextual display are capturing audiences that social platforms are missing for retail eyewear.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Larry: 'Is the Paid Social concentration a deliberate performance-driven choice, or is there appetite from Luxottica's brand teams to test incremental reach channels — particularly for Sunglass Hut and Oakley where visual creative and context matter?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's Online Video and Display inventory as a brand-safe, contextually relevant alternative that complements — not replaces — social, specifically for high-intent moments like UV index spikes, travel planning, and outdoor lifestyle content where eyewear purchase intent is elevated and social feeds are not the primary touchpoint.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Luxottica's Online Video spend is only $3.3M and Internet Display is $8.5M combined — less than 8% of digital. A modest reallocation of 5% of the $131.6M Paid Social budget ($6.6M) into premium video and display would more than double current investment in those channels and provide measurable reach extension data for the next agency review.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer Larry a competitive channel-mix analysis specific to the eyewear and optical retail vertical — delivered within 10 business days — as a no-commitment strategic asset he can use in his next planning conversation with Luxottica's marketing leadership.

talk_track_opener: Hey David, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together on the PMP side — we just renewed in January and had a deal in place through mid-2024 as well — so I wanted to reach out directly rather than just going through the agency. You've got a lot of moving parts across EyeMed, Sunglass Hut, and Oakley, and I think there's a real opportunity to build on what's already working in the PMP and add a direct layer that gives your brand teams more control around key seasonal windows. What I'm seeing from the outside is that Luxottica's digital mix is heavily weighted toward Paid Social — over $131M of a $159M digital budget — while Online Video and Display are relatively thin. For a brand like EyeMed or Sunglass Hut, where context and environment matter a lot, that feels like a gap we could help close. Quick question for you: when you're planning Q2 and Q3 for EyeMed, are you working from a separate brief than the Sunglass Hut team, or is there a unified brand-family calendar we should be building against?
open_questions: The last two Sunglass Hut direct buy proposals were both lost at $50K — can you tell us what the deciding factor was, and whether the issue was pricing, targeting, or timing relative to the agency brief cycle?
David, is EyeMed's media planning handled separately from Sunglass Hut and Oakley, or does a centralized team at Luxottica Group set the digital channel mix across all brands?

With Energy BBDO and McGarrah Jessee both holding AOR Creative scope, who owns the final media channel recommendation — is that decided at the agency level or does Luxottica's internal team set the channel strategy before briefing out?

Given that Paid Social is carrying over $131M of your digital budget, is there an internal performance threshold or reach-saturation point that would trigger a reallocation conversation for Q3 or Q4 planning?

Are there specific retail tentpole moments — back-to-school, Father's Day, summer UV season — where Sunglass Hut or EyeMed has historically seen the strongest conversion, and are those being planned with contextual or weather-driven targeting today?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: MGM has three consecutive PMP wins (2024, 2025, 2026) at nominal $1 values, signaling a programmatic relationship that has not yet been monetized at scale. With $9.9M in digital spend and $5.2M alone in display, there is significant budget to convert into higher-value direct or premium inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three-year PMP streak — 'We've had an active PMP relationship with MGM since 2023, and I want to make sure we're delivering the full value of what TWC's premium inventory can do against your $5.2M display budget. I'd love 20 minutes with you and Bianca at Mischief to walk through what we're seeing.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Of the $5.2M MGM is running in display today, how much is being evaluated on brand-safe, contextually relevant environments versus pure programmatic efficiency — and is that a conversation happening at the Mischief level or internally with Joseph Wattoo's team?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): MGM's Bombora signal shows active Entertainment intent this week. TWC's premium direct placements against travel and entertainment audiences — tied to Bellagio, Cosmopolitan, and MGM Grand messaging — offer brand-safe, high-attention environments that programmatic PMP buys cannot guarantee.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): MGM's digital spend is $9.9M total. Even converting 15% of the $5.2M display budget from PMP to direct premium inventory represents a ~$780K incremental opportunity. The lost BetMGM Q4 deal at $100K shows appetite for larger direct commitments exists — that deal just didn't close.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we set a 20-minute call with you and Bianca Guimaraes at Mischief before end of month to walk through a premium inventory proposal mapped to MGM's Entertainment intent signal and Q3 travel demand window?'

sales_plays.title: Displace the $1.6M Online Video Budget
sales_plays.summary: MGM is spending $1.6M on Online Video and $585K on CTV through channels that are not TWC. Given the active PMP relationship and MGM's Entertainment intent signal, this is a clear displacement opportunity to consolidate video spend onto TWC's CTV and streaming inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): 'We've been a trusted PMP partner for MGM three years running, and I noticed you're carrying $1.6M in Online Video and $585K in CTV spend that isn't flowing through us. Given your current Entertainment intent signal on Bombora, I want to show you what TWC's video environment delivers for a brand like Bellagio versus where that budget is likely sitting today.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Who owns the video and CTV allocation decision — is that being set by Joseph Wattoo's team directly, or is Viral Nation or RP3 influencing where the online video budget goes? And what KPIs is that $1.6M being held to?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's CTV and streaming inventory reaches high-intent travel and entertainment audiences in a premium, brand-safe context. For MGM properties like Mandalay Bay and Luxor, contextual adjacency to weather, travel planning, and entertainment content on TWC outperforms generic video placements on open exchanges.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The combined $2.185M in Online Video and CTV spend represents the displacement target. Even capturing 30% — roughly $655K — would be a meaningful revenue event and would more than offset the lost BetMGM Q4 deal of $100K that slipped in November 2024.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to send you and Bianca Guimaraes a one-page competitive comparison of TWC CTV versus where MGM's video budget is currently placed — can I get that to you by Thursday and schedule 15 minutes to walk through it the following week?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Joseph — I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with MGM on PMP deals three years in a row now, most recently closing the 2026 PMP in January, so we have real history together. We also work closely with Bianca Guimaraes and the Mischief team, so we understand how MGM's creative and media decisions get made. I wanted to reach out because I think we're underleveraging what this relationship can do. Looking at MGM's current digital footprint — $9.9M in digital, $5.2M in display, $1.6M in online video, $585K in CTV — a significant portion of that spend is in channels where TWC can deliver measurably better outcomes than what programmatic or open exchange is providing today. Your Bombora signal is showing active Entertainment intent this week, which tells me your audiences are in-market right now. I'd love 20 minutes with you and Bianca to show you specifically how we'd map TWC's premium inventory against Bellagio, Cosmopolitan, and MGM Grand's demand windows — and what a direct or video-forward buy would look like compared to the PMP structure we've been running. Would a call before end of month work?
open_questions: The PMP deals we've closed with MGM in 2024, 2025, and 2026 have all been at nominal value — is that a structural holdback on the programmatic side, or is there a direct buy conversation we haven't been invited into yet?
On the $1.6M in Online Video spend — is that being managed through RP3 or Viral Nation, and what performance benchmarks is that budget being held to right now?
The BetMGM Q4 deal in November 2024 didn't close with us — can you tell me what happened there and whether BetMGM's media is being planned separately from the MGM Resorts brand budget?

With Mischief as AOR Creative and Viral Nation handling social influencers, who owns the media planning and buying decision for display and CTV — is there a media AOR we should be talking to that isn't currently in our relationship map?

Joseph, as CMO, how are you thinking about the balance between programmatic efficiency and premium brand-safe environments for properties like Bellagio and Cosmopolitan — is that a conversation that's actively happening on your team right now?

The expansion play converts the existing PMP relationship into a higher-value direct or premium package by leveraging the established trust with Hayley Diamond at Spark Foundry and demonstrating incremental reach beyond what the PMP delivers.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Hayley Diamond at Spark Foundry referencing the two active 2026 PMP deals and acknowledge the partnership is live. Ask for a 20-minute working session to review delivery performance on those PMPs before Q2 planning locks.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Hayley: 'The three direct buys we pitched in 2025 — Back to School, Q2 Holiday, and Winter Holiday — didn't close. Was that a budget sequencing issue, a targeting gap, or a creative/format mismatch we can solve this cycle?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a premium direct buy layered on top of the existing PMP as a way to guarantee first-look inventory during Macy's highest-demand tentpole windows — specifically the moments the PMP alone cannot lock in reach and frequency against fashion and home audiences.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Macy's spends $527.7M in total measured media. Their CTV allocation is only $17.4M against $157.5M in Broadcast — a 9:1 imbalance. A direct CTV/premium video package of $250K–$500K represents less than 3% of their CTV budget and closes the gap between the $1 PMP floor and the $200K in direct spend they walked away from in 2025.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Hayley to schedule a 20-minute PMP performance review by [specific date two weeks out] and come prepared with Q3 tentpole dates — Back to School and Fall Fashion — so a direct add-on proposal can be ready before Spark Foundry's planning cycle closes.

sales_plays.title: Search-to-Weather-Intent Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Macy's allocates $217.3M to Digital Search — their single largest digital channel — while their weather-contextual and intent-driven display investment is minimal ($8.7M Internet Display, $915K Mobile App). This play targets Sharon Otterman directly to displace a portion of lower-funnel search spend with upper-funnel, weather-triggered contextual inventory that reaches Macy's fashion and home shoppers at the moment seasonal intent peaks.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Send Sharon Otterman a one-page insight note: 'Macy's spends $217.3M in paid search — capturing demand that already exists. Weather and seasonal context signals can create that demand 48–72 hours earlier, when shoppers are deciding what to buy before they search.' Reference the lost Winter Holiday and Back to School buys as proof the conversation has started but hasn't landed the right frame.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Sharon: 'When Macy's plans tentpole moments like Back to School or the Holiday push, is the media mix decision driven by last-click attribution, or is there room to fund upper-funnel contextual triggers that feed the search funnel downstream?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered contextual inventory as a demand-creation layer that sits upstream of search — reaching shoppers on mobile and CTV when a weather pattern (first cold snap, back-to-school season shift, spring transition) aligns with Macy's fashion and home categories, driving the intent that search then captures.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Macy's Online Video spend is only $2.5M and Mobile App is $915K against $217.3M in search. Reallocating even 1% of search budget ($2.2M) into weather-contextual video and mobile would represent a 2.4x increase in their entire current online video investment and a direct test of demand-creation efficiency against their existing search attribution model.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a 30-minute session with Sharon Otterman and Hayley Diamond together — framed as a 'Q3 tentpole planning alignment' — to present a weather-context attribution case study from a comparable retail brand and map it to Macy's Back to School and Fall Fashion windows before Spark Foundry's Q3 budgets are finalized.

talk_track_opener: Hey Hayley, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together on the two PMP deals that went live in January, and I wanted to get in front of you before Q2 and Q3 planning locks. I work with a number of Spark Foundry's retail accounts and I know how quickly the tentpole calendar fills up. Here's what caught my attention: Macy's has $217.3M sitting in paid search and only $17.4M in CTV against $157.5M in Broadcast — and we've had three direct buy conversations in 2025 that didn't close. I don't think that's a fit problem; I think it's a timing and framing problem. I'd love 20 minutes to show you how the PMP you already have running can be the foundation for a direct premium layer tied to Back to School and Fall Fashion — and walk through one idea for how weather-context inventory can feed the search funnel rather than compete with it. What does your calendar look like in the next two weeks?
open_questions: The three direct buys we proposed in 2025 — Back to School, Q2 Holiday, and Winter Holiday — all lost. Was the primary objection budget availability, attribution methodology, or the format and targeting approach we brought to the table?

Who at Macy's — Sharon Otterman's team or Spark Foundry — owns the decision to shift budget from paid search into upper-funnel contextual or video formats, and what does that approval process look like?
The two January 2026 PMPs are live — how is Macy's currently measuring performance on those deals, and is there a threshold that would trigger a conversation about upgrading to a direct buy?

With Bloomingdale's and Bluemercury operating under the same Macy's Inc. umbrella, is Spark Foundry planning media for those brands on a unified budget or separately — and is there a cross-brand expansion conversation we should be having?

As Macy's plans Back to School and Fall Fashion for Q3 2026, what is the current timeline for Spark Foundry to finalize channel allocations, and when does the window close for us to be included in that plan?

sales_plays.title: Mars Pet Nutrition Premium Inventory Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Mars has been a consistent PMP buyer since 2023 and has a 2026 placeholder working at $535K. With $30M in CTV and $97.8M in digital spend, there is clear headroom to move Mindy Barry's Pet Nutrition North America brand into higher-impact, direct-sold weather-contextual and audience-guaranteed inventory beyond the current PMP arrangement.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the active 2026 PMP relationship and the $535,294 placeholder currently in-flight. Email or call Mindy Barry directly: 'We've had a strong PMP partnership since 2023 and I want to make sure your 2026 Pet Nutrition investment is working as hard as possible — I have a specific upgrade path I'd like to walk you through.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Mindy: 'Within your $97.8M digital budget for Pet Nutrition, what percentage is currently allocated to guaranteed, direct-sold placements versus open-market or PMP — and are you seeing the audience quality and brand-safe context you need from the PMP buys?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct-sold, weather-triggered inventory as the natural next step from PMP: pet care purchase intent spikes during weather events (cold snaps, storms, seasonal transitions) — TWC's contextual targeting connects Mars Pet Nutrition brands to consumers actively planning household and pet needs in those moments.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Mars spends $30M in CTV and $8M in Online Video. Even a 10% shift of Online Video spend ($800K) from PMP to direct-sold weather-contextual units would represent a meaningful step up from the current $535K placeholder and deliver guaranteed impressions against a verified in-market pet owner audience.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Mindy for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar before end of April to review a custom inventory proposal built specifically around your Pet Nutrition tentpole moments for Q3 and Q4 2026?'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars with On-Property Video
sales_plays.summary: Mars allocates $70.3M to Paid Social — the single largest digital line item — while Online Video on TWC sits at a fraction of that. With agency partner Lisa Torres at Publicis North America leading multicultural practice, there is an opening to reframe TWC's on-property video as a brand-safe, measurable alternative for a portion of social spend that faces increasing scrutiny on brand suitability and measurement transparency.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Route through Lisa Torres at Publicis North America. Open with: 'Lisa, given the ongoing industry conversation around brand suitability on social platforms, I wanted to bring you a specific data point — Mars is currently running $70.3M through Paid Social. I have a proposal to redirect a test portion into TWC on-property video that I think would resonate with Mindy Barry's team at Mars Pet Nutrition.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Lisa: 'Is Publicis currently benchmarking brand suitability and viewability scores on Mars's social placements against premium publisher video — and is there internal pressure from Mars to diversify away from walled gardens for any of their CPG or pet care brands?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC on-property video as a transparent, measurable, brand-safe environment: 100% owned-and-operated inventory, no UGC adjacency risk, verified weather-contextual audience signals that align with Mars Pet Nutrition seasonal demand windows (winter pet care, spring/summer outdoor pet activity).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Mars's $70.3M Paid Social spend dwarfs its $8M Online Video allocation. A 5% reallocation from social to TWC on-property video equals $3.5M — a 44% increase over current Online Video spend and a meaningful new revenue line well above the existing PMP relationship.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Lisa for a joint agency-client briefing: 'Could you help us get 45 minutes with Mindy Barry's team and your planning leads before the Q3 planning cycle closes — we'll bring a side-by-side suitability and reach comparison for Paid Social versus TWC video for the Pet Nutrition portfolio?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Mindy, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working with Mars on PMP deals going back to 2023 and I know your team has a 2026 placement already in motion. I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a bigger opportunity we haven't fully explored together yet. Looking at Mars Pet Nutrition's media mix, you're running $30M in CTV and nearly $98M in digital, but the PMP relationship we have today is really just a starting point. Weather is one of the strongest behavioral triggers for pet care purchases — cold snaps, seasonal transitions, storm prep — and right now we're not connecting those moments to your brands in a direct, guaranteed way. I'd love to show you what a more intentional, direct-sold program built around your 2026 tentpole calendar could look like. Would you have 30 minutes in the next couple of weeks to dig into that?
open_questions: What performance benchmarks is Mars Pet Nutrition using to evaluate the $70.3M Paid Social investment — and are brand suitability or viewability gaps creating any internal pressure to diversify that spend?
Who at Mars Pet Nutrition North America, alongside Mindy Barry, owns the final decision on shifting budget from PMP to direct-sold or on-property video formats for 2026?

Is Publicis North America currently running any cross-brand planning for Mars's pet care and snacking portfolios together, or are those budgets managed in separate silos that would require separate conversations?

What are the two or three biggest retail tentpole or cultural moments Mars Pet Nutrition is building media plans around for Q3 and Q4 2026 — and is weather-contextual targeting part of those plans today?

Given the $535,294 placeholder currently working for 2026, what would need to be true about audience quality, brand safety, or measurement for Mars to consider a significantly larger direct-sold commitment with TWC this year?

sales_plays.title: PMP to Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Mayo Clinic has two consecutive PMP wins (2024 and 2025) with TWC Interactive, signaling an established programmatic relationship. This play converts that PMP spend into a higher-value direct or sponsorship buy, expanding wallet share against their $95.6M total media footprint.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins — 'We've now delivered on the TWC Interactive PMP in both July 2024 and January 2025. I want to show Sherri and Chris what a direct premium placement looks like alongside that foundation, given Mayo's scale at $95.6M in total spend.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'With $12.3M already in CTV and $7.5M in digital, how is Mayo currently evaluating the gap between programmatic reach and premium contextual alignment — especially for health-system brand messaging versus patient acquisition?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's owned weather and health content environments as a natural extension of the PMP — moving from audience-targeted inventory to contextually premium, brand-safe placements that reinforce Mayo's 'largest integrated not-for-profit' positioning at moments of high health intent.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Your current PMP deals are logged at nominal value. Even a modest direct commitment — say 5% of your $12.3M CTV budget — would represent over $600K in incremental premium inventory. We can model CPM efficiency versus the open programmatic rates you're currently paying.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we set a 30-minute working session with you and Chris Sculles at Preston Spire before end of month to walk through a premium placement proposal built on top of your existing PMP framework?'

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Displacement — Shift Share from $42.5M Social Budget
sales_plays.summary: Mayo Clinic's paid social spend of $42.5M dwarfs every other digital line item and represents the clearest displacement opportunity. This play challenges the concentration risk of that allocation and positions TWC's digital and CTV environments as brand-safe, health-contextual alternatives.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a data observation to McGuffin Creative Group's Chris Sculles: 'I was looking at Mayo's media mix — $42.5M in paid social is a significant concentration. With healthcare advertisers facing increasing scrutiny on social platform brand safety and algorithm volatility, I'd love to share what we're seeing from comparable health-system advertisers who've diversified.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Is the $42.5M in paid social being evaluated on a performance basis — patient acquisition, appointment scheduling — or is a portion of that doing brand and awareness work that could live in a more controlled environment?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-adjacent health content, CTV inventory, and display environments as brand-safe, high-intent alternatives for the awareness and consideration portion of Mayo's social budget — particularly for audiences actively searching health information during seasonal illness cycles.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'If even 3% of the $42.5M paid social budget — roughly $1.27M — shifted to TWC premium digital and CTV, we can demonstrate comparable reach against health-intending audiences with full brand-safety controls and no algorithm dependency. We can build a side-by-side CPM and audience overlap model.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to bring a brief displacement analysis to Sherri Gilligan's team — a one-pager showing audience overlap between your current social targeting and our health-intent inventory. Can we get 20 minutes on the calendar this week or next with whoever owns the digital mix decisions?'

talk_track_opener: Hi Sherri — I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Mayo Clinic through your TWC Interactive PMP for the past two years, most recently closing the 2025 deal in January, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a meaningful opportunity we haven't fully explored together yet. We work with a number of large health systems and not-for-profit medical groups, and the combination of contextual health environments, CTV scale, and first-party weather-driven audience signals puts us in a pretty unique position for an organization like Mayo.
open_questions: The TWC Interactive PMP has renewed twice — who at Mayo or the agencies is evaluating whether to expand that relationship into direct placements, and what would that decision process look like?
With $42.5M in paid social, how is Mayo measuring the brand-safety and audience quality of that spend versus what you're seeing in more controlled environments like CTV or premium display?

Is Sherri Gilligan's team currently segmenting the media budget between patient acquisition performance spend and brand/awareness spend, and which agency — McGuffin or Preston Spire — owns the brand awareness strategy?

Given Mayo's focus on accessibility of care at home and global health impact, are there specific audience segments — caregivers, chronic condition patients, health-curious consumers — that are underserved by the current paid social and search-heavy mix?
With $16.9M in search and $42.5M in social already committed, is there a formal review process for the digital mix in 2025, and when does planning for the next budget cycle typically begin?

sales_plays.title: Expand Beyond PMP Into Premium Direct
sales_plays.summary: McDonald's is an active customer with a won PMP deal and a $500K placeholder in working upside. The goal is to convert that placeholder into a committed direct buy and layer in premium on-property inventory that the PMP cannot access.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the won Q1'26 Hot Honey PG deal ($200K) and the open $500K 2026 Placeholder with Ronnie Haligman at Starcom. Say: 'Ronnie, the Hot Honey PG activation closed strong in Q1. I want to make sure that $500K placeholder is structured to hit harder than a PMP can deliver — there's premium direct inventory I want to show you before Starcom locks the H2 plan.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'For the $500K placeholder, is Starcom prioritizing reach extension, contextual alignment, or audience precision — and has Alyssa Buetikofer's team set a specific cultural-moment or tentpole window that needs to be anchored?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position premium direct inventory as the layer the PMP misses — guaranteed placement, editorial context, and first-look on high-demand tentpole units that programmatic pipes cannot reserve. Tie to McDonald's four core pillars (Community Connection, Jobs & Inclusion) where contextual alignment matters most.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): McDonald's carries $605.6M in total estimated spend. The current TWC relationship represents under 0.12% of that wallet. Converting the $500K placeholder to a direct premium buy and adding one incremental on-property unit at $150K brings TWC share to roughly $870K — a 4x lift from the Q1 baseline.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Ronnie for a 30-minute working session before the Starcom H2 planning lock — propose a specific date two weeks out — to walk through two premium direct packages mapped to the tentpole windows already in McDonald's calendar.

sales_plays.title: Displace Lopez Negrete Multicultural Digital Budget
sales_plays.summary: Lopez Negrete Communications holds McDonald's multicultural AOR scope including media buying. McDonald's $87.3M paid social and $28.4M mobile app spend likely flows partly through this relationship. TWC's multicultural and culturally-indexed inventory is a direct displacement opportunity against Lopez Negrete's digital placement decisions.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a data-led observation to Alyssa Buetikofer or her team: 'McDonald's paid social is at $87.3M and mobile app at $28.4M — that's over $115M in digital formats where multicultural audience precision is the deciding factor. I want to show you where TWC inventory indexes against the exact audiences Lopez Negrete is trying to reach, in environments they may not currently have access to.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Within the Lopez Negrete scope, which multicultural segments — Hispanic, Black, bilingual — are showing the strongest sales lift correlation right now, and is the current digital mix actually reaching those audiences in contextually relevant moments or just demographic-targeted placements?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's culturally-indexed on-property inventory as a complement or partial displacement to Lopez Negrete's open-market digital buys — offering contextual relevance (weather, local cultural moments, community content) that a standard paid social or display buy cannot replicate, directly aligned with McDonald's Community Connection and Jobs & Inclusion pillars.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If TWC captures just 2% of the $115M paid social plus mobile app budget currently routed through Lopez Negrete's digital scope, that equals $2.3M in incremental revenue — against a current TWC footprint of under $720K across all 2026 deals combined.

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a brief with Alyssa Buetikofer's customer experience team and Lopez Negrete's planning lead to present a multicultural audience alignment study — offer to deliver it within 10 business days, no commitment required, framed as a planning input for Q3 cultural-moment windows.

talk_track_opener: Hey Ronnie, this is [Rep Name] from TWC. We just closed the Hot Honey PG activation together in Q1 at $200K and the PMP deal is live — so you already know we can execute. I want to make sure we're in the right conversations before Starcom finalizes the H2 plan, because there's premium direct inventory that the PMP pipe simply can't reserve, and I'd rather show you the options now than have you locked out of the best units. McDonald's is running over $605M in total estimated media spend this year, and the $500K placeholder you have open is the right moment to upgrade from programmatic to something with guaranteed placement and editorial context. What's the best 30 minutes on your calendar in the next two weeks to walk through two packages I've built specifically around the tentpole windows McDonald's already has on the calendar?
open_questions: Ronnie, the $500K 2026 Placeholder has been open since March — what does Starcom need to see from us to move it from Working Upside to a committed line item before the H2 plan locks?

For the multicultural digital budget that flows through Lopez Negrete's media buying scope, who at McDonald's corporate — Alyssa Buetikofer's team or a separate brand manager — has final approval authority on shifting placements to new inventory sources?

The Hot Honey PG activation closed at $200K in Q1 — was that performance measured against a specific sales lift or foot traffic metric, and did it hit the threshold that would justify a larger direct buy in Q3 or Q4?

McDonald's is running $87.3M in paid social — is Starcom seeing diminishing returns on incremental paid social spend, and is there internal pressure to diversify that budget into formats with stronger contextual relevance?

Are there specific cultural-moment or tentpole windows — back to school, Hispanic Heritage Month, a sports property — already on Alyssa Buetikofer's 2026 calendar where McDonald's needs inventory reserved now rather than bought programmatically at the last minute?

sales_plays.title: Digital Mix Upgrade: From Display to Premium Video
sales_plays.summary: Meijer is an active customer with a proven Holiday Q4 track record ($216K in 2025, $172K in 2024) but is significantly over-indexed in Paid Social ($21.2M) and under-invested in CTV ($262K) relative to their total digital footprint. This play targets Don Sanderson and Ben Kline to shift incremental budget from lower-funnel display into premium weather-contextual video and CTV.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Ben Kline at The Distillery Project referencing the back-to-back Holiday Q4 wins ($216K in 2025, $172K in 2024) and note that Meijer's CTV investment ($262K) is a fraction of their Online Video spend ($4.9M) — a gap worth a 30-minute portfolio conversation.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Ben: 'When you look at Meijer's $21.2M Paid Social line versus the $262K currently in CTV, what's the internal rationale for that allocation — and is there appetite to test incremental CTV reach against Midwest grocery shoppers this year?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-targeted CTV and premium video inventory as the bridge between Meijer's strong brand awareness in the Midwest and the conversion-driving moments (cold snaps driving soup/comfort food, spring grilling season) that Paid Social alone can't capture contextually.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Show that moving even 5% of the $21.2M Paid Social budget (~$1.06M) into weather-contextual CTV would represent a 4x increase over current CTV spend ($262K) and align with the vertical benchmark for Midwest supercenter retailers activating seasonal demand triggers.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Ben to schedule a 30-minute portfolio review with Don Sanderson before end of Q2 to map a CTV/video expansion plan against Meijer's summer and back-to-school tentpoles — offer to bring a Midwest-specific audience and weather-moment analysis to the meeting.

sales_plays.title: Tentpole Planning Window: Get Ahead of Summer & Back-to-School
sales_plays.summary: Meijer lost the Summer Campaign opportunity in April 2025 ($100K lost opp) — likely because planning happened too late. With Bombora showing active Entertainment intent this week, and Meijer's Midwest supercenter format naturally tied to seasonal demand, this play opens a planning conversation now (Q2) before summer and back-to-school budgets are locked.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the lost Summer 2025 opp directly with Ben Kline: 'We missed connecting on the summer campaign last April — I want to make sure we're in the room earlier this cycle. Meijer's Bombora signal is showing Entertainment intent right now, which often precedes summer lifestyle and outdoor planning spend.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'For Meijer's summer and back-to-school windows, when does The Distillery Project typically lock media plans — and is there a tentpole calendar we should be building against together?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's seasonal weather triggers (summer heat driving beverage/outdoor, back-to-school cold-weather prep in the Midwest) as a planning framework that gives Meijer contextual relevance across their full supercenter product mix — groceries, apparel, electronics, and home goods — in a single media partnership.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The Holiday Q4 2025 win ($216K) proves Meijer invests when the timing and context are right. A comparable summer activation — planned 60 days earlier than the lost April 2025 opp — could recover and exceed that $100K lost opportunity with a weather-contextual video and display package.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send Ben Kline a one-page Midwest seasonal demand calendar (summer grilling, back-to-school, Labor Day) with TWC inventory recommendations by end of this week — ask for a 20-minute call the following week to pressure-test it against Meijer's internal tentpole calendar.

talk_track_opener: Hey Ben, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company — we've been working with Meijer through you on the Q4 programs the last two years, most recently the Holiday campaign that closed at $216K in October. Really glad those have performed well and I want to make sure we're building on that momentum rather than just showing up at the same time each year. When I look at Meijer's overall media mix, there's something that stands out: $21.2M in Paid Social and $4.9M in Online Video, but only $262K in CTV — and the Summer 2025 campaign didn't come together, which I think we can fix this cycle. Meijer's Bombora signal is showing Entertainment intent right now, which is a classic early indicator of summer lifestyle planning, and given that Meijer's supercenter format spans groceries, outdoor, apparel, and electronics, there's a real opportunity to build a weather-contextual plan that works across the whole store. I'd love to get 30 minutes with you and Don Sanderson before the summer window closes in on us — would a planning session in the next two weeks work to map out the tentpole calendar together?
open_questions: When The Distillery Project locks Meijer's summer and back-to-school media plans, what's the typical timeline — and are those conversations happening internally right now?
The CTV line for Meijer is currently at $262K against $4.9M in Online Video — is that allocation a deliberate strategic choice, or is it a function of how proposals have come in historically?
Don Sanderson owns both merchandising and marketing — does that mean seasonal product priorities directly influence media channel decisions, and who else is in the room when those calls get made?

The Summer 2025 campaign didn't come together — was that a timing issue, a budget reallocation, or a competitive displacement, and what would need to be different for a summer program to get approved this year?
Meijer's Bombora signal is showing Entertainment intent this week — is that tied to a specific campaign or product category initiative we should be building a media recommendation around?

sales_plays.title: MSK Digital Efficiency Gap
sales_plays.summary: MSK is spending $3.0M in digital with Digitas managing execution, yet $153K in Online Video and $167K in Mobile App suggest underinvestment in high-impact video formats relative to their $976K CTV and $2.1M broadcast footprint. This play targets Paula Nevin to surface an efficiency and reach gap in their current digital mix.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Paula Nevin referencing MSK's known investment in broadcast ($2.1M) and CTV ($976K) and note that their online video allocation ($153K) appears disproportionately small relative to where cancer-care audiences are increasingly reachable — streaming, mobile, and digital video. Ask for 20 minutes to share audience data specific to oncology patients and caregivers in the NY/NJ market.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Paula: 'With Digitas managing your digital buy, how are you currently measuring cross-channel reach and frequency between your CTV investment and your online video spend — and where are you seeing the biggest gaps in connecting with caregivers versus patients directly?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our platform as the bridge between MSK's strong broadcast/CTV presence and their underdeveloped digital video footprint — specifically targeting oncology-intent audiences, caregivers, and health-system decision-makers in the NY/NJ DMA that broadcast alone cannot reach with precision.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): MSK's trailing 12-month spend is $9.87M. Their online video line ($153K) is less than 2% of total spend. A modest reallocation of even 5% of digital budget ($150K) into targeted digital video could extend their CTV reach by an estimated 30-40% among in-market health audiences — make this concrete with a custom reach projection for the NY/NJ market.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Paula for a 20-minute discovery call in the next two weeks: 'I'd love to bring a brief audience analysis showing where MSK's current media mix may have reach gaps among oncology patients and caregivers in NY/NJ. Would the week of [specific date] work for a short call?'

sales_plays.title: Alma Multicultural Agency Bridge
sales_plays.summary: Alma serves as MSK's AOR for Multicultural marketing. If there is an existing relationship between our team and anyone at Alma, this play routes the MSK introduction through that agency contact rather than cold-approaching Paula Nevin directly. Andrea Giraldo (Sr. Art Director at Alma) is the named contact and a viable entry point.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Check internally whether any rep or sales leader has an existing relationship with Alma. If yes, request a warm introduction to Andrea Giraldo, citing MSK's multicultural AOR scope and our ability to support diverse-audience targeting for oncology campaigns in NY/NJ. If no internal relationship exists, reach out to Andrea directly referencing Alma's multicultural mandate for MSK and a specific insight about reaching Hispanic and Black cancer-screening audiences — two segments with documented disparities in early detection.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Andrea: 'For MSK's multicultural campaigns, how are you currently measuring whether your media placements are reaching underserved communities in the NY/NJ market at the frequency needed to drive awareness and appointment intent — and is that measurement integrated with what Digitas is running on the general market side?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our platform as a tool Alma can use to demonstrate multicultural reach and attribution to MSK leadership — giving Alma a data story that reinforces their AOR value while extending MSK's $980K paid social and $859K display spend into more precisely targeted multicultural inventory.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): MSK's paid social spend is $980K and display is $859K — a combined $1.84M that Alma and Digitas are jointly influencing. Even a 10% shift of that budget toward multicultural-optimized placements through our platform represents roughly $184K in incremental opportunity, with a clear attribution story Alma can bring back to Paula Nevin.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Andrea for a 30-minute working session: 'I'd like to share a brief audience map showing multicultural oncology-intent audiences in NY/NJ that align with MSK's mission. Could we find 30 minutes in the next two weeks — I'll bring the data, you tell me if it fits what Alma is trying to accomplish for MSK this year?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Paula, I'm [Rep Name] from [Company] — we work with health systems and cancer care brands across the Northeast to help them reach oncology patients and caregivers with more precision than broadcast alone can deliver. We've built audience segments specifically around cancer-care intent, caregiver research behavior, and health-system navigation that are particularly relevant in the NY/NJ market. I noticed MSK has a strong broadcast and CTV presence — over $3M combined — but your online video footprint is relatively small at around $153K. That gap is actually pretty common for institutions that built their media mix around traditional reach, but it can mean missing a significant share of the audience that's doing active research on treatment options and clinical trials before they ever pick up the phone. I'd love to share a quick audience analysis we've put together for the NY/NJ oncology market — no pitch deck, just data — and get your read on whether it maps to where MSK is trying to grow. Would 20 minutes in the next couple of weeks work?
open_questions: Paula, with Digitas running digital and Publicis handling planning and buying, how are you personally measuring whether MSK's $3.0M digital investment is working in concert with your broadcast and CTV spend — or are those channels being evaluated in silos?

For MSK's multicultural campaigns managed through Alma, who ultimately approves the media plan — does that decision flow through you, through Publicis North America, or does Alma have independent budget authority?

Given that MSK's online video spend is currently around $153K, is that a deliberate strategic choice or more of a historical artifact of how the budget was built — and is there appetite to revisit it this planning cycle?

How does MSK measure the connection between media exposure and downstream patient acquisition or appointment volume — and is that attribution model something Digitas owns, or does it live internally with your team?

With clinical trials being a key part of MSK's mission, are there specific audience segments — like caregivers of late-stage patients or adults researching second opinions — that your current media plan is actively trying to reach but struggling to find at scale?

sales_plays.title: Premium Inventory Expansion: From PMP to Direct
sales_plays.summary: Mercedes is an active PMP customer with two consecutive wins in 2024-2025. With $68.4M in digital spend and $14.9M already in CTV, there is clear headroom to move from transactional PMP buys into a higher-commitment direct deal that captures premium placements and weather-driven demand windows.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins (Jan 2025 and Jul 2024) with Scott Gelber at Omnicom Media Group. Open by acknowledging the partnership momentum and share that TWC's premium direct inventory — weather-triggered automotive units — is now available for Q3/Q4 planning before upfront windows close.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Scott: 'Given Mercedes' $14.9M CTV investment and the PMP performance you've seen, where is Melody Lee's team still seeing gaps in reaching in-market luxury buyers during high-consideration weather moments — spring thaw or fall model-year launches?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct-sold, weather-contextual CTV and digital video inventory as the upgrade path from PMP. The 2023 lost opp at $216 signals price sensitivity was once a barrier — frame the direct deal around guaranteed premium placement and audience quality, not just CPM efficiency.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Mercedes runs $6.7M in online video and $5.7M in display. Even a 10% shift of that $12.4M combined budget into TWC direct inventory represents a $1.2M+ incremental opportunity on top of existing PMP spend. Tie this to measurable lift in luxury auto consideration during weather-relevant moments.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Scott for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to map Mercedes' Q3 2025 campaign calendar against TWC's premium weather-moment inventory windows — before Omnicom's upfront commitments are locked.

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Budget Displacement: Capture Mercedes' $50.2M Social Spend
sales_plays.summary: Mercedes is allocating $50.2M to Paid Social — the single largest line in their digital mix — while TWC's current relationship is limited to small PMP buys. The Entertainment intent signal this week suggests active content and audience planning, creating an opening to displace a portion of social spend with TWC's scaled, brand-safe digital and video environment.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the Bombora Entertainment intent signal detected this week for Mercedes. Reach out to Scott Gelber at Omnicom and note: 'We're seeing Mercedes showing active interest in Entertainment content environments right now — we have a timely angle to share that connects directly to where Melody Lee's team appears to be planning.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'With $50.2M going into Paid Social, how is the team currently evaluating brand-safe, premium content environments versus social platforms — especially as luxury brand adjacency and audience quality become bigger factors for Mercedes and Maybach positioning?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's digital and video inventory as a brand-safe, premium alternative to social feed placements. Highlight contextual relevance — weather-driven content moments align with luxury auto purchase consideration — and contrast TWC's editorial environment with the brand-safety risks inherent in social platforms for a luxury marque like Mercedes-Benz and Maybach.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 5% reallocation of Mercedes' $50.2M Paid Social budget equals $2.5M in incremental TWC spend — a 10x step-up from current PMP-level investment. Frame this as a test-and-learn pilot that Merkley + Partners can structure as an incremental line item without displacing existing social commitments in full.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a specific creative alignment call with both Scott Gelber (Omnicom) and a Merkley + Partners contact within the next 10 business days — timed to Mercedes' apparent Entertainment planning cycle — to present TWC audience data against Mercedes' luxury in-market buyer profile.

talk_track_opener: Hey Scott, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working together on the Mercedes PMP programs going back to mid-2024, and I wanted to reach out while your team is in active planning mode. We've built a real track record with Omnicom on the Mercedes business and I think there's a meaningful next step worth a conversation.
open_questions: Scott, the Mercedes PMP programs have run two consecutive cycles — is Melody Lee's team evaluating a move to direct-sold or guaranteed inventory for any Q3 or Q4 campaigns, or is the preference to stay in programmatic for now?

With $50.2M allocated to Paid Social, how is Omnicom currently benchmarking brand-safety and audience quality on social platforms against premium publisher environments for the Mercedes and Maybach brands?
The Bombora signal we're seeing around Entertainment this week — is that tied to a specific upcoming campaign or content partnership that Merkley + Partners is developing for Mercedes?
Who at Mercedes — Melody Lee's team or Omnicom — owns the final decision on shifting budget between digital channels like Paid Social, Online Video, and CTV for a mid-year reallocation?

Are there specific seasonal or weather-driven demand windows — spring model launches, fall inventory push — where Mercedes has historically seen stronger in-market buyer activity that we should be planning inventory around now?

sales_plays.title: Shift Digital Dollars On-Property
sales_plays.summary: Mercury is running $14.7M in digital spend — with $11.6M in Paid Social and $46.7M in Search — but has minimal presence in premium on-property environments. This play targets Erik Thompson to redirect a slice of that programmatic/social budget toward higher-intent, brand-safe placements.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Erik Thompson referencing Mercury's $11.6M Paid Social investment and note that auto insurance brands are seeing diminishing returns on Meta/social as CPMs rise and targeting restrictions tighten. Open with: 'I noticed Mercury is one of the heavier digital spenders in the LA auto insurance market — wanted to share something specific to your mix.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Of your $14.7M digital budget, how much is currently allocated to on-property placements versus open-exchange or social inventory — and are you satisfied with the quality of audiences you're reaching there?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our on-property inventory as a direct displacement of Mercury's $133K Internet Display and $83K Mobile App spend — audiences with verified intent, not cookie-based proxies. Contrast the brand-safe, contextually relevant environment against the fragmented reach of open exchange.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Mercury spends $763K in CTV and $426K in Online Video — both signals they value premium video environments. Show how shifting even 5% of the $11.6M Paid Social budget (~$580K) into on-property placements could deliver comparable reach with higher-intent audiences and measurable lift.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Erik for a 20-minute call in the next two weeks to walk through a custom audience overlap analysis against Mercury's current digital targeting parameters — no commitment, just data.

sales_plays.title: Carbon Creative Warm Introduction
sales_plays.summary: Carbon handles Creative Production Services for Mercury. Liam Chapple as Executive Creative Director is a natural entry point to build internal advocacy before approaching Erik Thompson directly — especially given Mercury's active Entertainment intent signal this week.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Liam Chapple at Carbon referencing Mercury's Entertainment intent signal and the $426K Online Video + $763K CTV spend. Frame it as: 'We're seeing Mercury showing strong interest in Entertainment content environments this week — wanted to connect with you before reaching out to Erik, since you're closest to the creative strategy.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Liam: 'Is Carbon currently being briefed on any upcoming campaigns where Mercury wants to align with entertainment or lifestyle content — and is there a media partner conversation happening alongside the creative development?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our platform as a natural creative distribution partner for the assets Carbon is producing — especially for CTV and Online Video formats where Mercury is already spending ($763K and $426K respectively). Offer to co-present with Liam to Erik on how the creative can perform in premium on-property environments.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Mercury's combined CTV + Online Video spend is $1.19M. If Carbon can advocate for our inventory as the distribution layer for those formats, even a 25% shift in placement strategy represents ~$297K in net-new revenue — and positions Carbon as a strategic media-plus-creative partner.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Liam for a 15-minute informal call this week to understand the current Mercury campaign calendar and whether a joint intro to Erik Thompson makes sense before the next planning cycle.

talk_track_opener: Hey Erik, I'm [Name] from [Company] — we work with a number of auto insurance brands in California helping them get more out of their digital investment, particularly as social and open-exchange inventory gets more expensive and harder to target precisely. We're not a fit for everyone, but I did want to reach out because Mercury's media mix caught my attention. You're running a serious digital program — over $14.7M in digital alone, with meaningful investment in Paid Social and CTV — and that tells me you're already thinking about multi-format reach at scale. What I noticed is that Mercury's on-property digital footprint looks relatively light compared to the overall spend, especially in display and mobile. I'd love to ask you one question: when you look at your $11.6M in Paid Social, how confident are you that you're reaching in-market auto insurance shoppers versus broad demographic proxies? That's the conversation I'd want to start — no pitch, just a quick data comparison. Would a 20-minute call in the next couple of weeks make sense?
open_questions: Of Mercury's $14.7M digital budget, how much is currently allocated to on-property placements versus programmatic open-exchange or social inventory — and how are you measuring quality of audience across those channels?
Is Erik Thompson the sole decision-maker on digital media partnerships, or does Carbon's Liam Chapple have influence over where Mercury's creative assets are distributed?

Mercury's Paid Social spend is $11.6M — are you seeing the targeting precision you need for auto insurance shoppers on those platforms, or are rising CPMs and signal loss creating pressure to diversify?
With $763K already in CTV, is Mercury planning to grow that investment in the next planning cycle, and is there a preference for direct publisher relationships versus programmatic CTV buys?

Given Mercury's Entertainment intent activity this week, is there an upcoming campaign in planning that's meant to align with entertainment content — and has a media partner been selected for that effort yet?
