sales_plays.title: Meta Brand Family Expansion: WhatsApp to Full Portfolio
sales_plays.summary: Meta is an active PMP customer with consecutive won deals in 2024 and 2025. Sophie Ho owns the WhatsApp brand globally, representing a distinct budget line separate from Meta's core brand spend. This play expands the existing PMP relationship into additional Meta brand lines (Quest 3S, Ray-Ban Meta AI glasses, Meta AI) and upgrades from PMP to direct/premium inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins (2024-10-03 and 2025-01-07) in your opener to Gina Mazzorana at Spark Foundry. Say: 'Gina, we've had two strong PMP cycles together — I want to show you what incremental reach looks like when we layer in direct placements alongside those deals, specifically for the hardware and AI product lines that are clearly getting budget this year.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Gina: 'Beyond WhatsApp, which Meta brand lines — Quest 3S, Ray-Ban Meta AI glasses, or Meta AI — are currently planned through Spark Foundry for H2, and are those budgets being activated separately from the core Meta AOR scope?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Meta's $43M Mobile App spend and $28.2M CTV budget signal heavy investment in performance and awareness simultaneously. Position TWC's premium CTV and mobile inventory as the bridge between those two objectives — awareness for hardware launches, performance for app-driven products — something PMP alone cannot deliver at the targeting precision Meta needs.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Meta's trailing 12-month spend is $187.7M. The current TWC PMP relationship is booked at nominal value ($1 deals). Even a 0.5% shift of Meta's $78.3M digital budget to TWC direct/premium inventory represents ~$391K in incremental revenue. Frame the conversation around unlocking a meaningful share of the $43M Mobile App line specifically.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Gina for a 30-minute working session before end of month to map Meta's H2 brand calendar against TWC's premium inventory packages — specifically CTV and mobile — with Sophie Ho looped in for the WhatsApp expansion piece.

sales_plays.title: Displace Mobile App Incumbent: Capture Meta's $43M Mobile Budget
sales_plays.summary: Meta's $43M Mobile App spend dwarfs its current TWC investment and is almost certainly flowing to incumbent mobile platforms. With an active PMP relationship as the trust foundation, this play targets Spark Foundry's digital buying team to redirect a portion of that mobile budget to TWC's on-property mobile inventory, displacing a competitor.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a data-led observation to Gina Mazzorana: 'Gina, we can see Meta is running significant mobile app investment — $43M is a meaningful line. I want to understand where that's currently placed and whether TWC's mobile audience, particularly around weather-driven app engagement moments, is in the mix at all.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Which mobile platforms are currently receiving the bulk of that $43M — and what does Meta's current measurement framework look like for mobile app installs or engagement against that spend?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's mobile app audience delivers contextually relevant, high-intent users at moments of active engagement — a differentiated signal compared to broad mobile DSP buys. For Meta's hardware products (Ray-Ban Meta AI glasses, Quest 3S) and AI features, TWC's mobile environment offers brand-safe, premium placement that incumbent performance networks cannot match on quality.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If TWC captures just 5% of Meta's $43M mobile app budget, that is $2.15M in new revenue. Benchmark against the existing PMP relationship (currently booked at $1) to show the magnitude of the whitespace. Present a tiered proposal starting at a $250K test flight to establish mobile baseline metrics.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive audit framing — offer Gina a side-by-side audience quality comparison between TWC mobile inventory and Meta's current mobile partners, deliverable within two weeks, with a specific focus on the Ray-Ban Meta AI glasses target demo.

talk_track_opener: Hey Gina, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together on the Meta PMP for a couple of cycles now — the 2024 and early 2025 deals — and I appreciate the partnership Spark Foundry has built with our team on that. I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a real opportunity we haven't fully explored yet on the broader Meta portfolio.
open_questions: Beyond the PMP structure we have in place, which Meta brand lines — WhatsApp, Quest 3S, Ray-Ban Meta AI glasses, or Meta AI — are Spark Foundry actively planning media for in H2 2025, and are those budgets separate from the core Meta AOR scope?

Who at Meta or Spark Foundry owns the mobile app investment decisions for the $43M mobile line — is that Gina's team directly, or is there a separate performance media buyer we should be speaking with?
What does Meta's current measurement framework look like for mobile app campaigns — are they optimizing on installs, engagement, or brand lift, and does TWC's inventory fit that attribution model?
Is the open Q4 2025 'Meta monetization of app inventory' opportunity something Spark Foundry is aware of and aligned on, or is that being driven from the Meta side directly through Sophie Ho's team?

What would it take for Spark Foundry to move a portion of Meta's mobile or CTV budget from an incumbent platform to TWC on a test basis — is there a minimum flight size or measurement requirement that would need to be met first?

sales_plays.title: Rain Season Direct Buy Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Michelin is an active PMP customer with two consecutive won deals. The CrossClimate2 direct buy was lost in July 2025, creating a clear gap to re-open with a premium direct placement tied to the rain and storm season lead-in window — before competitor tires own the weather moment.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email or call Tamara Douce referencing the two active PMP wins and acknowledge the CrossClimate2 opportunity that closed in July. Say: 'We've had a strong PMP relationship with Michelin since 2024 — I want to make sure we're positioned together for the rain and storm season window before Q4 demand peaks, especially given how weather-driven CrossClimate2 messaging performs on TWC.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Tamara: 'For the CrossClimate2 campaign, is the Q4 rain and storm season already locked into Signal Theory's media plan, or is there still flexibility to layer in a direct contextual buy on top of the existing PMP?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct weather-contextual inventory as the missing layer above the PMP — when a viewer is watching a storm forecast, a CrossClimate2 pre-roll or roadblock delivers message relevance that a programmatic PMP cannot guarantee by context alone.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Michelin's total media spend is $70.4M, with $41.6M in digital. The lost CrossClimate2 direct buy was valued at $100K — a fraction of the digital budget. Frame the ask as less than 0.25% of digital spend to own the highest-intent weather moment of the year.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 20-minute working session with Tamara and Staci Meyer at Signal Theory before August 15 to align on the rain season flight window and review TWC's weather-contextual audience data for CrossClimate2 consideration sets.

sales_plays.title: Shift Paid Social Dollars to Premium Video
sales_plays.summary: Michelin is spending $36.5M in Paid Social — 88% of their $41.6M digital budget — while Online Video sits at only $181K. This imbalance signals an opportunity to displace a portion of social spend with TWC's premium CTV and digital video inventory, where Michelin already has $11.4M in broadcast-adjacent comfort.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Staci Meyer at Signal Theory with a spend-mix observation: 'Looking at Michelin's digital footprint, $36.5M is flowing through Paid Social while Online Video is at $181K — that's a significant concentration. Given Michelin's $11.4M CTV investment, there may be a gap in mid-funnel premium video that TWC can fill with weather-contextual targeting.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Staci: 'Is the heavy Paid Social weighting a deliberate brand strategy for CrossClimate2 and the broader tire portfolio, or is it partly a function of where inventory was available and measurable last year?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium digital video and CTV as a brand-safe, high-attention environment that bridges Michelin's $11.4M CTV investment and their digital budget — capturing in-market tire shoppers during weather events when purchase intent is highest, something Paid Social algorithms cannot time to a storm.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Even a 5% shift from the $36.5M Paid Social budget equals $1.8M in incremental premium video. Against Michelin's $70.4M total spend, that reallocation is negligible in scale but would represent a 10x increase over current Online Video investment and a meaningful new revenue line for TWC.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send Staci a one-page displacement brief with TWC weather-audience reach data against Michelin's tire consideration audience by August 8, then schedule a 30-minute call with both Staci and Tamara Douce to walk through the findings before Signal Theory's Q4 plan is finalized.

talk_track_opener: Hey Tamara, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working with Michelin through the PMP relationship since early 2024 and I wanted to reach out directly before your Q4 planning locks in. We have a strong track record together and I want to make sure we're building on it, not just renewing it.
open_questions: The CrossClimate2 direct buy closed as lost on July 25 — was that a budget timing issue or a targeting/reach concern we should address differently for the rain season window?
Is Tamara Douce the final decision-maker on the CrossClimate2 media plan, or does Staci Meyer at Signal Theory have sign-off authority on the digital mix?

With $36.5M flowing through Paid Social, which platforms are carrying the majority of that spend — and is there a performance threshold where Signal Theory would consider reallocating to premium video?
Does Michelin's Q4 plan already have a weather-triggered or contextual activation strategy for CrossClimate2, or is that a gap in the current approach?

Given the two active PMP deals, is there a preference from Signal Theory or Tamara to keep Michelin's TWC relationship programmatic-only, or is a direct premium buy on the table for the right seasonal moment?

sales_plays.title: Beyond PMP: Expand Micron Into Premium Video & Display
sales_plays.summary: Micron has won 4 consecutive PMP deals with TWC Interactive, but their $898K digital mix shows $102K in Online Video and $46K in Display that is not yet flowing through our inventory. This play converts that existing trust into incremental premium formats.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Katie Mooney referencing the January 2025 PMP renewal win and note that Micron's $102K Online Video and $46K Display spend signals appetite for premium video environments we have not yet captured together. Ask for 20 minutes to share what peers in the semiconductor and tech space are doing with Weather Channel video inventory.
Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Katie: 'Outside of the PMP buys, where is the $102K in Online Video currently running, and what KPIs is MRM optimizing against for those placements?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium contextual video against Micron's AI-enabled memory and data center messaging — weather-triggered video units reaching IT decision-makers and tech-forward consumers during high-attention moments outperform open-exchange video on brand recall.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Micron's current TWC PMP investment is effectively $1 per deal in recorded value — the real opportunity is to align the $102K Online Video and $223K Mobile App budgets (totaling $325K) toward TWC formats, representing a potential 300x+ expansion of active spend on record.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Katie and Dawn Langeland at MRM to join a 20-minute format showcase before end of Q2, specifically to review a TWC video and mobile contextual proposal built around Micron's AI memory product line.

sales_plays.title: Retail Tentpole Planning Window: Lock Q4 Before MRM Commits Budgets
sales_plays.summary: Bombora shows Micron is actively researching Entertainment this week, a plausible signal that planning for retail tentpole moments (back-to-school, holiday, CES adjacency) is underway. This play uses that planning window to get TWC on Micron's Q3-Q4 media plan before MRM finalizes allocations.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Dawn Langeland at MRM directly, noting that Bombora is showing Micron spiking on Entertainment intent this week — a signal that retail and cultural-moment planning may be in motion. Reference the 4 consecutive PMP wins as proof of an established relationship worth building on.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Dawn: 'As you build out Micron's Q3 and Q4 plan, are there retail tentpole windows — back-to-school, holiday tech gifting, or CES — where you need incremental reach beyond the current PMP footprint?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's weather and seasonal contextual targeting aligns naturally with consumer electronics purchase triggers — cold-weather onset, back-to-school prep, and holiday gifting are all moments when memory and storage products see retail lift. Position a Q4 contextual package that connects Micron's product story to those moments.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Micron's $223K Mobile App spend is the largest single digital line item. A Q4 mobile contextual extension through TWC targeting weather-driven retail moments could absorb a meaningful share of that budget with measurable reach incrementality over the existing PMP buys.

Step 5 (Next Step): Send Dawn a one-page Q4 tentpole calendar showing TWC's top retail-adjacent weather moments and request a 15-minute planning call in the next two weeks — before MRM's Q3-Q4 budget lock.

talk_track_opener: Hey Katie, I'm with The Weather Channel — we've been working with Micron through MRM on the TWC Interactive PMP, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think we're only scratching the surface of what we can do together. We've closed four deals going back to mid-2024, and that consistency tells me there's real trust in the relationship worth building on. Here's what caught my attention: Micron is running $102K in Online Video and $223K in Mobile App spend right now, and Bombora is showing active interest in Entertainment content this week — which usually means planning conversations are happening internally. I'd love to ask you one question: when MRM is allocating those video and mobile budgets, is TWC already in the conversation, or are those dollars going elsewhere? I have a specific idea for how we could connect Micron's AI memory story to high-intent consumer moments on our platform, and I think it's worth 20 minutes of your time.
open_questions: Outside of the TWC PMP buys, where is Micron's $102K Online Video budget currently running, and what platform or partner is MRM using for those placements?
Does Katie Mooney or Dawn Langeland own the final decision on expanding the TWC relationship into new formats, or does that require sign-off from a broader media or brand team at Micron HQ in Boise?
Is Micron planning any Q3 or Q4 campaigns tied to retail tentpole moments — back-to-school, holiday tech gifting, or CES — where incremental reach beyond the current PMP would be valuable?
What KPIs is MRM optimizing against for the $223K Mobile App spend, and is contextual relevance or audience targeting the primary lever they are using?
The Bombora signal shows Micron researching Entertainment content this week — is that connected to a specific campaign brief or product launch that we should be aware of as we build a Q3-Q4 proposal?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Direct Upgrade: Unlock Premium Inventory Beyond the Deal
sales_plays.summary: Microsoft has won three consecutive PMP deals (2024, 2025, 2026) plus a LinkedIn DSP PMP, signaling a trusted programmatic relationship. This play converts that momentum into a broader, higher-value direct buy across Weather Channel premium placements — expanding beyond the current $1 deal-value PMPs into named, guaranteed inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the four consecutive won deals — including the January 2026 TWC-Interactive PMP — and open with: 'Kathleen, your team has been activating with us consistently since mid-2024. I want to show you what's sitting just outside the PMP wrapper that your current buys can't access.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Diana Bojaj at Carat USA: 'Beyond the PMP allocations you're currently running, what premium contextual or high-impact placements are you evaluating for Microsoft's brand campaigns in the next planning cycle?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct-sold, high-impact units — homepage takeovers, weather-triggered contextual placements, and branded content integrations — as inventory that is excluded from PMP pools and delivers stronger brand visibility than the current programmatic path.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Microsoft's total media spend is $583.9M trailing 12 months, with $415.9M in digital. The current TWC relationship is captured at nominal PMP value. Even a 0.5% reallocation of the digital budget equals ~$2.1M in incremental direct investment — a concrete anchor for the conversation.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Diana Bojaj for a 30-minute working session before the Q3 planning cycle closes: 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar in the next two weeks to walk through the TWC premium inventory roadmap for H2 2026 — specifically what's not available programmatically?'

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Displacement: Shift Mobile App & Social Dollars to Weather-Contextual
sales_plays.summary: Microsoft allocates $141.7M to Paid Social and $85.5M to Mobile App — two of the largest line items in its digital mix. VML holds the Social AOR. This play targets Diana Bojaj at Carat to redirect a portion of those dollars toward TWC's weather-triggered mobile and contextual units, where Microsoft's automotive and device verticals benefit from real-time demand signals.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a spend observation: 'Diana, I noticed Microsoft is running $141.7M in Paid Social and $85.5M in Mobile App. I want to show you a specific scenario where weather-contextual mobile placements on TWC are outperforming social CPMs for automotive and device categories — and how that maps to Microsoft's current vertical mix.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'For the automotive and consumer device lines within Microsoft's portfolio, how is Carat currently measuring the incremental lift from social and mobile app spend versus contextual or intent-driven placements?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's weather-triggered mobile units deliver contextual relevance that paid social cannot replicate — when a user checks weather before a commute or a road trip, Microsoft's automotive or Surface/device messaging lands in a high-intent, low-clutter environment. This is a direct displacement argument against generic social feed placements managed by VML.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 3% reallocation of the $141.7M Paid Social budget equals ~$4.3M. A 5% shift from the $85.5M Mobile App line equals ~$4.3M. Either scenario represents a significant net-new revenue opportunity for TWC while remaining a rounding error within Microsoft's $583.9M total spend.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer a competitive analysis deliverable: 'I can put together a one-page contextual vs. social efficiency comparison using TWC's automotive-vertical benchmarks — would that be useful to have before your next Microsoft planning review with Kathleen Hall's team?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Diana, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working together on Microsoft's PMP activations since mid-2024, and I'm really glad that relationship has continued into 2026. I wanted to reach out because I think there's a meaningful opportunity we haven't fully explored yet, and I'd rather bring it to you before the next planning cycle locks in. Looking at Microsoft's digital mix — $415.9M in digital spend, with $141.7M in Paid Social and $85.5M in Mobile App — there's a real question worth asking about where weather-contextual inventory fits into that picture, especially for the automotive and device verticals. The PMPs you're running with us are performing, but they're only touching a slice of what TWC can do for Microsoft. Can we find 30 minutes in the next couple of weeks to walk through what's available outside the programmatic wrapper and how it maps to Kathleen Hall's brand priorities for H2?
open_questions: Outside of the current PMP activations, what premium or high-impact placements is Carat evaluating for Microsoft's brand campaigns in the next planning cycle — and is TWC on that list?
Who within Microsoft's brand team — Kathleen Hall's group or a vertical-specific team — owns the decision to move budget from programmatic PMPs into direct-sold inventory?
How is Carat currently benchmarking the performance of Microsoft's $141.7M Paid Social spend against contextual or intent-driven alternatives for the automotive and device verticals?
Are there specific seasonal or weather-driven demand windows — winter driving, spring car-buying — where Microsoft's automotive campaigns are currently underinvested or underperforming?

Given that VML holds the Social AOR separately, how does Carat coordinate with VML when evaluating cross-channel reallocation opportunities that might shift dollars away from social into contextual or display?

sales_plays.title: Displace Programmatic Display with Premium Direct
sales_plays.summary: Minted is spending $1.4M on Internet Display programmatically with no visible direct publisher relationships. This play positions a premium, brand-safe direct buy as a quality and efficiency upgrade over open-exchange inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Ryan Kennedy referencing Minted's $1.4M Internet Display spend and note that open-exchange programmatic often delivers artist-brand creative in low-quality, non-contextual placements — a real risk for a design-forward brand like Minted.
Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Ryan: 'Where does your current display spend actually run, and do you have visibility into the editorial context surrounding Minted's creative on those placements?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position direct premium inventory as the antidote — guaranteed brand-safe, contextually relevant placements alongside design, home décor, and lifestyle editorial that mirrors Minted's product aesthetic and customer mindset.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame a pilot at 10-15% of their $1.4M display budget (~$140K-$210K) as a controlled test: same audience, measurably higher viewability and brand recall versus open-exchange benchmarks, with full placement transparency.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Ryan for a 20-minute call in the next two weeks to walk through a side-by-side placement quality audit — offer to bring a one-page inventory map showing where Minted's creative would live versus where it likely runs today.

sales_plays.title: Q4 Tentpole Planning Window — Get in Before the Budget Locks
sales_plays.summary: Minted's $7.3M digital spend and $3.5M Online Video budget are heavily indexed toward a brand that peaks at holiday gifting and cultural moments (weddings, graduations, Valentine's Day). With retail tentpole planning typically beginning 90-120 days out, a mid-summer outreach positions this rep ahead of Q4 budget commitments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Ryan Kennedy now — before Q4 plans lock — referencing Minted's $3.5M Online Video investment and the fact that holiday stationery and art gifting is one of the most competitive retail windows of the year. Note that Bombora shows Minted actively researching Email Marketing this week, signaling active planning mode.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Ryan: 'As you plan Q4 — holiday cards, gift wrap, art prints — how are you allocating your Online Video and Display budgets between always-on programmatic and reserved tentpole placements, and is that mix already locked?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position premium video and display inventory reserved specifically around holiday gifting, home décor, and design-culture editorial as a way to own the contextual moment when Minted's target buyer is actively in a gifting and decorating mindset — not just retargeted after the fact.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): With $3.5M in Online Video already committed annually, even a 10% reallocation (~$350K) into reserved Q4 tentpole placements could reduce auction-driven CPM inflation that typically spikes 30-50% in November-December, protecting both reach and budget efficiency.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Ryan for a 30-minute Q4 planning conversation before end of this month — offer to bring a tentpole calendar showing key cultural moments (holiday, Valentine's Day, wedding season) mapped to inventory availability and audience index data relevant to Minted's buyer.

talk_track_opener: Hey Ryan, I'm [Name] from [Publisher]. We work with a number of design-forward and direct-to-consumer retail brands helping them move their digital investment from open-exchange programmatic into premium, contextually relevant placements — and I came across Minted because your creative and brand positioning are genuinely distinctive in the market. When I looked at Minted's media mix, the $1.4M in Internet Display and $3.5M in Online Video stood out — that's a meaningful investment, and for a brand built on the quality and artistry of independent designers, where that creative actually appears matters a lot. I also noticed Minted is actively researching Email Marketing right now, which tells me your team is in planning mode. I'd love to ask you one question: as you look ahead to Q4 and the holiday gifting window, do you have a sense of how much of that video and display budget is already reserved in premium placements versus running open-auction — and is that mix something you're still shaping?
open_questions: Your Internet Display spend sits at $1.4M — is that running through a managed DSP, an agency trading desk, or direct publisher relationships, and do you have full visibility into where Minted's creative actually appears?
With Ryan owning brand awareness, who on your team owns the performance and paid social budget — and are brand and performance planned together or in separate budget cycles?

Minted's business has clear seasonal peaks around holiday gifting, Valentine's Day, and wedding season — how far in advance does your team typically lock Q4 media plans, and have those conversations started yet for this year?
Your Search spend at $15M is roughly 4x your combined Display and Video investment — is there a strategic push to grow upper-funnel brand investment, or is search still the primary growth lever?
We don't have an agency on file for Minted's media buying — is your digital media planned and bought in-house, or is there an agency partner we should be looping in for any proposal conversations?

sales_plays.title: Holiday Win-Back via BarkleyOKRP
sales_plays.summary: Missouri Lottery ran a $50K Holiday 2024 opportunity with TWC-Interactive that was lost. BarkleyOKRP holds full AOR scope including media buying. Re-engage through Dan Fromm before the next seasonal window to understand why the deal fell through and reposition.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Dan Fromm at BarkleyOKRP referencing the Holiday 2024 TWC-Interactive opportunity ($50K) that didn't close. Acknowledge the outcome directly: 'We didn't earn Missouri Lottery's business last holiday season — I'd like to understand why and show you what's changed.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Dan Fromm: 'What specifically fell short on the Holiday 2024 proposal — was it targeting reach, pricing, or fit with Rhonda's campaign goals?' Identify the exact objection that killed the $50K deal.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): If the loss was on reach or targeting, position against the $25K digital-only spend (100% Online Video, zero Display or Mobile App) — Missouri Lottery is underinvesting in digital relative to $1.2M broadcast, and there is clear whitespace in Display and Mobile App to capture incremental audiences.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the gap concretely: Missouri Lottery spends 98% of tracked media budget ($1.2M) on broadcast and only 2% ($25K) on digital. A rebalanced digital investment starting at the prior $50K proposal level represents a 100% increase in digital spend with measurable incremental reach beyond the broadcast audience.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Dan Fromm for a 20-minute call before the end of the month to walk through a revised proposal — one that directly addresses the Holiday 2024 objections — so BarkleyOKRP has options in hand before the next planning cycle opens.

sales_plays.title: Education Intent — Digital Expansion Play
sales_plays.summary: Bombora shows Missouri Lottery actively researching 'Education' this week. Missouri Lottery is constitutionally tied to education funding — this intent signal likely reflects a campaign or messaging initiative. With $0 in Display and $0 in Mobile App spend on record, there is a genuine new-format opportunity to open.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Rhonda Fehr directly, referencing the Missouri Lottery's education funding mission and noting that intent data shows elevated research activity around Education topics this week. Open with: 'We're seeing strong audience interest in education-related content right now — timing that aligns with Missouri Lottery's core funding story.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Rhonda: 'Is Missouri Lottery running or planning a campaign that connects lottery proceeds to education outcomes — and if so, which audiences and formats are you prioritizing beyond your current broadcast buy?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): With $0 allocated to Display and $0 to Mobile App in the current digital breakdown, position Display and Mobile as net-new formats that can extend the education narrative to audiences who are actively consuming education content — audiences already identified via Bombora intent.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Missouri Lottery's current digital footprint is $25K (Online Video only). Adding Display and Mobile App at even a modest $25K each would double digital investment to $75K while still representing less than 6% of the $1.2M broadcast budget — a low-risk test with measurable reach expansion.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send Rhonda a one-page audience brief showing education-intent reach available in Missouri this month, and request a 15-minute call with her and Dan Fromm together to align on whether this fits an active or upcoming campaign.

talk_track_opener: Hey Dan, this is [Rep Name] from [Company]. We worked with BarkleyOKRP on the Missouri Lottery Holiday 2024 campaign — that was the $50K TWC-Interactive opportunity. I know that one didn't land, and I'm not going to pretend otherwise. I wanted to reach out because I'd genuinely like to understand what we missed, and I think there's a real conversation worth having before the next planning cycle. Here's what caught my attention: Missouri Lottery is spending $1.2 million in broadcast and only $25,000 in digital — all of it in Online Video, with zero in Display or Mobile App. At the same time, our intent data is showing Missouri Lottery actively researching Education topics right now, which tracks with their funding mission. That's a signal worth acting on before a competitor does. Can we find 20 minutes this week so I can show you what a revised digital proposal would look like — one built around what Rhonda actually needs?
open_questions: What specifically caused the Holiday 2024 TWC-Interactive proposal to lose — was it pricing, targeting capabilities, or a shift in Missouri Lottery's campaign priorities?
Does Rhonda Fehr or BarkleyOKRP own the final call on digital format selection, or does the Missouri Lottery Commission require additional approval for new vendor categories?
Is the current Education intent activity tied to a specific campaign Missouri Lottery is planning, or is it background research for a future fiscal year initiative?

Given that Missouri Lottery currently spends $0 in Display and $0 in Mobile App, has BarkleyOKRP recommended testing those formats and been held back by budget constraints or internal preference for broadcast?
What does Missouri Lottery's planning calendar look like for the next major seasonal window — and when does BarkleyOKRP typically need proposals in hand to influence the media mix?

sales_plays.title: Digital-to-Direct Upgrade: Scale the PMP Win
sales_plays.summary: Mitsubishi Electric is already a paying customer via the TWC Interactive PMP closed Feb 2025. With $7.4M in digital and $252K in CTV spend, there is clear budget and appetite to move from programmatic access into premium direct placements and broader Weather Channel inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the active PMP relationship directly — 'Jason, Zak — the TWC Interactive PMP we activated in February is live and delivering. I want to show you what performance looks like when we layer in direct-sold premium placements on top of that programmatic foundation, especially given Mitsubishi Electric's $252K CTV line and the $7.4M digital footprint you're managing across Brunner and Launch.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Zak Cochran at Brunner: 'How is the current PMP being measured against your KPIs, and is there a threshold of performance that would unlock incremental budget from the $7.4M digital pool for a direct buy?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's owned-and-operated inventory — weather-triggered display, video, and CTV — as the natural next step above the PMP floor. Mitsubishi Electric's HVAC and automotive electronics lines are directly tied to weather-driven consumer behavior, making contextual adjacency on Weather Channel a brand-safe, high-relevance environment that programmatic alone cannot guarantee.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The current PMP closed at $1 (likely a placeholder/test). With $252K already allocated to CTV and $91K in display, a direct buy proposal in the $150K–$300K range represents less than 4% of the $7.4M digital budget — a low-risk expansion with measurable lift in brand-safe contextual reach for HVAC and automotive electronics.
Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Zak to schedule a 30-minute performance review of the PMP by end of Q2, with Jason Rosenthal looped in, to align on a Q3 direct buy proposal before summer HVAC demand peaks.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Waste with Weather-Contextual Video
sales_plays.summary: Mitsubishi Electric is spending $6.9M on Paid Social — the single largest line in their $12.5M total media budget. This is a displacement opportunity to redirect a portion of that spend toward contextually relevant, weather-triggered video and CTV inventory where purchase intent for HVAC and automotive electronics is demonstrably higher.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a spend observation to Zak Cochran at Brunner: 'Zak, I was looking at Mitsubishi Electric's media mix — $6.9M in Paid Social is doing a lot of heavy lifting in a $12.5M total budget. I want to have a conversation about whether that allocation is optimized for the moments when consumers are actually in-market for HVAC systems and automotive electronics, because we're seeing a real gap between social impression volume and contextual purchase intent.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'What does Brunner's current attribution model show for Paid Social performance on the HVAC and automotive electronics lines specifically — are you seeing lower-funnel conversion, or is it primarily an awareness vehicle at that $6.9M level?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered online video ($44K current spend is vastly underweighted) and CTV ($252K) as a contextual alternative. When a cold snap or heat wave hits a key DMA, Mitsubishi Electric's HVAC message on Weather Channel reaches a consumer actively searching for a solution — something a social feed algorithm cannot replicate. Launch (creative/digital agency) is already in the ecosystem and can adapt existing creative.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Shifting even 5% of the $6.9M Paid Social budget — roughly $345K — into weather-triggered video and CTV on TWC would represent a 6x increase over the current $44K online video spend and a meaningful scale of the $252K CTV line, all within a contextually verified, brand-safe environment tied directly to Mitsubishi Electric's core product demand cycles.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive analysis deck — delivered to Zak Cochran within two weeks — benchmarking Paid Social CPMs and engagement rates against TWC contextual video performance for HVAC and automotive electronics advertisers, with a specific Q3 weather-window planning hook for summer cooling season.

talk_track_opener: Hey Jason, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working with your team and Brunner since the TWC Interactive PMP closed in February, and I wanted to connect directly with you given the scale of what Mitsubishi Electric is doing in market. With over $12.5M in total media spend and a really significant digital footprint across search, social, and CTV, you're clearly investing heavily in reaching consumers at the right moments. We work with a number of HVAC and automotive electronics brands, and the contextual relevance we can deliver — especially around weather-driven demand windows for heating and cooling systems — is something that's hard to replicate in a social feed or a programmatic exchange. I wanted to ask you directly: as Brunner and Launch are planning the back half of 2025, is there a conversation happening about how the current PMP performance maps to broader goals for the HVAC line, and whether there's appetite to move into more premium, direct placements before summer demand kicks in?
open_questions: How is Brunner currently measuring the performance of the TWC Interactive PMP that went live in February — what KPIs are you tracking and how is it pacing against goals?

With $6.9M allocated to Paid Social, how does Brunner's attribution model differentiate performance for the HVAC line versus automotive electronics — are those treated as separate campaigns with separate KPIs?
Who at Mitsubishi Electric — Jason Rosenthal or someone on his team — owns the decision to reallocate budget between channels mid-year, and what does that approval process look like through Brunner?
Is there a Q3 planning cycle underway for summer HVAC demand, and has Brunner modeled any weather-triggered or contextual media scenarios for that window?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Direct Upgrade: Convert Programmatic Wins into Premium Inventory
sales_plays.summary: Mitsubishi has won three consecutive PMP deals (2024, 2025, 2026) through TWC-Interactive, signaling a reliable programmatic relationship. The opportunity is to convert that PMP spend into a higher-value direct or guaranteed buy that unlocks premium placements, audience guarantees, and creative formats not available in the PMP stack.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the active 2026 PMP win (closed 2026-02-20) in your outreach to Suzanne Irving at OMD. Acknowledge the multi-year PMP commitment and frame the conversation as a natural next step: 'You've now run three consecutive PMP cycles with us — I want to show you what a direct buy unlocks that the PMP can't deliver.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Suzanne: 'With Mitsubishi's $4.7M CTV and $4.2M online video budget, are there specific audience segments or daypart guarantees that the current PMP structure isn't consistently delivering against?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct/guaranteed inventory against the PMP's inherent variability — guaranteed impressions in high-demand weather and seasonal windows (spring/fall automotive consideration peaks) that programmatic floors can't protect.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Mitsubishi's total tracked spend is $20.9M. CTV alone is $4.7M. A direct buy capturing even 15% of that CTV budget (~$705K) at premium CPMs would deliver guaranteed reach against in-market auto shoppers during demand windows — quantifiable lift over the PMP's variable delivery.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Suzanne for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to map the 2026 PMP flight dates against TWC's premium inventory calendar and identify one specific campaign window to pilot a direct buy alongside the existing PMP.

sales_plays.title: Reclaim the Lost PG: Displace Q3 2025 Competitor with a Stronger Guaranteed Buy
sales_plays.summary: Mitsubishi's Q3 2025 Programmatic Guaranteed opportunity (valued at $50,000) was lost. That budget went to a competing platform or publisher. With the 2026 PMP already won, there is a clear opening to re-engage OMD and Critical Mass on a Q3 2026 PG deal before the competitor re-entrenches.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the lost PG_Q3_2025 deal directly with Suzanne Irving: 'We lost the Q3 2025 PG opportunity — I'd like to understand what won that business and make sure we're in the conversation earlier for Q3 2026.' This signals accountability and positions you as a partner, not just a vendor.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'What did the winning platform offer on the Q3 2025 PG that we didn't match — was it pricing, audience scale, creative format, or something else on the measurement side?' This surfaces the exact displacement criteria.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Use the answer to position TWC's PG product against the incumbent's specific advantage. If it was audience scale, lead with TWC's weather-driven auto intender data. If it was measurement, lead with attribution capabilities tied to Mitsubishi's $2.2M mobile app and $1.5M paid social spend as cross-channel proof points.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost Q3 2025 PG was $50,000. Winning that back in Q3 2026 — and layering it on top of the active 2026 PMP — grows the TWC relationship from a single programmatic line to a multi-product investment. Frame it as: 'We're already your PMP partner; adding the PG gets you guaranteed delivery on top of your existing $20.9M media plan without adding a new vendor relationship.'

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a specific debrief call with Suzanne Irving and, if appropriate, loop in Kimberly Ito's team at Mitsubishi HQ in Franklin, TN — propose a 20-minute debrief on Q3 2025 outcomes and a forward-looking Q3 2026 planning conversation, targeted for no later than 6 weeks before the Q3 flight start.

talk_track_opener: Hey Suzanne, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together on Mitsubishi's PMP buys for three consecutive years now — the 2024, 2025, and the 2026 deal we just closed in February — so I wanted to reach out as a partner, not just to check a box. I also know we lost the Q3 2025 PG to someone else, and I'd rather have that conversation directly than pretend it didn't happen. Here's what I'm seeing: Mitsubishi is running $4.7M in CTV and $4.2M in online video, and the PMP has been a reliable foundation — but I think there's a real gap between what the PMP delivers and what a direct or guaranteed buy could unlock, especially around the seasonal demand windows where automotive consideration spikes. I'd love to spend 30 minutes with you mapping the 2026 flight calendar against our premium inventory and figuring out whether a PG or direct buy alongside the existing PMP makes sense. Would you have time in the next couple of weeks?
open_questions: When the Q3 2025 PG went to another platform, was the decision driven by pricing, audience targeting capabilities, or measurement/attribution — and is that same criteria in play for Q3 2026 planning?

With OMD managing media planning and buying and Critical Mass owning digital AOR, who has final sign-off on a programmatic guaranteed buy — does that decision sit with Suzanne Irving's team or does it require alignment with Critical Mass?

Mitsubishi's CTV spend is $4.7M — how much of that is currently running through guaranteed or reserved buys versus open auction and PMP, and is there a target ratio Kimberly Ito's team has set for 2026?

Are there specific Mitsubishi model launches or regional dealer campaigns in 2026 where weather-driven audience targeting would be a relevant planning input — particularly for the SUV and pickup truck lines?

Critical Mass holds the digital AOR scope covering mobile app and paid social — is there an active conversation happening between OMD and Critical Mass about consolidating digital video buying, and would TWC's online video inventory be relevant to that discussion?

sales_plays.title: Win Back the FY26 Brand Campaign
sales_plays.summary: Moffitt lost a $60K TWC-Interactive brand campaign opportunity in July 2025. This play re-engages Josh Adkins by acknowledging the loss, diagnosing what went wrong, and repositioning with a sharper value case tied to Moffitt's $12.2M total media footprint.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email or call Josh Adkins directly. Reference the FY26 Brand Campaign opp by name: 'Josh, I know we didn't earn the business on the FY26 Brand Campaign this past July — I wanted to reach out personally to understand what we missed and whether there's a path forward for the remainder of the fiscal year.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask the single most important diagnostic question: 'When the team evaluated the TWC-Interactive proposal, what was the deciding factor that moved the budget elsewhere — was it reach, format, pricing, or something about how we framed the audience?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Based on the answer, reposition against the specific gap. If reach was the issue, lead with Tampa DMA broadcast scale. If digital ROI was the concern, highlight that Moffitt's current CTV spend is only $79K against $10.2M in broadcast — an imbalance where TWC's CTV inventory can extend reach to cord-cutters already searching for cancer care.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the math: Moffitt spends $12.2M in total media annually. The lost opp was $60K — less than 0.5% of their total budget. Position a revised proposal as a low-risk test: 'A $60–80K digital or CTV package is a rounding error against your $12.2M footprint, but it gives us a chance to prove performance before a larger FY27 commitment.'

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 20-minute call in the next two weeks: 'Could we get 20 minutes before end of August to walk through a revised approach? I'll come with a specific audience and format recommendation — no deck, just a conversation.'

sales_plays.title: Unlock the Digital Imbalance Opportunity
sales_plays.summary: Moffitt's digital spend is heavily skewed toward Mobile App ($199K) and Search ($423K) with minimal investment in CTV ($79K), Online Video ($47K), and Paid Social ($45K). This play targets Josh Adkins with a net-new digital video and CTV proposal that addresses the underinvestment in upper-funnel awareness formats relative to Moffitt's $10.2M broadcast base.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a data-driven observation in the outreach: 'Josh, I was looking at how Moffitt allocates media across formats. You're running $10.2M in broadcast and $423K in search — but CTV is at $79K and online video at $47K. That gap is where patient audiences are increasingly unreachable by traditional TV alone. I'd like to show you what that looks like in the Tampa DMA.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Is the current CTV and digital video budget a deliberate choice to stay lower-funnel, or is it more a function of how the media plan was built historically — and is that something you're open to revisiting?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's CTV and digital video inventory as the bridge between Moffitt's strong broadcast presence and the cord-cutting patient population in Tampa. Emphasize that cancer care decisions are high-consideration and that upper-funnel video reach — especially in a health-anxious audience — drives downstream search volume, which Moffitt is already capturing at $423K.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Make the ROI concrete: 'If shifting even 10% of your broadcast budget — roughly $1M — into CTV and digital video extended your reach to cord-cutters in the Tampa DMA, you'd be covering a patient segment your current $79K CTV spend is largely missing. We can model that reach delta before you commit to anything.'

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer a specific, low-commitment deliverable: 'Let me put together a 1-page reach analysis showing the cord-cutter gap in the Tampa DMA for Moffitt's target patient demo. I can have it to you by end of this week — would that be useful to share with your team?'

talk_track_opener: Josh, I'm [Rep Name] with [TWC/Spectrum Reach] — we work with health systems and cancer centers across Florida, and we had a conversation with your team earlier this year around the FY26 Brand Campaign. I know we didn't land that one, and I'm not calling to relitigate it. I wanted to reach out because I've been looking at how Moffitt allocates its $12.2M in media spend, and I think there's a specific gap worth a conversation.
open_questions: When the FY26 Brand Campaign decision was made in July, what ultimately drove the budget to a different partner — was it audience targeting, pricing, format, or something about how the proposal was structured?
Is Josh Adkins the sole decision-maker on digital and CTV media buys, or does a media agency or internal media team influence those allocations?
Is Moffitt's CTV budget of $79K a deliberate cap for this fiscal year, or is it more of a legacy allocation that hasn't been revisited as cord-cutting has grown in the Tampa market?
With $423K in paid search already running, is there a view internally on how upper-funnel video or CTV spend influences downstream search volume and patient acquisition cost?
Does Moffitt work with an AOR or media buying agency for its $12.2M in annual spend, and if so, who manages the Tampa DMA broadcast and digital relationships?

sales_plays.title: Summer Heat Lead-In: Coors Light Premium Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Molson Coors is an active customer with a live FY26 All Brands Interact deal at $150K. With summer heat season approaching and Coors Light's core 'cold' brand equity, now is the time to expand that relationship into premium on-property placements before competitors lock up summer inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the active FY26 All Brands Interact deal ($150K) and the two Leinenkugel Magnite wins from Feb 2024. Tell Sofia Colucci or Chioma Aduba at Publicis Media: 'We have active momentum together heading into summer — I want to make sure Coors Light has first right of refusal on our highest-impact summer heat inventory before it's gone.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Chioma Aduba: 'For Coors Light's summer push, is the team prioritizing reach extension through programmatic, or are there specific on-property premium placements — like weather-triggered units — that Publicis Media is trying to secure direct?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position Weather Channel's weather-triggered ad units as the natural upgrade from the current programmatic Magnite path — Coors Light's 'cold' messaging activates precisely when temperatures spike, delivering contextual relevance that standard digital display ($6.4M) and online video ($776K) buys cannot replicate.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The current FY26 deal is $150K. The two lost Coors Light opportunities from Aug 2025 totaled $115K. Framing a weather-triggered summer package at $200–250K recaptures that lost value and layers premium on-property inventory on top of the existing Interact commitment — a net expansion of 33–67% over current spend.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute working session with Chioma Aduba and the Publicis Media planning team before end of April to map Coors Light's summer flight dates against our weather trigger windows — 'I can bring a heat-index forecast overlay for your top DMAs so we can build the brief together.'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Spend with Weather-Contextual Video
sales_plays.summary: Molson Coors is investing $33.7M in Paid Social — the single largest digital line item — with only $776K in Online Video. Given Bombora intent signals around Entertainment and the brand's need for contextual relevance, there is a clear case to redirect a portion of social spend toward premium weather-contextual video on TWC properties.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the data gap: 'Molson Coors is spending $33.7M in Paid Social but only $776K in Online Video. Given that Coors Light and Miller Lite are summer-occasion brands, I want to show Sofia Colucci's team how weather-triggered video is converting for CPG beverage advertisers at a fraction of the CPM waste in social feeds right now.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Chioma Aduba at Publicis Media: 'When Droga5 and Arc Worldwide are building creative for summer campaigns, are they developing video assets that could be activated contextually — or is most of the video budget locked into social platform placements where you have less control over the moment of delivery?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's weather-contextual video inventory places Coors Light and Vizzy Hard Seltzer creative at the exact moment a consumer checks the forecast before a weekend cookout or beach day — a high-intent entertainment moment that aligns directly with Bombora's current Entertainment interest signal for Molson Coors. This is a displacement of low-context social impressions with high-context video moments.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Even a 3% reallocation of the $33.7M Paid Social budget equals ~$1M in incremental video spend. Benchmarked against the $776K current Online Video baseline, that represents a 2x+ increase in video investment with measurable weather-occasion lift data TWC can provide post-campaign — a proof point Publicis Media can take back to Sofia Colucci.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send Chioma Aduba a one-page CPG beverage case study showing weather-triggered video performance vs. social video benchmarks by end of this week, then schedule a 20-minute call the following week to discuss whether Vizzy or Coors Light is the right test brand for a Q2 pilot.

talk_track_opener: Hey Chioma, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working together on the FY26 All Brands Interact deal and I wanted to get ahead of something before your summer planning locks in. We've had real wins together, including the Leinenkugel Magnite buys in early 2024, and I know Publicis Media is managing a significant portfolio across Coors Light, Miller Lite, and Vizzy heading into the heat season. I want to make sure we're in the brief early this year rather than coming in after budgets are set.
open_questions: The Coors Light Aug 2025 deal was lost — was that a budget timing issue, a competitive displacement, or a creative/format mismatch we should address differently for summer 2026?

sales_plays.title: Brand Family Expansion: From PMP to Premium Direct
sales_plays.summary: Mondelez is an active TWC customer with two won PMP deals (Jan 2026, Feb 2025) but lost direct campaign buys for Oreo and Halls in Q4 2024-2025. The opportunity is to convert PMP-only spend into premium direct placements across the broader Mondelez brand portfolio — Cadbury, belVita, Trident — not just Oreo and Halls.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two active PMP wins (Jan 2026 PMP, Feb 2025 PMP) with Ali Amarsy at Digitas: 'Ali, we've had two consecutive PMP deals with Mondelez and I want to make sure we're maximizing what that inventory can do for the full brand portfolio — not just the brands that have activated so far.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Ali or Martin Renaud: 'Beyond Oreo and Halls, which Mondelez brands — Cadbury, belVita, Trident — have Q2 or Q3 tentpole moments on the plan right now that don't yet have a weather or contextual layer attached?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's contextual targeting against Mondelez's $97.1M digital footprint — specifically the $84.1M in Paid Social — by showing how weather-triggered creative on TWC drives incremental reach that social algorithms can't replicate for snack purchase intent moments (rain days for comfort snacks, cold snaps for hot cocoa pairings with Cadbury).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost Q4 2025 direct buy was $50K and the lost Halls Q4 2024 buy was $200K — a combined $250K that went elsewhere. Reframe: 'If we can recover even one of those seasonal brand activations and layer in two additional Mondelez brands at similar scale, we're looking at $400K-$600K in incremental direct spend on top of your existing PMP commitment.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we set up a 30-minute brand planning session with you and the Digitas team before end of February to map Mondelez's Q2-Q3 tentpole calendar against TWC's contextual weather windows? I'll bring a brand-by-brand opportunity brief.'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars with Weather-Contextual Video
sales_plays.summary: Mondelez is allocating $84.1M to Paid Social versus only $623K to Online Video and $227K to Display — a heavily social-skewed digital mix. This imbalance is a displacement opportunity: redirect a portion of social budget into TWC's premium video and contextual environments where snack purchase intent is weather-driven and brand-safe.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with Martin Renaud or Ali Amarsy at Digitas with a data observation: 'I was looking at Mondelez's digital mix — $84.1M in Paid Social against $623K in Online Video. For a snack portfolio driven by impulse and occasion, that ratio leaves a lot of contextual purchase-intent moments on the table that social feeds don't capture.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When Digitas evaluates the Paid Social allocation for brands like Oreo or Cadbury, what's the primary KPI driving that channel weighting — is it reach, frequency, or lower-funnel conversion, and has anyone stress-tested whether weather-contextual video can hit the same KPI at a comparable CPM?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium video inventory as a brand-safe, contextually relevant alternative: snack consumption spikes on rainy days, cold fronts, and holiday eves — moments TWC can target in real time that Meta and TikTok algorithms cannot. The Oreo 'StayPlayful' campaign that was lost in Nov 2024 is a direct example of a playful, moment-driven brief that fits TWC's contextual video environment.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 3-5% reallocation of the $84.1M Paid Social budget equals $2.5M-$4.2M in redirected spend. Even a conservative test at $500K in TWC Online Video would represent an 80x increase over the current $623K Online Video line and give Digitas a clean A/B data set against social performance.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to put together a one-page displacement test proposal — $500K pilot, Q2 flight, Oreo or Cadbury as the test brand — and share it with you and the Digitas team by end of next week. Does Thursday work for a 20-minute walk-through?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Martin — I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel. We've been working with the Digitas team on Mondelez's programmatic strategy for the past two years, and we just closed the 2026 PMP together in January, so I want to make sure we're building on that momentum rather than just renewing the same line item year over year. We've got real skin in the game with this account and I want to earn more of it. What I'm seeing in Mondelez's media mix is a $97M digital footprint that's almost entirely concentrated in Paid Social — $84M out of $97M — with very little going into contextual video environments where snack purchase intent is actually highest. For a portfolio like yours — Oreo, Cadbury, Halls, belVita — there are specific weather moments, cold snaps, rainy afternoons, holiday eves, where consumers are actively in a snack mindset and TWC is the only premium environment that can reach them in that exact context. I'd love to ask you one question: beyond Oreo and Halls, which Mondelez brands are on the Q2-Q3 plan right now that don't yet have a contextual or weather layer built in?
open_questions: The Oreo StayPlayful campaign brief from November 2024 felt like a natural fit for contextual, moment-driven media — what ultimately drove that budget to a different channel, and is that brief still active for 2025 or 2026?

Ali, when Digitas evaluates the $84.1M Paid Social allocation for Mondelez brands, who at Mondelez owns the decision to test or shift budget into new digital channels — is that Martin Renaud's office or a brand-level CMO?
Which Mondelez brands outside of Oreo and Halls have Q2 or Q3 tentpole moments on the 2026 plan that don't yet have a contextual or weather-triggered media layer?

The Halls Q4 2024 direct buy was $200K and went to a competitor — what would Halls need to see in a TWC proposal to bring that back for Q4 2026, and is the decision made at the brand level or through Digitas?

Given that Cadbury's Easter window is one of the biggest retail tentpoles in the Mondelez calendar, is there a planning conversation happening right now for Q1-Q2 2027 where TWC's contextual targeting could be included from the start rather than added late?

Open with: 'We've seen wealth management brands reallocate 10-15% of programmatic display budgets to direct, contextually aligned environments and see measurable lifts in brand recall and qualified lead volume — wanted to share what that looks like for a brand at Morgan Stanley's scale.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Chaz: 'Of the $32.8M in display and $21.9M in online video Morgan Stanley is running programmatically, what percentage is currently hitting verified high-net-worth or mass-affluent audience segments versus broad reach buys?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position on-property direct inventory as the antidote to programmatic waste — our audience is verified, contextually relevant to financial decision-making, and brand-safe by design, which directly addresses the brand safety and audience quality risks inherent in open-exchange buys at this spend level.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the math: even a 10% reallocation of the $54.7M combined display + online video budget equals $5.47M in redirected spend — enough to run a meaningful always-on direct campaign. sales_plays.title: Julie Collins Warm Path via Agency Advocate
sales_plays.summary: With Julie Collins holding the title of Chief Operations Officer, Executive Director & Head of Global Media & Web Governance at Morgan Stanley, she is the internal decision-maker who sets media governance standards. This play uses a relationship-first approach — building credibility with Chaz Wilson at Mediahub first, then requesting a warm introduction to Julie to align on governance, brand standards, and direct partnership criteria.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): After an initial exchange with Chaz Wilson, reference the governance angle directly: 'Given that Julie Collins oversees global media and web governance at Morgan Stanley, I imagine any new direct media partnership needs to clear a formal evaluation process. Has your team had a chance to bring direct publisher relationships through that process recently?'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Chaz: 'What does Julie's evaluation framework look like for approving new direct media partners — is it primarily brand safety, audience verification, reporting standards, or something else?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a direct partnership as governance-friendly: we offer transparent reporting, first-party audience verification, brand-safe editorial environments, and dedicated account management — all of which reduce the operational and reputational risk that a Head of Media Governance is accountable for.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Note that Morgan Stanley's $224M total media footprint means even a pilot direct buy of $1-2M represents less than 1% of annual spend — a low-risk proof point that fits within a governance-approved test-and-learn framework.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Chaz to facilitate a brief intro call or email introduction to Julie Collins, framed as a governance and partnership standards conversation — not a sales pitch. Suggest a 15-minute call within the next three weeks, with Chaz optionally present.

talk_track_opener: Hey Chaz, I'm reaching out because we work with a number of wealth management and financial services brands running at scale similar to Morgan Stanley — helping them get more out of their digital investment by moving dollars from open-exchange programmatic into direct, audience-verified environments. We've seen real results when brands at this spend level make even modest shifts away from broad display buys toward contextually aligned, on-property placements. I wanted to connect with you specifically because of your role on the Morgan Stanley account at Mediahub. Looking at the shape of their digital spend — particularly the $32.8M in display and $21.9M in online video — there's a real question worth exploring around how much of that is actually reaching verified high-net-worth or mass-affluent audiences versus broad programmatic reach. I'd love to spend 20 minutes walking through an audience overlap analysis to see if there's a fit. Would you have time in the next couple of weeks?
open_questions: Of the $32.8M Morgan Stanley is spending on internet display, how much is running on open exchange versus private marketplace deals — and is there a mandate to shift more toward curated or direct inventory?
Does Julie Collins' global media and web governance role include approving new direct publisher partnerships, or does that decision sit with Chaz and the Mediahub planning team?
With Mediahub Worldwide Boston also involved in media planning, which office owns the digital channel strategy for Morgan Stanley's wealth management brand campaigns specifically?
Morgan Stanley is spending $17.6M on paid social — is that budget being evaluated separately from display and video, or is there a unified digital audience strategy that connects all three channels?

Has Morgan Stanley set any brand safety or audience verification requirements that currently limit which programmatic exchanges or publishers are eligible to receive their display and online video spend?

sales_plays.title: PMP to Premium Direct: Expand Motley Fool's TWC Footprint
sales_plays.summary: Motley Fool has won two consecutive TWC Interactive PMP deals (July 2024 and January 2025). This play converts that programmatic relationship into a higher-value direct buy, unlocking premium placements and audience targeting unavailable in the PMP layer.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Rebekah Hughes referencing the two consecutive TWC Interactive PMP wins (July 2024, January 2025) and open with: 'You've now run two successful PMP campaigns with us — I want to show you what's sitting just above that layer that your current setup can't access.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Rebekah: 'With Stock Advisor and Epic driving your paid membership growth, are your current PMP buys hitting the right investor-intent audience at the frequency you need, or are you seeing diminishing returns on the same segments?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct premium inventory — weather-contextual placements, homepage takeovers, and first-party financial audience segments — as the logical upgrade from PMP, offering guaranteed impressions, brand-safe environments, and richer targeting against the 9.6M digital and $207.9M search-heavy media mix Motley Fool already runs.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Motley Fool's digital display spend is currently only $800K against a $9.6M digital budget and $207.9M in search. Even a modest reallocation of 5% of search budget into premium direct display represents roughly $10.4M in incremental opportunity — frame the TWC direct buy as a high-efficiency complement, not a cost add.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Rebekah for a 20-minute call in the next two weeks to walk through a custom audience overlap report showing how TWC's first-party financial-intent users index against Motley Fool's Stock Advisor subscriber profile.

sales_plays.title: Displace the Search-Heavy Mix: Shift Budget from Search to TWC Premium
sales_plays.summary: Motley Fool's media mix is overwhelmingly search-dependent ($207.9M in search vs. $800K in display). This play challenges the efficiency of that concentration and positions TWC premium inventory as a brand-building and audience-expansion vehicle that search cannot replicate.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a data-led observation to Rebekah Hughes: 'Your search spend at $207.9M is doing heavy lifting, but with Paid Social at $7.4M and display at only $800K, there's a real gap in upper-funnel brand reach — especially for investors who aren't yet searching for Stock Advisor or Epic by name.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'How much of your current acquisition is coming from brand-search vs. non-brand terms, and is MikeWorldWide evaluating any channels to build awareness among investors who don't yet know Motley Fool?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium weather and financial-context inventory as a displacement of marginal search dollars — specifically targeting the upper-funnel investor audience that is actively engaged with market-moving content (weather impacts on commodities, economic events) and not yet reachable via search alone. Reference Bombora's current Retail Technology intent signal as evidence Motley Fool is already thinking about new audience channels.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If Motley Fool shifted even 1% of its $207.9M search budget ($2.08M) into TWC premium display and video, it would represent a 260% increase over the current $800K display spend — with guaranteed brand-safe, contextually relevant placements against a financially engaged audience.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a joint working session with Rebekah Hughes and, if appropriate, Bret Werner at MikeWorldWide to present a channel diversification model showing projected reach and frequency gains from a $2M TWC premium test against the existing search-dominant plan — offer to have the deck ready within 10 business days.

talk_track_opener: Hey Rebekah, I'm reaching out from The Weather Channel — we've had a great run together with two consecutive TWC Interactive PMP wins, most recently closing in January 2025, and I wanted to make sure we're building on that momentum rather than just renewing the same structure each cycle. We work with a number of FinServ brands helping them reach financially engaged audiences in high-attention, contextually relevant environments, and I think there's a real opportunity we haven't fully explored together yet. Looking at Motley Fool's media mix, the search investment at $207.9M is significant, but display is sitting at $800K — and with Stock Advisor and Epic competing for investor attention, there's an upper-funnel audience that search alone isn't capturing. I'd love to spend 20 minutes walking you through how TWC's first-party financial-intent audience overlaps with your subscriber profile, and whether a direct premium buy makes sense as the next step beyond the PMP. Would you have time in the next couple of weeks?
open_questions: The two PMP buys in 2024 and 2025 — are those being evaluated on performance metrics like CPA or subscriber acquisition cost, and how are they tracking against your search benchmarks?

With Rebekah owning marketing and MikeWorldWide scoped to PR, who is leading media planning and channel allocation decisions for Stock Advisor and Epic campaigns — is that an internal team or a separate media agency?
Your search spend is at $207.9M — what percentage of that is non-brand terms, and are you seeing saturation or rising CPCs in the financial services category?

The Bombora signal shows interest in Retail Technology this week — is Motley Fool exploring new audience segments or verticals beyond your core individual investor base, and how does that affect your media strategy?

Given the gap between your $7.4M Paid Social investment and your $800K display spend, is there an internal conversation about expanding upper-funnel reach, and what would a successful test case need to look like to get budget approved?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Nature's Way has two consecutive Won PMP deals (Feb 2025, Oct 2024) and $15.7M in total estimated spend. The relationship is active and the PMP history signals programmatic comfort — the opportunity is to convert that into higher-value, direct guaranteed or sponsorship inventory before the next renewal cycle.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the back-to-back PMP wins with Geoff Wills directly: 'Geoff, we've now run two consecutive TWC Interactive PMP deals together — Feb 2025 and Oct 2024 — and I want to show you what a direct, guaranteed buy alongside that PMP foundation could unlock in terms of audience reach and brand safety controls that programmatic alone can't guarantee.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Geoff: 'Of your $7.1M in CTV and $1.7M in online video spend, how much is currently running through PMPs versus direct guaranteed buys, and are you seeing the frequency and brand-safe context controls you need for a supplement brand operating under FTC scrutiny?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct inventory — weather-contextual CTV and digital placements — as the brand-safe, high-attention environment that complements the existing PMP buys. Nature's Way's ingredient quality and sustainability story needs premium, contextually relevant placements, not just programmatic reach.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Nature's Way's $7.1M CTV + $1.7M online video = $8.8M in video spend. Even capturing 10% of that in direct guaranteed TWC inventory represents an $880K incremental opportunity on top of the current PMP relationship.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Geoff for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to walk through TWC's direct CTV and sponsorship packages — frame it as a 'next chapter' conversation building on the two PMP wins already on the books.

sales_plays.title: Reclaim the Lost Sponsorship Budget
sales_plays.summary: Nature's Way lost a $200K 'New Year New You' Q1 2023 sponsorship deal — budget that went elsewhere. With $15.7M in total estimated spend and an active PMP relationship, there is clear budget authority and a proven willingness to invest in sponsorships. The play is to displace whoever captured that Q1 wellness-moment budget and bring it back to TWC.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the lost sponsorship directly with Geoff Wills: 'Geoff, back in late 2023 we had a $200K New Year New You sponsorship on the table that didn't close. I'd like to understand what won that budget instead and whether the results justified it — because the Q1 wellness moment is one of the highest-intent windows for supplement brands, and TWC's audience is actively searching for health solutions in that period.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Who ended up running that Q1 wellness sponsorship in 2024 and 2025, and how are you measuring the lift from those placements against your CTV and digital spend — specifically for brands like Alive! or Sambucol that have a seasonal demand spike in January?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-driven, contextual sponsorship inventory as uniquely suited to the New Year wellness moment — cold and flu season, resolution-driven health behavior, and weather stress all converge in Q1, making TWC's audience more receptive to supplement messaging than a generic digital or social placement.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost deal was $200K. Nature's Way's $2.5M in paid social (managed by Justin Buchbinder at Opinionated) and $627K in display suggest significant off-property spend that could be partially redirected. Recapturing even the original $200K sponsorship budget represents a 20x return on the current $1 PMP deal value and a concrete expansion of the relationship.

Step 5 (Next Step): Engage both Geoff Wills and Justin Buchbinder at Opinionated simultaneously — send Geoff a brief case study on Q1 supplement brand sponsorship performance on TWC, and ask Justin for a 20-minute call to align on whether a Q1 2026 sponsorship fits the creative scope Opinionated is already building.

talk_track_opener: Hey Geoff, I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've actually been working together for over a year now, with two TWC Interactive PMP deals closed in October 2024 and February 2025. I wanted to reach out directly because I think we're only scratching the surface of what this partnership can do for Nature's Way across your portfolio.
open_questions: Of the two TWC Interactive PMP deals we've run together, which Nature's Way brand or product line drove the most engagement — and is that brand getting proportional investment in your 2025 media plan?
The $200K New Year New You sponsorship in Q1 2023 didn't close with us — can you tell me what ran instead and whether you're planning a similar wellness-moment activation for Q1 2026?
With $7.1M in CTV spend, who are your primary direct CTV partners today, and how are you evaluating contextual relevance versus pure reach when buying that inventory?
Is Geoff Wills the final decision-maker on sponsorship and direct buys, or does Justin Buchbinder at Opinionated have approval authority over media placements beyond paid social?

Nature's Way operates across multiple supplement brands — are the PMP buys we've run brand-specific or portfolio-level, and is there an opportunity to expand TWC targeting across additional brands in the family?
