sales_plays.summary: Pandora is an active customer with two won PMP deals but minimal realized revenue ($1 each). With $47.1M in digital spend and $31.8M in CTV, there is a clear opportunity to convert programmatic access into a direct premium buy that captures more of their existing budget.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two won PMP deals (2024 and 2025) and acknowledge the relationship. Tell Diana Bojaj at Carat USA: 'We've been in your programmatic stack for two years — I want to show you what a direct buy unlocks that PMP can't deliver, specifically for Pandora's $47.1M digital footprint.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Diana: 'Of Pandora's $47.1M in digital spend, how much is currently allocated to premium direct placements versus open or private marketplace — and is Carat evaluating any rebalancing for 2026?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's owned-and-operated weather-contextual inventory as a direct buy advantage: guaranteed brand-safe placements, weather-triggered creative delivery timed to gifting moments (Valentine's Day, Mother's Day, holiday), and audience targeting that PMP cannot guarantee at scale.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost Valentine's Day Takeover proposal was valued at $270,000. A direct premium package at even half that value ($135K) would represent a 135,000x revenue increase over the current $1 PMP deals. Frame the conversation around recapturing that Valentine's Day budget with a broader 2026 tentpole calendar.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Diana Bojaj for a 30-minute working session before end of Q2 2026 to map Pandora's 2026 tentpole calendar against TWC's premium direct inventory — specifically Valentine's Day, Mother's Day, and holiday windows. Offer to bring a draft audience overlap analysis using Pandora's existing PMP data.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars with Weather-Contextual Video
sales_plays.summary: Pandora is spending $41.0M on Paid Social and $472K on Online Video — a significant imbalance that signals over-reliance on walled-garden social platforms. TWC's premium video and CTV-adjacent inventory offers a brand-safe, contextually relevant alternative that can capture a portion of that social budget.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with Stephen Fairchild, CCO/SVP, on a creative angle: 'Pandora's $41M in Paid Social is doing a lot of heavy lifting — but social platforms are increasingly noisy for jewelry brands around gifting moments. I want to show you how weather-contextual video inventory delivers the same emotional reach with stronger brand control.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Stephen: 'When Carat and your team evaluate the $41M Paid Social allocation, what are the primary KPIs — is it reach, ROAS, brand lift, or something else — and are there any platforms in that mix where performance has plateaued?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's online video and CTV inventory as a displacement target for underperforming social placements. Weather-triggered video ads (e.g., cold-weather gifting prompts, first snow of season for holiday charm campaigns) deliver contextual relevance that Meta and TikTok cannot replicate, with full brand-safe editorial control.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Pandora's Online Video spend is only $472K against $41M in Paid Social — a 87:1 ratio. Shifting even 2% of the Paid Social budget ($820K) into TWC premium video would represent a 74x increase over current online video investment and a meaningful new revenue line. Benchmark against the $270K Valentine's Day Takeover proposal as a proof-of-concept deal size.

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a creative brief review session with Stephen Fairchild within the next 3 weeks. Offer to bring a weather-moment content map aligned to Pandora's known gifting calendar (Valentine's Day, Mother's Day, holiday) showing where TWC video placements would complement — and partially replace — current social creative runs.

talk_track_opener: Hey Diana, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Pandora through Carat for a couple of years now — the PMP deals in 2024 and 2025 gave us a foundation, and I want to build on that relationship in a more meaningful way heading into 2026. We know Pandora is running a serious media program — $139M in total estimated spend, $47M in digital alone — and we think there's a real gap between what the PMP access delivers and what Pandora could get from a direct premium partnership with us. Here's what caught my attention: Pandora came very close to a $270K Valentine's Day Takeover with us in early 2024, and that deal didn't close. With Valentine's Day planning for 2026 starting now, I'd love to understand what would need to be different for that kind of tentpole investment to make sense — and whether there's appetite to look at a broader gifting-moment calendar that spans Valentine's Day, Mother's Day, and holiday. Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar before end of Q2 to map that out together?
open_questions: The Valentine's Day Takeover proposal in Q1 2024 was valued at $270K but didn't close — what were the primary objections from Pandora or Carat's side, and have those factors changed heading into 2026 planning?

Carat USA holds full AOR scope for Pandora — does Diana Bojaj's team own the final investment decision on premium direct buys, or does Stephen Fairchild's team at Pandora need to approve deals above a certain threshold?

Pandora is spending $41M on Paid Social versus $472K on Online Video — is that ratio intentional and performing, or is there internal pressure from Pandora's brand team to diversify away from walled-garden platforms?
With $31.8M already in CTV, is Pandora's team evaluating any convergence between their CTV and digital video strategies for 2026, and is there a unified video budget or are those managed separately?

For Pandora's 2026 tentpole calendar — Valentine's Day, Mother's Day, holiday — how far in advance is Carat locking in premium direct placements, and is there a planning window open right now that we should be part of?

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Cara: 'Right now, how is the digital team at BASIC/DEPT measuring audience overlap between the PMP buys and the Online Video and CTV placements — are those planned and measured as one connected funnel or in separate silos?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-targeted CTV and Online Video inventory as the connective tissue between the PMP audiences Patagonia already trusts and the upper-funnel video spend that currently runs without that same audience signal — closing the attribution gap between awareness and conversion.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The PMP deals have been symbolic ($1 each) — the real prize is capturing a share of the $2.2M Online Video + $1.8M CTV = $4M in video spend. sales_plays.title: Trails Season Weather Window
sales_plays.summary: Patagonia's Trails Division (Jason Gonzales) sells gear tied directly to outdoor activity windows driven by weather and season. Planning for spring trail season and key retail tentpoles (Earth Day, Memorial Day) typically begins 8–12 weeks out. Getting in front of Jason and Cara before Q1 budget locks positions TWC as the planning partner, not a reactive vendor.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Jason Gonzales directly — referencing his Trails Division ownership — with a timely hook: 'Trail season planning is usually underway by late January for spring. Given Patagonia's environmental mission and the audience overlap between weather-driven outdoor intent and your Trails customer, I'd love to share how TWC is helping outdoor retail brands get ahead of the spring activation window.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jason: 'For the Trails Division specifically, which retail tentpoles — Earth Day, Memorial Day, or the early spring weather break — drive the most urgency in your media planning calendar, and how far in advance is that budget committed?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's weather-triggered targeting lets Patagonia Trails serve ads when local forecast conditions (warming temps, low precipitation) signal the precise moment a consumer is likely to plan a trail outing — aligning ad delivery to real behavioral intent, not just a calendar date.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Patagonia's Mobile App spend is $575K and Online Video is $2.2M — both formats where weather-moment targeting can improve relevance and reduce wasted impressions. A weather-triggered layer on even 20% of the $575K Mobile App budget (~$115K) could be a low-risk pilot with measurable lift in app engagement during peak trail-planning moments.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer Jason a 20-minute 'seasonal planning preview' call in the next 10 days — bring a one-page showing TWC weather audience overlap with Patagonia's Trails customer profile and propose a Q2 pilot tied to the Earth Day or Memorial Day window before budgets are locked.

talk_track_opener: Hey Cara, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with the Patagonia team for three consecutive years now on the TWC Interactive PMP — 2024, 2025, and the 2026 renewal just closed in January — so I feel like we've built a real foundation of trust there. steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Route through Reilly Dugery at Empower Ocean Media Group with a data-led note: 'PetSmart is running $3.2M in display against a $139M total spend base — industry benchmarks show display CTR has dropped 40% in the past three years. sales_plays.title: Displace the $26.9M Paid Social Budget

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's contextual targeting (weather-triggered health moments, allergy season, respiratory conditions) as a brand-safe, policy-compliant alternative that reaches the same health-conscious consumer without relying on social platform health data signals.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'If even 10% of the $26.9M paid social budget — roughly $2.7M — were redirected to contextual health-moment inventory on TWC, Pfizer would gain measurable reach among condition-relevant audiences with zero platform policy exposure. That's a defensible reallocation, not a risk.'

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Bartman for permission to prepare a brief market-level weather-audience analysis for Omaha and key Physicians Mutual coverage markets, with the explicit goal of giving him something he can bring to Chad Wells as a Q2 planning input. Set a two-week turnaround and a follow-up call to walk through it together.

talk_track_opener: Hey Mark, I'm with The Weather Company — we work with a number of insurance advertisers who are using weather-triggered digital inventory to reach consumers at the exact moment storm and rain season puts protection top of mind. Given Intermark's AOR role on Physicians Mutual, I wanted to connect before Q2 planning locks rather than after. I know you're managing a significant media mix for them and I didn't want to show up mid-flight with something that should have been in the plan from the start. We've seen Midwest storm-season consideration spikes that tend to outpace national benchmarks, and I think there's a real case to be made for contextual digital as a complement to the broadcast base. I have some market-level data for Omaha and surrounding coverage areas that I think would be worth 20 minutes of your time — would a quick working session in the next two weeks make sense before spring planning finalizes?
open_questions: How is Intermark currently planning Physicians Mutual's digital spend around storm and rain season — is there a weather or seasonality trigger built into the buy, or is it always-on?
For the $4.8M in paid social, who owns the performance benchmarks — is Chad Wells evaluating that line against brand lift, direct response, or something else?
Given that broadcast is $55.9M and digital is $5.2M, is there an internal conversation at Physicians Mutual about shifting more budget toward digital, or is broadcast expected to hold that share?
Does Mark Bartman at Intermark have discretion to test new digital channels within the existing plan, or does any new vendor require Chad Wells's direct approval?
Physicians Mutual's CTV spend is only $172K against $55.9M in broadcast — is there a reason CTV hasn't scaled, and is that a budget decision or a measurement confidence issue?

sales_plays.title: From PMP to Premium Direct: Expand Plymouth Rock's Weather-Contextual Footprint
sales_plays.summary: Plymouth Rock has won two consecutive PMP deals (2024 and 2025) and carries $22.4M in total media spend with $502K in CTV and $18.8M in digital. This play converts the existing PMP relationship into a broader, higher-value direct buy that layers in weather-contextual targeting, CTV, and on-property placements ahead of Northeast winter and spring driving demand windows.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins with Attention Arc's Jasmine Dadlani and open with a performance review framing: 'We've now run two successful PMP cycles together — I want to bring Jasmine and Colleen Finn a proposal that builds on what's working and captures the Northeast weather demand windows we haven't fully activated yet.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jasmine Dadlani: 'With $502K already committed to CTV and $9.8M in Paid Social, where is Attention Arc seeing the strongest cost-per-quote efficiency right now, and is there a channel where Plymouth Rock feels underweighted heading into Q3 and Q4?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a direct, weather-triggered package that combines TWC on-property inventory (weather.com, app, CTV) with contextual targeting tied to Northeast winter driving conditions — ice, snow, and storm events — directly relevant to Plymouth Rock's Crashbusters and claims-support messaging.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Plymouth Rock's $22.4M total spend includes only $303K in Online Video and $223K in Mobile App — both underweight relative to the $9.8M Paid Social commitment. A direct buy reallocating even 5% of Paid Social ($490K) into weather-contextual CTV and on-property video would double their current CTV investment and reach Northeast drivers at the highest-intent moment.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jasmine Dadlani for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar before end of month to walk through a weather-contextual media plan for Q3-Q4? I'll bring audience reach data for the Northeast auto insurance shopper segment so you and Colleen can pressure-test it against your current mix.'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Overweight with Weather-Contextual Video
sales_plays.summary: Plymouth Rock's media mix is heavily skewed toward Paid Social at $9.8M — nearly 44% of total digital spend — while weather-contextual and on-property video formats remain minimal ($303K Online Video, $223K Mobile App). This play targets Attention Arc's media planning team to reframe that imbalance and displace a portion of social spend with higher-intent, weather-triggered environments where Plymouth Rock's Northeast auto and home insurance messaging is most relevant.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a spend-mix observation to Jasmine Dadlani at Attention Arc: 'Looking at Plymouth Rock's current digital allocation, Paid Social is running at $9.8M — about 44 cents of every digital dollar. For a Northeast-focused auto and home insurer, that's a significant concentration in a channel that doesn't offer weather context or moment-of-need targeting. I want to show you what a rebalanced mix could look like.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When Attention Arc evaluates channel performance for Plymouth Rock, how are you measuring the quality of the audience at the moment of ad exposure — and is weather context or driving-condition relevance part of that conversation with Colleen Finn's team?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-contextual inventory as a displacement candidate for a defined slice of the $9.8M Paid Social budget — specifically targeting Northeast auto insurance shoppers during high-intent weather moments (winter storms, spring rain, ice events) that directly correlate with Crashbusters claims volume and new policy consideration.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Shifting $750K–$1M from Paid Social into TWC weather-contextual video and mobile app placements would represent less than 10% of the current social budget but would more than triple Plymouth Rock's current Online Video ($303K) and Mobile App ($223K) investment combined — with the added benefit of reaching the same Northeast audience in a brand-safe, high-relevance environment.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive analysis deliverable: 'I can put together a one-page channel-mix comparison showing how other Northeast regional insurers are using weather-contextual video versus Paid Social. Would that be a useful input for Attention Arc's next planning cycle with Plymouth Rock? I can have it to you within a week.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Jasmine, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've had a great run with Plymouth Rock over the past two cycles — two consecutive PMP wins in 2024 and 2025 — and I wanted to reach out because I think we're only scratching the surface of what we can do together for Colleen and the team. We've built real credibility here, and I want to use that as a foundation to bring you something more strategic ahead of Q3 planning. Here's what caught my attention: Plymouth Rock is running $9.8M in Paid Social and only $303K in Online Video — but they're a Northeast-focused auto and home insurer whose core value props, Crashbusters, claims support, Door to Door service, are most resonant exactly when the weather turns. That's a real opportunity to shift some of that social weight into weather-contextual environments where Plymouth Rock's message lands at the highest-intent moment. I'd love 30 minutes to walk you through a rebalanced channel mix proposal — would that be worth a conversation before end of month?
open_questions: The two PMP buys we've run together have been booked at nominal value — how is Attention Arc measuring the downstream performance of those placements for Plymouth Rock, and what would a stronger ROI case need to show to justify a larger direct commitment?

With Colleen Finn overseeing the Home division specifically, is there a separate media budget or planning cycle for Plymouth Rock's home insurance line that Attention Arc manages independently from auto?

Paid Social is running at $9.8M — is that allocation locked for the year, or is there flexibility in Attention Arc's planning process to shift dollars toward higher-intent channels if the performance case is strong?
Plymouth Rock's CTV spend is at $502K — who owns that buying decision at Attention Arc, and is there an active RFP or review process for CTV partners heading into Q3?
Given Plymouth Rock's Northeast focus, is weather-event timing currently factored into Attention Arc's media flight scheduling, or is the current calendar based on general seasonal patterns?

sales_plays.title: Brand Family Expansion Beyond Core & RZR
sales_plays.summary: Polaris is running active campaigns for Core, RZR, and NorthStar lines but has not yet activated Victory Motorcycles, Pure Polaris accessories, or Ranger utility vehicles as standalone media investments. With 86 closed opportunities and $700K+ in Q2 2026 wins alone, there is clear appetite to grow wallet share across the full brand portfolio.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three Q2 2026 wins — Core ($206K), RZR ($129K), and NorthStar ($181K) — and open with: 'Taylor, you've had a strong Q2 across three lines. I want to show you how we're helping similar powersports brands activate their utility and accessories lines in the same digital environment where your Core campaigns are already performing.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Taylor Marks at rEvolution: 'Which Polaris brand lines — Ranger, Victory Motorcycles, or Pure Polaris accessories — are currently without a dedicated media budget, and is that a planning gap or a deliberate hold?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's audience targeting against Polaris's existing $358K digital footprint — specifically the $2.2M Search investment — by showing how display, OLV, and CTV can capture in-market utility vehicle and accessories shoppers who are already searching but not being retargeted across the funnel.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The NorthStar line alone has generated $365K across two consecutive quarters (Q1 $184K + Q2 $181K). A comparable activation for Ranger or Pure Polaris at even 50% of that scale represents $90K–$100K in incremental quarterly spend — a concrete number to bring to Pam Kermisch for budget approval.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Taylor for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to map which brand lines are in rEvolution's Q3 planning cycle, with a goal of presenting a Ranger or Pure Polaris proposal before the Q3 budget lock.

sales_plays.title: Entertainment Intent Window — Lifestyle Content Alignment
sales_plays.summary: Bombora signals show Polaris is actively researching Entertainment topics this week. Given Polaris's product mix — snowmobiles, ATVs, side-by-sides — this likely reflects interest in lifestyle, outdoor entertainment, or experiential content partnerships. This is a narrow window to introduce a premium content or sponsorship angle before the signal cools.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the Bombora signal directly: 'Taylor, our intent data flagged Polaris surging on Entertainment topics this week. Given your current digital mix — $26K in OLV, $143K in CTV — I want to share a specific content alignment opportunity that fits what your team appears to be evaluating right now.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Is the Entertainment interest tied to a specific campaign brief — maybe a lifestyle or experiential push for RZR or Ranger — or is this more of an exploratory conversation Polaris is having internally about content partnerships?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Connect TWC Interactive's weather-adjacent outdoor and adventure content environments to Polaris's core buyer — someone planning off-road trips, snowmobile seasons, or outdoor recreation — and show how Entertainment-category placements extend the $143K CTV investment into premium contextual moments.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Polaris's current OLV spend is $26K and CTV is $143K — both underdeveloped relative to the $2.2M Search budget. A content sponsorship or entertainment-aligned OLV extension at $50K–$75K would still represent less than 4% of total digital spend while adding a high-visibility, brand-safe environment that Search cannot replicate.

Step 5 (Next Step): Send Taylor a one-page opportunity brief within 48 hours — before the Bombora signal window closes — and request a 20-minute call with both Taylor and Pam Kermisch to validate whether this aligns with an active brief or a Q3 planning conversation.

talk_track_opener: Hey Taylor, this is [Rep Name] from TWC Interactive. We've been working closely with rEvolution across your Polaris portfolio — Core, RZR, and NorthStar all closed strong in Q2, and we really appreciate the partnership your team has built with us across those campaigns. I wanted to reach out personally because you're one of our most active accounts and I think there's a meaningful opportunity we haven't fully explored together yet. Here's what caught my attention this week: our Bombora intent data flagged Polaris surging on Entertainment topics, and when I looked at your current media mix — $2.2M in Search, $143K in CTV, but only $26K in OLV — it made me think there's a content or lifestyle angle your team might be evaluating that we could support. At the same time, I noticed Ranger, Victory Motorcycles, and Pure Polaris accessories don't appear to have standalone digital activations the way Core and NorthStar do. I'd love 20 minutes to understand whether those lines are in your Q3 planning cycle and whether the Entertainment signal maps to something specific. Would next week work?
open_questions: Which Polaris brand lines — Ranger, Victory Motorcycles, or Pure Polaris accessories — are currently in rEvolution's Q3 media plan, and which are still without a dedicated digital budget?

The Bombora data shows Polaris surging on Entertainment topics this week — is that tied to a specific brief Taylor's team is working on, or is it an exploratory conversation happening at the Polaris brand level with Pam Kermisch?
NorthStar has run at $180K+ for two consecutive quarters — is that budget locked annually or re-approved each quarter, and who at Polaris owns that decision?
Given that Search is $2.2M and OLV is only $26K, is there a strategic reason video has been kept small, or is that a planning gap rEvolution would be open to revisiting for Q3?

With SixSpeed handling AOR Creative and rEvolution owning media planning, how do new brand line activations typically get initiated — does the brief come from Pam Kermisch's team first, or does rEvolution bring opportunities to Polaris proactively?

sales_plays.title: Spring/Summer Tentpole Window
sales_plays.summary: Polaroid Eyewear is actively promoting Spring/Summer 2026 collections and Easter promos. Reaching Jason Scott Russell now — before the demand window opens — positions our platform as part of their seasonal planning cycle rather than a reactive add-on.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Jason Scott Russell referencing Polaroid's Spring/Summer 2026 launch and Easter promo push visible on their site. Note that their current $545K search spend and $40K paid social suggest strong digital intent but limited upper-funnel reach — open with: 'I noticed Polaroid is already teasing the S/S 2026 collection and Easter promos. Most eyewear brands we work with start locking in upper-funnel support 8–10 weeks out — wanted to connect before your planning window closes.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'For your Spring/Summer tentpole and Easter campaigns, where does the brief currently start — with Jason's team internally or does R&J Strategic Communications take the lead on channel planning?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position against the gap between their $545K search investment and their thin upper-funnel presence ($10K online video, $7K display). Explain how contextual video and high-intent display environments can capture consumers earlier in the sunglasses purchase journey — before they're searching — especially for seasonal gifting and outdoor lifestyle moments.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame it concretely: 'Your current $602K digital mix is heavily weighted toward bottom-funnel. A modest reallocation — even 15–20% toward upper-funnel video and display — could meaningfully expand reach ahead of the Easter and S/S demand spike without touching your search budget.'

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 20-minute call with Jason Scott Russell in the next two weeks: 'Would a quick 20-minute call before [specific date 2 weeks out] make sense to walk through how other retail eyewear brands are structuring their S/S tentpole mix? No pitch deck — just a planning conversation.'

sales_plays.title: R&J Agency Door Opener
sales_plays.summary: R&J Strategic Communications holds the AOR Digital Public Relations scope for Polaroid Eyewear. Scott Marioni as President is a warm entry point to build agency trust before going direct — especially valuable given Polaroid is net-new with no CRM history.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Scott Marioni at R&J Strategic Communications directly. Reference R&J's AOR relationship with Polaroid Eyewear and lead with value to the agency: 'Scott — I know R&J handles digital PR for Polaroid Eyewear. We work with several retail eyewear and lifestyle brands on paid media that complements PR-driven moments. Wanted to see if there's a conversation worth having as Polaroid heads into their Spring/Summer 2026 push.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'When Polaroid activates around a tentpole like Easter or a new collection launch, does R&J coordinate the paid amplification strategy alongside the PR, or does that sit separately with Jason Scott Russell's team?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position as a resource that makes R&J look proactive to their client. Offer to bring a brief or category insight on eyewear/retail seasonal media trends that Scott can share internally with Polaroid — framing it as agency-first, not a bypass.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Anchor on Polaroid's $1.22M trailing 12-month spend and the current digital mix skew toward search ($545K). Note: 'There's meaningful budget already in market — the opportunity is helping Polaroid get more out of it by layering in formats that PR alone can't deliver, like targeted video or contextual display around outdoor and lifestyle content.'

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Scott for a 15-minute intro call and offer to bring a one-page retail eyewear seasonal media brief as a leave-behind for his Polaroid conversations: 'Would a quick 15-minute call this week or next work? I can put together a short S/S eyewear media brief you could use with Jason's team — no strings attached.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Jason — I'm [Name] from [Company]. We work with retail eyewear and lifestyle brands on digital media that drives measurable results across the full purchase funnel, from awareness through conversion. I've worked with brands in your category that run a similar mix of search and paid social heading into seasonal peaks.
open_questions: For your Spring/Summer 2026 and Easter campaigns, how far in advance does your media planning typically lock — and is Jason Scott Russell driving that internally or does R&J Strategic Communications lead channel allocation?
Your search investment is substantial at $545K — is that managed in-house or through a separate paid media agency beyond R&J's PR scope?
With collections spanning Sport, Contemporary, Gummy, and New Collection, are you running separate campaigns per line or consolidating under a single brand message for tentpole moments?
Outside of paid search and paid social, has Polaroid tested any upper-funnel formats — video, streaming, contextual display — in the Americas market in the past 12 months?
Who ultimately owns the paid media budget decision for the Americas — Jason Scott Russell as Retail Marketing Manager, or is there a VP or CMO above him who needs to be in the room?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Direct Upgrade: Unlock Premium Inventory
sales_plays.summary: Poshmark has won two consecutive PMP deals with TWC Interactive (2024 and 2025). With $22.8M in digital spend and $4.0M already in CTV, there is clear appetite for premium inventory. This play converts the existing PMP relationship into a direct, higher-value buy with expanded formats and audience targeting.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins — 'We've delivered on the TWC Interactive PMP in both 2024 and 2025, and I want to show Steven and Jonathan what a direct buy unlocks that the PMP layer can't.' Lead with performance continuity, not a cold pitch.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jonathan Hino at Glass and Marker: 'Beyond the PMP placements, where is Poshmark's $22.8M digital budget underperforming on reach or brand-safety — and is there a format or audience segment the current PMP structure isn't covering?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a direct buy against the PMP ceiling — direct access to TWC's premium weather-contextual inventory, first-look on high-demand tentpole windows (Back-to-School, Holiday, New Year New You), and guaranteed CPMs vs. auction volatility on Poshmark's $6.0M paid social and $1.6M online video lines.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Poshmark spends $46.2M total. The two PMP deals closed at nominal value, meaning the direct revenue opportunity is essentially uncaptured. Even a 5% shift of the $22.8M digital budget to a direct TWC buy represents ~$1.1M in incremental revenue — a concrete number to anchor the conversation.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jonathan Hino for a 30-minute planning call in the next two weeks to map Poshmark's Q3–Q4 tentpole calendar against TWC's premium inventory windows — 'Can we get 30 minutes with you and ideally Steven before the end of the month to align on what a direct package could look like for Back-to-School?'

sales_plays.title: Broadcast-to-CTV Displacement: Shift Linear Dollars to Addressable
sales_plays.summary: Poshmark spends $16.5M on Broadcast and only $4.0M on CTV — a ratio that is out of step with where Poshmark's mobile-first, fashion-forward audience is consuming content. This play makes the case to CMO Steven Tristan Young to reallocate a portion of the $16.5M broadcast budget into TWC's CTV environment, where audience targeting and attribution are measurable.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open directly with Steven Tristan Young: 'You're running $16.5M in broadcast against an audience that is predominantly mobile-first and app-driven — the same audience that downloaded Poshmark. I want to show you what happens when we move even 10% of that into addressable CTV on TWC.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Steven, on the $16.5M broadcast side — what does your current attribution model look like for measuring brand lift or app installs driven by linear TV, and is that a gap your team is trying to close?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's CTV environment offers weather-triggered creative delivery, audience segments aligned to Poshmark's resale and fashion shopper profile, and measurable app-install attribution — capabilities that linear broadcast cannot provide. This directly addresses the attribution gap on a $16.5M line that is largely dark from a performance standpoint.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 10% reallocation of the $16.5M broadcast budget equals $1.65M into CTV. At Poshmark's current CTV spend of $4.0M, that represents a 41% increase in addressable video investment — with measurable lift data to justify further shifts in future quarters.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a brief attribution audit conversation: 'Can we set up a 20-minute call with you and your media team before the end of this quarter? I want to bring our CTV measurement framework and show you exactly how we'd track app installs and brand lift against the dollars you shift — no commitment, just the data.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Jonathan, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've worked together on the Poshmark PMP in both 2024 and 2025, so you know what we can deliver. I wanted to reach out because I've been looking at Poshmark's broader media mix and I think there's a real opportunity we haven't tapped yet together. Poshmark is running $16.5M in broadcast and $4.0M in CTV, with another $22.8M across digital — and the two PMP wins we've had are honestly just a starting point relative to what that budget could do on a direct or expanded buy. I'd love to get 30 minutes with you and Steven to talk through where the current PMP is leaving inventory and targeting on the table, and what a direct package for Q3 and Q4 could look like. Would you be open to connecting this week or next?
open_questions: Jonathan, now that we've run the PMP in both 2024 and 2025, what performance metrics is Poshmark using to evaluate whether to expand that investment — and who owns that decision, you or Steven's team directly?
Steven, on the $16.5M broadcast budget — what attribution model are you using to connect linear TV exposure to app installs or resale transactions on Poshmark?
Is Glass and Marker managing Poshmark's media planning and buying in addition to creative, or is there a separate media AOR we should be coordinating with on the CTV and digital side?

As Poshmark plans for Back-to-School and Holiday, are there specific audience segments — first-time resellers, luxury buyers, Gen Z fashion shoppers — that you're prioritizing and that the current PMP structure isn't reaching efficiently?
Given that Poshmark's $22.8M digital budget is split across six sub-channels, is there a consolidation or premium-inventory strategy on the roadmap for 2025 that a direct TWC buy could fit into?

sales_plays.title: Demand Gen Intent Window — Strike Before Q3 Planning Locks
sales_plays.summary: Bombora shows Prestige Consumer Healthcare actively researching Demand Generation this week. Combined with a recent FY27 RFP loss on Goody's ($250K, January 2026), this intent signal suggests Prestige is actively evaluating demand generation partners ahead of the next planning cycle. The window to influence budget allocation is now, before agency plans are finalized with The Shipyard.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the intent signal as a conversation opener to Lance Porigow at The Shipyard: 'Our data is showing Prestige in active research mode around Demand Generation right now. Given the FY27 Goody's RFP that recently went out, I want to make sure we're in the room before the next plan gets locked. Can we connect this week?'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Lance: 'For the brands where we lost — Goody's FY27 and Chloraseptic — what was the primary objection: pricing, reach, measurement, or something else? I want to make sure our next proposal addresses the actual gap.'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): 'Prestige's $5.0M digital mix is heavily weighted toward Paid Social at $2.9M. If the demand gen research is pointing toward lower-funnel performance or incremental reach, we can show how CTV — currently only $496K in the mix — and Online Video at $366K can extend demand gen impact beyond social audiences and into connected TV households that index for OTC healthcare purchase intent.'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'The Goody's FY27 RFP was valued at $250,000. If we can re-enter that conversation with a demand gen-specific angle — showing incremental reach against the Analgesics buyer that Paid Social alone isn't capturing — we have a clear path to recovering that lost revenue and potentially expanding the total engagement.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to send Lance a one-page demand gen brief by end of week — specific to the Goody's and Chloraseptic audiences — so The Shipyard has something concrete to bring to Rory Otto before the next internal planning session. Does Thursday work for a quick alignment call?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Rory, this is [Rep Name] from [Company]. We've been working together on the Goody's business for a while now — the FY26 TWC campaign closed at $443,900 and the Q1 2025 campaign at $255,600 — so I feel like we have a real baseline of what works for the Analgesics portfolio. I also work closely with Lance Porigow at The Shipyard on the planning side, so I want to make sure anything I bring you is already pressure-tested through the agency before it lands on your desk. I'm not here to pitch you cold. I'm reaching out because our intent data flagged Prestige in active research mode around Demand Generation this week, and I know the FY27 Goody's RFP just went through a cycle where we didn't get the win. I'd love to understand what the gap was — and whether there's an angle on Chloraseptic or Sleep-Eze where we haven't had the right conversation yet. Would a 20-minute call this week make sense to dig into where the portfolio is headed next?
open_questions: The Goody's FY27 RFP came in at $250,000 and we didn't win it — can you tell me what the deciding factor was so we can address it directly in any future proposal?
For Chloraseptic and Sleep-Eze, does Rory Otto own those brand budgets as well, or is there a separate marketing lead we should be building a relationship with?
The Shipyard is AOR for media planning and buying — does Lance Porigow have final say on vendor selection for non-Goody's brands, or does that approval run through Prestige directly?

With $2.9M concentrated in Paid Social, is the team satisfied with the incremental reach it's delivering, or is there internal pressure to diversify the demand gen mix heading into the next fiscal year?
Is the Demand Generation research we're seeing from Prestige this week tied to a specific brand initiative or a broader portfolio-level strategy review?

sales_plays.title: Shift Search Dollars Into Premium Display
sales_plays.summary: Priority Health is an active PMP customer with $984K concentrated in Search and only $8K in Display. The imbalance signals an untapped upper-funnel opportunity to grow wallet share with Kyla King and BVK's Michael Voss.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Kyla King referencing the active 2026 PMP win and note that Priority Health's $984K Search investment is doing heavy lifting at the bottom of the funnel — ask for 20 minutes to show how TWC's premium Display inventory can extend reach to Michigan health shoppers earlier in the decision cycle.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Kyla: 'Outside of Search, where is BVK currently allocating upper-funnel budget for individual and family plan enrollment, and is there a target cost-per-quote or cost-per-enrollment that guides those decisions?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's Michigan-targeted Display and Online Video inventory as the complement to Search — capturing in-market health insurance shoppers on weather-adjacent content before they reach a search query, reducing CPC pressure on the $984K Search line.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Priority Health's current Display spend is $8K against a $1.38M total budget — less than 0.6% of spend. A modest reallocation of even 5% of the Search budget ($49K) into TWC Display would represent a 6x increase in Display investment and a meaningful new reach layer without cutting proven Search performance.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Michael Voss at BVK to schedule a 30-minute media mix review before the next planning cycle — offer to bring a Michigan market reach analysis showing TWC's unduplicated audience against Priority Health's current Search footprint.

sales_plays.title: Open Enrollment Pre-Season Activation
sales_plays.summary: Health insurance open enrollment for individual and ACA plans typically runs November 1 through January 15 in Michigan. Priority Health's CRM history shows PMP deals closing in January each of the last three years, suggesting budget activates late. This play gets in front of BVK before Q3 planning locks to capture pre-enrollment awareness spend.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Michael Voss at BVK in late June, noting that Priority Health has renewed the TWC PMP in January 2024, 2025, and 2026 — and that starting the conversation now gives BVK the runway to layer in awareness formats (Online Video, Mobile App) ahead of the November 1 open enrollment window rather than activating at the bottom of the funnel.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Michael: 'For the 2026 open enrollment push, is BVK planning any pre-enrollment awareness flight in September or October, or does the current plan concentrate spend after November 1 when competition for Search inventory peaks?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's Michigan-local audience — particularly weather app and video users — as a high-attention environment to build Priority Health brand preference in September–October before competitors flood Search and Broadcast in November, when Priority Health already spends $156K in Broadcast.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Priority Health's Mobile App spend is $12K and Online Video is $12K — both formats that index well for pre-enrollment awareness. A pre-season flight of $25K–$40K in these formats on TWC would double current investment in each and create a measurable lift baseline before the November enrollment window opens.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer Kyla King a one-page Michigan open enrollment competitive landscape brief — delivered by mid-July — showing when competitor health plans activate on digital and how a September TWC flight positions Priority Health ahead of the noise. Ask for a 20-minute call to walk through it.

talk_track_opener: Hey Kyla, I'm [Rep Name] with The Weather Company — we've been working with Priority Health through BVK for the past three years on your PMP, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a real opportunity we haven't fully explored together. You've built a strong Search foundation at nearly a million dollars, and the PMP renewals show the partnership is working — I just want to make sure we're maximizing what that relationship can do for you. Looking at Priority Health's current media mix, Search is carrying the vast majority of digital spend at $984K, while Display sits at $8K and Mobile App and Online Video are each at $12K. For a Michigan-focused health plan competing for individual and family enrollment, that's a lot of pressure on one channel. I'd love to spend 20 minutes with you and Michael Voss at BVK to walk through how TWC's Michigan audience — especially in weather and video environments — could extend your reach upstream and take some of the cost pressure off Search. Would a quick call in the next two weeks work?
open_questions: Outside of Search, what upper-funnel formats is BVK currently using to build Priority Health brand awareness ahead of Michigan open enrollment, and is there a target cost-per-quote guiding those decisions?
Does Kyla King or BVK own the decision to test new digital formats like Display or Online Video, or does that require a separate approval process at Priority Health?
Is the January PMP renewal driven by open enrollment budget timing, or is there a separate always-on digital budget that could support a pre-enrollment flight in September or October?
How is Priority Health currently measuring the incremental reach of Search against Broadcast — and is there a gap in mid-funnel attribution that a Display or video layer could help close?

With BVK holding full AOR scope, is Michael Voss the right person to bring a Michigan market reach analysis to, or is there a dedicated digital planner at BVK who manages the Priority Health account day-to-day?

sales_plays.title: Magnite PMP to Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: P&G is an active customer with two won Magnite Web and App Pre-roll PMP deals in Q1-Q2 2025, plus a $100K Swiffer Duster win. The play converts proven programmatic PMP performance into higher-value direct/reserved inventory across additional P&G brand families.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Zaki Khalid at Hearts & Science referencing the two Magnite PMP wins (Web Pre-roll and App Pre-roll, both closed April-May 2025) and the $100K Swiffer Duster buy. Ask for a 20-minute call to share performance benchmarks from comparable CPG brands that scaled from PMP to direct reserved inventory.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Beyond Swiffer, which P&G brand families — Tide, Pampers, Gillette, Oral-B — are Hearts & Science and Quigley-Simpson currently planning Q3/Q4 tentpole budgets for, and is any of that spend still unallocated to a premium weather-contextual environment?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's on-property premium video and display inventory as the logical next step above the Magnite PMP layer — same audience, higher share-of-voice, brand-safe editorial context, and weather-moment targeting that CPG household brands like Tide and Swiffer are uniquely suited for (laundry-day weather, allergy season, back-to-school).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): P&G's total trailing-12-month media spend is $3.08B, with $683.5M in digital and $448.4M in CTV. The two Magnite PMP wins represent a small proof-of-concept. Even a 0.5% shift of the $683.5M digital budget to TWC direct inventory equals ~$3.4M in incremental revenue — frame the conversation around unlocking that headroom brand by brand.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Zaki Khalid for a 20-minute working session before end of this quarter to map which P&G brand families have Q3 tentpole budgets still in planning, with the goal of presenting a multi-brand package proposal within 2 weeks of that call.

sales_plays.title: Displace the Lost OUAI & 120-Day Test Budget
sales_plays.summary: Two recent opportunities — the 2025 OUAI Launch ($50K) and the 120-Day Test — were both lost in late 2025/early 2026. These losses signal a competing platform captured that spend. The play identifies where that budget went and displaces the incumbent with a differentiated weather-contextual and audience-targeting pitch.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two lost opportunities directly in outreach to Zaki Khalid: 'We lost the OUAI Launch and the 120-Day Test earlier this year — I'd value 15 minutes to understand what the winning solution offered that we didn't, so we can come back with something more relevant for the next P&G brand launch in the pipeline.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'For the OUAI Launch and the 120-Day Test, which platform or publisher won that budget, and was the decision driven by audience targeting capabilities, pricing, or measurement/attribution methodology?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Once the incumbent is identified, position TWC's differentiated assets — weather-triggered creative delivery, first-party location and lifestyle audience data, and verified brand-safe editorial environment — as capabilities the likely winning platform (social or open-web display) cannot replicate at scale for a premium beauty or household brand like OUAI.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The OUAI loss was $50K — a small test. P&G's Paid Social spend alone is $468.6M and Online Video is $50.7M. Frame the re-engagement not as recovering $50K but as earning the right to compete for a share of the $519M combined social and online video budget that Saatchi & Saatchi X and Hearts & Science control.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Marc Pritchard's team (via Zaki Khalid as the agency path) for a 30-minute competitive debrief call within the next 3 weeks, with a commitment to return with a revised proposal tied to the next P&G brand launch or Q3 cultural-moment campaign before budgets are locked.

talk_track_opener: Hey Zaki, I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together on a few P&G programs this year — the Magnite Web Pre-roll and App Pre-roll PMPs both closed successfully in the spring, and the Swiffer Duster campaign wrapped at $100K in March. I wanted to reach out directly because I think we've only scratched the surface of what's possible across the broader P&G portfolio. We work with several large CPG advertisers who started exactly where P&G is with us — programmatic PMP tests — and have since scaled into multi-brand direct partnerships worth seven figures annually, specifically because weather-contextual targeting performs for household and personal care categories in ways that standard demo targeting can't replicate. I noticed the OUAI Launch and the 120-Day Test didn't come our way, and I'd genuinely like to understand what we missed so we can come back stronger. P&G is running $683M in digital and $448M in CTV — even a small reallocation toward premium on-property inventory could be meaningful for both sides. Would you have 20 minutes before end of quarter to walk me through which brand families are still in planning for Q3 tentpoles? I want to make sure we're in the room before those budgets are locked.
open_questions: For the OUAI Launch and the 120-Day Test that went to another platform, was the decision driven by audience targeting, pricing, or measurement methodology — and is that vendor still active on P&G's approved list?

Between Hearts & Science and Quigley-Simpson, who owns the final budget allocation decision for digital video and CTV on household care brands like Tide and Swiffer — and is Zaki Khalid the right person to loop in on both?
Which P&G brand families are currently in Q3 planning with unallocated digital or CTV budget that hasn't been committed to a specific publisher yet?

P&G is running $468.6M in Paid Social — is there any internal pressure from Marc Pritchard's team to diversify away from social platforms for brand-safety or measurement reasons, and is that creating any reallocation conversations?

The Magnite PMP tests performed well enough to win — what performance metrics would need to be demonstrated on a direct or reserved buy to justify scaling TWC investment to a multi-brand level across the P&G portfolio?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Direct Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Publix is an active PMP customer (two consecutive wins in 2024 and 2025) spending $91.1M in digital. The play is to expand the relationship from PMP line items into higher-value, direct-sold premium inventory — capturing more of the digital budget that currently flows through Paid Social ($32.2M) and Display ($5.5M).
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins (Feb 2025, Sep 2024) in your outreach to Kevin Lang or via FIG's Ross Martin. Open with: 'We've now run two successful PMP cycles together — I want to show you what a direct, premium placement layer on top of that foundation could do for Publix's seasonal tentpole moments.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Kevin Lang: 'When FIG and your team plan around retail tentpoles like the Super Bowl, Easter, or back-to-school, how are you currently allocating between your $32.2M Paid Social budget and premium contextual placements — and is there a gap in weather-relevant or moment-based reach you're not fully covering?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position Weather Channel's premium direct inventory as the contextual bridge between Publix's existing PMP buys and the cultural/seasonal moments that drive grocery basket size — particularly relevant for a southeast-concentrated chain where weather drives shopping behavior (hurricane prep, summer grilling, holiday baking).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Publix's digital spend is $91.1M, with only $5.5M in Display and current PMP deals at nominal $1 line items. Even a 5% shift of the Paid Social budget ($1.6M) into premium direct placements represents a 10x+ expansion of the current TWC relationship. Frame the ask around a pilot at $150K–$250K tied to a specific tentpole (e.g., summer grilling or hurricane season).

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Ross Martin at FIG for a 30-minute planning call in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 30 minutes before end of month to map Publix's Q3 tentpole calendar against our premium inventory windows? I'll bring a proposal tied to their southeast footprint and weather-driven shopping moments.'

sales_plays.title: Search Intent Displacement — Capture the Bombora Signal
sales_plays.summary: Bombora shows Publix actively researching Search Marketing this week, yet their Search spend is only $935K — dramatically underweighted vs. $91.1M total digital. This signals either a vendor review or a budget reallocation conversation in progress. The play is to displace or supplement their current search/intent approach with TWC's contextual and audience targeting capabilities before a competitor locks in the conversation.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the Bombora signal as a credibility opener to Kevin Lang: 'We're seeing strong intent signals from Publix around Search Marketing this week — that tells me your team may be evaluating how search and intent-based targeting fit into your broader $91.1M digital mix. I'd love to share what we're seeing from a contextual intent angle that complements or extends what search alone can do.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'With Search currently at $935K against a $91.1M digital budget, is the team looking to scale search investment, or is the conversation more about finding intent-driven alternatives that reach grocery shoppers earlier in the decision journey — before they're already in a search engine?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's audience targeting and contextual placements as an upper-funnel intent layer that captures grocery shoppers when weather, seasons, and cultural moments drive purchase intent — before a competitor's search ad captures the click. This is especially relevant for Publix's southeast markets where weather events (tropical storms, heat waves) create predictable, high-intent shopping surges.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Publix's $935K search budget is less than 1% of their digital spend. If TWC can demonstrate that contextual/weather-triggered placements drive incremental basket lift or store traffic comparable to search — even at a $200K–$300K pilot — the ROI conversation becomes a displacement of lower-performing display ($5.5M) rather than a new budget ask.

Step 5 (Next Step): Send Kevin Lang a one-page brief within 48 hours showing TWC's southeast grocery audience reach and a weather-triggered campaign case study. Follow with a specific ask: 'Can we schedule a 20-minute call this week or next to walk through how this maps to Publix's Q3 planning cycle before your team finalizes search allocations?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Kevin — I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel. We've been working with Publix through FIG for the past two years, and I'm really proud of the PMP programs we've run together in 2024 and 2025. We've built a solid foundation, and I think there's a meaningful opportunity to do more together heading into the back half of the year. I work with a lot of southeast-focused retailers, and the contextual targeting we can offer around weather-driven shopping moments is something that's hard to replicate anywhere else — especially for a chain with Publix's footprint from Florida to Virginia. I was actually looking at your digital mix this week and noticed that your search investment is sitting at under $1M against a $91M digital budget — and our intent data is showing Publix actively researching search marketing right now. That tells me there's a conversation happening internally about how to get more out of intent-based targeting. I'd love to spend 20 minutes with you and Ross at FIG to understand where that conversation is headed and show you how we're helping other grocery brands capture high-intent shoppers before they ever hit a search bar. Would you have time this week or early next?
open_questions: The two PMP programs we've run together have been foundational — how is Kevin Lang and the FIG team thinking about evolving that relationship into direct or premium placements for Q3 and Q4 tentpoles?

With Publix's digital budget at $91.1M and Search at only $935K, who owns the decision to reallocate or scale intent-based targeting — is that Kevin Lang directly, or does Ross Martin at FIG drive that conversation?
Bombora is showing Publix actively researching Search Marketing this week — is that a vendor review, a budget planning exercise, or an internal push to expand search investment before summer?

How does FIG currently plan Publix's media strategy around southeast weather events like hurricane season or summer heat waves — is there a dedicated contextual or moment-based budget, or does that come out of the broader Paid Social allocation?

Publix's Paid Social spend is $32.2M — is FIG seeing performance pressure on that channel that might open a conversation about diversifying into contextual or weather-triggered placements for grocery-specific moments?

sales_plays.title: From PMP to Full-Funnel Partner
sales_plays.summary: Quest Diagnostics is an active PMP customer (two consecutive wins in 2024-2025) with $4.9M in total estimated media spend, yet current TWC investment is nominal. This play targets Garry Clark and the Siegel & Gale creative relationship to expand beyond the PMP into premium display, OLV, and mobile formats that align with Quest's existing $1.6M digital footprint.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Garry Clark directly referencing the two consecutive PMP wins (July 2024 and January 2025) and note that Quest's $1.6M digital investment signals a clear appetite for scaled digital reach — open by asking if the team is satisfied with the audience quality delivered through the current PMP.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Garry Clark: 'Beyond the PMP placements, which digital channels are you leaning on most heavily to reach patients actively researching lab testing or managing chronic conditions — and where are you seeing the strongest cost-per-engagement?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-driven audience targeting against Quest's need to reach patients at the moment health concerns spike — cold/flu season, allergy peaks, and heat-related illness windows all drive lab test demand and map directly to TWC's contextual inventory across display ($368K incumbent), mobile app ($258K incumbent), and OLV ($102K incumbent).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Quest currently spends $368K on Internet Display, $258K on Mobile App, and $102K on OLV with other partners. Capturing even a 20% shift of those three line items toward TWC would represent roughly $145K in incremental revenue — a concrete expansion target to bring to the conversation.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Garry Clark for a 30-minute working session with Siegel & Gale's John Gogarty included, within the next two weeks, to map TWC's contextual health audience segments against Quest's Q3 campaign calendar.

sales_plays.title: Cold & Flu Season Pre-Buy with Siegel & Gale
sales_plays.summary: Quest Diagnostics' core business — respiratory panels, flu tests, strep, and COVID diagnostics — surges during fall/winter illness season. This play routes through agency contact John Gogarty at Siegel & Gale to secure Q4 inventory before demand peaks, leveraging the existing PMP relationship as a trust anchor.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to John Gogarty at Siegel & Gale in late July/early August, referencing the two active PMP wins for Quest and noting that TWC's weather and health-intent audience data historically shows a measurable spike in lab-test-related search and content consumption beginning in late September — ask if Quest's Q4 media plan has been finalized.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask John Gogarty: 'For Quest's Q4 push around respiratory season, is the media plan currently weighted toward awareness or patient acquisition — and is there budget flexibility to add contextual health inventory before CPMs rise in October?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position a pre-Q4 TWC package combining Mobile App ($258K current spend category) and OLV ($102K current spend category) inventory, targeted to users actively checking weather during cold/flu season — a high-intent, health-adjacent moment that aligns with Quest's diagnostic test portfolio.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Quest's trailing 12-month spend is $4.9M total, with digital at $1.6M. A focused Q4 contextual health package at $150K-$200K would represent a meaningful but proportionate expansion of the existing TWC relationship without requiring a full budget reallocation from Siegel & Gale.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask John Gogarty for a brief 20-minute call before August 15 to share TWC's Q4 health audience reach data and hold preferred inventory — frame it as protecting Quest's access to premium placements before the fall demand window tightens.

talk_track_opener: Hey Garry, I'm reaching out from The Weather Channel — we've had the pleasure of working with Quest Diagnostics twice now through your PMP deals, most recently closed in January 2025, and I wanted to connect directly to make sure we're delivering the value you'd expect from those placements and to share a few ideas for what's next. We work with a number of health system and diagnostics brands and have seen strong performance when contextual weather signals are layered into patient outreach campaigns. Looking at Quest's broader digital investment — you're running meaningful spend across display, mobile app, paid social, and OLV — and I noticed there may be some whitespace where TWC's audience could complement what you're already doing with Siegel & Gale. Specifically, I'm curious: as you head into Q3 and Q4 planning, are you finding that your current digital mix is reaching patients at the right moment of health concern, or is there a gap between when people start feeling sick and when your messaging actually reaches them?

talk_track_opener: Hey Ann — I'm [Name] from The Weather Channel. We've been working with your team and Tandem Theory over the past year, and we've got two PMP wins together going back to October 2024. I wanted to reach out directly because we're seeing something interesting on our end that I think is worth a quick conversation. We're tracking live Bombora intent showing Rent a Center trending on Entertainment topics this week — which makes a lot of sense given your core product mix of TVs, gaming consoles, and home electronics. I wanted to connect before that window closes and share how we're helping retail brands like yours activate against that kind of in-market signal in premium video and CTV environments, not just display. You're running $12.5M in Online Video and $49.8M in Paid Social, and I think there's a real opportunity to put a slice of that to work in a higher-impact, brand-safe context — especially as we head into the planning cycles for retail tentpole moments. Would it make sense to grab 20 minutes this month — I can loop in Caitlyn at Tandem Theory — to walk through a custom proposal built specifically around Rent a Center's audience and SKU mix?
open_questions: With two PMP deals now live, is Tandem Theory evaluating a broader upfront or always-on commitment with Weather Channel, or are these being treated as one-off activations?

Who at Rent a Center or Tandem Theory owns the decision to shift budget from Paid Social into other digital channels — does that conversation happen at Ann's level in Plano or is it driven by Caitlyn's team?
Given the $49.8M in Paid Social managed by SOCi, how is Rent a Center currently measuring incremental reach beyond the social ecosystem — is there a cross-channel attribution model in place?
Is the $161K CTV budget a deliberate test-and-learn allocation, or is CTV scaling on the roadmap for the back half of 2025 as part of Tandem Theory's media plan?

With retail tentpole moments like back-to-school and holiday coming up, is Rent a Center planning campaigns around those windows now, and is there a brief already in flight with Tandem Theory that we should be responding to?

sales_plays.title: Steel Structures, Soft Digital Footprint
sales_plays.summary: RhinoSystems spends $11.15M in total media but only $461K in digital — less than 5% of budget. This play targets Ben Schibi with a data-driven case that their digital mix is dramatically underweighted relative to where commercial construction buyers and B2B decision-makers actually research prefabricated building solutions.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Ben Schibi referencing the imbalance: 'Rhino invests roughly $9.7M in broadcast but only $461K in digital — including just $49K in search. For a product where buyers actively research specs, codes, and suppliers online, that gap is worth a conversation.' Attach a one-pager benchmarking metal building advertisers' digital vs. broadcast mix.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'When a contractor or commercial buyer is evaluating a prefab steel kit, where does Rhino want to show up in that research journey — and do you feel your current $49K search budget is enough to own that moment?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position your platform's B2B audience targeting and intent-based digital solutions against Rhino's $329K paid social spend — showing how layering in targeted display and video ($61K and $19K respectively) leaves significant room to reach architects, contractors, and developers earlier in the funnel.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the opportunity: shifting even 10% of the $9.7M broadcast budget — roughly $970K — into targeted digital would nearly triple Rhino's current $461K digital investment and allow measurable, addressable reach against commercial construction decision-makers.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Ben for a 20-minute call in the next two weeks: 'I'd like to show you how two or three comparable building products advertisers are using digital to reach buyers your broadcast dollars can't address. Can we find 20 minutes before the end of the month?'

sales_plays.title: Account-Based Reach for Commercial Construction Buyers
sales_plays.summary: Rhino sells engineered-to-spec building kits to a definable universe of buyers — commercial developers, contractors, and architects — making them a strong fit for an ABM approach that targets named accounts or job-title segments rather than broad broadcast reach. This play positions your platform's ABM capabilities directly against Rhino's current $767K CTV and $61K display spend.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a LinkedIn or email message to Ben Schibi: 'Rhino's $767K CTV investment is a smart move — but CTV alone can't tell you which general contractors or developers actually saw your ad and then visited your site or requested a quote. I'd like to show you how an ABM layer changes that.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Do you currently have a defined list of target accounts — developers, contractors, or commercial buyers — that your sales team is actively pursuing, and are your media dollars mapped to those specific accounts?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position your platform's ABM targeting — firmographic, job-title, and account-list matching — as the connective tissue between Rhino's broadcast awareness spend ($9.7M) and their sales pipeline, turning anonymous impressions into measurable account engagement.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Illustrate with Rhino's own numbers: their $329K paid social spend is likely reaching a broad audience with limited B2B account precision. Reallocating even $75K–$100K of that into an ABM-structured campaign could generate account-level engagement data that justifies — or reshapes — the remaining $9.4M+ in traditional spend.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer a specific, low-commitment next step: 'If you can share a rough profile of your top 50 target accounts or buyer titles, I can come back with a reach estimate and a sample ABM campaign structure in under a week. Would that be useful?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Ben, I'm [Rep Name] with [Company]. We work with advertisers across the commercial construction and building products space, helping them connect their media investment to the buyers who actually spec and purchase — contractors, developers, architects. I wanted to reach out because we've done some work with companies in the prefab and structural building category and found a consistent pattern worth sharing. Looking at Rhino's media footprint — $11.15M in total spend, with $9.7M going to broadcast and only $461K in digital, including $49K in search — there's a meaningful gap between where Rhino is investing and where commercial buyers are doing their research. When someone is evaluating a prefabricated steel building kit, they're comparing specs, checking local code compliance, and vetting suppliers online before they ever call a sales rep. I'd love to ask you one question: does your current media mix feel like it's reaching those buyers at that research stage, or is most of your visibility happening at the awareness level?
open_questions: Your broadcast spend is $9.7M — how do you currently measure whether that investment is reaching the contractors and developers who actually spec and purchase prefab steel buildings?

With $49K in paid search, are you seeing enough volume and quality of inbound leads from buyers who are actively researching building kits online, or is most of your pipeline coming through other channels?
Do you work with a media agency to plan and buy your $11.15M in annual spend, or is that managed in-house through your marketing team?

On the $329K you're investing in paid social — are those campaigns optimized for B2B audiences like architects and commercial developers, or are they running against a broader consumer-style targeting set?

If you could add one capability to your current media mix — better attribution, more precise audience targeting, or stronger lower-funnel conversion — which would have the biggest impact on how you justify the media budget internally?

sales_plays.title: Rain & Storm Season Early Entry
sales_plays.summary: Bosch's wiper blades, windshield sensors, and MEMS-based vehicle safety systems see measurable demand spikes ahead of rain and storm season. With $960K in digital spend and Carat USA managing media planning, getting in front of this account 6-8 weeks before the demand window gives the rep a credible, data-backed reason to open a conversation now.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email or call Ivelina Valle, Senior Manager Channel Marketing eCommerce, referencing that Bosch's digital footprint ($960K total, $210K mobile app, $275K paid social) is well-positioned but that weather-triggered search and display demand for wiper blades and vehicle sensors typically spikes 6-8 weeks before peak rain season — and that most brands who win that window lock in placements now, not after the first storm alert.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Ivelina: 'When Carat USA plans your Q2 and Q3 digital buys for automotive accessories, is there a dedicated budget line for weather-triggered or seasonal demand moments, or does that get folded into the always-on display and search allocation?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position weather-triggered contextual inventory — connecting Bosch's MEMS sensor and wiper product lines to consumers actively searching during rain events — as a gap in their current $130K display and $91K search mix, which appears under-indexed for moment-based activation.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): With $1,051,228 in trailing 12-month spend, even a 10% reallocation toward weather-triggered placements represents roughly $105K in incremental targeted budget. Frame the opportunity as capturing high-intent in-market shoppers that Bosch's current paid social and mobile app spend ($485K combined) is not reaching at the moment of weather-driven need.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 20-minute call with Ivelina before end of the month — specifically to share a seasonal demand curve for automotive accessories tied to precipitation data — and offer to loop in Carat USA's Chioma Aduba if she prefers the conversation happen at the agency level first.

sales_plays.title: Carat USA Agency Door Opener
sales_plays.summary: Since Bosch is a net-new account with no CRM history, the fastest path to a qualified conversation is through Carat USA, which holds the media planning and buying scope. Chioma Aduba as President is the right entry point to establish credibility before going direct to Ivelina Valle on the brand side.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Chioma Aduba, President at Carat USA, referencing her agency's media planning and buying scope for Robert Bosch Automotive. Open by acknowledging Carat's role managing Bosch's $1,051,228 in annual digital spend and note that you have a seasonal timing insight — rain and storm season demand curves for automotive accessories — that may be relevant to their upcoming planning cycle.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Chioma: 'For Bosch's automotive accessories lines — wiper systems, sensors, power tools — does Carat currently activate any weather-triggered or contextual moment-based media, or is the current $130K display and $91K search budget running on standard always-on targeting?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position your platform as a way for Carat to add a high-ROI, weather-contextual layer to Bosch's existing digital mix — complementing rather than competing with the paid social ($275K) and mobile app ($210K) spend already in market — and frame it as a planning-cycle add that Carat can bring to Ivelina Valle as a proactive recommendation.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Show Chioma that Bosch's $960K digital footprint has a visible gap in moment-based activation. A weather-triggered pilot of $75K-$100K against wiper blade and sensor SKUs during the rain season lead-in could be benchmarked against the existing $91K search spend to demonstrate incremental reach and conversion lift.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Chioma for a 30-minute agency briefing — before Carat's next Bosch planning session — to walk through seasonal demand data and a proposed activation framework she can present internally. Offer to co-present with her to Ivelina Valle if that accelerates the conversation.

talk_track_opener: Hey Ivelina, my name is [Rep Name] from [Company] — we work with automotive brands and their agency partners to connect digital campaigns to real-world demand moments, specifically things like weather events and seasonal triggers that drive in-market purchase behavior for products like wiper systems, sensors, and vehicle accessories. We've helped brands in your category capture meaningful share during the rain and storm season lead-in by getting placements locked before the first major weather event hits and search volume spikes. Looking at Bosch's current digital footprint — roughly $960K across paid social, mobile app, display, and search — I noticed the mix is strong on always-on channels but lighter on moment-based or weather-triggered activation, which is typically where automotive accessory brands see the highest conversion intent. I'd love to ask you one quick question: when Carat USA is planning your Q2 and Q3 buys, is there a dedicated budget for seasonal or weather-triggered demand windows, or does that get absorbed into the broader always-on allocation? That answer would help me figure out whether what we do is actually relevant to your planning cycle right now.
open_questions: When Carat USA builds Bosch's Q2 and Q3 media plans, is there a dedicated budget line for seasonal or weather-triggered demand moments, or does that get folded into the always-on display and search allocation?
Who on the Bosch brand side — Ivelina Valle or someone above her — has final sign-off on net-new media partners and incremental budget allocations outside the existing Carat-managed plan?

Is Bosch's $210K mobile app spend currently running on owned-app placements, third-party inventory, or a mix — and is there appetite to extend that into contextual weather-triggered mobile environments?

Does Droga5's creative scope for Bosch include seasonal or weather-themed creative assets that could be activated programmatically, or would new creative need to be developed for a weather-triggered campaign?

Has Carat USA or Bosch previously tested any moment-based or real-time contextual media for the automotive accessories lines, and if so, what did performance look like relative to always-on benchmarks?
