sales_plays.title: FlexBreeze Win-Back: Weather-Driven CTV Reactivation
sales_plays.summary: SharkNinja has lost 5 consecutive opportunities totaling $265,000 since April 2024, including two FlexBreeze and two TowerFan buys. This play reopens the relationship through Horizon Next's Gene Turner before the next seasonal window, using CTV scale and weather-triggered targeting as the wedge.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Gene Turner at Horizon Next referencing the three lost FlexBreeze and TowerFan buys (May 2025, March 2025 x2) and acknowledge the pattern directly — 'We've been close three times on the fan and cooling portfolio. I want to understand what we missed so we can come back with something worth your time.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Gene: 'On the FlexBreeze and TowerFan losses, was the decision driven by pricing, targeting capability, or something about how we positioned the weather-trigger data layer against SharkNinja's CTV buy of $21.3M?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered CTV inventory as a direct complement to SharkNinja's existing $21.3M CTV spend — we reach consumers at the exact moment heat index spikes, which no standard CTV buy can replicate, and tie it to the Retail Technology intent signal Bombora is showing this week.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the ask around the $100K TowerFan buy that was lost in March — propose a $100K–$150K weather-activated CTV test against SharkNinja's cooling SKUs timed to the summer heat window, with measurable lift metrics to justify expansion into the broader $21.3M CTV budget.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Gene for a 20-minute call before June 15 to walk Kaitlyn Beauchemin's team through a revised FlexBreeze/TowerFan weather-trigger proposal — 'If we can get 20 minutes before the summer plan is locked, I'll bring a custom heat-index audience model for the cooling portfolio.'

sales_plays.title: Retail Tentpole Planning Trigger: Q4 Gifting Window Entry
sales_plays.summary: SharkNinja's Bombora signal on Retail Technology this week, combined with a $51.2M digital footprint and a CPG vertical that peaks around holiday gifting, creates a planning-season entry point. This play uses the Q4 tentpole window as a low-friction reason to re-engage Kaitlyn Beauchemin's team now, before budgets are committed.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Send Kaitlyn Beauchemin a brief note referencing SharkNinja's active Bombora intent on Retail Technology this week — 'Saw your team is researching retail tech right now. Given SharkNinja's scale in digital ($51.2M) and CTV ($21.3M), I wanted to reach out before Q4 plans get locked to share how brands in your category are using weather and cultural-moment data to sharpen holiday gifting campaigns.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Kaitlyn: 'For Q4 tentpole moments — Black Friday, holiday gifting — is SharkNinja planning to shift more weight into CTV and digital video, or is the $36.6M paid social budget carrying most of that load?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's audience intelligence and weather-moment targeting as a planning-stage tool that helps SharkNinja prioritize which SKUs (outdoor grill, tower fan, kitchen appliances) to activate by geography and temperature trigger during the gifting window — directly relevant to the Retail Technology research signal.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Reference SharkNinja's $21.3M CTV + $2.2M online video footprint and note that a 10% reallocation toward weather-triggered placements during the 6-week holiday window (Nov–Dec) represents a $2.3M activation opportunity with measurable incremental reach against in-market gift buyers.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a 30-minute Q4 planning session with Kaitlyn and Gene Turner at Horizon Next before July 31 — 'I can bring a category-level gifting audience analysis for small appliances so your team has a data point before the Horizon Next planning cycle closes.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Gene, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel. I work with consumer electronics and CPG brands on weather-triggered media — we've actually been in conversations with SharkNinja's team a few times over the past year on the FlexBreeze and TowerFan programs, so I know the portfolio well and I know we've been close. I wanted to reach out directly because I'd rather understand what we missed than keep submitting proposals that don't land. Here's what caught my attention this week: SharkNinja is showing active Bombora intent on Retail Technology right now, and with $21.3M already committed to CTV and $36.6M in paid social, there's real scale to work with. The question I keep coming back to is whether the weather-trigger data layer we bring is being evaluated against the right use case — cooling products like FlexBreeze and TowerFan are almost perfectly correlated with heat index spikes, and Q4 gifting is a window where small appliances consistently over-index. Before Horizon Next locks the Q4 plan, would you be open to a 20-minute conversation so I can show you what a weather-activated CTV model looks like against SharkNinja's actual SKU portfolio?
open_questions: On the three FlexBreeze and TowerFan proposals we lost in Q1 and Q2 2025, was the decision primarily about pricing, targeting differentiation, or something about how the weather-trigger data layer was presented to Kaitlyn's team?
Who ultimately made the call to pass on the TWC proposals — was that Kaitlyn Beauchemin directly, or did it come out of the Horizon Next planning process with Gene Turner?

With $21.3M committed to CTV, is SharkNinja evaluating weather-contextual inventory as a distinct line item in the plan, or is it being compared against standard demo-targeted CTV buys on the same CPM basis?

For Q4 gifting — Black Friday through holiday — which SharkNinja SKUs are getting the heaviest media support, and is the plan being built around cultural moments, weather triggers, or primarily platform-driven audience targeting through paid social?

Is the Bombora intent signal on Retail Technology this week connected to a specific initiative — retail media network evaluation, in-store tech, or something on the e-commerce side — that we should be aware of before bringing a revised proposal?

sales_plays.title: Sheetz Premium Inventory Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Sheetz has two consecutive won PMP deals with TWC Interactive and is actively spending $2.9M in digital. This play expands the relationship beyond PMP into adjacent premium formats — CTV, OLV, and weather-triggered display — where Sheetz has clear budget and intent.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two back-to-back won PMP deals (2024-07-24 and 2025-02-12) in your opener to Paul Crozier: 'Paul, we've now run two successful PMP campaigns together — I want to show you how brands at your spend level are layering in CTV and weather-triggered OLV to drive incremental foot traffic beyond what PMP alone can do.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Paul: 'Of your $2.9M digital budget, how much is currently allocated to formats that can dynamically respond to weather conditions — like a heat spike driving beverage or frozen treat demand at Sheetz locations?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered CTV and OLV against Sheetz's existing $2.0M CTV and $146K OLV spend — showing that weather-activated creative can make those dollars work harder by serving ads precisely when heat, cold snaps, or storms drive convenience store visits.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Sheetz is already spending $721K on Mobile App and $705K on Paid Social. A weather-triggered OLV or CTV extension of even 10% of that combined $1.4M would represent ~$140K in incremental TWC spend — tied directly to measurable foot traffic lift during high-intent weather moments.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Paul for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar before end of April to map a summer heat activation plan across CTV and OLV? I want to bring a proposal specific to your beverage and made-to-order food categories before the summer window opens.'

sales_plays.title: Summer Heat & Holiday Gifting Lead-In
sales_plays.summary: Sheetz has evidence-backed seasonal windows for summer heat (beverage, frozen, drive-thru) and holiday gifting (gift cards, loyalty). This play gets the rep in front of Planit and Paul Crozier before both demand windows open, using Bombora's active Demand Generation intent signal as the conversation hook.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the Bombora signal to Anna Rissanen at Planit or directly to Paul Crozier: 'We're seeing Sheetz showing active intent around Demand Generation this week on Bombora — that lines up exactly with what we'd expect heading into summer. I want to make sure we're not leaving weather-triggered inventory on the table for your heat-season push.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'For the summer heat window — think June through August — is Sheetz planning separate campaign flights for cold beverages and made-to-order food, or is it one unified push? And who at Planit or Red Tettemer is owning the media brief for that?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's summer heat trigger inventory: when temperatures exceed a defined threshold near Sheetz locations, serve CTV or mobile ads promoting frozen drinks, iced coffee, or drive-thru specials — directly connecting weather context to Sheetz's core 'fast, customizable' brand promise.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Sheetz's $721K Mobile App spend and $2.0M CTV budget confirm they're investing in high-reach, high-intent formats. A weather-triggered summer flight layered onto existing CTV — even a $200K–$300K incremental buy — would extend reach during the exact moments consumers are most likely to stop at a convenience store for a cold drink.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a joint call with Planit (Anna Rissanen) and Paul Crozier before May 1: 'I'd love to bring a summer heat activation brief to a 30-minute call with you and the Planit team before May 1 — that gives us enough runway to have creative and targeting locked before Memorial Day. Does that work?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Paul — this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel. We've had a great run together with two back-to-back PMP wins in 2024 and early 2025, and I want to make sure we're building on that momentum rather than just renewing the same play. You're running serious digital weight — $2.9M across digital, $2.0M in CTV, $721K in mobile — and I think there's a real opportunity to make those dollars work harder with weather-triggered activation that ties directly to Sheetz's peak demand moments. Here's what caught my attention this week: Bombora is showing Sheetz actively researching Demand Generation right now, and that lines up perfectly with the summer heat window coming fast. When temperatures spike, convenience store visits spike — especially for cold beverages, frozen treats, and drive-thru. We can serve CTV and mobile ads to consumers near Sheetz locations the moment a heat threshold is crossed. Before I put together a proposal, I want to ask: for your summer push, is the media brief sitting with Planit, Red Tettemer, or are you driving that from the category marketing side?
open_questions: Your PMP buys in 2024 and 2025 have both closed — what metrics did Sheetz use internally to evaluate those campaigns, and did the results influence how you're thinking about digital investment for the rest of 2025?

For the summer heat activation, is the media planning brief owned by Planit or Red Tettemer O'Connell + Partners, and does Paul Crozier have final approval on incremental digital buys above the current PMP commitment?
Sheetz is spending $721K on mobile app advertising — is that budget managed separately from the broader digital plan, and is there appetite to layer weather-triggered mobile formats into that flight?
With Bombora showing active Demand Generation research this week, is Sheetz evaluating new demand channels for summer, or is this more about scaling formats that are already working?

For the holiday gifting window, how early does Sheetz typically lock in media commitments for gift card and loyalty campaigns — and is Q4 planning already underway with any of your three agency partners?

sales_plays.title: Shein PMP Expansion: From $1 Test to Scaled Investment
sales_plays.summary: Shein has won two consecutive PMP deals (2024 and 2025) at nominal $1 CPM test levels. With $166M+ in total media spend and $52.2M in digital, there is a clear opportunity to convert these proof-of-concept wins into a meaningful, scaled budget commitment before the next retail tentpole window.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Claire Lin referencing the two consecutive PMP wins (July 2024 and January 2025) and open with: 'We've now run two successful PMP activations together — I'd love to show you what scaled performance looks like heading into the back-half retail calendar.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Claire: 'Of Shein's $52.2M in digital spend, how much is currently allocated to premium contextual environments versus open-exchange inventory — and is there a target for shifting that mix in 2025?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered, contextual audience as a direct upgrade path from the existing PMP test: Shein's fashion and lifestyle catalog maps directly to weather-driven purchase intent (seasonal apparel, outerwear, swimwear), which TWC can activate at scale with audience precision that open-exchange display at $4.6M cannot replicate.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Even a 5% reallocation of Shein's $52.2M digital budget equals $2.6M — more than 3x the current Mobile App line and a meaningful premium inventory commitment. Frame the ask around a phased scale-up tied to a specific tentpole (e.g., back-to-school or fall fashion transition) to make the number feel earned, not arbitrary.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Claire for a 30-minute planning call in the next two weeks to map Shein's Q3/Q4 retail calendar against TWC's seasonal audience peaks — offer to bring a custom audience overlap analysis as a pre-read.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars with Premium Video + Display
sales_plays.summary: Shein is spending $37.9M on Paid Social — the single largest digital line item outside of Search. With brand safety scrutiny intensifying around fast fashion and social platforms, TWC's brand-safe, premium contextual environment is a credible displacement candidate for a portion of that budget.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a market observation to Claire Lin: 'We're seeing several fashion and lifestyle brands actively diversifying away from Paid Social this year — particularly those managing brand perception carefully. Given Shein's $37.9M social investment, I wanted to share what that diversification looks like in practice for brands in your vertical.'
Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Is Shein's media mix review for 2025 evaluating any rebalancing away from Paid Social — and who internally owns that channel allocation decision alongside you?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Online Video ($811K currently in Shein's digital mix is a signal they're testing video) and TWC Display as a brand-safe, weather-contextual alternative that reaches Shein's core 18-35 demographic at high-intent moments — when they're checking weather before shopping, traveling, or planning seasonal outfit changes.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 5% shift of Shein's $37.9M Paid Social spend equals ~$1.9M — enough to fund a full-year premium video and display program on TWC that would diversify reach, improve brand safety scores, and complement the existing PMP wins already on the books.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to send Claire a one-page competitive displacement brief showing how a comparable fast-fashion or lifestyle brand allocated budget across TWC vs. social — request a 20-minute call to walk through it before end of month.

talk_track_opener: Hey Claire, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together since mid-2024 — you've now got two consecutive PMP wins with us, and I want to make sure we're building on that momentum rather than just renewing at the same level. You're running $166M in total media, with $52.2M in digital alone, and I think there's a real opportunity to make TWC a more meaningful part of that mix heading into the back half of the year. Here's what caught my attention: your Paid Social line is sitting at $37.9M, and your Online Video investment with us is still relatively early-stage. Given the brand safety environment right now and the fact that Shein's catalog — apparel, seasonal fashion, lifestyle — maps almost perfectly to weather-driven purchase intent, I think we're underleveraged together. I'd love to ask you one question before anything else: as you're planning Q3 and Q4, is the goal to scale what's already working in the PMP, or is there appetite to look at how TWC fits into a broader channel diversification conversation?
open_questions: The two PMP deals we've closed together have been at test investment levels — what would need to be true for Shein to scale those into a committed annual program in the $1M+ range?

With $37.9M going into Paid Social, is there a 2025 mandate to diversify channel mix or reduce platform concentration risk — and does that conversation sit with you or with a media agency partner we should be looping in?
Shein's Search investment at $113.4M is clearly the performance engine — how does the team think about upper-funnel spend as a driver of Search efficiency, and is TWC being evaluated in that context?
Are there specific retail tentpoles — back-to-school, fall fashion, holiday — where Shein is actively looking for incremental reach beyond what Paid Social and Search can deliver?

We don't have visibility into Shein's current agency roster — is media planning and buying handled in-house, or is there an agency partner we should be coordinating with to make sure TWC is on the plan?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Direct Upgrade: Unlock Shell's Full Weather Audience
sales_plays.summary: Shell has won two consecutive PMP deals (2024 and 2025) with TWC Interactive, signaling a trusted relationship. The opportunity is to convert that PMP foundation into a broader direct buy across Broadcast, CTV, and premium digital to capture the full $20.5M media budget rather than a nominal $1 PMP placement.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins — 'Shell has been a TWC Interactive partner through our PMP in both 2024 and 2025, and I want to make sure we're building on that momentum before Havas New York locks Q3 planning budgets.' Reach out directly to Andrea Bottini at Shell and copy Jason Xenopoulos at Havas New York.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Andrea: 'Beyond the PMP placements, how is Havas New York currently allocating Shell's $7.8M broadcast and $4.3M CTV budgets — and is weather-contextual targeting part of that conversation?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's owned-and-operated Broadcast + CTV inventory as the natural upgrade from the existing PMP relationship — same trusted environment, but with guaranteed premium placement, weather-triggered creative delivery, and audience reach that the PMP alone cannot guarantee.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Shell's combined Broadcast ($7.8M) and CTV ($4.3M) spend totals $12.1M — none of which is currently flowing through TWC direct. Even capturing 10% of that ($1.2M) represents a 1,200x revenue increase over the current $1 PMP deals. Frame this as a pilot direct buy against Shell's highest-reach formats.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute working session with Andrea Bottini and Jason Xenopoulos before the end of this quarter to walk through a custom TWC audience and weather-trigger proposal mapped to Shell's Q3 fuel and lubricants messaging calendar.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Spend: Move Shell's $5M Social Budget to Premium Video
sales_plays.summary: Shell is allocating $5.0M to Paid Social — a channel facing increasing brand-safety scrutiny and declining organic reach — while spending only $205K on Online Video and $76K on Display. TWC's premium, brand-safe video environment is a direct displacement opportunity for a brand like Shell that needs contextual credibility.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a brand-safety and attention-quality angle: 'Shell is investing $5M in Paid Social, and given the current platform volatility around brand adjacency, I wanted to share how several energy and automotive brands are reallocating a portion of that budget to premium video environments where context and brand safety are guaranteed.' Send this framing to Jason Xenopoulos at Havas New York as the media planning decision-maker.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jason: 'When Havas evaluates Shell's Paid Social performance, what benchmarks are you using for brand-safe reach and completed video views — and is there pressure from Shell to demonstrate premium context alongside scale?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's Online Video and CTV inventory as a brand-safe, weather-contextual alternative to Paid Social — Shell's fuel, motor oil (Rotella), and card services messaging lands with higher relevance when delivered alongside weather content that drives consumer decisions like fill-ups, road trips, and vehicle maintenance.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Shell's current Online Video spend is $205K against a $5M Paid Social budget. Shifting even 10% of Paid Social ($500K) into TWC premium video would represent a 2.4x increase in Shell's video investment with TWC and deliver measurably higher completion rates and brand-safe impressions than social feed placements.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to build a side-by-side comparison deck — TWC premium video vs. Paid Social benchmarks for the Energy vertical — and request a 20-minute call with Jason Xenopoulos at Havas New York within the next two weeks to review it before Q3 media plans are finalized.

talk_track_opener: Hey Andrea, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've had Shell as a TWC Interactive PMP partner for two consecutive years now — 2024 and 2025 — and I wanted to reach out personally because I think we're only scratching the surface of what we could be doing together. With Shell's total media investment running north of $20 million annually, and significant dollars in Broadcast, CTV, and Paid Social, there's a real opportunity to put more of that spend in an environment where weather context directly influences consumer behavior around fuel, lubricants, and road travel. I was looking at Shell's current digital mix — $5M in Paid Social, $205K in Online Video, and $76K in Display — and I kept thinking about how much stronger Shell's Rotella and fuels messaging would perform in a premium, weather-triggered video environment versus a social feed. Before Havas New York finalizes Q3 plans, I'd love 30 minutes with you and Jason Xenopoulos to walk through a proposal that builds on the PMP foundation and puts Shell's brand in front of the right audience at exactly the right weather moment. Would that be worth a conversation this month?

sales_plays.title: Simon Portfolio Expansion: From PMP to Full-Funnel
sales_plays.summary: Simon Property Group has two consecutive PMP wins (2024 and 2025) but their $955K digital footprint — anchored in display ($473K) and search ($271K) — shows clear whitespace in video and mobile. This play expands the relationship beyond PMP into premium formats that match Simon's 373-property footprint.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the back-to-back PMP wins: 'We've now delivered two consecutive PMP campaigns for Simon — I want to show Mikeal and Stephanie's team at Mindshare/Spark Foundry what incremental reach looks like when we layer in formats beyond display.' Pull the $473K display line as the baseline.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Mindshare or Spark Foundry: 'Of Simon's $955K digital allocation, how much is currently earmarked for video and mobile app formats — and is there a format cap or are those lines open for reallocation in the next planning cycle?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Simon's online video spend is only $24K against a $473K display base — a 5% video ratio for a brand with 373 properties and national mall traffic is a significant underinvestment. Position TWC's weather-triggered video and mobile units as the natural next layer on top of the existing PMP foundation.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Simon's trailing 12-month total estimated spend is $5.37M. TWC's current share is a fraction of that. Even capturing 10% of the $271K search budget as upper-funnel video would represent a meaningful format expansion. Model a $150K–$200K video/mobile test against Simon's highest-traffic mall markets.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Stephanie Spicer at Mindshare for a 30-minute format review call before the next planning cycle closes — specifically to walk through a TWC mobile + video proposal mapped to Simon's top 10 property markets. Suggest a date within the next two weeks.

sales_plays.title: Retail Tentpole Planning: Get on Simon's Calendar Before Q4 Locks
sales_plays.summary: Simon's retail vertical and active media relationship make them a strong candidate for early Q4 and holiday tentpole planning conversations. This play uses the planning-window timing hook to get TWC into Simon's budget discussion at Mindshare and Spark Foundry before allocations are finalized — not to pitch a specific campaign, but to secure a planning seat.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Stephanie Spicer (Mindshare President) and the Spark Foundry team with a forward-looking framing: 'Given the two PMP campaigns we've run together, I want to make sure TWC is part of Simon's Q4 and holiday planning conversation before budgets lock — retail tentpoles like back-to-school, Columbus Day weekend, and Black Friday are windows where weather context drives real mall traffic decisions.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When does Simon's Q4 media plan typically get finalized at Mindshare and Spark Foundry — and is Mikeal Thygesen's team involved in setting format priorities, or does that live entirely with the agencies?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Simon operates outlet centers and regional malls where weather directly influences foot traffic. TWC's ability to deliver weather-triggered messaging — e.g., cold-snap creative pushing outerwear at Premium Outlets, or rain-day promotions driving indoor mall visits — is a differentiated planning angle that generic display buys cannot replicate.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Simon's $955K digital spend is active but concentrated in display and search. A dedicated Q4 tentpole flight of $100K–$150K in weather-triggered mobile and display — timed to the 6–8 weeks before Black Friday — would represent a measurable incremental test against their existing PMP baseline, with clear foot-traffic correlation available post-campaign.

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a 20-minute 'Q4 planning preview' call with Stephanie Spicer and the Spark Foundry contact before end of current quarter. Offer to bring a one-page weather-retail timing brief showing TWC's reach in Simon's top 5 mall markets as a conversation starter — low commitment, high relevance.

talk_track_opener: Hey Stephanie — this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've had a great run with Simon over the past two cycles, delivering back-to-back PMP wins in 2024 and 2025, so I wanted to reach out directly to you and the Spark Foundry team before the next planning window closes. I work with retail and mixed-use property brands specifically, and I've seen how weather context can move the needle on mall foot traffic in ways that standard display just can't capture. Looking at Simon's current digital mix — $473K in display, $271K in search, but only $24K in online video across 373 properties — there's a real opportunity to build on the PMP foundation we've established. I'd love to ask you one question: when Simon's Q4 budget gets allocated across formats, is video and mobile still an open line, or has that already been committed elsewhere? That answer shapes exactly what I'd bring to the table for Mikeal's team.
open_questions: Of Simon's $955K digital allocation, how much is currently earmarked for video and mobile formats — and are those lines open for reallocation in the next planning cycle at Mindshare or Spark Foundry?
Who owns the final format-mix decision for Simon's digital campaigns — Mikeal Thygesen's team at Simon Brand Ventures, Mindshare, or Spark Foundry, and how does that approval chain work?
When does Simon's Q4 and holiday media plan typically get locked — and is there a planning brief or RFP process we should be part of before that window closes?
The two PMP campaigns we've run — are those evaluated at the property-market level or as a national brand buy, and does Simon track foot-traffic lift as a KPI against those placements?

Given Simon's international properties in Europe and Asia, is digital media planning for those markets handled through Mindshare globally, or is the TWC relationship scoped exclusively to North American properties?

sales_plays.title: Skechers CTV & On-Property Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Skechers is an active PMP customer with three consecutive won deals (2024–2026), yet their CTV investment ($5.5M) is dramatically underweight relative to their $92.5M broadcast spend. This play expands the relationship from PMP-only into premium CTV and on-property placements before the next retail tentpole window.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three-year PMP partnership (2024, 2025, 2026 wins) and open with: 'We've delivered consistent value on the interactive PMP side — I want to show you where we think there's a meaningful gap we can close together before the back-to-school and holiday planning cycles lock in.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'With $92.5M going into broadcast, how are you currently measuring the incremental reach CTV is adding — and is Siltanen & Partners benchmarking TWC's CTV inventory against your other video buys?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium CTV environment as the bridge between Skechers' dominant broadcast investment and their underdeveloped $5.5M CTV line — weather-triggered audience targeting aligns naturally with footwear seasonality (spring/summer outdoor, fall/winter boot cycles).

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Your CTV spend is currently about 6 cents for every dollar in broadcast. Shifting even 10% of broadcast dollars — roughly $9.25M — into TWC CTV would more than double your current CTV footprint and extend reach to cord-cutters your broadcast buy is missing entirely.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we get 30 minutes with you and Jennifer DeNick at Siltanen & Partners in the next two weeks to walk through a CTV reach analysis specific to Skechers' key DMAs before Q3 planning closes?'

sales_plays.title: Paid Social & Display Displacement — Weather-Triggered Precision
sales_plays.summary: Skechers is spending $35.7M in Paid Social and $22.7M in Internet Display — a combined $58.4M in digital environments where contextual relevance and audience precision are increasingly challenged by signal loss. This play positions TWC's weather-triggered digital inventory as a higher-intent displacement of a portion of that spend.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the spend data: 'We see Skechers running $35.7M in Paid Social and $22.7M in Display — that's a significant investment in environments where third-party signal deprecation is making audience targeting harder. I want to show you what weather-triggered intent looks like as a complement or partial replacement.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When Siltanen & Partners evaluates performance on those Display and Paid Social buys, what's the primary KPI — is it ROAS, foot traffic lift, or brand recall — and how is that benchmarked against contextual placements?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's weather-triggered display and mobile inventory connects Skechers' footwear categories directly to real-time consumer need states — rain drives boot and waterproof searches, heat drives sandal and walking shoe intent — creating contextual relevance that standard display and social feeds cannot replicate.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'If we displaced just 5% of the combined $58.4M Paid Social and Display budget — roughly $2.9M — into TWC weather-triggered placements, we can model expected lift in click-through and store visit attribution based on comparable footwear campaigns. That's a testable, measurable pilot against your existing benchmarks.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to send JD Moody and Jennifer DeNick a one-page weather-to-purchase intent brief for footwear by end of this week — would that be a useful starting point before we schedule a deeper conversation with the Siltanen planning team?'

talk_track_opener: Hey JD, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company — we've been working together on the interactive PMP side for three straight years now, most recently closing the 2026 deal in January, so I want to make sure we're being a real partner and not just a line item. We have strong relationships with the Siltanen & Partners team as well, so I want to bring something worth their time too. I'm reaching out because when I look at Skechers' overall media mix — $92.5M in broadcast, $82.2M in digital, and $5.5M in CTV — there's a real imbalance that I think we're uniquely positioned to help address, especially as cord-cutting accelerates into your core footwear consumer demos. I also see $58.4M combined across Paid Social and Display, and with signal deprecation pressuring those channels, I'd love to walk you through how weather-triggered contextual targeting has been performing for footwear advertisers in comparable spend tiers. Would it make sense to get 30 minutes on the calendar with you and Jennifer DeNick before Q3 planning locks in?
open_questions: How is Siltanen & Partners currently evaluating CTV performance against Skechers' linear broadcast buys — and is there a threshold ROAS or reach metric that would justify shifting budget from broadcast into CTV?
Who at Skechers owns the decision to expand beyond the existing PMP scope — is that JD Moody's call, or does it involve a broader media committee with Siltanen & Partners?
With $35.7M in Paid Social, how is Skechers managing audience targeting as Meta and other platforms reduce third-party signal access — and is there appetite to test contextual alternatives?
Is the 'Education' intent signal we're seeing this week tied to an active back-to-school campaign brief, and if so, what formats and channels are currently in the plan?

The 2023 Q2 and Q3 PMP opportunities were lost — what changed in 2024 that brought Skechers back to TWC, and are there any remaining objections or gaps in our current offering that we should be addressing proactively?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Sleep Number is an active PMP customer (two consecutive won deals in 2024-2025) with $25.9M in CTV and $25.1M in digital spend. This play converts their existing TWC Interactive PMP relationship into higher-value direct and premium inventory, leveraging the established trust with Horizon Media.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins (Jan 2025 and Jul 2024) in your outreach to Horizon Media's team. Email Maria Freda at Horizon acknowledging the active partnership and flag that Sleep Number's $25.9M CTV budget has room to move beyond PMP into premium guaranteed placements — especially ahead of retail tentpole moments like Presidents' Day and Memorial Day mattress sales cycles.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Maria Freda: 'For Sleep Number's next planning cycle, how much of the CTV budget is currently allocated to guaranteed versus auction-based buys, and is there pressure from Amber Minson's team to demonstrate incremental reach beyond what the PMP is delivering?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium direct inventory — weather-triggered CTV and contextual placements — as the logical next step from PMP. Sleep Number's comfort and sleep messaging aligns naturally with cold-weather content moments, and direct deals offer brand-safe, high-attention environments that auction PMP cannot guarantee.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Sleep Number spends $25.9M on CTV. Even a 10% shift from PMP to premium direct represents ~$2.6M in incremental deal value. Frame this as protecting Sleep Number's brand adjacency during high-competition retail windows when programmatic CPMs spike and brand-safe supply tightens.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Maria Freda for a 20-minute working session in the next two weeks to map Sleep Number's Q3 tentpole calendar against available premium inventory — offer to bring a customized audience overlap report showing PMP reach versus what premium direct adds incrementally.

sales_plays.title: Displace the Search-Heavy Mix
sales_plays.summary: Sleep Number allocates $46.6M to Digital Search — their single largest digital channel — while Online Video sits at only $235K and Display at $1.4M. This imbalance signals over-reliance on lower-funnel search at the expense of upper-funnel brand building, creating a displacement opportunity for TWC's video and contextual products.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a data-led observation to Amber Minson or her team: 'Sleep Number's search spend at $46.6M dwarfs your online video investment at $235K — that's a 200-to-1 ratio. In the mattress category, where purchase cycles are long and consideration is high, that mix may be leaving brand-building impact on the table.' Reference the Bombora Entertainment intent signal as evidence that Sleep Number's audience is reachable in content environments, not just search.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Amber Minson: 'What share of Sleep Number's new customer acquisition is currently attributed to upper-funnel brand exposure versus search retargeting, and has the team modeled what happens to search conversion rates if brand awareness investment drops?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-contextual video and CTV inventory as a high-attention upper-funnel alternative that feeds the search funnel rather than competing with it. Sleep Number's comfort messaging — warmth, rest, personalized sleep — maps directly to cold-weather and seasonal content moments where TWC's audience is actively engaged.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Reallocating even 2% of the $46.6M search budget (~$932K) into TWC video and contextual display would represent a 4x increase over Sleep Number's current $235K online video spend. Frame the ROI as reduced search CPCs over time as brand recall lifts organic and branded search volume.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to share a category benchmark report showing how comparable home goods and mattress advertisers allocate across search versus video, and propose a 30-minute call with Amber Minson's media lead and Horizon's Maria Freda within the next 10 business days to review the data together.

talk_track_opener: Hey Maria, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been partnering with Horizon and Sleep Number on the TWC Interactive PMP for the past two cycles — most recently closing in January 2025 — so I wanted to reach out as you're heading into mid-year planning. I work with a number of home goods and mattress advertisers on our platform and wanted to share something specific to Sleep Number's mix. Looking at Sleep Number's media allocation, the $46.6M in search spend versus $235K in online video is a pattern I see with brands that are optimizing hard for lower-funnel efficiency — but it can create a ceiling on new customer acquisition when brand awareness isn't feeding the funnel. Given that Sleep Number's audience is showing active interest in Entertainment content right now per our intent data, there's a real opening to test upper-funnel video and contextual placements in high-attention environments before the summer mattress retail window heats up. Would it make sense to get 20 minutes on the calendar to look at how the current PMP buy could expand into premium direct, and whether there's a case for shifting a small portion of search dollars into video to lift overall campaign efficiency?
open_questions: How is Horizon currently evaluating the performance of the TWC Interactive PMP buys relative to Sleep Number's broader CTV goals — are there reach or frequency benchmarks the PMP is being held to?
Who on Amber Minson's marketing team owns the decision to shift budget between search and upper-funnel video — is that a CMO-level call or does it sit with a performance marketing director?
Is Sleep Number's $46.6M search budget managed in-house or fully through Horizon, and is there appetite from the brand to test video as a search-efficiency lever in the next planning cycle?

What retail tentpole moments — Presidents' Day, Memorial Day, Labor Day — are highest priority for Sleep Number's 2025 media calendar, and how far in advance is Horizon locking in premium inventory for those windows?
Given the two Q1 2024 lost pitches totaling $70K, what feedback did Horizon or Sleep Number provide on why those proposals didn't advance, and has the buying criteria changed since then?

steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Stacie Jackson referencing Spanx's $516K Paid Social + $566K Display mix and note that brands in the women's apparel vertical who pair those channels with premium video inventory see stronger brand lift heading into holiday. sales_plays.summary: Bombora shows Spanx actively researching Agencies this week, and We Are BMF holds the AOR PR relationship. This play routes through Aaron Kirchner at We Are BMF to build a warm introduction to Stacie Jackson, leveraging agency trust before going direct.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Aaron Kirchner at We Are BMF directly, referencing that Bombora intent data shows Spanx researching agency and media partners right now. Position yourself as someone who works with AOR partners to bring media solutions that make the agency's work more measurable — not a threat to the relationship, an amplifier of it.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Aaron: 'As Spanx's AOR, are you involved in media planning conversations, or does that sit separately with Stacie Jackson's team? I want to make sure anything I bring is additive to what you're building for them.'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Show Aaron how a stronger video and CTV layer — built on top of Spanx's existing $516K Paid Social and $36K Online Video — would give We Are BMF's PR and creative work a paid amplification engine, making campaign storytelling more measurable and extending earned media reach.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Spanx's Online Video spend is only $36K today. A modest reallocation from Display ($566K) into premium video inventory could triple video reach while keeping total digital spend flat — a story Aaron can bring to Stacie as a strategic recommendation, not a cold vendor pitch.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Aaron for a 15-minute intro call or a warm email introduction to Stacie Jackson, framed as a joint conversation about Spanx's Q4 media mix — specific, low-friction, and positioned as a favor to the agency relationship, not a bypass of it.

talk_track_opener: Hey Stacie, I'm [Rep Name] from [Company] — we work with women's apparel and retail brands to help them get more out of their digital investment, especially when Search and Paid Social are doing the heavy lifting but video reach is still underleveraged. I noticed Spanx is running a strong performance media mix and wanted to connect before the holiday planning cycle gets locked in. Looking at Spanx's current channel mix — $4.8M in Search, $516K in Paid Social, but only $92K in CTV and $36K in Online Video — there's a real opportunity to build upper-funnel reach that feeds those lower-funnel channels more efficiently heading into your biggest tentpole moments. sales_plays.title: Brand Family Expansion Beyond HotShot
sales_plays.summary: Spectrum Brands is an active customer with 4 won HotShot weather-trigger deals in April 2025 plus a PMP win in January 2025. The opportunity is to expand the weather-trigger and data-driven model already proven with HotShot into adjacent Spectrum brands — FURminator, Spectracide, Remington, or Black+Decker — which have no visible CRM history.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email or call Tamoghna Ghosh referencing the 4 HotShot weather-trigger wins closed in April 2025 and the January 2025 PMP deal. Open with: 'We've now run weather-trigger and Nielsen product-index activations for HotShot across multiple formats — I want to show you how the same framework performed and what it could unlock for FURminator or Spectracide before summer peaks.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Tamoghna: 'Outside of HotShot, which brand in the portfolio has the biggest gap between its current digital investment and where leadership wants it to be — and is that brand currently using any weather or behavioral trigger logic in its media plan?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position the proven HotShot weather-trigger and Nielsen product-index methodology as a repeatable playbook. Spectracide (pest control) and FURminator (pet shedding seasonality) both have strong weather-correlated demand curves that map directly to the trigger logic already validated with HotShot.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Spectrum's total trailing 12-month spend is $9.46M, with $5.1M in digital. HotShot's trigger activations are a subset of that. A single brand expansion at even 10-15% of the current digital budget adds $510K–$765K in incremental investment — and the creative and data infrastructure is already built.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Tamoghna for a 30-minute brand portfolio review call within the next two weeks: 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar before end of May to walk through a trigger-readiness assessment for two or three other brands in the portfolio? I'll bring the HotShot performance benchmarks as the baseline.'

sales_plays.title: Retail Tentpole Planning Window — Agency Path via Echo Media Group
sales_plays.summary: Spectrum Brands allocates $2.9M to Paid Social managed by Echo Media Group. Q3 retail tentpoles (back-to-school, Labor Day, early fall lawn and garden/pest control season) are planning windows where budget decisions are made 8–12 weeks out. Reaching Echo Media Group's Alyson Shead now creates a pre-window advantage before budgets are locked.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Alyson Shead at Echo Media Group (AOR Digital Social, managing Spectrum's $2.9M paid social budget) and reference the existing TWC relationship: 'We've been working directly with Spectrum on weather-trigger activations for HotShot — I wanted to connect with you specifically because Echo owns the social side and I think there's a gap we can help close heading into Q3 tentpole season.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Alyson: 'For the Q3 retail tentpoles — back-to-school, Labor Day, early fall — how far out is Echo locking in the media mix for Spectrum's brands, and is weather or behavioral trigger data currently part of the social planning conversation?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's audience and weather-signal data as a complement to Echo's paid social execution — not a replacement. Brands like Spectracide, Black+Decker, and Remington all have seasonal demand spikes tied to weather and cultural moments that TWC data can surface earlier and more precisely than standard demographic targeting alone.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Spectrum's $2.9M paid social budget managed by Echo is the largest single digital line item. Even a 10% reallocation toward weather-triggered or contextually timed placements represents $290K in incremental or shifted spend — and the HotShot trigger wins in April 2025 provide a direct proof point for the model.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Alyson for a working session before June: 'Could we set up a 45-minute planning session before June — just Echo and TWC — to map Spectrum's Q3 brand calendar against our seasonal trigger windows? I can bring the HotShot activation data as a reference point so it's grounded in something you already know works.'

We've been working closely with your team on the HotShot activations — the weather-trigger, Nielsen product-index, and mosquito-activity campaigns that all closed in April — and the PMP we stood up together back in January. So we have a real track record here, not a cold conversation.

sales_plays.title: Digital Mix Upgrade: From Display to Premium Direct
sales_plays.summary: Sprouts is an active customer with a won Q3 Social Data Test and $58.6M in digital spend, yet TWC has captured only a small slice. This play targets the gap between Sprouts' heavy paid social and display investment ($25.4M + $10.5M) and their minimal TWC direct spend, positioning a premium on-property expansion across weather-contextual and food/health content.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the won Q3 Social Data Test with Lisa Nichols-Jell at Empower Ocean Media Group: 'Lisa, the Q3 Social Data Test we ran together showed proof of concept — I want to show you how we scale that signal into Sprouts' core $25.4M paid social and $10.5M display budget with TWC's on-property contextual inventory.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Lisa or Alisa Gmelich: 'Of the $10.5M going to Internet Display today, how much is running on premium contextual environments versus open exchange — and are you seeing the brand-safety and viewability benchmarks you need for Sprouts' better-for-you positioning?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's food, health, and weather-contextual on-property placements as a direct upgrade from open-exchange display — reaching Sprouts' core shopper (health-conscious, fresh-produce buyer) at the moment weather drives meal planning and grocery decisions, something generic display networks cannot replicate.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Sprouts has $10.5M in display and $6.4M in online video — even a 10% reallocation into TWC premium direct is $1.6M in incremental investment that buys verified contextual reach, not remnant inventory. Given the lost $50K Q3 Interactive opp, we have a clear benchmark to beat and a stronger product to bring back.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we set a 30-minute working session with you and Bloom Ads before end of this month to map Sprouts' Q2 tentpole calendar against TWC's contextual inventory — specifically spring grilling, fresh produce, and Earth Month moments — so we're ahead of the planning cycle?'

sales_plays.title: CTV Budget Displacement: Capture Sprouts' $2.8M CTV Spend
sales_plays.summary: Sprouts runs $2.8M in CTV and $6.6M in Broadcast but TWC has no won revenue in either channel. With Bombora showing active Entertainment intent this week and two lost Interactive opps totaling $100K in 2025, this play targets the CTV budget managed by Bloom Ads and Empower Ocean Media Group to displace incumbent CTV vendors with TWC's premium streaming and connected TV offering.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the Bombora Entertainment intent signal when reaching out to Lisa Nichols-Jell: 'Lisa, we're seeing Sprouts spike on Entertainment intent this week — that tells me there may be a content or streaming play in motion. I want to make sure TWC's CTV inventory is in the conversation before the buy is finalized.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'For the $2.8M Sprouts is putting into CTV right now, which platforms are getting the majority of that budget — and are you measuring incremental reach against the $6.6M broadcast base, or is CTV running as a separate KPI?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's CTV as the only premium weather-and-lifestyle contextual CTV environment that extends Sprouts' broadcast reach to cord-cutters while aligning ad delivery to real-time conditions that drive fresh grocery shopping — something incumbent CTV platforms cannot offer at the contextual precision Sprouts' health-focused brand requires.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'If even 15% of the $2.8M CTV budget — roughly $420K — moved to TWC CTV, Sprouts gains verified incremental reach on top of the broadcast $6.6M spend, with contextual triggers tied to weather-driven meal planning moments. That's a stronger ROI story than the $50K Interactive tests we've run, and a replicable model for Alisa Gmelich to take to the board.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to send you a one-page CTV reach analysis showing how TWC's audience overlaps and extends Sprouts' current broadcast footprint — can I get that to you by Thursday so Bloom Ads has it for the next planning review?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Lisa, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together on Sprouts for a while now — the Q3 Social Data Test we closed in August was a solid proof point, and I want to build on that momentum rather than let it sit as a one-off win. We have a real relationship here and I don't want to waste it on small tests when there's a much bigger opportunity on the table. I've been looking closely at Sprouts' media mix — $58.6M in digital, $10.5M in display, $2.8M in CTV, and we're seeing active Entertainment intent from Sprouts on Bombora this week. That combination tells me there's a content or streaming initiative being planned right now that TWC should be part of. Given the two Interactive opps that didn't close in Q3 and the holiday cycle, I want to understand what's driving those decisions — is it format, targeting, or measurement — so we can bring the right solution before the next planning window closes. Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar this week?
open_questions: Of the $50K Interactive opps that were lost in Q3 and the Vitamin/Health cycle, what was the primary objection — was it measurement, targeting capability, or budget prioritization — so we don't repeat the same proposal?
Who at Sprouts or Bloom Ads owns the final approval on CTV vendor selection for the $2.8M budget — is that Alisa Gmelich directly or does it route through Lisa Nichols-Jell's team at Empower?

With the Entertainment intent signal active this week, is Sprouts planning a content partnership or streaming activation for Q2 that we should be positioning TWC inventory against before the buy is finalized?

How is Sprouts currently measuring incrementality between the $6.6M broadcast spend and the $2.8M CTV budget — and is there a reach-extension KPI we could use to make the case for adding TWC CTV to the mix?

Given Sprouts' emphasis on organic, plant-based, and better-for-you products, are there upcoming retail tentpole moments — Earth Month, summer grilling, back-to-school — where Alisa Gmelich is looking for contextually relevant environments that paid social alone can't deliver?

steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Gina Mazzorana at Spark Foundry referencing the active Q2 2026 verbal at $50K and the March 2026 PMP win via TTD. Acknowledge the momentum and ask for a 20-minute call to share a cross-format performance brief before Q3 planning locks.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Gina: 'For Q3 and Q4, how is Spark Foundry currently allocating Starbucks' video budget across CTV versus premium digital video, and is there a target reach threshold that CTV alone isn't hitting?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's Interactive and Online Video inventory as the connective tissue between Starbucks' $1.5M CTV buy and their $600K Mobile App spend — closing the reach gap with weather-contextual, high-intent audiences that CTV alone misses.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Show that Starbucks' current Online Video allocation is only $32K against a $2.2M digital footprint — less than 1.5% of digital spend. A reallocation of even 10% of the $65K display budget plus incremental video investment could 3x their premium video presence on TWC properties.

sales_plays.title: Q4 Holiday Recovery — Win the Tentpole Before It Locks
sales_plays.summary: Starbucks lost a $100K Q4 Holiday Push opportunity in September 2025, suggesting they either went elsewhere or pulled back. Bombora shows active intent around 'Campaigns' this week. This play uses that signal to get ahead of Q4 2026 holiday planning before Spark Foundry finalizes commitments — framing TWC as the weather-contextual partner for fall and holiday cultural moments.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the lost Q4 Holiday Push opp ($100K, closed September 2025) directly with Gina Mazzorana: 'We didn't get to partner on last year's holiday push — I'd love to understand what won out and make sure we're in the conversation earlier this cycle.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'For the 2026 holiday season, is Spark Foundry planning to lock Starbucks' tentpole media commitments before or after Labor Day — and who owns the final sign-off between Gina's team and Tressie Lieberman's brand team?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered and seasonal audience segments as uniquely suited to Starbucks' fall/holiday cultural moments — pumpkin spice, holiday cup launches, cold-weather beverage occasions — reaching consumers at the exact moment weather drives hot beverage intent.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost Q4 opp was $100K. The active Q2 2026 verbal is $50K. A Q4 2026 holiday package that combines CTV contextual, Mobile App ($600K current baseline), and weather-triggered display could be scoped at $150K+ — recovering and exceeding the lost opportunity.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Gina for a 20-minute call in the next two weeks to present a Q4 holiday planning brief — specifically timed before Spark Foundry's summer planning review so TWC is included in the initial budget allocation, not added later.

We've been working with Starbucks through Spark Foundry on the Q2 2026 interactive buy and the March PMP via TTD, so I wanted to reach out directly while momentum is strong. sales_plays.title: Shift Paid Social Budget to TWC Premium Digital
sales_plays.summary: Stellantis allocates $137.4M to Paid Social — the single largest digital line item — dwarfing their $30.3M CTV and $4.4M Online Video spend. There is a clear case to redirect a portion of that social budget into TWC's premium, brand-safe, weather-contextual inventory.

Given where brand safety and signal deprecation conversations are heading, I'd love to share how we're helping other Tier A auto advertisers rebalance toward premium contextual environments.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Tom: 'Is Stellantis actively evaluating the efficiency of that Paid Social allocation against brand-safe, high-intent contextual channels — and who at the portfolio level owns that rebalancing conversation, Elizabeth or someone above her?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's weather-triggered display and video formats as a direct alternative to social feed placements — offering deterministic audience context (weather + location + auto purchase intent) that social platforms cannot replicate, especially for truck and SUV buyers in weather-sensitive markets.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Stellantis's Online Video spend is only $4.4M against $30.3M CTV — a 7x gap. Shifting even 2-3% of the $137.4M Paid Social budget (~$2.7M–$4.1M) into TWC premium digital would represent a 5-8x increase over current Online Video investment and materially expand the existing TWC relationship beyond the current $653,500 Q2'26 footprint.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive audit deck — delivered to Tom Hehir and looped to Elizabeth Strong — showing TWC's CPM, viewability, and contextual relevance benchmarks versus Paid Social norms in the automotive vertical. Offer to have it ready within 10 business days, ahead of Q3 agency planning cycles.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Waste: Redirect Steve Madden's $3.8M Social Budget
sales_plays.summary: Steve Madden's largest digital channel is paid social at $3.8M — a channel with rising CPMs and declining organic reach. This play positions TWC's contextual, weather-moment inventory as a smarter alternative for driving purchase intent across Steve Madden's multi-brand portfolio.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a data point in your outreach to Lauren Wilner: 'Paid social CPMs for fashion retail brands rose significantly in 2024, and Steve Madden is running $3.8M through that channel. I want to show you how two of your peer footwear brands are reallocating a portion of that budget to contextual placements that reach consumers at the moment they're making real-world decisions — like what shoes to wear in the rain.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'When you look at your paid social performance across Steve Madden, Dolce Vita, and Betsey Johnson, which brand is seeing the weakest return on social spend — and is there appetite to test an alternative channel against that brand specifically?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered display and video units as a displacement of underperforming paid social dollars. Rain, cold snaps, and seasonal transitions are natural purchase triggers for footwear and accessories — TWC delivers ads in those exact moments, which social feeds cannot replicate.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A $500K test reallocation from Steve Madden's $3.8M paid social budget into TWC weather-moment placements represents a low-risk pilot. sales_plays.title: Tentpole Planning Window

Connecting their work to broader video distribution could extend campaign impact without additional creative production cost.

sales_plays.title: Storm Season Premium Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Swatch Group is an active TWC PMP customer (two consecutive won deals, 2024 and 2025) with $45.7M in digital spend heavily weighted toward Paid Social ($38.5M). The rain and storm season lead-in is a high-confidence timing window to expand their TWC investment from PMP into direct, premium on-property placements before peak weather-driven audience engagement.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive TWC PMP wins (July 2024 and January 2025) in your outreach to Roberto Rodriguez at Mido. Open with: 'Given the momentum we've built together across the last two PMP cycles, I wanted to get ahead of the spring storm season window — TWC audience engagement spikes sharply in March through May, and I want to make sure Mido and the broader Swatch portfolio are positioned before that demand curve hits.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Roberto Rodriguez: 'Of the 16 Swatch Group brands you're activating across your $45.7M digital budget, which ones are currently NOT running on TWC — and is there a brand-level budget owner we should loop in at PHD USA or hi-gloss before Q2 planning locks?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's on-property direct placements (homepage takeovers, weather-triggered units) as the logical upgrade from the PMP foundation already in place. Swatch Group's $38.5M Paid Social concentration creates a reach gap in high-intent, contextually relevant environments — TWC's storm season audience is actively planning purchases, making it a premium complement to social retargeting.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Current TWC PMP investment is nominal ($1 recorded deal value) relative to a $45.7M digital footprint. Even a 1% reallocation from Paid Social ($385K) into TWC direct/premium inventory would represent a meaningful on-property expansion. Frame the ask around a pilot direct buy of $150K–$250K across 2–3 Swatch brands during the March–May storm season window.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Roberto Rodriguez for a 20-minute working session in the next two weeks — before PHD USA and hi-gloss finalize Q2 media plans — to walk through TWC's storm season audience data and a proposed multi-brand activation package. Suggest: 'Can we get 20 minutes on the calendar before end of January so I can share the storm season audience forecast and a draft package for your review before it goes to PHD?'

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Displacement — Weather-Triggered Precision
sales_plays.summary: With $38.5M of Swatch Group's $45.7M digital budget concentrated in Paid Social, there is a clear displacement opportunity to redirect a portion of that spend toward TWC's weather-triggered, contextually precise inventory — especially for watch brands where outdoor lifestyle and weather relevance are strong purchase signals.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Roberto Villazon, President at hi-gloss (AOR for Media Planning, Buying, and Social), and reference the existing TWC PMP relationship: 'We've been running PMP deals with Swatch Group since mid-2024 and I've been looking at their overall digital mix — $38.5M in Paid Social is a significant concentration. I'd love to share some data on where TWC's weather-triggered units are outperforming social CPMs for luxury retail, specifically in the storm season lead-in window.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Roberto Villazon: 'For the Swatch brands you're managing on social — are you seeing diminishing returns on CPMs or frequency caps that are limiting reach, and is there appetite from the client to test a contextual weather-trigger format as an incremental channel rather than a direct social replacement?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered display and video units as a precision layer that Paid Social cannot replicate — real-time storm and rain alerts create a contextual purchase moment for watch brands (waterproofing, outdoor durability, lifestyle). This is not a social replacement pitch; it's a 'contextual precision' add that justifies incremental budget from the $38.5M social pool.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Swatch Group's $337K in Online Video and $1.2M in Display are dramatically underweight relative to Paid Social. A $300K–$500K weather-triggered video and display test on TWC would double their current non-social digital video investment and give hi-gloss a measurable, reportable contextual performance story for the client. Frame against the $38.5M social baseline: this is less than 1.5% reallocation.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer Roberto Villazon a competitive displacement brief — a one-page data sheet showing TWC luxury retail CPM benchmarks vs. Paid Social averages, with a proposed weather-trigger pilot scope for 2–3 Swatch brands. Ask: 'If I send over a one-page benchmark brief by Friday, would you be open to a 15-minute call the following week to see if there's a fit for Q2?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Roberto, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working together on the Swatch Group PMP deals going back to mid-2024, so you already know what we can do in terms of reaching high-intent audiences at scale. I work specifically with luxury retail and watch brands on our premium inventory, and I've got some strong performance data from the storm season windows that I think is directly relevant to what you're planning for Q2.
open_questions: Of the 16 Swatch Group watch brands, which ones are currently NOT running on TWC — and are those brand budgets managed through PHD USA, hi-gloss, or Luxe Collective Group?
Is the $38.5M Paid Social budget managed centrally across all Swatch brands or allocated brand-by-brand, and who at hi-gloss owns the reallocation decision if we bring a contextual displacement case?
Roberto, for Mido specifically — are you planning any Q2 campaign activity tied to outdoor or water-resistance messaging that we could align to the March–May storm season window?
Are the TWC PMP deals running today tied to specific Swatch brands or running across the full group portfolio, and is there a performance report we can use to build the expansion case internally?
With Bombora showing active Branding intent this week, is Swatch Group in an active RFP or planning cycle right now — and what is the timeline before Q2 budgets are locked with PHD USA?

sales_plays.title: PMP to Premium Direct: Synchrony Brand Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Synchrony is an active TWC Interactive PMP customer (two consecutive wins, 2024 and 2025) with $10.7M in digital spend and a live Bombora signal on Branding. This play converts their programmatic PMP relationship into a higher-value direct buy — moving them from off-property inventory access to on-property premium placements that serve their branding intent.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email or call Chris Merrill referencing the two consecutive TWC Interactive PMP wins (2024 and 2025) and the active Bombora Branding intent signal. Open with: 'Chris, given Synchrony's back-to-back PMP activations with us and the branding push we're seeing in your intent data this week, I wanted to bring you something that goes beyond the PMP.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Chris: 'With $10.7M in digital spend and a clear branding priority right now, what does success look like for Synchrony's brand awareness goals — and is the current PMP setup giving you the contextual alignment and creative control you need to hit those benchmarks?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium on-property direct inventory — weather-contextual placements, homepage takeovers, and branded content units — as the natural upgrade from PMP. Synchrony's care, home, and BNPL products align directly with weather-driven consumer need states (home improvement, seasonal spending) that PMP cannot guarantee.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Synchrony is spending $60.5M in Paid Social and $34.7M in Display. Even a 3-5% reallocation toward premium direct TWC inventory represents $1.8M–$2.9M in incremental spend — a material step up from the nominal $1 PMP deals currently on record in SFDC.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Chris for a 30-minute working session with Phil Stephens at Stephens Direct included, to walk through a custom on-property proposal tied to Synchrony's current branding initiative. Suggest a specific date within the next 10 business days.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Waste: TWC vs. $60.5M Social Spend
sales_plays.summary: Synchrony is allocating $60.5M to Paid Social — the single largest line item in their media mix — while their Bombora signal flags active Branding interest. This play challenges the efficiency and brand-safety of that social allocation and positions TWC's premium inventory as a measurable, brand-safe alternative routed through Giant Spoon, LLC.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Route initial outreach through Giant Spoon, LLC (Creative Digital agency of record) rather than going direct to Chris Merrill. Reference the $60.5M Paid Social line and the live Branding intent signal: 'We're seeing Synchrony actively researching Branding solutions this week — given the scale of their social investment, we think there's a conversation worth having about where TWC fits as a complement or partial displacement.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Giant Spoon: 'With $60.5M running through Paid Social, what brand-safety and viewability benchmarks is Synchrony holding that inventory to — and is there pressure from Chris Merrill's team to show brand-building outcomes beyond click and conversion metrics?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Present TWC's premium digital inventory — $10.7M digital + CTV at $8.6M already in Synchrony's mix — as proof the relationship exists and can scale. Argue that TWC's contextual environment (weather, life moments, home and financial planning content) is structurally more brand-safe and contextually relevant for Synchrony's care, home, and BNPL products than broad social feeds.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 5% displacement of the $60.5M Paid Social budget equals $3M in redirected spend. Benchmarked against Synchrony's current $34.6M Search and $34.7M Display allocations, a $3M TWC direct buy is proportionally modest but would represent a 3,000x increase over the current nominal PMP SFDC revenue.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Giant Spoon to co-present a TWC brand-safety and contextual relevance audit to Chris Merrill within the next 3 weeks — framed as a media mix optimization review, not a pitch. Request a 45-minute slot and offer to prepare the deck.

talk_track_opener: Hey Chris, I'm [rep name] from The Weather Company — you may know us through the TWC Interactive PMP deals your team has activated two years running, most recently in February 2025. We've been a consistent part of Synchrony's digital mix and I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a bigger opportunity we haven't fully explored together yet. On our end, we're seeing Synchrony flagged in Bombora this week with active intent around Branding — which tells me there's a strategic initiative in motion right now. When I look at your media mix, $60.5M is sitting in Paid Social and $34.7M in Display, but the PMP relationship with us has stayed at a nominal level. I'd love to understand what's driving the branding push and whether the current allocation is giving you the contextual alignment and brand-safe environment that Synchrony's care and home financing products deserve. Would you and Phil Stephens at Stephens Direct have 30 minutes in the next couple of weeks to walk through what a more direct, on-property TWC program could look like?
open_questions: The TWC Interactive PMP deals in 2024 and 2025 have been consistent — what performance metrics has Chris Merrill's team been using to evaluate those activations, and have they hit the benchmarks Synchrony set internally?

With $60.5M allocated to Paid Social, who at Synchrony — Chris Merrill or someone on Phil Stephens' team at Stephens Direct — owns the decision to shift budget between channels if a stronger brand-safety or contextual relevance case is made?
Synchrony's Bombora signal shows active Branding intent this week — is there a specific campaign, product line (care, home, or BNPL), or audience segment driving that initiative right now?

Given Synchrony's FinServ regulatory environment, what brand-safety and contextual suitability standards is the team applying to the $34.7M Display and $60.5M Social buys — and is TWC's premium inventory currently being evaluated against those standards?

Giant Spoon holds the Creative Digital scope — are they currently involved in media planning decisions for Synchrony's digital buys, or is that sitting entirely with Stephens Direct and Chris Merrill's internal team?

sales_plays.title: Rain Season Displacement: Mobile App to On-Property
sales_plays.summary: TBC Corporation is spending $10M on Mobile App and $576K on Display with no visible on-property weather-triggered inventory. With rain and storm season approaching, this is the moment to displace off-property programmatic spend with contextually relevant, weather-activated placements that reach drivers at the exact moment tire and service urgency peaks.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Rachel Tibor at TBC Corp and CC Lance Porigow at The Shipyard. Reference TBC's $10M mobile app spend and note that weather-triggered, in-market tire shoppers are most reachable in the 2-3 weeks before rain and storm season peaks — not after. Ask for 20 minutes to show how on-property placements have outperformed off-property mobile buys for automotive service brands during seasonal demand windows.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Lance Porigow: 'Of TBC's $10M in mobile app spend, what share is currently weather-triggered or geo-fenced to drive-time moments — and how are you measuring lift to in-store service visits during storm season lead-in?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position on-property, weather-contextual inventory as a direct displacement of the $576K Display and a portion of the $10M Mobile App budget — showing that drivers actively consuming weather and traffic content convert at higher rates for tire and automotive service than broad mobile app placements.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): TBC's total digital footprint is $21.2M. Even redirecting 5% of the Mobile App budget ($500K) into weather-triggered on-property placements during the rain/storm season lead-in represents a testable, low-risk pilot with measurable store-visit attribution against TBC's existing franchise and company-operated center footprint.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Lance to schedule a 20-minute working session before the end of the current quarter — specifically to review a seasonal activation proposal timed to the storm season lead-in window, so TBC is live before competitors react.

sales_plays.title: Shipyard Referral: Agency-First Relationship for TBC Seasonal Activation
sales_plays.summary: The Shipyard holds Creative, Social, and PR scope for TBC Corporation. Lance Porigow as EVP of Growth is the agency door-opener. Building a relationship with Lance before approaching Rachel Tibor directly increases the probability of a warm introduction and positions the rep as a partner to the agency, not a threat.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Lance Porigow at The Shipyard directly — not through TBC. Reference The Shipyard's Creative and Social scope for TBC and note that you work with several automotive service brands on weather-triggered seasonal activations. Ask if Lance has 15 minutes to explore whether there's a fit for TBC's upcoming rain and storm season push, given TBC's $7.5M Paid Social and $10M Mobile App footprint.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Lance: 'As you plan TBC's Q2 and Q3 seasonal creative, are you currently integrating any weather-contextual or real-time trigger-based media placements alongside the Paid Social and Mobile App buys — and is that a conversation happening at the TBC level with Rachel's team?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position the opportunity as additive to The Shipyard's existing Social and Creative work — not competitive. Frame on-property weather-triggered inventory as a media channel that amplifies TBC's seasonal creative at the moment of highest consumer intent, making The Shipyard's work perform better and giving Lance a tangible ROI story to bring back to Rachel Tibor.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): TBC's trailing 12-month spend is $49.5M. The Shipyard's ability to influence even a small reallocation — say, $500K-$1M from the $10M Mobile App or $576K Display budget — into a weather-triggered seasonal pilot is a meaningful agency win and a low-risk proof point for Rachel's wholesale marketing team.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Lance for a warm introduction to Rachel Tibor or offer to present a joint seasonal activation proposal — co-branded with The Shipyard — before the rain and storm season lead-in window opens, so TBC is positioned ahead of competitors in the tire and automotive service category.

talk_track_opener: Hey Lance, my name is [Rep Name] — I work with automotive service and retail brands on weather-triggered media activations that are designed to capture in-market demand right before seasonal peaks hit. I know The Shipyard is doing creative and social work for TBC, and I wanted to connect with you first because the best programs I've seen in this space are built when the agency and media partner are aligned from the start. I'm not trying to disrupt what's working — I want to make TBC's seasonal creative work harder during the moments that matter most for tire and service sales.
open_questions: Of TBC's $10M in Mobile App spend, how much is currently weather-triggered or tied to real-time contextual signals like storm alerts or drive-time conditions?

sales_plays.title: Displace the Horizon Loss With a Weather-Triggered Contextual Buy
