sales_plays.summary: Target is actively surging on Entertainment intent this week per Bombora, and their $285.9M digital footprint is heavily weighted toward Search ($486.9M) and Paid Social ($102.8M). This play converts that intent signal into an on-property, brand-safe entertainment context buy before a competitor captures the moment.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Rick Gomez and cc Mike Tasik at EssenceMediaCom referencing the live Bombora Entertainment intent spike: 'We're seeing Target trending heavily on Entertainment topics in our intent data this week — wanted to flag a timely on-property opportunity before the window closes.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Mike Tasik: 'Is the Entertainment push tied to a specific tentpole — streaming launch, summer blockbuster, or a Target Circle promotion — and does EssenceMediaCom have budget allocated to contextual digital for that flight?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position Weather Channel's on-property entertainment and lifestyle content adjacencies as a brand-safe, high-attention environment that extends Target's $17.0M Online Video spend into a premium contextual layer EssenceMediaCom is not currently activating.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Target's Online Video allocation is $17.0M against a $285.9M digital total — roughly 6%. A modest reallocation of even 3-5% of that digital budget into on-property video would represent $8.6M–$14.3M in incremental premium inventory, dwarfing the $200K–$500K PMP losses from Q1–Q2 2026.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Mike Tasik for a 20-minute working session this week: 'Can we get 20 minutes on the calendar before Friday to walk through two Entertainment-context packages we've built specifically for retail — I'll send a one-pager today so you're not coming in cold.'

sales_plays.title: PMP Loss Recovery via Programmatic-to-Direct Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Target has lost $2.65M in PMP and seasonal opportunities across five consecutive deals (Q1–Q2 2026), suggesting the current programmatic path through EssenceMediaCom is not converting. This play displaces the PMP-only approach by routing a direct, guaranteed deal through Rick Gomez at the brand level, bypassing the agency bottleneck.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the pattern directly with Rick Gomez: 'Rick, I want to be transparent — we've had five proposals with Target over the past two quarters totaling $2.65M that haven't closed, including the Q2 PMPs at $500K and the Target Circle Week package at $200K. I'd rather solve the root cause than keep cycling proposals.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Rick: 'Is the decision on these PMP and seasonal buys sitting with EssenceMediaCom's trading desk, or does the brand have a direct say in which programmatic partners get prioritized for tentpole moments like Target Circle Week?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Propose a direct IO or guaranteed deal structure that removes PMP auction risk — positioning Weather Channel's first-party weather and location data as a deterministic targeting layer that EssenceMediaCom's current DSP stack (unnamed in their profile) cannot replicate programmatically.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The five lost deals averaged $530K each. A single direct guaranteed package at that level, structured around Target's Spring and Summer tentpoles, would recover the equivalent of one full quarter of lost revenue and provide Target with priority inventory access that open-market PMPs cannot guarantee.

Step 5 (Next Step): Request a brand-side strategy call with Rick Gomez — separate from the agency — within the next two weeks: 'Would you be open to a 30-minute call, just us, to align on what a direct partnership structure could look like for the back half of 2026? I can have a proposal framework to you 48 hours before we talk.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Rick — I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel. We've been working with Target's team for a while now, and across our 63 opportunities together we've built a real understanding of how Target approaches seasonal and tentpole media. We work with major national retailers on first-party weather and location-based targeting, and we know how to connect those moments to in-store and Target.com conversion.
open_questions: The Q1 and Q2 PMP proposals totaling $2M were both lost — is the decision on those buys sitting entirely with EssenceMediaCom's trading desk, or does Rick Gomez's team have direct input on which partners get prioritized?

EssenceMediaCom owns the digital scope, but no specific DSP or programmatic platform is named in your current setup — which platform is actually executing the PMP buys, and is that relationship up for review?
The Bombora Entertainment intent spike is live right now — is that tied to a specific campaign flight or tentpole that has budget already allocated, or is it still in planning?

Target Circle Week had a $200K proposal that didn't close — what would a package need to look like structurally (guaranteed vs. auction, targeting criteria, measurement) for that to get approved on the brand side?

With $102.8M in Paid Social and only $17.0M in Online Video, is there a strategic reason premium contextual video is underweighted, or is that a gap EssenceMediaCom is actively looking to close in the back half of 2026?

sales_plays.title: Brand Family Expansion Beyond PMP
sales_plays.summary: Teva has renewed its TWC Interactive PMP three consecutive years (2024, 2025, 2026), but total investment sits at symbolic $1 placeholder values with real media weight concentrated in Online Video ($387K) and Display ($184K) running outside this relationship. The play is to convert that external digital spend into incremental premium inventory through an expanded direct buy.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three-year PMP streak directly with Susan Hurst: 'Susan, you've renewed the TWC Interactive PMP in 2024, 2025, and again in January 2026 — that consistency tells me the partnership is working. I want to show you where we can extend that value into the rest of Teva's active digital footprint.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Susan: 'Of the roughly $387K Teva is putting into Online Video and $184K into Display right now, how much of that is running through Initiative on open-market buys versus curated private deals — and is there a performance gap you're trying to close?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium CTV and digital environments as the natural upgrade path: Teva already trusts the PMP rail; expanding into addressable CTV segments alongside existing $1.2M CTV spend creates a unified, brand-safe pharma context that open-market display cannot guarantee.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the opportunity concretely — Teva's combined Digital spend is $1.0M, with $571K in Online Video and Display alone. Even a 20% reallocation of that into TWC premium inventory represents a $114K incremental opportunity on top of the existing PMP relationship.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Susan for a 30-minute working session with Initiative's Michael Raymond (Creative Director) and the Initiative media planning team to map Teva's 2026 digital plan against TWC inventory — propose a date within the next two weeks before Q2 budgets lock.

sales_plays.title: Entertainment Intent Window — Content Alignment Play
sales_plays.summary: Bombora signals show Teva Pharmaceuticals USA actively researching Entertainment topics this week. While the direct campaign connection is unconfirmed, this intent spike — combined with Teva's existing $1.2M CTV investment — suggests a planning or content-strategy conversation is underway that TWC's entertainment-adjacent inventory is positioned to serve.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the intent signal as a conversation opener to Michael Raymond at adam&eveDDB or Initiative: 'We're seeing Teva showing elevated interest in Entertainment content this week on our intent data. Given the $1.2M already committed to CTV, I want to make sure you're aware of some entertainment-context placements we have opening up that would align with that interest.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Michael Raymond: 'Is Teva planning any campaign creative or media activations tied to entertainment programming — streaming, live events, or content partnerships — for Q2 or Q3 2026 that Initiative is currently mapping inventory for?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Connect Teva's Entertainment intent to TWC's content environment: TWC reaches audiences across weather-adjacent lifestyle and entertainment programming, offering brand-safe pharma placements in high-attention moments without the clutter of open exchanges where Teva's $17K Mobile App and $47K Paid Social budgets currently compete.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Teva's Paid Social spend is only $47K — a relatively thin investment for a company of 10,000–49,999 employees in a regulated vertical. A pilot entertainment-context sponsorship or content integration at even $75K–$100K would triple that social/digital engagement investment with measurable reach and frequency guarantees.

Step 5 (Next Step): Send Michael Raymond a one-page inventory brief this week — while the Entertainment intent signal is active — highlighting two to three specific TWC entertainment-context placements available in Q2 2026, and request a 20-minute call to pressure-test fit before the signal cools.

talk_track_opener: Hey Susan, this is [Rep Name] from TWC — we've been working together on the Interactive PMP for three straight years now, most recently renewing in January 2026, so I want to make sure we're being a real partner to Teva and not just a line item. We've helped a number of pharma advertisers bridge their PMP relationships into broader premium digital strategies, and I think there's a real opportunity to do that here. I noticed Teva is running meaningful Online Video and Display spend — around $571K combined — that isn't currently flowing through our relationship, and I'd love to understand how Initiative is allocating that and whether there's a performance gap we could help close. We're also seeing some interesting Entertainment intent signals from Teva this week that made me want to reach out now rather than wait. Would you and the Initiative team have 30 minutes in the next couple of weeks to walk through your 2026 digital plan together? I think we can find two or three specific places where expanding beyond the PMP would actually move the needle for you.
open_questions: Of the $387K Teva is spending on Online Video, how much is running through curated private deals versus open-market programmatic — and is Initiative satisfied with the brand-safety controls on those buys?
Who at Initiative or Teva owns the decision to expand the TWC PMP scope beyond its current parameters — is that Susan Hurst, or does it route through a separate Initiative investment lead?

Is the Entertainment intent signal we're seeing tied to a specific Teva brand campaign or therapeutic area — for example, a consumer-facing generics push or a branded specialty drug launch — that adam&eveDDB is currently developing creative for?

Given that Deloitte Digital's Heat holds the AOR Social mandate, what is the coordination model between Heat and Initiative when it comes to cross-channel digital planning — and is there a unified 2026 media brief we should be aware of?

Teva's Mobile App spend is only $17K — is that a deliberate deprioritization of mobile, or is there an unmet need for mobile-first pharma placements that TWC's addressable inventory could help address?

sales_plays.title: Summer Heat Upgrade: PMP to Premium Direct
sales_plays.summary: Tiffany has won two consecutive TWC Interactive PMP deals (Feb 2025, Oct 2024). With summer heat season approaching and $49M in digital spend on the table, now is the time to convert PMP dollars into a higher-impact direct buy before the demand window opens.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Melissa Wisehart at PHD USA referencing the two back-to-back PMP wins (Oct 2024, Feb 2025) and note that summer heat content moments on The Weather Channel are peaking in 6-8 weeks — ideal lead time to lock premium placements before inventory tightens.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Melissa: 'For the summer push, is PHD planning to stay in programmatic PMP or is there appetite to move Tiffany into guaranteed direct placements where we can build custom editorial context around warm-weather gifting and lifestyle moments?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium direct inventory as the natural upgrade from PMP — same audience, higher share of voice, custom content alignment with Tiffany's lifestyle and fragrance lines that benefit from summer seasonality.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Tiffany's current TWC PMP investment is nominal ($1 recorded per deal). Even a modest direct commitment of $250K–$500K against their $49M digital budget represents less than 1% reallocation but delivers guaranteed premium placement and editorial integration unavailable in PMP.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Melissa for a 20-minute call before May 15 to review summer inventory availability and co-build a proposal for Andrea Davey's approval — framed as a seasonal readiness briefing, not a pitch.

sales_plays.title: Shift Paid Social Dollars to High-Intent Weather Audiences
sales_plays.summary: Tiffany is allocating $40.7M to Paid Social — the dominant line in their $49M digital budget — while TWC's contextual weather audiences offer demonstrably higher purchase-intent environments for luxury gifting. This play targets a partial displacement of Meta/social spend toward TWC's digital and CTV inventory.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with Andrea Davey or route through Melissa Wisehart at PHD USA: reference the $40.7M Paid Social concentration and note that Bombora signals show Tiffany's own audience is actively engaging with Entertainment content this week — a signal that contextual, moment-based environments may outperform social feed placements for brand consideration.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'With $40.7M concentrated in Paid Social, how is PHD measuring incremental reach and brand lift beyond Meta's own reporting — and is there a test-and-learn budget available to validate a high-intent contextual channel like TWC against that baseline?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered digital and CTV inventory as a contextual complement — reaching consumers in a high-attention, lean-forward environment when they are actively planning activities, travel, and gifting occasions, versus passive social scrolling.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Tiffany's CTV spend is $3.8M versus $40.7M in Paid Social. A 5% reallocation from Paid Social (~$2M) into TWC CTV and digital would more than double their current CTV footprint on TWC and unlock audience extension across TWC's 100M+ monthly users in weather-intent moments.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a 30-day pilot brief — a defined $150K–$250K test on TWC digital displacing a slice of Paid Social — with agreed KPIs (reach duplication, brand lift, cost-per-engaged-visit) to present to Andrea Davey by end of Q2.

talk_track_opener: Hey Melissa, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've had the pleasure of working with PHD and Tiffany on two consecutive PMP deals, most recently closing in February. I wanted to reach out now, ahead of the summer season, because we're seeing inventory for premium weather-moment placements fill up fast and I'd hate for Tiffany to be locked out of the best real estate. We've built a strong foundation together and I think there's a real opportunity to take it to the next level this cycle. I was looking at Tiffany's media mix and noticed that Paid Social is carrying a significant share — around $40.7M — while TWC's contextual weather audiences are showing strong purchase-intent signals right now, especially with Bombora flagging active Entertainment interest from Tiffany's audience this week. Given the summer heat lead-in and Tiffany's broader lifestyle portfolio — fragrances, fine china, gifting occasions — I'd love 20 minutes to walk you through what a direct or test-and-learn buy could look like before the season peaks. Would the week of May 12 work for a quick call?
open_questions: Melissa, now that we've run two PMP cycles together, is PHD evaluating a move to guaranteed direct placements for Tiffany's summer campaigns, or is programmatic still the preferred transaction model for this account?
With $40.7M concentrated in Paid Social, how is PHD benchmarking incremental reach and brand lift for Tiffany beyond Meta's walled-garden reporting?
Is Andrea Davey's team setting specific KPIs around luxury audience quality and contextual brand safety that might favor a weather-intent environment over social feed placements?
Does Tiffany's summer media plan include activations for the fragrance and lifestyle categories specifically, or is the focus primarily on jewelry and core gifting?

Is there a test-and-learn or innovation budget at PHD USA for Tiffany that sits outside the core Paid Social allocation — something we could use to pilot a TWC direct buy without displacing committed spend?

sales_plays.title: Shift Paid Social Dollars to Premium Inventory
sales_plays.summary: Timberland is running $21.9M in Paid Social and only $623K in Online Video and $175K in CTV — a heavy concentration in one channel with minimal premium video presence. This play challenges that allocation by surfacing reach, brand-safety, and audience quality gaps that premium direct inventory solves.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Diana Soloaga at WITHIN referencing the observed $21.9M Paid Social concentration and note that for outdoor/workwear brands, social feed placements increasingly compete with UGC noise — ask for 20 minutes to share how peers in the outdoor-retail vertical are diversifying into premium video to protect brand equity.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Of the $21.9M going into Paid Social, how much is performance-driven versus brand-building, and is WITHIN seeing diminishing returns on reach as frequency caps tighten on Meta and TikTok?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position premium direct inventory — contextually aligned editorial environments around outdoor, work, and lifestyle content — as the brand-safe, high-attention complement that Paid Social cannot deliver, especially for Timberland PRO and the Premium 6 Inch boot collections targeting a professional and outdoor-enthusiast audience.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Even a 5% reallocation of the $21.9M Paid Social budget ($1.1M) into premium video and display would represent a 6x increase over current Online Video spend ($623K) and a 4x increase over CTV ($175K) — frame this as a test that de-risks the shift while generating incremental reach data.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Diana Soloaga to schedule a 20-minute inventory and audience walkthrough before the next quarterly planning cycle at WITHIN — propose a specific date two weeks out and offer a one-page competitive displacement analysis for the outdoor-footwear category as a leave-behind.

sales_plays.title: Pereira & O'Dell Creative Alignment Referral
sales_plays.summary: Pereira & O'Dell holds the AOR Creative relationship with Timberland. Building a warm introduction through the creative agency — before going direct to CMO Maisie Willoughby — reduces cold-call friction and positions our inventory as already vetted by a trusted partner in the account ecosystem.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Identify the Pereira & O'Dell account lead on the Timberland business and reach out referencing shared clients or category work in outdoor/retail — open with a question about how they are currently thinking about media environments that best showcase Timberland's visual brand identity for campaigns like the Premium 6 Inch or Stone Street collections.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When Pereira & O'Dell is developing creative for Timberland, which media placements do you feel are currently underserving the visual quality of the work — and is the $623K Online Video budget giving the creative enough room to perform?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Offer to co-present with Pereira & O'Dell to Maisie Willoughby or Diana Soloaga — framing our premium inventory as the media environment that makes their creative look best, turning the agency into an internal champion rather than a gatekeeper.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Reference that Timberland's total digital footprint is $26.3M, with CTV at only $175K — a brand of Timberland's scale and visual identity has a clear case for premium placements, and a joint proposal from Pereira & O'Dell plus our team carries more weight than a cold pitch to the CMO.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask the Pereira & O'Dell lead for a 15-minute internal alignment call this week — offer to bring a one-page brand-environment fit deck specific to Timberland's outdoor and workwear positioning so they can evaluate whether to co-present to Maisie Willoughby ahead of the next tentpole planning window.

talk_track_opener: Hey Diana, I'm reaching out from [Company] — we work with a number of outdoor and workwear brands helping them get more out of their media mix, particularly when a large share of budget is concentrated in Paid Social. I noticed Timberland is running a significant digital program through WITHIN, and I wanted to share something specific rather than a generic pitch. Looking at Timberland's current media footprint, $21.9M is sitting in Paid Social while Online Video is at $623K and CTV at $175K — that's a ratio that's pretty common in retail right now, but it's also one we're seeing brands in the outdoor vertical start to rebalance as social feed CPMs rise and brand-safety concerns grow. For a brand like Timberland where the product story — the craftsmanship, the outdoor context, the PRO workwear credibility — really needs room to breathe visually, I'd love to ask: is WITHIN actively evaluating premium video and direct inventory as part of the next planning cycle, or is the current allocation locked for the year?
open_questions: WITHIN manages Timberland's AOR Digital relationship — how is the $21.9M Paid Social budget currently split between brand-building and performance objectives, and is there a formal review process for rebalancing channel mix?

Who at Timberland — Maisie Willoughby or someone on her team — owns the final sign-off on net-new media partners, and does WITHIN have authority to pilot new inventory without a separate CMO approval?
Given Timberland's Bombora intent activity around Education this week, is there an active campaign or audience strategy targeting trade or workforce professionals for the Timberland PRO line?
With CTV at only $175K today, has WITHIN or Pereira & O'Dell presented Timberland with a case for scaling connected TV as part of the brand's upper-funnel mix ahead of Q4 tentpole planning?

Are there specific retail tentpole moments — back-to-work, holiday, or outdoor season peaks — where Timberland's current Paid Social-heavy mix has shown frequency fatigue or reach plateaus that a premium video layer could address?

sales_plays.summary: Timex is an active customer with a won PMP deal (closed Feb 2025) and $1.5M in total digital spend, but CTV is only $81K and Online Video is just $2K — a clear gap relative to their Paid Social-heavy mix. This play expands the relationship by moving Shari Fabiani and PHD USA toward premium video formats.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the February 2025 PMP win directly — 'Shari, the PMP we activated in February is a great foundation. I've been looking at your broader media mix and noticed CTV is at $81K and Online Video is essentially zero against $1.4M in Paid Social. I want to show you where we think there's an underweighted opportunity for Timex.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask PHD USA's Andrew Sacks: 'When you built the current plan, what was the rationale for keeping CTV so light relative to Paid Social — is that a reach strategy, a budget ceiling, or a measurement concern we can help solve?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium CTV and streaming inventory as the complement to Paid Social — 'Paid Social drives lower-funnel clicks, but Timex is a 170-year heritage brand. CTV on TWC puts the product in a premium, brand-safe environment alongside weather-relevant content that drives watch gifting and lifestyle consideration.'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Your current CTV investment is $81K — roughly 5% of your digital budget. A rebalance to 15-20% CTV, consistent with retail peers on our platform, would mean $225K–$300K in incremental CTV spend. We can model expected reach and frequency lift against your current Paid Social audience overlap before you commit.'
Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar with you and the PHD USA team in the next two weeks to walk through a custom CTV proposal built around Timex's Q3 brand priorities?'

sales_plays.title: Rain & Storm Season Lead-In — Weather-Triggered Watch Relevance
sales_plays.summary: Rain and storm season creates a high-confidence timing window for Timex — durable, water-resistant, and outdoor-lifestyle watches are directly relevant to weather-driven consumer mindsets. This play gets the rep in front of PHD USA and Shari Fabiani before the demand window opens, locking in inventory and creative alignment ahead of the season.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Andrew Sacks at AGENCYSACKS and PHD USA now — before the rain and storm season window opens: 'Andrew, we're seeing strong early signals that weather-driven retail categories outperform on TWC inventory heading into storm season. Timex's water-resistant and durable watch lines are a natural fit. I want to make sure we're not leaving that window on the table.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'Is Timex planning any campaign activations around outdoor, adventure, or durability messaging this spring or early summer — and has PHD USA built weather-triggered targeting into the media plan yet?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): 'TWC's weather-triggered ad units let Timex serve relevant creative — think Expedition or Ironman lines — precisely when users in storm-prone markets are checking forecasts. That's a contextual signal no social platform can replicate at that moment of intent.'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Timex is currently at $2K in Online Video. A weather-triggered video package timed to the storm season lead-in — even a $50K–$75K test — would represent a 25–35x increase in video presence at the exact moment outdoor and durability consideration spikes. We can benchmark against similar retail watch advertisers on our platform.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to send you and the PHD USA planning team a one-page weather-trigger brief specific to Timex's durable watch portfolio by end of this week — does that work, and is there a specific planner at PHD USA I should copy?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Shari — I'm [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Timex since the PMP deal we closed in February, and I've been digging into how that fits within your broader $1.5M digital footprint. What I'm seeing is a media mix that's heavily weighted toward Paid Social — $1.4M — with CTV at $81K and almost no Online Video investment. For a brand with Timex's heritage and product range, that feels like an underutilized opportunity on the premium video and contextual side. Here's what caught my attention specifically: rain and storm season is a high-confidence timing window for durable and outdoor watch categories — Expedition, Ironman, and similar lines map directly to the weather-driven mindset of consumers checking forecasts. We're already in market with PHD USA on the planning side, and I want to make sure we get in front of that window before it opens rather than after. Quick question — has your team built any weather-contextual or seasonal trigger strategy into the current plan, or is that still open territory for Q2 and Q3?
open_questions: The February PMP was your first activation with us — what were the performance metrics PHD USA used to evaluate it, and did it hit the benchmarks Shari's team set?
Who at PHD USA owns the Q2 and Q3 planning cycle for Timex, and is the media mix already locked or still in flight?
Given that $1.4M is in Paid Social, is Timex seeing diminishing returns on incremental social spend — and has there been internal conversation about diversifying into premium video or CTV?
Does Timex have any seasonal campaign windows tied to outdoor, sport, or gifting moments where weather-contextual targeting has been considered but not yet executed?
Is AGENCYSACKS or Forsman & Bodenfors developing any new creative for durable or outdoor watch lines that could be adapted for weather-triggered video units this spring?

sales_plays.title: Expand Beyond PMP Into Premium Broadcast Inventory
sales_plays.summary: Tommie Copper is an active customer with a won PMP deal (Feb 2025) and $4.6M in broadcast spend. The opportunity is to expand their TWC Interactive investment into premium linear or multiscreen inventory that complements their existing DR broadcast strategy.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the February 2025 PMP win with Josh Krauss directly — 'Josh, we closed your TWC Interactive PMP earlier this year and I want to make sure we're maximizing what that relationship can do for Tommie Copper given your $4.6M broadcast footprint.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Josh: 'How is the PMP performing against your DR KPIs — cost per order, ROAS — and are there dayparts or demos where you're seeing the strongest lift that we haven't yet scaled into?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium multiscreen and addressable inventory as a natural extension of the PMP — allowing Tommie Copper to reach the same pain-relief and active-aging audience across connected TV and linear with unified frequency management, reducing wasted impressions in their existing $4.6M broadcast buy.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'If we can shift even 10% of your $4.6M broadcast spend — roughly $460K — into premium addressable inventory on TWC, we can target compression and pain-relief intenders with measurable attribution that your current broad broadcast buy doesn't provide.'
Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Josh for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to review PMP performance data together and identify two or three inventory packages to test in Q3 2025.

sales_plays.title: Agency Whitespace — Engage Eicoff on Creative-to-Media Alignment
sales_plays.summary: Eicoff holds the creative scope for Tommie Copper, but there is no recorded media agency relationship in the CRM. This whitespace creates a timing trigger to engage Delia Marshall at Eicoff before any Q3 or Q4 media planning cycle locks in, positioning TWC as the preferred media partner alongside Eicoff's creative work.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Delia Marshall at Eicoff directly: 'Delia, we're actively working with Tommie Copper on their TWC Interactive PMP — I wanted to connect with you given Eicoff's creative scope to make sure we're aligned on how media and creative are working together for their DR campaigns.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Delia: 'Is Eicoff involved in media planning or buying recommendations for Tommie Copper, or is that managed separately by Josh's team — and are there upcoming creative flights we should be building media plans around?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC as the media partner that can give Eicoff's creative the highest-quality DR environment — reaching Tommie Copper's core audience of pain-relief seekers and active agers in brand-safe, premium inventory that amplifies the creative's performance.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): 'Tommie Copper is already investing $4.6M in broadcast. If Eicoff's creative is running in commodity inventory elsewhere, we can demonstrate how TWC's audience targeting and attribution tools can improve cost-per-order benchmarks on that same creative — giving Eicoff a measurable win to bring back to Josh.'

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Delia for a 20-minute call in the next 10 days to map Eicoff's upcoming creative calendar for Tommie Copper and identify where TWC inventory fits into Q3 or Q4 planning before budgets are finalized.

talk_track_opener: Hey Josh, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working together since we closed your TWC Interactive PMP back in February and I want to make sure we're building on that momentum rather than letting it sit. You're running a serious broadcast operation with over $4.6 million in spend, and I think there's a real opportunity to make that investment work harder for Tommie Copper. I've been looking at how your PMP is positioned relative to your broader DR strategy and I think there's a gap we can close together. Specifically, I'm curious — how is the PMP performing against your cost-per-order targets, and are there audience segments or dayparts where you're seeing stronger response that we haven't scaled into yet? I'd love to spend 30 minutes with you reviewing the numbers and mapping out what a Q3 expansion could look like.
open_questions: Josh, how is the TWC Interactive PMP performing against your DR benchmarks — specifically cost per order and ROAS — since it launched in February?
Is Eicoff involved in media planning and buying recommendations for Tommie Copper, or does your team manage media strategy independently from their creative scope?
What does your Q3 and Q4 media planning calendar look like — are budgets already allocated, or is there still flexibility to shift investment toward higher-performing inventory?
Beyond broadcast, are you currently running any connected TV or addressable campaigns for Tommie Copper, and if so, who is managing that inventory relationship?
Who else on your team or at Eicoff is involved in evaluating media performance and making recommendations on where to scale or pull back spend?

sales_plays.title: From PMP to Premium Direct: Expand Tommy John's TWC Footprint
sales_plays.summary: Tommy John has won two consecutive PMP deals (2024 and 2025) with TWC Interactive, signaling a reliable relationship. With $9M in digital spend and $6.1M in CTV, there is clear budget headroom to move from programmatic PMP into direct, premium inventory — including branded content, sponsorships, or weather-triggered placements that align with Tommy John's comfort-apparel positioning.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins — 'Catherine, we've now partnered with Tommy John two years running through your TWC Interactive PMPs, and I want to make sure we're bringing you the full picture of what's possible beyond the programmatic layer, especially heading into your key retail planning windows.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Catherine Sharkey: 'As you and the teams at Lockard & Wechsler and Mile Marker plan media for Q4 and tentpole moments like Father's Day or holiday gifting, where does premium contextual placement — not just programmatic reach — fit into the brief?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct inventory — weather-triggered video, homepage takeovers, and comfort/lifestyle editorial contexts — as the natural upgrade from PMP. Tommy John's loungewear and sleepwear lines are directly tied to weather and seasonal behavior (cold snaps, stay-home moments), making TWC's weather-intent audience a precision fit.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Tommy John carries $9M in digital spend and $6.1M in CTV. Even a 10% reallocation from open programmatic into TWC direct inventory represents roughly $1.5M in incremental partnership value — a meaningful step up from the current $1 PMP deal structure.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we set up a 30-minute working session with you and Cynthia Lambertson at Mile Marker before end of month to walk through our Q3/Q4 premium packages? I'll bring a tailored proposal tied to Tommy John's comfort and gifting moments.'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Dollars with Weather-Contextual Video
sales_plays.summary: Tommy John allocates $1.6M to Paid Social and $1.8M to Online Video — channels with rising CPMs and declining organic reach. TWC's weather-contextual video inventory offers a differentiated, lower-clutter alternative that can displace a portion of these dollars with measurable audience-intent alignment for a comfort-apparel brand.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a market observation: 'Catherine, we're seeing a number of comfort and lifestyle brands pulling back on Meta and YouTube spend as CPMs climb — and reallocating into contextual video where purchase intent is cleaner. Given Tommy John's $1.8M in online video and $1.6M in paid social, I wanted to share what that shift looks like in practice for brands in your space.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When Preacher and your media teams evaluate online video performance, what's the primary KPI — is it view-through rate, site traffic, or downstream conversion — and are you seeing the efficiency you need from your current social video placements?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's weather-triggered video units reach consumers at the exact moment comfort-apparel intent spikes — cold fronts, rain events, temperature drops — delivering contextual relevance that paid social algorithms cannot replicate. For a brand built on comfort, the context IS the creative advantage.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Tommy John's combined paid social and online video budget is $3.4M. Redirecting even 15–20% ($510K–$680K) into TWC weather-contextual video would represent a significant new direct investment, while likely improving cost-per-engaged-viewer against rising Meta/YouTube floor CPMs.

Step 5 (Next Step): 'I'd like to send you and Cynthia Lambertson a one-page comparison — TWC contextual video benchmarks versus category norms for comfort apparel — before our next call. Would Thursday or Friday this week work for a 20-minute follow-up?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Catherine, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel — we've been working with Tommy John for the past two years through your TWC Interactive PMP partnerships, and I genuinely appreciate the trust your team and the folks at Mile Marker and Lockard & Wechsler have placed in us. I wanted to reach out directly because I think we're only scratching the surface of what we can do together given the scale of Tommy John's media investment.
open_questions: Catherine, as you and the teams at Mile Marker and Lockard & Wechsler plan for Q4 and key gifting moments, how much of Tommy John's video budget is currently locked into programmatic versus available for direct or sponsorship buys?

Who ultimately owns the decision to shift spend from paid social or online video into a new channel — is that a call Catherine makes at the brand level, or does it require sign-off from the agency leads at Preacher or Mile Marker?
Are you seeing the efficiency you need from your $1.6M paid social investment, or are rising CPMs on Meta and Instagram creating pressure to find alternative video environments?
Has Tommy John explored weather-triggered or contextual media buys before, and if so, what did performance look like relative to your standard digital placements?

As Tommy John expands its women's line and broadens beyond men's underwear, are you planning separate media strategies or budget pools for those product categories — and could TWC play a role in reaching a female comfort-apparel audience?

sales_plays.title: PMP-to-Premium Direct Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Topgolf Callaway Brands is an active customer with a won PMP deal (Jan 2026) but has lost multiple Q4 2025 premium pitches. The goal is to convert the existing PMP relationship into a higher-value direct premium buy by leveraging the live PMP as proof of performance.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the won PMP deal from January 2026 with Alexis Westin at Mediahub Worldwide LA. Open with: 'Alexis, the PMP we activated in January is live — I want to share some early performance data and talk about what a direct premium extension could unlock that the PMP can't.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Alexis: 'For the Callaway and TravisMathew brands specifically, what KPIs did the PMP need to hit before Mediahub would recommend moving budget from programmatic into direct premium placements?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct premium inventory — weather-triggered units, homepage takeovers, and golf/outdoor contextual placements — as the next logical step above the PMP, offering guaranteed viewability, brand-safe context, and audience precision that programmatic pipes can't guarantee for a lifestyle brand like Callaway.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Topgolf Callaway Brands spends $73.1M in digital, with $53.1M in Paid Social and $8.1M in Display. Even a 2% reallocation of the display budget ($162K) into direct premium TWC inventory would represent a 5x+ step-up from the current PMP value of $1. Frame the Q4 2025 lost pitches (3x at $5K each) as a signal that the ask was too small — this time anchor at a $150K–$200K direct package.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Alexis for a 30-minute working session in the next two weeks to review PMP performance data together and co-build a Q2/Q3 direct premium proposal before the spring golf demand window opens.

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Displacement — Golf Lifestyle Moments
sales_plays.summary: With $53.1M of Topgolf Callaway's $73.1M digital budget concentrated in Paid Social, there is a significant opportunity to displace a portion of that spend toward TWC's weather-contextual and golf-lifestyle digital environments, where intent is higher and brand adjacency is stronger.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Bjoern Kreutzer, Director of Sports Marketing, directly: 'Bjoern, I've been looking at how Callaway and TravisMathew are showing up digitally — $53M in Paid Social is a serious commitment. I want to share a perspective on where weather-driven golf audiences are showing up outside of social feeds, and whether there's a case for diversifying into higher-intent environments.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Bjoern: 'When Callaway or TravisMathew runs a Paid Social campaign around a golf moment — Masters week, Father's Day, the spring equipment launch — what does the attribution model tell you about where the actual conversion is happening, and are you seeing diminishing returns on social CPMs?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-contextual digital inventory as a displacement candidate for 5–10% of the Paid Social budget. Golf equipment and apparel purchase intent spikes with weather signals (first warm weekends, spring forecast windows). TWC can serve Callaway creative to golfers actively checking conditions — a moment Paid Social cannot own.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): $53.1M in Paid Social at even a 5% displacement equals $2.65M in redirectable budget. A pilot at $250K–$500K on TWC weather-triggered digital and online video (current OLV spend is only $3.4M, well below category norms) would be a low-risk test with measurable lift data Mediahub can bring back to the broader planning conversation.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer Bjoern a brief — 'I can put together a one-pager showing golf audience weather-intent windows on TWC inventory with a proposed $250K pilot structure. Can I get 20 minutes with you and Alexis Westin together before end of month to walk through it?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Alexis, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together on the Topgolf Callaway Brands PMP that went live in January, and I wanted to connect now — before the spring golf season kicks into gear — because I think there's a real opportunity to build on what's already in market. We have a track record with this account across 45 opportunities, and I want to make sure we're getting you the most out of what TWC can actually do for Callaway and TravisMathew beyond the programmatic layer. Here's what caught my attention: Topgolf Callaway Brands is putting $53 million into Paid Social and only $3.4 million into Online Video — and the PMP we won together is essentially a $1 placeholder right now. That tells me there's a planning conversation that hasn't happened yet about where weather-contextual, high-intent golf audiences fit into the mix. My question for you is simple: when Mediahub is mapping out Q2 and Q3 for Callaway, what would it take to put a direct premium TWC buy on the plan alongside the PMP?
open_questions: The January 2026 PMP is live but showing minimal spend — what does Mediahub need to see from the PMP performance before recommending a direct premium buy for Callaway or TravisMathew?
With $53 million in Paid Social, is Mediahub seeing any CPM inflation or reach saturation on Meta and TikTok for the Callaway audience, and is there a mandate to diversify digital spend in 2026?

Bjoern, on the sports marketing side — are Callaway's spring equipment launch campaigns planned far enough in advance to incorporate weather-triggered media placements, or is the timeline too compressed for that kind of activation?
The Q4 2025 pitches were lost — can you help me understand whether those were budget decisions, timing issues, or a fit problem with the specific inventory we proposed?

With the planned separation of Topgolf from Callaway Brands, will media planning and buying for the two entities split across different agency relationships, or will Mediahub Worldwide continue to hold AOR responsibilities for both?

sales_plays.title: Snow Campaign Recovery & TDA Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Toyota is an active customer with won TDA deals in Kansas ($93K) and Denver ($275K), but lost the 2026 Snow campaign ($1.5M) and Tier 2 2026 Campaigns ($1M). This play expands the TDA footprint into additional markets while re-opening the weather-driven seasonal conversation ahead of the next planning cycle.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Mark Turner at Saatchi & Saatchi Dallas referencing the Denver TDA win ($275K, closed Dec 2025) and Kansas TDA win ($93K, closed Jan 2026) as proof of regional performance. Acknowledge the Snow campaign ($1.5M) did not close and ask for a 20-minute debrief to understand what the winning solution offered that TWC did not.

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Mark: 'Beyond Denver and Kansas, which TDA markets are still unallocated or underperforming in the 2026 Tier 2 plan — and is weather-driven creative sequencing part of the brief for those markets?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered programmatic and interactive units as the connective tissue between Saatchi Dallas's programmatic buying and the dealer-level TDA activation — specifically for markets where weather events (snow, ice, rain) drive RAV4, Tacoma, and 4Runner consideration spikes.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The two won TDA deals total $368K. The lost Snow campaign alone was $1.5M. Closing even 50% of the Snow-equivalent opportunity across 3–4 additional TDA markets represents $750K–$1.2M in incremental revenue against Toyota's $459.8M digital spend — a rounding error for them, a meaningful expansion for this account.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Mark Turner for a 20-minute working session before the end of the current quarter to map open TDA markets against TWC weather audience data — framed as a planning input, not a pitch.

sales_plays.title: Displace Programmatic Spend with Direct Weather-Triggered Inventory
sales_plays.summary: Toyota's $179.1M in Paid Social and $52.3M in Display are largely programmatic. The existing TWC PMP deal (won Jan 2026, $1) signals a test relationship. This play converts that PMP test into a direct, premium weather-triggered buy, displacing open-exchange or third-party programmatic dollars managed by Saatchi Dallas.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the Toyota PMP deal closed Jan 3, 2026 (currently $1 placeholder) and reach out to Mark Turner at Saatchi Dallas: 'The PMP is live — I want to show you what the data looks like so far and talk about whether a direct deal structure would give you better controls and pricing efficiency than the open-exchange path.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'For the programmatic display and online video budget Saatchi Dallas manages for Toyota — what percentage is currently running through PMPs versus open exchange, and is brand safety or weather-contextual alignment part of the evaluation criteria for upgrading those deals?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's direct weather-triggered inventory offers deterministic context (weather event + vehicle intent) that open-exchange programmatic cannot replicate. For Toyota's $52.3M Display and $39.4M Online Video budgets, even a 5–10% shift to direct contextual buys delivers measurable lift in relevance scores and dealer traffic correlation.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 5% shift of Toyota's combined Display + Online Video spend ($91.7M) equals $4.6M. A 2% shift equals $1.8M. Frame the ask as a $500K–$750K direct test against the existing PMP baseline — a number that fits within Saatchi Dallas's discretionary reallocation authority without requiring Toyota HQ approval.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a side-by-side performance comparison: PMP results to date vs. a direct weather-triggered line item running for 60 days. Ask Mark Turner to confirm the right trafficking contact at Saatchi Dallas to set up the test before the next quarterly budget review.

talk_track_opener: Hey Mark, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together on the Toyota TDA business — the Denver deal closed in December and Kansas wrapped up in January, so we've got real performance data in market right now. I also know the Snow campaign didn't come together for 2026, and I'd rather understand why than pretend it didn't happen. We've got a PMP live with Toyota as of January, so there's already a technical relationship in place — I want to build on that, not start from scratch. I'm reaching out because I think there's a logical next step that fits how Saatchi Dallas already operates. Toyota is running $52.3M in Display and $39.4M in Online Video, and a meaningful portion of that is likely flowing through open exchange. The PMP we have live is a starting point, but a direct weather-triggered buy gives you contextual precision — RAV4 and Tacoma creative served when a winter weather event is active in a specific DMA — that programmatic pipes just can't replicate. I'm not asking for a big commitment. I want 20 minutes to show you what the PMP data looks like and map out which TDA markets are still open for 2026. Can we find time before end of quarter?
open_questions: The Snow campaign ($1.5M) closed as Lost in December — can you share what the winning solution offered that made the difference, so we can close that gap before the next seasonal planning cycle?
Beyond Denver and Kansas, which TDA markets in the 2026 Tier 2 plan are still unallocated or under-indexed on weather-contextual digital — and who owns those market-level decisions at Saatchi Dallas?
The Toyota PMP is live as of January — who on your team is monitoring performance, and what KPIs would need to move for you to consider converting it to a direct deal with a larger commitment?

For the $52.3M Display and $39.4M Online Video budgets, what percentage is currently running through open exchange versus curated PMPs, and is there a brand safety or contextual alignment mandate from Toyota HQ that a direct weather-triggered buy could help satisfy?

Is Briana Nelson at Toyota USA Newsroom involved in the media planning process, or is all digital buying authority sitting with Saatchi Dallas — and is there a Toyota-side marketing contact we should be looping in for the TDA expansion conversation?

sales_plays.title: Win Back TSC on Weather-Driven Always On
sales_plays.summary: Tractor Supply Co has declined three consecutive proposals totaling ~$597K across 2025 and early 2026. This play re-engages CMO Kimberley Gardiner or agency partner Carrie Parks at Carmichael Lynch to understand what broke down and reposition around a refined, lower-friction entry point.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Carrie Parks at Carmichael Lynch referencing the three lost opps by name — TSC 1H 2026 ($240K), Always On & Extreme Weather ($257K), and 3Q'25 ($100K) — and acknowledge the pattern directly: 'We've proposed three times and haven't earned the business. I'd rather understand why than pitch a fourth time blind.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Carrie: 'When the Always On & Extreme Weather proposal came across in December, what was the single biggest reason it didn't move forward — was it budget timing, the format, the audience story, or something else entirely?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Once the objection is named, reframe: TSC's $2.6M broadcast and $842K CTV spend shows they're investing in upper-funnel video — position Weather Channel's contextual severe-weather and seasonal triggers as the connective tissue between that broadcast reach and the $14M search spend that captures demand downstream.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Reference the $597K in declined proposals and propose a pilot sized at roughly one-third — ~$100K Always On entry — with clear measurement tied to in-store traffic lift during weather events, a metric directly relevant to TSC's lawn, animal care, and equipment categories.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Carrie for a 20-minute call in the next two weeks specifically to debrief the December loss — frame it as a listening session, not a pitch, with Kimberley Gardiner looped in if she's open to it.

sales_plays.title: Get Ahead of TSC's Retail Tentpole Planning Window
sales_plays.summary: Bombora shows Tractor Supply Co actively researching Retail Technology this week, signaling planning-cycle activity. Combined with TSC's spring/summer seasonal peak for lawn, garden, and animal care, this is a credible moment to open a planning conversation before budgets are locked — not to pitch a specific package, but to earn a seat at the table.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Send a brief note to Kimberley Gardiner referencing the Bombora signal directly: 'We're seeing Tractor Supply Co showing active interest in Retail Technology this week — that usually means planning conversations are happening. I wanted to reach out before the spring budget window closes rather than after.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Kimberley: 'As you plan for spring and summer tentpole moments — think lawn season, Easter, Memorial Day — how are you currently thinking about connecting TSC's broadcast and CTV investment to the in-store demand those campaigns are meant to drive?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position Weather Channel as a planning partner for seasonal and cultural moments: TSC's core categories — lawn care, animal care, outdoor equipment — are directly weather-dependent, and contextual weather triggers (first frost, spring warm-up, storm prep) are natural demand signals that can be built into a tentpole calendar alongside TSC's existing $3.1M paid social and $14M search spend.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Note that TSC's $842K CTV allocation is relatively modest against a $2.6M broadcast base — a weather-contextual CTV layer tied to seasonal triggers could extend that reach efficiently without requiring a net-new budget line, framing this as optimization of existing spend rather than incremental ask.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute planning session in the next three weeks with Carrie Parks at Carmichael Lynch included — frame it as a seasonal calendar review, not a proposal, to lower the commitment threshold and rebuild the relationship before the spring window opens.

talk_track_opener: Hey Carrie, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Channel. We've worked with Carmichael Lynch on a few TSC proposals over the past year — the Always On and Extreme Weather package in December, the 1H 2026 media plan, and the 3Q initiative before that — and I'll be honest, we haven't earned the business yet and I'd rather understand why than just send another deck. I have a lot of respect for what the team is building with TSC and I don't want to waste your time with a fourth proposal that misses the mark the same way the last three did. I'm seeing some signals this week that TSC is in active planning mode around retail technology and seasonal campaigns, and given that spring is TSC's biggest demand window for lawn, animal care, and outdoor equipment, I think there's a real conversation worth having before budgets get locked. Would you be open to a quick 20-minute debrief on what didn't land in December? No pitch — I just want to understand the gap so that if we come back to the table, it's actually worth your time.
open_questions: When the Always On and Extreme Weather proposal came across in December at $257K, what was the primary reason it didn't advance — was it the budget size, the timing relative to your planning cycle, the audience targeting story, or something about the format itself?

Between Kimberley Gardiner on the brand side and Carrie Parks at Carmichael Lynch, who owns the final call on contextual video and weather-triggered inventory — and is that decision made at the agency or does it go back to Brentwood for CMO sign-off?

With $14M going into paid search, how is TSC currently measuring the upstream media — broadcast, CTV, paid social — that's driving the demand search is capturing, and is there a model you're using to connect those two?

As you plan for spring tentpole moments like lawn season and Memorial Day, are weather triggers and seasonal transitions already built into the campaign calendar, or is that still primarily driven by the retail promotional calendar?

The Retail Technology intent signal we're seeing this week — is that related to in-store tech, media measurement, or something on the e-commerce side? Understanding where that focus sits would help me figure out if there's a relevant conversation to be had beyond media.

sales_plays.title: Beyond the PMP: Expand Into Premium Video & CTV
sales_plays.summary: Tropical Smoothie Cafe is an active TWC Interactive PMP customer with two consecutive won deals but only $267 in recognized CRM revenue against a $21.3M total media budget. The gap between current spend with us and their $4.3M CTV + $4.2M digital allocation signals a major expansion opportunity.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins (2024 and 2025) with Deborah von Kutzleben's team and Horizon Media. Open by acknowledging the partnership is working, then pivot: 'Given the success of the PMP placements, I wanted to bring Deborah and Maria a look at where we think we can do more — specifically against your $4.3M CTV budget, which we're not currently touching.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Horizon Media's Maria Freda: 'How is the current CTV buy being evaluated — are you optimizing against reach, frequency, or lower-funnel conversion metrics like mobile app downloads or online orders?' This surfaces whether there's a performance gap we can solve.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium CTV and weather-triggered digital inventory as a complement to the existing PMP. Tropical Smoothie Cafe's $320K mobile app spend and $353K online video budget suggest they're testing lower-funnel video — we can consolidate and scale that with verified household reach and contextual relevance.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Their total digital + CTV allocation is $8.5M. Current TWC recognized revenue is $267. Even capturing 5% of the CTV budget represents a $215K incremental opportunity. Frame it: 'We're currently a small slice of a large pie — let's talk about what a more integrated buy looks like.'

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute working session with Deborah von Kutzleben and Horizon Media's planning team before the next upfront planning cycle: 'Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar in the next two weeks to walk through a CTV and premium digital proposal built around your Q3 and Q4 tentpole moments?'

sales_plays.title: Summer Smoothie Season: Get Into the Plan Before Budgets Lock
sales_plays.summary: Tropical Smoothie Cafe's core product set — smoothies, bowls, cold beverages — has natural demand peaks tied to warm weather and summer cultural moments. With $10.2M in broadcast and $4.3M in CTV already in market, getting into Horizon Media's Q2-Q3 planning conversation now positions TWC as a preferred partner before budgets are allocated.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Maria Freda at Horizon Media with a planning-forward message: 'We're seeing QSR and fast-casual brands in your portfolio starting Q3 planning conversations now. Given Tropical Smoothie Cafe's seasonal demand curve, I wanted to make sure we're in the room early — especially with their $4.3M CTV budget up for review.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask: 'Is Tropical Smoothie Cafe planning any summer tentpole campaigns — back-to-school, Memorial Day, or a summer LTO push — where incremental reach or weather-triggered targeting could amplify the broadcast buy?' This confirms whether a seasonal window exists and who owns it.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position weather-triggered and daypart-targeted inventory as a precision layer on top of Tropical Smoothie Cafe's existing $10.2M broadcast spend. When temperatures rise, smoothie and bowl consideration spikes — TWC's contextual and weather-activated placements can capture that intent in real time.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Tropical Smoothie Cafe spends $2.1M in paid social and $351K in display — both channels where summer creative is likely already in production. A weather-triggered digital extension of those assets through TWC costs a fraction of net-new creative and extends reach against an audience already in a warm-weather mindset.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a specific, low-lift next step: 'I can put together a one-page summer planning brief — seasonal reach data, weather-trigger examples from comparable QSR brands, and a proposed flight window — and have it to you and the Horizon team by end of next week. Does that work?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Deborah, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with your team and Horizon Media on the TWC Interactive PMP for the past two years running — both deals closed and in market — so I wanted to reach out directly because I think we're leaving a meaningful opportunity on the table together. You're running $4.3M in CTV and $4.2M in digital this year, and right now we're only touching a small piece of that. I'd love to change that. I was looking at your media mix — the broadcast anchor, the growing CTV investment, the mobile app and online video spend — and it maps really well to what we do best: premium video environments, weather-contextual targeting, and verified household reach. Tropical Smoothie Cafe's products are inherently seasonal and weather-sensitive, which means there's a real case for getting more precise about when and where you're showing up. Can we find 30 minutes with you and the Horizon planning team to walk through what a more integrated buy could look like heading into summer?
open_questions: How is Horizon Media currently evaluating the performance of the TWC Interactive PMP placements — and what metrics would need to move for you to consider expanding the investment into CTV?
Who at Tropical Smoothie Cafe or Horizon Media owns the CTV planning and buying decision — is that sitting with Maria Freda's team or with Deborah's brand team directly?
Is there a Q2 or Q3 summer campaign in planning right now — a seasonal LTO, a warm-weather push, or a back-to-school moment — where incremental reach or weather-triggered targeting is on the table?
How is the $2.1M paid social budget being evaluated relative to CTV — are those treated as separate channels or is there a unified video strategy across both?
What would it take to move TWC from a PMP line item to a preferred video partner across Tropical Smoothie Cafe's full digital and CTV investment?

sales_plays.title: Expand Beyond PMP Into Premium Digital Suite
sales_plays.summary: UPMC has won two consecutive PMP deals (Jan 2025 and Jul 2024) through Chemistry Communications, but their $5.4M digital spend and $2.6M CTV budget suggest significant untapped inventory beyond the current PMP relationship. This play targets Dean Walters and Jason Dille to expand wallet share across CTV, Online Video, and Mobile App.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the two consecutive PMP wins with Chemistry Communications (Jul 2024 and Jan 2025) and acknowledge the momentum. Email Jason Dille at Chemistry: 'Jason, we've had a strong run on the UPMC PMP placements — two consecutive wins is a great foundation. I'd love 20 minutes to show you where we're seeing the highest-performing inventory for health systems right now, specifically in CTV and Mobile App, given UPMC's $2.6M CTV and $1.5M Mobile App allocations.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Jason Dille: 'Of UPMC's $5.4M digital budget, how much is currently committed to partners outside our PMP — and are there specific audience segments like health-system patients or insurance-eligible households where you feel the current buy is underdelivering?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC Interactive's CTV and Online Video inventory as a natural extension of the existing PMP relationship — same trusted partner, broader reach across UPMC's western and central Pennsylvania service footprint, with addressable targeting for their 40-hospital network and 700+ outpatient centers.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): UPMC currently spends $779K on Online Video and $1.5M on Mobile App. Even a 15% shift of unallocated digital budget toward TWC Interactive's premium CTV and video units represents $300K–$400K in incremental revenue. Frame it as consolidating spend with a proven partner rather than adding a new vendor.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jason Dille for a 20-minute working session within the next two weeks: 'Can we get 20 minutes on the calendar before end of month to walk through a custom audience proposal for UPMC's CTV and Mobile App lines? I'll bring specific reach data for the Pittsburgh and western PA DMA.'

sales_plays.title: Retail Technology Intent — Insurance Enrollment Timing Hook
sales_plays.summary: Bombora shows UPMC is actively researching Retail Technology this week, which for a health system and insurer of UPMC's scale likely signals exploration of consumer-facing digital tools, retail health clinic expansion, or insurance enrollment technology. This is a low-friction entry point to engage Dean Walters on a forward-looking campaign before a potential enrollment or service-launch window.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the Bombora Retail Technology intent signal carefully and without overstating it. Reach out to Dean Walters directly: 'Dean, we're seeing signals that UPMC is actively exploring retail technology — whether that's consumer-facing health tools, retail clinic expansion, or insurance enrollment platforms, it tells us there may be a campaign or initiative on the horizon. Given our existing relationship through Chemistry, I wanted to get ahead of it.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Dean Walters: 'Is the retail technology exploration tied to a specific consumer initiative — like a new enrollment push, a retail health clinic rollout, or a digital patient acquisition campaign — and is there a launch window we should be building media strategy around?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): If the signal is tied to insurance enrollment or retail health expansion, position TWC Interactive's broadcast ($4.4M already in market), CTV, and digital inventory as the ideal awareness-to-action stack for reaching health-insurance-eligible households across UPMC's Pennsylvania, Maryland, and New York footprint ahead of any enrollment window.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): UPMC already invests $4.4M in Broadcast and $2.6M in CTV — a coordinated campaign layering in targeted digital and Online Video ($779K current) around a retail or enrollment moment could amplify reach efficiency without requiring net-new budget, simply reallocating toward a timed initiative with measurable conversion goals.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a brief discovery call within 10 days, before any potential campaign planning locks in: 'Dean, can we get 15 minutes this week or next to understand the initiative behind the retail technology interest? I want to make sure Chemistry has our inventory options in hand before planning gets too far along — Jason Dille is already a great partner on the PMP side and we can move fast.'

talk_track_opener: Hey Dean, this is [Rep Name] from TWC Interactive — we've been working closely with Jason Dille at Chemistry Communications on the UPMC PMP buys, and we've had two strong wins together going back to mid-2024. I wanted to reach out directly because I think there's a bigger opportunity we haven't fully explored yet. UPMC is running $5.4M in digital and $2.6M in CTV, and right now our relationship is really just touching the PMP layer of that. We're also seeing some intent signals suggesting UPMC is actively researching retail technology, which caught my attention given the scale of your insurance and outpatient business. I'd love to ask you one question: of the digital and CTV budget that's currently going to partners outside our existing PMP, is there a specific audience or geography — maybe western PA or your Maryland markets — where you feel like the current media mix isn't delivering the patient acquisition or brand awareness results you need? That's exactly where I think we can add value, and I'd rather have that conversation now before your next planning cycle locks in.
open_questions: Of UPMC's $5.4M digital budget, how much is currently allocated to TWC Interactive beyond the PMP placements — and are there digital sub-channels like Mobile App or Online Video where Chemistry is looking to consolidate vendors?
Who at UPMC or Chemistry owns the CTV planning decision — is that Jason Dille's team exclusively, or does Dean Walters weigh in on channel-level allocations above a certain threshold?

The Bombora data shows UPMC actively researching retail technology this week — is that tied to a specific consumer initiative like an insurance enrollment campaign or retail health clinic launch that would require dedicated media support?

UPMC runs $4.4M in Broadcast alongside $2.6M in CTV — is the current strategy to run those as separate buys with different partners, or is there an appetite to consolidate convergent TV planning under one trusted vendor relationship?

Given that Chemistry Communications holds AOR scope for creative, media planning, and media buying, does Jason Dille have full authority to expand the TWC Interactive relationship into new channels, or does a spend increase above a certain level require sign-off from Dean Walters directly?

sales_plays.title: Shift Digital Dollars On-Property
sales_plays.summary: UPS Store is running $31.5M in digital spend heavily weighted toward paid social ($19.8M) and search ($26.7M) — both off-property, third-party environments. This play targets displacing a slice of that budget toward a direct, owned-audience solution with stronger intent signals and lower CPL waste.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email CJ Gallagher referencing UPS Store's $19.8M paid social + $2.4M display allocation and open with: 'We've seen retail brands at your spend level hit diminishing returns on paid social CPMs — I'd love to share what a comparable shift has looked like for brands moving budget on-property.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask CJ: 'Of your $31.5M digital budget, how much is currently tied to performance KPIs vs. brand awareness — and which channel is Initiative flagging as underperforming heading into Q3 planning?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position our on-property audience solution against the $19.8M paid social spend: 'Rather than renting Meta's audience, we can deliver in-market small business owners and shipping-intent consumers directly — the exact segment UPS Store needs at tentpole moments like back-to-school and holiday.'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the math: 'If we captured even 10% of your $19.8M paid social budget — roughly $2M — and delivered a 15% lower CPL than your current social benchmarks, that's a measurable efficiency gain Initiative can take back to planning.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'Can we get 20 minutes on the calendar before Initiative's Q3 media plan locks — ideally in the next two weeks — so I can show you one comparable retail case study and a proposed audience overlay?'

sales_plays.title: Agency Door via Elizabeth Paul
sales_plays.summary: With no prior CRM history and CJ Gallagher as a mid-level marketing manager, the fastest path to a qualified conversation is through Initiative's Elizabeth Paul, EVP & Chief Brand Officer, who controls media planning and buying for UPS Store's $123.6M total spend.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Connect with Elizabeth Paul on LinkedIn with a brief note: 'Elizabeth — I work with several retail brands Initiative manages and wanted to share a media efficiency angle relevant to UPS Store's digital mix heading into H2. Would a quick intro call make sense?'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Elizabeth: 'For UPS Store's planning cycle, how is Initiative currently thinking about the balance between UPS Store's $26.7M search investment and upper-funnel digital — and is there appetite to test a new on-property channel before holiday planning locks?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position as an agency-friendly partner: 'We work directly through Initiative's buying workflow and can provide incremental reach against small business and consumer shipping audiences that complement what search and paid social are already doing for UPS Store.'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Reference the full spend picture: 'With $123.6M in total media, even a small test — say $500K carved from the $2.4M display line — gives Initiative a clean A/B data set to bring to CJ and the UPS Store brand team with real performance numbers.'

Step 5 (Next Step): 'If I send over a one-page audience and format overview tailored to UPS Store's retail tentpole calendar, would you be willing to loop in the right Initiative planner for a 15-minute alignment call this month?'

talk_track_opener: Hey CJ — I'm [Rep Name] with [Company]. We work with a number of national retail brands managing media at scale, and I came across UPS Store's media footprint while doing some research on how brands in your space are optimizing digital mix heading into the back half of the year. I wanted to reach out directly because the profile stood out to me.
open_questions: How is Initiative currently evaluating the performance of UPS Store's $19.8M paid social investment — are there specific KPIs where you're seeing efficiency plateau?
Who at UPS Store — CJ Gallagher or someone above him — has final sign-off on net-new channel tests, and does that decision flow through Initiative or directly through the brand team in San Diego?
With $26.7M in search already capturing bottom-funnel intent, is there a gap in how UPS Store is reaching small business owners earlier in the shipping decision journey — before they hit Google?

Is Initiative currently in Q3 and Q4 media planning for UPS Store, and are there specific retail tentpole moments — back-to-school, holiday shipping season — where the current channel mix feels underweighted?
EP+Co. handles digital and social creative for UPS Store — is there a workflow where new digital channel tests need EP+Co. creative involvement, or can Initiative run a test with existing assets?
