sales_plays.title: Uber Mobility Expansion: From PMP to Premium Direct
sales_plays.summary: Uber is an active TWC customer with three consecutive PMP wins (2024–2026) and a live $200K Airport/Spring Travelers Q1'26 proposal. The play converts their programmatic PMP relationship into higher-value direct and premium inventory buys, expanding wallet share against their $328.5M total media footprint.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the three consecutive PMP wins (2024, 2025, 2026) and the active Airport/Spring Travelers Q1'26 proposal at $200K. Open with: 'You've trusted TWC's PMP inventory three years running — I want to show you what a direct buy unlocks that PMP can't, especially heading into spring travel season.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Sam McCallum at Omnicom: 'Beyond the PMP line item, how is Omnicom currently allocating Uber's $115M digital budget across weather-contextual vs. non-contextual placements — and is there a direct-buy test budget earmarked for Q2?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's direct and premium CTV inventory against Uber's lost $500K Suburb Mobility Awareness deal (2H25). Acknowledge the loss and reframe: 'The 2H25 Suburb Mobility campaign didn't close — let's diagnose what the direct buy needs to look like so we're not leaving that $500K on the table again in 2H26.'

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Uber spends $33.6M in CTV and $25.8M in Mobile App. Even a 3% reallocation toward TWC premium direct inventory represents ~$1.8M in incremental spend. Frame the Airport/Spring proposal as the proof-of-concept that unlocks that larger conversation.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Sam McCallum for a 30-minute working session before March 15 to align the Airport/Spring Q1'26 proposal to a confirmed close, and to table a Q2 direct-buy brief for Jill Hazelbaker's team — while spring travel planning is still open.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Budget: Weather-Intent vs. Broad Social
sales_plays.summary: Uber allocates $29.6M to Paid Social — a channel with no weather-intent signal and high CPM competition. This play targets that budget line by positioning TWC's weather-triggered audience targeting as a superior demand-capture mechanism for mobility decisions, directly displacing a portion of Uber's Meta/social spend.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with a data observation to Sam McCallum: 'Uber is running $29.6M through Paid Social. Weather is the single biggest real-time trigger for ride-share demand — rain, extreme heat, winter storms. Paid Social can't activate on that signal. TWC can.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'When Omnicom built the $29.6M Paid Social allocation, was weather-triggered mobility demand modeled as a separate audience segment — or is it bundled into broad behavioral targeting?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-triggered programmatic and direct inventory as a precision displacement of broad social: riders searching for alternatives during weather events are in active decision mode, not passive scroll mode. TWC reaches them at the moment of need, not before or after.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 10% reallocation of Uber's $29.6M Paid Social budget equals $2.96M. Benchmark that against the $1 PMP wins and the $200K spring proposal — a weather-intent displacement test at even $500K–$1M would be 2–5x Uber's current direct TWC commitment and measurable against ride-request lift.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive analysis deck — delivered to Sam McCallum and cc'd to Jill Hazelbaker's team — comparing weather-triggered audience CPMs vs. Uber's current Paid Social CPMs, with a specific Q2 test proposal ready by March 21.

talk_track_opener: Hey Sam, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together on Uber's PMP buys for three consecutive years now — 2024, 2025, and 2026 — and we have the Airport and Spring Travelers proposal active right now at $200K. I appreciate that relationship and I don't take it for granted. I want to make sure we're bringing Omnicom and Uber's team real value beyond the PMP line, especially as you're planning Q2 and beyond with Jill's team.
open_questions: The 2H25 Suburb Mobility Awareness campaign was lost at $500K — can you share what the deciding factor was so we can structure the 2H26 conversation differently?
Who on Jill Hazelbaker's team at Uber owns the final sign-off on direct media buys above $250K — is that a CMO-level decision or does it sit with a media director?
How is Omnicom currently segmenting Uber's $115M digital budget between always-on programmatic and campaign-specific direct buys — and is there a dedicated test-and-learn budget for Q2?
Is the $29.6M Paid Social allocation locked for 2026, or is there flexibility to reallocate toward channels that can activate on real-time weather and mobility intent signals?
For the Airport/Spring Travelers Q1'26 proposal at $200K — what does Uber need to see in terms of audience targeting or measurement to move from proposal to confirmed buy before March 15?

sales_plays.title: PMP to Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Under Armour has run TWC Interactive PMPs for three consecutive years (2024, 2025, 2026). With $50.4M in digital spend — $47.5M of which is in Paid Social — there is clear appetite to diversify into higher-impact, on-property placements beyond the existing PMP relationship.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Grace Teng at OMD referencing the three consecutive TWC Interactive PMP wins and note that Under Armour's digital mix is heavily concentrated in Paid Social ($47.5M). Open with: 'Grace, given the three-year PMP relationship we've built with Under Armour, I wanted to share some on-property formats that could complement what you're already running with us and reduce reliance on social inventory.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Grace: 'Outside of the TWC Interactive PMP, where is OMD currently allocating Under Armour's $50.4M digital budget for performance-driven video and display — and is there a format gap you're trying to solve for in 2026?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position premium on-property placements (high-impact display, OLV, or sponsorship units) as a brand-safe, weather-contextual complement to the existing PMP — directly addressing the $199K Internet Display and $200K Online Video lines that are dramatically underfunded relative to the $47.5M Paid Social concentration.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Under Armour's Online Video spend is $200K and Display is $199K — together less than 1% of their $50.4M digital budget. Even a modest reallocation of 2-3% ($1M–$1.5M) into premium on-property video and display would represent a 3-4x expansion of the current TWC relationship beyond the nominal $1 PMP deals.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Grace for a 20-minute working session in the next two weeks: 'Can we get 20 minutes on the calendar before the end of the month to walk through two or three on-property formats that fit Under Armour's performance goals? I'll bring a short deck scoped to their current mix.'

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Concentration
sales_plays.summary: Under Armour's digital spend is dangerously concentrated — $47.5M of $50.4M (94%) sits in Paid Social. With Bombora showing active interest in Agencies this week and James Michelfelder owning global content, there is an opening to position premium video and contextual inventory as a displacement of over-indexed social spend.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to James Michelfelder, Senior Creative Director, Global Content, referencing the Bombora signal: 'James, we're seeing Under Armour actively researching agency and media mix topics right now — wanted to connect directly given your role in global content to share how brands in your category are diversifying away from Paid Social concentration.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask James: 'With $47.5M running through Paid Social, what's the internal conversation around brand-safe, premium video environments — and is there pressure from leadership to reduce dependence on any single platform?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's premium video and contextual weather-driven inventory as a high-reach, brand-safe alternative that delivers the performance metrics Under Armour needs without the platform risk of concentrating 94% of digital spend in Paid Social. Tie to Under Armour's performance brand identity — athletes train in all conditions, and weather-contextual targeting is a natural creative fit.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If Under Armour shifts just 5% of the $47.5M Paid Social budget ($2.375M) into premium on-property and OLV, it would represent a 2,375x increase over the current $1 PMP deal value and meaningfully diversify their digital risk profile.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a brief creative alignment call with both James Michelfelder and Grace Teng at OMD: 'I'd love to set up a 30-minute call with you and Grace Teng at OMD to show how two or three of our formats map directly to Under Armour's content priorities — can we find time in the next 10 days?'

talk_track_opener: Hey Grace, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company. We've been working with Under Armour through OMD for three consecutive years now — the TWC Interactive PMP has renewed in 2024, 2025, and again in January 2026, so I wanted to reach out as a true partner, not just a vendor. We've built a real foundation here and I think there's a meaningful opportunity to grow it together this year. I was looking at Under Armour's digital mix ahead of this call and one number really stood out — $47.5M of their $50.4M digital budget is sitting in Paid Social, which is about 94% concentration on a single channel type. Meanwhile, their Online Video and Display lines are each under $200K. Given that Under Armour is actively researching agency and media mix topics right now, I'd love to ask: is there a conversation happening internally about diversifying that mix, and is there a format or environment you feel is currently underserved in the plan?
open_questions: Grace, outside of the TWC Interactive PMP, which platforms are currently receiving the largest share of Under Armour's $50.4M digital budget, and is OMD evaluating any rebalancing for the back half of 2026?

sales_plays.title: HeatTech Season Expansion
sales_plays.summary: Uniqlo is an active customer with a won PMP in 2025 and $60.8M in total media spend. The Q4 2024 HeatTech campaign loss represents unfinished business — this play converts the existing PMP relationship into a broader seasonal direct buy before the fall/winter planning window opens.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the active 2025 PMP win and the Q4 2024 HeatTech campaign loss directly with Nicolas Cessot or Jill Metcalf at EssenceMediaCom: 'We're live together on the 2025 PMP, and I wanted to revisit the HeatTech campaign conversation from last November — we lost that one, but the weather targeting case for HeatTech is stronger than ever heading into Q4 planning.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'For the HeatTech and fall/winter tentpole campaigns, is EssenceMediaCom planning media buys against cold-weather trigger moments, and what does the current brief look like for Q4 2025?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Uniqlo's HeatTech line is inherently weather-dependent. TWC's dynamic weather-triggered ad units and CTV inventory allow Uniqlo to activate HeatTech creative precisely when temperatures drop in key markets — the exact moment a consumer is motivated to buy. The 2024 loss shows the budget existed ($50K); the play is to expand scope and format.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Uniqlo spends $6.6M in CTV and $10.8M in Online Video. A HeatTech direct buy layering weather-triggered CTV and OLV on top of the existing PMP would represent a modest 5–10% incremental shift — roughly $350K–$700K — against channels already in the plan, with measurable lift in cold-weather markets.
Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Jill Metcalf for a 30-minute Q4 planning call in the next two weeks to present a weather-triggered HeatTech activation proposal before EssenceMediaCom finalizes Q4 budgets.

sales_plays.title: Paid Social Budget Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Uniqlo allocates $26M — 61% of its digital spend — to Paid Social, a channel facing rising CPMs and measurement headwinds. This play positions TWC's premium contextual and audience-targeted inventory as a performance-efficient alternative to capture share of that concentrated social budget.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Open with a data-led observation to Nicolas Cessot: 'Uniqlo's digital mix shows roughly $26M in Paid Social — that's a significant concentration in a channel where CPMs have climbed and signal loss post-ATT has made attribution harder. I'd like to show you what we're seeing from retail advertisers who've shifted 10–15% of that budget toward contextual premium inventory.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): 'How is EssenceMediaCom currently evaluating the efficiency of the Paid Social allocation versus other digital channels, and is there appetite to test incremental reach against audiences you're not fully capturing on social?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): TWC's contextual targeting reaches high-intent consumers in a brand-safe, weather-relevant environment — particularly valuable for Uniqlo's basics and outerwear categories where purchase intent correlates with seasonal conditions. This is an off-social, on-premium-property shift that diversifies reach without abandoning performance accountability.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): A 10% reallocation of the $26M Paid Social budget equals $2.6M. Against Uniqlo's current TWC relationship (active PMP, $60.8M total spend), even a $500K–$1M test displacement buy would represent a 10x+ expansion of the TWC relationship and provide a clean A/B comparison against social CPMs.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a competitive analysis deck — delivered within one week — benchmarking Uniqlo's Paid Social CPMs and reach against TWC's retail audience segments, and request 30 minutes with Jill Metcalf at EssenceMediaCom to walk through it before the next media planning cycle.

talk_track_opener: Hey Nicolas — I'm [rep name] from The Weather Company. We've been working together on the PMP this year, and I wanted to reach out directly because I think we're underleveraged given the scale of what Uniqlo is doing in market. You're running $60M-plus in media annually, with serious investment in CTV and Online Video, and I see a real opportunity to connect that spend to weather-driven purchase moments in a way that goes beyond what the PMP alone can do. The HeatTech campaign conversation from last Q4 stuck with me — the budget was there, the timing was right, and I think we can build a stronger case this cycle. I've also been looking at how your digital mix is weighted, and I'd love to get your read on how the Paid Social allocation is performing relative to your other channels. Would you be open to a quick call in the next couple of weeks — I want to come in with something specific to your Q4 planning, not a generic pitch.

sales_plays.title: Expand Beyond Summer Heat Into Full-Season Portfolio
sales_plays.summary: United Rentals is an active customer with a won Summer Heat Campaign ($60K, Q3 2025) and two PMP wins. Their total media footprint is $4.7M, but TWC share is small. The goal is to expand wallet share by adding formats and quarters beyond the existing summer buy.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the Summer Heat Campaign win closed April 30, 2025. Email Robert Halsey directly: 'Robert, the Summer Heat buy is locked in — great. Given the $3.2M you're putting into Search and the $58K in Online Video, I want to show you how layering Weather Channel contextual video into Q4 and Q1 can extend the reach of those same dollars into high-intent demand windows.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Robert Halsey: 'Outside of the summer campaign, which quarters are you seeing the highest cost-per-lead in Search right now — and is LAVIDGE optimizing those buys against weather-triggered audience segments or purely keyword-based?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's contextual targeting against United Rentals' $3.2M Search spend. Construction and industrial rental demand spikes around weather events (storms, freeze-thaw cycles, spring thaw). TWC can intercept that intent before it reaches Google, lowering CPC and increasing qualified reach for earthmoving and aerial platform rentals.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): United Rentals' current TWC investment is ~$60K against a $4.7M total spend — roughly 1.3% share. A modest expansion to $150K–$200K across Q3–Q1 would still represent under 5% of total spend while adding weather-contextual video ($58K current OLV budget is a natural match) and display layers ($5K current display is significantly under-indexed).

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask for a 30-minute working session with Robert Halsey and Bob Case at LAVIDGE before June 15 to present a cross-quarter media plan that maps TWC inventory to United Rentals' seasonal demand calendar — specifically Q4 2025 and Q1 2026 planning windows.

sales_plays.title: Weather-Driven Demand Window: Get Ahead of Q4 Construction Season
sales_plays.summary: United Rentals serves construction, industrial, and government customers whose rental needs are influenced by weather cycles. With Q4 planning underway and a prior lost Construction Test Campaign ($50K, Q2 2025), there is an opportunity to re-enter the construction vertical conversation before budgets are locked.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the lost Construction Test Campaign from Q2 2025. Reach out to Bob Case at LAVIDGE: 'Bob, the Construction Test Campaign from Q2 didn't land — I'd like to understand what fell short and bring a revised approach for Q4 before LAVIDGE finalizes United Rentals' fall media plan. Weather-driven construction demand windows are a real planning lever we haven't fully activated together.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Bob Case: 'For United Rentals' construction vertical, is LAVIDGE currently planning any media that activates around weather triggers — freeze events, storm recovery, spring thaw — or is the Q4 construction push primarily evergreen Search and Social?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-contextual targeting as a planning-stage tool, not just a reactive buy. Construction equipment rental demand rises predictably around regional weather events. TWC can build audience segments tied to those moments — reaching project managers and site supervisors when conditions create urgent rental needs, something United Rentals' $36K Paid Social and $5K Display budgets are not currently capturing.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): The lost Q2 Construction Campaign was $50K. A Q4 weather-triggered construction play at a similar investment level, but built around TWC's contextual audience data, gives LAVIDGE a differentiated proof point for United Rentals' construction vertical — and a direct comparison to the Q3 Summer Heat win ($60K) to demonstrate incremental value across seasons.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a brief 20-minute call with Bob Case at LAVIDGE by June 20 to walk through a Q4 construction demand calendar and identify two or three weather trigger moments where TWC inventory can be pre-planned — before United Rentals' Q4 budgets are allocated.

We've built a strong foundation with the PMP deals and the summer buy, and I think there's a real opportunity to extend that into the construction and industrial verticals where weather is genuinely driving rental demand. Looking at your media mix, you're putting $3.2M into Search and $58K into Online Video — those are strong signals that you're investing in high-intent moments. What I'm seeing is that a meaningful share of that construction and industrial rental intent starts with a weather event — a storm, a freeze-thaw cycle, a regional disruption — and right now TWC isn't in the plan for those windows. sales_plays.title: Optum Rx Digital Expansion: From PMP to Premium Direct
sales_plays.summary: UnitedHealth Group has two consecutive PMP wins (Jan 2026, Feb 2025) but both closed at nominal $1 values, suggesting programmatic placeholder buys rather than meaningful direct investment. With $70.6M in digital spend and Steve Cragle leading Optum Rx marketing, there is a clear path to convert PMP activity into a premium direct buy.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reference the back-to-back PMP wins in Feb 2025 and Jan 2026 with Steve Cragle or route through Annemarie Norris at Horizon Media. Open with: 'We've had two consecutive PMP activations with Optum Rx — I want to show you what a direct, audience-guaranteed buy looks like against the same inventory at scale.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Annemarie Norris: 'Of the $70.6M UHG is deploying in digital, how much of Optum Rx's allocation is currently running through open or private exchange versus direct publisher relationships — and is there a performance gap between the two?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position direct-sold premium inventory against the known pain of PMP price volatility and audience dilution. Optum Rx needs verified, brand-safe reach for prescription benefit messaging — PMP cannot guarantee that at the CPM floors a healthcare brand requires.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): UHG spends $70.6M in digital and $17.0M in CTV. Even a 5% shift of digital budget from programmatic to direct equals ~$3.5M in incremental direct spend. The two prior PMP wins prove the relationship exists; the $150,000 lost open enrollment opp in Q3/Q4 2023 shows there is budget authority for meaningful buys when the pitch is right.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Annemarie Norris for a 30-minute working session before the next Horizon Media quarterly planning cycle to present a direct-buy proposal mapped to Optum Rx's 2026 media calendar — specifically targeting the open enrollment window where the $150K opp was previously lost.

sales_plays.title: Displace Paid Social Spend: Retail Health Intent Intercept
sales_plays.summary: UHG allocates $52.4M to Paid Social — the single largest digital line item — while Bombora signals show active interest in Retail Technology this week. This creates a displacement opportunity: redirect a portion of social spend toward contextually relevant, intent-matched inventory that reaches consumers already researching retail health and pharmacy topics.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Lead with the Bombora signal directly to Steve Cragle: 'Our intent data shows UnitedHealth Group's audience is actively researching Retail Technology right now — that maps directly to Optum Rx's pharmacy benefit and retail clinic positioning. Are you capturing that intent on social, or is it going to competitors?'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Steve Cragle: 'Of the $52.4M running through Paid Social for UHG brands, what share is attributed to Optum Rx specifically, and how are you measuring incremental reach versus audiences you're already retargeting?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Social platforms deliver frequency against known audiences but struggle with net-new intent capture. Our inventory intercepts consumers mid-research — when they are actively comparing pharmacy benefit options and retail health services — not after they've already engaged with a UHG brand asset.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): If even 3% of the $52.4M Paid Social budget ($1.57M) is reallocated to intent-matched contextual inventory, Optum Rx gains incremental reach among in-market Retail Technology researchers that social algorithms cannot efficiently identify. The lost $150,000 open enrollment opp in 2023 suggests budget exists when the targeting rationale is compelling.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer to build a side-by-side audience overlap analysis — UHG's current Paid Social reach versus our Retail Technology intent segment — and present it to Annemarie Norris at Horizon Media within the next two weeks, before the next paid social flight is locked.

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's weather-driven audience intelligence and seasonal content moments — summer heat driving back-to-school shopping intent — as a way to reach JanSport's core youth and parent audience at the exact moment purchase decisions are forming, something Paid Social at $2.9M alone cannot time precisely.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Back-to-school is one of retail's top-3 spending windows. JanSport's current digital footprint ($5K Mobile App, $67K Display) suggests the brand is underinvested in contextual and moment-based media. A $50K–$75K tentpole activation would mirror the scale of the lost North Face Rainwear pitch ($50K, Q1/Q2 2025) but target a brand with no prior TWC investment — pure incremental revenue.

Step 5 (Next Step): Propose a brief planning call with Lindsay and loop in Shannon Doyle at Party Land within the next 10 business days — frame it as a 'back-to-school readiness check' to ensure JanSport has a weather and cultural-moment strategy before the Q3 brief is finalized.

talk_track_opener: Hey Lindsay, this is [Rep Name] from The Weather Company — we've been partnered with VF Corporation on the PMP side since early 2025 and I work closely with the team on making sure the right brands in the portfolio are getting the most out of that relationship. I wanted to reach out directly to you because JanSport and Eastpak aren't yet part of the conversation and I think there's a real opportunity there. Looking at how VF Corp is currently invested — $2.9M in Paid Social, $123K in Online Video, and a live PMP — JanSport feels underrepresented in upper-funnel and contextual media, especially heading into back-to-school season. We're also seeing VF Corp showing active intent around Entertainment content this week, which made me curious: is that tied to something JanSport or Eastpak is planning, or is it more of a North Face initiative? I'd love 20 minutes to understand where JanSport's Q3 plan stands and whether there's a gap we could help fill before budgets get locked.
open_questions: For JanSport and Eastpak, is the current $2.9M Paid Social investment managed separately from the North Face budget, or does it roll up into a single VF Corp media plan that Party Land executes?
Who ultimately approves incremental media spend for JanSport — is that a decision you own, Lindsay, or does it require sign-off above the brand level at VF Corp?
The Bombora signal we're seeing around Entertainment this week — is that tied to a JanSport cultural partnership or sponsorship you're exploring, or is it coming from another brand in the portfolio?
Back-to-school is JanSport's biggest retail moment — has the Q3 media brief already been submitted to Party Land, or is there still a window to add a contextual or CTV layer to the plan?

Given that the North Face CTV and video pitches didn't move forward in 2025, is there a specific format or targeting capability that would need to be demonstrated for a JanSport video pilot to get internal approval?

sales_plays.title: PMP to Premium Direct Upgrade
sales_plays.summary: Valvoline has run two consecutive PMP wins (2024 and 2025) through Novus Media, but total SFDC revenue is only $1,705 — a signal that spend is flowing through low-CPM programmatic pipes rather than premium direct placements. This play converts that PMP relationship into higher-value, on-property direct buys.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Email Annie Scranton at Novus Media referencing the two consecutive PMP wins (July 2024 and Feb 2025) and open with: 'We've delivered on both Valvoline PMP commitments — I want to show you what the same budget unlocks in direct inventory before Q3 planning closes.'

Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Annie: 'For Valvoline's $22.8M digital budget, what percentage is currently allocated to guaranteed direct vs. programmatic — and is Robert Kenny pushing for more brand-safe, premium environments heading into summer?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Position TWC's on-property direct inventory against the $1.9M Internet Display and $754K Online Video lines — both of which are under-indexed relative to Valvoline's $5.2M CTV and $15.9M Paid Social spend, suggesting premium video and display are gaps we can fill.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Frame the upgrade: if even 10% of Valvoline's $22.8M digital budget shifts from open-market PMP to direct, that's $2.28M in incremental premium spend — against a current TWC footprint of roughly $1,705 in recognized revenue. Show CPM efficiency and brand-safety lift vs. open exchange.

Step 5 (Next Step): Ask Annie for a 20-minute working session with Robert Kenny's team before June 30 to present a direct vs. programmatic comparison using Valvoline's own spend mix — offer to bring a custom proposal tied to their Q3 flight dates.

sales_plays.title: Entertainment Intent to Weather-Contextual Displacement
sales_plays.summary: Bombora shows Valvoline actively researching Entertainment this week — likely tied to sponsorship or content-driven media buys that may be going to a competitor platform. This play intercepts that budget before it lands elsewhere by positioning TWC's weather-contextual and entertainment-adjacent inventory as a smarter contextual fit for automotive services.
steps: Step 1 (Opening Move): Reach out to Robert Kenny directly: 'Our intent data flagged Valvoline researching Entertainment media this week — before that budget gets allocated, I want to show you how TWC's contextual targeting connects weather-driven service demand moments to entertainment-adjacent audiences at scale.'
Step 2 (Discovery Question): Ask Robert: 'Is the Entertainment interest tied to a sponsorship, streaming, or content play — and who else is in the consideration set for that budget right now?'

Step 3 (Solution Bridge): Displace the entertainment platform consideration by showing that TWC's audience — people actively checking weather before road trips, commutes, and seasonal driving — is a higher-intent automotive audience than a general entertainment context. Reference that Valvoline's $18.7M search budget proves they already value intent; TWC extends that intent upstream.

Step 4 (Value Quantification): Valvoline spends $15.9M on Paid Social and $18.7M on Search — both intent and reach plays. If TWC captures even a $500K test from the Entertainment budget under consideration, it represents a 29x increase over current TWC recognized revenue ($1,705) and opens a new content/contextual line item in Novus Media's plan.

Step 5 (Next Step): Offer Robert Kenny a 15-minute call this week — before the Entertainment budget decision is finalized — to present a one-page contextual displacement brief comparing TWC weather-intent audiences vs. entertainment platform reach for Valvoline Instant Oil Change seasonal demand windows.

talk_track_opener: Hey Robert, I'm [rep name] from The Weather Company — we've been working with Valvoline through Novus Media on your PMP buys going back to mid-2024, so we have real history together and I know Annie's team well. We've delivered on both commitments and I want to make sure we're bringing you more than just programmatic pipes.
open_questions: Novus Media has run two PMP buys with us for Valvoline — are those being evaluated for renewal or upgrade in the current planning cycle, and who at Valvoline signs off on moving from programmatic to direct?

Robert, the $18.7M search budget tells me Valvoline values high-intent audiences — is there appetite to extend that intent strategy into contextual display and video environments ahead of summer driving season?

The Bombora signal shows Valvoline researching Entertainment this week — is that tied to a specific sponsorship or streaming buy, and is Novus Media running that RFP or is it being handled directly by Hill Holliday?

Valvoline's Online Video spend is only $754K against a $5.2M CTV budget — is the video strategy intentionally skewed toward connected TV, or is there room to test premium digital video placements in Q3?

For the Valvoline Instant Oil Change network specifically, are seasonal weather windows — spring and fall temperature swings — already built into the media calendar, or is that a planning gap we could help Novus address?
